['R. S. REPORTS.
 SALT LAKE STAKE.
 The Quarterly Conference  of Relief So-
cieties, Young Ladies' and  Primary  Asso-
ciations was held  in the  Fourteenth Ward
Assembly Uooms,  March 19  and  20, 1880.
Reports  received  for  the  quarter  ending
March  15.  Meeting  commenced  at  ten
o'clock  A. M. Friday, March  19. On the
stand were President of R. S. Stake, M.I.
Horne, and her counselors,  Elmina S. Tay-
lor and Sarepta M. Heywood, President Y.
L. M. I. A., Mary A. Freeze, Mrs. Sarah M.
Kimball, Mrs. M. P.  Young, and Secretary
R. S. Stake, Elizabeth Howard. Synopsis of 
proceedings, speeches, etc.:
 The meeting opened by singing. Prayer
by Mrs. Rebecca  Jones. Singing. Secre-
tary read  minutes  of last  Conference  and
reports from  the  branch  Relief Societies.
Pres. Horne made some remarks upon vocal
music;  also urged the sisters to be punctual
in sending  in reports, and to pay attention
to filling  up the  blanks properly, and learn
to be business women. Earnestly requested
that each ward in the Stake  be represented
verbally by its president or her  counselors,
and  invited all  the  presidents  who  were
present to  come and take seats on  the plat-
form.  Made  other  appropriate remarks,
after which  the  Doxology  was sung, and
meeting adjourned  until  2  P. M. Prayer
by Mrs. M. P. Young.
	Afternoon session:  Meeting  opened with
singing and prayer. Pres. Horne addressed
the sisters on  the  subjects  of unity, faith
and the responsibilities of parents, and par-
ticularly mothers. Mrs. Miller, Pres. R. S.
Twentieth Ward, referred to the large num-
ber of poor and aged they had to attend to
in that ward, and eulogized their late presi-
dent, Mrs. Anna Savage. Mrs. E. S. Taylor
spoke of having recently attended the Sis-
ter's Conference of Tooele Stake; said there
were many brethren present, which was a
great help to the sisters in their endeavors
to  do  good. Spoke  upon  unity  and obe-
dience, and the deference which all should
show to the Priesthood, even the humblest
office  pertaining  to  it.  Mrs.  S M. Hey-
wood said she felt much of the stupendous
work that devolves upon the women of this
Territory. We must  work  together  with
the brethren our interest cannot be divided.
A goodly number of us spent  the  strength
of our young days in coming here  to build
this Territory  but  let  us  try  to still
live to  be  useful  and  help  to  roll  on
this work. 
	Mrs Peart,  Farmers  Ward represented
that Society  as  in  good  condition. Mrs
Fielding 5th Ward S.L. City first Counselor
to  Mrs.  Brimley  spoke  encouragingly of
that Society. Mrs. P. Kimball:  I fear we
do not realize the megnitude  of  the work
we are engaged in, we are here to magnify
our position before God and to prepare our-
selves for every  responsibility  and duty in
life. Let us  teach  our  children  to  have
faith that we may be able to save them from
sickness and  death. It is a  credit to any
ward to take care of the poor;  a little thing
that will cheer and comfort  is a blessing. I
like this  world,  God  made it. I like  my
home, sisters let  our  homes be  houses  of
prayer, let us sustain  those  who  are over
us, and pray for them; let us have less pride
and vanity; *  *  *  Mrs. M. R.  Thomp-
son spoke upon benevolence and  the plenty
we  enjoy,  thought  we  should  do every-
thing that would tend to make  happy  the
sick and needy. Made excellent remarks
upon the  "Word  of  Wisdom,"  said we
should try to observe it that we may receive
the promises.
	Mrs. P, Woodruff:  said on one main point
we are united, that is that God speaks from
the heavens to  us. Let us do to others as
we would have  others  do  to us;  our dead
are watching us to  see if we  are faithful,
they are  anxious  we  should  do  for  them
what they  cannot do  for  themselves: and
let us be faithful and true to the priesthood:
and be of that number that will  be blessed
by entering into our Temples and doing the
work for our progenitors.
	Mrs E  Webb;  said she felt the great nec-
essity of our being united;  our labors are to
be exteuded in all directions, let us prepare
ourselves for the great work, we  are called
to do. Mrs. M. Wilcox; said  we  have the 
priesthood in our midst  and  in  proportion
to the strength given by that power through
the servants of God, the adversary  will  do
his  utmost to thwart the good.
 Mrs. A.  Neal  said  she  knew  the  Lord
loved  his daughters  as  well  as he did his
sons, and  we  should attain to  the  same
glory;  thought we should look  well after
our young  people and keep  them from all
outside  society  if  possible;  said  many
mothers are remiss in sending  the children
to the little meetings where they are taught
the ways of the Lord; made other good re-
marks on the subject of woman. 
	Pres. Horne; said a few words and sug-
gested to the sisters to read  the 9th Chapter
of Daniel. Singing. Meeting adjourned to
10  A.  M.  March  20. Benediction. Mrs.
Rachel  R. Grant.
	Statistical and  Financial  reports  of  the
Relief Societies  Salt  Lake  Stake  for  the
Quarter ending March 15, 1880.
 Statistical: teachers, 545;  members 2188;
meetings held 183; average attendance 698;
Woman's EXPONENT, taken 403.
 Financial: On hand at date of last  report
cash  $505,45; property  $949,06; receipts
during the quarter cash  $516,44; property
$478,39. 
	Disbursements: To the needy cash $309,
85;  property $380,  59;  temple cash $70,05; pro-
perty $15, 45; home industries cash $56, 09; 
books cash 14,49; building property 227,30;
Indians, merchandise $140,32; on hand cash
$344,20; property $4,891,08;  wheat 3,631
bushels.  
	This report does not include the 4th, 11th,
and 12th Wards  Salt  Lake  City  nor  Mill
Creek, East Mill Creek,  South Cottonwood,
West  Jordan, nor Mountain Dell Societies. 
These wards were not reported except South 
Cottonwood which did not arrive in time.
 Officers of Stake Organization  of  Relief
Society Salt Lake Co. Mrs. M. Isabella Horne,
Pres. Elmina  S.  Taylor,  and  Sarepta M.
Heywood, Counselors.  Elizabeth  Howard.
Secretary. Mariea R.  Wilcox,  Cor., Sec. 
Bathsheba W. Smith, Treasurer. 

	Report of  Primary Association of Spring
City Sanpete Co. Apr.  1,  1880. Organized
June  29th, 1979, by Sister  M. A. P. Hyde
and Counselors; Sister Ellis was chosen as
President; Sisters Hitchcock and Larson
Cousleors; Mary O. Allred Secretary;  Wm.
Barny Assistant; Flora G. Hyde Treasurer.
Have held 30 meetings;  160,  members  en-
rolled;  average  attendance  70; given  to
Temple $2,80cts; on hand 85 cts.;  4 bushels
of wheat gleaned 9lbs. of beans donated.
			MARY O. ALLRED Secretary.

	The Primary  Association  of American
Fork was  organized  October  23,  1878;
Sister Ann Rowley was appointed President,
Sisters Mary A.  Evans  and  Mary  Mott
counselors. We meet every Saturday after-
noon, at 3 o'clock. The members in at-
tendance, average from 75 to 100.
	The children have pieced  blocks,  for a
very nice quilt, and we have  given  it  as a
donation for the Temple, prized at $6,00.
 January 17th, 1880, we had our  first An-
niversary. Exercises consisted  of Recita-
tions and Singing; The Ariticles of Faith,
Restoration of the Gospel, etc. I think the
children are improving fast,  for  they  take
a great interest in  their  meetings. $3,15
worth if prizes were distributed among the 
children.
	These "Primary  Associations"  through
the blessing of "Our Heavenly Father," will
prove  a  great  benefit;  for  their young
minds are as blank paper, ready to be writ-
ten upon; and those principles  that  are
planted on their young  minds,  are not so
easily  erased for while  young  they  are as
clay, in the hands of the  potter ready to be
molded, to love and  to do the will of God.
	Hoping the Primary, and all other Asso-
ciations throughout the county will continue
to imporove, is my  earnest prayer.
	ANN ROWLEY Pres.
	MARY ELLEN SHELLEY Assist. Sec'y.
	14 years old.

	A few  items from  the Y. L. M. I. A, of
Springvile. The Society numbers 80 mem-
bers. A marked improvement is noticeable
in our meetings, and the desire to learn the
principles of the Gospel, and the duties andprivileges of the gentler  sex  is  demon-
strated by the interest manifested by many
of our sisters both young and old. We hold
weekly meedings of the Society also monthly
conjoint meetings with the Y. M. M. I. A.,
all of which  are spoken highly of, and the 
spirit prevailing at the same is commended
by the authorities of the ward.
	At our annual meeting  which  was held
March 15th the officers of  the  society were
re-elected by a unanimous vote; and the en-
tertainment  given  on  that  occasion will
long be  remembered  by  those  who were
present, to the number of 4 or 500 persons. 
The society have a library  of  47 volumes. 
We also have  some  wheat and  other  pro-
perty on hand. We have paid $25,00  this
winter towards  land  upon  which  to  raise
mulberry trees for  the  production  of silk.
The desire of the society is to be  useful  in
instructing the women  of  our  communtiy
in all good works, and to assist our brethren
who bear the holy Priesthood  in  preparing
the Saints for the advent  of  our  Lord and
Savior Jesus Christ. I subscribe myself
your sister in  the covenant of peace,
			CLARY SNOW, Cor. Sec'y.
	Springville, Apr.  18, 1880. 

	The Relief Society of Clarkston held their 
annual meeting on Thursday the 15th, of
April our worthy Bishop  John  Jardine and
a few of the brethren met  with  us; also
Bro.  Wm.  Griffin  Counselor  to  Bishop
Rigby of Newton and some of the  leading
sisters of that place attended.
 The meeting was addressed by the fol-
lowing named sisters and  brethren. Pres.
Mary Griffin, her two Counselors, Ann Keep
and Sarah Carbine; Marinda Jardine  Pres


asked the children by Sister E. R. Snow;
all the questions were answered promptly.
The Lord's Prayer was then repeated in
concert by them, after which they sang
"Redeemer of Israel."
	The minutes of the last meeting of each
ward, together with a report thereof, were
read by the Secretary of each society.
	The reports exhibited gratifying results,
both of the financial prosperity and religious
interest.
	President Taylor then addressed the au-
dience, directing his remarks mostly to the
children. When the Lord Jesus was upon
the earth, the children crowded around
him, and when they were told to retreat by
His disciples, Jesus said, "Suffer little chil-
dren to come unto me, for of such is the
kingdom of Heaven." Thus He showed
that He loved little children. He loved
them still, and when they were good He
would hear their prayers, and answer them,
though in person He was not with them on
the earth.
	Apostle F. D. Richards read; at the re-
quest of Pres. Taylor, a passage from the
8th chapter of Nephi, where Jesus com-
manded the people on this continent to
bring their children together that He might
bless them, and the signs which took place
in the heavens after this blessing.
	Pres. Taylor desired this passage read to
show how well Jesus loved little children.
The Lord had revealed His will to Joseph
the prophet regarding the principles He
wished observed by the people of the earth;
the Elders had brought our parents to the 
truth, and upon the children would soon
rest the responsibility of carrying on the
work of their parents; therefore they
should be good and kind, and avoid being
harsh and of a lighting nature, in order to
prepare themselves for the work before
them.
	Sister E. R. Snow, by the unanimous re-
quest of the children, next spoke, relating
stories whose morals showed that the pray-
ers of the Elders will heal the sick, also
that carefulness is a necessity in becoming
great,
	Sister Zina D. Young asked a number of
questions regarding interesting items to
the children, and told them to remember
the passage:

"Count that day lest
	Whose low descending sun
Views from thy hand
	No worthy action done."

	Sister Richards Apostle F. D. Richards,
Elder L. J. Herrick, and President D. H.
Peery then followed with pleasing remarks
to the children, each narrating some anec-
dote or story to impress upon their minds
the necessity of obedience, honesty, right-
eousness and truth in all their actions in
life.
	After singing, Sister Zina D. Young dis-
missed by prayer, and the large congrega-
tion dispersed, all going home happy and 
contented with what they had heard, seen
and learned.


WOMAN'S EXPONENT.


[Column 1]

', 'AN INVOCATION.
	“O My Father, Thou that dwellest,
		In the high and glorious place;
	When shall I regain Thy presence
		And again behold Thy face?
	In Thy holy habitation,
		Did my spirit once reside?
	In my first primeval childhood
		Was I nurtured near Thy side?

	“For a wise and glorious purpose
		Thou hast placed me here on earth,
	And withheld the recollection
		Of my former friends and birth.
	Yet oftimes a secret something
		Whispered ‘You’re a stranger here;’
	And I felt that I had wandered
		From a more exalted sphere.

	“I had learned to call Thee Father
		Through the Spirit from on high;
	But, until the Key of Knowledge
		Was restored, I knew not why.
	In the heavens are parents single?
		No, the thought makes reason stare! 
	Truth is reason; truth eternal
		Tells me I’ve a mother there.

	“When f leave this frail existence,
		When I lay this mortal by
	Father, mother, may I meet you
		In your royal courts on high?
	Then at length when I’ve completed
		All you sent me forth to do
	With your mutual approbation
		Let me come and dwell with you.”

	There are many interesting incidents
connected with the women of the Church
that would answer the question asked in
the title of this paper, but it is not pos-
sible to elaborate in so little time, as we
must touch upon the marriage question
and the everlasting covenant.
	Marriage seems now the great question
of the age. At any rate, it is the woman’s
special subject, it is certainly one of the
reasons why a woman should desire to be
a Mormon. Marriage is a part of our re-
ligion, we believe in marriage, and mar-
riage for eternity as well as for time.
Every one who believes in the Bible must
know marriage was ordained of God; and
the first requirement made of Adam and
Eve, was to multiply and replenish the
earth, etc. It is useless to quote to intel-
ligent women that “Man is not without
the woman, nor the woman without the
man in the Lord.” The saying is pro-
verbial.
	We believe in the strictest morality
and purity of life in both men and women
equally. One standard of morals, not-
withstanding all that has been said of us
as a people to the contrary. Leaving all
that out of the question, we believe in
what is termed Celestial Marriage, the
ceremony to be performed by an Elder
in the Church holding the authority to
unite men and women in the bonds of
matrimony for time and all eternity; that
covenant we hold sacred; we expect not
only to dwell together here, but to dwell
together in the family relation hereafter
throughout all the endless ages of eter-
nity; and as we do not think there is any
other class of people on the earth holding
the priesthood of God, which is after the
order of Melchisedec except the Latter-
day Saints, if we expect to attain that ex-
altation in the eternal world, then we must


[Column 1]
abide in that faith called “Mormonism.”
	If you look at the seventh chapter of
Hebrews, you will perhaps understand
what I mean better. “For this Melchise-
dec, king of Salem, priest of the Most
High God, who met Abraham returning
from the slaughter of the kings and
blessed him. To whom also Abraham
gave a tenth part of all, first being by in-
terpretation King of Righteousness, and
after that also King of Salem, which is
King of Peace.”
	I cannot take time to enter further into
this order of marriage except to say that
through these covenants we hope to at-
tain to celestial glory in the world to
come, which hope encourages us to per-
severe that we may have our mansions,
not ethereal, but real, to dwell in, and
eventually attain to greater glory, going
on and on in greater progression, worlds
without end.
	That there is no stopping place for
those united thus, who magnify their
calling and make their election sure. In
1. Corinthians, chapter XV, verse 40:
“There are also celestial bodies and bod-
ies terrestrial; but the glory of the celes-
tial is one, and the glory of the terrestrial
is another.” In verse 41: “There is one
glory of the sun and another glory of the
moon, and another glory of the stars, for
one star differeth from another in glory.”
	Perhaps you may think from what I
have said that we go directly to the man-
sions of glory when we die, but I do not
mean that, but we pass from here to the
spirit world and await the morning of the
resurrection, when we shall all be judged
according to the deeds done in the body.
There are many passages in the Bible
which to me prove the resurrection when
our bodies which we have laid down will
be reunited with our spirits and we shall
be glorified and go on unto perfection.
	In this connection, perhaps, it would
be well to say one of the purposes of
building Temples is for sacred ordinances
pertaining to the dead. Do you recall
the passage of scripture, 1 Peter 3:19-20,
“By which also, (speaking of Christ) He
went and preached unto the spirits in
prison which sometime were disobedient,
when the long-suffering of God waited in
the days of Noah,” etc. Also refer to
1 Cor., 15, 29: “Else what shall they do
who are baptized for the dead, if the dead
rise not at all? Why are they then bap-
tized for the dead?”
	There is proof also in the prophecies
of the ancient prophets in regard to the
coming of Elijah. In the closing chapter
of the Old Testament, the Prophet Mala-
chi says’ “For behold the day cometh
that shall burn as an oven.” And in the
last two verses he says: “Behold, I will
send you Elijah the prophet, before the
coming of the great and dreadful day of
the Lord; and he shall turn the heart of
the fathers to the children, and the heart
of the children to their fathers, lest I
come and smite the earth with a curse.”
	We testify that Elijah came. He ap-
peared to Joseph the Seer and Oliver
Cowdery in the Kirtland Temple, April 3,
1836, and he said to them, after repeating
the words of Malachi,” “The time has
fully come spoken by Malachi;” he also
said. “The keys of this dispensation are
committed into your hands.”
	As one evidence of the coming of Eli-
jah, the whole world, so to speak has
been gathering genealogical statistics for

[Column 2]
the last fifty years, proving conclusively
to the Latter-day Saints that the spirit of
Elijah is upon the people. though they
know it not. Family records have been
collected at great expense in some instan-
ces, and the labor has been arduous;
these records are of great value to our
people who are engaged in this work for
the dead. Many of those who have em-
braced the Gospel have been converted
through this one principle, that they
could do a vicarious work for those who
had died without a knowledge of the true
Gospel. Without our friends who have
passed away and not received the ordin-
ces of the Gospel, we could not be hap-
py. This is one of the most interesting
and astonishing principles revealed
through Joseph Smith, and I bear my
testimony to you that he was a prophet
of God, no man could have accomplished
what he did in so short a time except
through the divine teachings. If you
were to study Mormonism, the entire sub-
ject, you would see how much it contains
and one must acknowjedge, even though
prejudiced, that it is “a marvel and a
wonder.”
	Having said so much about the Gospel
and its principles, I desire now to tell
you of woman’s organized work in the
Church.
	The Relief Society, now incorporated
as the National Woman’s Relief Society
is, as you will know by its name, a chari-
table and philanthropic organization to
care for the needy, the sick, and unfor-
tunate, to minister spiritual consolation,
look after the morals of the community as
well and in a certain sense save souls.
	This great society has been and is a
wonderful power for good. more than I
can possibly tell you in a brief sketch.
It has been educational in the best sense
of the term to many thousands of women
gathered from different parts of the
world. It is this society that is affiliated
with the National Council of Women of
the United States. It has branches
wherever there are members of our
Church. It numbers more than 30,000
women, and is divided and subdivided in
such a way that it is comparatively easy
to reach all the members when necess-
sary. This Society was organized by
Joseph Smith himself in Nauvoo, Illinois,
March 17, 1842. We celebrated our jubi-
lee on the same date, 1892, all over the
world; that is in Europe and the islands
of the sea, Australia, Tasmania, New
South Wales, New Zealand, the Sand-
wich Islands, and also in Canada, and
Mexico as well as the United States. The
Society owns land, houses, granaries full
of wheat, and is not behind in spiritual
matters, faith is one of the foundation
principles of our religion, and we believe
in healing by the laying on of hands. The
sick are often healed by the administra-
tion of the sisters, but we take none of the
glory to ourselves, but give the Lord the
glory. This Society is presided over at
the head by five general officers and a
board of twenty-three women, including
the general officers. The officers are a
General President, two Vice-presidents, a
General Secretary and a General Treas-
urer. The society is divided into what
we term “stakes” an ecclesiastical term,
of these there are fifty-five, which also
have officers corresponding to the general
officers; besides these, there are missions
such as the branches on the islands and

[Column 3]
in foreign countries. In the stakes al-
luded to, there are organized branches
that have a similar board of officers, also
teachers who make regular house to
house visits. In this way, each society:
large or small, is aware of the condition
and circumstances of all in local districts.
These stakes are also visited at stated
times by members of the General Board,
and missionaries duly appointed.
	The young women also have an organ-
ization called the Young Ladies’ National
Mutual Improvement Association, also
affiliated with the National Council, which
is more for intellectual, moral and spirit-
ual development. Their work is broad
and extensive and far-reaching. It enters
into all the avenues of life, including
the home. The young men have a simi-
lar organization, and these two associa-
tions meet conjointly at regular intervals,
	Another large organization of our
Church is for children, both boys and
girls under fourteen years or thereabouts.
It is called the Primary Association. It is
much of it religious work, yet manners
and morals form a great part of the
teaching.
	Besides these, we have the Sunday
Schools, the public schools, high schools
and kindergartens, academies, colleges
and universities of learning.
	As you undoubtedly know, we have
women’s clubs, woman suffrage organ-
izations and Republican and Democratic
clubs and associations, and we are divided
on party lines like other people, both
Mormon and Gentile.
	Perhaps you will say after all that I
have not given a straightforward answer
to your question. I have endeavored to
have you understand what Mormonism
is, and how it appeals to me. Mormon
women are the happiest women in the
world, taken as a whole. I know them
intimately, in their homes, in their organ-
izations, in politics, in religion. We have
more actual freedom than most other
women, we are a very independent body
of women, we vote, we attend political
primaries and conventions and take part
in them, we have helped to make this
country, (some of us who were sort of
pioneers,) and we feel as if it belonged
to us, we are home-makers, we be-
lieve in homes, we are some of us col-
onizers, our young people marry and go
out into new lands, north and south and
bnild up new settlements somewhat as we
did when we came here, only they are
better supplied with what is needed.
	We have been and are trying to estab-
lish such institutions, educational, civil
and religious as will be a credit to our
great country. We are a patriotic peo-
ple. The Constitution of our country
and the Declaration of Independence are
sacred instruments to us. We have here
a society of the Daughters of the Revolu-
tion, and Daughters of the American
Revolution. There is a large number of
our people who are descendents of the
New England Puritans (myself among the
number). Joseph Smith, Brigham Young
Heber C. Kimball, and other Apostles
were of this lineage, born in Vermont.
Many other leaders of the Church were
born in other New England States, Wil-
ford Woodruff, President of the Church
at the time of the famous manifesto, pro-
hibiting the practice of plural marriage,
was from Connecticut. My husband,
Daniel H. Wells, was born in Trenton,


[Column 1]
New York, but of Puritan parentage.
Joseph F. Smith, the present President
of the Church, was born in Far West,
Missouri, while his father was in prison
for his religion, at the time of the terrible
persecution of the Saints when they were
driven at the peril of their lives into
Illinois. His father, Hyrum Smith, was
an older brother of Joseph Smith, and
was martyred hy his side, June 27, 1844,
in Carthage jail, Illinois. This may be
beside the subject, but my aim is to show
you who the Mormon people are, among
whom my lot is cast.
	I have known all the principal men and
women of the Church since 1843. I
knew Brigham Young intimately and all
his large family, have been closely asso-
ciated with them, and wish to say that he
was a noble, upright and honorable man,
a great leader a pioneer, knew how to
win the hearts of the people, lovely in his
family to wives and children. My hus-
band was his Counselor in the Church for
many years, until the death of Brigham
Young.
	I have traveled among this people and
have represented the women in Suffrage
Conventions and other assemblies of
women in the East, and in Washington
many times, have presented memorials in
behalf of ‘‘Mormon” women to members
of Congress, and Presidents of the United
States. I know whereof I speak when it
comes to “Mormon” women. I have pub-
lished the Woman’s Exponent for consid-
erably more than a quarter of a century,
and during that time have met and con-
versed with possibly thousands of people,
not of our faith; so that I know some-
thing of the women of the world, as well
as of the men. My work has been mostly
with women, except my political work,
and my great desire is and has been to
help women. As the General Secretary
of the National Woman’s Relief Society,
I have a grand opportunity of helpfulness
to women. I love my work and only
wish it were possible to do more. I stand
for the higher advancement of woman
the world over, for everything that will
better her condition, mentally, morally,
spiritually, temporally. I am aware of
the awakening of women the world over,
and I know its significance. Woman will
be redeemed from bondage, but she must
be taught how to help herself. This is
indeed the woman’s age, and I honor the
brave and noble women who have taken
the lead for the enfranchisement and
progress and higher education of women.
I know many of them personally.
	We have in our Church some of the
grandest types of women the world has
ever known. The president of our great
organizations, Mrs. Bathsheba W. Smith,
President of the National Woman’s Relief
Society, Mrs. Mary Isabella Horne, Mrs.
Jane S. Richards, all these women are
over eighty, active and energetic, spirit-
ually and temporally. Mrs. Elmina S.
Taylor, president of the Y. L. N. M. I.
A., numbering 25,000 young ladies, Mrs.
Louie Felt, president of the Primary

[Column 2]
Association of 50,000 children, and these
are but a few of the women of our Church
(we know many hundreds) who stand for
purity, for truth and righteousness. The
women of the past whose names appeal
to me, who have endured the severe
trials and persecutions rise up before me
when I think of them, and I see how they
developed the most admirable traits of
character while passing through such ter-
rible ordeals, driven from their homes,
wanderers in the wilderness without food
or shelter, for the sake of their religious
faith. I speak their names with rever-
ence. There are many heroines in the
“Mormon” Church whose names will ring
down the ages. Their faith in God is as
strong as the everlasting hills, that can-
not be moved. They are self-sacrificing
and reverent women. They are trying to
live by the Golden Rule.
	Respectfully submitted,
			EMMELINE B. WELLS.
Feb., 1903.


WOMAN’S EXPONENT.


WOMAN’S EXPONENT.


[Column 1]
', 'GRANITE STAKE.

	The sixth quarterly conference of the
Relief Society of the Granite Stake of Zion
was held at Big Cottonwood, Tuesday,
November 12, 1901, President Emma S.
Woodruff presiding.
	The choir and congregation sang “Praise
to the man.” The opening prayer was
offered by President Emily W. Stevenson.
Singing, “The Spirit of God like a fire is
burning."
	There were present of the General Relief
Society. President Bathsheba W. Smith,
Annie T. Hyde, her first Counselor, and
Emmeline B. Wells, secretary, President
M. Isabella Horne, of the Salt Lake Stake,
Sisters Mary Freeze and Helen W. Wood-
ruff, of the Y. L. M. I. A. Board, Sister
Margaret P. Young, Bishop S. A. Casto
and Elder William Taylor.
	The secretary called the roll, showing
all the Stake officers present and represen-
tatives from every ward except one. Min-
utes of morning session of previous confer-
ence read and approved.
	President Emma S. Woodruff kindly
welcomed the sisters and brethren and felt
blest to have so many of the leading sisters
with us today. Called for verbal reports
from the wards.
	President Sarah B. Gibson reported
Sugar Ward; Counselor Mary Ellen Sper-
ry, Farmers Ward; President Petriua Jen-
son, Forest Dale; President Mary B.
Hamilton, Mill Creek; Secretary Amelia
King, East Mill Creek; President Emily
W. Stevenson, Big Cottonwood; President
Mary Rawlins, South Cottonwood.
	Most of these sisters spoke of the increase
of membership in their respective branches,
their desire to store grain, aiding of the
missionaries and the union that existed
among them.
	President M. Isabella Horne: “My dear
brothers and sisters, I am very happy this
morning as I feel that I am among my
children and that I am their mother. I
know you have such a good president. I
have known her for many years. I know
her to be a steadfast and energetic woman.
We are laying up treasures in heaven, for
this is a charity work, clothing the naked,
feeding the poor, comforting and caring for
the sick. I am sorry there are not more
identified in the Relief Society. The books
are to be opened, and we are to be judged
out of the books; also the tithing books, we
should have our names enrolled therein. I
am thankful today for the events that are
transpiring. I am thankful that I have
lived to see President Joseph F. Smith
President of this Church. I feel that the
times need such a vigorous character. To-
morrow will be his birthday, he will be
sixty-three years old.” She spoke of the
perilous times at the time of his birth, and
the hardships and privations that his
mother and the Saints had passed through.
“And now,” said she, “President Joseph
F. Smith is at the head of the Church,
where his uncle was.”
	President Emma S. Woodruff said: “Sis-
ters, we all feel that Sister Horne is our
mother.”
	President Bathsheba W. Smith: “I am
pleased to meet the sisters here, also the
brethren. I am always glad to see them.”
Spoke of having been through the good
times and the bad, of the army when she
saw it coming upon the Saints. Felt
satisfied that they could not put down
Mormonism. Referred to the reports of

[Column 2]
the sisters, of the union among them.
“That is what I like to hear,” said Aunt
Bathsheba, “and the putting away of
jealousies and fault-finding is needed in
these days.” Spoke of having felt some-
what worried when President Snow passed
away without having organized the Relief
Society, soon after having a dream con-
cerning the Relief Society and seeing the
Prophet Joseph watching over them. This
satisfied her and she felt that the Prophet
would look after the Relief Society. Refered
to those words of the Prophet in regard to
the power, influence and growth of the
Relief Society. She urged the sisters to
be faithful and not go into bye and forbid-
den paths.
	President Emma W. Woodruff: “Sister
Smith said that she was glad to hear that
the sisters in the wards were so united. I
want to say that the sisters of the Stake
are also united and that I love the sisters
of Granite Stake.”
	“Sunshine in the Soul,” was sung with
feeling, by Miss Beatrice Crismon.
	Sister Helen Woodruff spoke of her
visit with Sister Mary A. Freeze to the
Granite Stake academy. Of the beautiful
spirit which prevailed in the school and
among the teachers and students. Of
the desire to take the counsel of their older
sisters and her love to hear these dear sis-
ters (Aunt Bathsheba and Sister Horne)
speak.
	Bishop S. A. Casto: “My dear sisters
and brethren, I have been pleased to hear
the reports, and especially to hear Sisters
Smith and Horne. I feel that they are our
mothers. I like to be with them and feel
their spirit. I pray the Lord to bless you
all. Amen.”
	Sister Mary Freeze said that she had a
desire to speak and testify of the recent
organization of the Relief Society
and Sister Bathsheba’s dream. Re-
ferred to the remarks of President Woodruff,
“that the eyes of the Prophet were over his
people,” and the words of President
Franklin D. Richards to the sisters, “How
thankful they ought to be that they could
be brought out from the worries and
anxieties of homes into these meetings to
partake of these heavenly influences.”
	Sister Elizabeth Boise: “I desire to
honor and fear God. Without His bless-
ings we could do nothing.” Felt pleased
to see the rising generation go forth and
gain a knowledge of the Gospel. Realized
that the Spirit of God was in our midst to-
day.
	President Emma S. Woodruff: “Sisters,
we wish to dismiss on time. I feel grate-
ful to the sisters for coming out to visit us.
This is their first mission out since their
organization, and I think we ought to re-
member and appreciate it. Sister Crismon’s
song so charmed me. She could not have
sung anything to suit me better.”
	President Emily W. Stevenson announ-
ced that they had provided dinner for all
present.
	After singing, “Guide us, O Thou great
Jehovah,” conference adjourned until two
p. m.
	Afternoon session, President Emma S.
Woodruff presiding. Opened with singing,
“For the strength of the hills.” Prayer
by President Mary Rawlins. Singing,
“Softly beams the sacred dawning.”
Minutes of the afternoon conference of
August 13, read and approved.
	Murray Ward reported by Counselor


(Column 1)
Sarah Ann Brown; Taylorsville, Counselor
Mary P. Brown; Hunter, President Char-
lotte Nielson.
	The reports were spirited and filled with
a desire to continue in the good work.
	Duett, “Sometime We'll Understand,”
sung by Sisters Maggie Hull and Maria B.
Winder, accompanied on the organ by Miss
Mabelle Snow, which was very much
appreciated.
	Counselor Elizabeth H. Park reported
Granger; President Eliza White, Wilford;
Counselor Minnie Myers, Grant.
	All gave in a good report and spoke of
their love for and determination to sustain
our aged sisters, the officers of the General
Board of the Relief Society who had
favored us with their presence today.
	President Emma Woodruff introduced
Sister Emmeline B. Wells as the General
Secretary of the Relief Society.
	Secretary E. B. Wells: “Mrs. President
and ladies, brothers and sisters: “I am
very much interested in Relief Society
work. It certainly is a grand organization.”
Referred to many who had died who had
been cared for and laid out by the Relief
Society sisters, and if there is sickness
among the poor the Relief Society sisters
care for them.	There are so many things
the Relief Society sisters do that are not on
the books. “I consider that the best educa-
tion that I have had, except the Gospel,
is in the Relief Society.	There are fifty
Stakes of Zion, each with a Stake organ-
zation of the Relief Society, besides the
missions.	There is a good society in
Chicago near Nauvoo, where the first Re-
ief Society was organized, and away in
the north Montana and Oregon and
branches in San Francisco, Los Angeles,
Sacramento and one or two smaller places
in California and wherever there is a
branch, there we have a Relief Society
organized.” Spoke of the good it would
do young men who intended going on
missions to visit the Relief Society and
learn something about how they were
managed, as they would likely need this
information when they get in their mission-
ary fields. Thought our young mothers
should attend and bring their babes with
them. “We want to take this work of
training our children into the Relief Society,
and I believe that in the school and in the
home love will do more than anything.
We are told that love will rule the world.
It is said of Aunt Zina that she loved every
one. Mother Whitney said that humility
would bring down the blessing of heaven.”
Made other interesting remarks on the
necessity of our young girls attending
these meetings, felt that they would some
day feel it a great privilege to belong to
the society organized by the Prophet
Joseph. Thought if President Winder
could do the many things he does at his age,
and President Smith preside over so many
organizations our young girls can surely
work in the Relief Society, and not slacken
their energies in their Mutuals.“Our
Woman’s Building I am very anxious
about. We want a place where we can
keep our records. We want a place where
we can have a library and a bureau where
information can be obtained concerning
organizations, and in fact, all information
concerning our church. This work is sure
to increase and I believe that the Relief
Society will increase in all its magnitude as
the Church advances. I ask the Lord to
bless our president, Sister Bathsheba, and

(Column 2)
her counselors and all who are here.”
	A solo was then sung with much feeling
by Sister Maggie Hull.
	Counselor Annie T. Hyde: “My dear
sisters, I do appreciate the invitation that I
have received.	I feel that we have had a
feast if we could only realize and put it into
practice.” She gave an interesting account
of her visit to the City of New York and of
the mission in Kansas City, where she
found a noble lot of young missionaries.
“You mothers,” said she, “have occasion
to be proud of them.” Spoke of the feel-
ing like death that came over her when
visiting the City of Nauvoo. Felt thank-
ful and thought what a great blessing to
our people that they were driven out and
permitted to come to a land where they
could broaden and grow. Feft that it was
all in the providence of the Lord. Prayed
that the Lord would bless this organiza-
tion and the new officers.
	President Woodruff called for some of the
missionaries to report briefly of their
missionary work. Sister Louie Stevenson,
from Forest Dale and a missionary from
East Mill Creek bore their testimony and
spoke of the enjoyment they had in mis-
sionary work.
	Brother William Taylor:	“As far as I
am concerned as an individual, I feel that
we have had a feast. We have had beauti-
ful singing and instructions and we have
had the Spirit of the Lord. These are
familiar faces, Sister Horne and Aunt
Bathsheba. These dear sisters have trod-
den paths that will take them back into the
presence of their Father, and thousands
are taking the same path. There is only
one path we can take safely, that is in the
line of our duty. I know that we are
engaged in the work of God and that God
lives, and I know that Joseph Smith was a
prophet of God.
	A song by little Miss Ruthie Stevenson,
accompanied by Miss Hattie Stevenson,
was sweetly sung.
	President Bathsheba W. Smith: “I’ve
been very highly interested in the speaking
and singing, especially, what she said
seemed so innocent and wise. I was not
very old when the Gospel was restored,
but I had a testimony that Brother Joseph
was a true prophet and the Book of Mormon
was true.	And when the armies came up
I knew that they could not put down
Mormonism. I have had great faith in all
our leaders, also all of our sisters, in Sister
Emma when she was president. I believe
they allayed prejudice when she and Sister
Eliza went to see the Governor of Illinois.
I have Sister Eliza’s book that she took
minutes in, and I read it over often. I feel
that the secretaries need to be prayed for as
well as the presidents. Related an incident
of President Young’s life when he saw the
Prophet Joseph and asked him this ques-
tion:	“Brother Joseph, what have you
got for me to say to the people.” “Tell
them to get the Holy Ghost.” Aunt
Bathsheba said that we should not neglect
the dead, and as often as we could should
go to the Temple and work for the dead.
	Counselor Annie T. Hyde made some
very interesting remarks concerning her
trip and visit to Washington, and one very
interesting feature was the expressed wish
to her from a congressman of Missouri to
present her to the President of the United
States.
	President Emma S. Woodruff urged the
sisters to visit the strangers when they

(Column 3)
come into their wards and the poor and
destitute. “Aud I am glad,” said she,
“to hear Sister Wells speak about our
young girls and young men visiting the
Relief Society and learning something of
the way they are carried on.” Thanked
the president of the Relief Society, Bishop,
people of the ward and all who have taken
part in any way to make our visit and
meeting interesting. Adjourned the confer-
ence for three months.
	The choir and congregation arose and
sang “Our God we raise to Thee.” Bene-
diction by Bishop S A. Casto.
']

['AROUSE.

MANKIND, to whom the gracious God has given
	The fairest gifts contained in Heaven and earth,
His vast sublime benignity has striven
	To make thee well contented from thy birth
	With his full bounties, and to prove thee worth
These blessings, freely placed thee on thine
	honor,
	Now hast thou surely brought about a dearth
Of virtue in the scales of the great Donor.
For slowly hast thou sunk in sin and error;
	The second step how easily the first
One follows, ’till thou look’st about in terror,
	To find thyself within a region cursed,
	The victim of a tortuous evil thirst;
Until, sore rent with an insane-desiring,
	The soul, its own perdition’s bonds to burst,
With all its strength the being fall’n is firing.
But presently the sweet soul’s voice grows
	fainter,
	And dies away unto eternal space,
And then thou givest o’er to thine attainter,
	The sensual, a[-]l thy pure and radiant grace;
	For guiling Sin, half veiling what is base,
The claims of life and love is undermining,
	That thou ere long may’st look upon his face,
A godless beauty in his shame defining.
And then the soul is stifled and is deadened,
	Submerged in pleasures gross and sorrow’s
	stream,
And when a sigh steals o’er the senses leadened,
	Like soft and sad reproaches in a dream,
	The wilful heart turns from the heavenly theme,
Responding, “What has God in goodness given
	To me? I’ve naught to lose nor yet redeem—
My soul has ever been with sorrow driven.

“My life has been an unfulfilled desiring,
	A restless search for what will satisfy;
Vain pleasure, naught but transient hope inspir-
	ing,
	Seems but a cruel phantom passing by,
	And God gives not to me for what I sigh;
So will I drift, and if I chance to please me
	With Sense’s charmers, that around me lie,
Whate’er they be, oh,therewith may I ease me.”
And askest thou what has our Maker given
	To thee—ah, know he gives to all mankind
The fairest gifts contained in earth and Heaven,
	That for us all his goodness has designed.
	He made the earth that thou a home shouldst
	find,
Adorned with rarest plants and fruits and flowers,
	The towering, misty mountains, richly lined
With hordes of treasures that may all be ours.

[Column 2]
Thou hast dominion over all that liveth
	And moveth on the earth and in the sea;
The Seasons, and the rain and snow He giveth
	To moist’ and fertilize the earth for thee,—
	The Sun’s great golden glory graciously
Is poured from out the Heavens in abundance,
	At night, the Moon’s soft silver—faithfully
All nature serves thee with her rich redundance.
And thou hast life and strength to onward bear
	thee
	And bend all that thou needest to thy will,
Thy senses’ sweet endowments, which have made
	thee,
	When rightly used, a power thy wants to fill.
	Thou hast an intellect, sublimer still,
Than any gift that governs life or motion,
	Which can thy deeds with depth and greatness
	thrill,
And steer thee wisely through life’s rolling ocean.
Thou hast a soul, that fills thee with a passion,
	Too pure and nobly wrought to be earth born,
That wakens to thy life in fervent fashion,
	The first rare radiance of a fairer morn
	Than e’er the breast terrestial may adorn,—
A soul that thrills thee with the fires of heaven,
	Responsive to the Infinite, they warn
Thee to retrieve thy being, folly-riven.
Then sigh not that thy life hath naught but sor-
	row
	To break the prairie of a desert waste,
That thou from degradation’s depths must bor-
	row
	That which to render it unto thy taste,—
	But live in yielding to that prowess chaste,
With which the intellect and soul hath crowned
	thee,
	Nor let false pleasure, painted and debased,
Her petty lies and larcenies e’er wound thee.
Oh, Mankind, rise, and breathe the air’s diffusion.
	Exhaled by virtue and simplicity;—
As Nature follows crime with retribution,
	So will she to the noble gracious be.
	Then Reason’s light doth teach thee well to
	see,
An earnest life will yield the deepest pleasures,
	The highest uses of thine entity
Will win thee trust and love, man’s greatest
	treasures.
', 'THAT SAD YET BEAUTIFUL STORY.

Christmas dawns again in gladness,
	Quick the years of earth roll round;
Banished now the thoughts of sadness,
	Happy thoughts to-day resound!
		Bless the day, the happy day!
			Christ's sweet birth forever may
		Brighten hearts of chastened hoping 
			Into love and joy, we pray!
 
Ne'er forget we in our joyance
	Them whom persecution doom
Unto suffering for obeyance
	To their God, in a living tomb.
		Brethren. sisters, let us pray
			That our faithful brethren may 
		Drink the cup of brightest gladness,
			By God's Spirit all this day.

'Tis night. Judea's throngs have gone to rest;
	A wandering pair for shelter seek in vain;
The crowded inn holds not another guest,
	And deafened ears are turned to cries of pain.

My Father! must I die?" the Virgin cries,
	While tears of anguish mark her fair young cheek;
'None careth for mine heavy agonies;
	Oh!give me aid—the humblest roof I seek!"

"None careth for thee, dear one? O, my love,
	My Virgin Bride, mine Angel, say not so!
For aid I've sought in vain; but, as a dove,
	Shall Peace soon bless thee; seeking it I go."

And faithful Joseph soon returns to say:
	"No other shelter than a stable's roof 
Can here be found, this night; content thee, pray, 
	And from this one, dear heart, stand not aloof,

"For cold the winds do pierce thy gentle form,
	And thy dear head is bowed in sickening pain;
Come, loved one, shelter from the raging storm
	At least 'twill give—and from the threatening rain."

She leans upon his arm, and sweetly tells,
	In looks, her gratitude for even this;
And from her heart a prayer of pure love swells,
	As on her cheek she feels a husband's kiss.

And through the weary, wakeful, lengthened night,
	Our Savior's mother strives with nature's chain,
As only mothers know; that heavy fight
	For life, and its belov'd reward through pain.

But all hours pass, the blackest and the bright;
	And those hours passed for her, and morning found
A nestling babe, that, born with dawning light,
	Caused heav'n and earth with brightest praise to sound.

And who but mothers know the happy thought
	Of her, the favored of all women blest?
Nay, mothers, even we, who have not brought
	The world a Savior, know not how she pressed

Her darling in her arms. The gentle one,
	Whose innocence was proof 'gainst all earth's wiles—
Oh, none can tell how gazed she on that son,
	As, charmed, she lingered on his baby smiles.

And never mourned her tender heart, for ruth
	Her little boy did never stray from right;
Truth, justice, righteoueness attend His youth,
	And these, with knowledge, make His manhood bright.

And ever and anon the tempter's darts
	Are hurled with vengeance at that head in vain;
And well He knows the griefs of human hearts,
	And well He feels life's keenest, heaviest pain.

[Column 2]

But God-like strength dwells in that holy breast;
	No wiles succeed, all righteous laws He gives!
Succeeding ages by His laws are blest,
	In Him alone a world's salvation lives.

Alas! for thee, Judea! Can thy ears
	Still close to moaning cries of pain and grief?
Thy hardened heart resisteth still those tears
	Of God-like manhood, pleading heav'n's relief!

Beersheba to Dan unite to call
	Upon their heads the curse of holy blood—
Alas! and still they clamor for the fall,
	Upon their children, heaven's vengeful flood.

Ascending lone the mount, the Suff'rer cries,
	"If it be possible, my Father great,
Then let this cup pass by!" His heart replies
	Quick to the prayer, "Thou hast not long to wait!

"Thy Father's glories soon shall shine around Thee;
	Bear up, my Son, for yet a little while;
Amid temptations, faithful have I found thee,
	Be faithful still and win Thy Father's smile.

"For myriads watch Thee now, mine own belov'd,
	Their fate eternal upon thee is laid;
Then stand thou firm, nor from Thy path be mov'd,
	A few short hours—the sacrifice is made!"

"Thy will be done, not mine! Forgive the weakness,
	O Father kind and wise, that in my grief
Petitioned Thee, in lowly, earnest meekness
	To send; e'en now, Thine aid for my relief.

"I go, I go, to meet a doom of suffering,
	Oh, well I know mine hour hath come to die!
Great God! receive in heaven my lonely offering—
	To save mankind, my Father, here am I.

"I have not sinned; my heart is pure; the wicked
	'Gainst me find no complaint. Alone I tread
The wine-press. O, my Father! by Thy Spirit
	Pour forth Thy strength upon my suffering head!"

The hour had come; the dreadful, awful time;
	So great that darkness covers all the earth!
Yet who can read the solemn depths sublime,
	That burst from dying lips of suffering Worth:

"And am I thus forsaken, O, my God!"
	While torment holds that pure and youthful form.
"And so, 'tis finished!" As that high head bowed,
	And death released Him from earth's blasting storm!

He dies, and we are saved! Ah, who can tell
	The gratitude we owe to that pure life!
And pray we e'er that we may serve Him well,
	And that, untarnished, we may win earth's strife.

For through that Savior glorious things are giv'n,
	To him that overcometh in the strife—
Pure rest eternal in God's highest heaven,
	And chastest joys amid eternal life!

		RUBY LAMONT.


', '﻿	HELEN AND VIRGINIA.

		[Concluded.]
*		*		*		*		*
And then ere many weeks had passed,
Again on Helen’s boy was laid
A wasting fever’s cruel power.
	O, just as keen the mother’s fears
As o’er the helpless child she prayed
	As though he knew her love and tears.

For in the mother’s heart there grew
For him, bereft of reason, more
Of watchful care and sympathy
	Than all the rest combined possessed;
Through the dark pall his spirit bore
	She loved him more than all the rest.

And, clinging to him, holding back
The life that blankly lingered here,
And pleading but to keep him still
	Within her loving care;
He awakened, and she turned to hear
	More than the answer to her prayer.

For e’en as fever’s blight had cast
His mind in gloom and silence long,
So the same strange and wond’rous power
	Consumed the fetters and restored
Freedom and light and reason strong,
	In the due season of the Lord.

“Mother,” he whispered—oh, her face
So lighted up with wond’ring joy
As she leaned o’er and took his hand;
	“Mother, have I been sick? Don’t go
Out of the room and leave your boy;
	It seems so strange here, I don’t know

Where I have been, or how I’m here.”
She searched his eyes, their look was bright,
He answered back her trembling kiss
	And raised his feeble arms as though
To clasp and keep her in his sight,
	Then fast her joyous tears did flow,

And kneeling by his couch’s side
She held him to her throbbing breast;
And, as they were alone with God–
	“Father, it is Thy power, I know,
For Thou hast heard my prayers and blessed
	My soul with joys that overflow.”

A step drew near; she raised her hand—
“Dear wife, may I not join you yet
And share the vigils of your love?”
	Then something in her face he saw,
And in the eyes his question met,
	That filled and thrilled his soul with awe.

And as their son looked up to him
The wond’rous miracle he learned
And knelt in gratitude with her.
	“Dear wife, it is thy soul’s reward—
Our dear child’s reason has returned
	To him by favor of the Lord.

“Mine eldest son, my firstborn hope! 
Through him the Lord thy spirit proved;
Thou didst not blame or vainly mourn,
	But bore in patience His decree,
And on the altar—faith unmoved—
	Brought down His blessing upon thee.

“For love thou gavest Virginia’s child
God hath restored to thee thine own,
Nor moved him from his birthright’s place.
	For this Thy mercy we renew
Onr covenants before Thy throne,
	While here on earth, Thy will to do.”

Like some sweet music, rapturous, new,
Virginia the glad tidings heard,
And hastening to Helen’s side
	Saw in the boy’s changed, gentle face,
A look that all her being stirred
	And made the room a ascred place.

Oh, happier than ever now
The blessed days and weeks went on,
For gratitude made blest content.
	Each felt the other’s soul to be
One which from God Himself had drawn
	Approval that all Saints might see.
*		*		*		*		*
Row above row the lights flashed out,
The music hushed, a pause, and then

[Column 2]
A prelude strangely sweet stole forth,
	Half dreamy waltz, then as some bird
High soaring over hill and glen
	Till in the clouds the last is heard.

And while the echoes scarce had died
The singer came; their greeting bore
Not welcome only, but their praise.
	Oh, grandly beautiful she stood,
And cadence, and expression wore
	A pathos that no heart withstood,

Virginia’s eyes grew large and dark,
Her tender face still paler grew,
And as the strange, sweet singer, rapt
	In harmonies all but divine,
Poured out her soul, one hearer knew
	A dying life inspired each line.

Virginia followed tone by tone,
She knew the weariness and woe; 
And when the wav’ring figure passed
	From sight she said: “Take me to her,
I knew her once, not long ago;”
	Her husband rose without demur.

Almost too late, for marble like
The earth worn singer fainting lay,
Alone, afar from home and love;
	Virginia stood beside her, then
Found strength above her fears to say:
	“Signora, do we meet again?”

A thrill passed o’er the weary face.
The lustrous eyes looked up to see
The gentle speaker. Radiant joy
	Illumed her face “Virginia—thou?
Virginia—never more leave me,
	For thou art all I cling to now.”

She sang no more. They brought her home,
And in the few fleet days they learned
The lovely wanderer’s hapless fate.
	The phantom love that lured her on
To faithlessness, neglect, had turned
	And hope and strength at last were gone.

“Teach me thy faith, Virginia, now 
What thou doth cherish I receive;
Teach me thy peace and hope so sure;
	My babe again? and linked with thee?
Reason and love thy words believe—
	I wait thee in eternity.”

So died Honora in her arms,
And soon the sacred work was done—
The ordinances for the dead;
	And unto him she loved she gave
The dear and lost, the gifted one
	Resting at last within the grave.

Oft through her sleep came holy dreams,
And waking hours their meaning brought,
And threw a light on sacred lines;
	And whether in that dearest place,
Her home, or where her footsteps sought
	The poor and sorrowing, her face

Grew sweeter, nobler, year by year.
Time wrote fresh truths on her soul’s page, 
Adding that royal mien which comes
	To those who teach, and lead, and bless.
Until more beautiful is age
	Than even youth’s own loveliness.

And while through daily scenes she passed. 
She listened for the “still small voice”.
And closer sought God’s mysteries.
	Counting no sacrifice as loss,
But in its offering to rejoice,
	As fires that burn away the dross.

Adown the waking hours of night
Grand intuitions softly came
Of earth’s and heaven’s futurity.
	And through long hours with prayer alone,
Around her swept the holy flame,
	And wond’rous glories there were shown.

No savant’s love so deep as this—
No gems so rich and joy so sweet—
And naught so poor as this vain life.
	Like one who but prepares to go,
Yet first each duty would complete,
	These lines my pen has striven to show.

And these are “Mormon women." Seek
Through every vale where Saints abide,

[Column 3]
Like faith and purity are there.
	Though waves of hate and death may roll,
They cannot quench God’s light, nor hide
	His pathway from each martyr’s soul.

And when these lines of mine are read,
How many there will be who’ll say,
"I did not think she knew my life!”
	For oh, this story is the fruit
Of one grand Tree whose strength to-day
	Finds in ten thousand homes its root.

“Bearing such love as theirs?” Ah, yes,
I meet and find it everywhere;
Dearer than life, more strong than death;
	I recognize it as the sign
Of that which Gods and angels share—
	A portion of the Love Divine.

And now, dear friend, if I have used
My truthful pen not all in vain'
And you would wish to further search
	Still other points we hold as well,
O may some abler pen make plain
	Their truth and beauty. So, farewell.
			AUGUSTA JOYCE CROCHERON.
Salt Lake City, 1882.
