['MARGARET CHERRY.
	In memory of Mrs. Margaret Cherry,
who with her husband and six children
came to Utah in 1847 from Kentucky,
where she was born February 11, 1811.
They moved in the summer of 1848 to
Centerville, where she has lived ever since
until her death, which occurred October 18,
1898.
	Sister Cherry was a widow for thirty-
four years. When the Relief Society was
organized she became a member and officer
of that society and opened her house for
them to meet in. She afterwards gave a
small house to the society which they met
in for twenty years. As age and infirmities
came on her she was unable to leave her
home for a number of years. She was of a
peaceful disposition, a good neighbor and
endeavored to live her religion and keep
the golden rule. She died in full faith and
left a large posterity, of whom nearly all
are in the Church.
			MARY ROCKWOOD.
Centerville Relief Society.
KATE LAUGHAN LEISHMAN.
	We have been called upon to record the
death of one of our beloved sisters, Kate
Maughan Leishman, who departed this life at
her home in Wellsville, Cache Co., Utah,
Jan 15, 1899.
	Sister Leishman has been a faithful work-
er in our Relief Society, and has endeared
herself to the little band of sisters in this
branch by her sweet and gentle disposition,
and we her sisters offer these resolutions of
respect as a slight token of our love for her
who has been called to a higher sphere of
action.
RESOLUTIONS OF RESPECT.
	Resolved, That as God in His infinite
wisdom has seen fit to call home our dear
sister, Kate Maughan Leishman, we the
officers and members of the Baker City
branch of the Relief Society, express our
heartfelt sympathy for the bereaved hus-
band and family in their loss; also that they
have been greatly blessed with her sweet
and gentle influence in her home; and be it
further
	Resolved, That these resolutions be
placed upon our society records, one sent to
the bereaved family and one sent to the
Woman’s Exponent for publication.
			REBECCA NIBLEY, Pres.
			NELLIE W. ROUNDY, Sec.
A SAD BEREAVEMENT.
	Sister Elizabeth Pehrson, beloved daugh-
ter of Eric John and Anna S. Pehrson,
died January 15, 1899, after a lingering
weakness for some time, but was not con-
fined to her bed till one week prior to her
death, when she was attacked with an in-
termittent fever. Her weakness became
greater, though she took nourishment till
the last. She passed away without a strug-
gle Sunday morning between five and six
o’clock, in the arms of her beloved mother
and sister.
	Sister Pehrson was born and raised in
the Gospel and died a faithful Latter day
Saint, placing her religion above every
thing else. She was honest, virtuous, kind
and patient, never refusing any call that
was made upon her, therefore holding
many responsible positions at her death.

[Column 2]
	She was a teacher in the Sabbath School,
secretary in the Relief Society, second
counselor in the Y. L. M. I. A., and secre-
tary in the Y. M. and Y. L. Improvement
Association; also held the position of school
trustee and postmistress. She was born
April 28, 1873, at Vernon, Tooele Co., Utah.
	On Friday 20, her angel like form was
borne in a beautiful white casket decorated
with wreaths and blooming roses, to the
meeting house where the funeral services
were held. The stand was draped in white
and decorated with flowers, giving the
whole a beautiful appearance. The choir
sang “Guide Me to Thee.” Prayer was
offered by Brother David Bennion. Sing-
ing, “Wanted on the Other Side.” Consol-
ing remarks were made by Stake President
H. S. Gowan and our beloved Bishop J. C.
Sharp. The choir sang “Sister Thou
wert Mild and Lovely.” Benediction by
Neils Fredrickson.
	The remains were conveyed to the ceme-
tery in a light vehicle drawn by white
horses. The dedicatory prayer was offered
by President H. S. Gowan. The choir
sang “There is Sweet Rest in Heaven.”
The body was then carefully sheltered by
mother earth to rest in peace.
							C.
FRISTENCE MAGDALENE ERIKSEN.
	It is our painful duty to announce that
on December 4, 1898, occurred the death of
Sister Fristence Magdalene Eriksen from
heart failure.
	Deceased was born in Otostrup, Wolstrup
Sogn, Denmark, on the 11th of March 1857.
She was baptized into the Church of Jesus
Christ of Latter-day Saints in 1880 and
emigrated to Utah in 1881. She was a
member of the Newton ward, Cache Stake
of Zion. She died in full faith of the Gos-
pel and with the hope of a glorious resur-
rection. She was a member of the Relief
Society and always took an active part in it.
	The following resolutions were adopted
by the society in her memory:
RESOLUTIONS OF RESPECT.
	Whereas in the providence of God it
has pleased Him to call away this our be-
loved sister to a higher sphere, and we
reverence Him who holds the destinies of
men and who permits nothing to come to
pass but for a wise and glorious purpose; and
	Whereas at the time of her death she
was an active member of the Relief Society
and was held in high esteem by all who
knew her, for her noble qualities, therefore
be it
	Resolved, That we the members of the
Relief Society of Newton ward tender to
the bereaved family our heartfelt sympathy
and condolence for the sad loss they have
sustained of a faithful wife and a loving
sister; and be it further
	Resolved, That a copy of this preamble
and resolutions be recorded in the minutes
of the Relief Society, and a copy printed in
the Woman’s Exponent.
		ELIZABETH FUNK,
		MARY JENKINS,
		MAGDALENE PETERSON,
						Committee.
RESOLUTIONS OF RESPECT AND 
CONDOLENCE. 
	In loving respect to Sister Anna Lang-
hurst Layne, one of our pioneer women
and a faithful teacher in the Cardston Re-
lief Society, Canada.

[Column 3]
	She was born February 22, 1849, at
Stow on the Woods, Glostershire, England.
Died December 23, 1898, in Cardston after
an illness of two days, leaving a husband
and ten children to mourn her departure.
	Sister Layne came to Cardston with her
husband and family in the year 1887. She
was true to her religion and her covenants,
having embraced the higher order of mar-
riage for eternity she has now a claim to a
higher celestial glory. She was always an
energetic and faithful worker in the Relief
Society, a sympathetic and faithful nurse,
ever ready to assist the sick and the poor
and to cheer up the downcast; a loving
wife, a devoted mother, looking after her
children with a love unceasing. We feel
that her wise counsel will be heeded by her
children, (the youngest of whom is eight
years of age) that they will profit by her
worthy example.
	Whereas our Heavenly Father in His
infinite wisdom has seen fit to call home
our beloved sister, Anna Layne,
	Resolved, That in the death of Sister
Layne we are deprived of the society of a
loving sister and a dear friend, one who
was always alive to her duties and will be
greatly missed; so willing in the hour of
need, many sad hearts have been brightened
by her cheerful words of encouragement;
therefore, be it
	Resolved, That we sincerely sympathize
with those who are bereft and are bound by
the nearest and dearest ties, and we share
with them the hope of a happy reunion in
the bright hereafter. We pray that heaven’s
choicest blessings may attend the bereaved
family, and the spirit of the Lord be with
them to comfort and cheer them.
	Resolved, That a copy of these resolutions
be given to the bereaved family, one placed
on record and one sent to the Woman’s
Exponent for publication.
			SARAH B. DAINES, Pres.
			C. ELNORA WIGHT, Sec.
			LOTTIE BARTON, Rec. Sec.


', 'IN MEMORIAM.
	I send you a brief sketch of the life of Sister
Elizabeth Brook Fox, who passed away April 9,
1906:
	Sister Fox was born at Leeds, Yorkshire, Eng-
land, January 5, 1825; was baptized into the
Church of Jesus Christ of Latter-day Saints in
March, 1851. On April 23, 1861, she sailed for
America in the ship Underwriter; she arrived at
Florence June 2nd, and at Salt Lake City on Sep-
tember 12, 1861; she then moved to Lehi, where
she married Elder Charles W. Fox, February,
1862. In November she with her husband moved
to Franklin.
	June 2nd, 1874, Sister Fox was set apart as pres-
ident of the Franklin Relief Society and served
in that capacity for ten years.
	When the Oneida Stake was organizsd she was
set apart as Stake President of the Relief Society;
she labored faithfully in that office for 16 years,
when failing health compelled her resignation.
	During the time she was president she and her
counselors, Sisters Mary Hawks nnd Anne Hale,

[Column 2]
traveled many miles visiting the branches in the
stake which at that time was a very large one.
	She was of a mild and loving disposition; her life
was full of good works and her many virtues en-
deared her to all who knew her.
	She was a noble woman and and the fruits of
her labors can be seen throughout the Oneida
Stake.
	Sister Fox was the mother of t[-]o children, one
son and one daughter, 17 grandchildren and 10
great grandchi dr[-]n.
	The funeral services were held Wednesday,
April 11 under the direction of Counselor Thomas
Durant. The remains were conveyed in a hearse
from the residence of her daughter, Mrs. L. L.
Hatch, to the me[-]ting-house, followed by the
family and members of the Relief Society, under
the direction of the president, Sister Annie
Challis.
	Services opened by the choir singing, "O my
Father;" prayer by I. B. Nash singing, "O grave,
where is thy victory?" Speakers were L. H. Hatch
of Logan and H. E. Hatch; solo by I. B. Nash,
"Shall we meet beyond the river?" speakers were
C. Go sland, President Geo. C. Parkinson, S R,
Parkinson and Thomas Durrant, all addressed
words of consolation to the bereaved family. Ben-
ediction by E. Buckley,
	The floral offerings were numerous and beauti-
ful. The grave was dedicated by L. H. Hatch,
ANNIE CHALLIS,
RESOLUTIONS OF RESPECT.
	Therefore it is resolved by the sisters of the
Franklin Relief Society,
	That we mourn the loss of our honored presi-
dent; but that we appreciate the goodness of her
life and the worthy example she has set us to fol-
low.
	Resolved that we extend to the bereaved our
sympathy, and pray God to comfort them, and
lead them to know what is their loss, is the gain of
those that were taken from them.
	Be it further resolved that a copy of these reso-
lutions be inscribed upon the records of the Soci-
ety and also published in the WOMAN'S EXPO-
NENT.
PRESIDENT ANNIE CHALLIS.
Franklin, Idaho.
RESOLUTIONS OF RESPECT.
	In memory of Mary I. Loveland of Deweyville,
Utah, who departed this life January 12th, 1906.
Deceased was born March 27th, 1861, she was the
daughter of Miles V. and Margaret M. Gardener,
and wife of Orson C Loveland. The deceased
was the mother of 10 children 9 girls and one boy.
	The officers of the Deweyville R. S. offer the
following resolutions:
	Whereas, our much esteemed sister and colabor-
er has by the hand of divine providence been re-
moved fr m our midst, and has, we feel confident-
ly assured gone to a better world, we her sisters,
who know of her loving deeds and appreciate her
many noble qualities, desire to express our tender
sympathy for the bereaved family and to acknowl-
edge her labors and ministrations of kindness as a
member of the R. S., and also as a friend and
neighbor in the best sense of the word. There-
fore be it
	Resolved we do humbly testify that charity and
humility were leading traits of her character, and
that she was a loving wife and mother; and be
it,
	Resolved that we, the sisters of the society of
which she was a member so long, may follow her
noble example of diligence and faithfulness,
	Resolved, that these resolutions be recorded in
our ward record, also to be printed in the Woman's
Exponent, and copies sent to the family.
	Hannah E. Loveland, President; Martha M.
Lish, Sec.; Bashua A. Chapman, Asst. Sec.
	In behalf of the Deweyville R. S.
SISTER ANNA M FOLKMAN.
	Sister Folkman passed away from this sphere of
action, March 26, 1906, after six weeks illne[-]s.
She was 84 years old the 5th of last February She
died as she had lived a faithful Latter-day Saint;
beloved by all who knew her, surrounded by her
husband and family and kind and loving friends,
her last words being "Be united." Until her last
sickness she was able to do her own work and take
care of her husband.
	Sister Anna Maria Peterson, was the daughter
of Jence C. and Anna C. Peterson, was born Feb.
5, 1852, in the city of Rands, Jydlund, Denmark;
her mother died when she was five years old and
when she was 12 her father died, she had one
brother younger than herself; they were then left
to themselves. She passed through many hard
trials, and much severe sickness. She was mar-
ried to J. G. Folkman, in the spring of April 26,

[Column 3]
1848, was converted to the Gospel after having
heard it preached three times. She with her hus-
band was baptized Dec. 5, 1851. Emigrated in
1853, came to New Orleans in a sailing vessel, then
up the Mississippi to St. Louis She had cholera
twice and was healed each time by the power of
the priesthood. Came across the plains with ox
teams and walked the entire way, arrived in Salt
Lake City Oct. [-], 1854. Brother and Sister [-]olk-
man first settled in Lehi, from there went to Plain
City as pioneers, from there they were among the
first to sett[-]e in Soda Springs, Idaho, and were
among the first Saints to settle in Gentile Valley,
Idaho, in each of these places she held one or
more offices in the R. S. She was teacher and
treasurer in the R. S. when it was first organized
in the latter place, which off[-]e she held as long as
she was able. Not having any children of her
own she took a great interest in helping to raise
her husband's children. This dear sister passed
through much sickness, and all the horrors of the
crusade, which was very trying on her, had small
pox as late as 1900, by which she almost lost her
eyesight. Her faith was very great in the power
of healing by the priesthood, and she was ever
ready to acknowledge the hand of the Lord in all
things. After much suffering she passed peace-
fully away. Her complaint for the last few years
was chronic bronchitis, followed by dropsy.
	The funeral services of Sister Anna M. Folk-
man were held in the new meeting house at
Thatcher Ward, Idaho, March 29, 1906, Bishop
J T. Pond presiding. Singing by the choir,
"When first the glorious light of truth. prayer by
Brother Nelce. Graham. Singing, "Sister, tho[-]
wast mild and lovely." The speakers were Coun-
se[-]ors Geo. C. Peck, Patriarch W. Sterrit, John
B. Thatcher and Christopher Folkman, her Broth-
er-in-law, of Plain City, who gave a sketch of her
early life; they all bore testimony of her excellent
qualities and firm faith in the principles of the
Gospel of Christ, said her doors were ever open
and her table spread for the Saints and Elders and
she was ever on hand to assist the poor and her
motto was to "return good for evil." Spoke of the
glorious resurrection that awaits the faithful,
	Stake President L S. Pond heartily endorsed all
that had been said, spoke of her many good
qualities, said no one had ever done Sister Folk-
man a favor without getting threefold in return.
Spoke of what a glorious meeting it would be when
her husband would be permitted to meet her in the
mansion prepared for the faithful. Bishop J. T.
Pond made some very consoling remarks. Sing-
ing, "Farewell, all earthly honors." Benediction,
Counselor Geo. D. Anderson.
COM.
RENDINA OLSEN.
	Resolutions of respect to Sister Rendina Olsen
who departed this life June 4, 1906.
	Sister Olsen was born in New Hedemarken, Nor-
way, September, 1848; embraced the Gospel in
her childhood and came to Utah in 1866. Sister
Olsen was an active member of the Relief So[-]i[-]ty.
having acted as one of the counselors to the presi-
dent of that association for a great many years,
and we realize that we have lost a dear friend and
efficient co-laborer, and while we mourn the loss
of her society here, we also realize that our loss
is her gain, and she has gone to meet her reward,
far from the storms of mortal life.
	The funeral was held in the Santaquin Ward
meetinghouse, which had been beautifully decor-
ated.
	The services were conducted by Bishop J. M.
Holladay. The speakers paid a beautiful tribute
to her life and character in their remarks, and the
choir sang appropriate hymns.
	The beautiful casket that held the mortal remains
of Sister Olsen was loaded with floral gifts. A
broken wheel was presented by the members of
the society. Her remains were followed to the
cemetery by a large number of carriages. May
she rest in peace.
	Whereas, It has pleased the Great Creator to
take unto Himself our beloved sister who has been
a faithful worker in the Relief Society, never tir-
ing in well doing, a devoted friend to the poor and
a kind and loving wife and mother, therefore
be it
	Resolved, by the sisters of the Santaquin Re-
lief Society that we tender our sincere sympathy
to the bereaved family and relatives, and while
we mourn not as those who have no hope, yet we
realize that we have lost a faithful counselor who
died as she had lived, in the hope of a glorious res-
urrection.
	Resolved, That these resolutions be put upon the
records of the society and be sent to the EXPO-
NENT for publication.
ALICE ELLSWORTH, Sec’y.
Santaquin, July 13, 1906.


WOMAN’S EXPONENT.


', "OBITUARIES.

	Thurza Hatt, aged 56 years, 5 months, 27 days, died
of cholera morbus; after an illness of four days. She
was the daughter of George and Avies Hitchune, and
wife of John Hatt. Born in Auebury Wiltshire, February
13, 1826; died August 10th 1882. She was the mother of
fourteen children, and grandmother of fifteen. Was
baptized in the year 1848; and emigrated to Utah in
1881. She was a kind mother, and a good wife, and was
respected by all who knew her. She endured much trib-
ulation and suffering, and died as she had lived, in the
full faith of a glorious resurrection.
						JANE MILLER, Pres.
						MARGARET SHARP, Sec'y.
20th Ward, Salt Lake City.


Died: At Holden, Millard Co., Utah, Sept. 2d, 1882.
Sarah Hirst Beecroft, daughter of Gideon and Mary
Wolcott Hirst, and wife of Joseph Beecroft.
Deceased was born August 5th, 1813, In St.Gilesparish
Middesex, London England. Married Joseph Beecroft
Oct 14th 1832. Joined the Church of Jesus Christ of
Latter-day Saints Oct. 7th, 1843. Came with the Saints
to Des Moines, Iowa, in 1856; there her husband was
taken very sick, and they were compelled to remain un-
til 1859, when they came to the valley.
She was the mother of seven children three of whom
are now living, she has fifteen grandchildren, and six
great grandchildren.
 She died as she had lived a faithful and consistent
Latter-day Saint. She has been an energetic member of
the Relief Society since its organization in this Ward,
and was always on hand to respond to every call that
was made of her. She was a most devoted wife and
mother, and her aged husband greatly mourns her loss. 
							Pauline Larson. 
				------------
  Died: Sept. 18,1882, in Beaver Utah, of consumption. 
Mrs. Parinthia O. T. Oakden. She had been for some
years a widow, and had also buried seven children. One
son 18 years old lives to mourn her loss, also a host of
relatives and friends. During her illness which was lin-
gering and painful, she was often administered to
by the Elders, thus her sufferings alleviated and her
heart comforted. She was patient and fall of faith in
God and the prayers of his people. She fell asleep at
last without a struggle or a groan, and has gone behind
the vail to join her husband and children, to await the
resurrection of the just. Deceased was the daughter of
Daniel and Ruth W. Tyler, and was born June 4,1839,
after the expulsion of the Saints from Mo. and has
shared the persecutions, the joys and sorrows, of the
Saints ever since, never expressing a doubt or fear.
With her husband she received the ordinances and bless-
ings necessary to secure an eternal union of their family
hereafter. Blessed are the dead which die in the Lord
henceforth. Yea, saith the spirit that they may rest from
their labors; and their works do follow them.
									COM.--
				  ---------
   DIED: At West Jordan Utah, Sept. 21st, 1882. Sister
Betsy Poulson Malstrom; Born May 23d. 1861. She was
the loved wife of John Malstrom, a faithful and devoted
wife and was the only child of her parents. Was a
member of the Relief Society and Y. L. M. I. Association,
and was faithful In the discharge of every duty. she
had been married about three years, she had but one
child, and It survived its mother but a few hours and its
body was lain in its mother’s arms for burial.
   Sister Malstrom has been taken from us in the midst
of a life of usefulness and will not soon be forgotten.
We deeply sympathize with her husband, and aged par-
ents in their great sorrow. “May she rest in peace” till
the morning of the resurrection, when she will again
join her loved ones where there will be no more sorrow
nor parting.	 DELILA GARDNER.
 Died: At Koosharem, Plute Co. Oct. 1st, 1882. Char-
lotta Enholm, born Sweeden Dec. 26th 1857. Wife of
John Enholm and only daughter of Gustave and Marie
Brandt.
   She embraced the Gosple in her youth in Sweden, left
her parents, and emigrated to Utah in 1872. She leaves
a husband and two children, the youngest just four weeks 
old, and father, mother, brother and many friends to 
mourn her loss, She was a noble and affectionate sister
and a useful member in the place. She filled the office 
of President of the Primary Association, and Treasurer
for the R.S., and she has faithfully preformed her duties
until our Heavenly father was pleased to call her to a 
higher sphere. May our Heavenly Father comfort the 
husband and parents, and those that mourn her loss. 
She was ill only 12 days, and the sisters of the Relief
Society during her sickness assisted her faithfully. The 
funeral services were well attended; the children of the 
Primary Association followed her to the grave, and 
sang a parting Hymn, as a token of farewell to their be-
loved President who had given them so many good in-
structions. 
MARGRETTE ANDERSEN, Pres. R. S. 
MATHILDA JARGENSEN, Coun., P. A.
					---------
   Died: In Payson, on Sunday morning, Oc. 8, 1882, of
scarletfever, Harry Butler, son of James and Jane But-
ler, aged 10 years.
RESOLUTIONS OF RESPECT,
Whereas, God in his all wise providence, has seen pro-
per to call from our midst, to a higher sphere, one of our 
members who was grealty beloved by us all, for his 
kind and gentle spirit, his faithfulness and diligence in
our society, and many other good qualities which he
possessed, and for the love and respect we have for him 
and for his parents and friends. 
Therefore, be it resolved, that we the members of the
 Primary Association do deeply mourn the loss of on
beloved brother, and we condole with his parents an
friends in their great loss. Yet intermingled with the 
joy of meeting him in a glorious resurrection. And, 
  Be it resolved, that we present a copy of these resolu-
tions to his parents, and publish the same in the WOM-
AN'S EXPONENT, and place a copy on record.
HYRUM REESE, 
MATTIE SARGENT>. 
Committee.


"]

MARICOPA STAKE.
	Report of the Relief Society conference of
the Maricopa Stake, held June 14, 1900,
opened with the usual exercises. Present
of the brethren were Stake President Collins
R. Hakes and Counselor H. C. Rogers, also
Ward Sunday School Superintendent George
T. Wilson. Four Stake officers of the Relief
Society were present and six ward officers.
Four out of the six wards were reported
favorably, though there had been much
sickness and some deaths in the Lamanite
ward since last conference. The sisters of
the Relief Society of that ward have had
work to perform in taking care of the sick
and laying out and dressing the dead, but
by being humble they are blessed.
	President Mabel A. Hakes had been to
Salt Lake to General Conference to repre-
sent our Stake since our last conference,
and was glad she could do so favorably.
The subjects spoken upon were storing
grain, or as we have to do, canning it, on
account of so many insects in this country,
following after the fashions of the world,
unity, charity and example, etc., all tend-
ing to strengthen and encourage the sisters
in well-doing, making this one of the best
conferences we have ever had.
			ANNA E. FULLER, Sec.
CASSIA STAKE.
	Minutes of the conference of the Relief
Society of Cassia Stake, convened at Elba,
June 22, 1900, President Louisa Haight
presiding. Present Counselor Ann Wilson,
various ward presidents, also Stake presi-
dent William T. Jack and counselors.
	Conference opened by the choir singing
“O Lord, our Father, let Thy grace.”
Prayer by Brother Wells Hess.
	President Louisa Haight felt pleased to
meet with the brethren and sisters of Elba,
had a love for the people and was glad to
visit here.
	Minutes of previous conference read and
accepted. Reports were given by Relief
Society presidents of various wards. Re-
ports showing many of the sisters were
energetic and faithful, while others were
dilatory; also that the sisters of Oakley had
a Relief Society house nearly completed.
She loved to labor with her sisters in this
work of love and grandeur.
	Counselor Ann Wilson advised the
mothers to teach their children the com-
mandments of God
	President William T. Jack spoke on the
influence of mothers in the home and with
her family, and the necessity of her attend-
ing to spiritual duties, so she will be fully
prepared for duties and responsibilities of
the home. Sisters should use their in-
fluence for good, both at home and abroad.
	Conference adjourned until 2 p. m. The
choir sang “Prayer is the soul’s sincere
desire.” Prayer by Bishop Thomas Taylor.
Afternoon session. Meeting opened by
the choir singing “Softly beams the sacred
dawning.” Prayer by Brother William F.
Brim. Singing “Our God we raise to
Thee.”
	President John L. Smith spoke on the
duty of teachers, we should go with the
Spirit of God and teach the principles of the
Gospel.
	Sister Rosabell A. Brim exhorted all to
improve every opportunity to learn the
principles of the Gospel.
	President William T. Harper said if we

[Column 2]
learn to do the will of the Lord it will lead
us to do good and be brave in the work.
We should set a good example before our
children and those around us. We can not
do too much good to lead mankind aright
and teach them aright and teach them the
Gospel.
	Sister King said it is for mothers to lay a
good foundation in their children, and in-
stil into their minds that which is right.
	President Louisa Haight spoke regarding
each society celebrating the 17th of March,
and each member giving ten cents and the
same be forwarded to the Central Board,
then we may have some of the presiding
sisters visit us. Spoke of having aids to
the Stake presidency. As aids Sister King,
of Almo, and Sister Cynthia Ward, of Elba,
were chosen and unanimously sustained.
	General and Stake authorities were pre-
sented and sustained by the conference.
	Conference adjourned for three months.
Congregation sang “The Spirit of God.”
Benediction was pronounced by Bishop
Thomas King.
		LUCYNTHIA ROBBINS, Sec.
BEAR LAKE STAKE.
	Minutes of the Relief Society conference
of Bear Lake Stake, held in St. Charles,
July 1, 1900.
	Our officers meeting was held in the
school house, commencing at 10 a. m.
President Julia P. Lindsay called the meet-
ing to order. Of the Stake officers present
there were Counselor L. M. Hart, Mary
Bagley, and Amelia Hoge, Stake aid.
	Hymn. Prayer by Bishop Kutch. Hymn.
Roll called, eleven Officers of the several
branches present. Minutes of the previous
conference lead and approved.
	The following branches of the society
were reported: St. Charles, president First
ward; Fish Haven, president Second, ward;
Garden City, Ovid, North Liberty, Bloom-
ington, Liberty, Bennington, Montpelier
and Wardboro.
	President Lindsay congratulated the
presidents on the success each branch was
making. Desired to impress upon the sisters
the necessity of taking proper care of their
grain. This mission is no less important
now than when given to us by President
Young many years ago.
	Remarks were made by President James
H. Hart, Counselor L. M. Hart and Sister
Amelia Hoge. Subjects of importance were
touched upon by the speakers. Members
of the Relief Society were encouraged to
continue in the noble work. The teachers
should be regular in their visits that the
sisters may be strengthened and encouraged
to labor in the accomplishment of the noble
purpose for which the Relief Society was
organized.
	Several questions were asked which were
answered by Stake officers to the satisfac-
tion of the inquirers.
	After singing, benediction was offered by
Elder Quinton.
	Afternoon session of conference, was held
in the meeting house, commencing at 2 p.
m., meeting called to order by President
Lindsay. Singing by the choir. Prayer
offered by Bishop Philemon Lindsay. Sing-
ing, “O, my Father.” Sacrament admin-
istered. While the bread and water was
being partaken of, the choir sang “Ye chil-
dren of our God.”
	President Lindsay said she had much
pleasure in meeting in conference, had
never yet performed a duty without receiv-

[Column 3]
ing blessings. Spoke at some length on
the great blessings we enjoy in this goodly
land, and of the promises made he sisters
by the Prophet Joseph, if they would bs
faithful. It is no light matter to neglect
our meetings after having our names en-
rolled, for we then covenant to uphold that
organization. We should economize our
time, that we may devote a portion of it to
spiritual culture..
	Counselor Mary Bagley expressed her
gratitude for being permitted to live in this
important day. Testified of the goodness
of God unto her, of being healed by His
power and of the many blessings received
through being a member of the Relief
Society.
	Elder Alfred Osmond esteemed it a great
honor to address the sisters on this occasion.
It requires constant effort for us to perform
the duties required of us. Much has
been accomplished through the Gospel; had
it not been for this, probably many of those
intelligent men of olden times, such as Paul
and others, would never have been known.
Spoke at some length on the advancement
woman is making, she has been and is to-
day by many looked upon as inferior to
man. Woman can accomplish a great
amount of good in spheres where man can
do but little, she has a mission here on earth
which no one else can fill.
	President James H. Hart said those who
commenced this great work in which we
are engaged had a hard road to travel.
Spoke of the necessity of our striving to
overcome our evil passions and subjecting
ourselves to the will of God.
	President N. E. Pregmire, of the Y. L.
M. I. A., spoke a short time. Felt proud
of the Gospel, more so than of anything
else. There is a great responsibility resting
upon the mothers, greater than many
realize.
	President Lindsay made a few closing re-
marks, the choir sang “Thy God
Reigneth” and benediction was pronounced
by Elder John Bagley.
		NANCY H ALLRED, Stake Sec. 
ST. JOSEPH STAKE.
	The semi-annual conference of the Relief
Society of the St. Joseph Stake was held at
Pima, Arizona, June 22, 1900, commencing
at 10 a. m., President Elizabeth Layton
presiding. After singing by the congre-
gation and prayer by Bishop James Cluff,
of Central, the officers’ roll was called,
showing an attendance of seventeen.
Minutes of the conference held December
17, 1899, read and approved.
	President Layton made opening remarks
in which she expressed her appreciation of
the attendance of brethren and sisters; urged
the presidents to see to it that their secre-
taries were alive to their duty, and por-
trayed the importance of each report being
correct.
	Verbal reports were heard from the Lay-
ton and Thatcher branches, showing that
the members were alive to their duties,
were storing grain and sending missionaries
monthly to the Y. L. and Primary Associa- 
tions.
	Stake Aid Elizabeth Moody felt that we
were greatly blessed by having our grain
and fruit farms matured. Spoke to the sis-
ters about drying and canning fruit and
making it secure from moth that it might
be kept for a time of scarcity. The prophets
have declared that famine would come and
their words will not fail. If we live pure,


[Column 1]
holy lives we will be preserved when the
judgments of the Lord are poured out upon 
the wicked.
	The Central ward was reported as having
missed some meetings during the last
quarter on account of the president and
several of the members attending Dr. 
Shipp’s class in obstetrics, but aside from
that they were progressing nicely, although
but few in number,
	The Graham society had had some little
misfortune with their wheat, but had tried
to make the best of it; had adopted the plan
of lecturing in their testimony meetings, 
and find it very good.
	Bishop John Taylor, of Pima, testified 
that water was scarcer than it had ever
been before in this valley, that some of the
grain had not been irrigated for three
months before harvest, yet notwithstanding
this fact we had an average good crop, in
many instances better than when water was
plentiful; he felt to give God the praise.
Urged the sisters to be diligent in paying
their tithing, and asked the blessing of the
Lord upon them.
	The Bryce and Pima branches were re-
ported by their respective presidents as
being in a thriving condition.
	Stake Aid Mary Taylor realized that
through the Relief Society a grest deal of
suffering was averted. We should prepare
ourselves for the events that await us. Said
it is not our mission to tear each other
down, but assist in building up.
	President William Johnson thought the
sisters have as great a labor to perform as
do the brethren, therefore it is just as neces-
sary that the sisters be diligent and faithful
in the performance of their duties as [--]ose
who hold the priesthood. Said the Relief
Society is the greatest aid that a Bishop can
have It is no time to slacken our efforts.
The signs of the times indicate the coming
of the Savior, therefore let us listen to and
obey the commandments of the Lord.
	Conference adjourned until 2 p. m.
Singing, “Know this, that every soul is
free.” Prayer by James McBride, senior.
	At 2 p. m. conference met according to
adjournment. Singing “Our God we raise
to Thee.” Prayer by Bishop John Taylor.
Singing, “Now let us rejoice.”
	President Olive Larson, of Mathewsville,
reported their society as flourishing.
	Stake Counselor Adelia Curtis advised
the sisters to study the articles of organiz-
ation. Be generous and charitable towards
each other and perform our every duty.
	The Eden society was reported as doing
well considering their scattered condition
and few members.
	Statistical and financial reports were read
by the secretary, Salina Phillips, who also
reported her visit to the last annual confer- 
ence in Salt Lake.
	Treasurer Maggie Allred read the
treasurer’s report for the six mouths past
and bore testimony to the divinity of the
work.
	Bishop Cluff said the Relief Society is
doing a great work. Asked the spirit of

[Column 2]
God to rest on all, that we might realize
the responsibility resting upon us.
	Rhoda Foster bore her testimony to the
goodness of God to her, said if we are liv-
ing in the line of our duty we can judge
between right and wrong.
	Stake President of the Y. L. M. I. A.
Laura Nuttall wished to be obedient to
every call. The duties of Relief Society
members are many and varied; the success
of the society depends on the members.
	President Layton was gratified with the
reports of the different branches, also the
remarks of the previous speakers. Urged
the sisters to subscribe for the EXPONENT,
that they might keep pace with similar
organizations. Spoke of the annual dona-
tion, requested the secretaries to record their
reports and not trust alone to preserving
the blanks.
	Congregation sang “Lord dismiss us”
and benediction was pronounced by
William Johnson.
', 'R. S. REPORTS.
UINTAH STAKE
Minutes of the 27th conference of the
Relief Society of the Uintah Stake, held
January 25, 1902, President Sarah Pope
presiding.
Opened by singing "Redeemer or Israel."
Prayer by Brother Nelson Merkly. Sing-
ing, "O Jesus, the Giver." Roll called,
showing four Stake and six ward officers
present. Minutes of previous conference
read and approved.
Counselor A. K. Bartlett said there were
but few present, but the Lord would bless
us in meeting together. It is just as nec-
essary for mothers to understand the prin-
ciples of the Gospel as it is for the mission-
aries who go to the nations of the earth.
The Relief Society should hold on to their
grain and not use it.
Brother George Merkly said he felt un-
able to say anything beneficial to any one
without the blessing of the Spirit of the Lord.
He always had a warm feeling toward the
Relief Society, he received much aid from
them while on his mission. We should al-
ways keep the commandments of the Lord
so we would have a claim on His blessings.
The Relief Society of the Vernal Ward,
Mill Ward, Naples Ward and Glen Ward
were all reported by their presidents as
doing a good work.
Morning session closed by singing, "Our
God we raise to Thee." Benediction by
Brother Thomas Bingham.
Afternoon session opened by singing,
"Now let us rejoice." Prayer by Counselor
A. K. Bartlett. Singing, "How firm a
foundation."
Brother Don B. Cotton thought the pre-
siding officers should not be discouraged on
account of no more being present, for they
had done their duty and would receive a
blessing.
Counselor Mary Hall thought we should
study the lives of our leading sisters and
emulate their example.
Sister C. A. Stringham read some verses
composed by Sister Esther Bennion.
President Sarah Pope made a few closing
remarks, the financial and statistical report
was read, the General and local officers sus-
tained and the meeting closed by singing,
"The time is far spent." Benediction by
President Sarah Pope.
ADDIE LONGHURST, Sec.
SALT LAKE STAKE.
The Relief Society conference of the Salt
Lake Stake convened March 1, 1902, at
10 a. m., in the Fourteenth Ward Hall,
President M. I. Horne presiding.
Meeting commenced with the usual
opening exercises, after which the roll was
called, the minutes read and approved and
the statistical and financial report read and
accepted.
President M. I. Horne said all are ex-
pected to pay their ten cent membership.
If any are not able to pay the society will
make up the deficiency.
The president of the 28th ward, Emma
Ridd, said she had been a teacher, but now
would do all she could in her new position
as president.
One of the counselors in the 25th ward
said they had held one meeting,   and felt 
if they were humble they would be
blest of the Lord.
Sarah R. Standring said their organiza-
tion was made last Fast day. They had
held one meeting.

[Column 2]
President S. J. McKean said they had
been organized three years.
Ann Sock, of the 27th ward, organized
last February, said there were nineteen
teachers, which was all that were present.
The 30th ward was reported by the presi-
dent, Sister Bowers. They have four
branches, their workers, are trying to do all
that is required of them.
President Horne related her experience
in trusting in the Lord in case of sickness.
She thought we could spend our time
profitably in sewing and working in our
societies.
Sister Margaret C. Hull said the Relief
Society is like a band of missionaries in
each ward. "We are not sent out into the
world, but we are sent to teach, comfort and
sustain those we work among."
The meeting adjourned until two o'clock.
Singing, "Lord dismiss us." Benediction
by Sister M. W. Wilcox.
Afternoon session opened at 2 o'clock
with President Horne presiding. The
Temple quartette sang "Redeemer of
Israel." Prayer by Elder C. J. Thomas.
The quartette sang another hymn and the
minutes were read and accepted.
Sister C. C. Cannon said we should re-
member we are working for the kingdom
of God, all that we have belongs to Him.
President B. W. Smith felt Relief Society
work was one of our first duties. Wished
we would cease to complain, be kind and
love one another. Liked to see us happy,
live holy lives and put away frivolity.
Sister E. J. Stevenson spoke of the re-
sponsibility of Relief Society teachers and
of the principle of faith and the work that
can be accomplished through it.
Sister M. W. Wilcox felt we should live
so that we would always have the spirit of
the Lord to dictate us in all that we do.
Sister M. C. Hull sang a solo.
Sister M. P. Young thought we were
greatly favored in being organized into this
society. Spoke of the greatness of the
Prophet Joseph Smith.
Sister E. Webb said "The Relief Society
is doing a great work. Let us set examples
that will bring forth fruit."
Julia S. Woolley spoke a few words on
records and reports, also on increasing our
faith by our works.
Sister Elizabeth Paul felt she was blessed
and privileged to be a member of the
Church
Sister Lambert bore a testimony of the
Prophet Joseph Smith.
President Horne counseled the sisters to
buy their wheat. She felt we should be
willing to say to the Lord, "Thy will, not
mine, be done."
The meeting adjourned. Singing, Doxol-
ogy. Benediction by Sister Barton.
J. S. WOOLLEY, Sec.
WAYNE STAKE.
The Wayne Stake Relief Society confer-
ence was held in the Thurber Relief Society
hall, December 5, 1901, Stake President
W. E. Robison, Counselor H. M. Hanson,
Patriarch E. H. Blackburn, Patriarch
George Coleman and other brethren and
sisters being present. President Jane S.
Coleman presided.
Meeting opened by singing, "The
happy day has rolled on." Prayer was
offered by Patriarch E. H. Blackburn.
Singing, "Joy to the world." Minutes of
last conference read and approved.
President J. S. Coleman said she felt
truly blest for the spirit present this morn-

[Column 3]
ing. Prayed our minds might ever be en-
lightened, and when we know our duty,
do it, lest we come under condemnation.
Counselor Sarah S. Forsyth said we
should use our influence for the further-
ance of the Gospel. Mothers should be
very careful in teaching their children cor-
rect principles.
Sister Sarah A. Robison was pleased
with the privilege of meeting in conference.
We should teach our children reverence for
the Priesthood. Great results could be
accomplished through calling our children
together and and reading to them, and planting
the principles of the Gospel within them in
their youth.
President W. E. Robison said he was
interested in Relief Society work, and when
he reflected upon the fact that the Prophet
Joseph organized the sisters in Relief
Society, which was so uncommon in those
days, and looked at what had been accom-
plished through this society since, it was
truly great. He appreciated the labors of
the sisters and felt they were a great help
in the wards, the labors of the brethren
would not be perfect without the sisters.
President Sarah G. Meeks, of Thurber
Relief Society, reported that branch in
good condition. The sisters were ever on
hand to assist in the ward and she felt
well in her labors.
President Harriet Stapley said the Loa
Relief Society was in good running order.
Teasdale Relief Society was reported by
Counselor L. C. Ostbery in good condition.
Counselor Ellen Heaton reported the
Torrey Relief Society doing well and the
sisters were united.
The Lyman Relief Society was reported
by Counselor Susan Turner as prospering,
and the sisters were desirous of doing their
part.
Morning session closed by singing,
"Redeemer of Israel." Benediction by
Patriarch George Coleman.
Afternoon session commenced with the
usual opening exercises, after which the
General Board and Stake officers were sus-
tained by the conference.
Patriarch E. H. Blackburn spoke. He
said he wished to bear testimony that
Joseph Smith was a prophet of the living
God and that President Joseph F. Smith
was also a prophet of God. Felt that
mothers should cultivate faith in the child
and should lay hold of every blessing.
Sister Leah Blackburn was pleased to be
present at conference. Said it is not what
we hear but what we practice that is bene-
ficial. We should be what we profess to
be, we shall be judged by our works.
President H. M. Hansen felt to add
a word of encouragement. He always
enjoyed meeting with the sisters. We
had heard many good instructions at this
conference that would be profitable to carry
out in our lives. Felt to encourage the
sisters of this Stake of Zion in the work of
sericulture.
President Jane S. Coleman bore testi-
mony to the truth of the Latter-day work.
Patriarch George Coleman felt thankful
for the privilege of being present at con-
ference. Bore testimony to the truth of
the Gospel, knew it was of God. Asked
the blessing of the Lord upon the Relief
Society.
Singing, "Praise to the man who com-
muned with Jehovah." Benediction by
Sister Bathsheba Grundy.
', 'RELIEF SOCIETY REPORTS.
	ST. GEORGE.
Dear Editor:
	We held our Relief Society conference
June 26, 1904. Apostle Rudger Clawson
and Brother Henry McCune, also the presi-
dency of the stake, Edward Snow and coun-
selors, present, and gave us some good
instructions. President Snow gave us a
great deal of eneouragement, he thought
that once a year we could be represented
in the tabernacle, so you see we are gain-
ing ground. The Lord has the work in
hand; all we have to do is to do right. He
will do the rest. My heart swells with
gratitude to my heavenly Father for His
loving kindness that He has preserved us in
the truth, that He has counted us worthy to
have a name and standing with His people.
I often wonder how many of this people
realize what a blessing it is to them and
their children to live in these peaceful vales.
The trials of life are nothing when we en.
joy the Spirit of the Lord. When we think
of the condition of the world and the little
hope they have for the future.
	Give my kind love to Annie and family,
and accept the same yourself and Sister
Lambert when you see her. I trust you
are well. I know you are busy. May
the Lord bless you with life and health and
every righteous desire of yonr heart is the
wish of your loving sister.
		ANN C. WOODBURY,
President of the Relief Society St. George
		Stake.
	NEBO STAKE.
	The annual conference of the Relief So-
ciety of the Nebo Stake was held in the
Payson tabernacle, President Lylius C. Page
presiding. Visiting sisters present—Mary
Alice C. Lambert and Carrie S. Thomas
from Salt Lake City. Sister Josephine
Peterson reported Payson First ward. Own
a meeting house and had started to build a
granary. Sister Christina Ellworth reported
the Second ward, Payson. Sisters donated
freely, owned granary, had 750 bushels of
wheat. Sister Carrie Holt reported the
First ward society, Spanish Fork. Sister
Pace Second ward, Spanish Fork, reported
that society—owned a meeting house and
have granary nearly completed. Sister
Mary Hallam, Spanish Fork Third ward
society— have about 27 widows, have about
199 bushels of wheat on hand, have donat-
ed $8.00 to the Woman's Building. Sister
Swenson reported Fourth ward society,
Spanish Fork—own meeting house and
granary. Paid $25.00 for Woman's Build-
ing, gathered Sunday eggs to the amount
of $92.00.
	Sister Mary A. Lambert, missionary of
the General Board, was pleased with the
reports given in, and especially those about
the wheat, encouragod the sisters to teach
the children to observe the Sabbath day,
teach them by example as well as precept;
wives should respect their husbands, teach
the children to pay their tithing and honor
their parents. Sister Bathsheba W. Smith
sends her love to the sisters, she is a lovely

[Column 2]
woman; spoke of the blessings received in
working in the Temple.
	Counselor Henry Gardner spoke of the
great benefit of the Relief Society, and en-
couraged the sisters to keep on in the good
work.
	Sister Carrie S. Thomas, member of the
General Board, spoke of the beautiful flow-
ers; but thought they were not half so beau-
tiful as the faces of the sisters; was pleased
with the reports; said how pleased Sister
Smith would be to know how united the
sisters all are.
	Sister Page invited all to stay for lunch.
Benediction by President Page.
	Afternoon session, Sister Page presiding.
After the opening exercises a song was
sung by Esther Coombs. Sister Page called
for more reports. Sister Ferguson reported
the Lake Shore society. The Leland so-
ciety reported by Sister Chisholm—the sis-
ters are all united, it is a new ward. Ben-
jamin society, reported by Sister Bingham
—the sisters are all united, but are so
scattered; own a granary, and are quite
earnest in taking care of the sick. Sister
Ball recited "Somebody's Mother."
	Sister Carrie S. Thomas said she would like
to speak about the nurses' class, which she
did at some length, hoped we would be
able to send some from our stake; read a
letter of instructions from Sister Bathsheba
W. Smith; spoke of woman's work.
	Sister M.A. Lambert spoke of the nurses'
class and the Woman's Building. Said we
should have confidence in our children and
impress upon their minds the value of
prayer; we should be saviors upon mount
Zion to bless every one.
	Sister Lucy Dixon recitsd “At a Stylish
Church.”
	The officers of the General Board and of
the Nebo Stake Relief Society were unan-
imously sustained.
	Stake Counselor Hyrum Lemmon spoke
of the duties of the Relief Society in taking
care of the sick and poor, also the plan of
having nurses to take care of our sick,
should all be united.
	Sister Dixon said the sisters of the Lake
Shore ward had invited us to come there
for our next conference, and we would ad-
journ this conference for three months, to be
held at Lake Shore. Read a sentiment and
urged all to think of the nurses' class, and
try and get some to go and take that course.
	Benediction by Sister Mary Roberts.
		HATTIE WRIDE, Cor. Sec.
	BEAR LAKE.
	The annual conference of the Relief So-
ciety of Bear Lake Stake convened in the
Bloomington meeting house at 2 p.m., July
9, 1904, President Julia P. Lindsay presid-
ing.
	President Lindsay addressed the sisters:
“We have claim upon the blessings of the
Lord. and we shall not be left to ourselves;
though we have not any visitors, we can
hear many good things from our sisters
in Bear Lake: It is necessary to obtain
that which we need for our sustenance, but
we should not allow all our time to be used
in our temporal labors, the spiritual food is


[Column 1]
also necessary for our welfare. God reigns
supreme.” She felt grateful for the pros-
perity that surrounded the Saints.
	Verbal reports as follows: Bloomington,
President S. Patterson; Liberty, President
M. Morgan; Sharon, President E. Miles;
Montpelier, President Agnes Pearce; Ben-
nington, President M.Van Orman; George-
town, Jeanet Thibets; Raymond, President
Martha Hart.
	Counselor Mary Bagley had noted the de-
votion manifested by the presidents. Would
not be deprived of attending Relief Society
if it were possible to attend for all this
world’s goods. The influence felt in asso-
ciating together is a power for good; enu-
merated many of the blessings received by
attending and being energetic in the per-
formance of duties, such as visiting the
sick, administering comfort to those around
us both by word and deed. Had the priv-
lege recently of listening to a sister who
lived in the days of the Prophet Joseph and
who was also a member of the first Relief
Society.
	Sister Sarah A. Allred, of the Stake Pres-
dency of Young Ladies, thought we were
all laboring for the same goal, should be
willing to divide our substance with those
who are less fortunate, charitable to all
God’s children, and pray for all regardless
of color or creed.
	Stake Missionary Elnora Austin felt that
it is good to have a clear conscience; it is a
great satisfaction to feel that we are trying
to keep the laws of God, Felt like trying
to overcome her weaknesses.
	Bishop Alma Findlay spoke on the object
of this probation. There is no reason why
we should not be happy, the Gospel brings
joy to the heart.
	President J. P. Lindsay expressed her
gratitude for the good advice and instruc-
tion given and for the Holy Spirit made
manifest. Prayer by Elder John Bagley.
	Relief Society officers’ meeting, July 10,
1904.
	Verbal reports not given on Saturday
were given: Fish Haven,by President Jessie
Nelson; St. Charles by President Sarah A.
Young; Wardboro and Dingle were report-
ed by President J. P. Lindsay, who with
other stake officers had recently visited, had
also filled vacancies where needed; Ovid,by
President Mary Jensen; Paris, First ward,
by President Jane Stucki; Paris. Second
ward, by President Sarah Ashley; Lanark,
by Counselor Sarah A. Ludlum; Cokeville,
by President J. Lindsay.
	President William Budge was pleased to
see the interest manifested by the attend-
ance. The reports have been interesting.
Should take care of the grain; good grana-
ries should be built. It would not be wis-
dom to donate wheat unless it can be pre-
served for our use in time of need. The
spirit of disobedience and independence is
growing among our children and young
people; they neglect their meetings and
other duties. Felt pleased to offer words of
encouragement to all who are devoted to
this noble work.
	Sunday afternoon, July 10.1904, 2 p. m.
	Meeting was presided over by Bishop
Alma Findlay until after the administration
of the Sacrament.
	President Julia Lindsay then conducted
the meeting and addressed a large congre-
gation in relation to our object in meeting
in conference. She earnestly solicited the
prayers of those present that she might
have wisdom and strength to instruct her
sisters. We have additional work in our

[Column 2]
society—that is, the Mothers Work. Asked
the young ladies to come and take advan-
tage of these opportunities, they are espec-
ially prepared for those who are raising
children. We can accomplish much good
if we are only united.
	President Nancy E. Pugmire, of the Y.
L. M. I. A. said, ‘‘It is a pleasant thought
to know that we are all true to the Gospel.
If we endure to the end we will be saved;
we should try and get all the sunshine into
our souls that is possible. We will be
known by the company we keep. Thought
that many of the parents were too indulg-
ent; we are ambitious for our children.
There is nothing that will bring us greater
satisfaction than a well spent life.
	Sister Elnora Austin said the Relief So-
ciety has a work among the young people
in trying to keep them in subjection to the
proper influences and not allow them to run
at large at all hours of the night.
	Sister Amelia Hoge endorsed the re-
marks made upon the government of our
children. Mothers should instruct their
daughters to become good wives and moth-
ers, that they may understand the rules
and regulations of a model home.
	Bishop W. W. Clark felt like supporting
the organization; they are indeed filling
their mission as a Relief Society. Missed
from our midst Sister E. Hart.  Pleased to
see sisters uncover their heads in religious
services. Many other valuable remarks
were given.
	Sister Mary Bagley said we should look
after those who are weak in the faith.
Jesus came to heal the sick. It is our duty
to look after those who are sick in body,
also in spirit, and comfort the distressed by
kind and encouraging words.
	Closing prayer by Bishop Alma Findlay.
LOUISE TUELLER, Cor. Secretary-
	PIONEER STAKE.
The first quarterly conference of the
Pioneer Stake Relief Society was held on
Jnne 9th, in the Seventh Ward Assembly
rooms, President Annie Wells Cannon pre-
siding. Commenced at 10 a.m. There
were present on the stand besides the reg-
ular stake officers, Sisters Phebe Y. Beatie
and Carrie S. Thomas of the General
Board and President Annie S. Musser and
counselors of Liberty Stake, President Mar-
garet Romney of Ensign Stake, Sister Mary
Alice Lambert and others. There was a
fine attendance of members and a splendid
spirit prevailed.
	After the opening exercises, the ward
presidents reported the condition of the
several branches of the stake, and then fol-
lowed some interesting remarks by Sister
Beatie, pertaining principally to the Relief
Society nurse work. She gave much valu-
able instruction in regard to the selecting
of young women for this work and also as
to the care of them by friends, in homes
when they come from outside settlements.
	Sister Carrie S. Thomas spoke on the
same subject, and referred to the charges of
nurses. Said she knew “the laborer was
worthy of his hire,” still the Latter-day
Saint women must have care, and the
nurse ought not to expect more than
the husband’s wage's for her services; that
she thought there should be a reasonable
arrangement made concerning the prices
for nurses. Spoke interestingly on the
mother's work and said by Relief Society
work we associated ourselves with the very
best women, and always received fine op-
portunities of mingling where the Spirit of

[Column 3]
the Lord abounded.
	President Musser addressed the meeting,
and spoke quite interestingly about her own
stake work and her newly aroused interest
in Relief Society work.
	President Annie W. Cannon urged the
sisters to heed the good counsel given and
spoke especially about the progress of the
Relief Society work, and how we kept on
reaching after new ideas and introducing
new lines of work; that the women of Zion
were always bringing forth new interests,
and that our work was given us through
inspiration, even as we were organized by
inspiration; that when President Brigham
Young called Sister Romania B. Pratt to go
away and study medicine, it was the open-
ing of a new era for our women. He saw
the great necessity among the people for
intelligent women doctors, and he placed
this mission upon her, to prepare herself
and to teach others, that there might be less
suffering and sorrow among the women of
Zion, and now we not only have women
physicians, but trained nurses throughout
the stakes of Zion. It is not in the City
where the want of good, intelligent care is
felt so much as in the outlying settlements,
and we must open our homes to these girls
who come here to learn nursing for the
benefit of our sisters.
	After singing and prayer, the meeting
took a recess until two o’clock.
	At two in the afternoon the sisters re-
assembled, and the hall was filled. On the
stand, beside the stake officers, were Pres-
dent McLachlan and Counselers Sylvester
Q. Cannon and Charles H. Hyde of Pioneer
Stake, and we were also honored by the
presence of the General President of the
Relief Society, Bathsheba W. Smith, and
the General Secretary, Emmeline B. Wells,
also Sister Julina L. Smith of the General
Board, besides Presidents Musser and Rom-
ney, Sister Thomas and others.
	After the opening exercises, SisterCannon
introduced Sister Bathsheba W, Smith, the
General President, snd the only person liv-
ing who was present at the organization of
the Relief Society by the Prophet Joseph
Smith. President Smith spoke briefly of her
great interest and devotion to Relief Society
and Temple work, and read some instruc-
tions given by the Prophet Joseph to the
Saints. She bore a strong testimony of the
truth of the Gospel.
	Sister Julina Smith followed and spoke
about raising our children in the princi-
pses of the Gospel, and the great help we
had now through the Primary, Sunday
School and Improvement work.
	Sister Emmeline B. Wells then spoke at
some length about Relief Society work in
its different and interesting branches. Spoke
of the great good the sisters had done;
related some reminiscences of Kirtland and
Nauvoo. Spoke oi the Relief Society as
the first auxiliary organization in the
Church, therefore standing next to the
priesthood.
	Miss Ruth Dahlquist then sang a solo,
after which President McLachlan spoke to
the sisters very interestingly indeed, nnd
gave excellent advice about attending their
meetings punctually and regularly, and
said he fully realized the great benefit of
the Relief Society organizations. He was
followed by Counselor Sylvester Q. Can-
non, who also made interesting and instruc-
tive remarks.
	The names of the General Officers of the
Relief Society and the Stake Officers were


then presented to the meeting and sus-
tained, and six missionaries were appointed
to assist the stake officers and sustained.
	After singing and prayer, the conference
adjourned until September 9, 1904.
	CLARA LITTLE CLAWSON, Secretary.
