Representative_Docs:
['ESSAY ON LITERATURE.

	Literature, as moderns understand the
term, comprises more especially all works,
of taste, sentiment, poetry, History and
eloquence; excluding abstract discussions,
mere-eruditions, positive sciences, mathe
matics &c.
	The field of literature, at the present day,
is a wide one; the multiplicity of books now
extant, afford facilities for the acquisition of
knowledge heretofore unequaled.
	In the past, there have been eras of re-
finement and art, which are plainly demon-
strated; but the press did not then, as now,
work for its perpetuity. In the so-consid-
ered barbaric ages, it is said the Egyptains
accumulated a very extensive library; the
burning of which is talked of even down,
to this day; and the ruthless Cailiff Omar
stigmatized as a vandal. Most bitterly has
it been regretted by all who desire to in-
form themselves concerning the customs,
education, morals, state &c., of society, as
they existed at the period. How much
might be known of that people by their
literature. Time was, not so long since,
when one newspaper was sufficient for the
demand in America; how different at this
present day. Numerous indeed have they
become, and though not always truthful, con-
sulted like an oracle by the masses of the
people.
	Societies for the gratuitious distribution
of the Bible and tracts of all kinds almost
thrust their books into the hands of the un-
willing and idle; true there is much that is
false and much that should be avoided if
possible, but truth will gain precedence;
much that is really bad, in contrast with the
good, makes its hideousness more manifest.
There is a vast amount of superficial read-
ing, most books contain chaff mixed with
the wheat, some even positive evil with
that which is good; but as my essay is not
upon reading discriminate or indiscriminate,
I will not intrude my ideas upon this subject,
or attempt to point out a selection for oth-
ers.
	"Books" says Dr. Channing "are the true
levelers, they give to all who right faithfully
use them, the society, the spiritual presence
of the best and greatest of our race.
	The whole world abounds in books, many
 of them by celebrated authors upon History,
music, poetry, oratory, and the drama; and
the cultivation of the intellect is at the
present time unparalleled. All this tends to
refinement of feeling and sentiment, more
especially music and poetry, the blending

[Column 2]
together of which is the most harmonious,
grand and sublime refinement possible of
atttainment, by mortals upon the earth. Elo-
quence charms also like music, and the pos-
sessor of it is truly mighty in power; to
seize upon the hearts of an audience, and
bind them for the time in willing captivity
in aid of belief or in spite of belief, with the
influence from within flowing outward with-
out consideration of one's self, above rude
criticism or apology, is more than enchant-
ment. Logic is necessary to carry power
and must form the strength of the dis-
course, but imagination may clothe it with
beauty, light up the eye, loosen the tongue,
and excite rapid and vehement declamation;
true eloquence embodies feelings as well as
facts.
	Poetry produces emotion—emotion elicits
thought. Shakepeare has been true to na-
ture in particularly portraying men at ease,
as well as in that far more difficult condi-
tion, men under the influence of the most
vehement passions; there are others who
stand prominently forward in the Dramatic
art; but none as yet have equaled the "Im-
mortal Shakespeare." We argue there is
much to be learned from the stage; it por-
trays human nature more vividly and forci-
bly than it is possible to make it, however
highly colored by books alone; consequently
it is a more effectual teacher, than any au-
thor unaided, can make himself. It opens
up a channel of thought, of feeling, and re-
finement of sentiment, entirely different
from solitary reading, even of the same ex-
pressions and sentiments. In times past, it
has been looked upon as a place for the en-
couragement of vice, and the engendering
of passions, but like all things else it re-
quires a high tone of cultivation, and there
is a bright prospect that the time is not far
distant when purity of sentiment, and the
strictest regard for morality, virtue and
chastity will prevail upon the stage; so
much has already been accomplished in
bringing about this wonderful change, in
the last few years, that none need despair
of its entire renovation.
	There is something singular in the fact,
that rarely indeed an author can give the
finest touches to the most exquisite senti-
ments of his own composition. He who can
most truly delineate character in acting,
can bring out all the most intricate and sub-
tle windings of the human mind as set
forth by the writer, is truly in his nature
and training artistic. How many most
beautifully written plays, are put upon the
stage only to be murdered by the actors.
How agonizing it must be to the author who
has expended time, money, energy, and
greater than all, thought, has studied hard
and earnestly to accomplish what his mind
had conceived, only to have some rough un-
cultivated and unappreciative mind cut up
and hack in pieces, what was in itself, so su-
premely grand and sublime.
	 There has been, at different times, a cer-
tain style, or class of literature which has
predominated in different countries and
among dissimilar classes of people, and
probably will be so until the end of time;
but wherever the highest tone of cultiva-
tion exists, the prevailing literature must
reach the same standard, and be accord-
ance with their fastidious tastes and senti-
ments.
	In the past, there have been some authors
of such power, who have spoke so convinc-
ingly to the hearts of the people that their
memory can never die, and whose works, it
is to be hoped, may never be out of date or
style, indeed they are forever immortalized;


and it seems doubtful whether any will ever
even in this highly cultivated and refined
age, attain such wonderful celebrity through
their powers of thought and expression;
people are too well off at the present day,
to cultivate the deepest powers of the mind
which the extremes of sorrow and poverty
call forth; they can never feel so deeply,
until they have suffered; there must be feel-
ings as well as facts; and to truly delineate
or portray feelings one must do so from ex-
perience. Who knows how to appreciate
truly and forcibly, unless they themselves
have passed through similar trials? Truly
strong human thinking upon any given sub-
ject or subjects, is necessary to stir the foun-
dation within us, and is capable of bring-
ing forth from the laboratory of the mind,
thoughts to sway a multitude of unthink-
ing. Alas that human beings capable of
thought should allow their natures to lie
dormant, and yet we see it every day.
	How many men and women have never
thought of studying themselves until they
were tried; sickness, death, ingratitude,
calumny, envy and slander will call forth
powers, thoughts and feelings that might
otherwise have slumbered on, unknown but
for this ordeal. Sorrow has its uses in mak-
ing us know ourselves; we must be put up-
on the rack, bound to the fiery wheel of an-
guish; ere we can understand the depths of 
our own intricate and wonderful nature. One
whose life has been all serene, cannot truly
commiserate others, or ingeniously sympa-
thize.
	As positive proof that sorrows, afflictions
and tribulations refine, cultivate and purify
the human heart, I would refer you to the
multitude whom John saw in his vision,
upon the Isle of Patmos, the hosts of the
redeemed, "who came up out of great tribu-
lation;" and also the example of the Sav-
ior "who suffered as never man suffered."
But perhaps you will say what has all this
to do with literature? I will answer you,
everything, for books, oratory, music &c.,
written and brought out under the influen-
ces of the most striking and forcible charac-
teristics, acting upon humanity, will have
a tendency to create a superior order of
minds and intellect and that is what the 
lovers of excellence in attainment, most
study and most desire, in an age of intel-
lectual advancement. To what extent the 
human mind is capable of cultivation has
never yet been decided; the most highly
gifted and intellectual persons are ready to
acknowlege there is an inexhaustible foun-
tain of knowledge yet unopened to them.
And in the future we can none of us dis-
cern what wonders may be unfolded to those
who having the advantages of the past ex-
periences of the great and wise; added to
their own indomitable energies and research,
shall cope with the light of intelligence to
understand the mysteries of the human
mind.
	Dec. 17th, 1874.    				 M.


WOMAN'S EXPONENT.


', 'ADDRESS OF CHANCELLOR O. F. WHITNEY.

Mr. President, Professors and Fellow-Stu-
dents:—

	For we are all students—if not in this Uni-
versity, in the University of life, the school of
human experience.
	I am not vain enough to suppose, in address-
ing you for a short time on this interesting oc-
casion, that I shall say anything new and start-
ling. To be original, in the sense of creating
something new, in an age like ours, and in a
world where history has so often repeated it-
self as to make each generation almost a pla-
giarism of its predecessors, is to be more than
"one man picked out of ten thousand," it is to
be an anomaly and a marvel in the eyes of
mankind.
	An editor once advised his readers to carry
with them a pencil and tablets, and be ready to
jot down any new idea that came into their
minds. Another editor observed sarcastically,
that some of them might carry the pencil a
thousand years, and not wear off the original
point.
	All that ordinary mortals can hope, at
this late day, I presume, is to pluck the fruit from
trees of others' planting, and by the various
methods of cookery known to this generation—
if I may use the figure—strive to make our
offering as acceptable as possible.
	There is an originality, however, which I be-
lieve it is the privilege of all men to exercise;
and not only their privilege, but their bounden
duty. It is to be original in the sense of being
independent, honest, conscientious, in all that
we say and do; in other words, to be original
in the sense of being true to ourselves.
	Says Shakespeare:

		To thine own self be true,
	And it must follow, as the night the day,
	Thou canst not then be false to any man.

	One of the greatest needs of humanity, in
all ages, is men and women of originality; of
truthfulness, sincerity, genuine motives and in-
dependence of character; men and women who
think for themselves, and dare to act according
to their thinking. As the present age is no ex-
ception, and I am speaking to some of the men
and women of the present and the future, it
will not be out of place for me to continue on
the subject I have begun.
	At first thought, it may seem a simple matter,
a very easy task, to be true to one's self; that
is, to be one's self, and not another; to defend
and serve self, when necessary; and to deny
self, when wisdom and duty dictate; to listen
to and obey the "still small voice" of con-
science, the voice of God in the human heart,
no matter how self-interest with silver tongue
may plead, or how darkly dangers may threaten;
to stand as a light-house on the storm-beaten
coast, unmoved by wind or wave, sending the
light of a heroic example over the tempestuous
waters, as a beacon of hope and of warning to
others.
	Let those who will, think it simple and easy.
Simple it may be—for simplest things are, as
a rule, the greatest—but to put such things in
practice has required, in all ages, the Hercu-
lean efforts and lion-like courage of earth's
giant souls.
     Says Emerson: "It is easy, in the world, to

[Column 2]

live after the world's opinion; it is easy, in soli-
tude, to live after our own; but the great man
is he, who, in the midst of the crowd, keeps,
with perfect sweetness, the independence of his
character."
	Independence of character does not consist
in a disposition to quarrel and contend on
every point at issue between us and our
neighbors. "Noise and fury" no more signify
independence, than the smoke and thunder of
battle tell the nature of the cause for which the
armies are contending. Silence and rest may
be quite as independent, and are often far more
dignified, than speech and action.
     The great man, the man of independence,
thinks, speaks and acts for himself. He will
never be found doing a thing, or saying a thing,
simply because it is popular. He will dare to
be himself, though the whole world oppose him.
He neither coincides in order to win favor, nor
opposes for opposition's sake. He will side
with the many, or with the few, according as
his conscience dictates. He is broad and liberal
in his views, and tolerant of the views of others.
He will dare to defend the weak against the
strong, and will never be found trampling on
the defenseless. No man is great, no man is
brave, no man is free, who feels or acts other-
wise.

	"They are slaves who fear to speak
	For the fallen and the weak;
	They are slaves who will not choose
	Hatred, scoffing and abuse,
	Rather than in silence shrink
	From the truth they needs must think;
	They are slaves who dare not be
	In the right, with two or three."

	Conscience makes cowards of all who fear to
follow her; and bigotry, intolerance—that
worst of tyrants—binds its votaries, far more
than its victims, in chains of veriest slavery.
	But let us consider the subject in some of its
lighter phases.
	It is always a sign of weakness, in real life,
to be an imitator; to be a mirror, as it were, of
other men's manners, or the echo of other
men's words and ideas. Such things be-
long to childhood or to the mimic stage. God
intended man to be original, to act his own
part in life's drama, to speak his own lines, and
no one else's.
	Even the actor, whose profession is to imitate,
to "hold the mirror up to nature" and the per-
fection of whose art is "the highest imitation
of nature;" even he must be original, or he
will never achieve greatness. There is an imi-
tation which even imitators must avoid—(ex-
cept it be in burlesque,—and that is the imita-
tion of each other's style of acting. Variety
is necessary. One Edwin Booth is enough.
One Sara Bernhardt is sufficient. We do not
care to see them duplicated in every supernu-
merary around them.
	The same holds good in literature. Turning
down the collar and drinking wine out of a 
skull goblet do not make a Lord Byron, by
any means. It may make a laugh, but it will
be at the expense of the simpleton who causes
it. "The poet is born, not made," and every
poet sent into the world must sing his own song,
and not another's, or the world will be little
better for his singing.
	Affectation of any kind is disgusting. It is
falsehood; it is theft; it is sinning against na-
ture. The young man, American-born, who
goes to England a Yankee, and comes back a
Cockney, exchanging his native nasal twang


bad as it is, for something insufferably worse—
a cultivated cold in the head; borrowing from
over the sea a foreign accent and mannerism,
simply because "its English you know," may
be the beau ideal of what is
termed a "dude," but that is about
the severest thing I would care to say of him.
	No more admirable—with all due regard for
gallantry—is the young lady who visits New
York, London or Paris, and comes back gazing
with eye-glasses and amazement at every object
familiar to her since childhood, and asks all
sorts of questions, that would puzzle the in-
vention of a hack-driver to answer, about Salt
Lake, Ogden or Provo—her birthplace.
	Be original, be genuine, despise affectation.
No man can be that which he is not. Sooner
or later he will be known for what he is, and
souls, like water, will seek and find their level.
Disgrace and failure await those who stand in
false positions, and who try—oh how vainly!—
to be any one but themselves.
	The lapdog in the fable delighted his master
by leaping upon him and stroking him with
his paws. But the poor donkey who tried to
play the lapdog, nearly killed his master, and
was soundly thrashed for his folly.
	Young man or young woman just commenc-
ing life! Be true to yourself; act your own
character; live the life for which you were in- 
tended, and you will succeed and be honored,
as surely as God intended you should. But if
you strive to be another than yourself, or to fill
a place for which you were never designed,
you will fall, as would fall the exotic before
an Arctic blast, or melt like the iceberg in the
tropics.


WOMAN'S EXPONENT.


[Column 1]

', 'HERE AND THERE.

	These are times fraught with the deepest inter-
est to the Latter-day Saints.  Every day furnishes
some fresh incident worthy of record in the annals
of their history.  Surely the Lord is hastening
his work in his time, for one event crowds so
quickly upon another that it is scarcely possible
to give each separate occurrence its proper
place in the hurry and bustle of chronicling things
as they transpire.  The newspaper reporter mnst
be more than ordinarily active to keep up with the
chapter of accidents and matters of a similar
character that transpire daily in the midst of this
moderate-sized inland city.  When we look around
over the United States and elsewhere abroad, and
consider the condition of the world and the won-
derful events transpiring from time to time, of
which we only learn a few; what then?  Every
person who believes in the prophecies of the Holy
Scripture must be more or less gravely impressed
by the “signs of the times,” and feel that there is
a power at work among the nations, above the
comprehension of finite beings. Latter-day Saints
know, or ought to know something about what
these marvelous and wonderful events portend,
and knowing and understanding as they do, should
seek continually for the Spirit of the Lord to be
with them, that they may be able to stand in the
day of trial, the test of integrity that will be re-
quired of each one, who has entered into holy
covenants to keep the commandments of God.
     It is not a time for vanity, folly or nonsense, and
yet how careless and indifferent many are in their
daily walk and conversation.  It is not expected
that young people will be restricted from healthful
amusements, or reasonable enjoyment; this peo-
ple have always sought to fnrnish places of recrea-
tion where the young folks could meet together in
theatres, parties, concerts, etc., and partake of all
innocent pastimes such as are good for the body
and the mind.  Parents have met with children
and children with parents, and good order has
been preserved and prayers offered, and the good
spirit that Saints cultivate when they are following
in the paths of duty and of righteousness, always
sheds its benign influence upon such social gather-
ings.  Excess in pleasure-seeking will inevitably
lead to dangerous results sooner or later; and
where there is a tendency to over-indulgence, the
wisest course would be to stop and let one’s rea-
son and judgment have time to act; the mind may
become so accustomed to excitement that when
over-wrought it refuses to be passive, and the
spirit of unrest takes possession of the person
and great unhappiness is often the consequence.
Young people frequently grow reckless with too
much pleasure and do and say things that are not
consistent, and without thought, not having had
perhaps in many instances proper training, that
under another influence they would not be per-
suaded to do upon any account.  They tire, so to
speak, of everything that is tame, and complain
of ennui, when wholesome exercise in the way of
actual work is what they most need, and would
be the best antidote for restlessness.

[column 2]
	It seems strange that Latter-day Saints should be
"lovers of pleasure more than lovers of God;" but it
is the spirit of the world, and it has obtained a foot-
hold here in Zion that this people would once have 
thought impossible.  It is high time some restrain-
ing influence should be brought to bear, to awaken
the young people to their true condition and their
own best interests.  After all, what is there in this
excess of pleasure that can give solid enjoyment
or permanent satisfaction.  It is vain and empty, 
it can never satisfy the cravings of the immortal
soul, nor even a laudable ambition.  It is a power-
ful evil influence to lead away and destroy those
who are capable of better things.  In many in-
stances upon record, one finds those on whom the
Creator had bestowed great gifts have allowed
 themselves to become entangled in the vortex of
fashion, and have yielded to temptation, and in an
evil hour have fallen a prey to the destroyer.
Sometimes through taking "a convivial glass," as
it is called, men have finally succumbed to a mor-
bid appetite, until they were lost to the proprieties
of life and even decency, and their existence has
became a burden.
     It does seem inconsistent that at a time when
persecution is being waged against the Latter-day
Saints, and the whole world arrayed against them, 
as it were, that such a spirit of pleasure-seeking
should have gained ground among the young, and
even many whose years and experience should
have given them greater wisdom.  After all it does
not happify; ask any one of those who have gone
through the whole giddy round of dazzling pleas-
ures, and have plunged deepest into the mazy
labyrinths of the world's most fascinating enjoy-
ments, and they will tell you that after years of 
vain searches after happiness, with shattered
nerves and broken health they have sought to
drown trouble in the unknown, mystic river,
Lethe, but all their plans and devices have failed.
Happiness in this life is not the highest good to 
be attained, and if one seeks for that even, they
must seek it in doing good to others; there is no
such thing as isolated happiness.  The consecra-
tion of life to some positive work, with the assur-
ance of its being in the line of duty, affords more
true, genuine happiness than the pursuit of dreamy
pleasure ever could.  Life is full of opportunities
for noble work, work for humanity, and the friv-
olous pastimes with which many fritter away the 
best years allotted to them on earth, is in many
cases a source of unceasing regret in after life.
     To moralize, however, was not our object, but to
enter an earnest, emphatic protest against so much
extravagant folly and excessive gaiety as prevails
at the present time, here and there among this
people.  The simplicity of the primitive days in 
these valleys, when carding, spinning, and color-
ing were daily vocations, and when homespun was
good enough for Sunday wear, or social parties,
would be far preferable and better for the health
and permanent happiness of the young, than the
habits many are now pursuing.  It is not a time
for extravagance, or great demonstrations of joy;
there is, on the contrary, particular need to econo-
mize and carefully husband the slender means at
hand and waste not in riotous living, that which
has been given to the Saints to enjoy.  Prosperity
in worldly matters ought not to make this people
forgetful of God, or irreverent in keeping His laws.
The Sabbath day should be kept holy if His bless-
ing is expected.  Moderation in all things should
be the motto of the Latter-day Saints.
     There are plenty of innocent recreations
for  the  body  and  the  mind,  and  they
are always within the reach of the young
people of Zion, and they have no need to min-
gle with an element that is opposite in its nature,
and would lead them downward; they should
beware of all such.  They should keep good com-
pany, and avoid evil influences as they would a 
loathsome disease, for it is far more to be dreaded.
Hearken to the counsel of those who are their

[column 3]
teachers and guides, and remember, that "obedi-
ence is better than sacrifice."  It is easy to wander
into by and forbidden paths, but hard to retrace
one's footsteps, and one seldom gets help to re-
turn, though allurements lay thick around when
following the delusive and gilded bait of pleasure;
whose votaries are ever ready to tempt the inex-
perienced and unsophisticated.  Never get an idea
that you must go here and there in search of hap-
piness.  It is more often found at home than
abroad.  The best friends you can possibly have,
and the truest,  are those who have watched
over you from earliest childhood.  Father and
mother are the ones whose counsel should be
heeded.  The disregard of parental government
and control, has been a prolific source of evil, and
there is an inclination among the young in the
present day to assert their rights and maintain the
dignity of "Young America."  It is a pity that,
Latterday Saints should indulge in such ideas;
but "'tis true and more's the pity true it is," and
moreover such sentiments are not in harmony with
the spirit of the Gospel.
     Melancholy, which is often the cause of this
mad pursuit of pleasure, is quite frequently caused
by idleness and luxury.  Occupation is the rem-
edy, work and love of work is a protection against
many evils.  It is said by Samuel Smiles of one
of the greatest men, "work might almost be said
to form a part of his religion."  In this Church it 
might be truly said our work should be a part of 
our religion.  Work, "is the best discipline of
character, for it involves the exercise of diligence,
attention, self-denial, judgment, tact, knowledge of 
and sympathy with others.  Such a discipline is
 productive of happiness."
     These are times to be appreciated in other ways
than pleasure-seeking; the days the old prophets
and patriarchs "looked forward to and sought, but
never found." There is and must necessarily be
important work to be done, and missions to fill
that will require almost superhuman faith and
heroism.  And how can this labor be accom-
plished unless by men and women of indomitable
energy, and sublime characters.  Not those who 
pander to popularity, and spend their time in the
vain search for happiness, and are moral cowards;
but are men and women of action, who have the
courage of their convictions, and who fortify
themselves with the cultivation of those attributes
that give strength and vigor to all their energies, 
physical, moral, and spiritual.


']

Name: 4_children_child_home_mothers
Representative_Docs:
['A TALK ON CHILDREN. 
Paper read in Pioneer Stake Relief Society. 
	So much can be said about the rearing
of children that one hardly knows where
to begin. Every mother may have dif-
ferent ideas, different experiences as the
result of dealing with childrn of varied
dispositions, for we will find in any fam-
ily that there are rarely if ever two chil-
dren of the same temperament—two that
can be governed alike. What will per-
haps appeal to and govern one child will
have no effect upon another, while some
children are of such a sensitive nature
that the least word of reproof will cause 
them to shrink as it were into themselves. 
	That the back is fitted for the burden
is truly verified when mothers with a
great deal of wisdom and patience are en-
abled to rightly and justly cope with all
these conditions and to successfully un-
derstand their children. It is indeed sad
when children are misunderstood. A 
young man said to me a short time ago
that all his life he had been misunder-
stood. He told me of how especially as
a child his ideas and motives were mis-
construed, and he felt that if he had been
more thoroughly understood his youthful
days would have been much happier. I 
thought that it was indeed deplorable, 
for I knew he had as good a mother as 
ever lived—one who did everything a
mother could do for a child, and I believe
she came nearer sympathizing and under-
standing him than any one else, yet she
has often said that it had taken more tact
and patience to raise this child than all
the rest of her children together. 
	When children begin to form friend-
ships with other boys and girls, when
they are not content to remain at home
for all their pleasure, it is then that the
parents of these children should bestir
themselves if never before, and try to
make their home life so pleasant, so agre-
able, that no outside influence can tear
them away. We should not think more
of our homes than our children; there
should not be a corner too good for them. 
Let them see the inside of your parlors
occasionally, let them feel at liberty to in-
vite their friends in, have them spend the
evening around your fireside and do not
so far outlive your youth that you can join
in their games and be one with them if the
occasion presents itself. Most homes are
far too serious. There are some homes
which upon entering you can feel in the
very atmosphere that there is no joking
permitted, no laughter allowed, and if a
child makes the least move it is immedi-
ately told to hush up and keep quiet. Chil-
dren should be allowed to play and dance
to their hearts content, for they will get
rubs enough, knocks enough and a great
deal of the hard side of life later on, and
parents should resolve to make their child-
hood days so happy and joyous that when
they get out and have to meet the cold
world, combat with unpleasant conditions,
they can look back upon their home as the
one pleasant spot on earth, the sacred mem-
ory of which has saved many a person from
losing their self-respect. When children
are allowed to give vent to all that is
joyous and happy in their natures, they will
develop into happy and useful men and

[Column 2]
women instead of sad, suppressed, melan-
choly creatures. A writer said, “Mirth is
God’s medicine,” and we should all take of it
in abundance. Play to a child is what sun-
shine is to a plant. You cannot give them
too much sunshine, too much love. They
thrive upon pleasure and the home is the
place above all others where they should get
plenty of it. Some one has said, “If you
want to spoil your children, let them feel
that all their pleasure and mirth must be left
at the door on entering their home at night.”
And when our homes become regarded as
a place simply to eat, drink and sleep, a
work is started that cannot help but end un-
happily. Children should be taught to get
pleasure out of the common things of life. 
This will often prevent the restless tenden-
cies we sometimes find in children to believe
they would be happier somewhere else or
under other conditions. Teach children to
laugh at their misfortunes and to see the
bright side of unpleasant things which we
cannot always control. 
	A great misfortune to befall a growing
child is to have a mother who is always
tormented with nervous fears and when a
mother continually gives way to these fears
whether minute or morbid, she will in time
make the movements of her children one of
fear and timidity. A mother who never
makes a move or allows her children to
move without conjuring up a myriad of pos-
sibilities embitters their life with a slow-eat-
ing poison. Yet from our earliest existence
we have to a certain extent been under this
ban of fear. A child is cautioned perhaps a
thousand times a year to look out for this
or that. It might get hurt, it might get run
over, something terrible might happen to
it if it does so and so, and it is a most cruel
thing to impress on a child’s mind this in-
nate fear for it is like a letter cut upon a
sapling—it grows wider and deeper with
age. Do not fail to teach a child fearless-
ness, for it is a cruel thing to see a child in
constant terror of the dark or haunted with
fear of the unknown. Children should not
be frightened into obedience by being told
if they don’t go to sleep the boogie man will
get them or a big bear will eat them up. 
It is to be wondered how much sleep a
grown person would get in a position where
this might be possible. 
	Mothers waste a great deal of energy in
worrying about their children. How many
times in imagination have we seen our chil-
dren fall out of trees, off of sheds, have
seen our boys brought home drowned when
they have gone fishing or swimming, have
in imagination seen all kinds of horrible
things befall each one of the family, and
when none of these things really happen,
what have we to compensate for the hours
of mental anguish and the subsequent low-
ering of vitality and physical tone, for not
one wrinkle in a thousand or one gray hair
in a million have been caused by actual ills
and troubles. The things which make us
grow old, which takes the light from the eye
and the elasticity from the step are bridges
we have never crossed, troubles which have
never come. The trials and troubles which
are actually ours to bear are with rare ex-
ception trifling in comparison with the
things over which we worry but which never
have happened and a mother assumes great
responsibility when with some silly fear of
injury she forbids her children such physical 

[Column 3]
development as will promote courage, en-
durance, self-reliance and self-control. 
	Parents who are always harping on the
shortcomings of their children, scolding and
nagging at them will be sure to bring out
the worst that is in them. They can bring
out whatever quality they appeal to and
whatever thoughts radiate their own minds. 
If children do not confide in their mothers,
it is not always the chilnren’s fault, for
there are some mothers who are so crusted,
so unapproachable that their children really
dare not open up their hearts to them. Par-
ents and children should live in such confi-
dence, that when the children begin to out-
grow the stories told them of the stork’s
visit to their homes occasionally that mother
can then take their children, and in a sim-
ple, delicate and truthful manner explain
some of these things which are beginning to
trouble their little minds, for if a mother
neglects these things the children will get
their information elsewhere, perhaps from
an older playmate and in this manner chil-
dren quite often get these ideas so miscon-
strued that it has a demoralizing effect in
their young minds. 
	Parents should use their best judgment
in the punishment of their children. I be-
lieve there have been as many children
spoiled from an over dose of the rod as from
the sparing of the rod, for the fear of pun-
ishment has caused many a child to add de-
ceit and falsehood to whatever else they
may have done and some time the children
are severely whipped not so much for the
offense committed as a means for angry
parents giving vent to their feelings.
	I have heard young mothers express
themselves as dreading to see their children
start to school; they felt as though their
children would be growing away from them,
they wondered how they would be able to
get along. We cannot keep our children
“tied to our apron strings”—we cannot go
with them everywhere they go, watch every
word they utter or every act they do. We
have simply got to trust them, for sooner
or later they will have to meet the world—
will have to stand upon their own feet, be
able to choose between right and wrong,
and the sooner this is accomplished the
sooner your child will be able to take his
place in line in this great battle of life. 
	We are living in such a busy world that
I am sorry to say many little children would
have very little attention paid to their moral
training if it were not for the teaching
received in all Church organizations. Some
mothers seem to feel they have done their
duty by their children in seeing that they
are properly clothed and fed. We should
try to live near to our children, have heart
to heart talks with them on evenings when
their play and work is over, try to get them
round us, find out if we can what they have
been doing during the day—who they have
been playing with and what their play has
been, and in this manner we can get into
their confidence and things may come up
whereby we can impress upon them a last-
ing lesson in kindness, honesty, truthfulness,
etc. Orison Swett Marden, in one of his
wrintings, said “The best legacy a man can
leave his family is the memory of a clean
record and a life well spent.’ When I read
this it reminded me of what my father used
to say. It was that while he lived he would
gladly spend what he could accumulate for
the raising and advancement of his family,


but when he died, although perhaps he
would not be able to leave his children much
else, he hoped to leave them a memory of
a life of honesty and integrity that they
should always be proud of.” To bring your
children up to respect themselves, to love the
right and to hate the wrong, to be strong,
vigorous, self-reliant and independent, to do
their own thinking so that they might be-
come leaders instead of trailers, to trust in
God and love all mankind, this is to leave
your children something. 
', 'OBEDIENCE WITHOUT SEVERITY.

	(Read at the Mothers’ Congress in Salt
Lake City, Tuesday morning, April 11th,
1899.)
	Law is the governing force of the uni-
verse. If a man keeps himself in harmony
with nature, he must obey her laws; if he
enjoys citizenship in a country, he must
obey the laws of that country, and if he is
respected in his business relations, he must
adjust himself to the laws of the business
world.
	The necessity of obedience seems so self
evident that any discussion on that subject
would seem out of place. Nevertheless
there are persons who hesitate to compel
obedience fearing lest they might injure the

[Column 2]
child’s will power. But we think this is
not so. Too frequently where there is
strong will power displayed it is a mani-
festation of selfishness.
	Children do sometimes have strong
instinctive desires in the right direction;
therefore their motives should be most
cautiously considered. But it should be
kept in mind that there is a vast difference
between desirable will power and stubborn-
ness. If we would have our children con-
tented and happy themselves and if we
would make of them desirable associates, it
is imperative that we should have them
overcome blind and unreasonable stubborn-
ness. A stubborn, unconquered child is
sure to develop into a man so self-centered
and so determined to have his own way that
he will forego pleasures and even success
rather than retract, and will be as he was
when a child, his own greatest enemy.
	But this spirit of obedience so intimately
connected with peace, order and prosperity
and so beneficial to the young man in the
outer world comes only after long training
and unceasing effort in the home.
	It is here that his first ideas and im-
pressions are formed, and it is here that
he first learns to respect the rights of
others. However, this training in obedi-
ence should be pleasant to both parent and
child, it should result from a system of
kindness and love, rather than from one
of harshness; for while we aim to prepare
the child for his life’s work it is not less
important that the home be made pleasant
to himself and others.
	It is possible to train children into strict
obedience by violent methods; but a child
so trained will be obedient only through
fear; and when he assumes responsibility
over others he will use the same harsh
method. It is in this manner that tyrants
are made.—None are so tyrranical as they
who have been reared in bondage.
	Such a plan as described may produce
obedience; but it is obedience that is de-
structive to the life of the individual and
the home.
	If then we denounce obedience through
fear the question naturally arises,—“What
should be the motive prompting proper
obedience?” The right kind of obedience
is prompted not by fear but by love,
unselfishness, and a strong regard for the
rights of others. 
	Now we are confronted with another
question. We admit in the abstract that
love is the law which should govern our
effort, but how shall we put this thought
into practice?
	Educators say that the best way to
lessen evil and develop virtue is to begin
early—the earlier the better—to cultivate in
children desirable habits; that a good habit
well formed is the best fortification against
temptation. They even go further and say
that through the power of habit the possi-
bilities in moral training are infinite.
	We all know that habits formed in early
life are the most lasting; that those formed
in later years never attain the same stability;
therefore the mother should begin early,
and from babyhood to school age—a period

[Column 3]
wherein her influence is unopposed by any
outside one—she should work with per-
sistence and determination, so that by the
time the child is 6 or 8 years old the habit
of obedience will be thoroughly established.
	We have emphasized this thought be-
cause of the tendency of so many mothers
and guardians to say, “O, he’s the baby,
let him go,”—and thus they cultivate
habits entirely opposed to those they would
form.
	It will take time and patience to train the
infant or very young child to be obedient—
but less time and less patience, than it
takes to break up the habit of disobedience.
One writer even declares that it requires
more than four-fold the power to change a
habit, than to form one.
	Thus we would make habit building the
first step.
	In forming the habit of obedience in
children and in treating cases of disobedi-
ence mothers will find that there are many
methods of procedure; but she who gives
time, study and careful thought to this
subject will very soon conclude that she
will seldom need to resort to severe
methods.
	Bolton Hall says—“It is high time to
awake from sleep in things intellectual
as well as things spiritual; in things edu-
cational as well as in things material. We
have begun to deprecate either contempt or
neglect of the rights of man, but it has
hardly yet been admitted that children
have any rights which grown people are
bound to respect; so though we have ceased
to club women, we still beat children and
make the best excuse we can for our
brutality.”
	While this “beating process” is followed
up by many mothers of today, others are
spoiling their children by unwisely indulg-
ing them in every whim—a method which
fosters selfishness and like the rule with the
hard hand also makes tyrants.
	It would appear that punishment of some
sort is not to be avoided in this mortal
existence; but when it is necessary it should
be chosen wisely and be properly applied.
	Many parents are in favor of corporal
punishment because it requires LESS TIME
and brings partially desirable results—and
indeed the results are but partially desir-
able, for while it is natural to connect the
blow with the physical nature ONLY, the
harm done the child’s spiritual nature is
infinitely greater, for it makes obtuse and irre-
sponsive those fine and tender emotions 
upon which so much reliance must be
placed for future complete development. 
	A very small child who cannot reason
will perhaps profit by having his hands
spatted, but it seems plain that you can
teach a larger child nothing by beating him
“except that you are a bigger animal, and
as revengeful as he, and that a blow hurts
which he already knows.”
	We have admitted that punishment is
necessary and now we would state that
just punishment is just;—it is an irrevoc-
able law of nature.
	Nature stands as THE GREAT teacher
of humanity, and on this subject as on all


[Column 1]
others she has something to say. Many do
not understand the voice of nature and
either add an artificial punishment or some
corrective that in no way relates to the
offence. By way of explaining the first
thought we offer the following story: A
little boy who was forbidden to go into the
street alone was persistent in this action.
One day he came in contact with a horse
and cart and was knocked down but fortun-
ately was not injured. The mother seeing
his danger rushed out in the street and
when the frightened boy ran to meet her
she gave him a vigorous whipping.
	Now we ask was not the severe fright
from which the child suffered punishment
enough of itself? We think the natural
result of his disobedience was severe, and
sufficient to teach him his mistake; but the
punishment of his mother turned his
thoughts from it and very likely filled him
with malice and distrust.
	But I think I hear you say not all
offences are pursued to that issue where the
dire consequence is manifest. For instance
we take the familiar case of a child who
continually disregards his mother’s request
by going into the water. It may be of no
avail to tell him he will catch cold or take
some violent disease, for many children are
so blessed with health that these results do
not follow. In such a case depriving a
child of liberty by tieing him up or keeping
him in the house because he has disobeyed
the laws of liberty seems a more rational
method than whipping. Another illustra-
tion we think will serve to impress the
thought of rational punishment:
	A woman who had a large family of boys
and no girls made use of her boys in the
home. She appointed one—a lad of about
12 years—to take care of the wash tubs.
He was to put them in the cellar after
the washing and keep them there. She
explained what would happen to them
if they were left out in the sun, and gave all
the caution she thought necessary.
	However, not many days after she found
one of the tubs broken down. She did not
lose her temper and punish—she simply
told him he must try to set the tub up
again. After he had worked all the after-
noon he realized the graveness of his
neglect and was filled with regret.
	If this mother had inflicted a severe
punishment her son would very likely have
been filled with anger and would have felt
glad that her tub was destroyed.
	Those who discuss juvenile discipline
often attribute all the faults to children and
none to parents, but as we readily see that
the child is for the most part what his par-
ents have made him we do not hesitate
to say that much of the blame should go to
parents. The duties of the parent are
innumerable, but the first and most im-
portant of all, are those he owes to his
children and in this direction he should put
his best efforts.
	Perhaps the hardest and most beneficial
lesson for the mother to learn is the lesson
of self control. Some one has said that
a mother should be an angel and it seems
she would need to be if she were able
to control herself at all times. She cannot
be perfect in this respect but she can exert
herself toward this end, and by having
a definite purpose in view she can accom-
plish very much. The mother who does
this will find that she can coolly reason
when she is called upon to administer
punishment. She can look for the cause of
the disobedience and can decide on the

[Column 2]
proper punishment. She does not make
threats nor say fifty things she will re-
gret the next minute.
	On the other hand look at the mother
who knows no self control; she loses her
reason, whips, scolds, makes threats that
she never keeps and is a picture that
the child will always remember—yes and
imitate if he is ever left in control of baby
sister.
	It is this lack of self control that makes
a tyrant of the parent. A mother who
is thoughtful in dispensing reward or
punishment is a director—a sympathetic
adviser; while a parent who punishes be-
cause he feels irritable or angry is tyrrani-
cal. Parents who are swayed by differ-
ent emotions fall into many errors that tend
to make disobedient children.
	The mother should early establish her
authority—she should impress upon the
mind of the child that she has many re-
sponsibilities in caring for the home, that
she understands what is for the best of
all and more than all that she is the
authority and must be obeyed.
	There is nothing more desirable, as re-
gards the life of parents and children, than
that perfect confidence exist. And in order
to secure this confidence so desirable the
parent must be truthful. So often dis-
obedience is aggravated by parents who
disregard this law. It is sadly common
to hear mothers press children into obeying
by promising them results which they
know will never come. A mother should
be truthful and constant in all her dealings
with the child—if she tells him such a
consequence will follow a certain act
she should see that it does follow and that
it follows every time the act is committed.
	She should be firm but loving and
affectionate. If she says no she should
mean no. A mother who changes her
mind often, “who makes rules in haste and
repents of them at leisure, who treats an
offence with severity at one time and with
leniency the next, is laying up miseries for
herself and for her children.” Even if
a mother has a poor method it is better
to stick to it and be firm than to have
a good method and be changable. Lack
of firmness fosters the habit of coaxing—a
firm mother will seldom be annoyed in
this.
	When children make requests (or ask
for privileges) the mother should think
a moment before answering in order to
decide exactly in the right, for if she is
busy and worried, she is very apt to say
“no”—and on reflection find that the
request could as well as not have been
granted. But if she has said a thing she
should see that it is carried out at what-
ever cost; for it is better to deprive a child
of a pleasure than to first refuse him and
then give permission. Many enslave them-
selves by offering bribes and by threats.
Children should not be bribed into obedi-
ence—if once this habit is begun there
is no opportunity to stop and in time
children will not exert themselves in the
least degree unless they receive some re-
muneration. Threatening is also to be
avoided—a child soon learns that a THREAT
means nothing and pays little attention to it.
	A mother should be just and reasonable
—reasonable from her own standpoint and
from the standpoint of the child. Sbe
should never ask him to make promises
which she knows he cannot keep and
should not measure his judgment and
ability by her own.

[Column 3]
	In making requests of him she should be
polite, for if a reasonable request is made in
a polite way the mother is very apt to
be obeyed. In severe cases if it is necessary
to command she should do so—and with de-
cision but in no case should she nag.
	She should avoid asking a child to do a
thing when he is in a bad humor and
would just as soon refuse as to obey. If
she feels that he will refuse she should
not press him into disobedience—better
first divert his attention and thus avoid
an outbreak.
	There should be established between the
mother and child a standard of right and
upon this standard all their dealings with
each other should be based. She should
be conscientious herself and teach him to be
the same. It is much easier to be obedi-
ent to law because it is right than to
be obedient, because somebody has asked us
to be.
	The position of the child to the parents
is not unlike our position to God. The
child is placed in their care and is sub-
ject to their will and upon his conception
of their qualifications will be based his
faith in them. By a moment of reflection
it will be seen that our faith in God depends
entirely on our knowledge of His attributes
—we could not trust nor believe in a God if
He were without judgment, justice and
mercy; we could not have faith in Him
if He were varying in His action; neither
will the child have faith in a parent who
lacks these qualities—his faith in them
will be in proportion to the extent of their
perfection. Let parents then bear in mind
that if they would keep the faith of
their children they must so live that their
actions will appeal to the child.
	No doubt it occurs to many mothers
as they listen to the ideas advanced, that
they are very well as theories but will
not stand the test of practice. Let me say
to those mothers if any such be here that by
exerting ourselves in this direction much
more can be done than we may at first
think. In all cases the mother must use
her judgment. Each case will need some
special attention but the ultimate good
flowing from such patience and considera-
tion will be ample reward.
	The prophet uttered a truth that the
history of the world is ever emphasizing
when he said “obedience is better than
sacrifice, and to hearken than the fat of
rams.”
	Obedience is the great law that the
universe is ever emphasizing. One who
does not obey this divine command written
on every page of nature’s book finds himself
at war with everything in the world; while
the man who grows up in obedience finds
himself in harmony with everything in the
world.
	At home, in the nation, in the church, he
discovers this thought dominating all.—
“The path of obedience is the only one
that leads to absolute and perfect liberty.
It is the fundamental law of the great truth
which shall make us free.”
', "DAVIS STAKE.
Lecture I.—Motherhood aud Maternity:—

“O wondrous power! how little understood—
	Entrusted to the mother’s mind alone,
To fashion genius, form the soul for good,
	Inspire a West or train a Washington.”
		(a) How can we best prepare for
	motherhood?
		(b) Observing the Word of Wisdom
	as essential to physical development.
		(c) The Gospel as essential to spirit-
	ual development.
		(d) The development of the mother
	as effecting the unborn child. How
	can we overcome hereditary weak-
	nesses?
		(e) What effect will obedience to the
	moral law have upon our offspring?
	(Refer to St. Matthew i: 24; also Heb. xii: 9;
Rev. 12: 7-17.)
		(f) How does child-bearing effect
	the Latter-day work?
		(g) How does it effect our exalta-
	tion?

Lecture II.—Pre-natal Influences:

	“A pebble in the streamlet scant.
		Has turned the course of many a river,
	A dewdrop on the infant plant.
		Has dwarfed the giant oak forever.”

		(a) How does the physical, moral
	and spiritual development of the moth-
	er effect the unborn child?
		(b) How should we dress during
	gestation?
		(c) Why is plain, nourishing food
	and regular, light exercise essential to
	mother and child?
		(d) What responsibility has the fath-
	er during this period?
		(e) How can he assist in the one
	great mission to be performed—that of
	bringing forth a perfectly developed
	and well-balanced child?
		(f) Compare the amount of character
	building the mother may do during
	this period to that that may be done
	later.

Lecture III—Proper Care of Mother and In-
	fant:

	“Something to live for has come to our place,
		Something to die for, may be;
	Something to give even sorrow a grace,
		A dear little innocent baby.”

		(a) What nourishment is best adap-

[Column 3]
	ted to the mother’s condition?
		(b) Why should she be kept quiet?
		(c) Why is proper care a vital ques-
	tion at this time? 
		(d) Discuss the proper way to give
	baby’s first bath, its clothing, bed and.
	food.
		(e) How does cleanliness promote
	health?
		(f) Why should a baby have its
	wants anticipated?
		(g) Why is it best to teach it to go
	to sleep naturally?
		(h) Give practical illustrations of the
	effect of habit upon the new born babe.
		(i) Discuss the nursing period and
	how it effects the child’s future life.
		(j) How should we care for the
	child during teething?
		(k) How and when shall we wean
	our babies?

Lecture IV.— The Infant Child and its Early
	Training:

“A little bit of patience often makes the sunshine
	come,
And a little bit of love makes a very happy home.”

		(a) In what order are the senses
	developed?
		(b) In what order are the emotions
	developed?
		(c) How can these developments be
	used in the early training of the child?
		(d) Of what value are play moments
	in a child’s life?
		(e) Discuss the motto, “learn by
	doing.”
		(f) How soon does a child have a
	moral sense?
		(g) Why is firmness so essential in
	the training of the child?
		(g) When and how may obedience
	be cultivated—unselfishness, gentle-
	ness, kindness and truthfulness?
		(i) What effect has the parent’s self-
	government upon the government of
	the child?
		(j) Of what value are the first three
	years of the child’s life in the building
	of his character.

Lecture V.—Obedience—Necessity of its
	Early Enforcement:

“O’er wayward childhood, wouldst thou hold firm
		rule,
	And sun thee in the light of happy faces,
Love, Hope and Patience—these must be thy
		graces.
	And in thine own heart let them first keep
		school,”

		(a) Why should the lesson of obed-
	ience be one of the foundation stones
	of character building?
		(b) How will this principle effect
	the life of a Latter-day Saint through
	time and all eternity?
		(c) How is it connected with our
	spiritual progress?
		(d) How should obedience be
	taught?
	(Refer to Eph. vi: 1-9; I Saml. xv: 22; Juvenile
Instructor June 15, 1903, page 371.)

Lecture VI.—Childhood and its Proper Direc-
	tion:

	“He who checks a child with terror,
		Stops its play and stills its song,
	Not alone commits an error;
		But a grievous moral wrong.”

		(a) How do both paren[-]s constantly
	teach morals good and bad to their
	children?


[Column 1]	
	(b) How does a child get his first
ideas of God’s love, truth and justice?
	(c) Can extravagance at home teach
dishonesty?
	(d) Should discourtesy to children
be the rule at home?
	(e) Why should children have set
tasks and light responsibilities within
the home?
	(f) When should a child be permitted
social life outside the family?
	(g( Does the judgment of a child
develop early? Should he rule in the
home?
(Refer to Doc. Cov. Sec. 68, 25 verse; Juvenile
Instructor July 1, 1903.)
Lecture VII.—Child's Place in the Home:
“Gather them close to your loving heart,
	Cradle them on your breast,
They will soon enough leave your brooding care,
Soon enough mount youth’s topmost stair,
	Little ones in the nest.”
	(a) Should each child have some
part in keeping up the home?
	(b) What effect has responsibility on
character?
	(c) What is the effect of not feeling
one’s self a necessary element in the
home?
	(d) Why should mother and child be
united, and their interests be one?
	(e) How far should the freedom of
the child extend within the home?
	(f) Why should a child’s rights be
determined by obedience to law?
(Refer to Doc. and Cov. Sec. 83, verse 4.)
Lecture VIII.—Necessity of Retaining the
	Child’s Confidence:
	“Oh, what a tangled web we weave,
	When first we practice to deceive.’’
	(a) Why should fear be avoided in
the government of the young?
	(b) Define faith in God (Doc. and
Cov.)
	(c) Define faith or confidence in
father and mother.
	(d) Why should a mother listen to
the child’s experiences of the day.
	(e) Explain the danger of practicing
deception in the governing of children.
	(f) How does a parent’s self-gov-
ernment strengthen the confidence of
the child?
Lecture IX.—Employment and Occupation of
	Our Children:
	“Work for some good, be it ever so slowly
	Labor! all labor is noble and holy.”
	(a) Why is activity so essential in
the young?
	(b) How and by whom should it be
directed?
	(c) Why is reading to and story tell-
ing beneficial in the training of the
child?
	(d) Why is the study of music bene-
ficial, and at what age should it begin?
	(e) When should the child be given
tasks to perform?
	(f) When should he share responsi-
bility?
	(g) Why are the idle moments of
danger to the child?
(Refer to Doc. and Cov., Sec. 42, verse 42.)
Lecture X.—Our Girls and Boys—the Fath-
ers and Mothers of the Next Generation:

[Column 2]
“Oh ! thou child of many prayers.
Life hath quicksands—life hath snares,
Care and age come unawares.”
	(a) What special teaching should
boy receive and when?
	(b) How should we teach our daugh-
ters?
	(c) Dwell upon the mission of both.
	(d) Should boys and girls be allowed
to associate together?
	(e) Show the evil effect of an excess
of social life to our boys and girls.
	(f) Also show the evils of late hours
to their physical; and moral develop-
ment.
	(g) In what way do our hopes cen-
ter upon our boys and girls?
Lecture XI.—The Law of Chastity:
“A mother’s holiest task is to keep
	Her darlings pure and true,
Her constant care, her watchful prayer.
	Alone can guide them through.'
	(a) Give the true definition of chas-
tity.
	(b) How and where can personal
purity and chastity best be taught?
	(c) How can we as mothers best
avoid impure thoughts within our chil-
dren?
	(d) How can the home influence
and conversation promote purity of
heart?
	(e) What are the effects of breaking
these laws among us as a people to-
day?
(Refer to Juvenile Instructor, November 15,
1902, page 688; Matt. v: 8)
Lecture XII.—Marriage— Object and Sacred-
	ness of:
	“And again I say unto you that who so forbid-
deth to marry is not ordained of God, for marriage
is ordained of God unto man.” (Doc. and Cov.
Sec. 49, verse 15.)
	(a) What is the object of marriage?
(Gen. i: 28.)
	(b) How should we prepare for mar-
riage? (Doc. and Cov. Sec. 132, 15-66
verses.)
	(c) What are the existing evils re-
garding this covenant today?
	(d) How can we as Latter-days best
overcome these evils?

Nellie C. Taylor, Wealthy R. Clark,
Athalia M. Stead, Maria Clark, Committee.
Susan Grant, President Relief Society Davis
Stake.


WOMAN'S EXPONENT


[Column 1]
"]
