IV
	After the conversation between Sir Edward
and Dr. Bland, Miss Conway's mind was more at
ease, she felt that the responsibility must here-
after rest with the father, yet no understanding
had taken place between her cousin and her-
self, and she wondered whether her cousin had
replied to the letter from Mr. Walton, and felt
reluctant about making any inquiry or calling
the matter in question.
	A few days passed, and Sir Edward im-
proved rapidly, he was more cheerful and
talkative, though he never in any way referred

[Column 2]

to young Walton or the letter. Finally he
announced positively his intention of going
abroad soon, and requested the young
ladies to prepare their wardrobes, so as
to be ready on short notice. The Dr. had
given his permission, in fact had stated to Sir
Edward that it was the best thing for his
health and spirits. Sir Edward sent off to
London for his solicitor, so that all arrange-
ments might be made without his having to
leave temporarily the house, and the lawyer
was not long in answering the summons, for
knowing of the recent illness of his Lordship,
he naturally enough imagined that his affairs
should be looked into while there was oppor-
tunity.
	Mr. Burke (the family solicitor) knew more
of Sir Edward's family than even Dr. Bland,
and the three men were closeted together on
several occasions, which caused some anxiety
on the part of the household, especially the
young ladies. The upper servants gossiped
considerably over the matter, and the house-
keeper who assumed a great deal of import-
ance, having been left so much in charge pre-
vious to Miss Conway's coming, repeated to that
young woman some of the servants’ gossip, one
day when she was particularly loquacious, and
although the young lady did not think the
information altogether reliable, considering the
source, and feeling sure it had been obtained
by eaves-dropping, yet she felt there might be
a spice of truth in it and therefore listened a
few minutes to the housekeeper's version. Mrs.
Hess in her most gracious manner said "The
master would na go up to London to see after
his own business for fear Lady Anna might
get to see that young feller that came prowl-
ing around while he was away before—he's
after na good, I can tell ye Miss Conway
doubtless he's na better'n his father were, and
he stole Sir Edward's sweetheart away, and all
the time ptetendin to be his best friend. Rich
folk are na better'n poor ones, and not as 'onest
sometimes, do'ye know Miss Conway that
lawyer from London is makin a new will out,
and the old'un is burnt, and the master's afraid
he'll die in foreign parts and he wants things
settled so's if Lady Anna marries that feller
he'll not get his money not a shillin."
	Miss Conway thinking Mrs. Hess had gone
quite far enough into the secrets of the family
coolly replied, "I cannot listen to gossip like
this, excuse me, my uncle will be wanting me.',
The housekeeper was quite offended and
muttered to herself, "gentle folks are all alike
never want their affairs talked over, may be
she thinks we don't know how poor she was
when her uncle took her in, and now she don't
even want to be spoken to by a decent body
like me, that's been in this house ten years an'
more," and off she went to her own room to
regale herself with tea and something stronger
that she "al'ays kept by her in case o'sick-
ness."
	Miss Conway could not help thinking over
what the old lady had said notwithstanding
her distaste for 'servants gossip," and she felt
rather uneasy lest her cousin should be de-
frauded of her rights, still she placed great
coufidence in Dr. Bland's judgment and felt he
would be interested in having all legal mat-
ters disposed wisely.
	The same evening after this harangue of the
housekeeper's, a most unexpected occurrence,
transpired, that gave Miss Conway's sensibil-
ities another shock. The day was a very cold
one and the wind whistled and moaned ground
the old buildings, and swayed and bent the
great bare elms in the yard, as though in
agony and gloomy forebodings settled down
upon the mind of the Baronet's niece, she
seemed to dread something she knew not what:
she tried to throw off the feeling, took up a
book to read, unfortunately the book contained
only old legends that impressed her more

[Column 3]

gloomily she laid it down, stirred the fire in
the grate, the very air seemed oppressive, she
looked at her cousin, who sat silently musing,
it was growing dark, she rose and was about to
draw down the blinds, when she perceived the
shadow of a figure in the shrubbery, when she
looked more closely it had disappeared;
feeling sure some one was lurking  near
with a purpose in view, she left the library
where her cousin was sitting so composed, and
taking up a scarf that hung in the hall, and
carelessly wrapping it around her, so as to
screen her face a little, she stepped out
into the open air and soon reached the old
garden, where she felt sure some one was in
waiting. She was right, a woman came hastily
forward evidently greatly agitated and with-
out attempting to conceal her emotion in a soft
mellow voice and touching Miss Conway's arm
nquired, "Are you Lady Anna Hilliard?"
"No, madam, I am her cousin!" replied the
young lady, at the same time realizing she was
most awkwardly situated and by her own voli-
tion; she was sorry for having come into the
garden, and a hundred thoughts and evil pre-
monitions crept into her mind momentarily.
	The stranger hesitated when she learned it
was not the one she had supposed, and her
silence was only broken by suppressed sobs
that seemed to come from her inmost heart.
At last she said "I am treading on dangerous
ground, but my errand here justifies the
veuture. I am the mother of Lady Anna's
lover, and I must see her if it be possible, you
who refused my boy admittance, will surely not
deny his mother, I implore you to allow me to
speak to her for a few minutes here and alone.
My carriage waits outside the grounds of the
Castle. I have stolen my way hither, must I
ask in vain of you—a woman?" All this
seemed to come in a torrent of speech, and
Miss Conway regretted more than before that
she had precipitated this unexpected interview,
she did not dare to promise, she was afraid of
the consequences, but some reply must be made
immediately, and she answered with as much
assurance as she could collect. "My cousin is
not well; excitement would be dangerous for
her, my uncle is at home, and his consent
would be necessary." The lady hesitated and
finally said "I will even risk that if it can be
done without the knowledge of any other per-
son. My presence here would be ruinous to
all my son's hopes. I will wait until you re-
turn," and the lady shivered with nervous
tremor. She was wrapped in heavy furs, her
long cloak came to her very feet, her face
though sad was extremely lovely, and she had
the peculiar fascination that in woman seldom
fails to secure its object.
	While Miss Conway was absent she paced up
and down the old, wild garden, her thoughts
busy with the past, how strange that she
should come bending for favor to this place
where for nearly thirty years she had been an
utter stranger, and to him of all others, who
had thrown her love away as though it had
been a blight upon his life; the time seemed
long to this beautiful woman, yet it was only
a few minutes.
	Poor cousin Jane seemed doomed to be a
mediator and messenger of evil tidings and on
this occasion, strange, passing strange was her
message to her uncle she did not stop to con-
sider the danger to herself, or to him; she
thought only, how important must be the
matter that would cause a lady of rank and
station to come at night, alone, unbidden into
the garden of her sworn enemy; the very
thought gave her courage; she hastened to the
house knocked at the door of her uncle's room
and in a moment was in his presence. Before
he could speak, she hurriedly explained her
errand, and was surprised when he said, "I
will see Lady Walton myself, if she will come


[Column 1]

into the drawing room with you, but she must
not see Anna."
	Miss Conway did not wait for her uncle to
change his mind but flew to the garden, and
delivered his answer in the exact words.
Meantime Sir Edward suffered the most con-
flicting emotions. It was years since he had
seen this woman, he fancied that like himself
she would have grown old and clumsy, and
yet somehow his heart beat more rapidly as he
neared the drawing room;—he opened the
door. she was not there, he stood a moment
waiting, and growing more uncomfortable, for
such meetings are more awkward when one
waits, he heard footsteps and felt nervous
enough to hide himself like a bashful boy—
but Jane came to his relief, with the lady
leaning upon her arm, he was non plussed when
he saw this charming aristocratic woman in-
stead of one faded, wrinkled and grey that he
could scarcely even say "Lady Walton"—but
he was a man of the world, and he would not
let her see his embarrassment. His niece had
arranged seats for them by the fire, and with-
out the ceremony usual among people of rank
they were soon seated; while Jane stood as if in
doubt what the next move was to be. "I
make no apology for my presence here which
must be in the light of an intrusion, but I come
to speak for Maitland, my son," said the Lady,
"he has fallen desperately in love with Lady
Anna, your daughter, do not destroy his happi-
ness, and wreck his hopes as you once did
mine;" the Baronet rose to his feet, and turned
deathly pale—Jane feared he was going to
faint—she knew not what to do, or say, the
look upon his face was alarming, he tried to
speak—Lady Walton too had risen from her
chair, it was the first time they had met for
many years, and no explanation had ever been
made, and now the truth was coming to light,
the deception that had separated them, at last
he stammered, "I do not understand you, but
I cannot speak of that terrible past it is buried
forever." "Shall there be peace between us
then?" said Lady Walton in her sweetest voice,
the tears filling her eyes as she spoke, and
reaching out her hand to him, from which
she had hastily removed her glove. Sir Edward
would have been a brute indeed if he
could have refused such an offer, he was about
to raise the proffered hand to his lips, but she
withdrew it from his grasp and retreated a few
steps backward, and as she did so, said, "Sir
Edward what answer have you to make to my
question concerning our children?" "I cannot
answer on the impulse of the moment, this in-
terview has been a painful one I fear to both
of us, I will think the matter over, send the
young man to me I will see what there is in
him, as for Anna, she is a child, her mother
over again, she does not know what love
means, I am going to take her abroad, thought
of leaving the old ruin tomorrow;"—"but you
will wait until you have seen my boy Sir
Edward," said Lady Walton. "I suppose I
must, women generally have things their own
way, but remember I make no promises." And
so ended this strange meeting, without a good
nigh;t the Baronet insisted upon seeing her to
her carriage, but she would not permit it, his
niece accompanied her and saw her safely
seated inside, then hurried to the drawing room
where she had left her uncle; he was watching
for her to inquire what the Lady had said after
leaving him. "Not a word about you or the re-
sult of the interview, she pressed my hand at
parting and said, you are a wonderfully self-
possessed young woman, your presence gave
me great courage." "Sit down Jane" said her
uncle, "I want to talk to you, of the past, it
has been a sealed book for many long years;
but I must open its pages now, the time has
come, and you Jane are worthy my confi-
dence."
	It was late when the Baronet retired to rest

[Column 2]

that eventful night, and his nurse had to give
him an opiate, lest he should not sleep at all,
the remembrance of the past was awakened,
and the woman whom he had hated with
bitterness had come entreating a favor at his
hands; that she was still beautiful was greatly
to her advantage, had she been ugly and
wrinkled her cause might have been lost. He
had opened his heart to Jane, told her how
this woman had jilted him, and how after some
years of bachelorhood he had married Anna's
mother, a sweet-tempered, affectionate, angelic
young girl, an only daughter of an illustrious
family, who adored him with her whole soul.
	As for Jane she never closed her eyes, that
night at all, she hardly knew what to think,
she had only been in the house a few months,
and strange events had crowded one upon
another until her brain was almost bewildered
clear headed as she had thought herself; as
she lay trying to rest herself she pondered
upon the morrow, the coming of Maitland
Walton, her uncle had signified what his
answer would be to this young man, in his
conversation with her, and she could not help
wondering what would come next, and whether
after all they would go to Italy, at any rate
she hoped so, and that might change many
other things.


WOMAN’S EXPONENT.

['R. S. REPORTS.
	The Semi Monthly meeting of the Senior
and Junior Co-operative Retrenchment As-
sociation held at teh Fourteenth Ward As-
sembly Rooms, May 1st, 1875, was one of
unusual interest. Notwithstanding its be-
ing a "gala-day" the hall was crowded; the
speaking was spirited and inspirational.
President Mrs. M. I. Horne in addressing
the sisters upon the main subject of inter-
est at the present time, the establishing of
Home Industries, alluded to a remark made
by Sister E. R. Snow that those who en-
gaged in this work had the opportunity of
doing more towards building up Zion, than
the Elders who preach the Gospel abroad;
she believed it was a much greater work to
teach people how to live their religion at
home than to convert them. Counselor E.
R. Snow referred to the minutes of the
young ladies, and the increasing testimony
they were receiving in regard to the truth
of the Gospel, said this knowledge was only
received through revelation. She gave an
interesting sketch of her visit to Cedar
Fort, Fairfield and Lehi, where she had met
with the young people, who all seemed anx-
ious to be organized, gentlemen as well as
ladies; she had done a great deal of talking
in regard to the movement now in progres-
sion, and thought that more especially among
the young people, her labors had been ap-
preciated.  Many instructive and applica-
ble remarks were made by Counselors Z. D.
Young, P. Woodruff, and E. Howard, also
E. S. Barney upon the different branches of
Home Manufacture. Sister E. A. Whitney
sang "a song of Zion" which thrilled through
every heart in the whole assembly. Some
business relating to clothing for the Indians
was also transacted.
	EPHRAIM, SANPETE Co., April 30, 1874.
EDITOR WOMAN'S EXPONENT:
	It being some time since the organization
of the "Young Ladies' Retrenchment Asso-
ciation" of Ephraim, I am requested to
write you in regard to the association. Our
meetings were suspended during the win-
ter, but were again resumed on the 6th of
last March. Our President, Miss Carrie
Jensen, is anxious to have the meetings
[Column 2]
continue, that we may become useful. We
all hope that in time, we may be of some
use in the kingdom of God.
	Respectfully,
		HELEN E. ARMSTRONG,
							Secretary.
	BOUNTIFUL, DAVIS Co., April 20th, 1875.
EDITOR EXPONENT:
	Our Relief Society is prospering. We
intend having a Fair next month, the pro-
ceeds to go towards building a new hall.
Many of our sisters seem to realize that this
work is a mission placed upon them, and
are willing to forward it to the best of their
ability.
	I give here a few particulars of our last
meeting. Mrs. Zina D. Young, Mrs. Bar-
ney, Mrs. Steel and Mrs. Snider from Salt
Lake City were here; Bishop Call and other
gentlemen were also present.
	Meeting was opened with prayer by Mrs.
Zina D. Young. A Circular was read before
the meeting, soliciting the united influence
of the sisters, in sustaining Home Manufac-
ture.
	Mrs. Barney made some very excellent
and spirited remarks on Home Industry
showing how very necessary it was for us to
be a self-sustaining people, and help to
builb up Zion, &c.
	Mrs. Zina D. Young spoke on silk culture,
told how to raise Mulberry Trees. Encour-
aged the sisters to raise silk-worms, so that
silk and ribbons might be made in our midst.
Counseled them to cultivate the spirit of 
God, and give their children good teach-
ings.
	Several other ladies spoke on different
branches of Home Industry.
	Bishop Call spoke words of comfort and
encouragement to the sisters, he felt that
they held a high and responsible position
and could accomplish a great deal towards
building up the kingdom of God.
	Other Elders present made some very ex-
cellent remarks. The gifts of the Gospel
were manifested in our midst to our great
joy. We realized the Holy Spirit in our
hearts, bearing witness that we were ap-
proved by our Father in heaven.
	Meeting was adjourned for two weeks.
	Benediction by Elder Atkinson.
	ELIZABETH H. BARLOW, President.
				MARY CARTER, Secretary.
	The following is a brief synopsis of the
Minutes of a Meeting, for the Organization
of the Young Ladies of the 13th Ward, S.
L. City.
		Residence of Prof. C. J. Thomas,
						March 13th, 1875.
	Meeting opened by singing, "O my Fa-
ther &c.—On motion of Miss E. R. Snow,
Mrs. Rachel R. Grant was chosen to preside
over the meeting—Prayer by Mrs. Z. D.
Young—Singing, "Joy to the world, &c.
	Pres. Mrs. Grant spoke concerning the ob-
ject of the meeting, after which Mrs. Eliza-
beth Goddard was appointed Secretary pro.
tem., and the organization of the Rentrench-
ment Association effected as follows, by
unanimous vote, viz.:
	Mrs. Elizabeth Thomas, President,
	Miss Flora Shipp,		}
	"	Mary Ann McClean,}
	"	Ruth Woolley,		}
	"	Lillie Taylor,		}	Counselors.
	"	Lucy Stringam,	}
	"	May Wells,		}
	"	Louisa A. Ashby, Secretary,
	"	Annie Park, Assistant Secretary.
	On motion Miss Ruth Woolley was ap-
pointed to lead in Singing—Miss Annie
[Coumn 3]
Park was chosen Organist and Mrs. Fannie
Y. Thatcher Assistant Organist. After
which twenty-three young ladies were ad-
mitted as members, by unanimous vote.
	Mrs. Grant then addressed the Associa-
tion, exhorting all to be punctual in attend-
ance at meetings, and not let any trifling
consideration stand in their way. She re-
lated some of her own experience by way
of encouragement, in overcoming diffidence
by practice—how she had gradually gained
confidence, &c.
	Sister Grant was followed by Sister E. R.
Snow speaking words of encouragement to
the young ladies—bearing testimony of the
power of the Gospel, that the same mani-
festations were now produced as were expe-
rienced when Jesus and the ancient apostles
were on the earth. She said it was the
privilege of the young as well as the aged
to be in possession of the gifts of the Gos-
pel, such as healing the sick, prophesying,
speaking in tongues, interpreting, &c. That
the Spirit of God produced the purest and
greatest happiness that mortals can enjoy.
She related very interesting incidents that
occurred in the Kirtland Temple, and ex-
horted her young sisters to live for eternal
blessings, and fulfill the object of their
birth, by becoming noble women, saviors on
Mount Zion &c. Said she would like an
expression whether to fill the offices unto
which they had been chosen.
	Each of the young ladies arose by turn,
and expressed their satisfaction in being
present and participating in the privilege
which was accorded, and not only a willing-
ness but a determination, to fill their posi-
tions and discharge their duties in faithful-
ness.
	Mrs. Z. D. Young expressed her pleasure
in being present on this occasion. Testified
to having witnessed many manifestations of
power by the out-pouring of the Spirit of
God. Had heard singing of angels, seen
the sick healed, and she bore testimony that
many are made whole through the adminis-
tration of the sacred ordinances. Spoke of
the first time she read the Book of Mormon,
and how sacred it seemed to her. Exhorted
the young ladies to improve their great
privileges, &c.
	Mrs. L. G. Richards and Mrs. E. Thomas
gave much useful instruction—bore corrobo-
rative testimonies of the power of God
which they had witnessed in, and from their
childhood up to the present, and counseled
the young sisters to live for, and attain to a
fulness of blessing. 
	Meeting adjourned for one week—Sing-
ing, "Praise God from whom &c. Benedic-
tion by Mrs. E. Goddard.
					L. A. Ashby, Sec.
	SALT LAKE CITY May 10th, 1875.
DEAR EDITOR:
	By request of President Mrs. Rachel
Grant, I hand you the Sixth Annual Report
of the 13th Ward Relief Society meeting,
held in the 13th Ward Vestry, April 22nd,
1875. Showing that the Receipts, with a
small balance from last Report:
Amount in cash to.........................$208 00
Disbursed to the needy,.................. 206 10
	Leaving a balance in cash of.......90
Merchandise, clothing, etc., in Trea-
	sury from last report, valued at 80 00
Receipts in same during the past
	year......................................... 115 85
Disbursed to the needy................... 110 55
	Leaving a balance valued at..... 91 02


Cash invested in 13th ward Co-op
	and Saving Bank, with interest
	of the same, amounts to.......... 376 66
Work estimated........................... 28 75
	The meeting convened for reading the
Report, was well attended by the sisters a
good spirit prevailing.
	President Mrs. Grant regretted that so
little had been accomplished in the shape of
work, but was well pleased with the general
spirit of the meetings. Felt glad that we
were increasing in numbers and improving
in the good work, manifesting a more ear-
nest desire to be united, and be useful in
the Kingdom of God. Expressed a desire
to encourage and sustain Home Manufac-
ture.
	The sisters donate freely towards the
building of the Temple and other laudable
purposes. Most of them I believe, feel in-
terested in, and appreciate your valuable pa-
per.
	ELIZABETH H. GODDARD Secretary.


WOMAN'S EXPONENT.


[Column 1]
', 'R. S. REPORTS.
	The Semi-Monthly Meeeting of the Se-
nior and Junior Retrenchment Associations
in the Fourteenth Ward Assembly Rooms,
July 17, 1875, was very largely attended,
President Mrs. M. I. Horne and her Coun-
selors Mrs. P. Woodruff and Mrs. E. How-
ard, seemed inspired to speak to the sisters
upon the subject of obedience to coun-
sel, and to those in authority, and said it is
one of the saving principles: they also ex-
pressed their ideas upon the subject of ed-
ucation as regards the young, and the var-
iour influences in the midst of Zion that
have a tendency to draw them away from
the Gospel. They also sought to impress
upon the minds of all present, the great
need of seeking to lead the minds of the
young into the channel of truth; and let
their instructors be those who could while
imparting instruction from books &c., also
teach the principles of eternal life.
		PAYSON, U.T., July 6th, 1875.
EDITOR EXPONENT:
	I send you herewith a copy of the Annu-
al Report of our Relief Society which you
are at liberty to publish if you see fit. Our
Society is in a very properous condition
and we are trying to do all the good we can.
	We have recently had a visit from Sisters
Snow, Howard, Horne, Smoot and Barney,
which has very much strengthendd and en-
couraged us, so much so that we feel like
laying hold with renewed energy and hope
to accomplish much that we have hitherto
deemed almost impossible for our feeble
powers. May God bless and prosper these
our dear sisters in their labors of love among
the people, and may their noble example of
self-sacrifice in the good cause, inspire our
sex to deeds of emulation, that the cause of
Zion may receive an impetus that will be
felt throughout all her borders.
	I remain your sister in the Gospel of
Peace.
			MARY ANN HARDY,
					Secy. R. Society.
	Annual report of the financial condition 
of the society June 23d, 1875.
	Remaining in Treasury from
		last year's report............... $ 84.09 1/2
	Donations since...................	    171.47 1/2
	Disbursements as follows:
	For the Temple....................	  $ 12.00
	Expended on City Lot...........	     54.57 1/2
	For the Poor........................	    153.12
	Remaining in Treasury.........	      35.98
	Property belonging to the society is val-
ued at $424.87.
			SARAH FAIRBANKS,
						Treasurer.
			NEPHI, July 13th, 1875.
EDITOR EXPONENT:
	Feeling it a duty as well as a pleasure to
give you a short sketch of our prosperity, I
take this opportunity to say that we held
our Annual Meeting on the 26th of June.
[Column 2]
There was a general attendance of the sis-
ters. Our books show that we are progress-
ing slowly but surely. The faithful few
still try to keep the good spirit and to make
its influence felt in our meetings.
	On the 30th of June we had a visit from
Sisters Snow, Horne, Howard and Barney:
who gave us much good instruction in our
various duties as a Relief Society; I believe
all felt greatly benefitted and blessed by the
out-pouring of the Holy Spirit through their
teaching and counsel. And we feel to say,
may God grant them long life, to bless and
comfort our Relief Societies.
	We hope soon to be able to send subscrip-
tion money. Wishing you success in your
valuable paper, we subscribe ourselves your
sister in the gospel of peace,
			AMELIA GOLDSBROUGH, Prest.
	AMY  L. BIGLER, Sec.
			HARRISVILLE, July 1st, 1875.
EDITOR EXPONENT:
	I send you the following report of an in-
teresting meeting, held according to ap-
pointment to set apart the proper officers
for the Relief Society here, which had
been neglected.
	President Franklin D. Richards and Bish-
op L. J. Herrick were present, also Presi-
dent Mrs. Jane Richards and Counselors,
and a goodly number of other sisters. Pres-
ident F. D. Richards in the opening re-
marks said it was good to go to meeting but
few realized the benefit of speaking them-
selves; exhorted the sisters to let the spirit
of God light up their minds, and forget
their cares toils and trials; told them they
had gifts, especially the gift of healing, that
they had the right to wash and anoint, and
it would be a shield to the sick, as well as a
power of healing. Bishop L. J. Herrick
said he was much pleased with the minutes
of our last meeting, and exhorted mothers
to teach their little children to pray, said it
would keep them from wandering after
worldly things; spoke of the remembrance
he had always cherished of his mothers
teachings and prayers; said we were all de-
pendent upon each other for happiness,
more especially husbands and wives; and
added his blessing.
	President Mrs. Jane Richards said she
knew the Lord had blest her and also her
sisters who were helping in this great work.
She always felt happy in hearing them
speak; those who attended meeting kept
young longer by having a good spirit and
cheerful face; said we should never tire of
doing good; when meeting day comes we
should go, if you had visitors invite them
to go too, that would be doing right, said
we were capable of doing great wrong as
well as right. To the young girls she said
you have grown up in innocence, without
the traditions to overcome the older ones
have had: use no unkind words, partake of
a happy spirit, the Holy spirit teaches right
from wrong; said when she was set apart to
preside she did not know her duty but had
to learn it by degrees. To the Teachers she
said go with a good spirit when you visit in
that capacity, and if any are on the back
ground, you can do them good by a few
cheerful words, and always remember to
pray for your president. President Mrs. J.
Richard's Counselors and Mrs. Frodsham
made some good remarks, then Mrs. Melis-
sa Shirtliff made a few appropriate ones off-
ering to resign the presidency she held.
	President F. D. Richards then called a
vote to see if we could sustain our present
organization, and it was sustained unani-
mously.
[Column 3]
	Mrs. Melissa Shirtliff President, Mrs.
Sarah Jane Taylor and Mrs. Nancy Ransom
Counselors, were then ordained by Presi-
dent F. D. Richards and Bishop L. J. Her-
rick. Meeting then adjourned with the
usual ceremonies.
		S. A. SHIRTLIFF, Secretary.
	Minutes of a Retrenchment meeting held
in the First Ward at the residence of Mrs.
Margaret Steele, June 9th, 1875. After the
usual exercises and reading of the minutes,
Miss Annie Brown read an essay on Obe-
dience. President Mrs. Sarah Graham then
rose and said, the essay on obedience was a
good one; and that she was pleased to meet
with the society again; thanked them for
their prayers in her behalf, in her sickness,
said she was much better in health than she
had been, although not yet wholly recov-
ered; exhorted them to be punctual in at-
tending their meetings, and not to speak
evil of any one and to improve every mo-
ment of their precious time. Counselor
Mrs. Jane Van Tassell spoke of her desire
to do right, her interest in the work now
commenced among the young people, testi-
fied to the truth of the Gospel and the in-
spiration of President Young as a Prophet
of God. Desired they might all obtain a
standing in the church and kingdom of
God. Mrs. Alice Fletcher said she thought
it the duty of every one to speak, and try
to perfect themselves, and that the Lord
would bless them and give them wisdom.
Mrs. Margaret Steele said she was pleased
to meet with so many of the young sisters,
her whole heart was in the work; she always
felt edified and strengthened in these meet-
ings. They were all of one great family, and
should speak kindly to each other; a kind
word in a time of trouble was worth a great
deal. They should always go to their Heav-
enly Father with their troubles,said she knew
if they were humble and rose up to speak,
the gifts and blessings of God would be
poured out upon them; she advised all the
young girls to attend the meetings in the
Fourteenth Ward. Miss Zina Higgs, Miss
Janet Hill, Miss Josephine Young, Mrs.
Amelia Smith, Miss Rosy Smith, Miss Lyd-
ia Johnston, Miss Annie Brown, Miss Beu-
lah Adams, Miss Lena Searl, and Miss
Amy Adams each made a few remarks; af-
ter which President Mrs. Sarah Graham
spoke of the wish of President Young in
regard to discarding round dances, and said
she wished them all to bear it in mind and
not dance them; it was her desire and that
of her Counselors: there were many unprin-
cipled men in their midst who sought to
lead astray and ruin the young ladies, and
they must be watchful and on their guard
continually, and not yield to temptation,
but attend to their duties, to Sunday School
and all their meetings; use all their influ-
ence to sustain "HOME INDUSTRIES," and
trade among their own people, to keep
money within their own Territory that they
might help to build up Zion, and to assist
in building Temples and then they would
have the privilege of receiving blessings in
them. Exhorted all to attend the meetings
in the tabernacle and if they wanted to
walk out on the Sabbath day, that was the
proper place to go. Said she wished the
young men of the Ward would get the
spirit of reformation the young girls had,
there would be less rowdyism among them.
She again entreated the young girls to use
their utmost influence to get their young
companions to attend meeting. Meeting
then adjourned for two weeks, after sing-
ing and prayer.
		AMY ADAMS, Assistant Secretary.

		L. GREENE RICHARDS, Editor.

SALT LAKE CITY, AUGUST 1, 1875.


', 'R. S. REPORTS.
	The Semi-Monthly meeting of the Senior
and Junior Co-operative Retrenchment As-
sociation held at the Fourteenth Ward As-
sembly Rooms May 15th, 1875, was a par-
ticulary pleasant and interesting one. A
handsome wreath made of hair and nicely
framed, was presented by Miss E. R. Snow
to the President Mrs. M. I. Horne. Each
of the young lady secretaries had contribu-
ted a lock of their hair for the arranging of
the wreath, their names were also inscribed,
and it was addressed to the President as a
tribute of respect. To her it was entirely
unexpected, and it was with some difficulty
she could control her feelings sufficiently, to
return them her thauks and blessing. Coun-
selor E. R. Snow gave an entertaining dis-
cription of the Old Folks Excursion.
	Mrs. E. S. Barney made some suggestive
tive remarks in regard to the yonng ladies
engaging in the studies of medecine and
surgery; and the lectures which were to be
given on their account. The other speak-
ers were Mrs. S. A. Whitney, Mrs. R. R.
Grant and Mrs. Watmough each of them
seemingly inspired with the Spirit of the
Lord, prompting them to dwell upon the
subjects brought before the meeting by the
President and her counselors.
	HYRUM CACHE Co. May 15th, 1875.
DEAR EXPONENT:
	Our Relief Society under the Presidency
of Mrs. Christina Liljenquist held our Sev-
enth Annuel Meeting on the 10th inst. at
ten o’clock a. m.
[Column 2]
	On the stand were the brethren J. Wil-
son, C. C. Shaw and P. Christiansen.
	After singing and prayer, the meeting
was addressed by Counselor Mrs. Johnson,
upon the necessity of union and the respon-
sibilities of mothers. Mrs Stanley and Mrs.
Barnum spoke mostly upon the subject of
using good langauge. President Mrs. Lil-
jenquist thanked the sisters for decorating
the house for the occasion, gave some good
counsel and instruction—exhorted the young
ladies to use their influence to do away with
round dancing, and asked the Lord to bless
them.  The brethren then addressed
the meeting encouragingly; after which
Elder C. C. Shaw read an address to the 
Retrenchment Society by Miss E. R. Snow.
meeting then adjourned.
	At 2 p. m. the Society met at Elder P.
Christiansen's and partook of a sumptuous
dinner, and in the evening all that desired
attended a ball in the hall, and enjoyed
themselves in the dance.
	We are doing all in our power to sustain
home manufacture; we have sent two young
ladies to Brigham City to learn braiding,
millinery &c., and we hope with our own
exertions and the means we have on hand,
to build a house for the society, and to es-
tablish a school for the young ladies to
learn to make hats, bonnets and many of
the articles we are now importing.
	Our report shows we have paid $38, to
missionaries in St. George, $21 to the P. E.
Fund made two quilts the last year, besides
other articles, and ministered to the wants of
the poor. We have now in the Treasury $100.
We meet every two weeks to work and to 
transact all necessary business, also to im-
prove and comfort each other. We appre-
ciate your valuable paper and wish you suc-
cess.
	Your friend and well-wisher,
			NANCY M. NEILSON, Secretary.
		HARRISVILLE May 16th, 1875.
EDITOR EXPONENT:
	The Relief Society at Harrisville held
their Annual Meeting May 6th, 1875. It
was well attended, the speaking encourag-
ing and strengthening to those present.
	We have not accumulated as much means
as some societies, yet we let no one suffer
in our midst. All the society are willing
to give as much as they are able. We have,
at different times appropriated means to-
wards such purposes as are for the benefit
and building up of the kingdom of God.
	The health of our President Mrs. Melissa
Shirtliff has improved since our last report.
We have a good presidency ahd are progress-
ing gradually. Those who take the EXPO-
NENT appreciate its value.
	Yours Truly,
		MARY E. TAYLOR, Secretary.
	Minutes of The Organization Meeting of
the Young Ladies Retrenchment Associa-
tion of Cedar Fort held in the Meeting
house April 21st, 1875.
	Meeting opened by singing "Oh God, the
God that rules on high"—Prayer by Brother
Samuel Wilcox—Singing "Sweetly may
the blessed spirit—
	Miss E. R. Snow by request of Mrs. So-
phronia Cook, who presided, addressed the 
large audience, saying she was pleased to
see so many of the brethren manifest an
interest by being present on this occasion—
That in the Church and Kingdom of God
the interests of men and women are the
same; man has no interests separate from
that of women, however it may be in the
outside world, our interests are all united.
She was happy to see so many of the young
men—as a mother in Israel she felt that she
had a right to talk to them a little. She
spoke of the first organization of the young
[Column 3]
ladies—said it was introduced by revelation
from God &c. Gave some account of the
manner in which it was opposed; and even
by parents, who could see no necessity, for
young people to educate themselves in the
religion of Jesus. That parents only need-
ed this spiritual exercise. Said she was
truly thankful to see that some of the young
ladies of this place were brave enough to
manifest a wish to enter into an organiza-
tion.  She mentioned people calling on her
from the settlements, and saying that no
retrenchment was needed with them, they
were not extravagant in dress &c.—Then
she asked them if they had no ignorance to
retrench from, no uncouth manners, no evil
practices, notning at variance with the
spirit of the Gospel &c. which called forth 
responses, in favor of retrenchment organi-
zations, from their opponents. She referred
to the spiritual education of children early
in the church; saying that we had grown in-
to Sectarianism, so far as some of the gifts of
the gospel were concerned—young people
knew nothing of them, only by report.
Said she was in favor of book-learning but
that all the book-learning in the world,
would not qualify them to become Queens
and Priestesses in the courts above. It re-
quired a spiritual culture and education.
She suggested to the young gentlemen that
they move forward; lay hold of spiritual and
mental inprovement, and take the lead of
the young ladies. She urged them to re-
spect themselves too highly to submit to the
adulterating habits of the world, in the use
of tobacco and liquors. That they should
not defile their bodies, (which should be fit
temples for the Holy Ghost) with those
forbidden things. She expressed her be-
lief that the filthy ue of tobacco, so adul-
terated the stomach, and so destroyed the
natural powers of the digestive organs, as
to create an unnatural hankering for strong
drink, and lead to drunkenness. With
many other instructive remarks; after which
by request of the Prest. of the meeting she
proceeded to organize the society. Twenty-
six young ladies were admitted as members
by unanimous vote.
	Elder Samuel Wilcox, having been invi-
ted by President Mrs. Cook expressed his
sanction of what had been said, was thank-
ful for the move being made, and for every
movement for the good of Zion—said that
the cultivation of the young, on whom great
responsibility would soon devolve; was one
of the most important things, with other
words of instruction and encouragment.
	An Anthem was aung by the choir
Benediction by Brother Robert Parker.
		MELINDA COOK, Secretary.
			LEHI, May 20th, 1875.
EDITOR WOMEN'S EXPONENT:
	On April the 23rd, we had a pleasant vis-
it from Miss Eliza R. Snow, and Mrs. M. S.
Snow. Held a meeting in the Tabernacle
at 2 o’clock p. m. Sister E. R. Snow gave
some good instructions to the sisters, speak-
ing plainly of their duties as members of
the Relief Society, and showing the differ-
ence there would be in the future in the
positions of those who had attended to their
duties and magnified their calling, to those
who had neglected to do so; urged upon the
sisters the necessity of stepping forward
and helping to establish HOME INDUSTRIES;
said that President Young had made a spe-
cial call to the Relief Societies to assist in
this matter.
	A meeting was appointed for the evening


inviting all to attend, and with the permis-
sion of Bishop Evans. Miss E. R. Snow
would organize the young ladies.
	In the evening according to appointment
meeting opened in the usual way; after
which Mrs. Rebecca Standring was appoint-
ed secretary pro tem.
	Miss E. R. Snow spoke of the object of
these organizations, and referred to the
time when President Young requested her
to organize the young ladies in Salt Lake
City. Spoke with pleasure of the steady ad-
vancement of those who had stood firm to
their integrity from the first. Said it was
the privilege of the young to have a know-
ledge of the Gospel and enjoy its blessings
as well as their parents, and by uniting in
an association they would gain wisdom and
knowledge, and lay the foundation for fu-
ture usefulness in the kingdom of God.
Encouraged them to lay aside and retrench
from everything displeasing in the sight of
God and His servants and be determined to
live to the Lord; improving themselves in
all things, that they might become refined
and cultivated daughters of Zion, settng
an example before the young men worthy
of imitation.
	Said to the young brethren, it was their
place to lead, but inasmuch as they had not
done so in this matter, she hoped they
would follow, and improve the the good op-
portunity they now had to store their minds
with useful knowledge, and get a testimony
of the truth of this work for themselves
and not wait until they were sent on missions
to preach, for if they did they would not
only experience mortification themselves,
but disappoint others who were looking to
them as Elders of Israel from Zion, for in-
telligence they could not impart.
	The organization by unanimous vote
were:
	Miss Orinda Davis, President,
		"Barbara Evans,
		"Sarah Teryusson,
		"Sarah Wardles,
	Mrs. Lizzie Barns,			Counselors.
	Miss Sarah E. Wardles,
		"Lizzie Smith,
		"Sarah Taylor, Secretary,
		"Christie Gadly, Assistant Secretary.
	Seventy-nine young ladies were admit-
ted as members by unanimous vote.
	Miss E. R. Snow then spoke with great
power and earnestnes,s blessed the young la-
dies and asked God to bless them and in-
spire them to do right. She was followed
by Bishop Evans, who expressed his pleas-
ure in the movement, and his willingness
to organize the young gentlemen simi-
larly Mrs. Mary S. Snow made some re-
marks exhorting the young sisters to im-
prove their privileges.
	Meeting adjourned. Benediction by El-
der Isreal Evans.
']

Name: 12_resolved_resolutions_copy_resolutions respect
Representative_Docs:
['RESOLUTIONS OF RESPECT.

	We, The members of the Relief Society of Minersville,
unanimously adopt the following resolutions:
	Whereas, It has pleased our Heavenly Father to call
from our midst our beloved sister and friend,
	Therefore, Be it resolved, that we esteem her faithful
labors, and devotion to duty, as worthy the emulation
of us all.
	Resolved, That these resolutions be placed on the
records of the Relief Society, and that we condole and
sympathize with the husband and children, relations
and friends in their bereavement.
	Resolved, That a copy of these resolutions be presen-
ted to the family, also that a copy be sent to the EX-
PONENT for publication.
				MARY E. LIGHTNER,
				LYDIA BLACKBURN,	Committee.
', "RESOLUTIONS OF RESPECT.

	We the sisters of the Relief Society of the Second
Ward, Salt Lake City, unanimously adopt the following
Resolutions:
	Whereas, it has pleased an alwise Providence to call
from our midst by the hand of death, our beloved sister
and friend, Therefore,
	Resolved, that we do recognize in the death of our
Sister Caroline Iverson, the loss of an able, efficient and
faithful member of our Society.
	Resolved, that we esteem her past faithful labors and
devotion to duty as worthy the fellowship and emula-
tion of us all. 
	Resolved, that we condole and deeply sympathize
with the bereaved husband, children, relatives and
friends, in the loss of a loving wife, affectionate mother
and a true friend.
	Resolved, that these Resolutions be spread upon the
records of the Relief Society of the Second Ward, and
that a copy of them be sent to the bereaved husband,
also that a copy be sent to the WOMAN'S EXPONENT.
					SARAH WHEELER, President.
					SARAH MCGREGAR, Secretary.


", "IN MEMORIAM.

	DIED at Union Nov. 10th, ’96 after a lingering ill-
ness our beloved Sister Miriam Jones; she was a
faithful member of our Relief Society, as well as
being an active teacher for many years.
	She was charitable to the poor and beloved by
all who knew her. She leaves a loving husband
and three sons to mourn her loss besides a num-
ber of grand children.
	We desire to emulate her many virtues and
loving kindness to the needy: although through
failing health she was not able to meet with us
since our reorganization. We feel to appreciate
her labors in the past and desire a copy of these
resolutions placed on our record book and
published in the WOMAN'S EXPONENT.
	Now she’s gone we’ll not recall her
		From a paradise of bliss
	(Where no evil can befall her)
		To a changing world like this
	Her loved name will never perish
		Nor her memory crown the dust
	For the Saints of God will cherish
		The remembrance of the just.
				MARY A. SMART, President.
				EMMA BURGON, Rec. Sec.
RESOLUTIONS OF RESPECT.
	For Sister Mary Ann Stevens, wife of Edward
Stevens deceased, who departed this life February
6th, A. D. 1896.
	Whereas, an all-wise providence has called
away from our midst to a higher sphere, our be-
loved sister and co-worker in Payson Relief
Society, be it
	Resolved that in the departure of our beloved
sister we have lost an able and efficient worker,
also
	Resolved that we cherish her memory from the
fact that God and her religion were higher in her
thoughts than any worldly aims; that we do
esteem her faithful labors and devotion to the
Gospel as worthy of emulation to every member
of our society, further
	Resolved that we sincerely sympathize with the
bereaved children and friends in the loss of a
loving mother, sincere friend and true Latter-day
Saint, again 
	Resolved that these resolutions be placed upon

[Column 2]
the records of the Second Ward, Payson Relief
Society; a copy sent to the WOMAN'S EXPONENT
and one to her family, with these lines, appro-
priately selected,
	“Beautiful Toiler thy work all done
	Beautiful Soul into glory gone
	Beautiful life with its crowns now won,
		God giveth thee rest;
	Rest from all sorrows, watching and fears
	Rest from all possible sighing and tears
	Rest through all God’s endless years
		At home with the blest.”
	Resolutions of respect for Sister Sarah Brewer-
ton, wife of Charles Brewerton, who departed this
life April 28th, 1896.
	Since God in His infinite wisdom and mercy
has seen fit to remove to another sphere of action,
our respected and beloved sister Sarah, who held
many responsible positions in our Relief Society,
and was for many years a President of the
Primary Association; be it
	Resolved, that while we bow in humble sub-
mission to the will of ‘‘Him who doeth all things
well” we do recognize in her death the severe
loss of a faithful energetic and efficient teacher
and wise counselor in our Society; a true friend
to the needy a comforter in the hour of trouble,
a devoted wife, mother, and true Latter-day
Saint. We also recognize in her faithful labors
and devotion to the cause of Zion, an example
worthy of emulation of each member of our
society; further be it,
	Resolved, that we honor and cherish her
memory and many virtues, for peace and charity
covered her face as a mantle, cheerfulness beamed
from her countenance. We acknowledge the
hand of God in our loss, her charms endeared
her to us all, we shall miss her at our gatherings,
again,
	Resolved, that we tender our sympathy to the
bereaved husband and family relatives with the
bright thought of a glorious meeting in the world
to come, over which sin and sorrow have no con-
trol—and yet,
	Resolved, that these resolutions be spread up- 
on the records of the Relief Society of Payson
Second Ward, published in the WOMAN'S EX-
PONENT and a copy presented the husband and
family, with these selected appropriate lines, 
	“It was so sudden, our white lips said
	How we shall miss her, the beautiful dead,
	Who’ll take the place of the precious one fled
			But God knows best;
	We know He watches the sparrows that fall
	Hears the sad cry of grieved hearts that call,
	Friends, husband, children, He loveth them all
			We can trust for the rest.”
				JANE TANNER, President.
SARAH FINLAYSON, 
ELIZABETH B. STARK,	{Committee, in be-
MILLIE STARK.				half of Relief
							Society.


"]

Name: 13_water_sugar_flour_eggs
Representative_Docs:
['HOUSEHOLD HINTS.

	To Preserve Fruit Jellies from Mold.—Cover the
surface one fourth of an inch deep with fine pulver-
ized loaf sugar.  When thus protected, the jellies
will keep for years in good condition, and free from
mouldiness.
	Small Bread Pudding.—Boil one pint of milk
with a piece of cinnamon and lemon-peel; pour it
on two ounces of bread-crumbs; add two eggs, half
an ounce of currants and a little sugar: steam it in
a buttered mold for about an hour.
	Plain Baked Eggs.—Butter a dish that will bear
the heat of the oven; break the eggs carefully into
it and season with a little butter, salt, and pepper
if desired; putting the butter in small pieces over.
Place in a slack oven till set, and serve.
	Bread and Butter Pudding.—Butter a dish well,
and sprinkle some currants all around it; then lay
in slices of bread and butter; to two eggs well
whipped add one pint of boiling milk, sweeten
with sugar and pour it over the bread and butter.
Bake in a hot oven.
	Toast and Eggs.—Break the eggs into a stewpan
and add a little salt, pepper and fresh butter; set
the stewpan over a moderate fire and stir round
with a spoon, being careful to keep every particle
in motion until the whole has become a smooth and
delicate thickish substance; have ready convenient
sized crisp pieces of toast, pour the eggs upon
them and serve.
	Cottage Cheese.—Place thick, loppered milk on
the stove, and let it heat thoroughly, not to cook,
or it will be hard. Pour into a hot cloth, and let
it drain until dry; season with salt and butter.
Should it be rather dry, moisten with milk or
cream, and make into balls. Some prefer what is
called "smearkase." Prepare the milk as stated
above, but, instead of making into balls, thin with
sweet cream and add a little pepper.
	To Clean Floor Oil-Cloths.—Soap should not be
[Column 2]
used in cleaning oil-cloths. To half a pail of hot,
soft water, add half a cup of washing fluid, or a
piece of sal-soda half the size of an egg, with a
scrubbing-brush or broom rub hard, putting on only
sufficient water so that it will not run off; wipe dry
with a soft cloth; it will look as bright and shining
as when new. This is a good way to clean com-
mon paint, as it easily removes smoke, grease, or
dust, and does not destroy the gloss or varnish. It
is also useful in washing windows.
	Rice Milk.—Wash a tablespoonful of good rice,
drain and put into a stewpan with a pint of milk
upon the fire; when boiling place it at the corner
to simmer until the rice is quite tender, (for inva
lids it must be a pulp); sweeten with sugar and it
is quite ready. Or if preferred it may be sea-
soned with a little butter and salt. It should be
about the thickness of well made gruel, and it is
very nutritious especially for an invalid who has
been long ill.
	Poached Eggs,—Put a pint of water in a stew
pan; add half a teaspoonful of salt, place it over
the fire and when boiling break your eggs into it as
near the surface of the water as possible, let them
boil gently about three minutes; care should be
taken not to boil too many together; if the yolk
separates from the white it may be presumed that
the egg is not fresh, though the same thing may
happen through awkwardness in poaching. After
they are taken carefully from the water, placed in a
warm dish and drained, lay over them some pieces
of butter, and place before the fire or in a warm
oven for a moment only. Some people think to
add a small quantity of vinegar to the water in
which eggs are poached is an improvement. 
	For Cleansing the Hair and Scalp.—In the
warm days of summer, with dust and perspiration,
the heads of people, especially of laborers and
children, get very dirty, and common washing will
not remove this. Here is a recipe, given by a no-
ted lecturer on hygiene: In a pint bottle put a
spoonful of olive oil and add two ounces of best
spirits of ammonia or hartshorn; shake. Then
mix with three ounces of alcohol and, when thor-
oughly mixed fill the bottle with soft water. Re-
member to put in the ingredients in the order named
or you will have "stuff," and no chemical union at
all. To apply, take a spoonful or two of this, with
a little warm water; dip a sponge or rag in and
wet the scalp thoroughly, now rinse off in a plenty
of warm water, and you will be surprised at the
amount of dirt, though you may have thought your
head perfectly clean. For the heads of young in-
fants it is just the thing, as it instantly removes the
dirty scurf without pain or trouble. It should be
rather weaker for a babe than for an adult.


[Column 1]
', "HOUSEHOLD HINTS

	Grandmother's Gingerbread.—Cup and a half of
molasses, cup rich sour cream, teaspoonful salara
-tus, tablespoonful ginger, mix a little stiff.
	German toast.—To one egg, beaten well, add one
cup sweet milk or cream, season with a little salt
and pepper. Cut in slices. stale bread, and dip in
the milk to moisten, and fry in butter on a griddle.
This we think is an extra nice dish for breakfast.
	Good Johnny Cake.—Beat up one egg eith one-
third of a cup of sugar, add one tablespoonful of
melted butter, and one pint of sweet milk, two
and a half cups of meal, half cup of flour, rea-spoonful of cream-of-tartar and half teaspoonful of
soda.
	Sponge Gingerbread.—One cup of sour milk,
one cup of molasses, one-half cup of butter, two
eggs, one-half teaspoonful of salaratus, one table-
spoonful of ginger, flour to make it thick as pound
cake. Put the butter, molasses and ginger together
and make them quite warm, then add the milk,
flour and salaratus and bake as soon as possible.
	Sponge Cake.—The following recepie for sponge
cake is pronounced excellent: Ten eggs; the same
weight of sugar and one half the weight of flour.
The grated rind and juice of one lemon. Beat
yolks and sugar to a cream; then stir in gradually
and very gently the flour, and the whites of the
eggs, well beaten; add lemon.
	Doughnuts.—One teacupful of sugar, one tea-
cupful of sweet milk, one egg, one teaspoonful of
soda, one teaspoonful of cream of tartar, one tea-
spoonful of sweet cream or lump of butter the size
of a hickory nut and spice to taste, roll thin and
cut with a round tin, making a hole in the center
with a thimble; fry until a light brown.
	Breakfast Cake.—In absence of potatoes; or, to
be eaten with butter while warm. Equal parts
sifted corn meal and flour; stir or sift in carefully
while dry one teaspoonful of cream of tartar. Dis-
solve in milk (sufficient to mix, and form a batter
that will pour easily into baking tins), one level
teaspoonful each of carbonate of soda and salt;
sweeten to taste. Lastly, add one or two (accord-
ing to supply) well-beaten eggs to one cake, and
bake in a quick oven to a nice brown. Do not
scald and spoil your meal.
	How to Cook Oatmeal.—First, be sure to get new,
fresh oatmeal, as if it becomes damp or old it is
bitter. Put one quart of water into a tinned stew
pan; salt sufficient to be palatable; stir in carefully,
so as not to have it lumpy, three or four handfuls of
oatmeal. Put it over the fire and stir continually
until it has swollen all it will, using care not to
have it burn on the bottom. When it has swollen
all is will, add more water, and then put the stew
pan into a kettle of hot water and leave it cooking
several hours—the longer the better, as the longer
it is cooked the softer and more jelly-like it becomes.
Having the stew pan in another kettle of hot water
prevents its burning on the bottom, and you are re-
lieved from constant stirring, It is good with
milk, syrup, or sweetened milk; or even with but-ter alone.


[Column 1]
", 'HOUSEHOLD HINTS

     	Milk Stains.—Milk stains on serge dresses may
be removed by steeping the part in warm water.
	Jelly Cake—One cup butter, 1 cup sugar—4
eggs—11 cups flour—beat the eggs separately. 
	To Make Muffins.—Two eggs, two quarts of
flour, a pint of sweet milk, one pound of butter,
a gill of yeast, a teaspoonful of salt. 
	To Make Grandmother’s Gingerbread—Cup
and a half molasses, cup of rich sour cream, tea-
spoonful saleratus, teaspoonful of ginger; mix a 
little stiff.
	Clove Cake.—One cup molasses—1 cup sugar—
1 cup butter—1 cup buttermilk—3 eggs—3 cups
flour—1 tablespoon cloves—l of cinnamon—l tea-
spoon saleratus in milk—raisins. 
	Apple Sauce.—Two quarts of water, a pint of
molasses, a root of ginger, and boil altogether
twenty minutes; put in while boiling one peck of
pared, cored and quartered apples.  Stew till ten-
der. 
	Waffles.—One quart of flour, one pint of sour
milk, one teaspoonful of  soda, four eggs, a piece
of butter the size of a large egg, and a little salt.
Bake in waffle irons.  Sour cream and less butter
improves them. 
	Colors to Harmonize with Mahogany.—The
American Artisan says "mahogany must not be as-
sociated with red, scarlet or orange, otherwise its
effect is destroyed.  It harmonizes with almost
every other tint that is used in upholstery."
	Beef Stew.—Six pounds of the flank of beef,
cut in small pieces; boil in two quarts of water
until tender.  Then put in a dozen potatoes, a
dozen onions, and four turnips; cover it so that the
steam shall not escape; salt and pepper to taste. 
	Grape Jam.—Boil the Grapes in just water
enough to make them tender, strain them through
a colander, then in  one pound of pulp put one
pound of sugar; boil this half an hour; the com-
mon wild grape makes a nice jam. 
	Cream Cake.—Beat two eggs with one cup of
sugar, add one cup of thin cream, a teaspoonful of
salcratus and one of salt; flavor with nutmeg and
lemon or other spice, mix as stiff as will pour into
pans. A handful of currants improves it. Try
it. 
	Lamb Stew.—Take half a shoulder of lamb,
boil it in two quarts of water two hours.  Then
put in potatoes, onions if desired, and turnips cut in
quarters; two teaspoonfuls of salt and pepper to
taste.  Ten minutes before serving put in the
dumplings. 
 	Cream Pie. —One and a half cups of sugar,
three cups of flour, one cup of milk, two eggs, two

[Column 2]
tablespoonfuls of cream tartar, one teaspoonful of
saleratus. Cream for inside, one pint of milk,
one-half cup of sugar, a little butter and salt, two
tablespoonfuls of flour (or corn starch), Flavor
with lemon. (Make two pies). Stir into the
milk while boiling till sufficiently thick.
	Taking Care of Boots.—One who has experi-
ence is convinced that a coat of gum copal varnish
applied to the soles of boots and shoes, and repeat-
ed as it dries, until the pores are filled and the
surface shines like polished mahogany, will make
the soles waterproof, and also cause them to last
three times as long as ordinary soles.
 	Rice Pudding with Fruit.—Swell the rice with
a very little milk over the fire then mix fruit of any
kind with it, currants, goosberries scalded, pared and
quartered apples, raisins, or black currants, and
still better, red currant jelly, with one egg to bind
the rice; boil it well, and serve with powdered cin-
namon and sugar.
	Oatmeal Pancakes.—Stir into four cupsful of
cold water a teaspoonful of salt, a large handful of
graham flour and oatmeal till you have a thin bat-
ter.  Bake at once on a very hot griddle; the Gra-
ham flour improves the pancakes, as they are
more easily turned and not quite so dry as when
oatmeal only is used.  When preferred, one cup of
water can be left out, using instead of it a cupful
of buttermilk and a teaspoonful of soda. 
	Shin of Beef Soup.—Have the shin well
cracked up; put it to boil in five or six quarts of
water: boil it five or six hours; skim it very often.
Cut up very fine, half a white cabbage; chop two
turnips and three onions if desired; put them all
into the soup with pepper and salt, and boil it two
hours; take the bone and gristle out before serving.
If you have some raised dough, make up a dozen
balls, the size of a nutmeg, drop them into the
soup and let them boil half an hour.


[Column 1]
']

Name: 14_stake_conference_relief_society
Representative_Docs:
['WOMEN’S MEETINGS.
	The regular annual business meeting of
the National Woman’s Relief Society will
be held in the Salt Lake Assembly Hall on
Monday, October 1, at two o’clock p. m.
All officers and members of the society are
cordially invited to attend and participate in
the business of the meeting.
			E. B. WELLS, Secretary.
	The General Conference of the Relief
Society will be held in the Salt Lake
Assembly Hall, in this city, on Thursday,
October 4, meetings commencing at 10 a.
m. and 2 p. m. It is desired that there
should be a large attendance of the mem-
bers, and that each Stake organization
should be represented by its president, or
some one aifthorized to act for her. A
cordial invitation is extended to the Bishops
and all brethren interested in the welfare of
the society, also the Y. L. M. I. A. and
Primary Association.
		ZINA D. H. YOUNG,
						President.
		JANE S. RICHARDS
		BATHSHEBA W. SMITH,
		SARAH JENNE CANNON,
						Counselors.
	B. WELLS, Secretary.


', "EDITORIAL NOTES. 
	THE Secretaries of the Relief Society in this
Stake of Zion are hereby particularly requested
to prepare correct semi-annual reports, and for-
ward them not later than the 25th of February,
to the Stake Secretary, Mrs. Elizabeth Howard,
No. 60, Fourth South St., Salt Lake City, in order
that a full and complete report may be made in
time for the Stake Conference.  By order of Mrs.
M. Isabella Horne, President Relief Society, 
Salt Lake Stake.
	THE Secretary of the Central Board of the Y.
L. M. I. Associations is particularly anxious to
obtain correct semi-annual reports from each
Stake of Zion.  These should be forwarded so as
to reach her not later than March 10th, to
enable her to compile them in time for the General
Conference on April 6th.  Will the secretaries
of the several Stakes please send promptly to
Mary E. Cook, 323 East, Third South Street, Salt
Lake City, Utah.
	THE Stake secretaries of the Relief Society in
the various Stakes of Zion are urgently requested
to prepare semi-annual Stake reports and forward
them, so that they will reach this city on or before
the 20th of March, in order that a complete re-
port may be furnished the General Conference in
April.  Direct to Dr. Romania B. Pratt, corner
2nd West and First South Streets, Salt Lake
City.  By order of Sarah M. Kimball, Secretary.
	THE quarterly conference of the Relief Society
of this Stake of Zion will be held in the 14th
Ward Assembly Rooms, Thursday, March 14,
commencing at 10 a.m. and 2 p.m.  A representa-
tive from each branch Society in the County is
particularly requested, and a cordial invitation is
extended to all who are interested in the work of
the organization.  The Y. L. M. I. A. of this
Stake will meet in conference Friday, March 15,
and the Primary Association Saturday, the 16th.


A full representation from each Association is
very desireable at both these conferences.
	THE Secretary of the Central Board of the
Primary Associations is desirous of obtaining a
full report from each Stake of Zion for the com-
plete report to be prepared in time for the Apirl
Conference, and this semi-annual report should
be ready ten days before, or about the 25th of
March.  Consequently the reports from all points
ought to be in the hands of the Secretary as
early as the 10th of March at the latest, which
would give her then only two weeks time to pre-
pare them.  It is a very important thing, and the
Presidents of Primary Associations in the several 
Stakes of Zion, should see that this matter is
properly attended to, both as to correct figures
and statements and date of sending.  Forward
the Stake reports to Mrs. Cornelia H. Clayton,
No. 115 Third Street, Salt Lake City.
	MRS. EMILY S. RICHARDS, the delegate from
Utah to the Annual Convention of the N. W. S. A.
at Washington, D.C., returned home Wednesday
evening, Feb. 13.  She gives a pleasing account
of her association with the leading ladies of the
suffrage movement, and withal feels glad of the
success which seems to have attended her efforts
in behalf of representation for Utah, and is hope-
ful for the future. Mrs. Richards will probably
give us something definite concerning her work
there, and her views in reference to the interests of
suffrage before we issue another paper, and will
most likely speak in public before that time, and
we shall be pleased to publish such notes. Her
speech in another part of the paper, will meet the
approbation of all suffrage women, and they will
certainly feel she must have made a good im-
pression in their favor.


WOMAN'S EXPONENT.


", "EDITORIAL NOTES.

	THE Quarterly Conference of the Relief So-
ciety of the Juab Stake of Zion will be held in the
Nephi meeting house, on the 23rd of September.
The Young Ladies' Mutual Improvement Associa-
tion and Primary Association on the 24th of the
same month, the day following.
	THE Stake officers of Y. L. M. I. A. are hereby
officially notified to prepare semi-annual Stake re-
ports, carefully, upon printed blanks, and forward
them as early as the 15th of September, that a
complete and satisfactory report may be made to


the general conference in October. Direct the 
reports to Miss Mary E. Cook, 323 E., Third
South Street, Salt Lake City, Utah.
	THE Stake Secretaries of the Relief Society
are hereby officially notified to prepare semi-an-
nual Stake reports, and forward them directed to
Dr. Romania B. Pratt, Deseret Hospital, Salt
Lake City, Utah. All reports should be in the
hands of Dr. Pratt as early as September 20th, in
order that she may have sufficient time to prepare
the general report for the October Conference.
	THE Conference of the Relief Society of this
Stake of Zion will be held in the 14th Ward As-
sembly Rooms in this City, on the 15th of this
month (September) commencing at 10 a.m. and 2
p.m. It is hoped by the President and officers of
the Relief Society of this Stake that there will be
a fair representation from each organization in
in the county. As it is the time for written reports,
and as the secretaries have been so often reminded
to send the reports punctually to the secretary as
early as the 5th of the month, there will of course
be no failure in that respect. All those interested
in the Relief Society and its benevolent work are
cordially invited to attend.
	THE Y. L. M. I. A. Conference of this Stake
will be held on Friday, the 16th, at the 14th Ward
Assembly Rooms, commencing at 10 a.m. and 2
p.m., and it is earnestly desired there should be a
good representation from all the several Associa-
tions in the County. Brethren and sisters inter-
ested in the work of improvement mentally, moral-
ly and spiritually of the young people of Zion,
are invited to attend the meetings of the confer-
ence.
	THE Primary Associations of this Stake will
hold their conference on Saturday, the 17th inst.,
in the Salt Lake Assembly Hall, commencing at
10 a.m. and 2 p.m. It is expected there will be
an interesting programme and exercises by the
children, and it is hoped that each Association
in the county will be represented, and a large
number of children with the presidents and officers
from the several wards in the city. The spiritual
development and training of the little ones is cer-
tainly an important work, and those who have
charge of these Associations should receive en-
couragement from the parents in their labor of
love and duty.
