['WOMAN’S SUFFRAGE AT OGDEN.

	The monthly meeting of the Woman’s Suffrage
Association of Ogden convened at the County
Court House, July 1st 1889.
	Prest. E. Y. Standford called the assembly to 
order.	
	Prayer by the chaplain Mrs. Odell.
	The minutes of the previous meeting were read
and approved.
	Prof. L.D. Edwards rendered the solo, “Man
the Life Boat,” in excellent style.
	Mrs. E. B. Wells was next introduced to the
assembly. The lady ably handled that important
subject, “Woman’s Rights.”
	At the close of her highly interesting lecture, a
hearty vote of thanks was tendered Mrs. Wells.
	Prof. Edwards favored the audience with an
instrumental solo, performed with his usual skill	
	Mr. Scott Anderson next delivered a short ad-
dress.
	Meeting adjourned for one month. A full at-
tendance at the next meeting is earnestly solicited
	The Suffrage Association held an interesting
meeting Friday afternoon, August 2, in the court-
house. Mrs. Hilliard delivered an interesting ad-
dress on woman’s work, her influence in the world
for good, and the result of her labors through these
organizations in suppressing intemperance and its
attendant evils. Five minute speeches were made
by Mrs. Pingree, Williams, Careswell, Miss Can-
field and others. These ladies esteemed it their
duty to enter their protest against the increase of
saloons, the establishment of gambling dens,
variety shows and houses of ill-fame, and by a
petition would remonstrate against these evils and
ask the City officials of Ogden to enforce the laws
to crush them out of existence.
	The Misses McGaw delighted the audience in
their rendition of a song and duet, when the meet-
ing adjourned for one month.
							Aggie Herrick.
					Assistant Sec’y.

	Mrs. Wells address was published in the
Woman’s Exponent of July 15.


[Column 1]
W. S. A. Meeting.
	The Utah W. S. A. held its regular meeting
in the Social Hall on Friday July 19, Vice
Pres. Mrs. Nellie R. Webber in the chair.  On
the platform at her side the Ch. of the Ex.
Com. Mrs Ann E. Groosbeck, The Secretary
Mrs. C. H. Horne and the Treasurer Miss
Margie Dwyer were also present, Miss V. Neb-
eker furnished music on the organ, and a
prayer was offered by the Chaplain Dr. E. S.
Barney.  Minutes of foumer meeting were
read by the Secretary and also the revised By
Laws were read and voted upon, names of new
members presented and some micellaneous busi-
ness transacted.  A letter was read by the

[Column 2]
Secretary from the President Mrs. Margaret
Caine tendering her resignation, which was
formally accepted and a motion was made and
seconded that Mrs. L. D. Alder be acting
President the remainder of the year.
	The Chairman then called upon Mrs. S. M.
Kimball who had just recently returned from
the East to address the audience.
	Mrs. Kimball made some telling remarks
on the suffrage question, and then took up
the subject of dress reform. She had visited
the rooms of Annie Jenness Miller in New
York, and had examined into the details of
the matter somewhat deeply, and her remarks
were interesting and the subject as she pre-
sented it worthy the careful attention and in-
vestigation of all concerned in the future
welfare and well-being of mankind.
		MRS. E. B. WELLS.
		ADDRESS TO U. W. S. A.
	Mrs. President, Ladies and Gentlemen.—
Last night in pondering over the subject of an
address to this assembly which I had been re-
quested to make to-day, these words came
forcibly to my mind:

		I live for those who love me
		For those who know me true,
		For the heaven that smiles above me
		And the good that I can do.

	Otherwise in the midst of my multiplicity of
duties I should have given it up. If my ex-
perience or my views are of any benefit to those
less acquainted with the franchise, or who
have not had the same opportunities for ob-
taining information on these points, then I am
willing to do my best to help—hoping that I
may do some little grain of good.
	I do not, and never could understand why
sensible women with mature ideas should pro-
test against woman’s suffrage; the ballot for
women as well as men seems to me to be en-
tirely consistent with the principles of justice
and equity, and I cannot comprehend why
men need the franchise more than women;
those who believe that all men support and
protect all women, might say so, but every
woman that has kept her eyes open in this
world knows that this is not the case.
	There are to be sure honorable exceptions,
but it is not the general rule—think of it ye
who may! Women have quite as much at
stake in all matters pertaining to “life, liberty
and the pursuit of happiness" as men, and
they belong to the people, that is in our country
it has been so declared. The preamble of the
constitution says, “We the people of the
United States in order to form a more perfect
union, establish justice, ensure domestic tran-
quility, provide for the common defense, pro-
mote the general welfare, and secure the bless-
ings of liberty to ourselves and our posterity,
do ordain and establish this constitution for
the United States of America.” Certainly
women were a part and included in this great
body politic. They desire justice and are the
ones who do the most to promote domestic
tranquility, and when it says “for ourselves
and our posterity,” who is more interested than
the mother? In the days when these words
were written there is not the least doubt that
the women did their full share of the work,
and helped carry the burdens. We who have
been pioneers in colonizing can appreciate
something of their duties and the cares that must
necessarily have fallen upon these early settlers
of America.
	Are men really so much more capable than
their mothers, or wives, or sisters? That is a
question still open to discussion, and many
learned men and women have argued it in print
under the title of “sex in brain". and as far as
co-education is concerned where young men
and young women have been trained in
the same pursuits and studies, and by the
same methods, the girls quite frequently

[Column 3]
carry off the honors; which was notably the
case recently in San Francisco—as you who
read the papers must know.
	Article I. of the amendment to the con-
stitution says: “The right of the people to
peacably assemble and petition for a redress
of grievances, etc.” That right is one that
I think has never been denied to women,
though quite often the redress sought has not
been obtained. In this women are recognized
as a part of the people. But as we often say
in regard to the scriptnres, so it is in politics.
Man means woman in certain places, but when
the divines and law-givers want it to mean
man alone—they so decide. And so they con-
tinue to deal out to women, as they feel in-
clined, a little here and a little there, or none
at all as the case may be, place and power in
public life.
	I know some advocates of woman suffrage
say, if men will not give us our just rights—
equal privileges,we will not accept a part; we
want the whole loaf and not crumbs, but the
principle a little at a time is certainly better
than none at all—and when any new revolu-
tion in affairs comes gradually the people are
better prepared to take hold of it. Here is a
pretty poetical illustration:

	Little by little the torrent said
	As it swept along its narrow bed,
		Chafing in wrath and pride,
	Little by little and day by day,
	And with every wave it bore away
	A grain of sand from the banks which lay
		Like granite walls on either side

Whereupon you will readily, see I an in favor
of taking what we can get, let it be ever so
grudgingly given. The great point to be
gained now, is to prepare women for the polit-
ical privileges that are bound to come, if they
do come slowly.
	We often say the mothers need it, and that
is true, there are I believe more arguments in
favor of mothers than any other class, though
I would not state it positively; but there are
women and I have instances one or two,
especially in my mind, who are not mothers,
who are and have been public benefactors, and
I think how much more they could have ac-
complished if they had been privileged with
the political and I might almost say social
rights of men. They have been deprived of
motherhood such as has been vouchsafed to
others, perhaps for the greater good they have
accomplished as mothers of peoples.
	There is much that might be said definitely
on this subject, but there is one other of vital
importance I would like to touch, and that is
the mother’s right to her child—it is said of
one woman that when she heard that in the
State of Rhode Island—the father could ap-
point a guardian for his child and deprive the
mother of all right to it if he so chose, she
said: “Why do not all the women of America
cry aloud against this terrible injustice to
mothers?” Many of the states have had their
laws more or less modified in this respect, since
women began agitating equal rights, but much
injustice in this regard still exists.
	Next on the programme was a song very
sweetly rendered by Miss E. Morehead.
	ROMANIA B. PRATT M. D..
Some reasons for women studying politics.
	“Politics is the science of government, that
part of ethics which has to do with the regu-
lation and government of a nation or state.
The preservation of its safety, peace and pros-
perity, the defense of its existence and rights
against foreign control or conquest, the aug-
mentation of its strength and resources, and
the protection of its citizens in their rights,
with the perservation and improvement of their


[Column 1]
﻿morals.” The potency and pith of every line
n the above quotation find full application in
he home circle, the miniature type of all or-
ganizations, where women preside conjointly
with their husbands or should do so. If
women can be educated in and have a voice in
the government of the city, county, state or
territory in which they reside they would be a
mighty factor in restraining the rise and
flourishing of the myriads of dens of temp-
tation which now bristle from every corner of
all great cities, alluring the youthful and
curious mind into paths which lead to lives
not only pernicious to the welfare, peace and
prosperity of the community, but add to the
burdens of the moral and law-abiding citizens,
by forcing them to contribute of their hard
earnings in taxes to support prisons and jails,
in which the lawless are herded in idleness
usually, because useless and dangerous if at
liberty. Women I believe would be swift to
use every power vested in them, that would
preserve their sons and daughters in the paths
of truth and honor, and establish them in lives
of usefulness and intelligence. Their watch
care and solicitude over their home circles
would stimulate them to extra diligence to get
time to cast their votes, or lift their voices for
honest men and women as officers and against
all measures which endangered the morals of
their families. Where is the science of govern-
ment more required and capable of eternal and
far reaching good than in the home circle where
the seeds of justice, morality and law abiding
instincts are planted in young hearts yet at
the mothers knee? There would be better
home government, parents recognizing and
respecting the rights of their children and the
children the authority and instruction of
parents and of each other. The study of the
code of equal rights will benefit every one
whether they ever become voters or not. The
study of politics is a means of development
and therefore it is important that women pur-
sue it, and get a good understanding of it in
all its phases and make obsolete the favorite
objection of some men to women in politics,
that she does not know enough. Victor
Hugo has said the 19th century is the century
of women. “Revolutions go not backward but
forward,” and those of progress are growing
more rapid continually and cutting a wider
swath in the field of musty traditions and
deep worn ruts of thought and habit that have
so long environed women within the narrowest
limits possible. The spirit of the century set
its seal in the foreheads of a few brave souls of
womanly form, whose visions and realizing
sense of equity and justicew as of such strength,
brilliance and fixedness of purpose as to sink
into as deep insignificance all contemptuous
epithets as “strong minded, soured old maid
dissappointed in love” etc. as the moon pales be-
fore the brightness of the noonday sun. The
pioneers of all innovations have been strong or
never would the walls of traditions and old
customs have been broken down and swept
away, and a smooth path beaten for the feet of
weaker ones to follow. When great ends are
to be achieved, a persistence and perseverance
of purpose without a shadow of turning is the
only means of gaining victory. What is this
spirit of the 19th century, that is inspiring
men as well as women with a determined spirit
of resistance. Which says we are all equally the
children of the God of this earth and He
created it for us all to enjoy. Is it not the in-
nate spirit of Justice. Every one born
in the United States whose parents are citizens,
is a citizen by right of birth, but a birth does
not constitute or make the person a voter. To
vote is a privilege and not a right which has
been clearly explained to us in a previous
session and is regulated by the legislature of
each state governed by certain specifications
defined in the constitution of the state or or-
[Column 2]
ganic law of the territory. In the original
thirteen states sex was not a bar to voting but
gradually each state inserted “male” as a
qualification of a voter until now Vermont is
the only state which has not this word in the
suffrage clause of its organic law; but it has
inserted it in its election laws. Monopolies
are rising hydra-headed all over the land and
making it almost impossible for the poor to
live. Many thousand women earn their own
living and many earn it for their families also
doing the same work as men with only half
the wages.  Many women are equally capable
of filling a number of lucrative offices which
now are only eligible to men, where their work
is not heavy and would seem in the fitness of
things more suitable to women than men.
Why not let capacity and ability be the test of
eligibility and not sex? Power is held in re-
spect and when women hold the ballot they
will command more deference then, in virtue
of that power than they do now through the
mere sentiment of chivalry. It is affirmed by
the great workers in suffrage that the greatest
opposition to the cause is the apathy and in-
difference of women themselves. With some
it is from physical exhaustion, because they
have too much to do domestically to have
scarcely an hour a day to read or think of any
thing outside of the four walls of their own
houses and also in the fact that woman’s work
in a family is never done. Yet if the subject
can be so presented to them as to cause a
realization of its benefits to them and others,
an interest will be created and some time will
be found and utilized in investigating the
subject which will result in positive good, that
is now spent less usefully in moments of neces-
sary relaxation. But there is a large class who
have both the leisure, education and ability,
who from sheer apathy and indifference lan-
guidly say they have all the rights they wish
and have no desire to have a voice in politics.
The worst besetting sin of the world is selfish-
ness and will be the very last to loosen its
fangs from the human soul as it passes the
“Gates ajar.” If this great army of fortunate
women would use a few of their leisure hours
in reflecting upon the sad and despairing lives
of thousands of their unfortunate sisterhood,
girls of the factory whose lives are days of toil
for a mere pittance, sewing women with wages
scarcely adequate to keep the wolf from the
door, the drunkards wives who not only have
poverty and toil to contend with, but unkind-
ness and even blows, and numberless other con-
ditions of women too numerous to mention now,
and could be brought to realize that it is in
their power and would be philanthropic and
benevolent to work for the amelioration of this
unchristian condition of affairs, the fear of
being called strong-minded and masculine
would vanish and every man’s wife would in-
sist on knowing why shackles should remain
upon her sex when even the negro is freed, and
why barriers and bans are planted in her path
of progress as a citizen, when she is fully en-
titled to and should be protected by the strong
arm of the law, with equal rights to life,
liberty and the pursuit of happiness with her
brother. It does not follow as a natural
sequence if women possess the full franchise
that she must or will be elected or accept every
office in the gift of the people. This can
safely be trusted to her sense of propriety and
the good judgment of the people. Let us as
an association seek diligently to inform our-
selves on all matters of government, trying
with the simpler forms as the Municipal, then
County, Territorial and State and the United
States. This does not imply, we should devote
our whole time exclusively to them to the
neglect of our home or business duties, but to
take hold of the subject systematically and at the
beginning and follow it up slowly but surely.
We can then understand better what are our
[Column 3]
rights, and the best manner of petitioning for
them. Much hard and earnest work has been
fruitless, and valuable time lost by basing the
arguments in favor of certain legislature on
debatable premises, for instance the 16th
amendment to the constitution of the U. S.
Those unfavorble to woman suffrage contend-
ing it would be unconstitutional and impossible
while those in favor and apparently with good
logic arguing it as proper and feasible.
	Assertions are not proof, and railing against
and berating masculinity in general and going
to the other extreme of lauding women far
above men is all a waste of time and force. In
a nutshell our duties as suffragists are to in-
form ourselves and instruct each other in  the
science of government, to interest all our
friends in the movement, to convert our fathers,
brothers and husbands to the fact that we can
understand and wield an intelligent power in
politics, and still preside wisely and gracefully
at home, and that until woman’s voice is equally
incorporated in the vox populi that it is not vox
Dei.
	On motion of Mrs. Dougall a vote of thanks
was tendered the ladies, who had so ably
assisted the programme committee. Adjourned
for three months.
		Cornelia H. Clayton. Secretary,


WOMAN'S EXPONENT.


', 'WOMAN SUFFRAGE MEETING.
			[CONTINUED]
	A well written address was then delivered
by
		DR. MARTHA P. HUGHES.
	To make men and women nobler, or purer,
or more intelligent, is the object of every
worthy enterprise, political and social, and
without such an aim, every undertaking should 
fail. That such has been the endeavor of the
earnest workers in the field we are now investi-
gating is beyond a doubt in my mind. It is
remarked by one of our modern thinkers,
"that it is not enough to be prompted by a
good motive or be inspired with a zealous de-
sire for the welfare of men and women, for
often honest enthusiasm and conscientious
finaticism have been the exponents of ruinous
schemes and fearful woes, neither is it sufficient
to simply find the instruments for the work of
progress and reform, there must be a combina-
tion of motive and work, a blending of in-
tellect and emotion, a strong desire with a firm 
hand, a righteous purpose with competent skill
to accomplish any permanent good, or to carry
to success any needed reform."
	In the woman's suffrage movements of recent
times there has been exhibited nearly every con-
ceivable combination of motive and instru-
mentality, and the success attending the labors
of philanthropists who have been engaged in
this work of reform has been in many ways
equally vacillating, complicated and discon-
nected, yet out of the work of the last forty
years, those interested in woman's advance-
ment, can find much to encourage and repay
them, and woman to-day has attained a posi-
tion legal and social far above that she held
half a century ago. The women of to-day have
obtained from men their much desired and
much deserved liberty. In these times the
standards of value have been readjusted, and
whereas once physical force and reckless cour-
age, were held in the highest and almost exclu-
sive esteem of mankind, now the jewels of wis-
dom, purity, culture, beauty, love and skill
have a value, that outweigh and can purchase
that brute force once so priceless. Once to be 
born a female. was to become a plaything and
a slave, to-day in the best and most powerful
circles, woman is the peer of the noblest man.
	There was a time when women possessed
none of the rights which they have to day, and
the increasing respect, which the law-makers
are showing them, marks with its various gradu-
ations the advancement of civilization and
Christianity. The regard which men have for
culture, refinement and purity is measured by
their love of women.  "The grade of a man's
mind, and moral attainments, can be safely es-
timated by the respect he shows his lady
friends." To feel the value of true woman-
hood, and to appreciate its delicacy, requires a 
nice and acute intellectual power. Brutes 
never feel it. Barbarous or half civilized men
never recognize it.

[Column 2]
	"The laws of a land are the best indices of
the people's acquirements, and are throughout
all history, the standard by which we judge of 
national advancement in civilization and
morals. We have but little reason to be
ashamed of our judicical standard today, for
the most impartial minds admit that our legal
practice is more just and reasonable in its deal-
ing with women, than that of any nation, or of
any time mentioned in history. There is much
however remaining for great minds to do, and
for the masses to accept, before either male or
female citizens can be said to enjoy perfect free-
dom and perfect equality. Certain it is how-
ever that never has the code of any people
given to woman such extraordinary privileges,
as have been bestowed by the judicial decisions 
and statute laws of our land."
	With the exception of the right to vote and
hold certain political offices, there are no rights
given to unmarried men which unmarried wo-
men do not possess. But it is this right to vote
that we as women of Utah are now contending
for. Having once enjoyed the elective fran-
chise, and having had it taken away from us
without cause, we are justified in appealing to
the nation to regain that which rightly belongs 
to us. That the privilege of voting, and the
holding of political offices by women is going
to do all that the zealous advocates of woman's
suffrage now claim is doubtful in my mind, still
there are no justifiable grounds on which these 
privileges should be withheld from them.
	All men and women are created free and 
equal. All are morally responsible for their
own wrongs. "The difference in their natures is
not a difference in value; but in the kind of 
merit. He has qualities which she has not, she
possesses traits and genius he does not; they
differ from each other and yet, in their worth
to the world, and to God, they are equal with
each other."
	Every human being of sound and matured
judgment should be personally responsible for
his or her own criminal conduct. Sex merely
should not debar any person from public office
or emolument if a constituency be found desir-
ious of his or her service.
	"No privileged class either of sex, wealth, or
descent should be allowed to arise or exist, all
persons should have the legal right to be the
equal of every other, if they can." After wo-
men have attained equal rights in every respect
before the law, that men now enjoy, and it is
only a matter of time when this will be the
case, there will doubtless then be some
drunken, brutal husbands, some parsimonious
and contemptible heads of families, fraud and
wrong doing, aching heads and aching hearts,
perhaps as many as now, but the skirts of the
law will be clean, and will no longer be com-
pelled to bear the blame; and woman will then
stand where she rightfully belongs, on an equal
platform with man before the law.
	"Nature, civilization, Christianity and the
chivalry of the law, as it comes to us from 
Knights of English fendal ages, all combine to
impress upon us, the moral and religious pre-
cept that women should have more rights be-
fore the law than men." But we simply ask for
equal recognition, still I am forced to feel that
many of the hardships that women endure,
arise more from custom and fashion, than from
disabilities before the law.
	"Woman as much as man is the architect of
her own fortunes. There are yet many realms
of silence to be made vocal, many scientific
truths to be discovered, many arts to be per-
fected which require the hands and the minds
of woman. She must feel her new responsibil-
ities." She must begin as nearly all great men
have begun, and give herself to the acquisition
of wisdom and skill, and her very opposition
and difficulties will be gain. Woman must throw
off her bondage to fashion, her submission to

[Column 3]
folly, and desire for frivolities. She must de-
feat the enticements of pleasure, the flatteries
and censures of men. It is a shame, even a
crime under divine laws for a woman to lace
herself into clothing, which makes a consump-
tive suicide of herself, and deteriorates the
physical being of her offspring. It is folly for
a woman who has leisure, to spend her hours
in idle gossip, in reading trashy novels, in fash-
ionable cold-hearted calls after the custom of
the world, instead of storing her mind with
useful knowledge, or instead of visiting the
sick, teaching the young, or assisting in the 
support of herself and others. 
	"All prejudices, all notions of propriety, tend
to genius and culture, a well disciplined mind
and a generous heart will look the Lion Opin-
ion in the eye and move on! He will quail be-
fore her and will assail her no more."
	May the day be hastened when we will not
only have our rights, but also know them, and
knowing dare maintain.
	The next speaker called upon was the
President of the Woman's Suffrage Association
of Ogden,
		      MRS. STANFORD
	She did not understand that the ladies of
this organization claimed equal political posi-
tions with men. They did not ask for the
offices which men only were fitted to fill. They
claimed the right of voting—the right to select
men who are to make the laws which both
sexes are required to obey. Women desired
that such men should be honorable, upright
and truth-loving. She contended that woman
ought to be acquainted with political as well as
other affairs, and that the world would be
better for her knowledge of and participation 
in them.
		HON. C. W. PENROSE
was called upon and he explained that the
right to vote and the right to hold office were 
not identical. For many years the women of
Utah exercised the former, but not the latter.
He was in favor of making no discrimination
against woman on the statute books. There
were offices which women might be able to fill
with ability and profit to themselves and the
public; there were others for which they were not
adapted. The good sense of the voters should
determine this. There were men who were
not fitted for office, but this did not debar them
from voting. He mentioned favorably a num-
ber of the leading ladies in the National Wo-
man Suffrage movement, and said he believed
they were inspired to work for the elevation of
their sex. There was one mistake they made,
and that was in demanding as a natural right
that which had been decided to be but a politi-
cal privilege. Citizenship and the suffrage
were distinct and separate. Every person born
in the United States is a citizen thereof. A
wife, daughter or widow of a citizen, even if of 
foreign birth, is a citizen. Citizenship comes
by birth or naturalization; the right to vote is
conferred by law. A woman has as much in-
herent right to vote as a man, but it is not in
either. The Legislature prescribes the quali-
fications of voters. If voting was an inherent
right, one would have a right to vote as soon as
born. The legal qualifications for voting
differed in different States and countries.
Another mistake made by some so called
“strong-minded” women suffragists was in be-
rating "the monster man" from whom they ex-
pected to obtain the elective franchise. This 
was not good policy, to say the least. A great 
obstacle in the way was the fact that most of 
the women were either opposed or indifferent
to woman suffrage. The work before the asso-
ciation was to spread information and convert
their own sex. When the women of the United
States unite to demand the elective franchise
they will get it. He considered a mistake was


[Column 1]
﻿made in trying for an amendment to the
national Constitution. The suffrage was a 
matter for each State to regulate and if one
State could be captured for woman suffrage
others would follow and eventually all would
come into line. Man and woman should be to-
gether in all things. The male and female ele-
ments were found in all nature and they were
so designed by the Creator in the beginning
and redeemed and exalted men and women
were to have dominion together for all the ages
of eternity. 
	HON. GEORGE Q. CANNON
having entered the building was requested to
address the meeting. He said: I take great
interest in every question that effects men and
women. I have always believed that if there
should be any qualifications for suffrage, sex
should not enter.  Women should have the
right to vote equal with men. I have seen
very many ignorant men exercise the right of
suffrage when they knew as little about the
questions involved as an animal, and at the
same time intelligent women were deprived of
that right. I think this a great injustice. Pro-
hibition is one of the issues of the times. The
question of intemperance, to whom does it ap-
peal most strongly, who is most interested?
The woman, naturally. It has a deeper inter-
est for woman than any one else. Woman's
voice in this and other affairs would doubtless
have a most excellent effect. I do not think
that because a woman was allowed to vote she
would necessarily be called into service in the
army or navy.
	There are those who argue this way, but I
think this is begging the question. I believe
that there are offices that woman could fill, and
many that she would not want to fill. For in-
stance, I do not imagine that a woman would
make a good constable to arrest men, or a
sheriff, to hang men. I believe the right of
suffrage would broaden the minds of the women
of this country. I have never seen any effects
in connection with woman suffrage to deplore.
The speaker referred to the good work per-
formed by able women for their sex and to the
voluntary defense of the people of Utah by
several notable ladies in Washington. He
closed by saying: I pray God to bless you in
this movement. Do not allow division or
jealousy to arise in your midst, but show to all
that you are laboring unitedly for the ad-
vancement of a great cause.
	MRS. NELLIE WEBBER.
	At the close of the present meeting an op-
portunity will be offered to all ladies who have 
not yet joined the Woman Suffrage Associa-
tion of Utah, to enroll their names with the
secretary.
	Fully believing that "in union is strength,"
we have associated ourselves with the National
Woman's Suffrage Association, so that we may
more effectively prosecute the aim and end we
have in view, namely, the enfranchisement of
woman and placing her upon a higher plane
politically than she occupies to-day. To be-
come an auxillary of the national association
we are required to pay a fee of 25 cents each.
For the first fifty enrolled members we are en-
titled to three delegates, and for each addi-
tional twenty-five members an additional dele-
gate. Our enrollment should be as large ss 
possible, because we not only want to show to
our eastern sisters that we are in real earnest
and have the cause of woman's political ad-
vancement at heart, but it gives us greater in-
fluence in the councils of the national associa-
tion, at the last meeting of which we had two
delegates to represent us, and I am pleased to 
say that our cause was most ably and satis-
factorily presented.
	For the benefit of the associations now being

[Column 2]
formed throughout the Territory, who may per-
chance adopt our present by-laws, I will say
that after having worked under them for three
months, we find that they need amending. This 
was under consideration at our last executive
session, and we hope soon to complete and pub-
lish an amended and more perfect set.
	In placing our initiation fee at 25 cents we
were laboring under a misunderstanding as to
the amount per member to be paid to the
national association. Now we learn that in-
stead of its being 25 per cent of our fee it is 25
cents per member, and as the matter now
stands our treasury is empty of funds. But
to increase the fee now would be an injustice to
incoming members, so it has been decided to
allow the present charge for membership to re-
main to the end of the year, when the fee will 
be increased. 
	Associations wishing to become auxiliary to
us can do so upon application and payment of
10 cents for each member enrolled by them.
	For the government of our meetings and ex-
ecutive sessions we have adopted "Roberts'
Rules of Order" and recommend them to all
new organizations. 
	Ladies' should take an interest in this matter
and aid us to place woman side by side politi-
cally with her husband and brother. Our
fathers contended that "taxation without repre-
sentation" was a wrong, and if it were so a hun-
dred years ago, it is no less so today. There
are many women in this Territory who pay
taxes and yet have no voice in the selection of 
public officers through whose hands this money 
is disbursed. I am satisfied that when women
hold the franchise universally we shall not only
have a better administration of public affairs,
but greater economy in all public expenditures;
and I therefore urge upon the ladies to come
forward at once and enroll their names in the
great cause for which we are struggling.
	A vote of thanks was given to the speakers
and the meeting was adjourned subject to the
call of the Secretary; to be published in the 
papers. Several ladies’ came forward and en-
rolled their names as members. The movement
is worthy the support of the women of Utah
and we hope to hear of branch associations all

WOMAN'S EXPONENT.


', 'WOMAN SUFFRAGE MEETING.
	The meeting of the Utah Woman Suffrage
Association at the Assembly Hall on Thursday
April 11th, was well attended, and the proceed-
ings were highly interesting.  In the absence
of the President, Mrs. Margaret T. Caine, who
was unable to attend, Mrs. Lydia D. Alder
presided, and performed the duty ably and
with becoming dignity.  A number of promi-
nent ladies occupied the stand.
	After the preliminary exercises, including
prayer by the Chaplain, Mrs. Sarah M. Kim-
ball, the minutes of the preceding meeting and
the names of the members were read by the
Secretary, Cornelia H. Clayton, and some
amendments to the by-laws and other executive
business were submitted and voted upon.  The
meeting was then addressed by
			MRS. ZINA D. H. YOUNG,
who deplored the injustice of depriving the wo-
men of Utah of the right of suffrage which they
had exercised for many years without abusing
it.  The mothers of the United States had
always been found side by side with the men in

[Column 2]
their battles for independence, justice and
right.  Who implanted within the breast of
her children the desire for a great and free
government?  The mother.  We look for the
great government of the United States to again
confer upon us the God-given right of suffrage,
because by and with it we will be enabled to do
vast good to the world.  For this we expect to
contend; we expect to reach out our hands to
the women of America, and say we are one
with you in this grand struggle.
		MRS. IDA F. GIBBS,
	I have always been an advocate of woman
suffrage, which I consider a God-given right.
Taxation and representation should always go
together.  The rights of women are eternally
paramount with those of men.  I heard a
young lady not long ago say she would attend
our meetings if they were not so dry and unin-
teresting.  She is a poor woman who will villify
her own sex.  She referred to the great and
glorious labors of the leading suffragists of
America.  At one time in the world's history
women were merely slaves, but gradually they
are gaining their way, and each year sees an
increased number espouse the cause of suffrage,
which ere long shall triumph.  They talk
about the patience of men.  Why, one day's
experience of the average man with the duties
of a wife with a growing family would drive
him into a lunatic asylum.  The more we do
for ourselves, the more the opposite sex respect
us.  I have heard it said that woman was too
good and pure to enter politics; that she would
rapidly be degraded.  I do not think so.  Pol-
itics cannot degrade woman, but woman can
elevate and purify politics.  There is every-
thing to gain and nothing to lose in the cause
in which we are engaged.  I do not believe
that it is necessary for a woman to neglect her
household because she believes in woman suf-
frage.  The question of woman suffrage does
not hinge upon the query as to whether the
majority of women want it or not.  The ques-
tion simply is, whether the right to vote is use-
ful or beneficial.  If it is so to men, it certainly
should be to women.
	The following address was then delivered by
		BISHOP O. F. WHITNEY:
	"I have consented to appear here to-day with
considerable reluctance; not because I was un-
willing to add my mite for the advancement of
a worthy cause, but because I feel painfully
my inefficiency to speak in such a cause.  It is
one to which I have given but little thought
and attention, owing mainly, no doubt, to pre-
occupation in other matters.  If I am thank-
ful for one thing, however, it is that I was born
with a heart free, to a great extent, from
bigotry.  It seems more natural for me to be-
lieve than to doubt, and, believing in woman
suffrage, I never could understand why she
should be denied that privilege, or the enjoy-
ment of any right to which she is justly en-
titled.  I never could see those great and in-
surmountable barriers which many imagine to
intervene between her and the rights which she
justly claims.  Woman is the other half of
man; he is not complete without her.  They
are brother and sister, offspring of the same
heavenly Parentage, and should go hand in
hand in every righteous effort, in every worthy
cause.  It was because I recognized justice and
consistency in her claim for the franchise, that
I never felt called upon to oppose it, while, as
I have said, preoccupation in other matters has
prevented my becoming as active a champion
of her cause as I otherwise might have been.
	Woman is typical of two things in which I
place great faith and confidence, namely; the
Earth upon which we dwell, our mortal
mother, from whom we derive physical nourish-
ment, and the Church of Christ, our spiritual
mother, who ministers to our higher necessities,

[Column 3]
Earth gives us our mortal bodies, which must
return to her bosom from whence they were
taken, and after that, by means of the resur-
rection, she gives them to us again in a new
birth, purified, immortal and eternal, and we
become the children of the Church of Christ in
everlasting glory.  As earth will be glorified
and the Church exalted, so woman will be
glorified and exalted, through obedience and
faithfulness, to the presence of the Highest. 
Woman is the gate-way by which we enter this
world, just as Christ, our Lord, is the gate-way
to the glories that lie beyond.  We cannot
afford to ignore her to whom God has given so
great a mission.  I believe that if the history
of the world—of which we have but a frag-
ment—were entirely told, we would find that
woman has typified in all her experiences the
experience of the Church of God in all ages.
The Church in this dispensation will never be
thrown down or given to another people.
Neither will the cause of woman fail, as at other
times; nevermore will she be trampled on, led
captive,or sold into slavery.  The hour of martyr-
dom for both is past; the day of their triumph
and victory has dawned.
	But there is order in all things that are
divine.  God is not the author of confusion.
"Order is heaven's first law," the poet says, but
we believe that obedience is heaven's first law,
and that order is the result of obedience to
righteous rule.  There must be a head, and in
the same sense that Christ is the head of the
Church, man is the head of the woman. So
says Paul.  Now, this being the case, the ad-
vancement of one means the advancement of
the other, and the degradation of either is the
degradation of both.  Exalt the head and
abase the body and separation ensues; decapi-
tation takes place, and disruption and death
are the result.  What a spectacle a human
body would present with its head swinging in
mid-aid, while the trunk,arms and legs remained
walking below!  No, we cannot exalt man and
degrade woman.  Christ is glorified when his
Church is exalted, and man is lifted up, or
cast down, according as woman is elevated or
depressed.
	But woman should not be idolized, or
worshiped as a deity.  It would seem that
many in modern times were actuated by a de-
sire to make amends in this respect for the
shortcomings of their ancestors.  Hence they
worship woman, or pretend to worship her, and
where but yesterday she was treated as a
slave, to-day she reigns a queen "with hearts
and passions prostrate at her feet."  One ex-
treme is as wrong as the other.  If man is
king, woman is queen; no more, no less.  They
reign together, side by side.  And when we
reach heaven, where God our Father rules the
universe, we will find Him seated on His
throne, a King, and our Mother at His side, a
Queen; not under His feet, nor above His
head.  Earthly things foreshow, or should fore-
show, things heavenly.
	I have often marveled at the inconsistency
of human nature, even in some of its highest
aspects.  We seem unwilling to extend to
others the same rights which we claim for our-
selves.  The Puritans of England fought for
freedom to worship God, and cut off the head
of a tyrant king to file a lasting protest against
oppression, and yet came here and established
a religious despotism, persecuting those who
differed from their peculiar views.  Nor did it
end there.  To-day the poets and patriots of
New England, who have sung and fought for
freedom for whites and blacks in former years,
seem oblivious to deeds of outrage and tyranny
committed right here in Utah.  They seem
unable or unwilling to look beyond their own
state boundary lines.  They will sing
eloquently of the rights of the black man; they
have girded their weapons of war and fought,
bled and died for the liberation of the negro


[Column 1]﻿
slave, but when the rights of the Mormons are
assailed, or the red men are the victims of
wrong, their harps are mute; their muses find
no utterance, their poetic and patriotic souls
fail to respond. Listen while I quote to you
the burning words of one of their living
masters of the lyre.

		"Men! whose boast it is that ye
		Come of fathers brave and free,
		If there breathe on earth a slave,
		Are ye truly free and brave?
		If ye do not feel the chain,
		When it works a brother's pain,
		Are ye not base slaves indeed,
		Slaves unworthy to be freed?

		Women! who shall one day bear
		Sons to breathe New England air,
		If ye hear, without a blush,
		Deeds to make the roused blood rush
		Like red lava through your veins,
		For your sisters now in chains,—
		Answer! are ye fit to be
		Mothers of the brave and free?

		Is true Freedom but to break
		Fetters for our own dear sake,
		And, with leathern hearts, forget
		That we owe mankind a debt?
		No! true freedom is to share
		All the chains our brothers wear,
		And, with heart and hand, to be
		Earnest to make others free!

		They are slaves who fear to speak
		For the fallen and the weak;
		They are slaves who will not choose
		Hatred, scoffing, and abuse,
		Rather than in silence shrink
		From the truth they needs must think;
		They are slaves who dare not be
		In the right with two or three."

	This for the cause of the black man, the
negro slave of the south. But I have heard
no poet sing thus eloquently for the down-
trodden people of Utah, nor for the rights of
the red man, oppressed and trampled on. Why
is it? Is it because they foresaw that the freed
negro would have a vote, and that the Indian
would not? Great heaven! has it come to this,
that poets and patriots can only string their
lyres and unsheathe their swords for votes?—
to secure a party election? I marvel at their
silence in these times. Again, why is it that
notable women, champions of liberty and
standard-bearers of thought, can come into our
midst and survey, unmoved and uncompassion-
ate, the woes and sorrows of their Mormon
sisters, left husbandless and unprotected by the
exile or imprisonment of their companions, or
thrown into dungeons themselves, for the sake
of conscience, and the freedom to worship God
in a land of religious liberty? How can a
Julia Ward Howe and an Emily Faithful,
women whose names are written high on the
scroll of fame, justify their souls in coming here
to see things for themselves, but, instead of
piercing beneath the social crust and telling
what they might have seen and heard, giving
a hasty glance at the surface of things, believ-
ing all the tales told them by the people's
enemies, and going away to repeat to the world
the stale slanders of local falsifiers?
	But I know to some extent what ought to be,
if it does not agree entirely with that which is.
I know that all men are the children of God;
the sons and daughters of one common Paren-
tage; and that we cannot prove our love for
Him unless we love each other and labor
zealously and devotedly for the general wel-
fare. May God speed the day when justice will
be done to all, of every creed and clime, of
every race, sex and condition; when bigotry
and prejudice will melt away like mist before
the morning sun, and the light of freedom,
peace and righteousness universally prevail.

[Column 2]
"For mankind are one in spirit, and an impulse bears
	along
Round the earth's electric circle the swift flash of right or
	wrong;
Whether conscious or unconscious, yet humanity’s vast
	frame,
Through its ocean-sundered fibres, feels the gush of joy or
	shame;
In the gain or loss of one race all the rest have equal
	claim."

	An excellent speech was then made by the
Delegate to the National Suffrage Convention
at Washington, D. C.,
		MRS. EMILY S. RICHARES.
	Many people suppose that the movement for
Woman's Suffrage was inaugarated by certain
strong minded women, because they desired to
usurp the prerogatives of men in govermental
affairs, and that the mere desire of that class
of women to vote is all that is now embraced
in the subject. Such an idea is not only in-
correct but unjust. The question of Woman's
Suffrage is the natural outgrowth of the agita-
tion of Woman's rights, which was commenced
in this country more than fifty years ago, and
which had been going on nearly a score of
years, when Elizabeth Cady Stanton startled her
associates in the Seneca Falls Convention of
1848, with her memorable resolution in favor
of the elective franchise for women. And
while the issue now seems to be upon the ques-
tion of suffrage, there is still lying behind it
that grand idea the elevation of women and the
equality of the sexes.
	In order to clearly understand the suffrage
movement as it now presents itself to us, we
must bear in mind the causes which led to its
inception, and remember the conditions of wo-
men which it sought to change. We will then
see that its adherents desire it as a means, by
which to obtain the civil and political rights
and privileges that belong to women, as well as
men, rather than as a means of gratifying any
personal desire or ambition for prominence in
public life; although the latter has been
charged by many as the real motive for agitat-
ing the question. The facts do not sustain this
charge.
	The following resolution, declaring for Wo-
men's rights, which was unanimously adopted
by the Convention of 1848, gives a fair idea of
the position of its adherents:
	"Resolved, that being invested by the
Creator with the same capabilities and the same
consciousness of responsibility for their exer-
cise, it is demonstrably the right and duty of
Woman, equally with Man, to promote every
righteous cause by every righteous means, and
especially in regard to the great subjects of
morals and religion, it is self-evidently her
right to participate with her brother in teach-
ing them both in private and in public, by
writing, speaking, and by any instrumental-
ities proper to be used, and in any assemblies
proper to be held; and this being a self evident
truth, growing out of the divinely implanted
principles of human nature, any custom or
authority adverse to it, whether modern or
wearing the hoary sanction of antiquity, is to
be regarded as a self evident falsehood and at
war with mankind."
	No fair minded person could object, either
then or now, to the sentiments expressed in
that resolution. but when Mrs. Stanton won for
herself the honor of having initiated the move-
ment for Woman's Suffrage, by presenting to
the same convention her resolution on franchise,
it caused much discussion and was only carried
by a small minority, although only its senti-
ment, and not its terms, was open to criticism.
It read as follows:
	"Resolved that it is the duty of the women
of this country to secure to themselves their
sacred right to the elective franchise."
	The anti-Slavery movements that took place

[Column 3]
during the early part of the present century,
seem to have had a potential influence in start-
ing agitation of the question of Woman's rights,
and it is claimed that the arbitrary exclusion
of Lucretia Mott and her American colleagues
from the "World's Anti-Slavery Convention,"
held at London in 1840, was the immediate
cause for calling a "Woman's rights Conven-
tion" in America, and the inaugaration of the
great work that has since been prosecuted here
for the emancipation of women.
	Lucretia Mott had already spoken in her na-
tive land against the enthraldom of her sex.
She frankly admitted "all the differences that
the Creator had made in the relation of man
and woman" and disclaimed any desire to dis-
turb this relation, but she insisted that the civil,
social and religious disabilities of woman were
such as "to impede her progress, enervate her
mind and paralyse her powers;" thus preventing
her from becoming a "true woman" and a
"moral responsible being." Angelina Grimke,
Abby Kelly and others had enunciated similar
views in different parts of our nation.
	To fully appreciate and justify the striking
words of these noble women, we must pause for
a moment and briefly consider a few of the
disabilities to which our sex were then subject.
An eminent legal author, writing on this sub-
ject says, that the theory of the common law,
which then prevailed with regard to women,
was "a slavish one and a disgrace to any civil-
ized nation;" that "with regard to political
rights, women formed a positive exception to
the general doctrine of equality," and that
language describing their condition, if "applied
to males, would be the exact definition of
political slavery;" that the legal theory made
"the husband and wife one person, and that
person the husband," while there was "scarcely
a legal act of any description" that woman was
competent to perform.
	This is indeed strong language but the facts
justified its use. The Women of America who
to-day enjoy the legal right, without let or
hindrance of any one, to aquire, hold, and
dispose of property, real and personal, without
limit or restriction as to kind or value, can
hardly realize the abject condition of their
sisters, who were disqualified by law from even
holding the legal titles to the homes in which
they dwelt, and whose entire estates, including
the very clothes they wore and the food they
ate, belonged absolutely to their husbands, al-
though it might have been earned, by the
weary toil of their own hands and heads.
	That women are fully capable of filling the
positions of editors, reporters, lawyers, doctors,
merchants, clerks, cashiers, telegraph and tele-
phone operators, typewriters, artists, musicians,
printers, photographers, and scores of other like
professions and callings, has been fully demon-
strated by experience. Many instances are of
record where they have shown equal skill with
men, and greater endurance in the trying
duties of literary and journalistic pursuits, as
well as in professional life. This was notably
the case with women reporters during the last
national political campaign. Many of them
did more work than men, and that too without
stimulants of any kind, while the men resorted
to strong drinks and other stimulants to enable
them to endure the strain of incessant work.
	When we think of the changes that have
been wrought in the few short years since
women were first admitted into the positions I
have named, and others which might be men-
tioned, we are prone to lose sight of the power-
ful agencies that have been at work, during all
these years, breaking down the inexorable laws
and customs of ages, which held woman in their
cruel grasp and prevented her from becoming,
in all the walks of life, the intellectual, moral
and social companion of man.
	How often do we stop to think that this is
the noble work in which the advocates of Wo-


[Column 1]
man's rights and Woman's suffrage have been
so ardently engaged, and who of us can tell how
much of the progress that has been made in
that direction is due to the patient labors of
such women as Lucretia Mott, Elizabeth Cady
Stanton, Susan B. Anthony, Belva A. Lock-
wood, Lucy Stone, Laura O. Chant, Isabella
Beecher Hooker, Frances E. Willard, Mary A;
Livermore, Julia Ward Howe, Matilda Joslyn
Gage, Elizabeth Lisle Saxon, and scores of 
others who have devoted their lives to the
amelioration of the condition of women? Years
ago they received as encouragement from the
world, derision and scorn, but now the wreath
of laurel and the victor's crown are beginning
to fall upon the furrowed brows of these hon-
ored champions of their sex.
	Still the work of redemption has only just
begun. While the professions and higher
walks of life are open to women of ability, who
can avail themselves of such advantages, their
less favored sisters, the seamstress, the factory
girl, and others similarly situated, are still
struggling to free themselves from the terrors
of that inhuman custom, which prevents them  
from getting the same remuneration for their
labor as that realized by men for the same
work; and thousands of women are now wear-
ing away their lives, (in this great land which
has been consecrated to liberty,) by unremitt-
ing toil, for which many of them receive less
than a quarter of a dollar for a day's work of
nearly eighteen hours; and out of this mis-
erable pittance some of them have to support
their children as well as themselves. Is it not
worthy the best efforts of the best women to
remedy this great evil, and, by enabling
women to get just compensation for the work
they do, abolish much of the suffering that now
exists and obviate many of the necessities that
lead to sin and crime? Such a work must be
worthy of Divine favor, and it lies at the very
foundation of the Woman's Suffrage movement.
	Then the evils of intemperance must be over-
come. The amount of sorrow, suffering
and sin that this demon has entailed upon the 
human race is beyond computation or accurate
estimation. Happy homes have been invaded
and domestic felicity ruthlessly destroyed.
Where peace and plenty were wont to abound,
poverty and strife now take up their abode.
Dutiful wives have become miserable outcasts,
and innocent children have been driven into
dens of vice. No tongue can tell, nor pen de-
pict the misery that drink has wrought. It is
the peculiar work of woman that is destined to 
vanquish the insatiable monster and free her
sex from its most deadly enemy. The great
temperance work that has already been done,
in different parts of the Union, under the direc-
tion of such noble women as Frances Willard, 
Susan B. Anthony and their associates, com-
mands the admiration of the world and fully
justifies the prediction of ultimate triumph.
	The National Woman's Suffrage Association
is the most prominent, as well as the most com-
prehensive organization of women in the land.
While its ostensible object, as indicated by its
name, is to secure the elective franchise for
women, its ultimate purpose is to effect the dis-
enthrallment of woman in all the walks of life,
and every branch of woman's work has the 
hearty sympathy and support of this Associa-
tion. For many years past it has held its
 annual conventions, composed of Delegates
from the different States and Territories, at the
National Capital, and, of late years, United
States Senators, and Congressmen, and others
of National repute, have taken part in their
meetings. Last year an "International Council
of Women" was held at Washington, D. C.,
under the auspices of this Association, at which
were represented nearly every State and Terri-
in the Union, as well as the leading countries
of Europe and other parts of the civilized
world. The Council lasted a week; its pro-

[Column 2]
ceedings are published in a large volume, which
gives a comprehensive and almost marvelous
account of woman's work. Although some-
what familiar with the subject I was not only
delighted but surprised at the varied and 
mammoth proportions of the cause. 
By means of auxiliary associations through-
out the country, such as the one recently organ-
ized in this Territory, the National is able to
carry on systematic work in all the different
departments. When the temperance cause
needs strength, in Kansas or elsewhere, able 
and efficient women are sent from different
parts of the land to wage the warfare there, and
when the labor move needs help in the East,
valiant women are assigned to that department;
and so the good work goes on with quiet but
irresistible momentum.
	Since the National has been holding its con-
ventions at Washington, much work has been
done before Committees of Congress, as well as
with individual members. Several important
hearings have been had, at which the subject
of suffrage has been discussed, and lively de-
bates have taken place in the Senate and 
House of Representatives on this question. At
first it was difficult to find champions for the
cause in either branch of the National Legisla-
ture, but since Senators Blair and Palmer
made their powerful appeals for Woman's Suf-
frage, the number of adherents to the cause has
materially increased and, were it not for the
 stern opposition of Southern Senators—which
is founded on the fact that the enfranchisement
of women means, to the south, an increase of
the colored vote, and consequent negro rule—
the relief sought for could have been obtained
before now.
	While the general work has been going on,
Utah has not been neglected by the National
Association. On several occasions leading suf-
frage women have appeared before Committees
of Congress and protested in the strongest
terms against the taking away of our franchise.
Mrs. Lockwood and Miss Anthony are deserv-
ing of particular mention in this connection,
and their efforts were ably seconded by other
eloquent, warm-hearted women. Having been
present on some of these occasions, I can speak
feelingly of the work that has been done, and 
reverently of the noble women who did it. My
soul has often breathed the prayer: "God
bless and reward them for all they have done
and said for us."
	The woman suffragists are represented by
many talented writers and eloquent speakers.
Their newspapers and periodicals are ably con-
ducted and wield a power for good in the 
land. On the lecture platform and in the
pulpit are heard words of burning eloquence
and poetic pathos from women's lips; and had
I the time, it would afford me pleasure to pay
my humble tribute to some of these distinguised
women, but I must forebear. My purpose in
this brief address has been to show that there
is something really meritorious in the Woman's
Suffrage movement, and that by promoting it,
we do not necessarily depart from woman's
true sphere in life, nor make her usurp man's
prerogatives. Men and women can and should
work together, for the amelioration of the race,
and should share with each other the
responsibilities, as well as the pleasures of 
life.
	Let us use our strength and influence for the
advancement of this noble work, for "woman's
rights are human rights." Let us remember
that however great our own reluctance to
occupy political positions, there are those of
our sex whose lives and honor depend upon
their possession of political power. Let us
not put obstacles in their way, by declaring
that we have all the rights we want, but rather
let us individually petition the powers that be
to grant them deliverance from their oppres-
sions. In doing this we will not try to create

[Column 3]
for man a rival, a superior, or a head, but we
will try to make woman what our beneficient
Creator designed that she should be, the com-
panion, the sister, and the helpmeet of man.  
			(To be continued.)


WOMAN’S EXPONENT.


WOMAN'S EXPONENT.


Celebration of the one hundredth anniver-
sary of the inauguration of Prest. Geo. Wash-
ington in the Tabernacle, this city, April 30,
Lieut. R. W. Young, orator of the day.


Training School for Nurses

	On the fifteenth of May, the Second Term of Dr.
Hughes’ Training School for Nurses begins, a class
of twelve (12) students completing their first term of
three months on the fourth of May. This is the
first Training School for Nurses established in
Utah, and the instruction given embraces the best
methods of teaching of the Eastern Schools, whose
systems have been thoroughly investigated by the
teacher. The purpose of this school is to give a
wider knowledge of waiting upon the sick, and all
the branches pertaining to nursing will be thorough-
ly discussed in the successive terms. The second
term will be held in Dr. Hughes’ new residence, on
First West Street. Students desiring to enter, should
make application as early as possible, so that ar-
rangements can be made for securing text books.
	TUITION,	-	-	-	$25.00.
	Special arrangements can be made for Obstetrical
teaching. Address:
				M. P. Hughes,
P. O. Box 575.				Salt Lake City, Utah.
