Name: 16_said_man_asked_lady
Representative_Docs:
['LITTLE FOLKS.

	A LITTLE girl asked a minister, "Do you
think my father will go to heaven?" Why,
yes my child, "Why do you ask!" "Well,
because if he don't have his own way,
there, he won't stay long, I was thinking"
	SUNDAY school teacher to pupil—"Now,
my little man, can you explain to us the
cause of Adam's fall?" Little man (em-
phatically)—"Yes, sir; cause he hadn't any
ashes to throw on the sidewalk."—Brooklin
Argus.
	ON Monday morning we met a little
French boy lustily blowing a fish horn in
honor of the holiday. An Irish playmate of
the boy saluted him with, "What are you
blowing that horn for? George Washing-
ton did not die to save France."—Newbury-
port Herald.
	"Now, boys, suppose twelve men divide
forty-eight bushels of corn, equally, how
many is that for each?" Boldest boy:
"Please sir, we've not gone that far." "Your
teacher told me you had learned all the first
four rules. Boy: Yes, sir; but we have al-
ways done our sums in potatoes or turnips;
we never had any corn."
	"GRANDMA, do you know why I can see
up in the sky so far?" asked Charlie, a lit-
tle four-year-old, of the venerable lady who
sat on the garden seat knitting.  "No, my
dear, why is it?" said grandma, bending
her ear eager to catch and remember the
wise saying of the precious little pet "Be-
cause there is nothing in the way," replied
the young philosopher, resuming his astro-
nomical research, and grandma her knit-
ting.
	A WISCONSIN school boy handed in the
following composition recently: "I go to
school to learn to read and rite and syphor
and so forth to slide on the ice and traid
off an old nife if I have one, in summer to
pick wildflowers and strawberries and to get
out of work hot days, some boys has to go
to school to get out of their mother's road,
but I would rather stay in winter than to
go miles and set by a cold stove and freeze
my toes.  I like to go to school to see the
teacher scold the big girls when they cut
up.  Some goes to school to fool but I go to
study, when we are old we can't go to school
and then we will feel sorry that we fooled
when we were young and went to school. I
don't get no time to fool anyway for I have
enough to do when it comes to my geogra-
phy.
	DON'T GIVE UP BUT TRY.—A man trav-
eling in the northern part of Ireland, heard
the voices of children and stopped to lis-
en.
Finding the sound came from a small
building used as a school-house, he drew
near; as the door was open he went in, and
listened to the words the boys were spell-
ing.
	One little fellow stood apart, looking very
sad.
	'Why does that boy stand there?' asked
the gentleman.
	'Oh, he is good for nothing!' replied the
teacher. 'There's nothing in him.  I can
make nothing of him.  He is the most
stupid boy in school."
	The gentleman was surprised at this an-
swer. He saw that the teacher was so stern
and rough, that the younger and more timid
were nearly crushed. After a few words to
them, placing his hands on the noble brow
of the little fellow who stood apart he said,
—One of these days you may be a fine
scholar; don't give up; try, my boy, try.'

[Column 2]
	The boy's soul was aroused. His sleep-
ing mind awoke. A new purpose was
formed. From that hour he became anx-
ious to excel. And he did become a fine
scholar, and the author of a well known
commentary on the Bible; a great and good
man, beloved and honored.  It was Dr.
Adam Clarke,
	The secret of his success is worth know-
ing: 'Don't give up; but try, my boy.'


', 'DIVERSIONS.
	THE man question.—What is he worth?
	WHY is a new-born baby like a gale of
wind? Because it begins with a squall.
	WHAT is that which goes from New York
to Albany every day and yet never stirs?—
The turnpike.
	WHAT is the difference between a jewel-
ler and a jailer? One sells watches and the
other watches cells.
	A LITTLE boy asked a lady who made her
teeth."My Creator," she replied. "Well,"
said the youngster, "Dr. —— made my ma's,
and they beat your'n clean out o' sight."
	A LADY having brought a suit to recover
a chignon from a hair-dresser with whom
she had left it for repair, her little boy
wanted to know "if that chignon was what
is called 'hair-at-law?'"
	MARYLAND girls wont marry in the full of
the moon, believing they would have ill-
luck through life, but a Massachusetts girl
wouldn't let forty full moons stop her ten
seconds.
	A YOUNG dandy, who sported an enor-
mous mustache, asked a lady what she
thought of his looks? "Why," said she
"you look as if you had swallowed a pony,
and left the tail sticking out of your
mouth."
	AT a collection recently made at a charity
fair, a lady passed the plate to a very wealthy
man who was well known for his stinginess.
"I have nothing," was his curt reply. "Then
take something, sir," she answered, "You
know I am begging for the poor."
	"Do you enjoy going to church now?,,
asked a lady of Mrs. Partington. "La, me,
I do," replied Mrs. P. Nothing does me so
much good as to get up early on Sunday
morning and go to church, and hear a popu-
lous minister dispense with the gospel."
	"I DON'T like these shoes," said a lady
customer, "because the soles are too thick."
"Is that the only objection?" blandly asked
the salesman. "Yes," was the reply.
"Then, madam, if you take the shoes, I can
assure you that the objection will gradually
wear away."
	THE first Marquis of Huntley, on his first
arrival at court, forgot the usual tribute of
obeisance, and was asked why he did not
bow? He begged his Majesty's pardon, and
excused his want of respect, by saying, he
was just come from a place where everybody
bowed to him.
	"A MEMBER of our Legislature," says an
Ohio paper, "who was on a dead-head excur-
sion out on the plains, lost his pass, and had
to walk seventy miles before he could find a
man who had sufficient confidence in his ap-
pearance to lend him money to telegraph
home to his wife to sell the cooking-stove
and remit him the proceeds at once."
	A SUBURBAN minister applied to a ticket
agent on one of the railroads for a "clergy-
man's ticket," and on the official expressing
a doubt  to his clerical character, exclaim-
ed, "If you don't believe I'm a clergyman,
I'll read you one of my sermons!" The
agent gave him a ticket without insisting
upon the proof.
	SMART reply.—A humorous fellow being
subpoenaed as a witness on a trial for an as-
sault, one of the counsel, who had been no-
torious for brow-beating witnesses, asked
him what distance he was from the parties
when the assault happened; he answered,
"Just four feet five inches and a half."—
[Column 2]
"How came you to be so very exact, fellow?"
said the counsel. "Because I expected some
fool or other would ask me," said he, "so I
measured it."
	A WASHINGTON paper tells this good
story about Hon. S. S. Cox, the irrepressible
Congressman from New York City. The
other day Cox was unusually active, spurty
and irrepressible in the House. He was
continually at the front, "catching on the
fly," and rampaging round generally. Fi-
nally, when the House came to an agreement
to proceed with the debate on the salary bill
in twenty minute speeches, Cox despatched
a page with the following note:
	"Dear Mr. Speaker.—Put me down for
twenty minutes.				S. S. C.
	To which the Speaker replied;
	"Dear Cox.—I would be delighted if I
could keep you down half that time.
J. G, B,"


', 'LITTLE FOLKS.

	"WON'T you cut open a penny for me,
father?" said a little girl when she came
home from school one day. "Cut open a
penny! What do you want me to do that
for?" asked her father. "Cause," said the
little girl, "our teacher says that in every
penny there are four farthings, and I want
to see them."
	UPON being promised a penny by his
mother if he would take a dose of caster-
oil, a bad little boy obtained the money, and
then told his parent that she might "casther oil" into the street. He will make a
humorous newspaper paragraphist one of
these days.
	"COME my little fellow," said a Washing-
ton gentleman to a youngster of 5 years,
while sitting in a parlor where a large com-
pany were assembled, "do you know me?"
"Yeth, thir?" "Who am I? Let me here?"
"You ith the man who kithed mamma
when papa was in New York." Correct.
	SAID a distinguished politician to his son.
"Look on me! I began as an Alderman, and
here I am at the top of the tree; and what
is my reward? Why, when I die, my son
will be the greatest rascal in the city." To
this the young hopeful replied:—"Yes, dad
when you die—but not till then."
	A LITTLE fellow who was at a neighbor's
house about noon the other day watched
the preparations for dinner with a great deal
of interest, but when asked to stay and eat
something, he promptly refused. "Why,
yes, Johnny, you'd better stay," said the
lady, "why can't you?" "Well, cause,"
said the little fellow, "ma said I mustn't un-
less you asked me three times." They in-
vited him twice more right off.
	NOT CATCHING.—A lady schoolteacher in
a town in California, having an inordinate
dread of the small-pox, sent home a little
girl because she said her mother was sick
and had marks on her face. The next day
the girl appeared at the school-house with
her finger in her mouth, and her little bon-
net swinging by the strings, and said to
the teacher: "Miss——, we've got a leetle
baby at our house, and mother told me to
tell you 'it isn't catchin'.'" The teacher
said she was glad, and told her pupil to
take her seat.
	PAPA will soon be here, said mamma, to her
three year old boy, "what can Georgy do to
welcome him?" and the mother glanced at
the child's playthings, which lay scattered
in wild confusion on the carpet. "Make the
room neat," replied the bright little one,
understanding the look, and at once begin-
ning to gather his toys into a basket.
"What more can we do to welcome papa?"
asked mamma, when nothing was wanting
to the neatness of the room. "Be happy to
him when he comes!" cried the dear little
fellow, jumping up and down with eager-
ness, as he watched at the window for his
father's coming. Now—as all the diction-
ary makers will testify—it is very hard to
give good definitions; but did not little
Georgy give the very substance of a wel-
come?—"Be happy to him when he comes."


A FRENCHMAN stepped into a store the
other day; and asked to see some shoes.
The affable salesman succeeded in finding a
pair to suit his customer's fancy, and the
Frenchman asked the important question,
viz, the price he was expected to pay.
	"I can let you have these for six dollars;
they are the last of the lot."


"Seex dollar!" exclaimed the Frenchman,
"You meestake me, sare. I do not veesh to
purchase ze whole estableeshmong. Vat is
ze price of zis pair?"
	"Couldn't sell them for less than six dol-
lars; and for such a well-made pair of shoes,
I can assure you they are cheap."
	"Sheep! you call zem sheep? Vy, zat is
vare strange. Zey vessel I came to Ameri-
ca in zey call zat a sheep; ze animal wiz ze
wool on his back zey call sheep, and now
you call ze shoes sheep. Oh, I shall nevare,
nevare comprehend ze Engleesh language."
Yankee Dick.


[Column 1]
']

Name: 17_sister_said_spoke_felt
Representative_Docs:
['LADIES’ SEMI-MONTHLY MEETING.

	THE semi-monthly meeting held in the
Fourteenth Ward Assembly Hall, May 6,
1894. Counselor E. Stevenson conducted
the meeting. Meeting opened by singing,
“O, my Father.” Prayer by Counselor E.
Webb. Continued by singing, ‘‘Welcome,
best of all good meetings.” Minutes of
last meeting read and approved. Minutes of
Primary Association of Fifteenth Ward read.
	President M. Isabella Horne said she was
pleased with the minutes read by the little
girl. Thought mothers should be careful
and prompt in sending the children to the
Primary. We should be careful of pride,
and dress the children plainly. A proprietor
of a store said he sold more gingham to
clothe the children of the rich, by far, than
to clothe the children of the poorer classes.
Children should be taught to respect their
parents. Young mothers be wise and
humble. We are all God’s children, and
should be wise and humble, striving to
raise our children to fear God. Told of the
sad fate of a Prophet, because he did not
restrain his children. President Woodruff
has said that when the Temple was dedica-
ted the angels had been told to reap down
the earth, and the spirit of destruction was
everywhere except in Zion. Spoke of the
many virtues and good deeds of Sister
McMaster, President of the Relief Society
of the Eleventh Ward, who had passed
away since our last meeting; also Sister
Player, one of the Counselors to the Presi-
dent of the Relief Society of the Nineteenth
Ward; they were noble women, valiant for
the truth, and had passed to the great be-
yond, to mingle with loved ones, and enjoy
the reward of the righteous.
	A sister in the congregation testified that
this was the Church of God. Sister Julia
Pack bore a strong testimony to the truth
of the Gospel, spoke of the Prophet Joseph,
how his face shone with glory when he told
of the truths of the Gospel, and also spoke
of seeing the murdered Prophet and Patri-
arch side by side in death.
	Sisters Rawlings, Forsythe, Morris,
Phelps, Clark, Brown and Nunn, each
spoke upon healing and concerning faith,
promises fulfilled, and the testimony of the
Gospel and its gifts and powers.
	Sisters M. W. Wilcox, Mary Ann Hardy
and Williams, each bore strong testimonies
and were pleased with the sentiment of the
meeting. Sister J. C. Howe said the sub-
jects treated upon had been timely. “I am
thankful that my kind parents taught me
to revere the Sabbath day. God has an-
swered my prayers in a remarkable manner."
	Sister Jane James bore a faithful testi-
mony, then spoke in tongues.—Interpreta-
tion given by Sister Phelps.
	Counselor Stevenson—Is this not the
school of the prophets to us? Spoke at
some length on the duties of mothers, mak-
ing many good remarks. President Horne
made a few closing remarks. Meeting ad-
journed for two weeks. Singing, “This
God is the God we adore.” Benediction.
Sister L. D. Alder.

	Ladies’ semi-monthly meeting held in the
Fourteenth Ward Assembly Hall, May 19,
1894. Sister M. Isabella Horne presiding.

[Column 2]
Meeting opened by singing, “When the
voice of friendship’s heard.” Prayer by
Sister Margaret C. Brown. Continued by
singing, “Come, come ye Saints.” Minutes
of last meeting read and approved.
	President Horne hoped the sisters would
bear testimony that we might have a time
of refreshing from the Lord. God knows
when we bear testimony. “Angels bear re-
cords to the worlds on high. By our re-
cords we shall be judged. Let us keep His
commandments, that He may not say to us,
“Depart, you have not done the things I
commanded you.” Of all women we are
the most blessed, if we have the good spirit
in our heart.”
	Minutes of Primary of the Fifteenth
Ward were read by the secretary, which
were very instructive.
	By request President Horne granted per-
mission for the patrichal blessing, given by
the Patriarch Hyrum Smith to Sister Jane
James, to be read in the meeting. The
blessing was read by Sister Alder.
	Sister Elmina Taylor said, it is comfort-
ing to know that who doeth well shall be
blessed. We have the gifts of the Gospel
among us. We frequently have the gift of
tongues, also the gift of healing (I have
been healed many times myself,) and all the
other gifts are in the church as promised.
Should we not live better lives? Should
we not be careful of our association, and be
calm here in Zion while the dreadful calam-
ities pass over the earth? God said that in
the last days He would pour out judgments
on the wicked. May we so live that to us
He will say, “Well and faithfully done.”
	Sisters Corbet, Diana Reed, Peterson,
Hayon, Holland, Larsen and Forsythe
bore excellent testimonies. Sisters sang,
“Arise, my soul arise.”
	Sister Rawlings, Kiddle, Frances Smith
and Workman each bore testimony concern-
ing the healing power, fulfillment of prophecy
and of promises made by the servants
of God.
	Counselor E. Webb spoke at some length
on Temple work, urged the sisters to at-
tend to this important duty, also of the
dreadful calamities befalling the children
of men, disasters by sea and by land, at
home and abroad; felt that we were living
in the time spoken of by the prophets,
knew the gift of healing was in the Church
at the present time as well as in the past,
and that she was a living monument of
God’s mercy. Urged the sisters to redeem
their dead.
	Other sisters in the congregation bore
testimony, and desired to be true and faith-
ful.
	Sisters Morris and Ford bore testimony
that Joseph was a prophet of God, was
thankful for this knowledge.
	Sister Lydia D. Alder also bore testimony.
	President Horne felt to thank God for
the many excellent testimonies borne to-
day, and felt to bless the sisters. The gift
of tongues was poured out. The inter-
pretation was given by Sister Phelps, and
was a rich blessing to the sisters. Sister
Morris testified of the good spirit in the
house. President Horne spoke of laboring
in the Temple, and of a vision that a sister
had just been given there. She saw her
daughter (who had but lately passed away)
with the heavenly choir, and when the
Temple choir sang the Hosannah, the
heavenly choir all sang Hosannah, and her
daughter was among them. Made other
closing remarks. Meeting was adjourned
for two weeks. President Horne spoke of

[Column 3]
the secretary’s absence, that she was often
prevented by circumstances, it was sugges-
ted that the sisters pray that Sister Young
might be enabled to attend. Sisters sang,
“There’s a feast of fat things.” Benedic-
tion by Sister Julia Pack.
LYDIA D. ALDER, Ast. Sec.


', 'LADIES’ SEMI-MONTHLY MEETING.

	MINUTES of Ladies’ Semi-Monthly
Meeting held in the 14th Ward Assembly

[Column 2]
Hall, April 17, 1897, President M. I.
Horne presiding. Singing “Lord we
come before Thee now;” prayer by Sister
M. E. Kimball; singing “O say what is
truth.” The minutes of the previous meet-
ing were read and approved. President
Horne felt very happy in meeting with the
sisters today, more so if possible, for the
reason she had not been here for some
time. Felt these meetings were more
educational than any others the sisters
could attend; thought that President
Young was inspired when he organized
these Associations. Said that those who
feared God met often and spoke one to
another, and a book of remembrance was
kept and these testimonies were all entered.
Referred to some remarks of President
Young wherein he said that if the Saints
would be humble and more united, it
would not be long before the rest of the
Book of Mormon would be given us.
Spoke of the goodness of God to us; those
who gave to the poor loaned to the Lord.
President Horne felt this Hall had been
kept from desecration and from evil spirits;
for we could all testify to the good times
we have had in this building. Prayed we
might always be faithful, and that the good
spirit might continue to be with us. Sis-
ter Mary Silver felt glad she was a Latter-
day Saint and bore her testimony. Sister
Morris bore her testimony and asked for
the faith and prayers of the sisters.
	A sister from Brigham City felt pleased
to be here in this meeting today, had look-
ed forward to it, for she felt she would re-
ceive a blessing; was very much interested
in the work and said when she received
her EXPONENT the first thing she read
was the minutes of these meetings; bore a
strong testimony.
	Sister Wright made some remarks Felt
very thankful for her many blessings and
more especially that she could go into the
Temple and do work for her kindred. Re-
ferred to the time when she first joined the
Church, and said her desire was to do
good. Singing “O give me back my pro-
phet dear.”
	Counselor E. Webb felt thankful to hear
the sisters appreciated the Gospel in the
way they did. Said we could go to our
Heavenly Father with any and everything
and ask Him to help and comfort us, and
were sure of an answer. He was always
near us to help when He was sought
earnestly. Spoke of Temple work and
urged the sisters who lived near to came to
the Temple and do for their dead all that
was possible. Prayed the spirit of testi-
mony might be poured out upon the sisters.
	Sister Louie Stevenson made some re-
marks, and spoke of her son who is on a
mission, and how he was received by the
outsiders. Sister Margaret O. Brown re-
ferred to the last April conference, felt it
was the best conference we had ever had,
the brethren had all been inspired who
spoke to us. God always answered her
prayers; whenever she wanted to know any-
thing she asked Him and He always en-
lightened her.
	A sister in the congregation spoke of her
Temple work and what a grand work she
considered it was. Exhorted the sisters to
be kind to one another, and not to judge
but to help and bless one another. Prayed
God to bless us all.
	Sister Catherine Forsythe bore her testi-
mony and spoke on prayer. Sister Mar-
garet Thompson bore her testimony, and

[Column 3]
said she felt it was good to be here. Sing- 
ing “The trials of the present day.”
	Sister Jane James felt she could not keep 
away from these meetings if she tried.
Spoke of her children and the anxiety she
felt for them; felt to bless Sister Horne for
the teachings she had given to her. Sister
Betsy Brower bore her testimony.
	Sister Elizabeth Brown felt she must 
bear her testimony she felt so overjoyed
for the Gospel, also that we could ask the
Lord for blessings and be sure that they
would be given us; made some beautiful
remarks.
	Sister Davis bore her testimony, said she
felt very grateful for the Gospel, and spoke
of when she first joined the Mormons; felt 
very anxious over her friends who were in 
the East and who do not believe as she 
does
	Sister Sarah Phelps said her constant
prayer and desire was that the honest in
heart might be gathered out from the judg-
ments that were coming upon them. Urged
the sisters to be faithful and diligent, and
do all they could. Prayed God to bless us.
	Sister Julia C. Howe wished to testify to
the goodness of God, and said she had been
greatly blessed and comforted by being
here today. Spoke of her Temple work
and how she had been blessed in it; hoped
the sisters would be more diligent and
faithful in serving the Lord. Said we
must encourage one another, that our ex-
ample meant much. Spoke of a party she
attended when President Young was alive,
also referred to his remarks. Prayed God
to bless Sister Horne for her words of com-
fort and wisdom.
	Sister Harriet Badger bore her testi-
mony and made some beautiful remarks.
Sister Margaret Taylor said she was invited
to attend the matinee this afternoon, and
was in a quandary whether she would go
there or attend this meeting, she felt she
had chosen wisely by coming here for she
had been greatly blest. Prayed God to
bless us all.
	Counselor E. Stevenson urged the sisters
to come to the Temple at least one day a
week; felt our time was short. Referred
to the remarks of President Woodruff at
Brigham City when he said the Angel of
Destruction had gone forth; she said we
could testify to this when we pick up the
daily papers. Prayed we might be more
diligent and faithful.
	President Horne made a few closing re-
marks, and meeting adjourned for two
weeks. Singing “Weep, weep not for
me Zion.” Benediction by Sister Annie
T. Hyde.
			ZINA H. BULL, Sec.


', 'LADIES’ SEMI-MONTHLY MEETING.

	HELD in the 14th ward Assembly Hall,
Jan. 8 ’97, Counselor Elizabeth Webb pre-
siding until President Horne arrived.
Singing “Once more we come before our
Lord;” prayer by Sister Clara C. Cannon;
continued by singing “All hail the new
born year;” minutes of last meeting read
and accepted. Pres’t. M. Isabella Horne
wished all the sisters a Happy New Year.
Last year we lost four of our good, true,
faithful saints, Sister Ellen C. Clawson,
Sister Helen Mar Whitney, Sister Hannah
Rawlins Pres’t. Draper and Sister Mary A.
Hazen Pres’t. of Brighton Society. “Let
us put our trust in our Father in heaven,
for we are all weak and have to wage war
against sin.” Spoke of our late conference,
said we had an excellent conference.
“Satan gathers his forces here where the
priesthood is; he has great power, places
stumbling blocks in our way and evil sug-
gestions, but everything good comes from
our Father. We are in the midst of trials
today, but God has given a key-word to be
faithful and humble in prayer. Let us
look over our actions each night and thus
strengthen ourselves day by day. We
must serve Him and keep His command-
ments; if we fail in this life we will fail in
the next; He scourges every son and every
daughter whom He receives. I can testify
that Joseph was a prophet of God, and his
blood was shed as a testimony to the world
of the truth of his mission. I desire to be
faithful to the calling where unto we have
been called.”
	Pres’t. Zina D. H. Young said “It is
over twenty-seven years since Pres’t. Brig-
ham Young organized these meetings and I
am pleased to see you, my sisters; we come
here and learn to love each other, and feast
on the good words of peace and salvation.

[Column 2]
I received a testimony of the truth of this
work before I was baptized and I have
never doubted its truth.” Spoke of the
falling away in the days of Kirtland, when
six of the twelve forsook Joseph. “O, my
sisters I have seen the sorrow of those who
have fallen by the way; the results of deny-
ing the faith are so terrible. I feel to bear
my testimony to the truth of this work, and
the truth of celestial marriage. I thank
my heavenly Father that the light has been
given; it is nothing to die if we have been
faithful. Keep free from finding fault and
you shall be blessed. May we meet here
for years and years to come, and be faithful
in all things.”
	Sister Laura Miner said, “I can say I
have never doubted the truth of this Gos-
pel.” Spoke of the Relief Society work in
Logan and made other encouraging re-
marks.
	Another sister bore an excellent testi-
mony to the truth of the Gospel. Sister
Margaret Brown spoke of her labors in the
Temple and desired to go on in such glori-
ous principles. Sister Julia Samson spoke
of the many things we had to make us re-
joice, and hoped we would be stronger in
another year. Sister Caroline C. R. Wells,
bore her testimony that the Gospel was true
and she had never doubted. A sister in
the congregation also bore a strong testi-
mony. Sister Nunn bore her testimony to
the truth of this work and the power of the
priesthood, and also the power of dreams.
	The sisters sang “Guide us O, thou
Great Jehovah.”
	Sister E. B. Wells thought it beautiful to
hear the sisters bear their testimonies,
spoke of the WOMEN’S EXPONENT; Pres’t.
Young was anxious that the sisters should
sustain their own paper. The women of
of this church should not let this paper fail,
spoke earnestly and impressively; spoke
about the silk, said the Commission was
appointed and twenty-five cents a pound
bounty is sure to you on every pound; also
spoke, of the kindergarten, also that we
should pray that the right one
should be sent down to Washington as
Senator. This matter concerns us; let us
pray about this matter that the Lord will
choose him. “I pray the Lord will bless
us.”
	Pres’t. Horne made closing remarks.
Urged the sisters to sustain the EXPONENT,
read from it at their meetings and sustain
it. Meeting adjourned for two weeks.
Singing “Come let us anew;” benediction
L. D. Alder.
		L. D. ALDER, Sec. pro tem.

	Minutes of Ladies’ Semi-Monthly Meet-
ing held in the 14th ward Hall, Jan. 23,
1897, Sister Lydia D. Alder presiding.
Opened by singing “Come, come ye
Saints;" prayer by Sister Julia C. Howe
singing “Welcome best of all good meet
ings.” Sister Alder made some remarks,
urging the sisters to faithfulness, if we only
proved humble and prayerful. She hoped
the sisters that were wont to preside would
find that we had enjoyed a goodly portion
of the Holy Spirit.
	A sister in the congregation bore a faith-
ful testimony, hoped we would be careful
in what we had to say. Sister Morris sang
“Redeemer of Israel, ” also bore her testi-
mony; she had never seen the day she did
not rejoice in this work.
	Dr. E. S. Barney also made some re-
marks, said she knew this was the work of


God and the Spirit of the Lord was with
us, we should do better every day, if we
could not hear we could read and think,
thereby obtaining the Spirit of God.
Read some remarks made by Henry Ward
Beecher.
	Sister Mary P. Silver bore her testimony,
said she had always known this was the
work of God. A Sister in the congrega-
tion bore a testimony to the truth of what
Sister Barney had said, also spoke of bless-
ngs we derived from prayer.
	Sister Nunn spoke of her labors in the
Temple, of seeing her aunt whom she was
working for, and the many blessings she
was the recipient of day by day, also the
blessings derived by fasting and prayer.
	A sister in the congregation bore a very
faithful testimony, said she felt to rejoice in
the Gospel.
	Sister Young felt to rejoice in the Gos-
pel, had been in the Church forty-nine
years, desired us to pray for her sister who
was sick, prayed the Lord to bless us all.
	Sister Sarah Phelps also spoke of the
truth of the Gospel in this our day, she felt
we ought to pray to the Lord more ferver-
ently than we had ever done before.
	Sister Silver sang “How firm a founda-
tion.” Sister Wilkins felt pleased that she
had the privilege of meeting with us.
	Sister Mina Hardy bore a faithful testi-
mony. Another sister felt to rejoice but
felt she had a testimony to bear, and knew
we could call down the blessings of heaven
if we would only ask for them.
	Sister Mary A. Hardy also bore a faithful
testimony.
	Sister Held knew if she did not say a few
words she would feel condemned. Singing
“We thank Thee O, God.” Sister B. W.
Smith knew the Prophet Joseph was a true
prophet; she asked the Lord to bless our
young sisters who had spoken in our midst,
prayed for our aged prophet Brother Wood-
ruff.
	Pres’t Horne having arrived made some
remarks, was pleased to see so many
present, thought there was signs of a re-
vival, thought we should have to retire to
the Assembly Hall; she had often thought
of the Lord’s bringing forth this work
through his servants and handmaidens,
that the Lord knew whom he was choosing.
“Let us set an example before our young
sisters.” Sister Horne said she had been
attending a meeting ever since eleven
o’clock this morning. She hoped the sis-
ters would continue to come to these meet-
ings. The first patriarchal blessing she re-
ceived she was told she would have a great
deal of sickness which she had passed
through; she also had a letter read, after
which the meeting adjourned for two
weeks. Singing Doxology; benediction by
Sister E. S. Barney.
	MARTHA BARROW, Sec. pro. tem.

Name: 18_women_utah_women utah_suffrage
Representative_Docs:
['WOMEN IN POLITICS.
	On the twelfth of February, eighteen hun-
dred and seventy, the legislative assembly of
Utah passed an act confering upon women the
elective franchise; this act, having been ap-
proved by the Governor, became a law. Mrs.
Wells writes: "The peculiar exigencies and ex-
periences of Mormon life, have had a tendency
to make women self-helpful and self-reliant,
and have given them indomitable energy and
undaunted courage, and these characteristics
prepare them to use more discretely any trust
or confidence reposed in them." This is most
true, and has been fully proven by the manner
in which they exercised their privileges as free
citizens; we say tree, for no man or woman is a
free citizen who cannot have a voice at the
polls. For seventeen years the women of Utah
voted regularly for territorial, county and city
officers; also for delegate to Congress, Having
themselves the right of suffrage, the women of
Utah have always felt an interest in their sis-
ters in other states, and have assisted as best
they could, by petitions and other means, the
suffrage movement.
	The women were very eager to vote, but
took no other political privileges much before
eighteen seventy-eight, when they attended the
caucusses and primary meetings. Out of the
five different precincts in Salt Lake City, three
of them sent women delegates to the County
Convention. At this Convention, the first that
women had a voice in, Mrs. Emmeline B. Wells
was chosen as Secretary, and also nominated
for County Treasurer, but owing to the Statutes
of the Territory, the nomination could not be
sustained by the Convention. Though the law
allowed women to vote, the legal objection to
women holding office was found in the compiled
laws of Utah, which read, "Male citizens."
The gentlemen of this Convention showed great
liberality towards and pleasure in having the

[Column 2]

women associated with them, and offered to the
lady nominee for treasurer a vote of confidence
and assurance that she should have been sus-
tained if the  statutes had allowed. At this
same Convention three ladies' names—E.
B. Wells, M. I. Horne and S. M. Kim-
ball—were added to the People's Central Com-
mittee. The work of this Convention was
quite an advanced step for the women of the
Territory. These three women remained on
the Central Committee, and worked with sin-
cerity up to the time of the disfranchisement
of women. In several following conventions
women have held the offices of vice presidents
and secretaries.
	During this year of seventy-eight, when
women were making such progress in political
affairs. the subject of plural marriage was
greatly agitated, and active measures were
taken by certain parties to arouse public senti-
ment against its practice. A mass meeting of
non-Mormon women was held, and after some
speech-making against the Mormons in general,
certain resolutions were adopted and a circular
sent to Congress against polygamy, and speak-
ing in favor of the disfranchisement of the
women of Utah. Thus, the first blow against
Woman's Rights in Utah was struck by women.
It has often been remarked, that if it were not
for women themselves, women might vote,
and it is, to a great extent, true. At any
rate, the non-Mormon women of Utah have
shown little inclination to vote, and have been
very earnest in their efforts against the rights
of Mormon women.
	This Anti-Polygamy Meeting was followed
by a Mass Meeting of the women of Utah in the
Salt Lake Theatre. There were present at
least two thousand women; such a gathering
as is seldom seen in any place. There were,
perhaps, some fifteen or twenty newspaper re-
porters present, the only men admitted. There
were some eight or nine addresses by prominent
women, and then resolutions were read and
adopted, wherein the women declared them-
selves loyal citizens, and claimed the right to
defend themselves against the ruthless assaults
being made upon their sacred and constitutional
rights. It was a brave and a great defense,
when two thousand women rose en masse and
declared themselves determined to maintain
and defend their rights. Mass meetings of
women were held all over the Territory endors-
ing the sentiments expressed, and adopting the
resolutions presented at this mass meeting.
Not only were the women of Utah themselves
aroused, but the noble women of the suffrage
associations were alike enraged at the crusade
which had begun, and defended the women of
Utah in the the halls of Congress. Lillie
Devereaux Blake, when the subject of disfran-
chisement first appeared, wrote a very cutting
and fitting article against such a movement,
showing, in her pointed satire, the absurdity of
trying to prevent polygamy by taking the vote
from the women.
	The next year Utah sent two delegates—
Mrs. E. B. Wells and Zina Y. Williams—to
the National Woman's Suffrage Convention at
Washington, who were allowed many oppor-
tunities of speaking in behalf of the Mormon
women. During an interview with President
Hayes, he was so impressed by their statements
as to request them to put their case in writing,
before him, as he did not wish to trust so im-
portant a matter to a verbal interview. They
also prepared, while in Washington, a touching
and telling memorial to Congress, asking for a
repeal of the anti-polygamy law of eighteen
sixty-two, and for legislation to protect the
women and children of Utah. This memorial
was referred to the Judiciary Committee, be-
fore which these ladies had several hearings.
When the women of this Territory were first
threatened with disfranchisement they went to
the Nation's Capital to assert their rights, and

[Column 3]

to the National Woman's Suffrage Association
to ask assistance, and they did not ask in vain.
The suffragists have been most kind in speaking
for our rights, and we always have a representa
tion at their Conventions.
	The women of Utah petitioned the Terri-
torial Legislature of eighteen eighty, praying
for the removal of their political disabilities,
stating that for nine years they had enjoyed
the right of suffrage, and had proven them-
selves worthy of the trust; they argued, that
owing to the word male in the territorial
statutes, they were prohibited from holding any
official position, and prayed that some enact-
ment might be passed to remove this obstacle,
and allow them further rights. This prayer
was granted by the assembly, a bill having
been pressented and passed in accordance with
their wishes, viz: "A bill removing the political
disabilities of the women of Utah." This bill
passed the House with a large majority vote,
and the Council unanimously, but was ignored
by the Governor, as was also a similar bill pre-
sented some two years before. It is a sad
reflection on the Government, which boasts
such unparallelled freedom, that the contempt
of a single man, who has neither property or
interest of any kind in the Territory, can pre-
vent the passage of an act which is in accord-
ance with the wishes of the voters and the law-
makers of the community. At the time
the legislature again convened, the in-
famous Edmunds Bill was pending in Congress,
and as that was passed in March, 1882, all
women who were the wives of polygamous men
were disfranchised.
	The next effort on the part of the people for
rights was to frame a State Constitution and
present the same to Congress, asking for ad-
mission, and it was the intention to have a
Woman's Suffrage Plank in its platform. In
eighteen eighty-four there were four representa-
tive women in Washington working before
committees for the admission of Utah, and for
the repeal or modification of the Edmunds
Bill, which was then causing such great suffer-
ing and trouble among the people of this Terri-
tory. So great had this trouble become, that
the women had no heart to fight for political
rights; it seemed as though they scarcely had
the right to live at all. In eighteen eighty-six
the dreadful Edmunds-Tucker Bill became a
law, and from that time all tue women have
been disfranchised.
	It has been questioned some why the women
of Utah, not plural wives, do not make a stand
and plead for their former rights; and 'twould
seem if they were at all interested in suffrage
that they would certainly do so; their silence
speaks against them. If they would only try
to regain the ballot, they might do good to
others as well as themselves. We scarcely
need call it their right, it is their duty; your
brothers and sisters are bound; they have no
freedom. Obtain your rights, free women of
Utah, and do your duty to your country. Mrs.
Stanton says, "I want women to feel that it is
their religious duty to take part in government,
the most elevated of all sciences."


WOMAN’S EXPONENT.


Danske Dandrige is a woman whose verse
first sees the light in a slender volume, "Joy
and Other Poems," just issued by Putnam. If
her poems have before appeared in the maga-
zines, I have been unable to find them; but one
of them, "To my comrade tree," bears every
test a good poem must endure before it is ac-
cepted as a worthy addition to the great stream
of English verse. It can be read alone and
aloud in good company, it can be re-read, and,
more than all this, it enriches the chambered
imagery of the mind and adds a new cement to
life. It is not a great poem, nor one consum-
mate in form; but it is tender and true, touches
the heart and kindles its better feelings.—Book
News.


WOMAN'S EXPONENT.


', 'UTAH'S LADY DELEGATE.

	Mrs. Emily S. Richards, who left here on
the 13th of January to attend the Annual
Convention of the National Woman Suffrage
Association in Washington, as a Delegate
from Utah, arrived at the Capitol previous to
the opening of the Convention, and was al-
lowed time for an address, notwithstanding the
previously arranged programme. Mrs. Rich-
ards was received with considerable applause,
and made a decidedly favorable impression.
We give her speech in full, as published in a
letter to the Deseret News from the special cor-
respondent in Washington.—ED

	"'Tis better to have voted and been disfran-
chised, than never to have voted at all."
	This is a very prosaic paraphrase of a very
poetical sentiment, but it is true, and behind it
lies a great political wrong.
	I am a native born citizen of the United
States, and for fifteen years enjoyed all the
rights and privileges of the elective franchise.
I voted for every kind of elective officer in the
Territory of Utah, from delegate in Congress
to school trustee. I never once sold my vote,
nor was I influenced in casting it by either
fear or favor. I never attempted to repeat; no
man for whom I ever cast a ballot was ever
charged with malfeasance in office, or with any
other violation of his official oath. Yet to-day
I am disfranchised and on the same political
plane as you, my sisters, and the rest of our
sex in this "land of liberty."
	I think I may safely challenge any male
voter of the United States to show a better
use of the rights of American citizenship. I
do make that challenge for myself, and on
behalf of the thousands of women in Utah
whom I represent, and whose political record
is above reproach. And on their behalf, with
all possible respect to the distinguished gentle-
men who sit in Congressional majesty on Capi-
tol Hill, I protest against the wresting from us,
by the right of might, of that franchise which
we had never abused, and which was our vested
right. 
	In 1870 women were given the elective fran-
chise in Utah.
	Subsequently an act was passed by the legis-
lature striking the word male from the law
prescribing qualifications for holding office, so
that women might be equally eligible with men
to any office in the Territory. This act never
became a law because it was disapproved by
the Governor, who is appointed without the
consent of the people, and his vote is absolute,
even when opposed to the unanimous vote of
the entire legislature. And so the will of the
people was defeated by this unrepublican one
man political power.
	Just as one who has loved and lost is always
deemed the best authority upon affairs of the
heart, so I venture to claim a particular knowl-
edge of the use, the power of the franchise in
woman's hands. And the experience which I
gained with the women of Utah should not be
concealed from this Convention; it belongs to
you; it is essentially a part of woman's history
in this great Republic; it is the best practical
illustration of the problem whiah has yet been
given, and it is likely to obtain that pre-emin-
ence for years to come, because in all the seven-
teen years during which the women of Utah
exercised their right to vote, they held the
balance of power between the two parties, and

[Column 2]

no man was elected to office without their ap-
proval.
	During all these years we voted without
disturbance and without injury to the manners,
morals or domestic comfort of the men. We
were able to determine whom we wished to
sustain by our votes, and no one had the right,
the power, and, I will say for our husbands,
brothers and fathers, the wish or desire to pre-
vent, intimidate, or coerce us in the least de-
gree. We were citizens of the United States,
armed with that all-potent yet peaceful politi-
cal weapon, the ballot, and we challenge the
world to show where, in a single instance, we
wielded it wrongfully. And let me say right
here, that we have not had to contend against
the male opposition common in other places,
for the majority of men in Utah believe that
woman was designed by the Creator to be
man's companion in all things, whether in the
Church or in the State, in time and in eternity,
and to share dominion with him over all created
things.
	While we enjoyed the franchise we took part
in the primaries and nominating Conventions,
acted as delegates, and did our share of the
political business.
	Two years ago Congress abrogated the right
of woman's suffrage in Utah, and thousands of
us were summarily deprived of our political
power. We had committed no offense against
the sacred franchise and had violated no law
of our opponents, and, therefore, in their esti-
mation we were not worthy to vote at all. The
same logic would have disfranchised men as
well as women, but their ballots were pre-
served.
	It has been intimated that the women of
Utah were compelled to vote as required by
the men. This is not true. No coercion was
ever attempted, and, if it had been, the effort
to accomplish it would have failed because,
under the law, we had a perfectly secret ballot,
which put it beyond all human power to find
out how any person voted unless the elector
chose to tell.
	It is also an error to suppose that women
voters of Utah used the ballot to uphold or
perpetuate any system or practice in opposition
to the laws, or the will, or common custom of
this country. No such question was ever voted
on at the polls in that Territory. Votes of
men and women alike were merely cast for
local officers, and we chose to elect such men
as we believed would act honestly, fairly and
for the public good, in the interests of tem-
perance, order, morality and economy, and not
for private ends or personal greed. That was
"the head of our offending."
	The deprivation which we have sustained is
not more cruel to us than to you. The con-
tinuance of our political power meant that you
could have pointed to our work as a proof that
the general cause was worthy of success. But
Congress, in the almost infinite wisdom of
legislative man, decreed to dig up the new
budding tree before it could bear fruit and
give seed. It was not within the male purpose
that this little acorn, planted nineteen years
ago, should grow into the sturdy oak.
	But while we have lost our franchise in Utah,
we still retain our property rights. Unmarried
women have all the legal rights of men, so far
as the acquisition and disposition of property
is concerned, whether real or personal. Mar-
ried women have entire control of all the


[Column 1]
property they held at the time of marriage
and can acquire, hold and sell, or otherwise
dispose of real and personal property after
marriage without the consent of their hus-
bands, but the husband must have the consent
of the wife. In addition to this they have the
right of dower, by which they inherit one-third
of the husband's estate at his death. The law
of inheritance is the same from the wife to the
husband as from the husband to the wife; each
inherits from the other under similar condi-
tions. 
	For many years the women of Utah have
been organized into Relief Societies and Mu-
tual  Improvement Associations. Their sym-
pathies have always been with the Woman
Suffrage movement. After the visit to Utah,
last summer, of Mrs. Colby and Mrs. Saxon,
officers of this Association, who lectured on
“Woman Suffrage; the International Council
of Women and the relation of woman’s ballot
to all reforms,” a Woman’s Suffrage Associa-
tion was organized as an auxiliary to the Na-
tional. Its members number about two hun-
dred and are rapidly increasing. In this move-
ment we did not receive the aid or sanction of
our vice-president, because she did not think
woman suffrage good for Utah. The organiza-
tion was effected by the chairman of the execu-
tive committee. It is complete in every par-
ticular, and the by-laws declare the object of
the association to be “to work for the enfran-
chisement of women without regard to party,
sect, or creed, and to take such steps from time
to time as shall seem to be wise and expedient
for the political advancement of the women
of Utah.”  The Association has sent two dele-
gates to this convention. The very large ma-
jority of people in Utah are in favor of woman
suffrage. As an evidence of this, we secured
8,393 signatures to the national enrollment in
three weeks, 3000 of them being the names of 
men, and we have but just begun our work. 
	All trades, professions and occupations in
Utah are open to women. We have editors,
doctors—who have graduated in the best col-
leges of the country—lawyers, school teachers,
artists, poets, musicians, lecturers, merchants,
clerks, cashiers, photographers, printers, book-
binders, telegraph and telephone operators, silk
workers, weavers, factory hands and artisans
in every kind of work that women are able to 
do, as well as in those domestic labors which
some narrow-minded people think comprise
the sphere of woman. We have no “superflu-
ous women” in the Territory, and none who
need remain without employment; it is often
impossible to hire women for domestic ser-
vice. 
Women, in the male estimation, are not as
fit as men to perform jury service, because it is
said they would be governed by sentiment. If
the premises be true, the argument is still false.
The sentiment which would most often actuate
women is pity. With men it is too often bus-
iness, avarice or social cowardice. Women
might favor the poor and friendless. Men cer-
tainly do show illegal leniency to the rich and 
powerful. 
	We are told that suffrage means turmoil of 
mind, which women should avoid. I do not
concede that this is necessarily so, but I do say
that, if it were so, my sentiment would be that 
of the heroic Roman who said: “Better a dis-
turbed liberty than a quiet slavery.”
	For one, in a general sense, I object to the
cruel withholding of suffrage from women, in
a land consecrated by blood to universal liber-
ty. In a special sense I object to the arbitrary
edict by which thousands of intelligent voters
were deprived of a sacred right, simply be-
cause they were women. 
	The mightiest human problem vexing the
world to-day is the relation of the rich and the
poor—the profit eaters and the profit makers.
Man alone will never solve it. There are
[Column 2]
cases of wounds and illness where woman’s
nursing is absolutely necessary. This is a 
desperate case of social disorder, where woman
must minister with all her skill and tender-
ness to bring the patient through the crisis.
The cry which we now offer up is the cry of
the ages. It is the protest of the few against
the many, the argument of thought against
bigotry. To-day the world is correcting the
active wrongs of last century, and is making
recompense to the demon of intolerance, by be-
ing blind and deaf to the oppressed of to-day.
The wonder which men feel now that their an-
cestors could have been so intolerant will be
felt a generation hence by their sons regarding
these same men.
	The “federation of the world” will not be
accomplished while the most advanced nation
on earth is but half redeemed. But the cause
of woman’s enfranchisement is gaining irresist-
ible momentum. Its triumph must soon come.
When it is victorious, but not till then, will
might cease to her arbiter of right. 
	When a cause is irrevocably lost, it is then 
too late for words. To discuss inevitable sor-
row is only to give intensity to grief. But the
women of Utah do not regard their own fran-
chise as being forever lost. We hope and ex-
pect to see this great struggle terminate speedi-
ly and triumphantly for the cause of woman,
and to see political liberty enjoyed without re-
gard to sex. When that sublime hour comes
it will mark the beginning of the end of tyran-
ny, oppression and misrule. God speed the 
day.


﻿WOMAN'S EXPONENT.


[Column 1]

', "WOMAN’S DAY AT SALTAIR.

	THE Relief Society of Salt Lake Stake
and the Women of the State arranged for an
excursion to Saltair on Tuesday, August
20. The affair was in the hands of a com-
mittee of women represented by Mrs. M. I.
Horne, President of the Relief Society of
Salt Lake Stake, and Mrs. E. B. Wells Pre-
sident of the State Suffrage Association. The
day was not very satisfactory even though the
Carnival of Madrid was well advertised,
and consequently many who had intended
to patronize the ladies did not attend; how-
ever there was quite a good-sized gathering
and a rather pleasing program of exercises,
though one of the distinguished gentlemen
who was expected to speak disappointed
the audience by his absence, Judge W. H.
King. The exercises commenced at 5 p.
m. General John Q. Cannon acting as
Master of Ceremonies, called the assembly
to order and announced prayer by Sister
Zina D. H. Young, who then offered an
eloquent prayer, and next came the Pream-
ble and Sentiments by ladies in different
parts of the state, a part of which were
read by General Cannon in a clear distinct
voice, followed by the Governor whose re-
marks to the women of the state are given
here.
 PREAMBLE.
	IN all the years past since the pioneers enter-
ed this then barren, desert valley, years full
of startling episodes and thrilling incidents,
woman has fulfilled her part of the pro-
gramme equally with man, and has emulat-
ed the noble example of the heroic pilgrim
mothers of New England, in bearing the
burdens and sharing the toil of the men
who have made the “desert blossom as the
rose,” and after many years of patient
struggle and endurance have at last suc-
ceeded in establishing a great common-
wealth, ranking equal with other states in
this free Republic, aud in one respect
surpassing all, except Wyoming and Colo-
rado, in according to women equal suffrage
with men. Thus has a new era opened up
for woman. She has now entered into her
land of promise; the eyes of the world are turn-
ed hither to see what the women of Utah will
do with this boasted right, the ballot. We
must not disappoint our friends or gratify
our opponents by our indifference or neglect,
but show our true patriotism in this hour
of our country’s greatest need. We should
prove worthy the sublime confidence re-
posed in us by those valiant men, who
championed our cause in the memorable
Constitutional Convention of 1895.
	We cannot too strongly urge upon wo-
men voters the grave responsibility
devolving upon them in the present condi-
tion of affairs in our state and nation, and
entreat them to come proudly forward and
take part in the struggle for right now be-
ing agitated, by casting their votes and us-
ing their utmost influence to maintain the
principles of justice, freedom and liberty,
our noble inheritance from our revered fore-


[Column 1]
fathers, according to their honest convic-
tions of right and wrong.
SENTIMENTS
	THE sacred right, but at the same .time
privilege, of the franchise has been obtained
by the women of Utah.
	We are watched by three classes of men
and women. By the cynic who expects
that we will make but a poor use or, per-
chance, no use at all of it, by the timid
women who fear for “the consequences”,
and by earnest women of many states who
look to us to vindicate their hopes and as-
pirations. And deeper down in the heart
of things there is the great need for the
good, pure resolute women of these United
States to rise in their might and help right
the deplorable condition of our country!
Let every woman voter in Utah feel her
responsibility and study the questions at
issue, and then vote her convictions.

	WOMAN’S domestic qualities will in no
sense deteriorate because of her political
equalities; she will lose none of her woman-
ly wifely attributes; her influence will be
exerted to purify the political atmosphere
and secure a rigorous enforcement of all
just laws, and an equally prompt repeal of
obnoxious ones.
	The female pioneer element, which is
largely represented in this state, now as in
days of old, developes the noblest womanly
characteristics, and already is observed a
marked independence of action and charact-
er which is seldom found in older civiliza-
tions where fashion and social distinction
rule with autocratic sway.

	The franchise is one of the highest privi-
leges granted to the individual under a free
government. If to vote is one of the great-
est privileges, is it not then a sacred duty
which no woman who desires the sanctity
of her home protected, the right to life,
liberty and the pursuit of happiness, sus-
tained and preserved inviolate, both of her
loved ones and herself, will neglect to per-
form? It is now an imperative obligation
devolving upon the women of Utah, to en-
courage, teach and sustain each other in
voting intelligently for honest efficient
officers, who will labor unswervingly for
the best interest of the people trusting
them with the affairs of state, county or
city. 
	Whether women purify the ballot or not
remains to be seen; but most certainly they
will add an element tending to greater per-
fection, the same as exemplified in the
family, which is the unit of government.
	A one sided organization only half per-
forms the work; when the other half is
added it rounds out into a perfect whole.
	If the study of politics is the main work of
women’s clubs from now until November,
a more determining vote for the good
of the whole country will be polled than
ever before in Utah. Women all rise to
the hight of your privileges!

	A victory gained for a great principle if
barren of results, is more to be deplored
than defeat; for defeat leads to renewed
effort, while victory followed by apathy
and indifference, is like Dead Sea Fruit and
the Apples of Sodom. If the women of
Utah who one year ago were earnestly de-
manding and striving for the right of suf-
frage, now sit down supinely and fold their
hands and say, “We have the ballot, we

[Column 2]
will vote but beyond that will take no fur-
ther interest in politics”, they are not
worthy of the rights of citizenship in a
growing state or great nation.  
	The acceptance of the position and privi-
leges of a citizen carries with it the obliga-
tions and responsibilities of citizenship, and
in my opinion, it is the duty of every wo-
man, not only to vote, but inform herself
on the vital issues of the day, and acquaint
herself thoroughly with the great principles
that uplift and benefit humanity, and un-
derlie all correct legislation, good govern-
ment and true prosperity. Having done
this for herself, I believe she is then bound
by her duty to the state and nation to dis-
seminate those principles, and to see, as far
as she can assist them, that all other women
within her reach, vote and understand what
they are voting for. It is only through the
efforts and influence of women alive to
their duties and responsibilities that the
state or nation will be benefited by wo-
man’s enfranchisememt.

	I think we have had a great privilege
granted us in having the franchise at such
an important time, when great political
questions are confronting us as at the pre-
sent. When we as citizens of this great
Republic, will feel in duty bound to take
part in the great issues that lie before us;
and may we do so conscientiously according
to our convictions of right, and strive with
all diligence to understand our duties and
the great responsibilities that rest upon us
as citizens of this great Republic, as well as
of our own fair state of Utah.
	We know we are on the eve of a great
financial crisis; its importance we can only
realize in part; how it will end the Lord
only knows. We must use the wisdom of
Solomon, aided by the Spirit of the Lord to
direct us that we may be on His side, then
we need not fear what the result would be.
	I often think of the reply that President
Joseph Smith made to Senator Douglas
when he asked him how he managed to
govern the people. He said he taught
them correct principles and let them govern
themselves.
	That is what we want today, to under-
stand the correct principles that underlie
our Government. It is this lack of know-
ledge with us as a people that I fear at this
time when there is so much at stake. We
have never had such a responsibility resting
upon us before, as the privilege of voting
for the highest official of this great Com-
monwealth; and may we give this question
and every other, that due deliberation, that
we may vote intelligently upon all ques-
tions that come before us, even holding it
as a sacred trust that can be neither bought
nor sold for any consideration. And may
we always have reason to be grateful to
those who worked so faithfully that we
might enjoy political freedom.
GOVERNOR HEBER M. WELLS.
	We are making history rapidly What-
ever future centuries may bring forth, they
must concede to the Nineteenth, that it
revolutionized the world industrially, polti-
ically and religiously.
	Freedom of thought and action was
never so great as now, the yoke of religious
creed or despotic government never so far
removed or successfully repelled, the main-
tainance of human liberties never so well
sustained.
	What wonder then, that this progressive

[Column 3]
age of human enlightenment should witness
the breaking forth of women from the
chrysalis of exclusive domesticity into ac-
tive participation in Government affairs,
and that man’s assumption of the right to
vote and govern, without consultation with
his equal and natural partner and compan-
ion, should be relegated to the past.
	The women of Utah are to be congratu-
lated upon their accession to the rights of
American citizens. To attain these rights
Herculean work has been performed; wo-
men, themselves, as well as men have had
to be converted to the cause, strong antipa-
thies and prejudices have had to be over-
come in both, and only the most persistent
and determined effort could have accom-
plished so much in comparatively so short
a time.
	The credit of this great work belongs to
the women of Utah, for whom no words
of commendation can be misplaced. Their
reward is great for they now enjoy many
privileges that are vouchsafed to but com-
paratively few women of our country.
They will not be content with this, how-
ever, as their cause is too great to permit of
their resting satisfied with merely local
advantages—it is the enfranchisement of
half the Nation, the better half—they are
contending for, and no doubt, the influence
of the women of Utah will be felt in behalf
of “Woman Suffrage” until the last woman
in this broad land shall have been enfran-
chised.
	History will record you among the pion-
eers of this great work, and the memory of
your good deeds will live after you.
	After the Governor’s address W. C. Dun-
bar delighted the audience with selections
upon his Highland bagpipes, and was
warmly applauded. Senator Frank J.
Cannon made a thrilling speech in which
he dwelt strongly upon the present condi-
tion of the masses of the people here and
in the United States, and paid a glowing
tribute to the women of Utah, their cour-
age, integrity and patient endurance of
toil and hardship, and cited incidents in
the life and experience of the General
President of the Relief Society, making a
pathetic picture and extoling her virtues
and excellence of character. No greater or
more eloquent compliment could be paid
to a mother in Israel. We expected his
remarks in brief to publish, but hope now
to have them for the next issue of the
paper.
	Miss Webster’s skipping-rope dance
came next and was very pretty and grace-
ful and in perfect time. Miss Webster is
quite a favorite wherever she appears.
The bagpipes were introduced again and
were as welcome as at first. Then followed
a further reading of sentiments and resolu-
tions.

	THIS is our sentiment: Woman, the
equal of man, and the crowning glory of
the celestial kingdom.

	I think that as women are amenable to
the laws of our state and bear equally the
responsibilities with men, woman should
also share in all the privileges accorded to
men, not only in voting, but in holding
offices of honor, emolument and trust suit-
able to their sex.

	IN my judgment the women of Utah,
having been given the elective franchise,
would do well to read the editorial and


[Column 1]
political portions of our own state press,
which not only has the welfare of the peo-
ple of the state in view, but is as patriotic
as any portion of our national press; and
they should endeavor to post themselves
upon the questions that are to be settled by
the ballot, and in voting should be guided
more by judgment and principle than by
partisanship.

	FOR a long time I have been in favor of
suffrage. I believe it will be the means of
elevating us. Woman can accomplish
more than man if she is prepared for the
work. She can have statesmen born and
raised under her influence so that they can
become a power in the land. I believe
there is a need for such men, and it can
never be accomplished while women are
considered inferior to men. I don’t see
why we cannot understand the political
situation of our country. I am convinced
that if we are united we can do a great
deal of good towards elevating our race.

	HAVING, after a long struggle, gained
the right of suffrage, I think it becomes
our duty as citizens of the new state of
Utah, to arouse from our lethargy and use
all our energy and the intelligence God has
given us to study the best way to use this right
not only in this state but also in the nation,
and also use our influence in getting them
to see the necessity of the same.
	Statistics show that a large percent of the
women in the United States are self-sup-
porting.
	They are expected to pay taxes and help
support the Government. This being the
case should they not take an interest in
national affairs? “Taxation without repre-
sentation is tyranny.”
	I would like to ask you gentlemen
and ladies, if the franchise rightly used,
will not tend to morally revolutionize our
government and place it on that grand un-
selfish, unintriguing political platform that
was presented to us by our forefathers.

	WE ought to be alive to the best interest
of the country. We want silver restored to
its full value (sixteen to one) that we can
pay our debts from the products of our
farm's without selling real estate. We
ought to let all other considerations be as
minor ones until that is accomplished, then
see that our laws are carried out, or some
of them mended, and not leave the law in
the hands of men that will not carry them
out, there are thousands of cases where
justice sleeps, and I for one am tired of it.
Can we as women sit by and see such out-
rages commited and not say anything about
it? I say out with such men!
	Let us have honorable men and women
for our officers, and see to it that we get
them. Don’t let us shirk our duties. See
that all women are registered and vote, and
also attend our Primaries. Keep abreast of
the times; stand for our rights and do not
be daunted. These are some of my views
also the most of the women that I am ac-
quainted with. They seem to be waking
up—the women of Utah—to their rights.

	EVERY person of observation who has
noticed the great strides every nation is
making in more humane and liberal govern-
ment. has also noticed the rapid advance-
ment of women throughout the world.
Co-eval with all greatness among nations,
comes the God-given liberty of women.

[Column 2]
Just as the misty clouds of superstition and
fogyism about woman’s sphere gives way
to the scearch-light of truth, will she take
her place politically, and will not only take
her place, but man will willingly recognize
it, learning at last that all their interests are
one. Now that equal suffrage has been
granted to the women of Utah, the obsta-
cles raised by our opponents have all melted
away. Everything works in harmony, and
all seem perfectly satisfied. Ladies who
declared they would never exercise the
franchise, are today enthusiastic over the
great questions that are holding the atten-
tion of the nation, and are ready to cast
their ballots. I see nothing discouraging,
but believe the little stone of Woman Suf-
frage set rolling here in the West will yet fill
the whole earth.

	I think the status of the women in the
new state is very good and is destined to
place woman upon the high elevation des-
tined by her Creator; nor will the true
woman of the state fill less her domestic
relations as a wife and mother because she
has a right estimate of her position and new
responsibilities. She will preserve her
self respect and dignity in her new position
as in her home life, and seek with true
nobility to refine and purify the political
and moral atmosphere.
	The influence of woman is the same
everywhere. Her condition influences the
morals and character of the people in the
community of which she is a member. The
granting of the franchise to women shows
the political status of the people. The wo-
men of the new state asked many times for
this great boon ere it was again bestowed;
and having received it again, I have no
fears that she will abuse this great blessing,
nor transcend the proper limits of womanly
dignity, but by study and thought prove
herself the intellectual peer of her brother
man. Her education is extended; her
knowledge is increased; she is proving her
capabilities to grasp and understand, and
discuss the leading questions of the day as
intelligently as the majority of the other
sex. In many cases her conceptions are
clearer, while her opportunities are increas-
ing and her field of labor widening, I feel
assured she will prove herself equal to the
occasion. I think the women of the new
state will prove to our opponents and the
world at large, that we do appreciate the
franchise, by studying the political issues
of the day and voting for men and measures
that are best calculated to advance the in-
terests of all mankind. Therefore, in my
humble opinion, the granting of the fran-
chise or equal suffrage, has raised the status
of the women In the new state materially
and will eventually prove a great blessing
not only to herself, but to future genera-
tions. Let the good work go on until all
women shall receive the same blessing that
the women of Utah now enjoy, by using
and not abusing this great boon.
RESOLUTIONS.
	WHEREAS, The constitution of the state
provides that the rights of citizens of the
state of Utah to vote and hold office shall
not be denied or abridged on account of
sex; and
	WHEREAS, Experience has taught us
that by woman’s entering into the profes-
sions of law, medicine and teaching, those
professions have been raised to a higher
place; that by her admission to the colleges
and universities of our country the moral

[Column 3]
tone of our institutions of learning has been
elevated and the standard of scholarship ad-
vanced; that by the product of her brain
and pen the progress of the whole human 
race has been promoted; that in the limited
opportunities offered her she has demonstrat-
ed her capacity to grasp governmental
questions and her ability to solve them; to
be equal to that of her male contemporaries;
and
	WHEREAS, Woman possesses in a very
high degree the power to reform, ennoble
and purify every organization of a religious,
social or political nature; now, therefore, be
it
	RESOLVED, That it is the duty of every
woman of the state possessing the requisite
qualifications, to enter into active political
work, to the end that our new privileges
may be exercised in behalf of pure govern-
ment.
	RESOLVED, That we agree to identify
ourselves with the political party that is the
best exponent of our political ideas, and
that we will participate in the Primaries and
Conventions of our respective parties as well
as vote upon election day that our voice
may be heard in formulating our party’s
policy, and that candidates may be nomi-
nated worthy of our support.
	RESOLVED, That we extend our hearty
greetings to our sister voters of the neigh-
boring states of Wyoming and Colorado and
we express the desire that ere long the
people of our whole nation may recognize 
the inestimable blessings conferred upon the 
body politic by the admission of man and
his helpmate, woman, to equal political
privileges.
	(Signed.)
ELIZABETH J. MCFARLANE.
MRS. J. FEWSON SMITH,
ETIZABETH M. PRICE,
				Committee.
FINAL RESOLUTION.
	RESOLVED, That the women of Utah
take the heartiest interest in all organiza-
tions tending to ameliorate the condition of 
women throughout the world.
	That they recognize the great education-
al work of the Federation of Clubs, and will
as far as they are able, co-operate with
them.
	That they sympathize with the persecut-
ed inhabitants of Armenia, and call upon
the nation to show their interest by gener-
ous contributions to a starving nation.
	Mrs. Elizabeth J. McFarlane was intro
duced and recited a very appropriate
selection in a most pleasing style. The
address was entitled “To Our Brothers.’’


WOMAN'S EXPONENT.


WOMAN'S EXPONENT.


"]

Name: 19_sister_zina_eliza_smith
