['ELIZABETH D. GARDNER, Sec'y.

KANARRA, March 7, 1878.
EDITOR EXPONENT:
 The Relief Society of this place being
desirous of recognition with other Societies,
offer a few items for publication.  Our So-
ciety was organized December 14, 1875, by
Bishop Lorenzo W. Roundy.  Officers elect-
ed and sustained:
Susanna Roundy, President.
Sophia Parker,        Counselors.
Mary Jane Perkins,
Lennie Allen, Secretary.
Deney Berry, Assistant Secretary.
Priscilla Roundy, Treasurer.
 We have also six Teachers.  We were
prospering, until the 24th of May, 1876,
when the sad accident of my dear husband
being drowned in the Colorado River cast a
gloom over all of us, and over the whole
town.  He was a kind husband, a loving
father, and O! how we miss him.  The
same summer we buried three grand-chil-
dren, and on April 19, 1877, Mary Jane
Perkins died of heart disease.  She was
the wife  of William I. and Betsey Willis,
aged thirty-five years.  She was one of my
Counselors, and was taken from our midst;
she was a good woman in our Society, was
on hand for anything that she could do, and
she was beloved of all who knew her, for 
the integrity of her heart.  She was one of
the working-bees of the hive of Deseret,
and we miss her very much in our Society.
No language can express my feelings when
I heard of her death.
 We are trying to do all we can for this
kingdom; we hold our meetings regularly
once in two weeks; there are but few at-
tend, but they are willing to do all they can.
We have made several quilts, and fifty or
sixty yards of carpet are ready to weave;
we have also some lace and tidies on hand,
and some money.  Last spring we set out
three hundred mulberry cuttings, but only
about a hundred lived; the water is so
scarce we could not get sufficient to water
them. 
	We have had a very disagreeable winter,
and there are a great many people sick; our
Secretaries are both ill, and have been all
winter, though they are getting better now;
when they recover we will send a list of all
we have done for the Temple and for the
poor. They keep strict account of all that
is done.  It is hard for us to store up grain
here, we cannot raise half what we use.
If we could make anything to sell for wheat
we would be glad to do so and store some
up here.  We beg an interest in the sisters'
prayers, that we may have strength to do
our duty.  We feel happy that we have
been working for the good of Zion.  We
feel well as a Society, and wish at all times
to be dictated by the Spirit of God, that
our efforts may prove successful.  Your
sister, &c.,
SUSANNA ROUNDY, Pres't.
CHESTER, March 10, 1878.
EDITOR EXPONENT.
Being a newly organized branch of the 
Relief Society, we thought a word from us
would not be out of place.  This small
branch was organized January 13, 1876,
with the following sisters as officers:
Sarepta C. Cowles, President.
Lucy Allred,                          Counselors.
Emma Heyward,
Hettie Allred, Secretary.
Annie Maria Madsen, Treasurer.
	We have eighteen members enrolled;
we meet once a month, and our meetings
are well attended considering the scattered
condition of our settlement; the sisters ap-
pear to enjoy meeting together and speak-
ing words of encouragement to each other
of the great work in which they are en-
gaged, and manifest a willingness to do
good by throwing in their mites, to assist
in starting a work which we hope will
prove a benefit and blessing in some future
day.  There appears to be a feeling of one-
ness and lenity one toward the other; we
desire to be one with our sisters of the
many branches of Relief Society in striv-
ing to carry out the instruction which we
receive from those who are placed at our
head to make our duties plain unto us.  We
desire an interest in the prayers of all
faithful Latter-day Saints that we may en-
dure to the end, and obtain the blessings
that are in store for the faithful, which is
the prayer of your sister and co-worker in
the Gospel, HETTIE ALLRED,
                                         Secretary.
SUGAR HOUSE WARD,
March 14, 1878.
EDITOR EXPONENT:
Brief report of a meeting of the Relief
Society, held in the Society Hall, Jan. 2d,
1878.  President Mrs. S. A. Gibson, presid-
ing.  After the usual exercises of singing
and prayer, the Financial report was read
for the year ending December 4, 1877:
Donations received.......................... $88. 25
Proceeds of Sister's Party, for New
Hall.................................................. 48. 75
                              --------
Total                                                 137. 00
Disbursements--To Temple fund.      28. 50
To Library....................................      21. 50
To plastering Sister Hall.............       25. 00
Blinds, stoves, chairs, lumber for 
seats............................................       25. 00
To the Silk Association.............. 2. 00
To the Poor................................. 25. 00
Cash on hand............................. 10. 00
                                  -----------
Total                                               137. 00
Grain, 34 bushels......................       34. 00
Much good instruction was given by
Bishop Appollos Driggs and his Counselor,
Brother E. Eldrege; they urged the sisters
to faithfulness and good works.  Said the 
mission of the sisters was very important;
counseled them to be diligent in the stor-
ing of grain for Zion.  Spoke upon the 
"signs of the times," and urged us to live
as becometh Saints of God.
Hoping our little paper, the EXPONENT,
may do much good, yours respectfully,
SARAH WHEELER, Secretary.


SINCERE friendship makes no loud boasts
but manifests itself by an eloquence which
exquisitely appeals to the soul.


', 'EDITOR EXPONENT:

	Our first Quarterly Meeting of the Stake
organization of the Relief Society was
held April 21st, 1878, at the Nephi Meeting
House. Opened by singing and prayer.
Pres. Amelia Goldsbrough made a few re-
marks on the object of the meeting and
the Financial Report which was read, after
which Pres. George Teasdale said the ob-
ject of these Quarterly Meetings are to
keep track of the business of the Society,
that you may all understand what property
you own; also to amalgamate your feelings,
that you may be enabled to work together
harmoniously. He also gave much good
counsel, and encouraged the sisters to per-
severe in the culture of silk, and wished
them to practice what they preach and as
far as possible wear home production; said
we should feel proud of our dress when we
did this. He encouraged the sisters to be
faithful in the discharge of every duty.
	Owing to the weather being very stormy
our meeting was not very well attended.
Meeting adjourned to the 21st of July.
Benediction by Pres. Amelia Goldsbrough.
	Second Quarterly Meeting of the Relief
Society, held in the Nephi Meeting House,
July 21st, 1878. Mrs. Amelia Goldsbrough,
President of Relief Society, presiding.
Counselors Mrs. Elizabeth Kendall and
Jane Pixton, Secretary Mrs. Mary Pitch-
forth, Corresponding Secretary Mrs. Amy
L. Bigler, Mrs. Aagard, President of Levan
Branch, Mrs. Stringham, of S. L. City,
Pres. Geo. Teasdale, Apostle Erastus Snow,
Patriarch Jacob G. Bigler, and several
others, both brethren and sisters, occupied
the stand.
	After singing, and prayer by Pres. Geo.
Teasdale, the Secretary read the minutes
of the last Conference, which were accepted,
and then the report of Nephi, Levan and
Mona Branches.
	President Goldsbrough then made a few
remarks, a portion of which are as follows:
	"I am pleased to meet with you at this
time. I am proud to represent the Relief
Society, and I feel determined, by the help
of my Father in Heaven, to discharge every
duty that devolves upon me. When I re-
ceived the Gospel, I made a covenant with
God that I would strive, with the help of
His Spirit, to keep His commandments. I
am here at this time because it is a duty,
and I earnestly request you, my sisters here
in Nephi, to come to meeting that you may
understand what we are doing. When the
Teachers go to visit, I want them to go as
comforters to the Saints, and not to beg; if
the people have anything to give they will
give it without asking for it. I pray God
to bless us all."
	Mrs. Susan Stringham then exhibited
a silk scarf and some skein-silk of home-
production.
	Pres. Mrs. Aagard, of Levan, then made
a few remarks, showing the willingness of
the sisters of that Branch to do all they
could to help roll an the great work in these
last days.
	Mrs. Hannah Jones was appointed to re-
ceive the fast day donations in the North
Ward, and Mrs. Sarah Ann Andrews to
continue to receive them in the South
Ward.
	Pres. George Teasdale then said: "The
Relief Society have been in the habit of
receiving and disbursing our fast day do-
nations, and I want them to continue to do

[Column 2]
so; I want a strict account kept of all re-
ceived and disbursed. I consider it no
disgrace to receive when we need; it is a
great blessing that we can obtain what we
need by asking for it. I am proud of my
sisters, and I pray God to bless our Socie-
ties and all who sustain them."
	Apostle Erastus Snow said: "I am pleased
that Bro. Teasdale encourages the sisters to
look after the poor; the sisters are more
capable of doing this than the brethren.
Many of our sisters labor hard to sustain
themselves and their children, and would
suffer and not complain, but they need the
help of the mothers in Israel in these mat-
ters. There are others who have never
been taught to work; they need experienced
sisters to teach them. Teach the people
industries. It is right to accumulate
riches, but we must not set our minds on
this world's goods. I like to hear the sis-
ters speak in these meetings, also in Fast
Meetings. You can work in your sphere.
A good, faithful mother will teach her sons
to keep the commandments of God; a
mother has great influence in her house;
she can sustain the husband and encourage
him to be a better man. Your works are
appreciated in Zion; there is no place that
the labors of the faithful are more needed
than in Zion. May God help you to be
valiant in the cause; I ask God to bless you.
Amen.
	Mrs. Sarah Ann Andrews then sang "My
own land Deseret.
	Dismissed by Elder Snow.
				AMY L. BIGLER, Cor. Sec'y.

', 'EDITOR EXPONENT:
	I think we are improving a little; as a
Society we number seventy members, about
forty average attendants.  Our meetings
are on the first Thursday of every month.
	The 24th of July was celebrated here in
a very pleasant manner; we had a dinner
provided at the schoolhouse, superintended
by the president of the Relief Society, Mrs.
Sarah Greenhatch, and the teachers, when
I believe every family in Bloomington were
invited to partake of the good things pro-
vided.  We had a meeting in the forenoon,
and were addressed by Presidents J. H.
Hart and George Osman, and Bishop
Hulme and others.  The Sunday School
children enjoyed themselves in the dance,
and we had a very good time.
	We have done very little about storing
grain yet; we have about thirteen bushels
of wheat and four hundred pounds of flour,
and intend, if we have health and strength,
to have more in a little while.  Our Presi-
dent has appointed a committee to purchase
wheat and store it up until a time of need.
A number of the young ladies of the Im-
provement Society, as well as some of the
older ones, go to the field and glean wheat
three days in each week, and they feel
blessed and happy in their work.  Bishop
Hulme called on the brethren to help the
gleaners by taking them in their wagons to
and from the field, and bringing their grain
home for them.
	Our financial condition for the past year
is as follows:

Disbursed to the Logan Temple............... $36 00
To the poor..................................................   28 60
Shares in Co-op store.................................   70 00

	The Lord has blessed the people in Bear
Lake Valley this year with a beautiful har-
vest.
	Praying that the blessing of God may at-
tend you, and that your beautiful little pa-
per may be more widely circulated, that
many others may rejoice in its good teach-
ings, we remain,
		SARAH GREENHATCH, President,
		ELIZABETH LANG, Secretary.

					EPHRAIM, Aug. 20th, 1878.
EDITOR EXPONENT:
	The following is our Quarterly Report.

Monthly donations in sundries.............$ 51 07
Cheese made by the sisters during
	the quarterly term 755 lbs @ 20.   153 00
Sunday eggs, 307 1/3 doz., @13 cts....     39 91
	Total income during the term  243 98 1/2

To poor, in storepay................................ 41 00
Rents......................................................... 10 00
Sundries................................................... 6 55
To Temple at Manti, 138 lbs of
	cheese @ 20 cts per lb.................... 27 60
Labor performed by J. Broderick........... 18 75
Paid to shares on the range................... 5 00
	Total  51 35

Total outlay from May 17 till Aug.
	17, 1878.............................................. 108 80
				S. A. Petersen, President,
				Ellen Madsen, } Counselors,
				Ellen Dorius,
				Agnes S. Armstrong, Sec'y.

				SANTAQUIN, Sept. 22d, 1878.
EDITOR EXPONENT:
	The Relief Society of this place held
their Tenth Annual meeting Sept. 5, 1878.
Our reports were all duly read by our Sec-
retary showing that we had received dur-
ing the year in donations, labor, &c., $210 82;
disbursed during the year to the Temple,
missionaries, the poor, and for charitable
purposes, $102 90.  A report of the past ten
years was also read, commencing Sept. 1st,
1868, ending Aug. 31st, 1878; showing that
we had received during the ten years, in
donations, dividends and labor, $1896 83;
disbursed during the ten years to the Tem-
ple, missionaries, the poor and for charit-
able purposes, $1092 29.
	Our meeting was well attended; we had
sisters present from Salt Lake City, Provo,
Goshen, Benjamin City, and Spring Lake.
The meeting was addressed by Mrs. M. T.
Smoot, Lucy B. Young, Pres. Stickney, and
our Bishop, George Halladay, all of whom
expressed their entire satisfaction at hear-
ing our accounts read, thinking for this
small place we were doing well, and alive
to our duties.  Our Schoolhouse was neatly
decorated for the occasion with articles of
home industry, consisting of quilts, cover-
lids, shawls, tidies, homespun cloth, and a
towel made of sweet clover, by Sister Mar-
garet Arleen, and also nearly three pecks
of cocoons were displayed for the inspec-
tion of our brethren and sisters, which ex-
cited a great deal of curiosity.
	We are striving in our weak way to do
all the good we can to help build up the
kingdom of God.  Wishing you great suc-
cess, we remain yours in the new and ever-
lasting covenant.
				E. J. STICKNEY, Pres't.
				E. CARTER, Sec'y.

			GLENWOOD, Sept. 2d, 1878.
EDITOR EXPONENT:
	Although slow to report ourselves, we are
not behind in the good work;  We number
nearly fifty members, average attendance
between twenty and thirty.  Within the
last four years we have donated.

St. George Temple................................ $25 75
Meeting House......................................  34 75
Welsh Emigration Fund........................  25 00
Manti Temple........................................  17 50
In Co-op Store.......................................  55 00
To the poor............................................ 7 00
	We have stored 161 bu. of wheat.  We
are endeavoring to do the best we can; this
is a new country, and there are no rich
among us, yet we are not poor.  The sisters
generally feel well.  We have the "Women
of Mormondom," and we take the EXPO-
NENT, which we find very interesting.
		Your sisters in the Gospel,
			Mary Ann Buchanan, President,
			Martha Sampson, } Counselors,
			Maria Powell,
			Maria Powell, Secretary,
			Amelia Buchanan, Treasurer.


']

['SILK  ASSOCIATION MEETING.

	THE Utah Woman’s Silk Association met
Thursday, April 4th, in the Assembly Hall,
Mrs. Salisbury in the chair. A large num-
ber of members and interested listeners
were present. The secretary’s report was
read and accepted.
	Mrs. E. B. Wells gave an interesting ac-
count of her visit to Washington in the in-
terest of the silk industry. She found sena-
tors and congressmen pretty well informed
with regard to the industry. A bill had

[Column 2]
passed the Senate at the last session of Con-
gress which provided for the establishment
of five silk experiment stations, but had
been lost sight of in the House, Mr. Cannon,
our present delegate, had promised to pre-
pare a bill to establish a silk experiment
station. Members of Congress assured
Mrs. Wells that such a bill properly pushed
would pass both houses. The paper which
Mrs. Wells read before the National Coun-
cil of Women gave a brief account of the
silk industry in Utah and aroused much in-
terest. At Atlanta Mrs. Wells spoke be-
fore the Women’s Board of Managers of the
Utah silk industry.
	Mrs. Zina D. H. Young spoke a few
words of encouragement to silk producers.
Let people who have little to do undertake
something of this kind, and they will be
happy and at the same time add to the re-
sources of the Territory. The quality of
our silk is so good that we would need but
few garments made of it. The children
could help in the care of the worms and
the young ladies can learn to reel.
	Mrs. Margaret Csine spoke of the suc-
cess of the women who raised cocoons last
year in St. George. They made about five
hundred dollars from cocoons. A fortune
cannot be made from the silk industry im-
mediately. No one would expect to take
up any trade or business and master all its
details at once. One must have experience
in any trade before it can become remunera-
tive. The cocoon to be of any value must
be of the best quality. No food must be
used for the worms except the mulberry
leaves. The worms, it was true, would
thrive on lettuce leaves or currant leaves,
but the object was not to raise worms but to
produce silk. The silk produced from any
kind of food but mulberry leaves is worth-
less. If the people of Utah do not take
up this industry others will do so. There
have already been inquiries from Italians,
Japanese and Hindoos as to the price of land
and the chances of success for a silk rais-
ing colony. The people of Utah should
keep this industry in their own hands.
The woolen mills of the Territory use every
year thousands of dollars worth of silk fill-
ing. We must learn to reel and keep this
money at home.
	Mrs F. S. Richards related some interest-
ing experiences in silk culture. She be-
lieved it a duty for women to take pride in
home made silk and home made clothes.
The women can in this practical way make
our Territory prosperous.
	Mrs. Ann Woodbury, of St. George, re-
ported that last year a good many pounds of
cocoons were raised in Washington county,
and this year still more will be raised.
The price realized for cocoons was about one
dollar a pound.
	Miss Harrison spoke of the art of reel-
ing, declaring it to be an easy one to learn.
She would gladly teach others what she had
learned. One can easily reel half a pound
of silk a day.
	Mrs. Gilmer advocated a systematic effort
to secure a market for home raised cocoons
and silk.
	The president promised that the matter
should receive immediate attention.
	Mrs. Salisbury read the articles which
might be exhibited at the Cotton exposition
at Atlanta. It would be most important to
make a good showing there. All articles
should be submitted to the committee ap-
pointed to take charge of the exhibit.
	Mrs. Wells moved that the association

[Column 3]
make an exhibit of Utah silk at Atlanta
The motion was unanimously carried.
	In response to a question with regard to a
supply of eggs, the president announced
that Miss Harrison has Japanese eggs for
sale at $2.50 a card, each card containing
thirty or forty thousand eggs.
	Adjourned to meet at the call of the
president.
CORINNE M. ALLEN, Sec.


', 'SILK CULTURE.

	WE are very happy to note the sudden
interest taken in the silk industry, and hope
it will lead to active work in such a way
that those who have labored so faithfully
will receive an impetus for more work and
a more thorough system will be adopted.
The beautiful portieres and furniture made
from the Utah silk awaked quite an interest
in the subject. Our own people were sur-
prised to discover how much had been done,
but the newer residents of Utah were most
surprised and greatly delighted. Mrs. C.
E. Allen recently prepared and read at an
entertainment given before the ladies of the
Unitarian Church, a very able article on
silk culture in Utah, and though in the last
issue we published an article on the same
subject by Mrs. Stringham, we believe a
synopsis of Mrs. Allen’s article will be in-
teresting to our readers:—
	“The opportunity to make an exhibit of
Utah silk in the woman’s building was
not given until just before the opening of
the fair, but the committee have labored
assiduously, and have obtained a consider-
able amount. Mrs. Caine has gone, up
and down the Territory collecting silk

[Column 2]
material, and the people have been very
generous in their response to her appeal for
contributions. She has collected dress
silk, pongee, lace, twist, reeled silk and
cocoons. Some of the dress material is
very beautiful, though made upon a hand
loom, the same that is used for weaving
rag carpet.
	No attempt will be made to obtain a prize
on manufactured goods, but it is believed
that Utah silk is of a very superior quality,
and if a prize can be obtained for our raw
material it will give a great impulse to the
industry.
	A very brief account of the silk industry
in other countries will help to show what
our Utah industry may become. The pro-
cess of rearing the silk worm, reeling the
thread and making it into cloth was dis-
covered in China 2,600 B. C.
	The knowledge of the art spread through
eastern Asia, before the Christian era. In
the year 555 A. D. two Nubian monks
brought silk worm eggs to Constantinople
and the Emperor Justinian established the
industry there. Sericulture spread rapidly
through Europe and became one of its chief
industries. In France at the present time
silk is manufactured to the value of one
hundred and twenty million francs annually
and from 800,000 to 1,000,000 operatives
are employed.
	Silk culture was introduced into America
in 1522 by Cortez. Georgia was founded
as a silk colony. John I. endeavored to
force Virginia to raise silk but they pre-
ferred to produce tobacco. Many of the
colonies made successful attempts at silk
culture, but during the revolution it was
entirely suspended. From 1825 to 1844
the interest revived and silk culture might
have become well established if it had not
been for the schemes of some jealous men.
The price of mulberry cuttings ran ’from
$3 a hundred up to $300 and even $500.
Many persons lost their entire fortunes.
	It was not long after this revival of inter-
est in sericulture that the Latter-day Saints
came to this valley. Brigham Young quickly
perceived how well adapted this climate
was to silk culture. He mentioned the fact
when fixing the site for the temple, and in
his first sermon in the valley spoke of
sericulture as one of the industries which
should make this people prosperous. He
sent to France for mulberry seed which he
had planted in his own garden, and person-
ally superintended the work. He gave
away cuttings to all who would take them.
Later on he sent to France for silk worm
eggs, distributing eggs and leaves to all
who cared to experiment with them.
About 1865 he built a cocoonery at Forest
Dale and placed a competent person in
charge. Soon after this, a systematic at-
tempt was made to introduce the industry
throughout the territory. Mrs. Zina D.
Young went from ward to ward explaining
the method of caring for the worms and
urging the importance of the industry.
	A general interest was soon awakened
and many trees were planted. When the
people were not able to build a cocoonery
they made a cocoonery of their houses for
the required six weeks in the spring,
literally turning themselves out of doors.
In 1876 the Deseret Silk association was
formed. An exhibit of beautifully reeled
silk was made at the Centennial which re-
ceived a diploma. At the Territorial fair
in 1877 there were exhibited numerous silk
fabrics—handkerchiefs, lace, stockings,

[Column 3]
artificial flower work, and dress silks of
various kinds.
	Soon after the Legislature appropriated
fifteen hundred dollars to encourage the in-
dustry. This sum was too small to equip
an adequate manufacturing plant and an
effort was made to obtain some capital by
forming the Utah Silk association. They
invested about seven thousand dollars .and
did a good business for a time. Utah county
still has a flourishing silk association and
produce some good material.
	Silk is an indispensible article of com-
merce. The demand for it is growing all
the time. If Utah could produce more of
the necessary raw material it would greatly
add to our prosperity.
	At the close of the talk there was ex-
hibited the sage green silk in the sage de-
sign by Mrs. Dallin: threads from the
material of the Utah curtain, spun silk, silk
from perforated cocoons, Utah and Japanese
cocoons and a fine piece of Utah silk which
had been in use constantly for fifteen years.”
—S. L. Herald.


', 'ST. GEORGE LETTER.

	A few words in regard to the silk indus-
try of this country might be of interest to
your readers. During the early part of
this year Miss Horrocks of Salt Lake City,
under the direction of the Utah Silk Com-
misson, taught fifteen married and twenty-
one unmarried ladies who took a course in
reeling the silk upon the half dozen Japan-
ese reels recently purchased from Japan.
The most of them were delighted with the
work and made rapid progress.
	Last spring there were twenty-six fami-
lies engaged in silk culture in the county.
The product so far reported amounts to
three hundred and sixty-four pounds of
cocoons. These when reeled will make
about ninety pounds of reeled silk.
A good reeler can reel this silk at the rate
of half a pound per day. Within the last
year we have had about ninety yards of
silk woven; it was not of the best quality
owing to the lack of experienced weavers,
we are pleased to say, however, that we
have now secured the services of a skillful
weaver, and we hope in the near future to
have some silk that we can put before the
market.
	There are a great many mulberry trees
in St. George, and in most of the Dixie set-
tlements there are a great many; they make
rapid growth for shade trees. In
St. George Miss Horrocks estimated


[Column 1]
there was enough to feed the worms to
make a thousand pounds of cocoons, and
every year there will be a great increase in
the number set out, owing to the increase
in the water supply since we have the
cottonwood water in town. It is not much
trouble to raise the trees when you can
keep them watered.
	There are many men looking toward the
silk industry as a source of revenue and
with that end in view they expect to plant
mulberries by the acre, and in talking with
influential men on this subject they ex-
press a desire to know what to do to ad-
vance it the most. Of course the right
kind of mulberry tree is the first considera-
tion. There has been an increased interest
in the work since we have used the Japa-
nese reels, and also since we have had silk
woven. Of course we have had many diffi-
culties to contend with, but in beginning
on a small scale and getting the silk into
cloth it showed the people that we meant
business and they can see what can be ac-
complished with a little capital. Expert
reelers must be the first consideration in es-
tablishing factories. Now we have quite a
quantity of reeled silk on hand and a large
number of good reelers. All they want is
encouragement. They ask: “What will we
do with the silk when we get it reeled?”
If we had the necessary means to buy the
reeled silk from those who have produced it,
it would encourage them to push the work.
There is not much encouragement to work
unless they can get such pay as they can
use, for they need it. There are not many
who can afford to reel it and lay it away.
	A young lady from one of the settlements
sent me three quarters of a pound of reeled
silk, very beautiful, and wanted to sell it.
We have such chances as this one often.
We give this industry all the encourage-
ment we can and intend to while life lasts,
or until we see it the blessing and pride of
this country.
			ANN C. WOODBURY.
THE SILK INDUSTRY.
	So much has been said on this subject
that when requested to write I thought,
“What more can I add to what has already
been said and written?” Still if I can add
one word of encouragement to lead our
young people to see the value of this in-
dustry I cheerfully do so.
	How many times I have been asked the
question, “Does it pay to raise silk?” and
I always answer, “Yes, it certainly does.”
But there are some things to be remembered.
First of all we must have mulberry trees, it
is just as necessary that we should have
trees to raise silk, as it is that we should
have land to raise grain; and, by the way,
this fall or next spring will be a good time
to plant trees, and let every one make it a
special point to plant several and in this
way a great deal would be done towards
advancing the cause of the silk industry.
	The Pioneer Nursery Company will be
glad to furnish mulberry trees from five to
fifteen feet high at prices from ten to thirty
cents each.
	After our trees have grown large enough,
which will be about three years, let each
one get right to work. Get just enough
eggs so that the worms can be well fed and
cared for, and in a short time you will be
rewarded by seeing them spin you a nice
lot of beautiful cocoons. The next step is
the reeling; this has been a great drawback
in the past, and how many days have

[Column 2]
spent in trying to get some one to reel my
silk and at last have it rendered unfit for
first class silk. But this is now all over-
come; through the earnest efforts of the Silk
Commission we are now enabled to obtain
reels and the law provides for a person to
teach the reeling at the expense of the State.
There is also bounty of twenty-five cents
on each pound of cocoons raised, which has
been provided by law and ought to arouse
an interest in silk culture.
	We have people in this State who under-
stand the different branches of this indus-
try, such as dyeing, cleansing etc., and as
soon as the demand is sufficient we hope
machinery will be obtained to make the
work lighter
	And now we do hope that those men and
women who are elected to represent us both
in Washington and in the State, whatever
their political belief, will appreciate the
efforts that have been made in this direction
and will give us aid, not only by their in-
fluence but will seek to have us helped in a
financial way. Let each one, no matter
what his or her political belief, seek to get
good men and women in office, who will
work to build up this important branch of
work and other home industries in the
State.
	Let me urge the mothers and daughters
to see that every mulberry tree in the State
is utilized in producing silk. Even now
we can produce a quantity and quality that
is surprising. Speaking of the latter, I
wish every one could see the difference in
the imported silk and our own. I had the
privilege recently of seeing pieces of the
imported and the homemade silk compared,
and really the imported hardly looked like
silk in comparison to our own homemade.
	Let us be more united in our efforts to
develop this industry, for it will be the
little that each one does that will make up
the whole.
			MARIA ZUNDELL.
SILK CULTURE PAST AND PRESENT IN
SANPETE.
	Since Utah has become a state the sub-
ject of home industry and especially seri-
culture has received more attention than
for some years previous, and with judicious
protection and encouragement there is no
good reason why it should not become a
source of wealth and independence to the
people of these mountain valleys.
	Owing to advanced age and infirmities I
am unable to take an active part in the
further development of this important in-
dustry, but if my experience in the past in
this line will be of any benefit to the young-
er generation I shall feel that I am not liv-
ing in vain.
	From my early childhood I have been
perfectly familiar with this business. In
my native state (Connecticut) the scanty
income which the farmers were enabled to
wring from the worn out soil was largely
supplemented by the proceeds of a small
cocoonery which nearly every family main-
tained in their own house. An unused,
vacant, airy chamber furnishing the neces-
sary accomodation for the worms with very
little expense, and very much more profit
than where large sums have been invested
in building expensive structures for this
purpose. So far as my experience goes
both in New England and Illinois, large
cocooneries have invariably resulted in loss
and failure.
	In the year 1873 I again engaged in silk

[Column 3]
culture at my home in Manti as an experi-
ment, and my success far excelled my anti-
cipations. I have demonstrated to my en-
tire satisfaction that the climate and atmos-
phere of Utah is far more favorable to the
health and development of the silk worm
than that of the eastern states. The dis-
ease superinduced by the continuous wet
weather does not exist in our dry climate,
and in fact the only obstacle in the way of
complete success is the present lack of suf-
ficient mulberry trees which can be easily
remedied wherever water is available. There
should be no lack of energy in this direc-
tion as an initial step in accomplishing
great results. But in this as in every other
undertaking patience and perseverance are
necessary to success. The silk worm sea-
son comes during the summer vacation
of the schools, thus giving the children
time and opportunity to pick the leaves and
assist in caring for the worms. If they are
familiar with them from infancy as I was
they do not feel the aversion toward them
that many of our young ladies manifest at
present. Also give the little ones to under-
stand that diligence on their part will be re-
warded when the silk is sold.
	In the year 1876 Sister E. R. Snow or-
ganized a silk association in Manti for San-
pete county, and for several years a con-
siderable amount of silk was produced in
Manti by Sister Maria Weibye and myself,
also in Ephraim by a Sister Peterson and
in Gunnison by Sister Helena Madsen.
All these sisters are deserving of much
credit for their pioneer labors in behalf of
silk culture. Sister Elizabeth Higgs has
also been indefatigable in her labors as silk
agent, canvassing the town and urging up-
on the sisters the immediate necessity of
planting mulberry trees.
	In one season I made sufficient silk for a
dress but it was unfortunately spoiled in
the weaving. Two years ago my grand-
daughter raised sufficient cocoons to obtain
$2.25 bounty. She also sold them to Mrs.
Margaret A. Caine for the Omaha Exposi-
tion. She is a teacher in the public schools
but she thinks she can raise silk during her
summer vacation. I hope that many more
young ladies may feel impressed to go and
do likewise. I feel that there is a great
future for this State if our people will only
be wise in their day and generation, and by
wise legislation and individual effort care-
fully utilize the elements within our reach,
which will tend to make us a prosperous
and independent people.
	Let us hope that our new legislature
will give this important subject the atten-
tion it deserves during the coming session.
Let our sisters realize that the silk indus-
try will open a new field of occupation for
their children, which will be the means of
adding many comforts to the homes of those
who are in straightened circumstances if
they avail themselves of the opportunities
offered. Let our young ladies see to it that
their adornment shall be the work of their
own hands and not the products of Babylon.
	And above all let us realize the purpose
for which we have come to this promised
land, and strive diligently to become the
self-sustaining people the Lord designs us
to be.
		REBECCA WAREHAM.
ENCOURAGING SILK CULTURE.
	One of the first acts of the legislature of
Utah after Statehood had been declared, was
the passing of a bill to encourage silk cul-


[Column 1]
ture in the new state. No act could have
been more commendable. In doing this
those who are the leaders of thought, de-
clared their interest in the welfare of the
people at large. They showed their belief
in a great principle that it is the duty of
the State to take the responsibility of en-
couraging industry, of assisting the isolated
and dependent to united action which shall
result to the good of all. The personnel of
the commission appointed by Governor
Wells to carry out the idea of the act of the
legislature were a guarantee that the inten-
tion of the bill would be faithfully adhered
to. Mrs. Zina D. H Young, Mrs. C. W.
Bennett, Mrs. M. A. Caine and two others,
one in St. George and one in Springville,
should be forever held in grateful remem-
brance by the people of Utah for the con-
scientious way in which they have executed
their trust. It is gratifying to note the
great increase of interest in the silk culture
in Utah from year to year since the silk
bill was passed. The increase of one hun-
dred dollars in bounties this last year
means a great increase in production. May
another year bring still greater increase.
	If the women of the State generally
will give as much time and labor to raising
cocoons as the women of the Silk Commis-
sion have given to the necessary administra-
tive work connected with the distribution of
eggs and testing the cocoons, in a few
years the silk industry will be on the road
to permanent establishment.
	It is interesting to go back in history
over the story of the growth of the produc-
tion of silk fabrics.
	The process of rearing the worms, reel-
ing the thread and weaving the cloth is
said to have been discovered in China, 2600
B. C. The secret of silk culture was
guarded most jealously by the Chinese. It
was death to reveal the process to a foreign-
er, and so perfectly was the will of the
government in this matter carried out, that
it was not until 555 A. D., that the world
east of China had any knowledge of the
precious art. At that time two Mesbian
monks brought silk worm eggs concealed
in their staves to the Emperor Justinian at
Constantinople. Silk culture immediately
became established in Europe.
	France has become the greatest silk pro-
ducing country because there the govern-
ment has done most to foster and protect
the industry. At one time silk workers
were exempt from taxation and every in-
ducement was offered to engage in the pro- 
duction of cocoons. The wisdom of this
national policy in France has been proved
by the result. At the present time in
France silk is manufactured at the value of
one hundred and twenty million francs an-
nually, and from 800,000 to 1,000,000 oper-
ators are employed. It is hardly too much
to say that the silk industry in France
makes the difference in that country be-
tween prosperity and the want of it.
	Efforts in America to establish silk cul-
ture have been numerous but spasmodic.
Georgia was established as a silk colony, but
the vicissitudes of those early colonists are
sufficient explanation of the failure of the
project. Before the war of the Revolution
many of the colonists were successfully en-
gaged in silk culture, but when the war be-
gan the industry was entirely suspended.
From 1825 to 1844 there was a great reviv-
al of interest in silk culture throughout the
country, but one of those inexplicable ex-
citements to which nations are subject, tem-

[Column 2]
porarily suppressed the industry. The
gambling in the stocks of mulberry cuttings
reached a point only equalled by the tulip
craze in Holland. Many men lost their en-
tire fortunes and every one was foolishly
turned from the idea of engaging in silk
culture.
	The people of Utah are quite familiar
with the history of sericulture in this State.
There has been a constant attempt on the
part of the leaders of the people to guide
them to industries of all kinds which shall
make them useful and prosperous. Silk
culture is an important industry to which.
Utah is well adapted by its climatic condi-
tions. What has already been done in silk
culture in Utah is a promise that great
things can be accomplished in the future.
The time should be near at hand when all
the silk required in the various cloth manu-
factories in Utah should be supplied at
home. The thousands of dollars then earned
will help to educate our children, build
comfortable houses, improve our roads and
eventually prove one of the foundation
stones upon which the prosperity of the
State shall rest.
	Let every woman who raises a few pounds
of cocoons in her little home feel that she
is a factor in a great enterprise which the
State is undertaking for the good of us all.
As she watches the wonderful mystery of
the assimilation of the fibers of the mul-
berry leaves and the spinning of the golden
web, may she feel that this wonderful pro-
cess of nature is to be made use of for com-
munity life. The perfect adaptability of
God’s plan of nature to the needs of man,
to my mind, has never been more clearly
indicated than in the development of the
silk industry. May the State continue to
foster it and patriotic women continually re-
spond to the efforts of the State.
']

["UTAH WOMAN’S PRESS CLUB.
FIRST DECADE OF ITS ORGANIZATION.
	By special invitation the December meet-
ing was held at the residence of Mrs. I.
Cameron Brown, at which, by request, our
charming hostess favored the club with a
realistic and entertaining paper on the
“Life and Works of Frances Burney.”
	The names af Mrs. S. O. B. Cummings,
Mrs. Rachel Miller and Dr. Orielle Curtis
were presented for membership. Vote car-
ried. In behalf of the club a vote of
thanks was tendered Mrs. Brown, who has
upon different occasions entertained the
club so generously.
	At the January meeting, on motion of
C. C. R. Wells, the president appointed
Mesdames R. B. Pratt, E. R. Shipp and
L. D. Alder a committee to prepare suitable
resolutions, congratulating the U. W. P.
Club, and Mrs. I. Cameron Brown (one of
its members) who is at present the recipient
of Congressional honors. Another motion
was made that the same committee make
honorable mention of the 75th birthday of

[Column 2]
Zina D. H. Young, one of our members,
and honored president of the N. W. R. S.,
and that such resolutions be sent to the
ladies named, and be recorded in the
minutes. Carried.
	Mrs Antoinette Brown Kinney was re-
ceived as a member.
	A paper on the Single Tax was read by
Mrs. R. B. Pratt, which caused much
lively discussion.
	At the February meeting Mrs. Pratt
continued her paper on the same subject,
the discussion again becoming interesting,
and on motion it was continued to another
meeting. The subject of Parliamentary
Law was also continued.
	The president stated that she had re-
ceived a letter from the Countess Annie d’
Montaigu, asking for information concern-
ing the club, to publish in an Eastern
magazine. This shows that the club is
becoming well known. The name of
Margaret A. Caine appears at this meeting.
	At the March meeting Miss S. L. Monroe
was received as a member.
	By previous invitation the April meet-
ing was held at the residence of Mrs. S. L.
Dickinson. Dr. Elliot was introduced as
the guest of the evening. She entertained
the club with interesting facts in the life
and labors of Miss Barton. Gave the
origin of the Red Cross. A letter was
read from Mrs. Brown, written in Washing-
ton.
	Miss Sybil Stewart was the guest at
the June meeting. Mrs. Emma J. Mc-
Vicker was received as a member.
	July 1, the June meeting was held at
the residence of the president, Dr. Ellis
R. Shipp. It was a delightful summer
evening, and the occasion was one long to
be remembered. We were honored by the
presence, as visitors, of Mrs. Jennings,
president of the Utah Federation of
Women’s Clubs, of local fame, and the
noted suffrage speaker of national reputa-
tion, Miss Elizabeth Upham Yates. Mrs.
McVicker spoke on her visit to Denver,
after which the visitors spoke interestingly
at some length.
	Mrs. I. Cameron Brown gave a talk on
her recent sojourn in Washington. At the
earnest request of the club Miss Yates gave
a graphic and vivid account of her visit in
China.
	In response to the kindly invitation of
Mrs. Rachel Miller the July meeting was
held at her home, Mrs. Mary J. Lambert
and Mrs. Martha Miller were received as
members. A critic was appointed. This
had long been the desire of the club. Mrs.
McVicker was the first to hold this posi-
tion.
	An invitation from the Ladies’ Literary
Club was read to the U. W. P. C. mem-
bers to be present at their opening day
exercises, which would include a reception
to the retiring president.
	At the September meeting Dr. Curtis
gave an interesting account of her summer
vacation. Mrs. Mary J. Lambert, after
reading a poem on the beautiful Island of

[Column 3]
Hawaii, by request spoke in that musical
language.
	The annual meeting held in October was
devoted to character representations. The
officers for the ensuing year were also in-
stalled.
	The names of Maria Miller Johnson,
Julia Ivins McDonald and Augusta Joyce
Crocheron were presented as candidates for
honorary membership, and received by
unanimous vote. A committee was
appointed to prepare a book of all the
officers and members of the club, past and
present.
	At the November meeting Miss Mae
Taylor was received as a member.
	At the December meeting Mrs. Emily
S. Richards was received as a member.
	The February meeting, 1897, was held
at the home of its president, Mrs. Ruth
M. Fox. The birthday of our honored
Mrs. E. B. Wells was fittingly observed.
Mrs. Wells gave a talk on her Eastern
trip, and the club presented her with a
small token of esteem and appreciation.
	Dr. Ellis R. Shipp entertained the club
for the March meeting at her home on
Center Street. Miss A. M. Beecher was
the guest of honor. A large number, of
guests and club members were present to
greet her. In response to the request of
the ladies Miss Beecher gave an informal
talk on topics of interest to all; Economics,
the influence of women in politics, and
the political status of the Eastern woman,
each was touched upon.
	At the June meeting a program in honor
of Queen Victoria’s 60th Jubilee was car-
ried out as follows: “Victoria as a girl,
Mrs. E. S. Wilcox; Victoria as a wife, Dr.
R. B. Pratt; Victoria as a mother, Mrs.
M. A. Freeze; Victoria as a queen, Mrs. E
B. Wells; Victoria’s Jubilee poem, Mrs.
L. D. Alder. Mrs. Wells presented an
invitation from the National Press Club,
asking the U. W. P. C. to send a delegate
to the meeting to be held at Nashville,
October 25.
	The July program of 1897 was in com-
memoration of the Utah pioneers. The
first paper, “The Pioneers,” was by Miss
Laura Hyde, “The Jubilee” was by Mrs.
Fox. Dr. Orielle Curtis was appointed
special correspondent for the Review, by
request of its editor, Mrs. Bradley.
		(Continued.)


Woman's Exponent.
The Ballot in the Hands of the Women of Utah should be a Power to better the Home, the State and the Nation
SALT LAKE CITY, UTAH, MARCH 1902 
No. 11


", "FIRST DECADE OF ITS ORGANIZATION

	The U. W. P. C. was organized October
31, 1891, at the office of the Woman's
Exponent. A number of women writers
met together, as a result of letters which
had been sent to call a meeting.
	Mrs. E. B. Wells was chosen temporary
chairman and Mrs. Julia I. McDonald
secretary pro tem. The permanent organ-
zation was effected, and Mrs. E. B. Wells
was elected President, Susa Y. Gates,
First Vice-President and L. Tula Greene
Richards Second Vice-President, Martha A.
Y. Greenhalgh, Vice-President-at-large,
Annie Wells Cannon Cor. Sec.; Josephine
Spencer, Asst. Cor. Sec.; Dr. Ellis R. Shipp,
Rec. Sec.; Ruth M. Fox, Treasurer; Dr.
Romania B. Pratt, Auditor.
	The name, Utah Woman’s Press Club,
was adopted and the Chair appointed a
committee of three to prepare a constitution
and by-laws. Susa Young Gates of Provo,
Romania B. Pratt, Salt Lake, and Lucy A.
Clark Farmington were the committee.
	It was suggested that at the first meeting
to be held November 30, 1891, each mem-
ber contribute an original article or ad-
dress, or such a selected article as would
further the object for which the club was
organized, or be subject to a fine not to ex-
ceed twenty-five cents.
	The charter members were: E. B. Wells,
Susa Y. Gates, Romania B. Pratt, Ellis R.
Shipp, L. L. Greene Richards, Julia A.
McDonald, Lucy A. Clark and Ruth M. Fox:
	At the meeting held November 30,
Mrs. E. B. Wells made an address and three
original articles and three original poems
were read  They were, first, from the pen
of Susa Y. Gates, “The Local Literary
Field for Women.” A poem, “The
Mother Author,” Lula G. Richards. An
article on the “Suffrage Question” by
Sarah M. Kimball. Julia I. McDonald,
“The Poet’s Ideal Woman.” Ruth M.
Fox, an original poem, “Greeting,” and
Ellis R. Shipp, “Love Divine.”
	At the next meeting it was stated that
the object of the Club was purely literary,
and all foreign topics would be excluded.
	At this meeting, Miss N. V. Davis, who
afterwards contributed so largely to the
edification of the Club and charmed us
with her bright originality, was admitted
as a member, and is mentioned as telling a
very pleasing Christmas story and singing
the German Christmas hymn and a German
song.
	Miss Gladys Woodmansee is spoken of
as entertaining the Club with some fine
instrumental selections on the piano.
	At the third meeting, held January 30,
1892, Mrs. C. C. R. Wells is mentioned.


[Column 1]
President E. B. Wells read a communica-
tion from Washington, in reference to
uniting with the International Federation
of Press Clubs, for the consideration of the
ladies. Spoke of the advantage to be
gained in press work and extended oppor-
tunities at the coming World’s Fair.
	A motion was made that the club join
the Federation. Carried.
	The name of Louise Boyden appeared at
this date, who afterward added so much to
the enjoyment of the Club by her really
very fine elocutionary powers. The
minutes state that five new members were
received at this meeting.
	February 27 it is reported that eight new
members were admitted, and the names
of Phebe C. Young, Ellen Jakeman L.
L. Dalton and Julia P. M. Farnsworth
app ear.
	According to pre-arrangement of some of
the ladies the meeting took the form of a
birthday gathering to our time-honored
president and founder of the Club, E. B.
Wells. A token of love and esteem was
presented, congratulatory speeches wer,e
made and poems were read by each mem-
ber present, suitable to the occasion ex-
pressing tender love and affection, and true
appreciation of the one each desired to
honor. Mrs. Wells responded in fitting
words of thanks, saying: “Do not over-
estimate me, but love me all you will, for
love will yet rule the world.”
	At the March meeting the name of Mary
A Freeze appears as author of “The Bene-
fits of the Club, a very instructive article.
The names of Lillie T. Freeze and Miss
Genevieve L. Brown are recorded. In the
July minutes the name of L L. Dickinson
appears as the author of a poem, “The
Dreams of Childhood.”
	At the annual meeting, October 31, the
names of Mrs. Georgina Young and Dr.
Maggie C. Shipp are recorded as being
received as members of the Club, and
Josephine Spencer read an original poem,
The discussion of a pin or badge for mem-
bers is mentioned.
	Miss N. V. Davis spoke of the work of
the Club. Thought if we set apart certain
evenings for special subjects it would be
advantageous, and said: “That I am al-
ways present shows that I enjoy the time
spent here, and when I go away from
Utah I feel I shall know something of the
people.”
	January 31, 1893, Augusta Joyce Crocli-
eron is mentioned as contributing an article,
also Mrs. M. A. Y. Greenhalgh and Mrs
Ella W. Hyde and Miss May Preston were
received as members.
	Mrs. L. L. Dickinson entertained the
Club at a Valentine party in February,
1893, at her pleasant home. She com-
posed a poem for the occasion and very
artistically arranged everything for tile
enjoyment of her guests, serving refresh-
ments, exhibiting her large collection of
Indian relics and proving herself a charm-
ing hostess.
	March 22. 1893, Lydia D. Alder was re-
ceived as a member. The badge committee

[Column 2]
made a final report, submitting two designs
to select from; one with a bee and one with
a Greek lamp. It was decided to have a
gold pin with the Greek lamp, with U.
W. P. C. in raised letters. There is no
mention as to when the Club colors were
chosen, but they are lavender and sage
green. Mrs. Mamie Freeze was received
as a member.
	At the April meeting President E. B.
Wells spoke regarding the U. W. P. C.
becoming associated with the Territorial
Federation of Clubs. A communication
from the Federation explained some of the
benefits to be derived from the connection.
It was decided by unanimous vote that we
join the Federation.
	Article II., of the by-laws, was moved
by vote. Mrs. Lizzie Wilcox, Mrs. Ida
Haag and Miss Mamie Conrad received as
members. By unanimous vote Mrs. E. B.
Wells was sustained as delegate from the
Club to the Press Convention of Women to
be held in Chicago during the World’s
Fair, and Miss May Preston named as
alternate. Mrs. Maggie Bassett is men-
tioned as favoring the Club with some
choice music and Miss Pearl Russell as
singing very sweetly.
	At the May meeting little Ellis Shipp is
spoken of as very graphically reciting the
“Prairie Fire.”
	At the July meeting President Wells
was again in the chair. Mrs. Zina D. H.
Young, Mrs. Elmina S. Taylor and Miss
Ada Patterson were received as members.
Miss Maude Pratt is mentioned as favoring
the Club with some choice music, one
selection from “Il Trovatore.”
	“The Sixth Sense” was discussed with
much interest. 
	An article appears in the EXPONENT of
September 1, 1893, which speaks of the
women in Journalism in the Rocky
mountains, most of whom are members of
the Press Club. It is from the pen of the
editor, E. B. Wells, and was written for
the World’s Fair held in Chicago in 1893.
			 (Continued.)


WOMAN'S EXPONENT.


[Column 1]
", 'HISTORY OF THE U. W. PRESS CLUB.
		(Continued from page 64.)
	A number of the Club members appeared
in costume, among the characters represent-
ed, Helen of Troy, Penelope, The Empress
Eugenie, Spring and a Puritan Mother.
	At the December meeting of the Club,
original papers were read on the customs of
celebrating Christmas in other lands. Ger-
many by Mrs. Lillian Maeser, France and
India by Mrs. Mary F. Kelly, New England
States by Mrs. E. B. Wells.
	At the January meeting, 1905, papers were
read, one by Mrs. M. J. Snow on “The Youth
of George Washington,” and one on “The
Friendship of Lafayette and Washington” by
Mrs. Lillian Maeser. Original poem, “Betsy
Ross and the Flag,” by Mrs. Susa Y. Gates.
Two dollars were contributed to the Utah Fe-
deration of Women’s Clubs, for the Travel-
ing Library of the U. F. W. Clubs.
	The February meeting was held at the
home of Mrs. E. S. Wilcox, where both the
Press and Reapers Club were guests, in honor
of the birthday of their founder Mrs. E. B.
Wells. Sixty ladies were present. The pro-
gram, consisted of the reading of poems from
Mrs. Wells own volume of verse, “Musings
and Memories,” songs and recitations with a
love gift to the guest of honor.
	At the May meeting Mrs. Jensen read a
very able paper on the war between Russia
and Japan, from the Russian standpoint.
	Mrs. Wilcox addressed the Club, more par-
ticularly on the wonderful record, of the lit-
tle Chinese girl, Lillie King, at the Fremont
school, who had passed all competitors by
two per cent.
	At the September meeting, Mrs. E. B.
Wells, spoke upon the early history of the
U. W. Press Club, and impressed upon its
members the importance of its position as
one of the National Women’s Clubs. Mrs.
Francis read a paper on “The Idealism of
the Home.” An original poem, “The Burgo-
master of Berne,” was read by its author, Mrs.
Kelly. Two representatives were appointed
to attend the Convention to be held at
Springville, namely Mrs. Francis, President,
and Mrs. E. S. Wilcox. Mrs. Lydia Alder
was specially invited to speak of women in
other lands by Mrs. Kinney.
	The Annual Meeting of the Club was held
in October, at the home of Dr. E. R. Shipp.
	The election of officers, resulted in Mrs.
Emma Jensen as President; Mrs. M. F. Kelly,
First Vice President; Mrs. Lillian Maeser,
Second Vice President; Mrs. Margaret Caine,
Secretary; Mrs. M. J. Snow, Cor. Secretary;
Florence Critchlow, Treasurer.
	A program consisting of sketches of women
of Biblical times, was well rendered.
	At the November meeting of the Club,held
in the Exponent office, Dr. Ellis R. Shipp
addressed the Club, voicing her gratitude to
Mrs. Wells as the founder of the Press Club,
and her untiring efforts, in encouraging
mental development in the women of Utah.
Mrs. Mary E. Howell was appointed as de-
legate to the George Washington Memorial
Association meeting to be held in Washing-
ton D. C., February 1906.
	The December meeting of the Club was
held at the home of Mrs. Francis and Mrs.
Kelly. Original poems on Christmas and
other subjects were read by Dr. E. R. Shipp.
Mrs. Kelly read Pax Vobiscum, her poem,
which had received first honorable mention,
in the Deseret News Prize Competition.
	The January meeting, 1906, of the Club
was held at the home of Prest. Mrs. Jensen.
Mrs. Jensen favored the company with an

[Column 2]
excellent talk on the Bloodless War between
Norway and Sweden, giving the history of
these countries back for several centuries,
showing how the kingdoms came to be unit-
ed, and the reasons which made a disunion
without enmity possible. Mrs. Jensen gave
a fine paper on Christmas in Norway.
	Miss Eva Jensen presented a pretty pict-
ure, dressed in the peasant costume of Swe-
den.
	Music, recitations and refreshments, added
to the pleasure of the many guests.
	The February meeting was held at the
Exponent office, and was devoted to cele-
brating the birthday of its Honorary Presi-
dent and Founder Mrs. E. B. Wells. A paper
by Mrs. R. M. Fox, referred to Mrs. Wells
womanliness, and the noble rolls of wife-
hood and motherhood.
	One of Mrs. Wells’ daughters, Annie Wells
Cannon, paid a touching tribute to the truth
of the statements of Mrs. Fox.
	Mrs. M. J. Snow spoke of Mrs. Wells’ loy-
alty to every form of women’s work, for the
up-building of humanity, also as to her work
as a suffragist, and in the Woman’s Clubs.
	Mrs. Lydia D. Alder (but recently returned
from her extended trip abroad, whither she
had gone as a speaker to the Quinquennial
Congress, held in Berlin, June 1904) spoke
especially on Mrs. Wells’ work in literature,
referring particularly to her work as editor of
the WOMAN’S EXPONENT; and of their being
together in England, whither they had gone
as members of the Quinquennial Congress,
held in London, June 1899.
	Mrs. Hattie Jensen, Mrs. M. J. Snow, and
Miss Sarah Eddington were received as
members.
	The March meeting was held at the home
of Mrs. Florence Critchlow; Mrs. M. J. Snow
was elected secretary. The Club was called
upon, by the State Federation of Club’s, that
each member contribute a book on American
history or literature, to aid in replacing the
library, which was destroyed at Sunnyside,
by fire in November last.
	A sketch of the life of Eliza R. Snow, was
ably given by Mrs. Roundy.
	Mrs. Hattie Jensen spoke of the patriotic
fervor, expressed in Miss Snow’s poems.
	Mrs. Lillie Maeser spoke of her as poetess,
especially referring to her most popular
poem, “O, My Father.”
	The April meeting of 1906 was held at the
home of Dr. E. R. Shipp. Selections from
the writings of the poetess Hannah T. King
were read. Mrs. Lydia D. Alder gave an in-
teresting sketch of her life referring to the
noble stature and bearing of this gifted
woman, and of their intimate friendship
which had lasted over many years. The
death of Gladys Woodmansee, a member of
the Club, a bright sparkling, precious woman,
was announced. Also the death of Mrs. E.
J. Stevensen. A motion was made and car-
ried that the committee appointed by the
Club, draft resolutions of respect. Mrs. Alder
for Gladys Woodmansee, Dr. Shipp, Mrs.
Stevensen.
	The September, 1906, meeting was held
at the home of Mrs. Francis. Mrs. Wood-
mansee was to have been guest of honor,
but was too ill to be present. A number of
her old friends, girlhood friends in England
were present, among them Mrs. Phebe Sloan,
and Mrs. Margaret Young, were present by spe-
cial invitation. Many incidents of interest
were related, the flood of years rendering up
many treasures of the past. The poems read
during the evening were selected by Mrs.
Woodmansee herself, yet all regretted her

[Column 3]
enforced absence from the bosom of her
friends.
	The October, 1906 meeting of the Press
Club was held at the home of its President,
Mrs. E. H. Jensen. Mrs. E. B. Wells was
called to the Chair. A report of the year’s
work was read by the President, and Mrs.
Zina Y. Card was received as a member.
	Mrs. E. B. Wells spoke of the George
Washington Memorial; also said that the
shares held by the Press and Reaper’s Clubs,
gave us an interest in the Woman’s Memo-
rial Association at Washington; also stated
that Utah was the first state in the Union,
that paid a subscription toward the George
Washington Memorial, and that it was col-
lected from the children of the Public Schools
of Utah. An original poem, “What is the
Best for us to do?” was read by Dr. E. R.
Shipp. Mrs. Minnie J. Snow spoke at some
length of her visit to the State Council
of Women at Ohio, which numbers some
800,000 women, some of whom make this
their life work, spending fortunes in the
cause.
	The club officers for the coming year were
Mrs. Minnie J. Snow, President; Dr. E. R.
Shipp, First Vice President; Mrs. Maria
Francis, Second Vice President; Mrs. Flor-
ence S. Critchlow, Rec. Secretary; Mrs. E.
H. Jensen, Treasurer.
	Mrs. E. B. Wells moved that a new office
be created by the Club, that of Historian,
and named Mrs. L. D. Alder for the office,
as she has written the Club History for the
first decade. Moved and carried that this
motion be acted upon later, which was done
agreeable to Mrs. Wells’ suggestion.
	This closed the history of the Club for the
past five years. Many of its old members
still attend, and give of their best efforts for
its up-building. That the Press Club is dear
to “Aunt Em’s” heart, goes without saying,
and that true and lasting friendships have
grown out of the associations formed in its
members by delving into the world of
Thought and Events. “Birds of a feather,
flock together” was said of old. We have
proven it to be still true, and the reason,
that we see and understand alike, the re-
quisite of the true friendship.

The pen is mightier than the sword,
The written than the spoken word,
The written-unborn ages sway,
The spoken, breezes drift away,
So full Thoughts store house, it o’erflows,
Yet ’tis not less for all one knows;
Outstretch thine hand, Thought’s flowers are fair,
Pluck all thou wilt, none less are there.
					LYDIA D. ALDER.


']

Name: 24_river_trees_miles_mountains
Representative_Docs:
['AN INTERESTING LETTER.
	NORTHAMPTON, MASS.,
					July 12, 1904,
Dear Sister Wells:
	Being in your native state, my thoughts
turn to you and to my friends who left this
beautiful place to assist in building up the
barren country to which the Lord directed
His people
	Acording to your wish, I will give you
a brief account of our travels. My daugh-
ter and myself left our home June 1 to
visit Canada, being invited by my nephew,

[Column 2]
John W. Wolf, to accompany himself and
wife to their home in Cardston on their re-
turn from the June conference.
	After a very pleasant trip of two days we
arrived in Sterling June 14, where we
were met at the depot by Brother Brandley.
After partaking of his kind hospitality over
night, we proceeded on to Cardston, where
we were welcomed by relatives and friends.
We improved the time for two weeks vis-
ting and looking at the country, which,
at this time of the year, presents a very
beautiful appearance, the weather being as
pleasant as we could desire. The people
were busily engaged preparing for “Do-
minion Day,” which is the 1st of July.
	As we had our trip arranged for the
Eastern States, we did not participate in
the celebration. Starting on our tour June
27th, we took the Great Northern train
and sped along through northern Montana,
Dakota and Minnesota without changing
cars or stopping off until we reached St.
Paul, viewing as we passed the large
ranches of Montana, on which were com-
paratively small houses, which were
changed for the immense grain fields with
beautiful large houses, those which appear-
ed to us as ideal farm homes. The coun-
try looked like one great, green field as far
as the eye could see, now and then passing
a village or city.
	At St. Paul we changed cars en route for
Chicago. The country now presents a dif-
ferent appearance as we come to the beautiful
dells and lakes of the Wisconsin, which de-
lighted the eye for hours.
	We arrived at Chicago Thursday even-
ing, June 30th, where we were met by
Brother Ellsworth, who is laboring in the
missionary field. Friday and Saturday we
were engaged taking in some of the sights
of Chicago, visiting the University grounds,
where we met friends from home; also
visited some of the large buildings, parks,
etc , and enjoyed a delightful ride on Lake
Michigan. Sunday we attended meetings,
where we were kindly greeted by Elders
and Saints, we also attended a street meet-
ing, where we listened to Elders and sisters
proclaim the plan of life and salvation to
the people as they passed by. Now and
then one would stop and listen for a while,
then go on, and others would stop and lis-
ten until the meeting closed; occasionally
there would be a disturbance by some one
calling out in disrespectful terms, but the
Elders went on unheeding, so that those
who did desire might hear the truth.
	Being invited by President Asahel
Woodruff, we remained in Chicago to
spend the 4th of July, which was Monday.
We accompanied himself and family to
Washington Park, where Elders, Saints
and students arranged to celebrate. The
day was an ideal one. The beginning of
the programme seemed to be handshaking
and introducing one to another, as many
of the party were from different parts of
Zion, who were delghted to meet a friend
so far from home, while others were just
as pleased to meet a brother or a sister in
the Gospel, which is especially appreciated
in the world, where there are few in num-
ber.

[Column 3]
	About 1 o’clock cloths were spread upon
the green grass, and all partook of a boun-
teous lunch prepared by the sisters, after
which a meeting was called by Brother
Ellsworth, Marshal of the day, which con-
sisted of speeches, recitations and songs
suitable to the occasion, followed by sports
of different kinds, racing, games, boat rid-
ing, etc. After enjoying a very pleasant
day we bade the party good-by and took
the train en route for Massachusetts and
Maine, stopping, however, to view the
beautiful scenery of Niagara. Continuing
our travels we arrived at Boston, where we
remained but one day. We visited Bunker
Hill monument, Harvard University
grounds, and called on the Elders who
were laboring there, as well as all other
Elders we visited, they were feeling well
and enjoying the spirit of their calling.
Meeting with the Elders was among the
most enjoyable events of our trip, as we
felt we had met a friend and a brother.
	The evening of the 7th we took the
steamship for Portland, Maine, President
Martin, who had charge of this part of the
mission, accompanying us.
	We landed at Portland about 8 o’clock
in the morning, where we were kindly
received by (my husband’s) relatives. We
called on a sister of Grandma Foss, as she
was formerly known, who was also a sister
of Aunt Phoebe Woodruff, our late Presi-
dent Woodruff’s departed wife, Mrs. Shuah
Moulton, who has attained the age of nine-
ty-three years. She was in good health
and spirits for one of her age; was pleased
to hear of her sisters’ families who left
their homes here so many years ago. We
were indeed pleased to see her; she re-
membered all of her departed sisters; in-
quired about their famiies, calling them by
name. She has a family of useful children
around her—a son who is a leading lawyer
in Portland, and two daughters, one being
a school teacher. She told us how she had
striven to educate them and keep them
around her, as she was left a widow when
they were young. She seemed interested
in the current topics of the day, on which
she conversed. Also the signs of the times
as referred to in the scripture. which
seemed to her were pointing to the last
days.
	Our time being limited we did not stay
long, as we had an appointment to meet
Miss Foss, my husband’s cousin, who was
going to show us around Scarborough and
Soco, from which places my husband’s
mother and grandmother came We started
on our ride about 11 o’clock and continued
until 6 o’clock. The scenery of the coun-
try was a change for us. being hills and
hollows, with large tracts of woods, with
here and there a farm house and a patch of
green grass around it, which was the farm,
with generally an orchard. The grass,
however, was tall and thick, vegetation
being very luxuriant everywhere. Among
the woods are beautiful ferns and flowers.
	We visited the home of Gradma Foss a[-]d
Aunt Phoebe Woodruff, also the birthplace
and childhood home of my husband’s
mother. The houses are in good condition,
one of them being over a hundred years


[Column 1]
old, and looks as fresh as though it had
just been completed. A striking feature
of these homes is their freshness, being
painted often to preserve them, as they are
made of wood. Most of them are painted
white, and look very pretty among the
green trees. We also visited the family
cemetery. After visiting the beautiful
places here we were driven to “Prout’s
Neck,” a beautiful resort within a stone's
throw of the ocean, owned by Ira Foss and
his two sisters, my husband's cousins. 
There were about one hundred guests at
the resort, which is commodious enough to 
accommodate two hundred, the grounds
with the woods near by are very beautiful.
For the first time in my life I gathered 
shells on the sea shore and heard the waves
dash against the rocks. In the evening
the family gathered in their parlors, where
the time was pleasantly spent in relating
incidents of progenitors who lived about
here so many years ago. Next morning
we bade goodby to friends, and to one of
the most beautiful places it has been my
pleasure to visit, and retnrned to Boston. 
	We visited while in Portland the birth-
place of Henry W. Longfellow, and an-
other home he once occupied. The next
day being Sunday we remained in Boston
to attend the Latter-day Saints' meeting,
after which we went on to Northampton,
where we arrived a short time before dark.
The names of the places now began to be-
come familiar to me, as I had heard my
mother speak of them so often, but not so
with the country for I had not imagined
anything so beautiful. We walked leisure-
ly along enjoying the appearance of the
streets and houses as we passed. The side-
walks and streets were almost covered
with grass which continued on to the doors,
and with so many large trees the places
looked like a continuous garden. It surely
is the prettiest, cleanest little city I have
ever visited. The principal manufacturing
is that of silk thread. We walked around
the grounds of the Smith College for girls
where we were told that five thousand
girls attended school.
	We continued our walk until dark when
we took rooms for the night. After getting
located, we went out again to get the air,
the evening being very warm. Thinking
we were near my cousin's home we inquired
where Charles Gloyd lived, the gentleman
answered, "This is the place, come in."
They were expecting us having already
met one train. We went in, but did not
intend to stay to stay this time as it was
getting late, but said we would call in the
morning. He said he felt he had something
to say about that and requested that we re-
main at his home while in the city and as
long as we could, and this being the feel-
ing of his wife we were pleased to remain,
as we wished to spend all the time we
could with our people, which was the ob-
ject of our visit. We did not retire until
late, we were so interested in talking of
loved ones. My cousin remembered my
mother well, especially the morning she aad
her cousin left their home to take the long
journey with ox teams to the Rocky moun-
tains. He thought they must have had
great courage to undertake such a journey.
I told him they exhibited this same cour-
ageous spirit throughout their lives, that
nothing would have induced them to give
up their pioneer life and leave the great
work which would gain for them an exalta-
tion in the world to come. They had heard
but little of the Gospel for which my moth-

[Column 2]

er had left her home, and made some in-
quiries, especially becoming interested in
the Redemption for the Dead. We re-
mained at their home until the following
afternoon when we started for Cumming-
ton, the birthplace and early home of my
mother, my cousin accompanying us. We
took the street car to Williamsburg, a dis-
tance of ten miles, here we procured a team
as we had about fifteen more miles to travel,
passing over a beautiful country, the road
winding around hills which were covered
with dense forests of maple, oak, elm, cher-
ry and different kinds of nuts. Along the
roadside which followed the Westfield
river were many beautiful ferns and shrub-
bery of different kinds, the sumach especi-
ally being noticable with its beautiful red
berries. Occasionally we would see beauti-
ful flowers, the laurel, tiger lily, roses,
daisies, etc., as well as a variety of herbs.
I thought it no wonder our grandparents so
well understood the use of them as medi-
cines.
	On entering Cummington we saw the
Bryant Library, which had been presented
to the town by William Cullen Bryant. In
the evening we met my cousin's son and
daughter and son’s wife who were spending
a few days in Cummington, which is a cool,
delightful place in summer. Early in the
morning we started out to look around at
the places of interest. Visited a paper [-]ill,
called on some friends who were neighbors
to my mother, whose names were as fami-
liar to me as the names of my own personal
friends, and they seemed very pleased to
see us, asking that we remain with them
longer. After being entertained for several
hours by two of my cousin's nieces we went
driving around the William Cullen Bryant
[-]ummer home. It was here, we were told,
he wrote "Thanatopsis." The home is
now occupied by his daughter. It is situ-
ated on a hill with other hills surrounding
it, covered with woods, a large orchard is
near by and a grassy plot surrounds the
house. A little further on we came to the
place where my mother was born lived
for many years. This is also on a hill
and other hills surrounding it are covered
with maple trees that my grandfather
owned, behind it rises Deer Hill. A
group of houses are at the foot of the hill,
where a stream of water runs on where my
grandfather's woolen mills once stood. The
home has been abandoned for years, as one
could tell by its appearance. It has recent-
ly been purchased by parties who are going
to repair it. The lady invited us in to look
at the house, being pleased that we were
so much interested in it, and thought she
would now like the home better herself.
Having brought our lunch, we ate it under
a large maple tree in the front yard and
for desert we gathered wild raspberries
near by. We drank from the spring which
furnished water for our kindred people,
and plucked roses to press and preserve
that grew near the door, and which were
planted by them, while a short time
before we had gathered flowers from their 
graves. We walked through the tall grass
to look at the old mill wheel, the only re-
mains of Grandfather Gloyd's woolen mills 
in which my mother worked to secure
means which enabled her to gather with
the people who had the Gospel that was so
dear to her. After looking around we came 
to the conclusion that there had once been
a comfortable and prosperous home, and
were told that times were lively enough

[Column 3]

when our grandfather was there carrying
on his business.
	I now realize more keenly than ever be-
fore the sacrifice our parents made in leav-
ing their homes for the Gospel sake. With
a prayer in my heart to my Heavenly
Father that my mother had been inspired
of Him to receive the Gospel and gather
with His people whereby she could be the
means of redeeming her father's household,
and also with feelings of gratitude in my
heart for the privilege of realizing this
much desired visit, we rode away and the
day closed on one of the most interesting
and enjoyable times of my life. 
', 'M. J. TANNER.

Continued.
	Monday, April 27, 1885. We went over to
New York to-day and called at the hotel to see
Bro. Hart. We visited several places of inter-
est, among which were some large stores. We
saw so many beautiful things that I quite
longed for a full purse or an unlimited bank
account. We called at the A. T. Stewart
building; the present proprietors are Denning
& Co. It is a magnificent building of iron
and marble, and covers, we are told, two and a
half acres of ground. It is eight stories high,
and has not a partition wall on the whole
