building. All the floors are supported by iron
pillars.  In the center is a circular dome, reach-
ing to the roof, which is covered with glass and
helps to light the building. At every floor
is a railing of molding or carving, and all is
white. The building covers an entire block,
the front entrance being on Broadway. It is
lighted by windows on all sides, and there are
several doors to enter by. The building is
unique, and we are told there are no other
stores in New York just like it. We did not
go all through, as they were preparing to close,
so I cannot give a fuller description. The
counters, seats and goods were being draped to
keep off the dust. We saw costly ornaments
and beautiful statuary, and laces which, no
doubt, bore fabulous prices. We were informed
that they would send goods at retail prices to
any address in the United States free of express.
It was getting late and we took a street car to
the ferry. After crossing on the boat we took
another car, which brought us home. Here
we found a cousin waiting to see us, Miss Alice
Newman of Red Bank, N. J. We were pleased
to meet, and soon became sociable, running a
talking match until about twelve o'clock. We
talked of Utah, of Mormonism, of polygamy
in fact of about everything that could be talked,
on in that length of time. We quoted Scrip-
ture, history and philosophy, and wound up
with a desire for a better acquaintance, and an
invitation to visit her at Red Bank. I also
made out quite a list of genealogies.
	Tuesday, April 28th. Cousin Alice went
home this morning, promising to come again
before I leave. Mrs. K. is very sick, and I
feel quite out of sorts. So much talking and
sight-seeing are somewhat exhaustive. Towards
night it begins storming, and storms hard all
night.

[Column 2]

	Friday, May 1st, 1885. It rains this morn-
ing, but I have promised to meet Bro. Henry
to-day, and he will meet me in Philadelphia.
My cousin's son, Eddie Kimball, escorts me to
Jersey City, and puts me safely on the car. I
take my bearings and mark the route, for I am
going to be very brave and come back alone.
As I travel on out into the great world alone I
feel a little nervous, but I trust to Providence
and let the future take care of itself. I enjoy
myself looking at the country, and finally find
myself at the waiting room at Philadelphia
ready to be called for.
	I look around and see a good many people
sitting or walking about, and I wonder if they
all know where they are going, or if some of
them are waiting to be called for, and if every-
body knows I am a stranger and a little green.
I draw myself up and try to put on a look of
importance, just as though waiting at depots
was an every day occurrence. Presently I see
an elderly gentleman walking about as though
he was looking for somebody. As he passes
me I look for his right hand. He holds the
proverbial blue cotton umbrella in it, and I see
that the thumb is gone; I know he lost it in the
civil war. I look at him and he looks at me.
I venture to ask if he is looking for some one.
He assures me he is. I tell him my name is
Tanner; that is his name. I follow him out,
he takes my satchel and we board a street car,
the rain still falling. After a few minutes ride
we stop and enter a restaurant. Here I am
introduced to the proprietor and his wife, who
are some sort of relations. We take dinner,
and as it is still over two hours before the boat
leaves we wait there, for it rains too hard for
us to see anything of the city.
	Finally we start out again, he with my
satchel in one hand and the umbrella in the
other, I holding to his arm and trying to take
care of my dress. After a short walk, during
which I see very little but people and houses,
we are on board the steamer, "Jersey Blue,"
floating down the broad waters of the Deleware
River.
	Brother Henry tries to show me all points
of interest, but the weather is too cloudy and
foggy to see things clearly at a distance. A
foreign vessel passes us drawn by a "tug." It
carries the flag of the Hawaiian Marchant with
the U. S. colors floating in front. There are
a great many people on deck. I have never
seen a ship before, and it amuses me to see the
great big boat moving lazily along after the
saucy little boat called a "tug," which went
puff, puff, as if swelling with an overdue sense
of its importance. Here a noted battle was
fought in the Revolutionary War, and there
was another point of interest. I try to stand
out under the awning where I can see better,
but it is so cold I am glad to go in the cabin
and look through the windows.
	After a ride of twelve miles, we land at Bil-
lingsport.  It has nearly stopped raining, and
we walk up to the house, about half a mile.
It is a very pleasant road. bordered with cedar
trees, with fields on each side, and an occasional
house. We are greeted with a warm welcome
by his wife and daughters, two fat dogs and
five spotted cats. They hasten to get us some
supper, and as I am very tired I retire early.
	Paulsboro, Saturday, May 2nd 1885. It is
not raining, but is cold and windy. We have
fresh shad and sweet potatoes for breakfast,
and eat off a table which served the officers in
the Revolutianary War. It is a black walnut


[Column 1]

table, and the shad and sweet potatoes are ex-
cellent. I am shown a corner cupboard and
some china and silver ware which, I am assured,
are over a hundred years old, and did service
before the revolution. Brother Henry knew
something of Mormonism, having been a mem-
ber of the Church through Kirtland, Missouri
and Nauvoo. He went east from Iowa and
staid there, married and settled in New Jersey.
He served in the civil war and lost his thumb
—a small member to lose, but serious in its
effects, as it unfits him for such employment as
he depended on for a living. I am not such a
curiosity to them, but wherever I am intro-
duced there is a flutter of excitement. Henry
took us for a ride this evening to Paulsboro,
which is their post office address, they living a
little way out of the town.
	Sunday, May 3rd. Went to-day for a visit
to "Cousin Jesse Brown's." It is six miles
through a beautiful country. We spent a
pleasant day.  "Cousin Jesse" and his wife—
he is a cousin of Henry's wife—came to Utah
thirteen years ago to visit her sisters, Mrs.
Whipple and Mrs. Snyder. They called on
me at the time, and I told them I had come to
return their visit. They have a beautiful
home, on a farm, and showed us many places
of interest; among other things was a sassafras
tree, which stood before the Revolution, and
was supposed to be two hundred years old. We
returned by a different road, which gave us
quite an interesting view of the country.
Everything is green and pleasant. The coun-
try is level, and the hamlets and farm houses
are so pleasant, speaking, in many places, of
wealth and refinement. We stopped at the
Eggleton Cemetery, one of the loveliest resting
places for the dead that I have ever seen. I
could but contrast it with some of our own
cemeteries, or more properly called "grave-
yards." Of course much of the desolate ap-
pearance of ours is caused by lack of rain.
We gathered a few pieces of evergreen as
souvenirs, and came away from the city of the
dead, thinking how much more pleasant it was
than some of the houses of the living.
	Monday, the 4th. I go in the woods this
morning to gather wintergreen. The berries
are gone, but we pick the leaves and have a
pleasant walk. It takes me back to my child-
hood to ramble in the woods. I spend a pleas-
ant day in visiting, and in the evening Henry
takes me to the lighthouse; we go up in the
tower where the lamp is and see the lens,
which, we are told, cost $500. There are three
lighthouses near here; they are called range
lights, and protect navigation on the river.
The lights look in the distance like balls of fire
in the darkness. The river is dotted here and
there with lights from the little fishing boats.
The lighthouse keeper is a Mr. Preston. He
was wounded in the civil war, and, after pass-
ing through untold suffering in the hospital,
came out crippled for life in one leg and foot.
	Tuesday, May 5th. We go to Philadelphia
to-day. We go up on the boat and take a car
for Fairmount Park. The main buildings of
the Centennial Exhibition are removed, and
the ground is being prepared for lawns or flower
gardens. The smell of the compost is any-
thing but pleasant. We enter Memorial Hall
and look through the museum. As the clock
struck twelve two figures of men stepped out
and played sweet music; when it ceased striking
they glided back and the door closed after
them. We are tired and hungry, and go to a
restaurant and call for a lunch of bread and
butter and a cup of tea, for which we pay ten
cents. Being rested and refreshed, we visit
Horticultural Hall. These are the only build-
ings left standing of the grand Exposition.
The city considered it too expensive to keep
them up, and they have been sold or suitably
disposed of. Entering the hall the air is very
warm; it is not oppressive, but gives us a kind

[Column 2]

of Turkish bath, starting the perspiration at
every pore. Here are rare trees and plants
from every part of the tropical world. My
pen cannot describe the scene, but it is deeply
impressed on my mind. We do not see many
flowers; I suppose they are not in season. We
stand under palms, one leaf of which covers
us, and if broken off, would no doubt, be more
than we could lift. Here are bamboo trees
reaching to the roof; I do not know how high
it is, but it is very high. Here are india rub-
ber trees, century plants, and ferns in endless
variety. We get so warm we are afraid to go
out in the wind. We wrap our shawls around
us and sit down in the sun to rest. We try to
catch a car to take us to Fairmount Water
Works; we get wrongly directed, and are so
tired we give it up and go to the city. Car
fare here is six and eight cents. We stop at
Wannamakers. It is an immense store and
seems to comprise every department of trade.
We visit Independence Hall, a life insurance
building and several other places of interest.
We pass a cemetery, where we see the tombs of
Benjamen Franklin and his wife, covered by
large granite slabs, lying flat, with inscriptions
on them. Here is a beautiful conservatory,
with grass next the sidewalk, where we can
look through and see such lovely flowers.
There is quite a little romance connected
with it. The owner, once a poor boy, was
passionately fond of flowers, and he felt it
so keenly that only the rich could enjoy
such things, that he said if ever he was rich
he would have flowers where poor people
could enjoy them. In course of time he
was gratified, and built his conservatory
where everyone could look in and see the
lovely flowers.
	We do not go back by the boat, but ferrry
across the river and take the train for Pauls-
boro. We change cars at Woodbury; it is
certainly the neatest little depot I ever saw,
laid out with lawns and flowerbeds. Here is
the residence of G. G. Green, the manufac-
turer of August Flower and Greenman Syrup.
He has a handsome residence, with grounds
beautifully laid out, the lawns nicely kept
and beds of tulips blooming here and there.
They contain several large buildings, where
the medicines are manufactured. A few
years ago he was a poor man, not fond of
work nor able to live without it. No one
thought him of any consequence. He com-
menced making patent medicines, and, presto,
change, here he is. It looks like a fairy tale.
Henry is waiting for us at Paulsboro with
the carriage, and we get home quite tired
out.


WOMAN'S EXPONENT.


', '﻿LEAVES FROM MY JOURNAL.
		BY M. J. TANNER.
			[Continued.]
	Monday June 15, 1885. Out again: How
I love to travel: It seems to infuse fresh vigor
in my life. Everything is new and novel, and
there is an excitement in it that is very fasci-
nating. The country is pleasant and the
scenery constantly changing. The houses we
pass are all built of frame and painted white.
The latest style is olive-green trimmed with
red. Here is one pea-green and a slate-colored
one. Many houses are poor looking and have
no paint. On, on we go, past farms and sta-
tions. Here the land is rocky. Large piles of
stones are scattered about where they have been
gathered from off the land, and grass and
weeds form a border around them. It is in-
teresting to see the people who get on the train
at different towns. Cousin Emily and I were
weighed while waiting at the depot. I weighed
117 lbs. Gained about 8 lbs., since leaving
home.
	Rochester, 10 a. m. More like the eastern
cities judging by the depot. Thickly settled,
high houses, covered depot, buildings all of 
brick. I am so busy taking notes that I do
not notice that every one has left the car un-
til a man tells me that I must change cars here.
I look around and see that I am the only per-
son left, gather up my things in haste and get
on another car as directed. I seat myself and
in looking over my belongings find that I have
left my parasol. It is too late to get it now for
the car is moving. I once more take out my
note book and pencil. 
	Near Lyons, pleasantly situated on a hill, is 
a large brick building which is pointed out to
me as an asylum for idiots. And here is Lyons. 
11 a. m., of which little can be seen as we run
[Column 2]
in between two rows of freight cars. The car
is full and I have as a seat mate a gentleman
who has traveled all night coming from
Chicago, he is sleeping and I have no one to
tell me about the country.
	Syracuse 12.15. Twenty minutes for dinner.
I have my lunch and eat it on the train as do
some others. I see by the sign that it is 130
miles to Schenectady. Syracuse is a large city
but there is little to be seen from the depot,
and one cannot tell much about people and
places on so slight an acquaintance. I can look
into the dining room and see the people eating.
Collared waiters are passing straw-berries.
Some ladies are walking on the platform
dressed in lovely costumes. As we start out
we pass through what appears to be Main
Street. On each side are very high brick
houses, and a continuous line of stores, mil-
linery shops, business houses and churches. 
Near the suburbs stands "Wards Temperance
Hotel.” It looks small and poorly patronized.
Out in the country, we pass large hop farms.
	Oneida 1.20 p. m. There is a beautiful lawn
around the depot and the word Oneida laid in
white cobble stones.
	Rome, 1.55. Here people crowd on the 
train, more than there are seats for. The
weather is hot. My neighbor gives his seat to
a lady.
	Utica, 2.20. Here several people leave the 
train. 3.15. We are traveling through Herkimer
Co., celebrated for its large dairies. Through
the valley of the Mohawk River. It is a 
beautiful country. Change cars at Schenectady.
getting a ticket to Caldwell where they tell me
I will have to stay all night and go down the
lake on a steamboat in the morning to Balton.
I meet a  pleasant young lady who is going to
Caldwell and tells me something about the
route and what hotel to stay at in Caldwell.
The face of the country changes. Ballstown
seems burried in hills and woods.
	Saritoga. Change cars, waiting about half
an hour. Here at the depot is the "United
States Hotel,” the largest house I think I ever
saw. The two sides which we see from the
platform reach a block each way. I walk down
the road to the corner and find that it extends
along the third side of the block. I am afraid
to take time to explore any farther. Many
people get off here for it is a famous resort for
the world of fashion and must, no doubt allure
many adventurers. I think I should not like
to stay here. We travel on and change cars
at Fort Edward where I part with my com-
panion. The country grows more hilly and
thicker wooded as we go on.
	Glens Falls—Caldwell.  Here is the end of
the road, for we are at the head of the beautiful
and far famed Lake George, and twilight is 
gathering around us. A steamboat stands at
the landing which connects with depot but it
will not go until morning. It is 8 o'clock and
I turn to go toward the cab stand after getting
the above information. I see a big, burly,
pleasant looking man in an old felt hat, look-
ing something after the fashion of the boys hats
at home, with the rim lapped down, holding a
whip in his hand. I take him for a cabby and
ask which cab will take me to the American
Hotel. He says he does not know, and I pass
 on. I wish I had asked him if he knows any-
one by the name of Putney. Still it would be
useless as they will not expect me until I reach
Balton where they will be sure to meet me to-
morrow. But Oh, how I do hate to stop at a
hotel among strangers. I feel so shrinking and
diffident away off here alone. If the boat had
only gone down tonight how nice it would
have been. However there is no time now for
regrets, I must hasten to secure a cab before
they are gone. It would not be pleasant walk-
ing through town in search of a hotel. I reach
the line of cabs and ask for the American 
Hotel. A cabby takes my satchel and seats


[Column 1]
﻿me in the last cab on the line. Several delays
occur and finally we are just starting out when
a voice from the outside asks if Mrs. Myron
Tanner is in here. I say that is my name and
ask if he has come for me. He replies that
he has and I get out of the cab thinking at
the time that the American Hotel has lost one
customer. He tells me to wait on the plat-
form while he brings the horse around. I recog-
nize in him the man I spoke to on the platform.
Presently he appears with a buckboard of
ancient and uncertain appearance drawn by a
horse which to ride after might lay one liable to
prosecution for cruelty to animals My heart
fails me for a moment and I feel that possibly
I am in a “delima.” However, I yield to the
force of circumstances and allow him to help
me into the vehicle and while we are being
whirled away he very politely informes me
that he is Caleb. I am glad to hear it and
kindly enquire what our relationship may be.
He says he is Caleb Putney son of my husband’s
Aunt Maria, and tells me that he has a brother
and two sisters. In the meantime darkness
is gathering around us and we have a ride of
ten miles before us. I enquire if the road is
good and receive an affirmative answer. There
is no moon and the sky is cloudy, as we enter
the woods and drive between rows of tall trees
it is sometimes so dark that we cannot see the
horse. We necessarily travel rather slowly,
and our long and somewhat lively conversation
is frequently interrupted by an apostraphy to
the horse. “Get up there you old lazy if you
don’t go faster I shall have to whip you dread-
ful hard.”
	I am asked many questions about Utah and
in return for information given am made ac-
quainted with the history of the family at home.
There had been a family council held about
coming to meet me. “Father” said I would
come up on the boat in the morning and they
would meet me at the landing. But he thought
I would not like to stay at a hotel among
strangers, which I assure him I was decidedly
averse to.
	The seat of our conveyance has scarcely any
back and he is very anxious lest I should go
to sleep and fall off, and what shall he do with
me if I fall and break my neck. Box me up
and send me back to Utah, I say, but he
would rather send me back alive, and so en-
joines me to be very careful not to fall, even
offering, if I think there is any danger, to hold
me on. Whenever there is a lull in the con-
versation he enquires if I am sleepy and en-
joines me not to fall off. I am certainly not
sleepy. The situation is so novel, the surround-
ings so strange and the air so bracing after the
hot day’s ride, and the conversation of my
companion so (what shall I say) exhilerating
that altogether I seem possessed of a spirit of
fun, and almost fancy myself a girl again.
	I learn that he is the only one of his fathers
children that has married though David and
Rebecka are both older than he. That he has
five children living and has burried one. That
his girls are dreadful good singers. That his
mother is a dreadful good woman. That his
horse is dreadful poor. Father would not let
his horse come because it was afraid of the
train. He has no horse, but a yoke of cattle
and the boy claims them. He also has some
sheep and the boy claims them. I see some
water and ask if that is Lake George. No
that is only “the pond.” He will take me to
Lake George in the morning. I hear a coarse
grum voice and ask “what is that?” Why!
that is a bull-frog. Did I never hear a bull-
frog before? I supposed I never had if that
was the kind of noise they made. Our frogs in
Utah made a different noise. They were a
small green frog. “Am I afraid?” “Oh no,
not of a bull-frog.” But I am beginning to
think the ride “dreadful” long, for I am
getting “dreadful” tired. He thinks it must
[Column 2]
be getting dreadful late and wonders if the girls
will be up. We get home at last, 11.15 p. m.
find the girls up and supper waiting. We sit
down to supper and he asks the blessing. The
first blessing I have heard since leaving home,
though my friends all profess religion. It gives
me a very comfortable homelike feeling. His
father gets up to welcome me and after supper
I am shown to a clean comfortable room and I
go to rest feeling that I am among friendly,
good people. Caleb’s home is a mile farther.
	Balton June 16,1885.—Get up late this
morning feeling weary after my long ride. I
am introduced to Caleb’s mother, who is a tall
slim elderly woman, very quiet and pleasant in
her ways. She gives me a kind welcome and
is glad to see one of her sister’s family. The
men are all gone to their work and after
breakfast Rebecka, or as she is called in the
family, Becky Ann goes down to the pond
with me. It is called the Trout Lake, and is
a pretty body of water two miles long and half
a mile wide surrounded with trees. Their home
is only a short distance from the bank but
owing to the thick growth of bushes we cannot
see it from the house. I think I should like to
see the frog that makes such a great noise but
he is rather shy and we cannot find one.  We
take a walk in the woods and pick winter-
green. The fragrant leaves of which are tender
and sweet, the berries are nearly gone but now
and then a red berry rewards our search.
	After dinner Caleb comes over and gets his
father’s horse and buggy and takes me to Lake
George. It is a pleasant ride through the
woods of about two and a half miles. The
country here is all hills, and woods, with here
and there a rock thrown in. The rock is black
granite and ranges from the size of a trunk to
the size of a house, or as the boy said “about
as big as a lump of chalk,” which may be quite
as definite. We call at the old homestead
that Father Tanner used to own, and where
my husband was born. The lady of the
house, Mrs. Vandenberg is very pleasant and
shows us through all the rooms and tells us the
alterations that have been made. The kitchen
and woodshed, (everybody here has a wood-
shed well filled with stove wood) has been
taken from the west side and built on the
south end, making the whole house face the
street which runs east of the house, between it
and the Lake. The portico is taken away and
a porch built the whole length, which makes a
lovely place to sit in the afternoon, with the
beautiful lake spread out before us and the
mountains, thickly covered with trees on the
opposite side. The house has been newly
weatherboarded and painted white. Since
Father Tanner left there with his family fifty
years ago, considerable of the land has been
sold in small lots and quite a village now
stands on what used to be known as the Tanner
Farm. A large hotel stands on the bank of
the lake a few rods east of the house, known
as the “Balton House,” and a steamboat land-
ing is made there. Mrs. V. takes me down to
the lake and shows me through the hotel.
From the observatory we have a fine view of
the surrounding country. She invites me to
spend a few days with her and visit various
points of interest. We go from there and call
on Morgan Tanner, a grandson of my hus-
band’s uncle, Pardon Tanner. He is of medium
size, dark complexion with keen black eyes,
about thirty years of age and a widower. I
tell him I am looking up family history and he
promises to help me all he can. From there
we go to Green Island. It once belonged to
the Tanner estate. It is now laid out in parks
and groves and a fine hotel, and some cottages
are built there as it is a fashionable summer
resort. It comprises sixty-five acres of land;
has a steamboat landing and is connected with
the mainland by a bridge. It is called the
Sagamore, and is certainly a lovely summer
[Column 3]
retreat. Little boats are kept there for rowing
on the lake, which is certainly a very pleasant
pastime. A steamboat comes puffing toward
the landing and our horse gets restive, so we
get in the buggy and start for home. She
trots nicely while she hears the engine and we
think it would be well to have a steamboat be-
hind us all the way. We stop at the post-
office to get some stamps and mail some letters.
Caleb introduces me to several of his friends
and they all have some joke to make about
Utah. It rains a little before we get home.


WOMAN’S EXPONENT.


']

Name: 25_beautiful_old_palace_castle
Representative_Docs:
['FLOATING DOWN THE RHINE.
The Rhine, the Rhine, the beautiful Rhine.

	Durham House, 295, Edge Lane,
        			Liverpool, England.
					February 1908.
	The broad, deep, placid, blue river of ro-
mance and of most enchanting scenery,
	The shores on either side are dotted with

[Column 2]
towns and villages and castles,many old and
ruined, built in times of long ago, having
each a legend weird, romantic and myster-
ious. Many castles are built on the highest
pinnacle of a mountain, in the most inac-
cessible positions to enable them to with-
stand long sieges from hostile and preda-
tory neighbors. They are huge piles of tur-
reted towers and solid masonry which seem
to bid defiance to time and all men. They
are magnificent, grim and grand. Many are
in a complete state of ruin, through the de-
structive invasion of the French. Some are
inhabited in the safest parts aud all are in-
tensely interesting as landmarks in the his-
tory of a people long since dead. The view
is all too fleeting, sailing down on the bosom
of the lovely river. One desires to stop and
ramble through the deserted halls and ban-
quet rooms and revel in imagination of the
gay and beautiful women and heroic knights
who once trod in pride these wondrous struc-
tures. Renowned towns and villages came
in view at nearly every turn of the frequently
bending river, which has been the birthplace
of some never to be forgotten person or ex-
ploits of some ancient worthies. The river
winds majestically along, washing the feet
of high mountains, precipitous rocks and
rolling hills, for 730 miles down to the North
sea. The middle section for a little over
one hundred miles from Frankfort to Cologne
has the most rugged and mountainous scen-
ery. Many of the steep sides of mountains
and hills are planted to vineyards almost to
their very tops. They have to be terraced
before planting. The breaking up of this
ground has all to be done by hand, for no
animal or plow could work on the steep
places. This utilization of this kind of
ground could not be carried on in Utah,
where every vine has to have a streamlet of
water to it, or it will wither up and die. In
Rhineland every year is not a fruitful one on
account of drouth. A fruitful vintage is
only once in a while. They say the grapes
are not pleasant to eat, only good for wine.
We thought that we would like to taste the
renowned Rhine wine. I had about two
tablespoonfuls poured into my glass and
tasted—ugh—flat vinegar. A spoonful of
good vinegar, water and sugar, like my
mother often gave us in summer time with
our lunch, was much more to my taste. Per-
haps my taste is uncultivated, but they can
have their wine if they like it. This reminds
me of another difference in taste. At the
table where we sampled our wine, sat a fine
old German and his daughter. On a plate
there was some slices of moist, very black
rye bread. The German insisted I taste
this bread, saying it was the finest in the
world. I did, and thought I had taken a
quid of tobacco, it looked, smelled and
tasted like it, although I do not believe I
ever tasted tobacco.
	To describe at any length the scenes,
towns, castles, ruins and bridges noted and
renowned, places of history and interest, as
one passes them sailing down the river,
would make the article entirely too long.
So a short notice and a legend attached will
be all that can be done. We left Frankfort
on the Main and took a train to Biebrech, a
small town where we were to take the boat,
There were two trains leaving about the
same time to this village, one going into it
and the other going on down to the wharf.
Of course we took the wrong one, so we had
to walk, or trot, as we had not much time
to correct our mistake, and catch the boat.
But the walk of little over a mile was
through a fine park in which was a pretty


[Column 1]

old ruin, a hasty glimpse of which repaid us
for our walk. We arrived in time to go on a
boat named “Barbarossa.” It was a fine large
boat and had quite a number a passengers.
We had a book describing points as we came
to them and it kept us busy watching first one
side and then the other, so as not to miss
anything of note.
	Bingen, the chief town, in one of the divi-
ions of Hessland, is of very ancient origin,
was noted in Roman times. A bridge on
one side of it built over the river Nahe,
dates from 13 B. C. Burg Klopp, a castle
built in the center of the town, was built in
the enclosing wall of a Roman fort. The
French invaded the town and nearly de-
stroyed it and also the castle. Every one re-
members the pathetic song of “Bingen on the
Rhine,” in which a dying soldier sends his
last message to his bereaved mother. Pass-
ing Bingen you come in view of an interest-
ing castle, the Mansethurm, built on an
island in the river, but very near the shore.
It is merely a tower to which is attached the
legend that a miser, fearing there might
come a famine, filled the tower with wheat.
The famine did come, and he selfishly shut
himself up in his tower with his wheat and
was deaf to the distress of the people around
him. After a great while the tower was
broken open and it was found that the mice
had eaten up the wheat and the miser too.
	Nearly all of the legends are of the im-
possible, but that very fact and the mystery
veiling them is their principal charm. An-
other turn in the river, and we see a very
fine monument erected to commemorate the
victories of the German people and the rest-
oration of the German empire in 1870-1871.
It is 110 feet high, and the figure “German-
ia,” crowns it. On the substructure, which 
is 80 feet high, are the figures of Emperor
William I, and two hundred contemporary
kings and princes.
	We now come to a large, real castle,
Rheinstein, perched on an impregnable pre-
cipitous rock. Tower rises above tower,
grim, gray and ivy-covered, full of mystery,
and stories of love and sorrow, if the walls
could only speak. It has one legend of false
wooing. The Knight Siegfried von
Rheinstein, a warrior, during one of his pre-
datory excursions, captured a beautiful
maiden named Jutta and married her. He
ceased then his pillaging and carousing. In
time a little girl was born to them and Jutta
died. The little girl was named Gerda, who
grew to be so beautiful that suitors from far
and near sued for her love and hand. Her
heart was won by a neighboring knight
named Kuno von Riechenstein. It was the
custom of the times for young lovers to have
a mediator to conduct their suit, so they
chose Kurt von Ehrenfels, Kuno’s uncle.
But faithless to his nephew, he ingratiated
himself into the good graces of Gerda’s
father and gained his consent for himself.
No entreaty of the betrayed Gerda had any
effect with her father after he had given his
word, especially as the uncle was possessed
of great wealth and her lover of very little.
The wedding day dawned, and the festal
procession neared the Klemenkapelle,
where the marriage ceremony was to take
place, when a swarm of gadflies came out
of the bushes. One of them stung the silver
gray palfry of Gerda and it reared and
dashed out of the procession. Gerda
spurred it on, and the steed flew with her to
Reichenstien, the castle of her lover. Kurt,
the uncle, rode after her, but was thrown in
the pursuit and killed, also her father in try-
ing to stop the run away horse, was hurt, 

[Column 2]
but recovered and gave his blessing to the
lovers.
	The next interesting point is the “Lore-
lei,” a basalt rock rising out of the water
430 feet, almost perpendicularly. The river
here is said to be 75 feet deep—the greatest
depth in the river. It is the only place
where salmon is found. When the river is
low seven stones can be seen above the sur-
face. The legend of them is that they rep-
resent seven maidens, who were fickle and
hard hearted, and had deceived their lovers
and at the curse of the Lorelei were turned
to stone.
	The word Lorelei means in German the
“blustering rock,” or echo rock with us.
Standing below the rock twelve echoes can
be heard resounding from point to point.
Another legend more noted than the above
is that a beautiful (always beautiful) en-
chantress whose wonderful power lay in her
eyes, befooled so many men that she was
ordered shut up in a convent. On her way
there accompanied by a knight, as they
were riding up the Rhine they came to the
high rock. She begged the knight to allow
her to ascend the rock in order to view once
more the castle of her beloved. While gaz-
ing, in the distance she suddenly caught
sight of a ship bearing the colors of her
knight. Then she recognized the returning
loved one standing on the deck. In her
great joy she made a false step and fell
headlong into the deep river and was
drowned. A gale of wind dashed the ship
against the rock and upset it, and thus the
lovers were united in death. Mystery affirms
that from the 13th century a treasure has
been shut up in the rock Lorelei.
	Soon after rounding the sharp curve of
the river at Lorelei, we came in sight of
St. Goar, just above which rises the great
fortress Rheinfels. Some say it is the most
magnificent ruin on the Rhine. It was built
in 1255 and destroyed by the French in
1797. The ruins were sold in 1812 for
2,500 francs or $500.00 in American money.
This castle was built to endure for ages. Its
foundations were laid deep and strong and
were well nigh impregnable. Sixty Rhine
cities combined in vain in 1255 to lay seige
to it, the seige lasting one year and fourteen
weeks. In 1692 a French commander with
17,000 men attacked it with an equally
fruitless result. In 1758 it was treacherous-
ly delivered into the hands of the French
commander Marquis de Castries. The his-
tory of the ruthless destruction and desola-
tion in the German country by the French
explains the slumbering hate that raged in
the bosoms of the German nation which
burst forth in all its fury in the subsequent
war which despoiled France perhaps very
justly of a large slice of her possessions.
	Sailing around the sharpest curve of the
river we come in view of the pretty and an-
cient town of Boppard. It has many tow-
ers, two of the old Parish churches dating
from the beginning of the I2th and 13th
centuries. It has a hydropathic establish-
ment and a beautiful avenue extending for
more than a mile along the river. Passing
along reveling in the glory of the mountains
with vine and tree covered sides, we arrive
in view of the fine monument erected to the
memory of Emperor William I. It was un-
veiled in the presence of Emperor William
II, and many princes, August, 1897. We
next arrive at Coblentz, the chief town of
the Rhine province, said to be the most
beautifully located of all the large towns on
the Rhine. Its origin goes back to Roman
times. It is situated at the junction of the

[Column 3]

Moselle and Rhine rivers. One of its most
interesting places is the Kastorkirche. This
church is built in the form of a fine Roman-
esque Basilica, and dates from the end of the
12th century. It gets its name from a her-
mit of the 4th century, Kastor, whose bones
are said to be deposited in it. In the left
aisle is the monument erected in 1725 to the
saintly Rizza, who went every morning dry-
footed over the Rhine to worship in the 
Kastorkirche, so the legend runs.
	We now glide on past many interesting
places and come to Siebengebirge (seven
mountains) and the castle Drachenfels
(Dragon Rock). This name was a favorite
one for high mountain castles in the middle
ages. This one certainly deserves the name
as it forms the pinnacle of a very steep, in-
accessible mountain. The legend is that
Siegfried, a hero, visited the old Frankish
king Childerich in his vine covered castle
and found him sick and deserted. His
beautiful daughter Gunhild had been car-
ried away by a rejected suitor, Duke Hun-
old, who was  a magician, and placed her in a
cavern guarded by a huge dragon. King
Childerich offered in vain his throne to any
one who would rescue his daughter Gun-
hild. Thirty knights had already been de-
voured by the dragon in their attempt to 
rescue her. Siegfred, hearing the story, de-
clared himself ready for battle with the
monster. In the battle the monster swal-
lows his spear, which he thrusts into its
jaws, then he draws his famous sword, Bal-
mung, and with one terrible stroke of its in-
vincible blade lays the monster dead.
	The fetters of Gunhild are loosened in the
same instant, and Hunold’s soul is taken to 
hell. Unfortunately the usual sequel of
marriage and a long life of happiness does
not follow, for Siegfried is already betrothed
to a lovely woman. Lord Byron has im-
mortalized Drachenfels in one of his canto’s,
in Child Harold. One verse is as follows:

“The castle crag of Drachenfels
	Frowns o’er the wide and winding Rhine,
Whose breast of waters broadly swells,
	Between the banks which bear the vine,
And hills all rich with blossomed trees,
	And fields which promise corn and wine,
And scattered cities crowning these,
	Whose far white walls along them shine,
Have strew’d a scene which I should see,
	With double joy wert thou with me.’’

	We now come to Bonn, a pretty little
town and a wonderful bridge across the
Rhine. This town has the distinction  of
being the birthplace of the great musician
Ludwig von Beethoven. His father was
a tenor singer to the elector. On the Mun-
sterplatz stands a monument to his mem-
ory unveiled 1845. The Bonn Rhine Bridge
spoken of above is a magnificent piece of
engineering, its central span of 610 feet
being the second largest span in Europe.
	A little back from the river is a villa with
a cupola. This is the residence of the Im-
perial Princes while studying in Rome.
	We now soon arrive at Cologne, the end of
our never to be forgotten ride down the
Rhine.
	Cologne is the most important town of the
Rhine Province, though not the capital. It
was an important place on the Rhine in
Roman times, 38 B. C. The city is the seat
of an Archbishop and of a Governor.
	To it was intrusted in 1164 the preserva-
tion of the remains of the three Kings (the
Magi). This has caused pilgrims from all
parts of the earth continually to be drawn to
the city. The grandest and most amazing
structure in the city is the cathedral, called


The Dom. I have gazed upon and gone
into many cathedrals, but none excel this
to me. One can gaze with enraptured awe
standing in front of this amazing structure,
with its two towers 530 feet from the
ground, for hours without being weary or
feeling that half had been seen. From the
ground to the pinnacle it is most wonderfully
ornate. The history of its building is pa-
thetic in its long struggle to be finished.
	The foundation stone was laid in 1248.
It is of Gothic architecture designed by Ger-
hard yon Riehl. The choir was consecrated
in 1322 and the temporary wall with which
they enclosed it fn order to hold divine
services remained standing until 1863. It
was finally finished and on the 15th of Oct.,
1880, Emperor William I, surrounded by
the whole of the royal family and many of
the princes of Germany, laid the capstone of
this grand and noble building, thus this
sublimest monument of architectural skill
was completed. The interior is in keeping
in beauty with the exterior, the immense
fluted pillars and stained glass windows of
surpassing beauty enchain the admiration
of the beholder and cause one to feel that
the worshipers in such a delightful building
should have the light of true worship. We
attended a short service and came away
with sad hearts, having witnessed the mock-
ery of religion, and the darkness, ignorance,
and mystery that fills the hearts of the wor-
shipers. At the window of our room in the
hotel opposite the Dom I sat long gazing on 
this grand structure, and I wondered when
the mummery which is used in the name 
of religion would be stilled and the sublime 
structure dedicated to the true and liviag
God.
', 'LEAVES FROM MY JOURNAL.

		BOLOGNA, Italy.
		Friday, Dec. 6, 1895.
	Express train via Padua to Venice.
Padua, “Italian Padova,” was founded
according to “Ancient tradition” by
Antenor, the brother-in-law of Priam, and is
famous for its University. Among its
alumni were Savonarola, Tasso, Petrarch
and Galileo, whose statues adorn the Pizza
Vittosia Emanuele.
	The church S. Antonio begun in 1256, is
very imposing with seven domes, and is
rich in paintings, sculptures and works of
Art. The Scuola del Santo has frescoes by
Titian and his pupils. S. Maria dell’
Arena, or Giotto’s chapel contains frescoes
which are among his best works. In one
church is an altar piece by Paul Veronese.
	“Venice in June,” gave our approach
via Adriatic Sea.
	We now approach the city by a railway
bridge more than two miles long, consist-
ing of long embankments and 222 arches,
costing nearly a million dollars.
	We left the cars and entered the gon-
dolas waiting for us, a few moments'
brought us to the Grand Hotel. Although
the winter day was sombre and there were
no singers, the gondola ride and the Medie-
val Palaces were still enchanting.
	We dined, then spent the evening ramb-
ling through the quaint alleys and on the
Grand Square of St. Mark, the pigeons
were so numerous and tame, it was difficult
to walk and not step upon them.
	This square is the centre of all that is
loveliest in Venice. On the E. side is St.
Marks Church, the N. the Procuratie
Vecchi, with the clock tower, (La Torre
dell’ Orologio,) built in 1496, the hours
are struck on a bell by two bronze figures.
The Piazzetta is a small square opening to
the South near the angle it makes with the
Piazza, is the Campanile and on the E. side
is the Ducal Palace, on the W. the Library
and Mint, to the seaward the two columns
of granite one bearing the winged lion of St.
Mark, the other the statue of St. Theodore.
St. Mark was built in the 11th Cent.,
Byzantine style with Gothic additions of the
14th Cent. and Renaissance alterations of
the 17th Cent. Above the portal are the

[Column 2]
bronze horses which Constantine carried
from Rome to Constantinople, whence Zeno
broght back in 1205; they were taken to
Paris by Nap. in 1797 and restored to Venice
in 1815. The church has a great dome in
thecentre and four smaller ones crown the
arms of the cruciform structure the Mosaics
of the exterior and interior cover more than
an acre, are on a background of gold gild-
ing and are very beautiful, the bronze and
rich marbles are splendid.
	It is impossible to describe the Ducal
Palace. We entered by the Scala dei
Giganti or Giants staircase from the court-
yard. The interior is a rich gallery of
Venetian Art, the great Council Hall con-
tains portraits of 76 Doges and Tintorretto’s
Paradise, the Palace is connected on the E.
side with prison (Le Prigione) by the
Bridge of Sighs, so called because when the
prisoners crossed over for their trial, if
innocent they left the Palace by the
front door, if guilty were placed in the dun-
geons below and never again crossed the
bridge.
	Saturday, Dec. 7—Early morning found
us in our gondolas to see the Old Palaces
situated on the Grand Canal. Our hotel
was once the Palace Terro (14th Cent.) and
the Palace Tini Wimpffen, and next to it
is the pretty Palazzo Contarini Tasau,
known as the Palace of Desdemona, the
Palace Mocenigo was occupied by Byron.
	The Arsenal has a gateway of Renaissance
workmanship. “Sharp and expressive in
its ornamental sculpture,” in front are
four Greek lions brought from the Piraens
in 1687, the museum contains old arms,
armor and instruments of torture.
	We visited the factory which is very
interesting. The rest of the day we de-
voted to churches—that of the Jesuits, the
interior is curious, the walls being covered with
imitation of drapery in white marble inlaid
with green, it contains Titians Martyrdom
of St Laurence. St. Sebastine is famous for
pictures by P. Veronise who is buried here.
St. Rocco has pictures by Tintoretto and
Scuola di San Rocco is one of the three
most precious buildings in Italy, the others
being the Sistine Chapel at Rome and the
Campo Santo at Pisa.
	Sunday, Dec. 8—Attended Church San
Marco, the sermon was no doubt eloquent
but could not understand a word, the music
was fine, the organ filled the Church with
rich, full tones. After lunch went to the
Scotch Presbyterian Church but the Pastor’s
wife stood at the door saying, Dr. Robert-
son was ill and there would be no service,
she gave us her card, inviting us to tea on
Monday, saying on that day they received
strangers, and if they felt lonely might
make or meet friends, in a strange land all
the world was kin. Was sorry not to attend
but left Venice on that day.
	Monday, Dec. 9—Early morning train
for Milan via Verona, 72 miles from Venice.
On our other Tour we stopped at Verona.
The churches are very fine mostly Gothic,
the Cathedral is of the 14th Cent. and con-
tains an assumption by Titian and S.
Fermo Maggiore 14th Cent., has a carved
roof of larch wood and in one chapel a fine
altar-piece by Caroto, other churches con-
tain valuable pictures by Tintoretto, P.
Veronise and other artists. Our desire was
to see in this old city the tomb of Juliet.
A rude Sarcophagus was kept in the garden
of an old Franciscan Monastery on the right
bank of the Aolige, the house of the Capu-
lets is on the street San Sabastino.

[Column 3]
	From Verona to Milan is 93 miles. When
I came before took the other route, Turin
to Milan. That road passes through
Novara near which the battle was fought,
and through the memorable battle field of
Magenta. This is the most interesting
route. We pass the Castles of the Montec-
chi, the Montagues of Romeo and Juliet
and Lake Garda, near and toward this was
fought the battle of Solferino, 24th of June,
1859, Austrians defeated by Nap. III.
Saw the house at Villafranca, where the
treaty between the French and Austrian
Emperors was signed July 11, 1859. The
City of Milan, Capitol of Lombardy, is en-
tered through a beautiful arch. When last
here it was warm, the ladies were out on
the streets with lace veils or shawls draped
on their heads something like the Spanish
style. It is a walled city, the interior of
the walls laid out with gardens and planted
with trees, the streets are elegantly paved,
it is a pleasure to walk on them, the foot
paths next the houses are six feet wide of
smooth granite. We drove about the city
and to the suppressed Monastery of St.
Maria delle Grazie, to see Da Vincis Last
Supper. The painting is on the wall and
much defaced as the church was once used
as a stable in time of war; in spite of which
you can see how beautiful was the painting,
although of the 14th Cent. the beautiful
colors still remain. The Church St. Am-
brose founded in the 14th Cent. in its pre-
sent form to the 12th is most interesing.
Here the old Lombard kings received the
“Iron Crowns,” the frescoes and mosaics
are beautiful it has a golden altar-front.
The Brera, a Palace of Science and Art con-
tains in the picture gallery Raphael’s
Sposalizio, or the marriage of the Virgin.
Next we visited the Cathedral with its
delicate tracery and forest of pinnacles, built
in the form of a Latin cross, its interior is
490 feet in length, 185 feet in width, with
nave 155 feet in height, the dome is 220 ft.
in height from the pavement to the top of
the tower 360 feet. The roof has 98 Gothic
turrets, the exterior is adorned with 4,400
marble statues, its beautiful tower and
spires are all surmounted by a statue, the
nterior has double clustered pillars, lofty
arches, niches in the walls filled with figures
and grand monuments. The scene with
the sun shining through the magnificent
stained windows and rose window is strik-
ingly beautiful, just that wavy beautiful
effect of light and shade one gets in a for-
est.
	Among the treasures is a Veil from the
true cross, a silver altar, solid silver
statues, Bibles with the covers filled with
rare jewels, robes of cloth of gold and cloth
of silver, rare laces and wealth untold.
One room is nearly all silver. A Bishop of
Milan lies there in a crystal casket with
gold trimmings, a screen of gold and silver
covers this. He is robed in cloth of gold
with crown and scepter, crosses and rings
of dimaonds, rubies, emeralds and sapphires.
Upon a silver table the candles, and around
the room in solid silver bas-relief is his his-
tory from the cradle to the grave, while up-
on all the chairs is his monogram in silver.
We ascended to the roof of the Cathedral
from which we had superb views of the
plains of Lombardy and the snowy Alps.
To morrow we say farewell to sunny Italy.
							KIM.
	BARONESS HIRSCH has given $250,000 to
endow a home for Hebrew consumptives
in England.


WOMAN'S EXPONENT.


[Column 1]
UTAH.

STILL shall New England classic hills be dear,
Where’er the children of the pilgrims go; 
Her sacred name our hearts shall still revere
Her story fill us with responsive glow.
Where smooth Potomac’s placid waters flow, 
Virginia’s stately homes in beauty rise—
Beloved for him who taught the world to know
His country's freedom is the patriot's prize,
Not dearly bought, though won with costliest
	sacrifice.

The downy peaches waft their fruity breath
From sun-kissed orchards of the Delaware;
And flowers that know no winter’s icy death,
Garland the plains of Florida the fair;
’Neath California’s skies of beauty rare,
Her yellow poppies nod and roses twine;
Sweet scent of orange-blooms perfumes the air,
And amber clusters load the fragrant vine—
Each sphere of chalice filled with sweet, trans-
	lucent wine.

Fair was the heritage our father’s bought,
Stretching o’er hill and vale, from sea to sea;
Each new-born state some separate charm has
	brought,
Some grace distinctive, as its birth-right fee.
From Maine’s cold pine-woods, where the winds
	blow free,
And whistling, rivet tyrant winter’s chains.
The sunny rice-fields, where the bird and bee,
In slumberous tones, chant summer’s sweet re-
	frains.
Love’s loyal faith, sincere, each part the best
	maintains.

Let others boast their prairies spreading wide
Whose lonesome plains the far horizon meet;
Still let them paint with fond exultant pride,
Their miles of corn-fields and their waving wheat;
Or, let them tell of vast woods’ dim retreat,
With vaulted arches thick with evergreen,
Where vines run wild and mosses’ velvet feet
Climb o’er each tangled bough—a living screen
Whose solemn gloom of shade no sun-ray falls be-
	tween.

How would our eyes grow dim o’er those broad
	plains,
With home-sick longing for our russet hills!
Nor could we bear the depths where twilight 
	reigns,
Whose deep recesses cloistered silence fills.
Dearer to us the cliff whose grandeur thrills,
With its mute eloquence, the heart’s unrest;
Dearer the water-falls and leaping rills,
And canyons wild of Utah’s caverned breast,
Though other scenes be fair, we love our Utah
	best.

As when through leagues of blistering, shimmer,
	ing sand,
Its toilsome way the caravan has made,
Sees o’er the waste the cool, green palm-tree
	stand,
And rapturous, hails their bliss of restful shade—
And as the ship by tempest long delayed,
Beaten by storm-waves, shattered by the blast,
Gains the sure harbor, and, with anchor weighed,
Rests on the peaceful billow, safe at last,
Happy in perils shunned and dangers overpast—

So with their faces toward the golden west,
Through weary leagues, toil on the pilgrim train ; 
Storm-worn, but dauntless in their weary quest,
Cross the broad river and the treeless plain,
Till Utah’s rock-built height their footsteps gain.
Circled by cliffs, like some great eagle’s nest,
Lies a lone valley—nature’s wild domain :
'Here," says their chieftain, “here may Zion
	rest;
Here shall God’s temple stand, and here His
	Saints be blest."

[Column 2]
As on New England’s cold, surf-beaten shore,
The earlier pilgrims built devotion’s shrine;
’Mid the loud anthem of the ocean’s roar,
To great Jehovah tuned their swelling line,
And saw His love o’er stern, hard duty shine—
So knelt these toil-worn  souls—faith’s struggling
	band—
Asking in earnest prayer, the power divine
To shield them in the wild, waste, barren land.
And guide them in their toil up heaven’s direct-
	ing hand.

The world is richer by such pioneers;
Faith, through their toil, its golden fruitage sees,
And life’s hard front in softer light appears;
That the slow-moving world may walk at ease,
Truth builds her highways broad, by such as
	these.
They found a desert—made a garden fair,
Where luscious fruits bend low the clustering
	trees,
And flowers of every tint perfume the air,
Where towns and cities broad and equal blessing
	share.
I’ve heard a story of the days of old,
Told by Ute warriors when the pale-face  came—
How long ago a spirit, overbold,
With mighty Manitou made rival claim
With silver, gold and gems of every name,
He built proud turrets on the Wasatch range;
But the Great Spirit from his mount of flame.
With thunder-bolts of fire and lightnings strange,
Made walls and tower and throne to shapeless
	ruins change.
I know not if the tale be strictly true;
But yet our Utah mountains strangely show
Bright lines of yellow gold, and still we view
Masses of silver, heaped and strewn as though
From some vast silver temple’s overthrow—
Marbles and precious ore that might bedeck
The halls of monarchs with resplendent glow—
Jasper and syenite and jeweled speck—
As though a world of mines had burst in plen-
	teous wreck.

Fair land of marvels! Where Aladdin’s lamp
And Ali Baba’s treasure-cave are ours!
Where giant force has left his Titan stamp,
And genial nature gemmed her vales with
	flowers!
Thy gushing spring shall send refreshing
	showers,
Till all thy waste lands ring with harvest glee.
The weary thousands seek the restful bowers
Where health springs, jocund, from thy island
	sea,
Thy glad streams turn the wheels of strong-
	armed industry.
In thy young days thy horoscope we draw,
And stars auspicious tell of fates benign.
Symbol of order, industry and law,
The murmurous hive is emblem fitly thine.
Still ’round that hive thy sego lilies twine—
Manna for man and nectar cup for bee;
Born in the mountain where the sunbeams shine.
Its peerless bloom is type of liberty;
Be loyal like thy bees, and, like thy lilies, free.

Where from the heights the mountain torrents
	spring,
The eagle builds aloft her craggy nest,
Meets the new morning, poised on steady wing.
And soars through ether from the topmost
	crest—
Thy eagle’s flight be thine, oh, Utah blest
In that, from earliest days, thy children stand
With equal power to seek the highest, best,
And noblest; knowing thy impartial hand
Fetters no God-given soul in all thy favored land.

Onward and upward! of the sister states
Youngest; but gifted with a priceless dower!
Fortune waits, knocking at thy opening gates;
Press to the front in thine auspicious hour!

[Column 3]
Give to the world the best work in thy power:
Reach to each noble cause a helping hand;
And should dark clouds above the nation lower,
Rise in thy might, for God and native land,
Wave wide thy stars and stripes, and with them
	conquering stand.
						ORIELLE CURTIS.


', 'TOUR OF GERMANY.

	One event after another has followed in such
quick succession as to appropriate all my time,
so that I have had no time for letter writing.
	In traveling through Germany most beautiful,
wonderful sights have I beheld, and so much
have I seen that has delighted me that it would
give me much pleasure even briefly to describe
it all, and write a full account of the same, but
this I cannot do, as a full description would fill
whole volumes, for the whole broad Empire of
Germany have I seen, and oh, how quickly and
how happily to me the days have passed, almost
like a long, beautiful dream has it seemed, as the
ruins, the mountains, the forests, the pretty vil-
lages and the grand old cities have each and all
been beheld by my admiring eyes like the
scenes of a gorgeous panorama to gaze at them
for a moment and then pass to something
strange!  I will try to tell you something of the
things which have pleased me most.  We spent
a few hours in Carlsruhe, the capital of the
Grand Duchy of Baden, then went to Heidel-
berg, a visit I shall never forget.  We arrived
there in the evening, and the following morn-
ing we started out early to walk to the Castle.
It was a beautiful spring morning, the air so
fresh and filled with the perfume of the blos-
soming fruit trees, and the songs of birds just
returning from southern homes and rebuilding
their nests in the magnificent trees of Heidel-
berg.  The trees were just adorning themselves
in their green foliage, and all nature was smiling
at its own loveliness.  It made me happier than
ever to have such a bright day for our visit, for
I long had thought and wished for the pleasure 
of visiting these famous old ruins and rambling
among them. 
	The only attraction at Heidelberg is the old
castle, but when one once sees that, he no long-
er wonders that thousands of people travel every
summer to the place only to look at the ruins.
The castle stands on the side of a high moun-
tain which is situated at the edge of the Black
Forest, and from its elevated position presents a
very imposing appearance.  Climbing up the
terraced walks the castle itself is lost to the
sight, but the great garden walls and the tall, 
graceful trees present a charming appearance, 
and partly prepare one for the beautiful scene
awaiting us farther on.  These walls inside of
walls, and the deep most all around show how
strongly the place was fortified, and how safe it
seemed from all intruders; now its boldness and
greatness has departe, and the bare grey walls
are mellowed by the greenest moss, the most
beautiful ivy, the most perfect and delicate
ferns, and the ruins stand as a monument in
memory of past glories, but the most beautiful
monument i have ever seen, so full of tender-
ness, sadness and noble sentiments engraven by
the hand of age in characters more touching
than a chisel ever wrought, in words more sub-
lime than pen ever wrote; the deep lines and
crevices, the broken towers, the mouldering
walls all covered with clinging ivy, which
hangs so gracefully from every side, softening
and brightening the otherwise rude and sombre
walls that there is no went of other ornament
here, for nature has wrought a work more beau-
tiful than man could do.
	The castle overlooks the city and the Neckar
rier; as I gazed at it from the old bridge it im-
pressed me with a feeling of awe and admiration.
Its broken towers and ivy covered ruins tell their
own sad history, such tales of pomp and power, 
of distress and ruin.  They stand like monu-
ments of past glory and the mars and blurs and
cracks and fissures are like engraven histories,
telling the deeds of the kings, princesa nd war-
riors who have dwelt therein.  We strayed from
room to room, from balcony to balcony, from
tower to tower, all was bare, old and broken, yet
an occasional piece of plaster with a faded fres-
coe on, or a broken piece of stone carving told
that once the place had been adorned with ar-
tistic workmanship.  As we strolled through
the first long halls nothing could be seen but
bare walls, low ceilings and an iron grating, 
which enclosed a dark cell; with a shudder at
the thought of the poor unfortunate who had
been imprisoned there, we passed on- through a
large, low archway into the inner court. how 
could we better tell what the past has been--for
on the inner wall still remains some of the beau-
tiful decorations, and this court yard was once 
very grand.
	The plainness of the outside was all for the
sake of having the walls firmer and stronger for
defense, but the inner walls were most magnifi-
cently ornamented, and the flowers and foun-
tains were very tastefully arranged.  On the left
of the palace, and it still possesses many of its
features of elegance.  It is said to have been one
of the finest places of architecture in the world. 
We first entered this part and from throne
room to cooking apartments wandered, and
found now no difference; all had assumed the
same homely aspect.  The walls are all very
thick, in some parts twenty-seven feet, and the
halls and passages are so narrow and crooked
that one might always feel safe though an one
my were without trying to gain entrance; but
with all its strength and plans for defense this
mighty castle was taken by the French and
kept by them from some time.  The tall tower of
this otto Heinrich's bau was twice struck by
lightning, and what an enemy could not do to
destroy it the elements did. When the French
were compelled to leave the castle they tried to
destroy it by exploding powder but so well is the
place built that little harm was done by even
that. The old tower at the back, called the blown
up tower, was parted, and half lies in the moat
below, while half still stands in its old place in 
the wall. 
	This is the prettiest and most picturesque part 
of the castle, the ivy grows more luxuriantly
here, and the ferns hang temptingly over the
shattered walls.  We spent a long morning
wandering from tower to cellar, but all is now
the same, a wreck, a ruin.  From some parts of 
the castle most beautiful views can be had of
the queer old city below, the Neckar river with
its boats and fishermen, and hte mountains
skirting the Black Forest.  A part of the castle
is in good preservation and is used for a muse-
um; here are kept old relics of the astle and
pictures of  different princes who lived there,
swords and armour, manuscripts and jewels, 
quite an interesting collection.
	Here are also pictures of a Heidleberg student
named Sand, who drew the fatal number, and
so had to kill Kotzbue, a writer, who had writ-
ten articles displeasing the students.  Young
Sand took leave of his companions and walked
to annheim, where he fulfilled his design, com-
mitting the dreadful deed for which he was af-
terwards beheaded.  We very reluctantly left
the castle after we had viewd it from every pos-
sible way.
	There seemed to be a certain charmior fascina
tion about the place which made me long to lin-
ger, but we had to leave for Manheim in the
afternoon, and so slowly and thoughtfully wend-
ed our way down the pretty paths towards the
city.
	In Mannheim Brother Lyman met us at the
station and took us to the home of a Brother
Pfost, where we had dinner and met several
of our Church members.  In Mannheim and
Ludwigshafen we have quite a large branch,
and Brother Lyman is working so well with 
them. We stayed three days, visiting all the
time among the Saints, most of them are hard
working people, but they made us so welcome, 
and seemed delighted to see us.  There is no
liberty for holding meetings, and it seemed very
sad to see so many souls thirsting for knowledge
and longing for the privilge of worshipping God
in the proper way.
	The Rhine divides the two cities, Mannheim
and Ludwigshafen, but they are connected by a 
fine iron bridge, over which both the train and
the street cars run; at these places the people
suffered much at the time of the Rhine mun-
dation, and one of our brethren had his home
swept entirely away.  Sister Postel who enter-
tained us very nicelyand has done so much to-
wards entertaining the Elders, died since we
were there.  She was a very sweet woman, and
I guess many of our missionaries will long re-
member her for the kindness she has shown
them.  The walls of her home were decorated
with the pictures of the different missionaries
who had been there, and among them I saw
Rulon's and Abram's, others that I knew and
several whose faces were not familiar, but whose
names were.  The sisters came to the house
where we had stayed when we were leaving to
bid us good-bye, and they gave me a beautiful
bouquet of flowers and seemed so sorry to have
us leave. 
	We went from there to Frankfort on the Main.This city is one of the first in Germany, noted
principally for its extensive commerce.  It con-
tains many large and splendid stores, and the
narrowest and crookedest streets.  Here is the
home of the Baron Rothschild, his office is on 
the Juden Strausse, or street of the Jews, an
old narrow and dirty street said to have once
been very picturesque, and is still one of the
sights of the city.  In this street most of the
Jews lived, and on public days and holidays
they were locked in this street and not allowed
to go into any other part of the city; but they
thrived and flourished, nevertheless, and now
own the first stores and houses in the city; The
Rothschild family were among these persecuted
Jews.
	This city was the birthplace of Goethe, and all
tourists visit his old home; there in an open 
square stands a large monument of Goethe,
which is very fine.  The nicest building in the city
is the Opera House, it is built in the style of the 
Grand opera at Paris.  Here we heard Wagner's 
deed, and I was so glad to have an opportunity
of hearing it.  Between the acts we walked
through the grand promenades and into the res-
taurants; such gorgeous rooms and staircases,


[Column 1]
and the ladies were most beautifully dressed,
but Jewesess always are finely clad, and most of
the audience were of that race. 
	At Berlin we went through the Berlin Muse-
um, the pride of the city. I enjoyed the sights 
in that famous city very much. At Dresden I 
spent a most glorious day among the sublime
paintings of the Dresden gallery. We arrived
there on the evening of the fifth of April, and 
saw a fine historical play in the theatre; in the 
morning we visited the castle, and were shown 
through the Green Vault, where the finest dis-
play of jewels and works of art in the world are 
exhibited. I was much interested, and would 
have been still more so had not our guide inci-
dentally remarked that Raphael's Sistine Ma-
donna was covered and could not at present be
seen. My disappointment was too great for
words, and my eyes kept filling with tears to
think that I had come so far and now right in 
the city with this wonderful work of the great
Raphael and must be denied the pleasure of see-
ing it.	*	*	I waited to pass through al
the beautiful rooms and looked most admiringly
at the great diamonds which August the Strong
used to wear for coat and vest buttons; the larg-
est green diamond in the world; and oh such
cases of emeralds, sapphires, rubies, and three
cases of diamonds alone in the most beautiful 
settings, sword handles, hat plumes, belt buc-
kles, and all sorts of ornaments. When we had 
finished looking at them we hastened to the gal-
lery and passed through the different rooms, in 
search of this treasure which we feared more
and more we could not see, for repairing and 
cleaning was going on all around, and we went 
through many empty rooms. At last the room 
was reached, and I entered almost trembling,
for the walls in front were bare, but turning
round the beautiful face of the Madonna greeted
me.
	I almost wept now with delight, and we stood
silently and earnestly gazing upon this most 
touching and eloquent picture. It seemed most

[Column 2]
could surpass it, if indeed it is equalled. Van-
derwerff's Expulsion of Hagar and Ishmael is 
very lovely and pleased me greatly. There are 
quite a number of Vanderwerff's pictures, an
all are said to have once been worth thei
weight in gold. Of the modern masters, De
freggio's pictures are most pleasing, still there
are many others very fine, among them a life
size picture of Napoleon in his royal robes; this
was presented to the King of Saxony by Napo-
leon himself, and is a work of great beauty. 
	At Leinz'g we saw some interesting histori-
cal places. It was where the great battle of na-
tions was fought, and the French under Napo-
leon were defeated and driven from the city. A
stone on an elevation some few miles out from 
the city marks the place where Napoleon stood
watching the movements of the battle. 
	Last night we took supper in "Auerbach's
Keller," the old place where Ooethe first re-
ceived ideas for his Faust. The city of Leipzig
is not so very beautiful, every thing is so old
and dingy, but it is, nevertheless, most interest-
ing for many reasons, principally for having
been the birthplace of so many of Germany's
greatest men. Wagner was born here, Schiller, 
Goethe, Boch and Mozart all lived here many
years. One of the finest musical conservatories
in Germany is here, and the supreme law courts
of the nation are also at Leipzig. How strange
it seems that I should be visiting the birthplaces
and homes of these great poets and musicians. 
Each place we go to there is some historical as-
sociation connected with it to render interest
and attraction, and, oh, I am so delighted with 
it all. 
	Eisenach is a quiet little city, and its chief
attraction is the castle of Wartburg; it is also
the birthplace of Bach, the musician, and the 
early home of Luther; the home is still shown 
where he lived while attending school here. I 
spent a pleasant afternoon visiting the castle
and looking at the Therringian forest from its 
tower. 
In my next I will give you the description of 

[Column 3]
ter-day Saints, and involves much which is not
understood by the outside world. President
Taylor and  a party of brethren and sisters went
up on Wednesday morning, the 14th, that pre-
liminary preparations might be made. The oc-
casion of the dedication will be one of rejoicing, 
as it opens up another channel whereby the 
living may act in holy ordinances, not only for 
themselves, but those who have already passed
into the spirit world. 
The present number of the paper closes the 
twilfth volume of th EXPONENT. As the years
have rolled by the paper has gradually increased
in popularity and influence, as well as in its sub-
scription list. During the past year and a half
some serious difficulties have opposed the finan-
cial interests of the paper. In January, 1883,
the bursting of the water pipes in the Council
Houee destroyed the Editorial Office and much 
of the furniture, as well as valuable books and
papers to a considerable amount. In June the 
fire that destroyed the Council House destroyed
all except the desk containing books and a few 
other things, we cannot estimate the loss at less
than three hundred and fifty dollars, besides
many of the things being such as could not be
replaced. However, we hope to do something
towards making the EXPONENT more interesting
in its appeara ce, at least, as it enters upon its 
"teens." There has never been a time when so
many sample copies were sent for from all parts 
of the civilized world, as there has been during
the past few months. The question now agitat-
ing the  public mind in regard to Mormon wom
en and their views of marriage have awakened
many who desire to ascertain facts for them-
selves. Let Mormon women speak for them-
selves and give their own views upon all the 
subjects that interest the community at large,
and as far as is practical discuss the popular 
questions of the day. By assisting to awaken
thought, arousing dormant energies, enlighten-
ment often comes, and there is no better medi-
um for the growth and improvement than the 
press. Mormon women are much talked of then


will be by all minds of the same calibre as his
own. It is really refreshing to read something
from an intelligent, well-educated gentleman,
that has in its views liberality, justice and com-
mon sense–all of which are exemplified in his
"new view of Mormonism," its Federal rulers,
their administration, and other phenomana.
	At this point I took up the pamphlet that lay
by me, thinking I; would make a few extracts,
but it is all so good and true I found I must ex-
tract the whole if I began, so I will only pray
that it may have a wide circulation, that the
prejudices of its readers may be removed as were
those of the gentlemanly writer of "An English
view of the case."
						HANNAH T. KING.
	Salt Lake City, May 5th, 1884.


WOMAN'S EXPONENT. 189

The Cincinnati Musical Festival opened
on the 11th of May and was held four days;
the chorus numbered 825 voices and the
band 102 pieces.  It is said to have rivaled
in its character and importance the most
ambitious efforts of New York or Boston and

decorated as if for a great victory; every
public building was hung with banners,
