WOMAN'S EXPONENT.


', 'SOME INTERESTING FACTS.

BY DR. E. S. BARNEY.

	Bathing may be considered from a two fold
point of view; as hygiene, or as a means of
preserving health, by keeping the skin in a
state fit for performing its functions, and as a
therapeutic agent of great power, or for the
cure of diseases.
	In a general sense it means the immersion of
the body in a fluid different in kind or temper-
ature from the common air. Water is the or-
dinary medium employed for baths, but vapor
and heated air are likewise in use for the pur-
pose.
	The great use of bathing must be obvious
when we consider that the skin is not to be re-
garded as a mere covering of the body, but as
an organ, the healthy condition of which is of
vast importance to the well being of the whole
frame, but especially of the stomach and liv-
ing membrane of the lungs, with which as mu-
cous membrane, it has the closest sympathy;
in which, if chronic inflamation exists, a cold
bath should not be used. When we consider
the anatomy of the skin, that in each papilla
of the skin is located the terminal extremity of
a nerve, then we can realize why a restless
child can so quietly sleep after the soothing ef-
fect of a short warm morning both; and a
tired, fevered man after a hard days work can
arise in the morning so refreshed and invigora-
ted, having had a cool or warm bath the night
before; it lowered the temperature, removed
the soreness of the flesh, the irritableness of
the nerves.
	The temperature of the human frame, in a
state of health, continues nearly the same in
whatever circnmstance it is placed. The tem-
perature of an adult is generally from 97° to
98 1\2°; the latter is called normal. In some
cases of disease it is above this standard, in
other conditions it sinks far below. The func-
tion by which the body is enabled to preserve
this uniformity of temperature are intimately
connected with the nervous system, and the
respiratory organs by which heat is developed.
The skin performs the function of regulating
it, especially in reducing it when too high.
When the body is placed in a medium, or low-
er temperature than itself, the heat is abstract-
ed more or less rapidly from the surface, which
would have the effect of chilling it were not
this counteracted by the internal development.
(Should never use cold baths at night after the
fatigue of the day, but warm water instead, as
the frame then requires soothing.) When sur-
rounded by an aëriform medium much higher
than itself, the exhalation from the surface,
both of the skin and lungs are greatly aug-

[Column 2]

mented; that from the skin being thrown off in
sensible and insensible prespiration, that from
the lungs in the form of vapor, the evapora-
tion attending these processes occasion a reduc-
tion of temperature. The quantity of water
thus disposed of in twenty-four hours is esti-
mated at 1 1\2 pints. The skin is capable of re-
moving, when acting in harmony with the laws
of life, in the same length of time, by the esti-
mate of Lavoisier, 20 oz. of waste; the reten-
tion of this from unchanged clothing and other
causes results in injury to the system; if this
channel is neglected it becomes dry and harsh
and unfit for its functions; some other organs
must then supply its place; as the kidneys, but
this organ may have too much to do, thus
working for both, and may become diseased;
in fact neglect of cleanliness is the most fertile
source of disease, both of the skin itself and
other parts. Cleanliness in our habitation is
as necessary to health as personal cleanliness.
Some people are too indolent to bathe at all,
and some at different times of the day before
and after breakfast, another will persist in tak-
ing a cold shower bath before breakfast; it may
thus invigorate or debilitate. There is a pro-
cess in the system which is called the excreting
process; now if this is deficient in its action and
a retention in the system from closed pores,
and a glued substance over those pores, then
the health becomes impaired. Remove the
scales of the dead epidermis and the effete ma-
terial and prevent its absorption, and keep the
pores open, especially in fever; then apply oil
to soften the skin and allay irritation of the
nerves and aid nutrition by absorption.
	The sea ebbs and flows twice a day, but the
great river of life ebbs and flows once in twen-
ty-four hours, attaining its maximum height at
12 m., and its minumum at midnight, hence,
at 11 a.m. is a good time to take a bath before
dinner. 
	How often should a healthy person bathe?
Twice a week in winter months, and every
other day in summer months. Young child-
ren breathe but imperfectly through the lungs,
hence, depend on the skin for a portion of the
arëation of the blood, and they should be
bathed every day in warm water, in a warm
room, and not exposed to cold, always using the
thermometer, for if too hot water is used it wiil
be very depressing. Delicate, feeble persons
of all ages require a higher temperature of a
bath, and a shorter stay in it than others, as
85° or 90°. Baths, when properly conducted,
are a luxury, and a child will crave it and
leap from its mother's lap with joy on its ap-
proach; it only injures them by improper appli-
cation; hence, children should be accompanied
in their baths with an adult, in order to regu.
late the manner of their bath. From the child
that has his face and neck washed every morn-
ing in cold water you seldom hear a complaint;
But a person wearing the "boa," and is doubly
wrapped is constantly exposed. View the sai-
lor, who accustoms himself to an open collar,
his skin is prepared to resist exposure of the
open sea. Nothing is better to harden and
toughen the tender skin than water and air;
this fact being recently considered, the surgeon
does not hasten to wrap, but exposes the ex-
tremity of an amputated limb. The cold bath,
when used by a person in health, increases the
tone of the habit, strengthens the digestive or-
gans, and by diminishing the sensibility of the
whole system, especially of the skin, ren-
ders it less susceptible to atmospheric impres-


[Column 1]

sions from cold, wet and sudden changes of
temperature—thus contributing to what is
termed a robust, or athletic constitution;
hence, the beneficial effects of using a cold bath
daily, and that in the early morning when just
out of bed by surgeons, and those exposed to
frequent changes of climate. The coldness
and length of the bath should be used accord-
ing to the vigor, and debility, and age of the
person. When the shock, as a stimulus to the
nervous system in case of debility, is the prin-
ciple desired, the water should be very cold,
and the time of remaining in the bath only mo-
mentary; the water may be even dashed on,
and immediately and thoroughly dried to bring
on the reaction; by reaction I mean a sensation
of warmth, after the cold of the first immer-
sion. The fluids are repelled from the surface
to the internal organs, which are thus stimula-
ted and roused to action, in consequence of
which the blood is propelled through the whole
capillary system, more particularly of the sur-
face, carrying with it life and renovated ener-
gy. At first the skin shrinks, becomes cooler
and paler, and the respirations are more hur-
ried than before; but this is almost immediately
succeeded by an equally universal sensation of
warmth, or a reaction; if the water is left be-
fore a second attack of cold or chill the pulse
becomes fuller and stronger than before immer-
sion. The skin recovers the plumpness it has
lost, and is succeeded by a feeling of buoyancy,
and an increased animal power. No second
glow ever succeeds the second chill, above men-
tioned, but the debility increases with the
length of immersion.
	A cold duck is sometimes used to sluggish
ulcers.
	If to obviate the shock instantly applied to
the body, and the object is to obtain a refresh-
ing action as in febrile diseases, and inflammation,
water should be applied at first, not suddenly,
only cool and with a sponge, repeating the ap-
plication till the desired effect is produced.
	The amount of reaction depends on the cold-
ness and length of time. One says that a cold
bath is stimulating; another a sedative, or even
debilitating; so it may be, but the truth is, it is
the mode of using it; whether salt or fresh, hot
or cold, and the condition of the patient, the
time of a bath should not be more than ten or
twenty minutes. After a cold bath there
should be a reaction, if not it is injurious; if
the strength of the patient is exhausted then
it would be debilitating; while to another, in
full vigor, it would be a tonic in the highest de-
gree, and this is in proportion to the vigor of
the circulation on the surface. With feeble
circulation and cold extremities, should not
use the cold bath, unless improving the circula-
tion and warmth, before entering by an inter-
nal remedy or exercise. The whole effect of a
bath may be distinguished into, 1st, the shock,
the effect of which is stimulating; 2nd, the cool-
ing effect; 3rd, the counteracting or astringent
effect; 4th, the reaction.
	Cold bathing may be applied so as to insure
one or the other of these actions over any of
the rest, according to circumstances, when all
are not desired. The most important circum-
stance respecting a bath of any kind is its tem-
perature.
	Cold, sea bathing is a sedative, an indirect
tonic. The warm bath is a relaxant. The hot
bath a stimulant.
	Cold bath is from 33° to 60°; cool, from 60°
to 70°; temperate, 75° to 85°; tepid, 85° to 92°;
warm, 92° to 98°; hot, 98° to 112°; vapor, 100°
to 130°, and upwards. Tepid bath, used for
invalids and children, and for cleanliness, 20
or 30 minutes. Warm baths are slightly stim-
ulating, then soothing, and allay irritation of
the nerves, mends the pulse and appetite. Hot
baths are a powerful stimulant and one should
never remain in till exhausted; 5 or 10 minutes
arouses the nerves and general vital action.

[Column 2]

	Time of a bath for young children, 3 min-
utes. A child at 20 weeks, the temperature
should be above blood heat, 98 1/2°, or not less
than 92°. At 10 months, 90°. At 8 or 10
years, not below 78°, which is considered sum-
mer heat. In a high fever a person can be lift-
ed in a sheet and laid in a cold bath at 90°,
and cooled down to 68°, or about 20° less than
when placed in; it then produces the cooling ef-
fect instead of the shock, which might produce
too great a reaction for a debilitated person if
at once placed into the cold. Water can be
sprinkled on them from a watering pot, while
they are lying on an oil cloth, and hot bottles
can be placed at the feet at the same time. If
heat is abstracted from the body too rapidly it
may cause an exhausting effect on the part of
the system to keep up the temperature.
	One German professor said, when the pa-
tient's temperature was 104° to put them into a
bath of 94° and reduce it to 68°. Another
said, put them in 10 minutes, at 68°, and re-
peat it so as to keep down the temperature.
	In Vienna hospitals they put a patient into a
bath of 65° for 20 or 30 minutes, and repeat it
seven or eight times in twenty-four hours, and
it was reported that it was a very successful
treatment. If there is slight chilliness felt the
patient must be moved immediately to a warm
bed. Some of the hospitals in Germany put
their patients into a bath and let them stay so
long that the skin is fairly parboiled in appear-
ance. Rain water is nice for a bath, or a little
soda added to our common running water for
a soft bath. Bathing to prevent disease as a
medicine, it can be used as a tonic, a cool spray
regularly applied so as to produce the tonic ef-
fect. Heat, suddenly applied, will cause mus-
cular contraction. A nerve in a muscle dip-
ped in water 110° will contract violently.
Baths, as means of administering medicine, by
placing in the bath such substances as will
stimulate the skin. As an antipyretic cold
bath, vinegar has a good effect. Friction baths
can be used wet or dry—dilute alcohol, ammo-
nia, a little red pepper, either will be accept-
able. Keep a bottle filled with alcohol, sprin-
kle a little on crash, made in the form of
gloves, and with them rub the body, one's self
or by assistant. Some patients are chronic, or
aenemic, and too weak to produce a glow by
friction, then it must be done for them; for if
the glow is not produced after a bath it does
harm. Massage has proven to be of great val-
ue, sometimes, in cases of dyspepsia; the pa-
tient needs active action. Formerly cold baths
in lung diseases and typhoid fever, were contra
indicated, but not now considered so; yet in
case of ulceration of the bowels in typhoid fe-
ver, the moving of a patient in bathing might
cause perforation and serious results, and a pa-
tient being low, then the shock might paralyze
the heart, and death, or the lungs might be-
come engarged by a cold bath, and death take
place. Immersion in cold water to a delicate
person is not without possible injurious effects,
and may be followed by depression. A deli-
cate person should take a good breakfast an
hour or two before bathing. Care should be
taken in this as in all other therapeutic agents.
Wine in some cases can be given, as it relaxes
the capillaries of the surfaces, and while the cold
bathe drives the blood internally and contracts
the capillaries, the wine may benefit in this
case by dilating the capillaries, and thus invit-
ing the blood to the surface.
	In pneumonia and pleurisy the Germans use
cold compresses. An acid bath is used in some
Heptaic (liver) disease, 3 pints of muriatic acid
and 2 pints of nitric acid; use 1/2 teacupful to 2
gallons of water. Mercurial baths are good in
some cutaneous diseases. Electric baths are
good in paralysis; they excite or stimulate all
the functions, especially the circulation and
secretion. Iodine baths to produce a aubefaci-
ent, sometimes even to the pealing off of the

[Column 3]

skin, 3 quarts of Iodine, and twice as much
of Iodite of Potash, to 1 gallon of water. 
Vapor or Russian baths, water is placed over
a hot stone, and a patient will pass from them
to a cool or cold bath with pleasure. Used in
rheumatism and cutaneous affections, or a hot
duck bath of 110°, in rheumatism is used.
Water baths are used as an eliminative in dis-
eases of the kidneys; nothing removes the
watery secretions more effectual than the Tur-
kish bath, used with care, nothing is better, un-
less it is a Gabaranda sweat. A Turkish bath
is where there is a gradual increase of temper-
ature, after which the patient undergoes sham-
pooning.
	The skin is to the man what the foliage is to
the tree; a vast, external lung, with millions of
pores, of glandules and of papillae, and an im-
mense net-work of arteries and nerves. It is
the complement and supplement of the internal
lungs. With the aid of the Turkish bath the
skin breathes and transpires in place of the
lungs. There is an analogy between the skin
and mucous membrane, as the internal skin is
a continuation of the external, at the continu-
ity of which the epidermis is changed to epith-
elium. The external skin, modified by the
Turkish bath, becomes the modifier of the in-
ternal skin; hence a bath modifies and im-
proves digestion.
	Sweaty hands and feet are improved by
bathing them in cold water and then rubbing
them with a small quantity of the Tincture of
Belladonna. Cold feet are inproved by bath-
ing them in cold water, then apply rapid fric-
tion.
	Cold Sit Baths are in some complaints brac-
ing or tonic, twenty minutes at a time. If on
taking cold there may be Uremie convulsions,
sometimes just before death, where crystals
form on the body. A man had these crystals
form on his mustache as fast as wiped away, he
had convulsions and died in a few hours.
Baths, then, should be used to remove from
the blood urea and uric acid, as they are pois-
onous. In Eczema a person will have oxylu-
ria; whether one is the cause of the other is
not proven, but there is a condition of the skin
that hinders oxydation.
	What of sea bathing? The amount of good
derived from these watering places is principal-
ly from the change of air and scenery, diet,
etc, more than the bath. The exposure to cold
and the unsuited temperature of the water, and
the long remaining in the water, and that, too, 
sometimes when the body is over heated, is
positively injurious. Sometimes it has result-
ed in congestion of the internal organs, cramps
and spasms of some of the muscular organs, or
the muscles themselves, and in some cases pa-
ralysis of a greater or less portion of the body,
and the rays of the hot sun on the head while
in the water is also deleterious, sometimes pro-
ducing what is called sun stroke, and this may
result in paralysis. On the whole more harm
results from sea bathing than good, and young
people come in contact in these places with
a degree of immorality and vice. Sea bathing
is more beneficial where the water is shallow,
and has become warmed by the rays of the sun.
	A person bathing all over is less liable to
take cold than when only a portion of the body
is bathed. A sponge bath and a shower bath
are similar in effect. In no disease is a cold
bath more beneficial than in the intervals of
asthma. The chest may be sponged daily with
water, or vinegar and water; with friction it is
also good for common cough—but not suited
to those who have a tendency to consumption.
Its principal action is a preventative of catarrh
and cold. The best time for a spray is on get-
ting out of bed, when there is no perspiration;
it increases the appetite and digestion. Spong-
ing is more proper for delicate persons than a
shower bath, its effect is nearly similar. Ex-
clude the cold air.


']

Name: 30_mrs_guests_wells_party
Representative_Docs:
['EDITORIAL NOTES.
	We had the pleasure of attending the
annual meeting of the Primary Association
in the Tenth ward, Thursday, May 24. It
was twenty-one years since the organization
was effected and was a sort of gala day for
the children. The meeting house was
prettily decorated with flowers of great
variety, and the president, Sister Howarth,
looked as happy as the little ones. She had
prepared a short program in which the chil-
dren did themselves and their teachers great
credit, both in singing and reciting. Of the
Salt Lake Stake Primary officers there were
present Superintendent Camilla Cobb and
Assistant Mary L. Morris, also President
Elizabeth Paul, of the Tenth ward Relief
Society. Brief remarks were made by the
sisters and by Brother Keddington, coun-
selor to the Bishop. The little folks spent
the afternoon at Liberty Park to celebrate
their anniversary. This is one of the Primary
Associations we had the privilege and honor
of organizing, in May 1879. Sister Jennie
McLean was its first president, and was
most faithful and energetic in this and in
all her duties. Sister Howarth seems ad-
mirably adapted to work with the little
ones, and we certainly wish her earnest
endeavors may prove successful in training
some of the children of Zion.
	Mrs. A. W. Carlson gave a Kensington at
her very pretty home, on Friday, June 29,
or one might with all propriety call it a
garden party, for the heat was so intense
that most of the guests preferred to sit out
on the lawn and in the shade of the beauti-
ful trees and the hedge of pretty soft
shrubbery. The view from Mrs. Carlson’s
home is particularly fine, magnificent at
sunset. It was an enjoyable afternoon
notwithstanding the heat. Mrs. Carlson
has a happy faculty of entertaining her
guests, and is a graceful and charming
hostess. There were perhaps forty ladies in
the party, many of them old friends who
had not met each other for years, and re-
newing old acquaintances in this pleasant
fashion makes one feel doubly indebted to
the hostess. Surely gatherings of this sort
without parade or ostentation are pleasant
places in one’s memory afterwards, a change
from the humdrum of daily toil; and really
one should occasionally take these breath-
ing spells on life’s highway, and value the
opportunity of a few hours interchange in
social converse. There were delicious
refreshments served in a rare hospitable
way, five o’clock tea with all the dainties

[Column 2]
one could imagine as well, quite unlike the
English pretty custom of serving tea and
wafers. Mrs. Carlson may certainly con-
gratulate herself upon the success of her
Kensington, tho’ as for sewing, etc. the
ladies were usually occupied in using fans
instead of needles.
	Saturday, July 16, at 3 p. m., the Society
D. R. met at the residence of the writer to
celebrate two important days, “Old Glory”
and the ’’Battle of Bunker Hill.” The
parlors were decorated in blue, and buff and
the stars and stripes were conspicuous here
and there. The portico was festooned with
bunting, while roses in great variety, car-
nations, California poppies, pansies and
larkspur gave the rooms a gala day appear-
ance. Vice-Regent Maria Y. Dougall
presided. Meeting opened with prayer by
Mrs. M. W. Wilcox. The ladies sang
America, Mrs Willard Cannon leading, Miss
Vera Cannon accompanist. Roll call was
responded to by patriotic sentiments.
Several letters were read from absent mem-
bers, and informally recollections of the
original occasion being observed were made
the theme of the speakers. Mrs. Willard
Cannon sang with spirit and thrilling
melody, “The Star Spangled Banner,” and
Mrs. Annie W. Cannon read most feelingly,
“The Flag Goes By.” After the
exercises ice cream, cake and lemonade
were partaken of, and as the guests were
saying their good byes, they carried away
handsfull of roses. May the fragrance of
that day be as fresh and sweet in their re-
membrance as the roses were on that bright,
sunny day in beautiful June.
	On Tuesday, June 25, Mr. and Mrs.
Morris B. Young gave a dinner party and
evening entertainment at their hospitable
home in Forest Dale. Notwithstanding
the heat was intense a sumptuous dinner
was served at 3 o’clock in the afternoon.
The dining room was fragrant with the
sweet flowers and the table decorations
were very pretty. The guests of honor
were Mrs. Hannah Snow Lewis and Mr.
and Mrs. Oliver Snow, of Ohio, cousins of
President Lorenzo Snow and of the hostess,
Mrs. Young, his daughter. Other guests
were: Mrs. Z. D. H. Young, Mrs. M. I.
Horne, Mrs. Margaret P. Young, Mrs.
Emma Woodruff, Mrs. Alice and Mrs.
Regina Pond and the writer. The conver-
sation was very highly flavored with rem-
iniscences of early days in the valley, in
which Sister Eliza R. Snow and the vener-
able President Lorenzo Snow had taken
part. Later on towards evening quite a
party arrived and there was music and
singing, and the porches and shade of the
trees was filled with the younger people.
President F. Y. Taylor and wife, of Granite
Stake, Bishop Millen C. Atwood and wife,
of Sugar Ward; Drs. Leslie W. and M. O.
Snow; Elder Le Roi C. Snow and Miss
Ford and many others of the Snow family.
Ice cream, cake and lemonade were served
to all the large number of guests.
	On Friday afternoon, June 15, quite a
large party of ladies assembled by invitation
of Mrs. C. R. Howe, at her residence in this
city, in honor of the 77th birthday anniver-
sary of her mother-in-law, Sister Julia C.
Howe, who is one of the best known
women in the city, and is universally be-
loved. She has been connected with chari-
table and philanthropic work ever since
coming among our people, and is a woman
of exceptional attainments in many respects,

[Column 3]
and has had large opportunities in early
life, and is particularly well acquainted with 
the Bible, which of itself is a rare acquisition.
Sister Howe has not only been associated
with the work of the Relief Society, but
also with the Y. L. M. I. A. and the
Primary Association, and in all the posi-
tions she has been called to fill she has been 
efficient and untiring in her efforts, and she
has the adaptability necessary to success in
managing young people: She has a fine
personality and is remarkably well preserved
for her age, looking much younger than
her years. There were present at the party
Sisters M. Isabella Horne, Bathsheba W.
Smith, Margaret Y. Taylor, Matilda M.
Barratt, Elizabeth Webb, Elizabeth J.
Stevenson, Susan Smith, Lydia Ann, Han-
nah and Susan Wells, and Maria W. Wil-
cox, whose birthday occurs on the same
day, though she is a few years younger.
There were many other sisters present, in
all thirty or more, and the afternoon was
very happily spent in congratulations and
general social conversation and birthday
greetings. The rooms were very prettily
decorated, there were roses, carnations and
sweet peas in abundance. Delicious refresh-
ments were served in dainty fashion, the
hostess, young Mrs. Howe, knowing ex-
actly how to entertain her guests, which is
a happy faculty. May Sister Howe have 
many more such delightful birthdays, tho’ 
we are aware that every year takes from us
one or more who have been accustomed to
meet with us at such reunions.


', 'EMMELINE B. WELLS, LIT. D.
	The year 1912 among other remarkable
things has a twenty-ninth of February thus
giving our dear “Aunt Em” a birthday.
It is quite customary for a family to cele-
brate the coming of age of one of its mem-
bers and this rule was strictly adhered to
by the people of Utah in the coming of
age of their gifted and loved citizen Mrs.
Emmeline B. Wells, who though eighty-
four years old was permitted on this day to
celebrate her twenty-first birthday. It
would be almost impossible to tell the many
expressions of love and admiration sent
to Mrs. Wells during the weeks preceding
and following her anniversary. Beautiful
letters from stake presidents of Relief Soci-
eties throughout the Church, cards and
letters from friends in distant lands, tele-
grams from distinguished people,came pour-
ing in upon her until she was almost over-
whelmed with such evidences of admiration.
DAUGHTERS OF THE PIONEERS.
	On Saturday the 24th of February the
Daughters of the Pioneers devoted their
regular monthly meeting to her honor, the
program including a sketch of Mrs. Wells’s
social and home life by her daughter Mrs.
Annie Wells Cannon, and readings from
her poems by her granddaughter Miss
Katharine Cannon. At this meeting the
Summerhays quartet sang the beautiful
song “Mother o’er Israel” composed in Mrs.
Wells’s honor by Prof. Edward P. Kimball.
The meeting was presided over by Mrs.
Elizabeth R. Wright, the president, who
also paid a tribute to Mrs. Wells. The
honored guest responded in a very quaint
and pretty speech and related some of her
pioneer experiences. The Relief Society
parlors in the Bishop’s building were beauti-
fully decorated for the occasion and refresh-
ments were served during a social hour
after the program.
	UTAH WOMAN’S PRESS CLUB.
	On the evening of the 28th of February
Mrs. Wells was guest of honor at a splendid
entertainment given by the Utah Woman’s
Press Club of which club Mrs. Wells is
founder and honorary president. The
auditorium in the Bishop’s building was
the scene of this occasion, where gathered
a large and representative number of Mrs.
Wells’s friends and admirers to do her honor.
The large room was beautifully decorated
in the national colors with clusters of small
flags about the room and two large ones
at the head of the building forming a dra-
pery for a life size portrait of the guest
of honor which occupied a central space.
Flowers were everywhere in evidence, there
were palms and potted plants and vases
of jonquils, roses and carnations. The
event was presided over by Dr. Jane Skol-
field, president of the club, and the numbers
on the program were introduced by the
secretary, Mrs. Blake.
	A most delightful part of the evening
were the musical numbers furnished by
one of Utah’s most talented daughters, Mrs.
Maggie Tout Browning, who not only sang
her numbers on the program but very hap-
pily responded to the demands of the en-
thusiastic audience and sang a number of

[Column 2]
ballads, “In my Garden,” “The Rosary” and
others.
	“My Mountain Home So Dear,” com-
posed by Mrs. Wells, was the first number,
a quartet, rendered by Fred C. Graham,
Julian Thomas, Jennie Skolfield and Mrs.
Browning.
	Prof. Kimball’s song by the Summerhays
quartet was repeated on this occasion by
request.
	On the stand with Mrs. Wells and the
officers of the Press Club were Governor
William Spry, ex-Governor Thomas, Hon.
B. H. Roberts and other noted people, while
the audience included prominent club wom-
en, educators and literary people of the city.
	In Governor Spry’s address he said that
Mrs. Wells, because of her labors in many
directions for the welfare of the state, be-
longed to the state, for her interest in hu-
manity had been limited by neither creed,
rank, nor politics. “It is her faith, her
dreams, her efforts put into practice
throughout her long life which have helped
to put you and me where we are today.”
	Hon. B. H. Roberts paid a humorous and
pretty eulogy to the guest of honor, saying
he had been watching the career of “Aunt
Em” through her teens and was glad to 
see her emerge from them and arrive at
her majority. He hoped her maturity would
reap the fruits of her youthful dreams
and efforts and that she might live to see
every dream fulfilled. Mr. Roberts said he
would take the liberty of bestowing on
“Aunt Em” a universal degree, for she be-
longed not only to the state but had inter-
national claim both for sympathy and for
admiration, for her work had been of world-
wide scope in aim and effort; he wished
therefore to confer the degree of D. E. W.
L.T., which interpreted means, “Dear Em-
meline, we love thee.” This sentiment
was greeted with great applause.
	Mrs. Annie Wells Cannon read excerpts
from Longfellow’s poem, “Morituri Salu-
tamus,” a favorite poem of her mother’s,
and Miss Nellie Shipp recited one of Mrs.
Wells’s poems, “My Dear Old Garden.” The
Press Club presented Mrs. Wells with a
basket of roses, the presentation being made
by little Miss Done.
	Governor Thomas and others took part in
the program, after which refreshments were
served.
	AT THE BEE HIVE HOUSE.
	Another part of the paper tells of the
dinner party given by President and Mrs.
Julina L. Smith at the Beehive House on
the afternoon of the 29th. After this func-
tion the General Board of the Relief Society
became hostesses at a large reception in the
auditorium of the Bishop’s building which
was the crowning event of the festivities in
honor of this distinguished lady.
	RELIEF SOCIETY RECEPTION.
	On the evening of the 29th the General
Officers and Board of Directors of the Re-
lief Society held an open reception in honor
of their president, Emmeline B. Wells.
	A very large number of friends and rela-
tives gathered on this occasion which was
quite informal and very pleasing.
	The event was distinguished by some
gems of speech and literary thought, many
of them quite humorous, but the event of
the evening was the conferring on Mrs.
Wells the degree of Doctor of Literature by
the Brigham Young University. Among the
guests were the First Presidency of the
Church, members of the Church board of
education, and members of the faculty of
the Brigham Young University, including
President Brimhall. Prof, and Mrs. Keeler,
and Mrs. Ida S. Dusenberry. Ex-Governor
Wells acted as master of ceremonies and
after a felicitous opening speech recited the
following original jingle which together
with Mr. Whitney’s very interesting and
reminiscent speech we reproduce at the re-
quest of many readers.
	JUST OUT OF HER TEENS.
Our dear young Aunt Em is just out of her
	teens,
Now hearken, my children, and learn what it
	means.
She’s had twenty-one birthdays—that’s reck-
	oned a score,
And so she should be twenty-one and no more.
How comes it then, children, that history tells
She has long been the widow of Daniel H.
	Wells?
Has daughters who own up to forty or more,
And children and grand ones and great ones
galore?
She published her poems way back in the
	eighties,
And two of her girls are older than Kate is;
For full forty years she has had no opponent
As writer in chief for the Woman’s Exponent.
All over the world, attending conventions
And lifting her voice for woman’s contentions,
Julia Ward Howe and Anna H. Shaw
And Susan B. Anthony all called her “Ma.”
The pioneers crossed the plains very early,
Yet in one of their trains was our dear little
	girlie;
And the people who lived back in that gener-
	ation
Seemed to regard her with some veneration.
And after the days of the “Mormon” hegira,
in Kanosh, Koosharem, Kanab and Kanarrah,
She founded and organized women’s societies—
Like the pickles of seven and fifty varieties.
The oldest inhabitant recalls with some sport
How he took her to parties down in the Old
	Fort,
How she danced and cavorted and ne’er would
	surrender,
So long as the fiddle was played by Hop
Pender.
The Salt Lake policeman at night on his beat
Knows perfectly well those tired young feet;
She’s the girl without rubbers, stays out until
	two,
Goes home in a hack to East Waterloo.
And there isn’t a boy to a grasshopper knee
	high,
In Manti, Moroni or Nephi or Lehi,
Who wouldn’t stoop down and kiss at the hem
Of that old Paisley shawl that covers Aunt Em.
And who is the man so bold and heroic
Who could stand up and say like a blooming
	old stoic
That evidence counts her age eighty-four,
When of birthdays she’s had but a limited
	score?
Now hearken, my children, and you’ll surely
	see
The good Lord above us knows better than we.
He said to Aunt Em as she came down the
	pike,
“You are going to live just as long as you like.”
So he gave her one birthday in four, don’t you
	see,
And that explains all the controversy.
So hearken, my children, and learn what it
	means,
This dear old young girl is just out of her
	teens.


[Column 1]
	Junius F. Wells also paid a tribute to
“Aunt Em,” expressing the affection with
which the Wells family regarded her, one
of his allusions being that “Aunt Em” had
married Bishop Newel K. Whitney, which
was no doubt the reason that the Bishop
Whitney of today was so famous a poet.
	H. G. Whitney, who was asked to repre-
sent the Whitneys, gave a five minute ad-
dress, largely reminiscent in character, of the
early days passed in “Aunt Em’s” cottage
on State street, almost a second home to
the Whitneys. He praised any movement
that looked to the payment of tributes to
the pioneers while they were still in the
flesh, and said more funeral orations ought
to be delivered in advance of the funerals.
Speaking of the tie between the Whitneys
and Wellses, he said:
	“I remember that when we were very,
very young, I was aware that there was a
family tie of some remote kind that united
us with ‘Aunt Em’s’ branch of the Wells
family, and my brother Orson and I used
to feel a great pride in it, I remember,
indefinable as it was to us. I think we re-
garded the Wells family in the light of a
great clan, something like the McGregors
of Scotland, with ourselves as humble off-
shoots, and ‘Aunt Em’ the link that con-
nected us. I know that the great domain
of the Wellses as it stretched, to my boyish
eyes, over a wide expanse, south of the City
Creek wilds where we Whitneys ranged,
seemed vast and illimitable. To our childish
fancy, the great house that stood on the
Templeton corner was a sort of a huge,
feudal castle, containing an army of servi-
tors, with Brother Snarr as the dreaded
chief warder of the tower.”
	He also mentioned the meetings of the
old Wasatch society that met in “Aunt
Em’s” home, at which she and Squire Wells
were interested listeners. He concluded by
saying that he could not do justice to the
subject of “Aunt Em” if he confined him-
self to prose, and with her permission he
would lapse into poetry with the following:
Proud Wellses, tho’ you claim Aunt Em,
	Your title is the worst,
Your mortgage is a second one,
	The Whitneys hold the first.
So while we’re willing you should share
	Our claim—and won’t oppose it
Please keep that mortgage well in mind,
	Lest some day we foreclose it.
	This couplet was greeted with laughter
and applause and so moved one of the Wells
clan in the audience (Rulon S.) that he
turned out the following impromptu which
he tendered “Aunt Em” at the end of the
session.
	The Whitneys claim a prior right,
		And threaten to foreclose,
	The adjudication of this case
		We’ll leave to Him who knows;
	But this I know and do maintain,
		That you are ours pro tern,
	And in the great eternity
		You’ll always be “Aunt Em.”
	Bishop Orson F. Whitney made some re-
marks and recited two poems. Elder J.
Golden Kimball followed with a characteris-
tic talk on why he loved “Aunt Em.” He
said, “I love Aunt Em because of her can-
dor, truthfulness, honesty, integrity and loy-
alty to her friends, the Church of Jesus
Christ and her fellow beings. She is big

[Column 2]
hearted and generous in her sentiments. The
golden rule is her great morality and
daily life and all its wonderful problems her
great theme. She is not narow minded, in-
tolerant or bigoted, because she has an en-
lightened mind. She is just a great, big-
hearted little woman and I love her because
can’t help it.”
	Mrs. Margaret Blaine Salisbury paid a
loving tribute to Mrs. Wells and said she
was honored in being present and having the
opportunity of voicing her admiration for
one whom she considered a gifted and re-
markable woman.
	President Lund reviewed some of the
labors of the honored guest and eulogized
her wonderful life work, while President
Penrose gave a brief review of her life in a
humorous and appreciative way dwelling
somewhat on her interest in vital public
questions, especially the suffrage for which
she had made a gallant fight.
	The chief feature of the occasion was the
conferring of the degree of Doctor of Litera-
ture by the Brigham Young University of
Provo, the ceremony being conducted by
President Joseph F. Smith, who in a short
address recited the history of the accordance
of the degree, the first to be given any woman
in the state. They felt that she had won this
distinction through her attainments, and
while it added nothing to her individual
gifts, already in evidence, yet it marked the
appreciation in which they were held. He
took pleasure therefore in handing to her
the document conferring the degree.
		THE DEGREE.
	Brigham Young University,
	Provo City, Utah, U. S. A.
	In recognition of the eminent literary
attainments of Mrs. Emmeline Blanche
Wells and upon recommendation of the
General Church Board of Education, and of
the Faculty of Brigham Young University,
the Board of Trustees, by virtue of the au-
thority vested in it, confers upon her the de-
gree of Doctor of Literature with all the
rights, privileges and honors thereunto ap-
pertaining.
	Given this Twenty-ninth day of February,
in the year of our Lord the Nineteen Hun-
dred Twelfth, and of the Brigham Young
University the Thirty-sixth.
			JOSEPH F. SMITH,
		President of the Board of Trustees,
			GEORGE H. BRIMHALL,
				President of the Faculty.
	As the words were spoken the entire
audience rose, and many eyes were moist
during the impressive scene, as Mrs. Wells
responded, wearing the cap and gown. She
expressed her deep feeling at the honor ac-
corded her, and said that it meant much to
her not only as a personal tribute, but as a
matter of honor to her sex. It had always
been a matter of regret to her that great
educational institutions had withheld this
distinction from women, and she hoped that
this would have its influence in showing that
Utah withheld nothing from the women of
the state.
	Mrs. Wells also gave a number of per-
sonal reminiscences.
		AN UNIQUE HONOR.
	No such honor has ever been done a wom-
an before in Utah or indeed in the West;
it is rare anywhere in the world. Yet it
is most fitting that Utah, where equal suf-

[Column 3]
frage prevails and has been popular for
years, should be the first of new common-
wealths where such high recognition should
be given to so thoroughly worthy a recipient
as in this instance.
	The addresses and exercises of the eve-
ning were interspersed with delightful musi-
cal selections, Miss Romania Hyde render-
ing beautiful violin numbers, and a quartet
composed of Geo. D. Pyper, Horace Ensign,
H. G. Whitney and J. D. Spencer giving
two songs.

	All during the day letters, telegrams and
gifts, the latter mostly in the way of flowers,
were showered upon “Aunt Em.” Among
the gifts was a handsome white brocaded
silk gown from the Relief Society Board, a
handsome silk lace scarf from the Oneida
Stake Relief Society and an embroidered
hand bag made of Utah silk from the Box
Elder Stake Relief Society. So many hon-
ors and so much love would almost over-
whelm any one and dear little “Aunt Em”
can only say in reply to it all, “I love you
every one, and my service is yours and our
Father’s in Heaven.”
	TELEGRAMS AND SENTIMENTS.
“There are moments of life that we never
	forget.
Which brighten, and brighten, as time steals
	away;
They give a new charm to the happiest lot,
And they shine on the gloom of the lone-
	liest day.”
			TELEGRAMS.
	Following are a few of the telegrams and
sentiments, the former received on the 29th
of February, the latter received some on that
day and the rest within a few days subse-
quently. There were so many of these in
the form of poems, letters and expressions
of good will that it has been found quite
impossible to use them all.
			ALICE MERRILL HORNE.
					OGDEN, UTAH.
President Emmeline B. Wells,
	Salt Lake City, Utah.
	I cannot refrain from extending my
congratulations and best wishes upon this
anniversary of your birth. . I sincerely hope
you may live to cheer, comfort and bless
your family and the sisters as long as life
is a pleasure to you, and that your last may
be your happiest days.
				JANE S. RICHARDS.
		OCEAN PARK, CAL., Feb. 29, 1912.
	Love and congratulations; may each suc-
ceeding birthday be happier than the last.
			CLARISSA S. WILLIAMS.
				WASHINGTON, D. C.
	Allie joins me in sending on this, your
natal day, greetings, love and best wishes.
Peace, joy and God’s blessings be yours as
long as you live.
					REED SMOOT.
			NEW YORK, Feb. 29, 1912.
	May you be spared to your friends to
continue the good work done for them and
Utah, in health and happiness.
			BERTHA G. M. BAMBERGER.
		OCEAN PARK, CAL., Feb. 29, 1912.
	Congratulations and best wishes on this
day of days. 
				ELIZABETH S. WILCOX.
		(Continued on page 54.)


', 'EDITORIAL NOTES.
	There have been so many entertainments
that ought to be mentioned in this little pa-
per, and so litte space to give to each one
that we have decided to make only brief
notes and apologize to our patrons for not


[Column 1]
giving fuller accounts of interesting social
events.
	June 4, 1908, the 40th anniversary of
the marriage of Brother and Sister W. B. Dou-
gall was celebrated in their handsome new
home N. State Street, in a most beautiful
way without ostentation or show. The
rooms were artistically decorated with roses
principally, as June is the month of roses.
Old friends were invited to participate in
the festivities, and many fervent congratu-
lations were offered as well as gifts and
blessings. The great feature of enjoyment
was the choice vocal and instrumental music
exquisitely rendered for the delectation of
the guests, the selections being made from
old masters. Another feature that will al-
ways mark the event as of great significance
was the eloquent prayer offered by Presi-
dent Joseph F. Smith, dedicating by re-
quest the new home of Brother and Sister
Dougall. The evening was rainy outside,
but within all was peaceful and enjoyable.
The bride of forty years wore her wedding
gown of long ago, and carried a bouquet of
bride roses, and looked as charmingly happy
as though she were in reality the young
bride. The bridegroom bore his honors as
grandly as he could have done on that great
occasion forty years before.
She full of grace and rich in happiness.
He with the sober years of manliness.
	A reception was tendered United States
Senator Reed Smoot, Tuesday evening, June
9, at the home of Brother and Sister Jacob
F. Gates. The affair was unique in many
respects, and rather informal, and very de-
lightful. Hon. Thomas Hull was master of
ceremonies and did the honors admirably,
relieving the host and hostess, so that their
pleasure was enhanced instead of hindered.
Clive’s band furnished excellent music, and
the speeches were short and interesting,
wit and brilliancy interspersed. The speak-
ers were Elder J. G. McQuarrie of the East-
erm Mission, President Joseph F. Smith,
President F. M. Lyman, Hon. Reed Smoot
and Mrs. Reed Smoot, who gave a glimpse
of Washington society, and a real Utah
home in Washington. There were recita-
tions by young talented women, Maud May
Babcock and Mrs. Belle Ross, Apostle H.
J. Grant sang “The flag without a stain,”
and there were other distinguished gentle-
men who spoke briefly and interestingly.
Light refreshments were served during the
evening. Among the distinguished ladies
present we recall the venerable lady Presi-
dent Bathsheba W. Smith, Sister Rhoda
Lyman, and many younger women, wives
of Apostles, and Presidents of Stakes.
Brother and Sister Gates are to be congratu-
lated on having made a perfect success of
this reception.
	Wednesday, June 10, 1908, annual meet-
ing of the Daughters of the Revolution, Utah
State Society, met in room 509 Templeton
Building, to elect officers for the ensuing
year. After preliminany exercises and the
transaction of some business, the following
ladies were elected by unanimous vote of
the members present: Regent, Mrs. Elizabeth
R. Wright; First Vice-Regent, Mrs. Emily Y.
Clawson; Second Vice-Regent, Mrs. Lucy W.
Smith; Recording Secretary, Mrs. Clarissa
Y. Spencer; Corresponding Secretary, Mrs.
Julia F. Lund; Treasurer, Mrs. Emily H.
Willey; Registrar. Mrs. Isabel M. W. Sears;
Historian, Mrs. Julia P. M. Farnsworth;
Chaplain, Mrs. Rachel Campbell. After
the officers were elected the annual meet-

[Column 2]
ing was adjourned for one year, and notice
given that the next regular meeting would
be held on Wednesday, September 16, as
usual after summer vacation.
	Thursday, June 11, was the formal open-
ing of Whitney Hall, a new building erected
in the 18th ward of this city, adjoining the
Ward Chapel. The occasion was some-
thing of a house-warming and was in a way
informal, as the hall is not yet to be dedi-
cated. There were opening exercises of
prayer and singing conducted by the Bishop,
T. A. Clawson, and an eloquent address
suited to the occasion by Elder Orson F.
Whitney, Bishop of the ward for many
years, giving a sort of history of the ward in
the past and explanations as to the name,
all of which was very briefly summed up
necessarily. President Joseph F. Smith
spoke feelingly and reverently of those who
had rendered service in the ward in the past,
and congratulated those who had assisted in
getting so commodious a place for social en-
tertainments. There was excellent music,
vocal and instrumental, followed by danc-
ing and supper. At some future time we
hope to give a good sketch of this building
named for the late Bishop Orson F. Whit-
ney and his illustrious grandfather Presid-
ing Bishop Newel K. Whitney.
	Friday Evening, June 12, the General
Board of the Young Ladies M. I. A. gave a
reception in Whitney Hall to the visiting
Stake officers, the First Presidency, Apos-
tles and Church officials, and the General
officers of the Relief Society and Primary.
It was a very grand affair on a broad scale,
and the music, dancing and refreshments
were all of a high order of excellence. The
spirit of unity and good fellowship was par-
ticularly apparent, and it equaled if it did
not surpass, the yearly entertainments or re-
ceptions of previous years. It is a sort of
Herculean undertaking to give these large
affairs and make them a success. Our young
ladies however have never failed to give sat-
isfaction to all invited, find it is a great
pleasure to entertain so royally those who
are laboring so effectually in the various lo-
calities, far away from headquarters when
they come once a year to receive instruction
from those who preside over all.
	Saturday, June 13, 1908, was Primary day
for the General Society and Stake officers.
Every year the day of the “outing” is look-
ed forward to with eager anticipation by
those from a distance particularly. Wanda-
mere now seems a very popular resort for
summer pic nics and excursions, it has many
attractions, and is kept free from "hood-
lums” which is very desirable. This year
the Primary chose Wandamere, for the enter-
tainment of the Association and guests; the
day was not very propitious but one cannot
always have fine weal her even in June. There
were a large number assembled and more
than an abundance of good things to eat;
the meeting of old friends at times like these
is a very great pleasure and the General
Board of the Primary is noted for the great
courtesy and liberality extended to all inter-
ested in this important work for the children
of Zion.
	This year Utah Daughters of the Pioneers
gathered at Wandamere the 15th of June for
their annual outing, and as Flag Day, June
14, came on Sunday it happened to combine
the two things. The President Mrs. Susa
Young Gates conducted the exercises, had

[Column 3]
Clives band engaged and progra[-] arranged.
There was considerable enthusiasm, some
very fine singing by talented young girls and
excellent music, and quite a number of vet-
erans were present, belonging to various
organizations. Some very good speeches
were made, the President herself being
most happy in her remarks and several of
the speakers particularly the ladies gave
entertaining reminiscences.
	The marriage of Zina Young Card and
Hugh B. Brown took place in the Salt Lake
Temple, Wednesday, June 17, 1908, the
bride is the granddaughter of President
B[-]igham Young and of Sister Zina D. H.
Young one of the most noted women of her
time in the Church, and the daughter of Pre-
sident O. S. and Zina Y. Card. After the
ceremony a wedding breakfast was given at
the home of the bride’s mother at which
were assembled the immediate family of
Sister Zina and a very few invited guests,
among the number the editor who con-
sidered it quite an honor, to be one of so hap-
py a wedding party. The most elaborate
congratulations were given and many gifts
of love for the young couple to take with
them to Canada, where they intend to make
their permanent home, and may it be a pa-
radise on earth and the Holy Spirit dwell
therein.
“Across the threshold led
And every tear kissed off as soon as shed,
His house she enters, there to be a light,
Shining within when all without is night;
A guardian angel o’er his life presiding,
Doubling his pleasures, and his cares dividing.”
	Wednesday from 3 to 6 p. m., June 17,
Mrs. C. F. Wilcox and her daughter Miss
Mona who had but just graduated from the
Utah University gave a reception in honor
of the happy event. It was a very pleasant
and enjoyable affair, although the rain pour-
ed down in torrents, the ladies came, many
in full evening dress; the decorations were
artistic and exquisite and the delicacies
served in the way of refreshments were dain-
tily delicious. As it was also the birthday
of Dr. Wilcox’s mother; she assisted in
receiving the entire afternoon; excellent
music rendered by a string band, added to
the pleasure of the guests who seemed to
enjoy every moment. It was indeed a charm-
ing affair and everything was delightful ex-
cept the weather, and that was out of doors.
	At the home of Mrs. John Groesbeck, Jr.,
Tuesday, June 23, a luncheon party was
given for her mother Margaret G. Clawson,
the guests were most of them Temple work-
ers about 25 or 30. The rooms were prettily
decorated with roses and other June flowers,
and the luncheon was par excellence, and
naturally very appetizing and seasoned with
wit and humor necessarily when Margaret
Clawson and Phebe Kimball get together;
this sort of conversation is good for diges-
tion. Half the pleasure of Sister M. G.
Clawson’s parties is the fine viands and the
other half is the enlivening conversation, it
does not depend much on her guests. She
has always been popular with the young
people, and now she is becoming very popu-
lar with the elderly sisters.
	Mrs. A. W. Carlson gave a garden party
on June 23, at her own residence, out doors
and in; some of Sister Clawson’s guests left
early to attend this Garden party, which was
made very attractive by the beautiful trees
and shrubbery, and especially by the cherry
trees, full of ripe red cherries, that added


[Column 1]
greatly to the beauty of the landscape. Mrs.
Carlson is a charming hostess and a prime
favorite with her friends, who are very num-
erous. We are sorry we have not space to
elaborate more fully on these two parties 
that occurred on the same day, but we can
remember them, and the ladies we met at
both places, and the good fellowship that
brings those who meet often closer to-
gether and year after year the ties grow
stronger.
	Thursday, July 9, was the anniversary of
the birthday of Mrs. Sophia Sharp and her
daughter Mrs. James E. Jennings gave a 
party in her honor to a large number of her
mother’s relatives and friends, in her hand-
some and commodious home. As it was
the midsummer many of the ladies were in
white, and all in light beautiful raiment
making an artistic picture in the midst of
the elegant furnishings of the rooms and the
exquisite floral decorations. The refresh-
ments were dainty and delicious and beauti-
fully served by little girls in white with rib-
bons, etc.,  the pretty grand daughters of
Mrs. Sophia Sharp and Mrs. Priscilla Jen-
nings. One may be sure there was no lack
of interesting conversation where so many
intelligent ladies were gathered and the par-
ty was therefore a brilliant success in every
respect. The Exponent extends congratula-
tions to the dear little lady, and wishes for
her many more returns of the day with health
and strength to enjoy life, and may her lines
be cast in pleasant places henceforth.
	Saturday, at 12 m., July 11, a luncheon
was given at the Utah University by the
Dean of Domestic Science Miss Lucy Van
Cott, and some special invitations were giv-
in a way informally, the General Officers and
Board of the Relief Society and of the Young
Ladies Associations were invited and the
affair was made very delightful and inter-
esting. New methods were explained, and
the general condition and status of this par-
ticular department were practically explain-
ed. Miss Van Cott seems to have made
good use of her advanced training in Eastern
Colleges, and is very competent to undertake
the superintending of a wider system of the
work, than is possible at present even in the
new University building that her students
occupy at present. She gave a fine talk on
the subject which is in reality a home topic,
Prof. Wm. Stewart spoke on the kindergarten
and kindred subjects a department which is
his pet theme, and Prof. Kingsbury occupied
a few minutes on the general situation of
the University and in praise of the pioneers,
and of their descendants taking a greater in-
terest in the institution founded by them so
many years ago in the new west. The writer
was requested to speak and incidentally al-
luded to the topic which Prof. Kingsbury
had so eloquently and ably referred to, and
also expressed the pride felt by all the early
settlers in the growth and excellence of this
University established in the Great west at
such an early period. Mrs. Martha H. Tin-
gey spoke by special request, expressing the
feeling entertained by the young women in
that organization, to help forward the good
work undertaken by Miss Van Cott which 
was certainly a laudable undertaking and
should be encouraged and patronized by the
community. The lady visitors were shown
through the buildings and explanations were
made to the party in regard to the buildings
and grounds.
	Tuesday, July 21, the Relief Society of the
four Stakes of Zion had their annual outing

[Column 2]
at Wandamere. The day was fair and a sort
of program was expected when the crowd
gathered at the dancing pavilion, Gen. John
Q. Cannon had been asked to act as Master
of ceremonies and though there was not
much of a program there was a very pretty
fairy dance by a dozen or more little girls in
fairy costume. Miss Ivy Evans who has a 
fine soprano voice sang a popular sang; and
Georgie Young played the violin very sweet-
ty, a very beautiful little girl gave a fancy
dance in the most delightful style, Clive’s
band was in attendance furnishing excellent
music.  After the announcement had been
made that the program was over, Brother T.
H. Morrison stepped forward and asked the
privilege of speaking a few words and mount-
ing the improvised platform, he announced
that he desired to present fifty dollars to the
President of the Relief Society, because of 
favors bestowed upon himself and family
when they came to Salt Lake City, strangers
in a strange land without home, and in time
of sickness and death, Sister Bathsheba W.
Smith, General President of the Relief Soci-
ety, came forward and received the donation
in gold, thanking him heartily for the gift.
Gratitude for favors in time of need is an
admirable quality in human character and
all of the sisters present felt to bless Brother
Morrison for his liberality.
