Representative_Docs:
['MALES OF UTAH.

	No man has the right to prescribe to another
what shall be his religious belief, and the
Latter-day Saints, who have added more to the
wealth of this nation than any other class or
colonists—by reclaiming and populating these
barren wastes, being the majority in this Terri-
tory claim the right of self-government, and as
Judge Black said, at the close of his argument
for Utah, (whose name we shall honor in con-
nection with others who have nobly plead the
cause of the oppressed,) "You have no author-
ity to legislate about marriage in a Territory; the
people of Utah have as good a right to their
religion as you have to yours." But if you, who
profess to have gained so much greater light
and wisdom, could show us a better way, or any
better fruits of true civilization, from your own
domestic relations, we would be willing to yield
the point. But when a people assume claims
and power which are unconstitutional and try
to force them upon us "by means flatly pro-
hibited" and "The right to say that the women
of Utah, or the men of Utah, shall not exercise
their religion as they may think it right and
proper, and that they shall be deprived of all
political power in this country because of their
entertainment of opinions upon this subject dif-
ferent from their own," and "that women who
are not found to be bigamists or polygamists," or
to have contracted this tie are to be disfran-
chised, and though we "Mormons" have never
infringed or violated the rights or claims of any


[Column 1]
﻿man or set of men, yet we are not to be allotted
even the claims of the robber or murderer or
criminals in general, but are to be convicted
and punished without judge or jury, we have
neither inclination nor intention to submit to
such an outrage quietly, without even a struggle.
And were we allowed to act upon our constitu-
tional political rights we would first expel from
our midst the characters who "openly and
notoriously" practice their immoralities and
also those who publish the lowest of slanders
against an innocent people, and it would in my
mind not "require the judgment of a court" to
remove such institutions. Let them cut out
their own cancer, which is the foulest blot that
could ever come upon civilization, and if the
feeders and supporters of these wicked and low
lived establishments were treated as they
should be they would not be permitted to vote
or to hold office. These criminal practices were
never countenanced in any "Mormon" com-
munity, while they were allowed to hold the
reins of government, but "where the carrion is
there will the eagles be gathered." This nation
is indebted not only to the "Mormon" men, but
to the "Mormon" women, and though we have
been wronged and borne our wrongs in silence
for many years and were willing to forgive and
"bury the hatchet," when we see that the very
worst enemies" to our Constitution are being
allowed to harass and afflict us, whom they
know to be the most peacably inclined, indus-
trious and enterprising class of people, and who
first colonized and made these once dreary and
barren deserts inhabitable, leaving no stone
unturned to rob and strip us again of our rights
as American citizens, and of every lawful
means of defence against the meanness and
violence the most corrupt set of lawless aggress-
ors that ever disgraced a nation, we do not
intend to yield quietly again, to be driven from
our happy homes, which are far dearer to us
now than were those which they robbed us of
in an early day and drove us into the wilder-
ness. We will publish our history and also
theirs, and expose to the world the abominable
schemos and hypocrisy of some of the wise and
learned of their "Scribes and Pharisees;" who are
trying to disfranchise the "Mormon" women
because, forsooth, we vote for the men of our
choice, being to our own interest to do so,
instead of for those who have openly acknowl-
edged themselves to be our most bitter enemies.
What an enormous "sacrifice" for the few Gen-
tile ladies who vote in Utah to deny themselves
this privilege long enough "to destroy the power
of the Mormon Church," expecting, as many do,
to share in the booty.
	We would call their attention to a time when
some were so very humaue as to propose the
right of suffrage to be conferred upon the "Mor-
mon" women that they might free themselves
from the awful bondage of the "Mormon"
Priesthood or hierarchy. This right having been
given us by the "Mormon" Legislature, over
twelve years ago, with the full consent of the
United States. We were not slow to avail
ourselves of it, and if we were in bondage and
felt our chains to be very galling we could most
assuredly have refused to live in, or sustain
plural marriage, and we are at perfect liberty, at
all times, to free ourselves if so disposed, and if
there was the least danger or necessity we
could call on this army—stationed here upon
the bench to protect us. This fact was proven
in an early day, when a few weak minded sisters
sought protection under the wing of Colonel
Steptoe's army, but which, I think, proved a
warning to others to beware of "wolves in
sheep's clothing." "Mormon" women are not
so ignorant as might be supposed, of the power
that they hold to declare polygamy illegal.

[Column 2]
But before Senator Edmunds and others allow
themselves to be convicted by overstepping their
authority in taking away our rights without
due process of law, they would do well to come
and visit some of "these poor females," for
whom they profess so much sympathy and'
wish to emancipate "from the slavery of voting.
(I wonder these words did not choke him)
Instead of trusting to the tales of those who are
using them as "cat's paws," and we warrant that
they "could get at the real opinion of the
"Mormon women," who are at liberty to speak
for themselves and are more than willing to
"give them the free exercise of those opinions,"
and they might possibly blush at their own
ignorance and bigotry, and return wi er if not
better judges of the human heart, and of the
superior blessings and privileges enjoyed by the
"Mormon" wives who have honored this Celes-
tial order. I believe that they would find more
advocates and those who would sustain this
order among the women than among the men.
Women are generally inspired by the strongest
religious motives; and they have the greatest
faith and courage to endure pain and the various
crosses in life. This has been demonstrated in
all ages. There are of course, exceptions to
every rule, but in the life of Christ, see the devo-
tion that was shown by woman. She was last
to linger at the Cross, and was first at the Sep-
ulchre. We read "when it was yet dark" and
"when the disciples went away again to their
own home" Mary still "stood without the Sep-
ulchre weeping," and she was the first to hear
the Savior's voice, who gave to her the message
to take to His brethren.
	I know of no plural wife who has felt fright-
ened enough to propose being divorced from her
husband, but I did hear of one man who, at the
first sound of the news that these commisioners
were to be sent here, gave to his second wife a
bill of divorcement. And I remember (not
many years ago) of a few dissatisfied men who
murmured against the authority of the Priest-
hood and when in darkness they sought revela-
tion from peepers and other spurious sources
and withdrew from the Church—and to gain
favor and popularity in the world they had to
put away their plural wives, though some had
become mothers and were devotedly attached to
their husbands and the parting between some
of them was enough to melt the hardest heart.
They had never given it a thought, when leav-
ing this Church that they would ever repudiate
any of their wives. But some of the first wives
gave their lords to understand that they had
no right to live with a plurality of wives out-
side of the Church, which they had withdrawn
from. So the virtuous portion sorrowfully sub-
mited to their fate with martyr-like fortitude
and the majority, losing what little light they
had fell into the worst darkness, spiritualism
and infidelity, and how much higher are they
now in the estimation of the world than are the
ones who held on to "Mormonism" and stood
out in defence of this Celestial principle of mar-
riage? I fail to see that they have gained any
thing; but they have lost every thing that was
essential to true happiness in this world and
all hope in the world to come. And as for the
demon jealousy, it flourishes more outside of
plural marriage than it does among the ones who
practice it whatever may be said to the contrary.
We all know and expect that our husbands will
acknowledge their wives before the world, there-
fore there is no deception nor necessity for prac-
ticing the system of hypocrisy and the awful,
vices of intrigue and lying which our most
zealous and over righteous accusers are addicted
to—encouraging secret transgressions; they
steal away from the only woman, whom they
honor with the name of wife, and indulge pro-

[Column 3]
miscuously without any regard to honor or mo-
rality, and many of them without the least
restraint, indulge in open profligacy, and then
adding insult to injury such characters set them-
selves up to judge us—denouncing the men and
the women who are "in the marriage relation"
as criminals, doomed to suffer any penalty which
they see fit to place upon us. But "people living
in glass houses should not throw stones." If it
is crime they wish to repress, let them begin at
home and first honor "that most sacred tie
which lies at the foundation of the home and
of society," (which we not only preach, but
paactice,) instead of saying, "I am more holy
than thou," because a new faith happens to
come in contact with the old rules and prejudices
and long established views of society, and their
opinions of "social order." "He that is without
sin among you let him cast a stone at us." But
we truly rejoice that we stand in our own posi-
tion to-day instead of theirs; for "he that is of
God heareth God's words," and the Latter-day
Saints have long been prepared for the scourges
and judgments which were to "commence at
the house of the Lord," and we see the calami-
ties already coming which were pronounced,
(through the Prophet Joseph Smith) upon
this favored nation, if they would not repent
and cease their persecuting God's people. The
things once viewed in the far future are now at
our doors, and do we rejoice at the sufferings of
these our fellow beings—even though some may
have sanctioned and had a hand in making these
wicked and inhuman laws to destroy and break
us up—rending assunder the dearest ties of con-
sanguinity, to rob and drive us again? No! there
is not one in possession of a Christian spirit, but
would be glad to save them if it were possible,
from the suffering which their own deeds are
bringing upon them. But when they not only
refuse to accept salvation when it is offered
them by God's chosen servants, and scorn the
warning voice, but continue to persecute them
to the death; they cannot eacape, for He has
long since decreed these things. We read in our
papers from day to day of the awful suffering
from disasters causing untold misery and an-
guish, such as no language has power to de-
cribe—sweeping thousands of God's creatures in-
to eternity without the least preparation or
warning, and to think that we have no power
to prevent it, and the only thing we can do is
to plead with that God of mercy, whose hand
has ever been over us for good, that he will show
mercy unto those who are innocent. But the
wicked of this nation have got a heavy debt to
pay. There are a few of God's predictions
which we see are being fulfilled. "Behold,
saith the Lord, I will bring plagues upon the
world, the sword, famine, death and destruction
I will hold my tongue no more. The innocent
and righteous blood crieth unto me and the
souls of the just complain continually. There-
fore, saith the Lord, I will surely avenge them,
and receive unto me all the innocent blood from
among them. Behold my people are led as a
flock to the slaughter. I will bring them with
a mighty hand and a stretched out arm.
	Woe to them that sin and keep not my com-
mandments! saith the Lord: Behold clouds from
the east and from the north unto the south,
and they are very horrible to look upon, full of
wrath and storm, and there shall be great fear-
fulness and trembling upon earth: and they
that see the wrath shall be afraid and trembling
shall come upon them. Fire and hail, and
many waters, that all fields may be full, and all
rivers, with the abundance of great waters, and
they shall break down the cities and walls—and
grass of the meadows, and their corn. Like as
thou hast done unto my chosen, saith the Lord,
even so shall God do unto thee, and shall deliver


thee into mischief—Thy children shall die of
hunger, and thou shalt fall through the sword,
thy cities shall be broken down, and all thine
shall perish with the sword in the field.” Our
God seeth all things, and He is surely avenging
the wrongs of an innocent oppressed people,
and our enemies will have no power to escape
the fierce judgments of the Almighty.
						H. M. Whitney.


WOMAN'S EXPONENT. 157


', 'EDITORIAL THOUGHTS.
	For several years past some very philanthropic
ladies of the East have been (one would think),
greatly troubled concerning the condition of the
women of Utah, and they have, in various ways,
sought to obtain money from charitable institu-
tions, and from the public treasury of the country,
ostensibly to assist in the suppression of plural
marriage and all "its terrible conditions," which,
according to their ideas, its practice has wrought
among the people here in these valleys of the
mountains. They forget the dreadful degradation
of women near home, in their own cities and
towns, or are totally blind to the sin and crime
that abounds in the very heart of the nation, or,
scripturally speaking, they fail to remove the beam
from their own eyes, but reach away over the Rocky
Mountains, penetrating into the very fastnesses to
search and discover the mote in the eyes of the 
"Mormons;" and they think, forsooth, that they
have discerned it in the practice of a principle
that the ancient prophets and patriarchs embraced,
and that God himself approved, he acknowledg-
ing those very men as doing His bidding, walking
in His paths, and in various other ways. But 
without entering into any discussion of the sub-
ject itself, or any dissertation upon the merits of
its practice (especially as Congress has decided
that men can no longer marry other wives, or
recognize those whom they had already taken
previous to the passage of laws provided on pur-
pose to punish the "Mormons"), instead of making
any defence even, because it is utterly useless
now, Congress having decided all things for the
people, as though one's conscience was not to be
considered at all, we only want to mention a few
facts in relation to the methods, by means of
which these anti-Mormon women obtain money
"to aid in suppressing polygamy."
	Foremost among the women of the world who 
have joined this campaign, and whose sympathies
have been so deeply aroused as to cause them to
weep freely, and pray long and loudly for "the griev-
ous wrongs in Utah, done under the cloak of relig-
ion," is Mrs. Angie F. Newman. She has continued
her intercessions in public and in private for years
wherever an opportunity offered, or she could
succeed in making an opening, to be heard on this
exciting topic. Her name is thus becoming widely
known, and she has made a national reputation by
crying down the "Mormons." What her lectures
and arguments are, which she uses so freely and
with such eloquence, as to draw money from the
public treasury, it is easy to see from the reports
to the leading papers of New York and Washing-
ton, and, strange to state, from the Congressional
Record also. It is well that her reports and testi-
monials are given to the public press, for through
these popular sources, "he who runs may read,"
[column 2]
and truth and error may meet in open conflict,
for these untruths cannot pass without contradic-
tion, and every honest-hearted person who ex-
amines without prejudice will discover the truth,
and "Mormon" women are not afraid of the result.
For instance, Mrs. Newman pictures plural wives
as wretched, ignorant, down-trodden, and totally
incapable of maintaining themselves temporally,
or protecting themselves morally; their situation
is deplorable by reason of their helplessness and
illiteracy, having never been taught independence
or self reliance, therefore, "Congress must pro-
vide for these incompetent persons."
	Mormon women themselves claim that they
know their own history and condition better than
any one else possibly can, and that they have no
need or occasion for the money obtained, or
methods introduced by these officious sympathizers
under false pretenses, to better their circum-
stances and lift them to the plane of modern
civilization, such as the Gentile world claim as the
EXCELSIOR.
	Recently in Mrs. Newman's lectures she told a
sensational story, one or more, to the W. C. T. U.
in New York, that perfectly horrified them, and
would have terrified the "Mormon" women still
more, had the stories been true; but when contra-
dicted by reputable non-Mormons, as well as real,
live Mormons, what will Mrs. Newman do for ma-
terial to draw upon in the future? Mrs. Newman
has a great deal of polish, and knows what will
take with the religious element outside, and withal
carries an influence that many stronger women of
positive character are not able to maintain. What
a pity her talents are not made use of in the right
direction—in the lifting up and reforming and
bringing back to lives of usefulness those who
have strayed from the paths of virtue, or fallen
victims to the seducer or betrayer of innocence.
If to such charities she would devote her energies
and efforts, she could find abundant opportunity,
and for such a class of women Congress would do
well to provide "Industrial" Homes.
	Here in Utah there are more domestic and other
industries already, than there are women to supply
places and the need for work. Every woman and
child is or may be provided for, because of the
perfect organizations that exist throughout all the
Stakes of Zion, that cover the land from east to 
west, from north to south, wherever the Latter-day
Saints are located. It is one of the principles of
the religion of this peculiar people that they pro-
vide for the poor; it is an injunction of the Savior,
"The poor ye have always with you." The condi-
tion of the Mormon women is only deplorable because
of the interference of Congress with their marital
relations; the breaking up of happy homes, and
the separation of those bound together by the
most solemn ties and covenants, and the love that
is stronger than death itself. These things have
come about through the misrepresentations and
falsehoods circulated by unprincipled people for
their own selfish purposes, and not for the love of
virtue and righteousness, by any means.
	The thousands of tourists that have visited Utah
during the last few years, some of whom have
taken pains to investigate a little into the condi-
tions of the people, know that no such state of
society exists here as is represented by these men
and women who, for the sake of gain and notoriety,
join the popular outcry against the "Mormons."
Ladies of culture and refinement who have visited
here and spent some time among the people,
meeting them in their homes and socially, have
informed us that on returning home and telling the
simple truth about the "Mormons," they have been
berated as though it was a crime to tell the truth
of a people so despised; and they were either
accused of having been "converted to polygamy"
or bought with "Mormon" money, or that they
had only seen what is termed by the outside world
"gilt-edged Mormonism."
[column 3]
	Recently, while traveling on a railway train, four
gentlemen in conversation struck upon "Mormon
ism," in our hearing. Evidently they knew very
little of its principles, but they had been in Salt
Lake, and each one seemed disposed to "tell it
all." One man said he had been in Salt Lake
City frequently, and that once while traveling
from Chicago to this city he met a charming young
woman, with whom he conversed considerably
about the "Mormons;" he denounced Brigham
Young as an impostor, and so on, and expressed
himself very emphatically against the practice of
polygamy, etc. She apparently acquiesced in all
that he said. After his arrival in Salt Lake, he
chanced to meet her on the street, and recognized
her; a gentleman in whose company he was at
the time, rather astonished at his knowing a
"Mormon lady, said: "Do you know her?" "Well,
yes," he replied, "we traveled together a part of
the way from Chicago." The friend then in-
formed him that she was the granddaughter of
Brigham Young, and that her husband was under-
ground, and had been for two or three years, and
that she was a firm believer in the doctrine of
polygamy, etc. Now, to one acquainted with the
family of Brigham Young this was very funny, to
say the least, but it shows how far off people get,
as Brigham Young has no granddaughter married
to any man who is either in exile, or under-ground.
This subject of "under-ground" was immediately 
taken up by the party conversing, and inquiries
elicited the following explanation from the man
who knew it all. He said the "Mormons" had a
system of under-ground tunnels, and that the
deputies and detectives, vigilant as they were,
and the best the government could employ, had
never been able to discover how it was managed,
or where ingress, or egress were obtained; that
the man that had given them the most troupble of
any, and the one they wanted to get the worst was
"Cannon," who was now the President of the
Church, and had come out and given himself up
since "Cleveland" had appointed a Democratic
Judge. He went on to say a great deal more
about "this" man "Cannon," and the "Mormons"
all being democrats and solid for Cleveland, etc.
One of the men who was older and evidently
wiser, and who had traveled in China and other
foreign countries, began to relate stories of the
condition of women and female children in  those
lands, and the conversation drifted off into the
greater absurdities he could tell, to which "Mor-
mon" stories sank into insignificance, and they
finally dropped the "Mormon" topic.
	To one so well acquainted as the writer with
"Mormonism" and the people, it was more than
absurd to sit silently and hear these ridiculous
tales about under-ground tunnels, "Mormon"
treasures, their politics, etc., and who presided
over the Church, and his unbounded influence
and a great deal more of the same kind of thing. 
No doubt the same stories are told over and over,
and each time exaggerated and elaborated, and
the people swallow these fabrications as facts, and
circulate them far and wide. Sensational stories
about "Mormons" are about like "Baron Mun-
chausen" and "Sinbad, the Sailor," tales, and will
by and by be looked upon by those who have read
and heard them as just as ridiculous. Mrs. New-
man and the Industrial Home business may
prosper for a time, but Congress will learn at last
that "Mormon" women will not accept help in
any such way, and that their belief in their re-
ligion is "without money and without price. They
are not to be bought in any such way, and how-
ever ignorant and uncultivated the world may
think them, they scorn and despise such methods
as these professedly "Christian" women resort to
to obtain money and favor and notoriety. They
can look with pity upon those afar off, who are
deceived in regard to the conditions here, but for
those who come among them and partake of their
hospitality and know the truth, and then go away


and boldly and openly declare falsely concerning
the situation, they can only have the most severe
contempt. But they are in the hands of One who
knows the secrets of all hearts, and who will
eventually “come out of His hiding place,” and
until that time wickedness will prevail, and those
who wish to keep the laws of God will be persecu-
ted; but for those who walk in the light of the
Gospel, and are steadfast, clinging to “the iron
rod,” there is no need of fear.
	Therefore, the women of the Latter-day Saints
can pursue the even tenor of their way, and per-
form the duties, of every-day life, laying a sure
foundation in the hearts and minds of their child-
ren, that they may grow up in the knowledge of
God and his ways, walking in His precepts and
obeying His commandments, and while they do
these things, and all others required, the Lord
will fight their battles and provide for their de-
liverance from the bondage of their enemies in
His own way, to the astonishment of those who
are waiting and watching for their downfall and
destruction.


WOMEN’S EXPONENT.


', 'WHAT THEY SAY.

	We publish in this issue of our paper some
extracts from the Cincinnati Enquirer" about
"Mormon women," as given in a lecture by
Mrs. Angie F. Newman, also a few items from
the lecture by another Gentile woman who was
in the audience, and who comments on the sub-
ject as well.
	The papers of Cincinnati announced that
Mrs. Newman, of Nebraska, was in the city,
and had spent much time in Utah and closely
studied the problem of Mormonism as it relates
to women, and so forth, and that it had been
proposed to call a meeting of the ladies of all
denominations to hear her speak. Mormon
women are fast becoming a popular subject for
lectures, and by such means the Gospel is being
proclaimed because it makes honest people anx-
ious to know "whether these things be so." It
is very annoying to be thus cruelly misrepre-
sented, but the women of this Church know
that the words of the Savior are true: "He that
would be godly in Christ Jesus must suffer per-
secution." The "Enquirer" said Mrs. Newman
was prepared to give much intelligent and re-
liable information on the subject of Mormon
women. Let us see. She tells her audience
she has held argumentative conversations with
Eliza R. Snow and other ladies. We would
be glad to know who they are, for Sister Eliza
R. Snow had never heard of her, nor any other
Mormon woman so far as we can learn. How-
ever, as Mrs. Newman has told the ladies of
Cincinnati so much about Utah, we feel called
upon to speak in defense of these women she
condemns, and the homes she represents as so
degraded.
	Milton said: "So Truth be in the field, let her
and Falsehood grapple," but as regards the
women of this Church, Truth is scarcely in the
field, considering how Falsehood has been
flaunting her banner in the wind of public opin-
ion. It is time Truth came forward to the res-
cue and threw down the gauntlet in defense of
her own honor. It has been said that there is
a time when forbearance ceases to be a virtue,
it seems to us there is now a time when silence
on the part of the women of Utah would be an
ignominious reproach.
	According to Mrs. Newman the women here
are not ALL fools, and it is highly proper for
them to express themselves on what is most em-
phatically their own question, and as the race
is not to the swift nor the battle to the strong,
it may yet follow that Mormon women can
maintain their own ground and vanquish the
enemy. For Truth will eventually prevail.
Quoting the speaker's words, "Every house is
a house of prostitution!" How any sane woman
dare make such an assertion seems incredible!

[Column 2]
This is too much! Mrs. Newman from her
own statement lived here for some time in this
hot bed of iniquity, and a great many other
Gentiles manage to exist among the "Mor-
mons," and to thrive and grow rich and exceed-
ingly prosperous. They visit in "Mormon"
houses, but they probably don't know that they
are such dens of infamy. Now that Mrs. New-
man has given such "reliable information" on
the subject all the Gentiles will run away im-
mediately, no doubt, for fear of being contami-
nated.
	Mrs. Newman's assertion that "a man may
take for his wives his mother, grand-mother, or
sister" is as false as it is cruel. To speak so
positive one should have the statement well au-
thenticated, but she heard it possibly from some
wicked, designing, malicious person, someone pre-
pared to say anything false to injure the Mor-
mons. Our Savior said: "Blessed are ye when
men shall revile you and persecute you, and say
all manner of evil against you, falsely, for my
name's sake, for great is your reward in heaven,
for so persecuted they the saints that were be-
fore you."
	Mormon women are not only virtuous, but
chaste. The principle of plural marriage
itself tends to the strictest chastity, and child-
ren born in this order of marriage will, from
ante-natal influences, be purer in character. To
enter upon an explanation of this principle and
its consequent results was not our object and
would make this article too lengthy, however
briefly dealt with, but suffice it to say, nowhere
on the earth exist purer women than right here
in Utah, those who have embraced this sacred
order of marriage the world is so ready to con-
demn. Mrs. Newman with all her pretensions
evidently knows nothing of Mormon women,
their homes or associations. She says "there is
no love, care or tenderness," nothing could be
more untrue. There are no stronger ties of af-
fection existing upon the earth than those
which bind together the families who live in
the order of celestial marriage. This intensity
of affection helps them to bear the scoffs and
reproaches heaped upon them by those who can-
not understand the position they occupy, nor
the motives which influence them, and they
find comfort and solace in the society of each
other which unites them in the strongest bonds
of human sympathy, and strengthens the so-
cial strata that the Gentiles such as Mrs. New-
man and others of her class are trying to break
down and destroy. "Money," she says, "is
what is needed, and needed at once." For what,
pray? Not for the "Mormons," they are abund-
antly able to feed, clothe and educate their own
children, and are in no need whatever of pecu-
niary help. When this people were driven from
their homes, destitute pilgrims, without shelter,
by mobs who threatened the lives of even aged
men and defenseless women and children, no
one offered them aid or protection, though many
hundreds perished in consequence of the priva-
tion and suffering they were compelled to en-
dure from the persecutions of their fellow citi-
zens in this boasted land of liberty. Now the
"Mormons" are prosperous, and they would
scorn the idea of receiving money gathered
from the people of the United States to educate
their children. What about the masses of un-
educated children growing up in the eastern
citses and towns without homes, without proper
food? We read in the Boston "Woman's Jour-
nal" this evening, that Chicago has 30,000 pau-
per, Cincinnati has no doubt a moderate pro-
portion, and Salt Lake City has none; the la-
dies who contributed so liberally at the close of
Mrs. Newman's lecture to the fund to educate
the children of the Mormons, who are already
well fed and housed, as well as taught, would do

[Column 3]
well to use the means to provide for the desti-
tute and homeless in their own proud city, or if
Foreign Missions are their grand ambition there
are plenty of barefooted, half-starved children
in Ireland, and in other lands across the sea,
who need their benevolence; as for the Latter-
day Saints they spurn such offerings. The treas-
ures of the earth and the riches of heaven are
for the Saints in the due time of the Lord, and
they have no fear. There is so much opposition
just now to the Latter-day work that one can
scarcely take up a magazine or newspaper with-
out seeing some extravagent article on the Mor-
mon problem, as it is very generally termed.
	Dio Lewis' Monthly for November contains
one of the most ridiculous we have seen, writ-
ten by Dora Darmore, (Mrs. A. J. Boyer) former-
ly editor of the Golden Dawn, in San Francisco.
The lady passed trhough here in the summer of
1881. We have seen one of the articles published
in a New York paper. She wants "an army of
moral, industrious, intelligent young men" sent
here to marry the bright Mormon girls. Now, as
there are many hundreds of bright Mormonboys
expecting to marry these Mormon girls what
will Dora Darmore do with them? They will
certainly be in the way of her pet scheme be-
ing carried out, for Mormon boys are not only
very bright, but physically strong and steadfast
in purpose, and they assuredly will not be inter-
fered with in this way, so her solution of the
problem will fail. The lady has credited the
writer with some things she did not say, but on
the whole it was so long ago she has probably
forgotten what sort of language was used on that
occasion. She alludes to the care-worn counte-
nances of the faces of women in the Tabernacle,
but forgets to tell us there are any unhappy
women in the world outside of Utah, but as
most Mormon women know plenty of unhappy
marraiges outside the pale of the Church they
know how much her words are worth. Mrs.
Boyer stayed a few days in Utah, and she had
very little opportunity of seeing or judging the
character or motives of Mormon men and women.
She says, "God help and pity the unhappy
women of Utah, for the men who own them,
body, and soul, have no pity." Now, if there
are anywhere women more happy in their home
with their domestic relations, more satisfied
withtheir surroundings than these women she
pities so, where are they? As for the help of
God, the Latter-day Saints have it, and expect
to while they are faithful in keeping His com-
mandments. As for men owning women, if
women are not free and independent here,
they are not anywhere, for there is more freedom
for women in the Gospel than in the laws of
men. Mormon women are not asking for pity
form any source, they simply want justice, no-
thing more. We do not expect the world to be-
lieve in celestial marriage, but to respect our
religious belief and accord us the right the Con-
stitution of the United States guarantees to all
its citizens, the freedom of religious liberty.

["﻿R S., Y. L. M. I. A. AND PRIMARY
			REPORTS.
			    _____
		MESA CITY, ARIZONA.
	Some time since initiatory steps were taken
to organize a Y. L. M. I. A., and on January
12th, 1883, the organization was effected, with
the following officers, who were set apart by
President A. F. Macdonald, Bros. Richens,
Stewart and Carter, viz., President, Mrs., Zulie
Kimball; Counselors, Miss Etta Pomeroy and
Miss Beckie Richens; Secretary, Miss Gertrude
Pomeroy; Corresponding Secretary, Mrs. Jennie
Bush; Treasurer, Phoebe Shepard.
	Our society meets weekly, and from the
energetic zeal manifested we hope to attain a
higher degree of excellence in the scale of
intellect.
				JENNIE BUSH,
		Corresponding Secretary for Y. L. A.
				_____
	   WEST BOUNTIFUL PRIMARY.
	Our Association is not a large one in point
of numbers, but the little folks are striving to
improve themselves, and most of them are
much interested. We have had two Primary
concerts here for the benefit of the Association,
which were well attended. They were a great
encouragement to the children, and reflected
credit upon their endeavors. Praying that
the Lord will bless all the Primary associations
and the efforts of those who are laboring in the
good cause.
		MISS JULIA FACKRELL,
							Secretary.
				_____
		AMITY, ARIZONA.
	On November 2nd, 1882, a special meeting
was called in Amity, Arizona, and Bishop
Albinon M. Holden organized a Relief Society
with the following officers, who were unani-
mously sustained by the vote of the people:
Mrs. Martha Bigelow, President; Susan John-
son and Lucinda Holden, Counselors; Mary
Emily Marble, Secretary; Celestina Marble,
Treasurer. Nineteen members are enrolled


and four teachers have been appointed to visit
among the sisters. We hold our meetings
twice a month at 2 o'clock p.m.
	A Young Ladies’ Association has also been
organized with Julia A. Johnson, President;
Lucy Norton and Melvina Gunn, Counselors;
Ellen Bigelow, Secretary; L. Jackson, Assis-
tant Secretary; and Maria S. Holden, Treasu-
rer. There are fourteen members enrolled.
	A Primary Association was organized with
Mrs. Polly Ann Greer, President; Priscilla
Hamblin and Lora A. Brown, Counselors;
Susan Greer, Secretary; and Ella Hamblin,
Treasurer. Thirty-four members enrolled.
The Association hold their meetings every
Saturday at 2 o’clock and they are generally
well attended.
	The sisters are diligent and faithful in the
performance of their duties, the Bishop and a
few of the other brethren occasionly visit our
meetings and counsel and encourage us. Al-
ways praying for the welfare of Zion and the
advancement of mankind, including the eleva-
tion of woman, and also for the success of the
EXPONENT as the advocate of woman’s cause,
I have the pleasure to subscribe myself,
			Yours in the Gospel,
				MARY E. MARBLE,
						Secretary.

﻿PRINCESS MARY, DAUGHTER OF
              CHARLES I.
                      ______
          BY HANNAH T. KING.
                      ______
The royal family of Stewart were remarka-
ble for their beauty, for many fine qualities,
but most of all for their misfortunes! The
Princess Mary, daughter of Charles I, was
an exception in the catalogue, and yet little
has been historically written of her; while
other princesses’ lives have been traced on
the page of history again, and again; she had
a little sister Elizabeth, who died at Carris-
brook Castle while a child, and Queen
Victoria has had a lovely monument erected
to her memory in St. Thomas’s Church, Isle
of Wight. It will be remembered that
Charles I. married a beautiful French Prin-
cess, named Henrietta Maria, the youngest
daughter of Henry IV of France, and his
Queen Mariede Medices; she was not consider-
ed a very wise or suitable wife for an English
king, partly on account of her being a zealous
Roman Catholic, and that her training and
education unfitted her for so important a
position; but she was beautiful, and the king
loved her, and she returned his love. Her
eldest son, afterwards Charles II of England,
was born at Greenwich; his mother declared
him to be an ugly, though a very fine child,
and so solemn in his infant ways, that she felt
he was far wiser than herself. The next ar-
rival was a little daughter, born at Whitehall,
London, on the 4th of Nov., 1631, and was
warmly welcomed by both parents, but she
was a very delicate child, and so caused them
much anxiety; on this account they had her
baptized when only a few days old, by the
name of Maria, after her grandmother; they
were very poor at this time, and it suited their
circumstances to have a private christening, as
the Parliament would not vote more supplies
for them. The child was provided with a
nurse, and the Countess of Roxburgh was her
governess from the first day of her life! When
two years old, an establishment was arranged
for her, which certainly did not indicate pov-
erty; besides a nurse and three watchers, a
lady of the bedchamber, a seamstress, a tailor
and shoemaker, a gentleman usher, two pages,
a laundress, three grooms, four footmen, five
kitchen servants, a coachman, and more im-
portant than all, a good physician, Dr. Mayer,
whose skill and care were supposed to have
saved her life when quite an infant, and con-
sequently was much esteemed by the king,
her father, who was obliged to go to Scotland
soon after Mary’s birth, and so was particular-
ly anxious that her health should be strictly
attended to in his absence; the diet provided
for the little lady’s nursery was more substan-
tial than is procurable for children in this age,
being “manchet and fine bread, mutton or
chicken with all,” and the breakfast must have
been early, as it is reported the children dined
between ten and twelve, their dinner was game
and meat of various kinds, custards and sweet 
tarts, and Gascony wine; on fast days fish
instead of meat, supper the same, but not quite
so heavy. They lived at St. James’s Palace,
London, the greater part of the year, which
was a favorite place with the queen, their
mother, and she loved to walk in the beautiful
garden with the children, carrying the baby
in her arms, singing to it, and talking and
laughing with them.
  Charles I. was anxious that his children
should all speak English as their mother-
tongue, though his wife gave him some trouble
by her preference for her native  language, but
as her sons grew up and visited the French
court, they were unable to converse except in
English! The queen was, of course, a Roman
Catholic, and used to give  Mary rosaries and
crosses, and to take her into the chapel for
mass; when Mary was eight years old the king
found out what was going on, and positively
forbade her being taken there again, and in-
sisted that nothing should ever be said to her
against the protestant faith. She grew to be
a pretty child, and a devoted daughter to her
kind and good father her brother Charles
was her favorite companion.
  A very important event in Mary’s life was
brought about in consequence of her grand-
mother, Marie de Medicie, visiting England;
she came without being invited because it
happened to be convenient to her; this visit
was not an agreeable one, though the queen
was pleased to present her children to her
mother: and she told her “the king had made
her a happy wife, happy mother and a happy
queen!” But even at this time trouble was
gathering around the king, who, while this
happy reunion was taking place, was marching
at the head of his army against his Scotch
subjects, causing his family much sorrow and
anxiety; little Mary began to notice day after
day the grief on her mother's face and the
tears shed by her governess, the countess of
Roxburgh, who had been a second mother to
her, and whom she loved very much, but
whose Husband and son were fighting against
the king; and they missed their pleasant
walks at Richmond or Hampton Court, and
thought it very strange and disagreeable never
to be allowed to go out of doors without a guard
of armed soldiers; even at night her sleep was
disturbed by the tramp of the sentries at the
gates, and the poor child began to look care-
worn like the queen, her mother; at this time,
though so young, a way was already being
made for her to escape from the evils that were
coming on her father’s house; for Marie de
Medicie was the bearer of a message from the
Prince of Orange, asking her in marriage for
his son. Charles I. was very willing to listen
to such proposals for his younger daughters,
but he had more ambitious views for Mary,
besides which he loved her so much, that
he shrank from parting with her.	But
he found that large offers of wealth
were made for the bride, and that the Prince
of Orange would undertake to assist him in
his struggle with his rebellious subjects, and,
moreover, the little girl, who perhaps under-
stood what she had seen with her parents,
that difference in religion caused much misery
between husband and wife, told her father
that she should prefer being married to a


[Column 1]

﻿protestant partner. This decided the matter,
and in April, 1641, the young prince came
over, escorted by twenty vessels, and landed
at Gravesend, where he was met by a royal
equipage, and driven through London to
Whitehall where Charles and Henrietta re-
ceived him most affectionately; Mary was not
there, for she was in bed, poor child, at Somer-
set House with a feverish cold and swollen
face, but her bridegroom of sixteen begged so
hard to be permitted to see her, that the king
and queen took him thither, and the prince
was introduced to her by them, and he kissed
the little girl in spite of her swollen face, and
told his father in his first letter that she was
sweet and lovely; although young, he was very
manly in his manners and was highly accom-
plished, spoke four European languages, a good
mathematician, well acquainted with history,
both of his own country and that of others,
and had long been admitted to sit in the coun-
cils of the state, and Mary, from sympathy
with the trials of those she loved, was older
than her years. Arundel House in the Strand
had been fitted up for his reception, and he
had a private key to the garden of Somerset
House. A frequent interchange of visits took
place, and marriage was proposed and earnest-
ly insisted upon; the king objected in consid-
eration of the youth of his daughter: but, alas!
heavy clouds had long been gathering around
him and his throne, and he had much desired
to find a safe asylum for his beloved family,
for Charles was a devoted husband and father;
it was now proposed by the Prince of Orange,
the prince's father, that the marriage should
be solemnized, that the young princess should
still remain under the care and jurisdiction of
her mother for two years, that the queen
should accompany the young bride to Hol-
land, and at the expiration of the two years,
she should pass to the house of her husband.
Charles at last bowed to a dire necessity that
surrounded him and his family; the marriage
took place with the usual court ceremonies, as
soon as preparations could be made ready.
A royal vessel was duly fitted out, and then
came the cruel separation, the king was per-
fectly unmanned at parting with his almost
worshiped child, for having a mind beyond
her years she had been the solace and comfort
of his troubled life; he repeatedly clasped her
in his arms, blest her and wept bitter tears
over her; probably he had a presentiment that
their separation was final. As long as the
vessel was in sight he stood on the shore mak-
ing fond signals of adieus, he then called for
a swift horse that he might rapidly ride to a
high eminence that still commanded a view of
the ship that bore away his heart's treasures!
Never to meet again on earth! Time passed—
the final tragedy of regicide is history. Mary
mourned the sad, sad finale of her beloved
father, but her mother and her husband blest
and consoled her; the two years with her
mother was religiously kept sacred and then
she was placed in the beautiful home that had
been fondly prepared for her by the hand of
love. Alas, a very few years elapsed before
this lovely and chaste union was dispelled by
the cruel hand of death, the young prince
was seized with malignant smallpox, and left
Mary a widow at nineteen.
	At the restoration of Charles the Second,
he invited his widowed neice and her boy to
come and make her home in England, she
accepted the kindly invitation, and Whitehall,
the home of her childhood, was appointed for
her residence. (A window is still shown in
that fine building from which Charles I.
emerged to walk to the fatal block, from
whence he was ushered into eternity.)
	Mary had not resided long in the beautiful
home of her childhood, when she was attacked
by smallpox, which in a short time terminated
her life in the very bloom of womanhood,

[Column 2]

leaving her son, who in after years ascended
the throne of England by the title of William
III, having married Mary, the eldest daugh-
ter of the deposed King, James II. Parlia-
ment desired that her name should be conjoint-
ly blended with his in the government of the
kingdom, and hence they ascended the throne
unitedly as William and Mary.


WOMAN’S EXPONENT.


", 'QUEEN VICTORIA'S GIRLHOOD.

	It is seldom the privilege of a biographer
 to have so interesting and so good a subject
as her R. H. Queen Victoria. By the way,
it seems odd to speak of the queen as a sub-

[Column 2]
ject, At the time of her birth it was well
known that she was a royal little personage,
being a direct descendant of Alfred the
Great, renowned by thirty-five generations,
and thirty-seventh from the first to be de-
clared and acknowledged King of all Eng-
land. Though being of such noble birth
there were also others, and it was by no
means certain that she would occupy the
throne. But it does appear, though she
herself did not declare it as one other did,
that she was a child of destiny. The nu-
merous escapes when her life was in jeopar-
dy, the gradual and silent slipping away of
those between her and the throne, her long
and glorious reign all would faypr this
theory. She is a grand-daughter of George
III., King of England at the time of the
American Revolution. Her father, who
was Edward Duke of Kent, was the fourth
son of the king, and only differed from his
family in being better than the others.
	He married a German princess, Victoria
of Leiningen. She was a widow and the
mother of two children. The Duke, who
was very philanthropic and noble in dis-
position, and on that account most
popular with his countrymen though only
a younger son, was also poor. His allow-
ance was miserably small compared with his
expenditures, and these were not made in
fashionable excesses. Upon his marriage
in 1818 he went to live in Germany with
his bride. The mother of Victoria was a
woman of superior wisdom, with much
common sense and determination. And
one knows not how much of the happiness
of this world today depends upon the wis-
dom, strength of character and goodness of
that one little woman as manifested in the
training of her daughter.
	Shortly before the birth of Victoria the
Duke and Duchess of Kent removed to
England, with a feeling of commendable
patriotism, that their child might be born
on English soil, or as her mother expressed
it ‘‘that she might be born and bred a
Briton."  And a bonny, winsome child it
was that came to bless their home. She
came on May 24, 1819, at Kensington
Palace, and was known to all England as
“the little Mayflower.’’ Her father named
her Alexandrina Victoria after the Czar of
Russia and her own beloved mother. She,
however, after arriving at woman’s estate,
though still young, perhaps vaguely realiz-
ed something of what her mother had done
for her. However, that may be, when she
came to affix her signature the first time as
monarch of the kingdom she determined
that no name should precede or supercede
that of her beloved mother and signed sim-
ply “Victoria.’’ She was but a few
months old when her father, who had un-
wisely remained out in the grounds until
he became damp and cold, upon coming in
could not restrain his impatience to see his
darling child but went straight to her nurs-
ery before putting on dry shoes. The re-
sult was a chill, pneumonia and death. It
is reputed that on one occasion when he
put his child in his attendants arms he said,
“ Take care of her, for she will be queen of
England.’’ That must surely have been
what in women would be called intuition.
	The widowed mother now had naught to
live for but her child. She was not greatly
beloved by her husband’s relatives, most
probably because she was not understood.
But a more devoted mother could not be.
She and her child were seldom parted even
for a short time. The little princess al-

[Column 3]
ways had her little bed beside her mamma,
at meals her little table was also beside her
mamma, and there she ate her bread and
milk and fruit. Her health was tenderly
cared for. As a very young child she was
not robust, but health and strength were
fostered by the discipline and daily regime
of her young life. As soon as she was old
enough she had her daily course of lessons,
followed by riding walking, and simple
recreations. Her half sister, Princess
Fedora, was her companion. She was
very fond of her little garden under the
palace windows, and has often been observ-
ed amusing herself there as other children
do, sometimes sprinkling her flowers and
herself simultaneously.
	Her education was conducted under the
watchful and faithful supervision of her
mother, the Baroness Lehzen and Bishop of
______. being her instructors. She be-
came very much attached to the baroness
and made her one of her chief court ladies.
It is said that lack of wealth in the family
was not an unmixed evil. The duchess
was obliged to practice economy. Think
of a duchess being obliged to be economical!
And it most likely laid the foundation for
the thrift and economy which have charac-
terized the reign of Victoria, and heightened
the power and influence of the action.
	Her life as a child moved with the regu-
larity and precision of clock work, and
orderly and regular habits have been ad-
hered to through her life. She studied
modern languages but did not like Latin.
However, it was part of a discipline. She
excelled in music, drawing and painting.
It is said she might have made a name for
herself if life had not been filled by the
duties of a queen. Her voice was clear and
musical, and to this day strangers note her
clear enunciation in speaking and her fine
tones. The education of the princess was
conducted in. such a way that she did not
know until she was twelve or fourteen
years of age, (biographers differ as to the
time) how near to the succession she was.
It was then her governess, Baroness Leh-
zen, thinking it time for her to know, set
before her a genealogical tree to draw out.
	Presently she said, “I can not see who
is to come after Uncle William unless it is
myself.’’ “So it is Madame,” replied the
governess. Then the princess uttered her
thoughts aloud: “Now many a child
would boast, not knowing the difficulty.
There is much splendor, but there is more
responsibility.” After this she put her
little hand into the hand of her teacher and
said, “I will be good. I will be good.”
	It seems to me her future life was fore-
shadowed by that circumstance. Her life
was as simple and quiet as any little country
maiden in many ways, but there was al-
ways the feeling with her trainers that they
were forming the character of a queen.
She saw but little of court life, and during
the time of her uncle, George IV., it was
well she did not. When William III. as
cended the throne, he was bluff and hearty,
for he had been the sailor prince. His
manners were far from ideally royal. One
has a right to expect fair manners and
noble graces and virtues from royalty; but 
such persons are often disappointing.
King William having no heirs and realizing
that Victoria was next in succession, rudely
remarked in the presence of the Duchess of
Kent and before a host of people, that he
hoped he would live to see the princess at-
tain her majority. He wanted no queen


[Column 1]
regent. The duchess turned quite pale at
the affront, but wisely spoke no words and
immediately withdrew from the royal com-
pany. As for the princess her eyes im-
mediately filled with tears at the humilia-
ation of her mother.
	When twelve years of age Victoria at-
tended her first drawing room and as heir
presumptive stood on the left of Queen
Adelaide. She was much admired for her
innocence and the dewy freshness of her
beauty, besides always having fine and en-
gaging manners. On the 24th of May,
1837 the princess attained her majority. It
was a day of great rejoicings. She was
awakened by a serenade, the words of the
song being composed for her. There were
feasts, illuminations and all kinds of holi-
day doings. The king, whose wish was
gratified in seeing her thus come of age,
gave a grand ball, “at which the princess
for the first time took precedence of her
mother” She is said to have blushed
sligh[-]ly at this change of conditions. The
king himself was too ill to attend and the
princess occupied the central chair of state
but the king sent his niece a present of a
piano worth a thousand dollars.
	During the evening she danced with the
Austrian Prince Esterhazy. He is known
to history chiefly for his attire. Grace
Greenwood says “he looked as if he had
been snowed on with pearls and rained on
with diamonds.’’ He really sparkled with
jewels from head to foot, they were even on
the heels of his boots. “It was said that
whenever he wore this splendid costume he
lost numerous gems without either know-
ing or caring.” Of course many congratu-
latory addresses were presented to this
Royal Princess who was now so near the
throne.
	On the morning of June 20th at 10
o’clock, less than a month after his niece
attained her majority, King William died.
As this result was expected a carriage had
been kept in waiting and immediately the
Archbishop of Canterbury of the Lord
Chamberlain drove to Kensington
Palace. Arriving about 4 o’clock they
found some difficulty in gaining admission,
and more in getting audience of H. R. H.
They desired an attendant to say they wish-
ed to see the H. R. H. on business of im-
portance. After some delay the messenger
returned to say the princess was in such a
sweet sleep she could not venture to dis-
turb her. They replied, “We are come on
business of state to the Queen and know
her sleep must give way to that."
	“It did; and to prove that she did not
keep them waiting in a few moments she
came into the room in a loose white night-
gown and shawl her night-cap thrown off
and her hair falling upon her shoulders,
and her feet in slippers, tears in her eyes,
but perfectly collected and dignified.” The
queen's first words, turning to the Primate,
were “I beg your Grace to pray for me,"
which he did. The next thing was to
write to the widowed queen, Adelaide a
sweet letter, in reply to a request that she
might stay at Windsor until after the funer-
al. It was addressed to “Her Majesty,
the Queen." Some one remarked that it
should be addressed to the Queen Dowager.
“I am aware of that.” was the reply, “but
will not be the first to remind her of her
altered position."
	At eleven o'clock of the same day she
entered upon her duties as queen, attend-
ing her first meeting of the Privy Council.

[Column 2]
The first impression she created was very
favorable, the most adverse, if there were
such could not help being struck by her
calm demeanor, and the quiet dignity,
mingled with girlish sweetness and
modesty.
	“The Duke of Wellington told the Clerk
of the Council, that if she had been his own
daughter he could not have desired to see
her perform her part better.’’ And this
was surely great praise from the Iron Duke,
	After the formal proceedings of taking
the oath etc., the Privy Council was sworn;
and she is said to have blushed to the tem-
ples when her elderly uncles knelt to do
her homage. The girlish feeling of family
respect was at that moment uppermost. An
hour after the Privy Council, came that of
the Cabinet Ministers. And the newly
fledged young queen presided over this also
with much ease and dignity, as though she
had always been accustomed to it. Shortly
after her accession the queen went through
the ronnd of balls, receptions, drawing-
rooms, concerts etc., in honor of the event.
“At Her Majesty’s first soiree there were
ten thousand gentlemen present. The
crush was so great that orders and decora-
tions were torn off and diamond buckles
lost from shoes.” Victoria had been queen
a year and a week, and a day when her
coronation took place.
	She ascended the throne at eighteen, was
crowned at nineteen and happily married at
twenty. If that is’nt fine enough for any
fairy story what could be?
	The coronation ceremonies were conduct-
ed with all the pomp and magnificence
that the occasion required, though the
young queen had shown her wisdom and
thrift by omitting certain portions of what
had been heretofore the ceremonial. She
showed her patriotism too, by having her
coronation robes made entirely in her
native land. The Honiton lace alone gave
work to many poor people in a time of
scarcity too. Is it any wonder she has
been beloved by her people from the first
day of her reign? It is said to be a very
imposing spectacle and one truly regal as
this young queen in her virgin freshness
and modesty marched to Westminister
abbey for the coronation. Her coach of
glass and gilding and her eight stately
white horses wiih their fine trappings, and the
noble escort of all grades of nobility formed
a great pageant. And the enthusiastic and
admiring multitude with their cheers, huz-
zas and waving of handkerchiefs, must have
been reassuring to the youthful sovereign.
The climax of spectacular grandeur was
reached when at the services the crown was
placed on the queen's head, and simultan-
eously the peers and peeresses crowned
themselves with their coronets. Some of
the ladies of nobility had remained in West-
minister abbey in their gala attire six or
seven hours or more. It must have taken
several sorts of emotions to sustain them
that long.
E. S. W.


WOMAN’S EXPONENT.


[Column 1]
', 'READ AT THE U. W. P. C., MAY 31, 1898.
	When we catch our first glimpse of the
Mediterranean about 1500 B.C., it is dot
ted with the sails of the Phoenician navi-
gators. They crossed the Mediterranean
in every direction distributing the manu-
factures of Asia.
	The longest voyages that they made were
to procure tin. which was in great demand
for the manufacture of “Bronze.” The near-
est regions where this metal was found was
the Caucasas on the eastern shore of the
Euxine (now called the Black sea.) To-
wards the close of the 11th century B. C.,
the Grecians were jealous and closed the
Ægean sea against these navigators. The
Phoenicians pushed out into the western
Mediterranean and opened the tin mines of
the Herian peninsula (now Spain.) A
hasty sketch is all I can give of the general
features of this period upon which we are-
now entering.
	The Phoenicians established naval sta-
tions and trading posts. Articles of Phoeni-

[Column 2]
cian workmanship are found in the earliest
tombs of the Greeks and Etruscans. The
Phoenician navigators were enterprising.
They built the Hellespont bridge for Xerxes,
they furnished ships for the King of Baby-
lon for naval operations, and they circum-
navigated Africa, an undertaking remark-
able considering the age, 604 B. C., 2’000
years before the Portugese navigator,
Magellan.
	The political strifes between 1500 B.C. to
133 B. C., when Spain became a Roman
province are too numerous to mention.
Nearly eight hundred years elapsed when
Alaric held his sword over Rome and threat-
ened its destruction if his demands were
not granted. Alaric was obliged to force
his way into the city, and Rome paid the
penalty of not yielding to him.
	For several years after the Saracen in-
vasion Spain was broken off into a number
of small but independent states. The king-
dom of Asturia, or Leon, had been growing
in power and in 758 A. D. a second king-
dom was founded in Sobrave. Thirty-six
years afterwards was founded the third
Christian kingdom, that of Navarre, and in
933 another independent monarchy was
formed in Castile and soon became the
most powerful of the Spanish states. The
kingdom of Arragon was the last founded.
	In 1035 A. D. there was a division in
Castile and Arragon, and they were not
united until the marriage of Ferdinand
and Isabella. King John II of Arragon
was greatly pleased at this match; he was
deeply sensible of the importance of consoli-
dating the monarchies of Spain under one
head. The marriage articles were signed
on the 7th of January, 1469, but the cere-
mony was not performed until the 19th of
October, 1469. So great was the poverty
of the parties that it was found necessary to
borrow money to defray the expenses of
the marriage.
	There were five children born to Ferdinand
and Isabella. The eldest daughter, Isa-
bella, was married to Alonzo, the heir to
the crown of Portugal, in 1490. Prince
John, heir to the Spanish crown, married
Margaret, daughter of Emperor Maxmillian.
Johanna married Phillip, son of Maxmillian.
Donna Catalina the youngest daughter,
married Arthur, Prince of Wales, (after-
ward to his brother, Henry VIII.) Prince
John married in April and in October of
the same year was stricken down with
fever and died. The prince dying without
an heir the crown of Castile and Arragon
descended to the son of Isabella of Portugal
who was confirmed by the Cortez of Castile.
Thus the three crowns, Castile, Arragon
and Portugal were suspended over one
head. This young heir died when about
two years old. Isabella of Castile died in
1506, and before she died she exacted a
promise confirmed by an oath from Ferdin-
and (of whom she was always jealous) that
he would not marry again. The death of
Isabella was the signal for disorder which
the genius and authority of Ferdinand were
unable to suppress. While the queen’s re-
mains were scarcely cold Ferdinand took
the usual measures for announcing the suc-
cession and renounced the crown of Castile in
favor of Joanna and Phillip, and to their
eldest son, Charles I. of Spain, on the
death of his mother. The reckless extra-
vagance and licentiousness of Phillip so
troubled the queen that she grew morbid
and finally her malady became incurable.
Phillip long since ceased to treat her with
[-]ffection of even decency, and fain would


[Column 1]
have persuaded the Cortez to authorize her
confinement as a person of disordered intel-
lect. Phillip’s reign was short as he died
on the 25th of September, 1506. Ferdinand
held the reins of government for Joanna;
finally his desire for a son to succeed him
was so great that shortly after Isabella’s
death he married Germaine, daughter of his
sister, (Leanora the queen of Navarre,)
His wish had nearly been realized, Queen
Germaine was delivered of a son in March
1509, but the infant only lived a few hours.
Ferdinand began to be impatient and mor-
bid and his health declined so rapidly that
it was evident that his days were numbered.
He passed the last month of his life at the
country seat of the Duke of Alva, and died
in January, 1516.
	By the death of his grandfather, (Ferdin-
and) Charles I. took possession of the throne
of Spain by the title of Charles V. On the
death of his paternal grandfather (Max-
millian) he was elected Emperor of Ger-
many. Under the reign of Charles V.
the nation continued to advance higher and
higher and he seemed to take everything
before him, until in 1552 he was obliged to
flee before Duke Maurice of Saxony. The
season of the most brilliant results is not
that of the greatest national prosperity, the
boasted reign was dearly purchased by the 
decline of industry and the loss of liberty.
Charles V. celebrated his own obsequies,
his domestics marched in funeral procession
with black tapers, he followed in his
shroud and was laid in his coffin with great
solemnity. The length of the ceremony or
the impression which this left on his mind
so affected him that he resigned his crown
and retired to a monastery and died in
1558.
	Phillip II. was the son of Charles V. and
Isabella of Portugal, he was an accom-
plished linguist and mathematician and a
connoisseur in architecture and fine arts.
In 1554 he married Mary Tudor, Queen of
England, daughter of Henry VIII. and
Catherine of Arragon. After Mary died he
made an attempt to gain the hand of Eliza-
beth but was unsuccessful, and finally mar-
ried Isabella of France. His life  was no-
toriously wicked and licentious, his suffer-
ings were terrible, his body was entirely
covered with ulcers; his death must have
been a great relief to him and all around
him.
	In 1598 on the death of his father Phillip
III. came to the throne, he was married to
Margaret of Austria, his second wife was
Isabella of France. His reign of twenty-
three years was chiefly noted for the cruel
expulsion of the Moors from all parts of
Spain where they had sought refuge during
the last reign. In 1609, 600,000 individuals
were forcibly driven from their homes and
landed on the shores of Africa, there to be
treated even worse by the most cruel and
perfidious of the human family.
	Phillip IV., son of Phillip III. and Isa-
bella of France, began to reign in 1621. In
1615 he married Elizabeth of France, his
second wife was Marie Anne of Austria.
He ascended the throne when but seven-
teen, he was extravagant and dissipated,
his reign was most unfortunate, he was at
war with France, Holland, Sweden and
England. In 1640 Portugal freed herself
from Spanish yoke. Since the days of
Roderick the Goth, a more disastrous reign
than his had not darkened the annals of
Spain.
	Charles II., son of Phillip IV. and Marie

[Column 2]
of Austria was feeble in body and almost an
imbecile in mind. His first wife was Louise,
niece of Louis XIV., and his second wife
was Mary Anne of Newbery. Charles was
the last of the Austrian dynasty. Glorious
as the condition of Spain was under the
earlier sovereigns it was brought now to the
verge of ruin. For nearly three centuries
it had held the foremost rank in Europe as
a military nation; now it began to decline
both in influence and glory.
	Charles II. was childless and bequeathed
his throne to Phillip V., Duke of Anjou.
Phillip V. was the grandson of Charles II.
half-sister. He was the second son of
Louis the Dauphin of France and Mary
Anne of Bavara. He assumed the title of
King of Spain by virtue of the will of
Charles II. His throne was contested by
the House of Austria in favor of Arch Duke
Charles. Phillip was the first of the Bour-
bon dynasty who occupied the throne of
Spain, and this was the occasion of the wars
of the Spanish succession. Phillip was
married twice, first to Princess Marie Louise
Gabrielle of Savoy, his second wife was
Elisabeth Farnese of Parma. Phillip be-
came a victim to melancholia and abdicated
in favor of his son Louis. Louis died a few
months after of small pox, and Phillip was
obliged to take the reins of government,
In 1731 Phillip invaded Naples and took
possession of that kingdom and gave it to
his son Don Carlos.
	Phillip V. was succeeded by his son
Ferdinand VI., surnamed the “Wise.” He
ascended the throne in 1746. Ferdinand
did not share his ambitious step-mother’s
desire for conquest in Italy, and withdrew
from war, recalling all his armies so quick-
ly from Italy that all the northern part fell
into the hands of the Austrians, who treat-
ed the people with the greatest inhumanity
and even attempted to harness them to
their heavy-artillery. During the thirteen
years of his reign he was one of the most
just and humane of the monarchs who ruled
over Spain. He promoted the welfare of
his country, reorganized the navy, en-
couraged and established manufactures,
and by his judicial and political conduct
placed his brother on the throne of Naples.
Ferdinand married Magdalene Theresa of
Portugal. He died in 1759. 
	Ferdinand left no heir so the throne
descended to his brother, Charles IV. of
Naples, and Charles III. of Spain. Charles
was a very good man, for promoting educa-
tion, reforming and expelling the Jesuits in
1767. Before this time England became
alarmed at the naval preparations of Spain
and declared war in 1762, and took Havana
in Cuba and Manilla in the East Indies.
After several years peace was declared and
Havana was restored.
	Charles III. died in 1788, and was suc-
ceeded by his second son Charles Anthony,
Prince of Asturia (the eldest son was in.
capable of inheriting' the crown.) Charles
IV. was married to Maria Louise Theresa
of Parma. Charles was governed entirely
by Manuel Godey, the lover of his wife and
an instrument of Napoleon’s. The second
revival of the country commenced, trade
aud commerce began to show signs of re-
turning activity. During this reign war
broke out with Great Britain, resulting in
disaster to Spain. In 1804 another war
arose and resulted the same. In 1808
Charles abdicated in favor of his son
Ferdinand VII. but two days afterwards
privately protested.

[Column 3] 	
	Shortly after the prince was elevated to
the throne, he and his father were induced
by Napoleon to take a journey to Bayonne,
but instead of pleasure to find themselves
prisoners in the hands of Napoleon. They
surrendered the crown to Napoleon and he
transferred it to his brother Joseph. The
Supreme Council of Seville declared war
against. France and Napoleon. England
recognized Ferdinand as king and sent an
army to aid the Spanish. After many
bloody battles the French were driven from
the country. In 1813 Ferdinand VII. was
restored to the throne. He married the
notorious Christina who bore him two chil-
dren, Isabella and the Infanta Maria Louise.
Having no sons Ferdinand revoked the
Salic law which excluded females, and es-
tablished a regular succession to the throne
of Spain, females as well as males.
	Ferdinand died in 1833 and bequeathed
his crown to his daughter Isabella. His
brother Don Carlos contested the legitama-
cy of the throne, and this led lo a long and
disastrous war. Isabella II. was only three
years old when she was proclaimed queen.
Don Carlos and his son refused to take the
oath of allegiance and a civil war broke out
which raged for seven years. Don Carlos
reduced to the last extremity fled to France
for refuge and renounced his claim to the
throne of Spain. Isabella’s mother Christina,
and Esparten the “Duke of Victory,” were
appointed regents during her minority. At
the age of thirteen she was proclaimed of
age and at sixteen was married to her cous-
in, Don Francisco de Assis. Married
against her will to a man said to be unfit
for the conjugal state, the private character
of Isabella has been exposed to much cen-
sure and scandal.
	Ever since Isabella ascended the throne
disorder and dissatisfaction seem to be
characteristic of Spain, and in 1845 Don
Carlos made another attempt to gain the
throne of Spain for his son, Charles Louis
“Prince of Asturia.” Isabella with her
royal daughter set out for Barcelona but
was hooted and insulted through the streets.
The cause of this was that she wanted a
bill passed which would place the chief
municipal appointments in the hands of
the crown and deprive the inhabitants of
of the towns of any control over their func-
tionaries. She carried her measure but
there was such an uproar she was obliged
to yield. In 1841 quite a dispute arose
between Christina and Esparten, each one
wanted to be sole regent. Esparten was
elected and continued at the head of the
government until the 14th of July, 1856,
then Gen. O’Donnell was appointed presi-
dent. O’Donnell did not reign long; he
was obliged to resign on the 13th of Octo-
ber, and a new ministry was formed under
Gen. Narvaez. On the 28th of November
the queen gave birth to a son who as heir
to the kingdom took the title of Prince of
Asturia. The insugentsand royalists were
always fighting and in 1868 Isabella was
dethroned and with the king and her four
children took refuge in France. A new
president took the field, Marshall Serrano,
there was much dissatisfaction and all kinds
of parties formed, and an unforeseen diffi-
culty arose, the rebellion of Cuba. It be-
gan at Mauganillo on the 10th of October.
Manuel Cespedes was chief of the Cuban
forces and asked for recognition for the 
new Republic of Cuba, this rebellion re-
mained throughout 1869. Then the Spanish
began to look around for a king.


In 1871 the Cortez elected Prince Amedeo
of Italy to be king. Finding the task a
hopeless one Amedeo abdicated in 1873,
then the form of government was changed
to a Republic. In 1874 Spain was the
scene of general anarchy and much blood
was shed.
	In December 1874, Alphonso, (Isabella’s
son) was declared king. In 1877 Alphonso
married Mercedes, a daughter of the Duke
and Duchess of Montpensier, thus fulfilling
a part of the scheme of Louis Phillip of
France. Mercedes was a beautiful woman,
an angelic disposition, and the first and
only love of Alphonso. The honeymoon
was scarcely over when Mercedes began to
droop and fade and died in 1878. The
Spanish people insisted upon an heir to the 
throne so the following year Alphonso mar-
ried Marie Christina, the daughter of the
Austrian Arch Duke Ferdinand. In 1885
Alphonso died and six months after a son
was born. Little Alphonso was not only
born a king but came into the world in
the presence of his Ministers of State, Cabi-
net officers, members of the regency and
Privy council. A rumor had been circu-
lated that a bogus heir would be placed up-
on the throne, and that was the reason that
every consideration of privacy due to a wo-
man at such a time had to yield to the de-
mands of political situation. Marie Chris-
tina is regent during the minority of Al-
phonso. According to the laws of Spain
the king attains his majority at the age of
fifteen. The little king is twelve now, what
will be left for him to rule over remains to
be seen.
	The queen regent is very much disliked. 
She began by losing the friendship of her
people and then lost their sympathy, now
she is about to lose their respect. She is
said to follow the advice of her mother, the
Arch Duchess Elizabeth of Austria, when
any difficulty arises. She surrounds her-
self with cardinals and bishops who are the 
masters at the Court. She almost remains
in retirement when there are requests for
her presence. The queen was not present
a few weeks ago at the patriotic "Gran
Co[-]rida," the receipts of which were des-
tined for the national subscription. The
Spanish people do not know her, conse-
quently can have no sympathy for her.
	Don Carlos has never been so near the
throne as now, his partisans grow daily
more numerous and powerful. If the queen
cannot keep her people together and the
little king has to abdicate, will it be a
Monarchy or a Republic? War abroad
and revolution at home—such is the condi-
tion of Spain now. A movement against
the queen regent has been started and seri-
ous complications will probably arise before
long. It is said from a reliable source that
a majority of the Spanish people would like
to be rid of the Phillipines and Cuba, and
that they ought to be given to the Ameri-
cans without war; however, that will be
left for those who come later.
	We will glance over the interval since
elapsed, during the first half of which the
nation wasted itself on schemes of mad
ambition, and the latter half has sunk al-
most into a state of oblivion. In glancing
over this period we must confess that the
reign of Ferdinand and Isabella was the
most glorious in the annals of Spain.
']

Name: 33_christmas_day_little_children
Representative_Docs:
