["CHRISTMAS AGAIN.

	Here comes Christmas again with all its
delightful accompaniments and memories.
No day in all the year for the children like
Christmas with its wonders and its legends,
fairy tales and above all Santa Claus, for 
whom they long and wait, and even pray,

[Column 2]
so enthusiastic are they about his coming.
Dear old Santa Claus! What gladness he
brings to many thousands of homes, and
alas for those bereft of children or where
none ever came to bless and kindle new
life in human hearts. But the children,
Santa Claus belongs especially to them,
they watch for him carefully, continuously,
getting up at unseasonable hours to try and
surprise him and to see which way he
comes; whether over the roof with his rein-
deers and sled and down the chimney, or
through the window, or stealing in softly
when the door is open and hiding until all
are asleep in the house to make his raid as
the Brownies do. What fantastic visions
the little folks conjure up of the visits of
Santa Claus. Christmas is the day of all
days for the little folks, no other day in the
year is so attractive, not even the 4th of
July with its flags, banners, music, parades
and booming of cannon; nor yet the child’s
own birthday when he is “cock of the
walk,” so to speak. Is it because of the
delusion of Santa Claus that fires the child’s
imagination? partly so it must be. And
those of older growth, they, too, are fasci-
nated with delusions and fancies. And it
this only happened one day in the year one
might be more excusable.
	This is the day of love in every house-
hold, of peace on earth and good will to all
mankind, and every one who feels the im-
portance of the sweet charity the Savior
taught and practised should himself exer-
cise that sweet spirit of sublime and heaven-
ly grace which reflects as much upon the
giver as the receiver. The scripture says
“It is more blessed to give than to receive,”
and it is a divine truth,as many can testify.
	To know how to give acceptably is an
art in itself and one which many people do
not understand; to give ostentatiously is al-
ways an offense, even though the giver
may have the best of motives. It is true
that Christmas opens a wide door for gifts
to loved ones as well as for charity, but
nevertheless, careless giving especially of
charity, sometimes makes the recipient feel
he would rather starve. We are all, or al-
most all, too neglectful of the little courte-
sies and proprieties of life; we excuse our-
selves because there is so much to be done,
and we say this is an age of hurry and bus-
tle. But when one looks around and finds
those who do things of this sort beautifully,
in such a way that there is no possible
chance of criticism, then we wonder what is
the reason that others could not do as well.
A gift is not so much in itself as in the
manner of giving.
	Children should be taught to give in the
spirit of love and sweetness, and then there
would be greater pleasure and more satis-
faction in small things. As a people we
have grown so selfish ourselves with the
prosperity the Lord has blessed us with,
that we feel unless we can give largely we
must not give at all. Yet when we look
back and recall the early days in this valley
and the few little comforts we had for the
children at Christmas time and compare it
with the present, its loads of toys and beau-
tiful as well as useful things it seems in-
credible. And are we happier or better, or
are the children any more satisfied ? We
think not. The molasses candy, ginger
bread and dough-nuts Santa Claus brought
then were as acceptable as the best sweet-
meats and Hiler’s candy and wax dolls that
can open and shut their eyes instead of the
rag babies made by their own mamma’s


deft fingers, into which she put so many
loving stitches.
	Yes we have a different Christmas now,
and this year we have much to be thankful
for and to rejoice over. Many little ones
whose father went to battle have him home
with them for the jolly Christmas. And
some whose homes are not so blest, yet
know the war is over and look forward to
the home coming with joyful anticipation.
	There has been a bountiful harvest, the
Lord has blest His people wonderfully and
the children should be made to realize this,
and to comprehend how the Lord has pro-
vided for the needs of His people and given
them an abundance and to spare, so that
on this Christmas day in all the valleys of
this Western land, there should be no
scarcity in any home and certainly not
where there are those, blessed messengers of
relief, Bishops and sisters of the Relief
Society. The dear, old people who are so
much alone in the world, more even than
the children should be richly remembered
at Christmas. They are as fond of dainties
as the children, though they cannot keep
up the delusion of Santa Claus, and if the
children were taught to divide with the
aged people of their own locality, those who
have no grandchildren of their own to come
and bring them delicacies, it would be a
greater blessing to the children than to
those to whom they minister in innocence
and love. So let us learn the Christ lesson.
If we only give to those who can give to us
in return, we are not the true followers of
the lowly Nazarene whose birth we cele-
brate, (even though we have long since
learned that it was not the day of his birth.)
Christmas has so long been kept on this
particular day that a change all over the
world could scarcely be effected.
	This year, 1898, Christmas comes on the
Sabbath day and therefore festivities will
be observed upon Saturday or Monday, or
both as the case may be, but the children
will probably receive fruitful lessons at the
Christmas Sunday School.
	Already the city has put on its holiday
appearance, and never was there more dis-
play. Stores, shops and business houses of
all kinds are in gala dress, and the brighter,
gayer colors that have been the rage the
last year or more add to the brilliancy of
the decorations. In the midst of all this
display perhaps there is as much delusion
as attaches to Santa Claus. The home and
the fireside is always connected with Christ-
mas so closely that one invariably thinks of
the yule log, or the huge pine knots and
the joys of social or even jovial family inter-
course. “The holly round the Christmas
hearth” has become proverbial, and

	‘‘At Christmas time the open hand,
	Scatters bounty o’er sea and land.”

	It is good that we have the holidays,
what would life be without these seasons of
renewal? It makes us almost young again
to participate in such rejoicings.


WOMAN'S EXPONENT.


[Column 1]
", 'CHRISTMAS FANCIES.
Peace and good will to all mankind.
	Blessed Christmas if indeed it would
bring peace and good-will to all mankind.
Nowadays the Christmas time seems more
of a festivity and giving and receiving
gifts, than a time to exercise good will and
good feeling toward one’s neighbors; to
forgive wrongs and make peace with those
with whom we have been at variance, if
there are any such, would indeed make the
day a happy one. Cheerfulness is better
than hilarity, and simplicity even in giv-
ing is in much better taste than making
elaborate presents, or parading one’s

[Column 2]
generosity, as really seems to have come
to be the custom in this age. We deal
largely in extremes, even in giving, and
we do not always deal wisely with our-
selves or our friends. “Kind hearts are
more than coronets,” and the scripture
tells us, “a word fitly spoken is like apples
of gold in pictures of silver.” Therefore,
though we have not wealth to lavish on
our loved ones, we can bestow kindness
even in words.
	As a people we are in the enjoyment of
many blessings, and we have great reason
to rejoice that prosperity abounds in these
vales of the mountains and that we have
peace here, though we read and hear of
wars in other lands; and we should not for-
get the Lord in the days of our prosperity.
	Feasting and enjoying the good things
of life does not make up the sum of happi-
ness, not even at Christmas time; elabor-
ate preparations are very wearisome, and
the mother is often too much exhausted to
share in the children’s enjoyment of games
or sports, or perhaps to answer their ques-
tions about the why’s and wherefore’s of
Christmas. The injunction placed upon
us all to take care of our bodies, is greatly
disregarded at holiday times, or overdone,
we work so hard for the pleasure of a few
hours, we cook so much and some of us
eat so much that we are not happy and we
show it in our faces, therefore we are not
beautiful, though we may have bestowed
abundant pains upon our decorations to
make the home beautiful. If the mother
is care-worn or exhausted, if she cannot
wear a smiling face, then the day is not a
success. It is not necessary to have all
the luxuries the market affords, not even
on Christmas, to bring happiness; light
hearts, cheerful faces and kind words are
a far better relish at the family dinner
than the most savory dish lacking these
graces. Yet one does not like to be always
sermonizing. There are many pretty 
fancies about Christmas that have come
down to us, that set it off, so to speak, as
a day apart from all others, in the minds
of the little folks at least; and perhaps
none of us are altogether without a taste
for the mystic, and the legends and folk-
lore of ye olden time and of other lands
and other people, their habits, customs
and beliefs, have a more intense interest
because of their remoteness and the mys-
tery in which they are necessarily en-
shrouded.
	From the early Christians who sang the
midnight mass on the eve of the nativity,
in commemoration of the birth of Christ,
we get the name Christmas (Christ’s mass.)
Formerly immense logs were brought from
the forests and, often with much ceremony,
burned at Christmas time with great re-
joicing. Although we do not hear of the
yule log these days, yet a great log is
chosen even now where wood is used for
fuel; we who use coal do not have the
same feeling about the Xmas log. The
pictures in the fire on Christmas were con-
sidered significant, and the old wrongs
were thought to die out in the blaze.
Numberless superstitions that cluster

[Column 3]
around Christmas observances in the olden
time, are interwoven with Pagan and some
of them even early Christian ceremonies.
	It is said that “in Sweden on Christmas
Eve the shoes of the family are placed in
a row side by side, that harmony may pre-
vail during the year to come.” 
	“Throughout Northern Germany the
tables are spread and the lights left burn-
ing, that the Virgin Mary and the angel 
who brings the presents may find refresh-
ments.”
	This to us seems very absurd, but if in
our day the children thought more of the
mother or father, sister, brother or friend,
who gives on these occasions, and paid
them the reverence which is their due, the
spirit of jealousy and contention would
cease, and greater reverence might be ac-
corded to those who are really the good
angels in disguise.
	Even to this day it is a belief in Corn-
wall, Devonshire, and in English villages,
that the bees hum and that the cattle kneel
on Christmas Eve in adoration of the
Holy Child; and it is said the powers of
darkness are unable to work evil on man-
kind. Shakespeare, who is more often
quoted than any other poet, says:

“Some say that ever ’gainst that season comes
Wherein our Savior’s birth is celebrated,
The bird of dawning singeth all night long;
And then, they say, no spirit can walk abroad;
No fairy takes, nor witch hath power to charm,
So hallowed and so precious is the time.”

	The customs connected with the chil-
dren’s idea of Christmas have their origin
in Germany and the Netherlands. Our
little ones imitate the child of Holland
when they hang their stockings in front of 
the fireplace, so Santa Claus will find
them when he descends the chimney. The
beautiful Christmas tree, its branches
laden and lights sparkling, a sine que non,
with children nowadays is distinctly a
heathen idea perpetuated by the German
and Scandinavian races. The floral deco-
rations, which add so much to the beauty
of the festival, were inaugurated in the
days of the Saturnalia when the Roman
temples and houses were decorated with
flowers and garlands in honor of the god
of light. In the days of early antiquity
the British Druids reverenced the mistletoe
as something holy, when they found it
growing on an oak, the favorite tree of 
their divinity, Tutanes, the sun. When
they celebrated the winter solstice the
ancient Britons, accompanied by Druids,
went with music and singing to gather the
sacred mistletoe, and it was believed to
have a healing power. Clothed in white
the chief priest cut the tree with a golden
knife and the mistletoe (a sort of parasite)
would fall into the robe of the priest stand-
ing below, who distributed it among the
people, who carried it home and hung it
under the roof as a protection and shelter
to the sylvan deities during the winter
frost and cold. The Druids regarded the
pearly berries of the plant as an emblem
of purity and used it in the marriage rite.
The mistletoe custom still observed of
snatching a kiss under the mistletoe is


[Column 1]
associated with the mystic traditions, and
is a relic of the days of Druidism. The old
song we used to sing says:
	“The mistleto hung in the castle hall,
	The holy branch shone on the old oak wall,
	And all the tenants were blithe and gay,
	Keeping their Christmas holiday.”
	Holly, evergreen and laurel were used
to decorate the churches in early days in
England, as they are now. Ivy, though
sometimes used, is considered more appro-
priate for solemn occasions. Late in the
17th century the Ecclesiastical Canon of
England issued a decree that all Christ-
mas decorations must be down by the last
of January, as it was thought to be un-
lucky to keep them up longer in private
houses.
	At the time when the Druids were gath-
ering their mistletoe and the Romans were
decorating their temples in honor of Sat-
urn, the Scandinavians kindled huge bon-
fires in honor of the sun god, Thor.
	The good cheer of old England at
Christmas time is proverbial, and little
wonder when one reads of the feasting.
The peacock with his gorgeous train was
a royal dish brought to the festal board by
some fair lady, and over him the knights
errant swore to do or die for their “ladyes
fayre.” From this custom comes Justice
Swallow’s oath, “By cock and pie.” In
Charles II. time turkeys and capons with
plum pudding became the regular dish,
and the mince pie still perpetuated, was a
special requisite at a Xmas dinner more
than a hundred years ago.
	The Newcastle Chronicle of 1770 gives the
contents of the famous “Christmas pye”
of the olden time. * * * Its ingredients
were 2 bushels of flour, 20 pounds of
butter, 4 geese, 2 turkeys, 2 rabbits, 4 wild
ducks, 2 woodcocks, 6 snipe, 4 partridges,
2 neats tongues, 2 curlews, 4 blackbirds
and six pigeons, with eggs, sugar, raisins,
lemons, oranges and various kinds of
spicery. “it weighed twelve stone.” One
would wonder where it could be baked;
and yet when one knows what great fire-
places and ovens they had in those old
halls and castles, one might imagine that
even such a pie could be put in and baked.
In the lower part of the Nottingham
Castle, which, by the by, is about all that
is left of it, we saw the largest ovens, just
like a great arch, one on each side of a
huge fireplace. Of course they are not
in very good preservation, but are still
standing; and at Kenilworth Castle we
counted the remains of nine or ten im-
mense fireplaces, in one area of the ruin,
doubtless there had been ovens there
to correspond with the size of the fire-
places. When one recalls the number of
the inmates of these great buildings and
the retainers, and that there were no bake
shops adjacent to buy food from, and
everything must be made on the premises,
one can fancy great quantities of food
cooked somewhere.
	The writer when standing in St. George’s
Hall, Windsor Castle, thought of the great
banquets that had been given there to the
English warriors, and also when the brave
heroes and men were initiated into the
order of the Knights of the Garter; what
eating, drinking and carousing must have
taken place, and what wonderful prepara-
tions in the way of cooking must have pre-
ceded these great occasions. At the pres-
ent time one can buy almost everything
ready cooked, and the facilities for prepar-

[Column 2]
ing food are altogether easier than they
were even fifty years ago. 
	Christmas in England this year cannot
be a very merrie one, judging from the
dispatches of the war in South Africa, and
although Queen Victoria is very heroic,
yet so many of Her Majesty’s officers have
been slain or wounded, that conditions
must necessarily have a depressing effect
upon the whole country. The Queen will
have the sympathy of all true Americans
in this time of anxiety, for she is justly
beloved by the people of the United States.
It is to be hoped that matters may be so
adjusted as to bring this cruel war to a
close; and now that the court of arbitration
is so near being established the outlook for
a final compact seems more likely to be
consummated.
	How ardently one wishes for the day
when war shall cease, and the angelic in-
junction, “On earth peace, good will to-
ward men,” find a hearty response from
all nations. The “Yule peace,” old as the
Runic stones of antiquity, a time of good
fellowship and brotherly love, quarrels all
healed, feuds forgotten, and the world ani-
mated by the universal spirit of love. Is
this too much to hope for? At any rate,
each one who strives to love his neighbor
as himself is doing his utmost to fufill the
law.  Our blessed Savior said:
	Love your enemies, do good to them which hate
you.
	Bless them that curse you, and pray for them
that despitefully use you and persecute you.
	How few there are who heed these
words in the least, yet desire to keep the
commandments and excel in many ways.
Jesus in His “Sermon on the Mount” said:
“Blessed are the peacemakers, for they
shall be called the children of God.” And
what more fitting time to strive to culti-
vate this in one’s heart than the day on
which we celebrate the nativity of the
Savior of the world?
	Many mothers and housewives are like
Martha, whom the Savior rebuked, they
work and worry and fret, over-burdened,
over-solicitous, “troubled about many
things.” It would be so much better if
there were less cooking, less variety, and
if mothers were not so weary and could
get a little more real enjoyment at Christ-
mas time.
	“At Christmas play and make good cheer,
	For Christmas comes but once a year.”
', 'CHRISTMAS IN SCANDINAVIA.
	Christmas is the most important of all
feasts or “holy days” in the northern
countries. It was originally the feast of
the sun, a feast of rejoicing because the
shortest day of the season had passed,
and the sun had commenced its return to
the north. After the introduction of
Christianity into Scandinavia, its signifi-
cance was changed, and instead of being
the feast of the sun, it became a feast
in honor of the advent of the Son of Man,
and it was for many centuries the begin-
ning of the year.
	In olden times the people commenced
making preparations for Christmas sev-
eral weeks before the time, and among
the most important of these preparations
was the making of Christmas beer, which
was done with great care. Even among
the poorer classes, where the family beer
was ordinarily very weak, a much better
quality was made for Christmas, and suf-
ficient of it must be had to supply the
family liberally, and to treat every visitor
during the thirteen successive Christmas
days. During Christmas quarreling and
fighting was strictly forbidden; all must
be peace and good will, and all court
proceedings were suspended. Should
anyone do harm to his fellow man during
Christmas, great punishment would be
meted out to him later. Nor were the
people permitted to do any kind of hard
manual labor, only the most necessary
work, such as feeding the stock, etc., was
permissible.
	The so-called Christmas peace was also
extended to the brute creation. As long
as Christmas lasted man should keep
peace even with their natural enemies,
such as wolves, bears, rats and mice. To
avoid giving offence, these animals were
not even to be called by their right
names; but the wolf was during Christ-
mas called “grey leg” the bear “the old
one in furs,” mice the “little greys,” and
rats “long tails.”
	As the 24th of December drew nearer
the confusion and disorder, incident to
the preparations, became greater, and on
the 23rd of December it was at its height.
It was baking, cooking and a thorough gen-
real house cleaning that had to be done.
Everything had to be ready by sundown on
the 24th of December. Among the prepa-
rations for Christmas was also the rubbing
of the teeth of all domestic animals, and
that being done, horses, catte, sheep, etc.
were treated to a double portion of the
best of their usual allowance. An old
legend says that at midnight after Christ-
mas eve, at the exact hour that Christ
was supposed to have been born, all
animals received the power of speech,

[Column 2]
and could for a few moments converse
with each other like human beings,
	Everything else being ready, the peo-
ple turned their attention to their person-
al appearance; a bath and a complete
change of clothing on Christmas eve was
universal; and when attired in their best
the members of the household greeted
each other appropriately with “Merry
Christmas, health and strength and all
that is dear.” Then followed exchanges
of Christmas gifts, and then all sat down
to a bounteous Christmas supper in the
decorated room. The meal consisted
usually of dried fish and sweet porridge,
or ham with green cabbage and mush,
usually rice with well-to-do people. Af-
ter the table was once set Christmas eve,
it was never cleared until Christmas was
through, as every visitor in the house
during the time must, without exception,
partake of the hospitality of the same.
None were forgotten on Christmas eve;
all poor people were remembered; a large
dish was set out for the Christmas Saint
(Santa Claus), and the birds were provid-
ed for liberally.
	The Christmas cake was a most impor-
tant feature in every household; some-
times it was made in the shape of a man,
a ram, or a fat hog, otherwise perfectly
round. Sometimes a gold or silver coin
was placed in the center of the cake.
Christmas gifts commenced with ex-
changes of these cakes, but later they
were accompanied with gifts. The cus-
tom pertaining to Christmas cake was
introduced into England by the Vikings
from Scandinavia a thousand years ago,
and then “Yule cake” (yule or jul is the
Danish for Christmas) is well known to
this day.
	While eating the Christmas supper,
the head of the family would always
present a toast for Christ and the New
Year, and all would drink in honor there-
of from the oldest and most costly vessel
which the household possessed. Beer
and “mjod,’ (mead made of honey) were
the beverages used at Christmas, and this
was freely used by both men and women.
Whiskey and strong beverages were sel-
dom used on Christmas eve. After the
more solid meal, “Ebleskiver” was served
and the meal was prolonged as much as
possible, as there existed an old su-
perstitious legend to the effect that
the one who got through eating first
would die before the year was through.
At length, when the meal was over, the
head of the family offered a benediction,
and then gave the hand to the housewife,
saying, “Tak for Mad” (thanks for the
food), and after that shook hands with
all in the house; then follows a general
handshaking with “Tak for Mad,” coup-
led with many good wishes; the children
and the servants come in last; they com-
mence with the oldest person of the
household and end with the youngest.
	Many superstitious customs were asso-
ciated with Christmas eve proceedings:
Thus, in the glare of the Christmas can-
dles, which were to burn all night, young
people could see their future mate, and
everybody, to a greater or less extent,
see their future, at least what would hap-
pen to them the next year.
	Christmas day (December 25th) was
observed as a solemn holiday. Then ev-
erybody went to church, but spent the
rest of the day quietly at home, and it
was considered a very offensive breach of

[Column 3]
Christmas etiquette for any visits to be
made on that day. But on the 26th were
commenced the general Christmas fes-
tivities, aside from the solemn assemblies,
and they continued until the 5th of Janu-
ary. An important feature of the second
Christmas day was the horse feast, which
originated in the ancient Scandinavian
mythology, when the god Frey rode
around among his people on a holy ani-
mal; but when Christianity was intro-
duced in Scandinavia, they substituted
Stephen, the first Christian martyr, for
the god Frey, and the custom finally
dwindled down to general Christmas
races.
	Numerous and varied were the sports
and festivities of Christmas in the Scan-
dinavian countries in the olden times, but
these have considerably reduced of late
years. Yet Christmas, even today, is
looked forward to with much joy and an-
ticipation, and considerable good will is
manifested by the people, one toward
another, during the Christmas holidays,
and the exchange of presents and friendly
greetings is in order, as much now as it
ever was. The Christmas tree festivities
were almost unknown in Scandinavia un-
til seventy-five years ago, but were uni-
versal in France as early as the thirteenth
century.
		EMMA HOWELL JENSON.


']

Name: 34_city_home affairs_lake_young
Representative_Docs:
['HOME AFFAIRS.
THE HANDEL and Hayden Society which 
has been organized in this city, have been pre-
paring themselves, we understand, to give a  
concert about the middle of November next.
THE ALLEGHANIANS closed their entertain-
ments in Salt Lake Saturday evening 9th ult.,
they drew tolerably full houses; the Matinee on 
Saturday afternoon was crowded and the ap-
plause was general.
A SOLICITATION was tendered by several gen-
tlemen of this city to Mr. Charles Vivian, to 
give another of his entertainments at the thea-
tre, before he returns to San Francisco; he ac-
cordingly complied with their request Thurs-
day last.
IN CONSEQUENCE of Chief Justice White
not having arrived in time to preside at the 
opening of the October term of the Third Dis-
trict Court, Monday the 11th inst., Gov. Emery
re-assigned Associate Justice Boreman to this
district, temporarily.
ELDER R. V. Morris in charge of the Wyo-
ming company of emigrants, arrived in this 
city with the company all safe and in good 
condition, Tuesday Oct. 5th.  They have had 
a speedy and pleasant journey.
THE FOURTEENTH Ward Literary Associa-
tion, re-opened Monday evening, Oct. 4th, at
7 o'clock in the Assembly Rooms, and will hold
a regular series of exercises every week on 
Monday evening, during the season.  The pub-
lic are invited to attend.
A METEOR was seen in this city on the even-
ing of October 12th, just about ten o'clock. It
was in a Westerly direction and descended in a 
North-Westerly at an angle of about 45[degree symbol]. When
first seen it seemed a pale blue tint, but after-
ward appeared a bright glowing crimson and fi-
nally exploded with a sparkling and resplend-
ent flash.
WE HAVE received from Mrs. William Wag-
staff three beautiful bouquets of a variety of 
flowers, among which were many colored ele-
gant dahlias, verbenas, honey-suckles and ge-
raniums, with ferns, grasses, etc, which added
much to their beauty; we accord the lady our 
most sincere thanks for the same, and also the 
luscious peaches which accompanied them and 
we shall not forget the pleasing compliment.
MONDAY OCT. 11th, there was a cheap excur-
sion to Lake Point, and it was well-timed to se-
cure a large party, and people from all quarters
availed themselves of the opportunity to enjoy
it, some of the party took a sea-bath, but the 
water was rather too cool for most people.  The
accommodations at the landing under the new
proprietors "The Southworth's" are very highly
spoken of.
THE DESERET Teachers Association held ad-
journed meetings conference week in the Uni-
versity Building, and elected their regular offi-
cers for the year; President, O H. Riggs; Vice
President, John R. Park; Secretary, F. M. 
Bishop; Treasurer, Karl G. Maeser.  After the 
transaction of necessary business for the season,
the Association adjourned "sine die".
THE MASSIVE and elegant front for the new
Deseret Bank Building arrived last week and 
has been put up.  The front of the story
above will be composed of brick and will be
similar in design to that of the South side of 
the building; the work is rapidly being for-
warded and it is expected it will be completed
before winter sets in.  The side and rear walls
of the new Co-operative Store are now up,
ready for the roof timbers; the iron front is ex-
pected very soon.  These two large new struct-
ures for public purposes are, besides being sol-
idly constructed, quite an addition to the ap-
pearance of the street.
A VERY elegant monument has been erected to 
the memory of the late Prest. Heber C. Kimball,
in the private burying ground belonging to that 
family.  The monument has a granite base and 
surbase; the body is formed into four gothic
panels of white veined marble on which the in-
scription is cut.  The monument is all of home
workmanship and is a fine specimen of artistic
skill.  The inscription is composed of about 1,-
700 letters and gives a concise account of the 
birth, life, travels ministry and death of this
distinguished man.  President Brigham Young
has also added a testimonial in his own words
which reads as follows: "He was a man of as 
much integrity, I presume as any man who 
ever lived upon the earth.  I have been person-
ally acquainted with him for forty-three years,
and I can testify that he has been a man of 
truth, a man of benevolence, a man that was 
to be trusted." On one of the panels is an in-
scription to the memory of Vilate Murray wife
of President Kimball who died Oct. 22nd, 1867.
MR. E. W. Tullidge who recently returned 
from New York, where he has been making ar-
rangements for the publication of his new work,
entitled the, "The Founders of Utah," informs
us that he is convinced the book he has been 
contemplating writing, and which he intends
bringing out immediately after this present
work, will be very popular.  He intends com-
prising and compiling in that work sketches
from the lives of the most prominent women
in this church and Territory.  Woman's work,
mission, sphere etc. are now extensively dis-
cussed, and people interested in these subjects
are especially curious to know something of the 
lives, motives, etc. of women who live in plu-
ral marriage, from one of their own people.
Mr. Tullidge also informs us that his last play
"Ben Israel" will shortly be brought out in 
Boston, by Miss Katharine Rogers and Mr.
Vincent.  We sincerely hope Mr. Tullidge may
realize some pecuniary remuneration from 
his works, as he has labored assiduously
in his vocation, and is certainly a brilliant,
pleasing and attractive writer.
THE VISIT of his Excellency President U.
S. Grant to this city, which took place recently,
was one of special interest and importance to
the people of this Territory.  His position, be-
ing the most elevated one in the nation, enti-
tles him to the highest respect and the most
distinguished attentions.  As soon as it was
authenticated that President Grant and party
were really coming to Salt Lake City, the City
Council held a special meeting, adopting reso-
lutions to give him a courteous, hearty and 
welcome reception, appropriate to his position.
Invitations were accordingly issued to federal,
civil and military officers, and some private
citizens, to constitute a party of welcome to go 
on a special train to Ogden to meet his Excel-
lency, and escort him to the city.  The party
left this city at half past nine, and arrived in 
Ogden at eleven o'clock.  The depot at Ogden
was thronged with people, anxious to avail
themselves of the opportunity of seeing the 
Chief Executive of the American Republic.
The special train on which the President was
traveling arrived in Ogden about half past
twelve o'clock.  His Excellency was standing
upon the platform of the car; the crowds of


people waved their hats as he approached,
which he gracefully acknowledged by taking
off his hat and bowing. The Ogden brass
brand welcomed him with their choicest se-
lections of music. Hon. George Q. Cannon, as
spokesman of the committee, approached Presi-
dent Grant, greeted him, and informed him of
the delegation, and presented to him the com-
mittee and other gentleman who accompanied
them. Soon after the train left Ogden, Presi-
dent Brigham Young, Hon. John Taylor,
Joseph F. Smith, Brigham Young, jr., and the
other gentlemen and ladies of the special train
party were presented to President Grant, who
stood upon the platform of the car, and were
also introduced to the President's party,
Mrs. President Grant, Col. Fred. Grant and
wife, Gen. O. E. Babcock, Hon. Hamilton Borie
ex-Secretary of the Navy, wife and niece,
and Governor Thayer of Wyoming. President
Young's meeting with President Grant was
particularly pleasant and cordial, and President 
Young, in his most happy manner, welcomed
his Excellency to Salt Lake City, and expressed
his desire that his visit might be enjoyable.
The President was met at the Salt Lake depot
by a long train of carriages, and the Sabbath
School children were arranged from the depot
to the Temple block; the most excellent order
prevailed everywhere. In the course of the
afternoon the President appeared upon the
balcony of the Walker House and was intro-
duced to the populace assembled there by Gov-
ernor Emery. On Monday morning President
Grant with his party, Gov. Emery and others,
visited the New Tabernacle; one incident con-
nected with this visit we wish to notice, which
is, Mrs. President Grant, when in the Taberna-
cle while the organ was playing, was deeply
affected, and her heart was drawn out in prayer
and she remarked afterwards that she 
then offered up a prayer to God in behalf of 
this people, "but remember," said she, "it was
a Methodist prayer." After their visit to the 
Tabernacle they drove up to Arsenal Hill to
get a good view of the city, then to Camp
Douglas. The President refused to receive any 
public demonstration. but said his visit was a 
private one, and did not warrant any firing of
cannons, martial music, etc. At one o'clock in
the afternoon he gave a public reception at the
Walker House, and thousands of people were
introduced and had the honor of shaking hands
with him. On the way to the depot President
Grant and party called upon Hon. Wm. Jen-
nings, where they were cordially received, and
refreshments suitable to the occasion served to
them. A select party of ladies and gentlemen 
accompanied them to Ogden. The President's 
private car was decorated with choice flowers 
in a tasteful and ornamental manner; the word
"Welcome" artistically arranged over the mir-
ror. President Grant, Mrs. Grant, Mrs. Fred.
Grant, Gen. Babcock, Ex-Secretary Borie, Gov-
ernor Thayer, Miss Borie and Mrs. Governor
Emery came and sat in the car with the Mor-
mon ladies and gentlemen, and were as socia-
ble and friendly as possible; Mr. Cannon very
politely introduced those ladies who had not
before been presented to the President and his
party. Everything passed off pleasantly; the
President and Mrs. Grant, in bidding the party
good bye, very graciously thanked the ladies
who decorated the car. When the President's 
car was ready to start, his Excellency appeared
upon the platform accompanied by his wife
and some others of their party; hearty cheers
resounded from all sides and the ladies waved
their handkerchiefs in token of adieu. Every
courtesy and attention possible, in so short a
time, was extended, to the President and party
during their brief stay.


WOMAN'S EXPONENT.


', 'HOME AFFAIRS.  

	RETURNED HOME—Mr. Don Carlos and Mr.
Feramorz L. Young, sons of the late President
Brigham Young, arrived home on Monday
evening, June 24, and Mr. W. G. Sharp, son of
Bishop John Sharp, arrived Thursday evening,
June 26, all in excellent health and spirits.
These young men graduated a few weeks since
at the Van Rensselaer Polytechnic Institute, at
Troy, New York. We welcome them back,
and trust, with the education they have re-
ceived, and the fine natural abilities they pos-
sess, they may make life a complete success.
	ON SATURDAY and Sunday, June 14 and 15,
we attended the Quarterly Conference of the
Relief Society of Tooele Stake, held at Grants-
vllle. Mrs. M. A. Hunter, President of the
R.S. of Tooele County, presiding. Sisters and
brethren too from the various settlements were
present. President F. M. Lyman, Bishop Tut-
tle of Tooele, and others from the different
precincts favored the sisters with their pres-
ence. Saturday afternoon the children of
Grantsville were organized into a Primary
Association. The instructions given at this
Conference by President Lyman, were very
encouraging to the sisters in that vicinity.
Miss E. R. Snow was also eloquent and earnest
in addressing the meetings, Mrs B. W. Smith
and other sisters contributed towards making
the conference an interesting one.
	WE ARE happy to announce the marriage of
our young relative, Mr. Junius F. Wells (Presi-
dent of the Young Men's Mutual Improvement
Associations,) to Miss Lena Fobes, of this city.
The marriage ceremony was performed by Pre-
sident D. H. Wells, father of the bridegroom,
in the Endowment House, Monday, June 16th,
at 5 o’clock p.m. A reception was given in the
evening at his father’s residence in the 12th
Ward, at which time were assembled all his
"aunt's, sisters and cousins," with a host of
other friends, who offered their fervent congra-
tulations. The happy pair were the recipients
alao of many useful and elegant presents.
	ON MONDAY, June 23d, another very brilliant
wedding was celebrated in this city. The
bride was Miss Jane Jennings, daughter of
Hon. Wm. Jennings, the bridegroom Mr. Jas.
A. Eldredge, son of Hon. Horace S. Eldredge.
President Taylor performed the ceremony, at
4 o’clock p.m., in the Endowment House. The
reception in honor of Miss Janie’s marriage, at
Devereux House, in the evening, was really
the most magnific[-]nt affair of the kind ever
celebrated in this city. To attempt a descrip-
tion of the elegance or enjoyment in one para-
graph is simply impossible. We offer our most
sincere and fervent congratulations to these
now voyagers on the matrimonial sea of life,

[COLUMN 2]
and trust the breezes may be favorable, and
should storms arise that the winds and waves
may at least be propitious.
	ON FRIDAY morning a party of ladies and
gentlemen left this city to be present at the
closing exercises of the B. Y. Academy at
Provo. Bishop O. F. Whitney, Professors J.
Toronto and J. Kingsbury of the Deseret Uni-
versity, James Dwyer, Esq., Miss Ada Dwyer,
Miss Keetle Heywood, and Miss Annie Wells,
were of the party. We regret we have not
space to give some account of the manner in
which the academy is conducted, under the
superior management of Prof. Carl G. Maeser,
with some gentlemen and ladies as assistants
in different departments. This school is very
highly recommended by all who have attended
and by all who have taken pains to examine
into the peculiar methods of teaching adopted
in the institution. 
	On Saturday morning a party of about fifty
of the teachers and students, including the
visitors from Salt Lake, started up Provo
Cañon, well supplied with pic-nic. From one
or two who participated in the trip we learn
that it was really most delightful, scenery
grand, beautiful flowers and shrubbery, clear
streams of water sparkling and rippling through
the valleys, and the waterfall exceeded in its
beauty the idea they had formed. They had a
feast of good things to eat, and a literary en-
tertainment of songs, speeches, recitations,
jokes and brilliant conversation. The visitors
from this city were extremely well pleased
with their visit to Provo and the people who
so agreeably entertained them.
	SINCE JUNE 15th number of the EXPONENT
was issued, our esteemed friend and Brother
Elder George Reynolds, has been committed
to prison in Lincoln, Nebraska. We had not
believed he would be imprisoned on account of
his being a test case brought forward purposely 
to decide a vexed question. Elder Reynolds,
implicitly believing as most of the Latter-day
Saints did, that the religious liberty granted
by the Constitution of the United States would
be his safeguard. We have no words to express
our feelings on this subject, having been im-
bued with the idea from childhood that in
America there was freedom and religious liber-
ty for all, of whatever color creed. We know
the Apostles and Elders were imprisoned for
their religion in Christ’s day, but that was not
under a free government. They rejoiced that
they were worthy to suffer. And so does our
good brother. He writes that his greatest dis-
appointment is in not being allowed pencil
and paper to scribble down his thoughts. 
	He says in a letter to his family, "my health
is good, I feel quite happy. * * *
God bless and comfort you all, be assured there
are many worse places than in prison for con-
science sake. It cannot take away the peace
which reigns in my heart. Repin[-] not, but
pray continually for yourselves and for me. Bro.
Reynolds speaks highly of the kindness of the of-
fficers who attended him East, and sends greet-
ing to all his old friends: those who desire.to
write to him: address Georgo Reynolds, Box C,
Lincoln, Nebraska. We hope we shall shortly
have some better news to publish on this sub-
ject, at any rate, we hope for his return to
Utah. We have heard that the Anti-Polygamy
ladies of this city have asked President Hayes
not to grant the pardon asked for Brother 
Reynold’s, but let him serve in the
penitenriary and that be not remanded to
the Utah penitentiary. We had understood
that the ladies of this Society had no wish to
have past offences punished, but to prevent any
action of this kind in the future. God forgive
them, they know not what they do, for what
they sow that also they must reap.

[COLUMN 3]
	THE RELIEF Society quarterly conference of
this stake of Zion, was held in the Fourteenth
Ward Assembly rooms, J[-]ne the 20th and 21st.,
beginning at 10 o’clock a. m. June 20th Mrs.
M. I. Horne presiding. The first part of the
morning was occupied with reports from the
various branches of the Belief Society in this
county.  These reports represented each of
these organizations in good condition. Mrs.
Horne made a few brief remarks and Miss. E.
R. Snow then addressed the conference. 
	Afternoon session. The presidents of the
different societies in wards and settlements
each made a verbal report, all, of which were
very satisfactory. When they had finished
Miss E. R. Snow made a few appropriate re-
marks and the conference adjourned until next
Saturday. Mrs. Horne announced that the
next morning would be occupied by the Young
Ladies Association. 
	Saturday morning. The Young Ladies Con-
ference. Mrs. Louie Felt presiding in, absence
of the President Mrs. Mary Freeze. The re-
ports of the several Y. L. M. I. A. of the coun-
ty were read, and then each president made a
verbal report. Mrs. Louie Felt occupied a lit-
tle time also. Miss E. R. Snow, and then El-
der W. W. Taylor, by invitation, made a few
remarks. He explained the order of filling out
the forms for reports, and showed the impor-
tance of practicing habits of order in financial 
matters, which would, if necessary, enable the
sisters to assist their husbands in keeping their
accounts, for there was no one in whom he
would have more confidence in such matters
than his wifə; and if habits of order were
taught the young, by example as well as pre-
cept, a foundation would be laid in them for
true greatness. He showed the great import
ance of striving to benefit those surrounding
us with the blessings enjoyed by the faithful,
thus creating an influence in behalf of righte-
ousness in the midst of the Saints, that the
children of the Saints might not be led astray.
	Saturday afternoon. Mrs. M. I. Horne pre-
siding, was devoted principally to reports from
Primary Associations.  Miss. Maggie Freeze
read the written reports, after which Presidents
of Primaries in several wards reported verbally.
Mrs. M. T. Horne made some remarks upon the
discipline and management of children, the
necessity of teaching them faith and a knowl-
edge of principle. Mrs. Zina D. Young, M[-]s.
S. M. Kimball, Miss E; R. Snow all spoke
upon the necessity of training the children in
the nurture and admonition of the Lord,
and expressed their pleasure in these organiza-
tions of the little children, and their belief
that through this spiritual training all would
come to a knowledge of the Lord from the
least unto the greatest. Pres. E. R. Snow
said that perhaps at the general meeting two
weeks from that day, they might organize the
P[--]mary Association in a stake capacity, and
wanted a full attendance of the sisters on that
occasion. Conference adjourned until Sept.
next 26th and 27th.


THE BOSTON "Woman’s Journal" May 31,
contains another of Mrs. Julia Ward Howe's
attractive letters written from abroad. It
is dated Paris, May 10. Mrs. Howe's let-
ters are charming, and would make a valu-
able book. There are many books of travel
that are wanting in that peculiar quality
that would make them really useful and
inspiring. Such books are dry and unen-
tertaining, because of the style in which
they are written. Mrs. Howe's letters from
abroad would, we believe, find ready sale,
not only on account of their real merit, but
her intelligent and happy manner of de-
scription of places and people, also her ad-
mirable power of expressing what she sees
and feels.


', 'HOME AFFAIRS.

	ON FRIDAY and Saturday evenings, Oct. 6
and 7th, select entertainments were given in
the 10th Ward School-house, consisting of
songs, recitations, dialogues, instrumental per-

[Column 2]
formances etc. The most striking and pleas
ing feature of the entertainment was the reci-
tation of "Beautiful Snow," by Miss Mattie
Paul. In character of expression, in voice a[-]d
gesture, her rendering of this pathetic poem
was equal to that of a cultivated artiste.
	OGDEN RELIEF SOCIETY.—On Saturday eve-
ning, Sept. 20, an entertainment was given for
the benefit of this society in the Ogden Thea-
tre. Misses Carrie Cogswell, Susie Marden and
Edith Clawson, and Messrs. Forster and Clarke
of this city, Mr. Alfred Thorne, and members
of the Ogden Dramatic Company, produced
the play of "Camille," which was followed by
an "Olio," and concluded with the "Tower
Scene" in the play of "King John," in which
Miss Clawson gave her clever rendition of
"Prince Arthur." There was a "packed"
house, and the general opinion given was that
it was an excellent dramatic entertainment.
	President F. D. Richards thanked the per-
formers, musi[-]ians, etc., all of whom had gen-
erously given their service gratuitously, and
the audience, on behalf of the Relief Society.
	Credit is due Sisters Richards and S. Herrick,
the instigators and managers of the entertain-
ment, from the proceeds of which the Relief
Society will be benefitted.—Ex.
	DESERET SILK ASSOCIATION.—The Deseret
Silk Associotion held a special meeting at Fire-
men's Hall, on Saturday afternoon, Oct. 7th,
Mrs. Zina D. Young presiding.
	Mrs. Young reported that she expected a reel
would be here in a few days. She had received
a large quantity of cocoons. This organiza-
tion was not for any personal benefit, but for
the benefit of the community of Utah. We
ought to be looking after our trees and cut-
tings, as it was very near time to plant them
out.
	Mr. Robbins said it was best to plant them
in the fall. He had had fifty years' experience
and had always found it best.
	Mrs. Barney reported that, while in the
north, she had collected $105. She could not
raise the worms, but felt a desire to see the
movement a success.
	There being quite a number present from the
various settlements, the remainder of the time
was taken up in asking questions and answer-
the same.
	The following donations were received:
Cynthia Clyde, $1; Mary S. Clark, $1; Martha
Thomas. 50c.
	Meeting adjourned till the first Wednesday
in November, at the same place.
					R. SIMPSON, Secretary.

	THE PEOPLE'S Territorial Convention for the
nomination of a Delegate to Congress, was
held in the Co[-]rt House in Salt Lake City,
Saturday, Oct. 7th, at half-past twelve, p. m.
David O. Calder, acting chairman, called the
convention to order; Judge Elias Smith, on
motion of Mr. Calder was chosen temporary
chairman. John T. Caine, on motion of Col. J.
R. Winder was elected temporary Secretary.
The business was all arranged and carried for-
ward in a quiet and methodical manner, and
no confusion whatever occurred. The com-
mittee, which had been appointed in the usual
form, reported the following officers f[--] a per-
manent organization. President D. H. Wells;
Vice Presidents, according to their districts,
A. O. Smoot and A. Hatch; E. Snow and J. N.
Smith; J. F. Smith and Brigham Young. Jr,
Secretaries, John T. Caine, Moses Thatcher,
and R. S. Horne; Chaplain, F. D. Richards;
Sergeant at Arms, Joel Grover. This report
was unanimously carried. Hon. W. H. Hoop-
er Chairman of the committee on resolutions,


reported the following subjects in form. State-
hood, Education, Irrigation, Silver Currency,
Stumpage, General. The convention sustained
the present people's territorial central com-
mittee. Hon. George Q. Cannon was formally
nominated and several speeches made on the
subject. The nomination was received with
universal applause.
	SUNDAY SCHOOL MOVEMENT.—A general
meeting of the Sunday school superintendents
and teachers of the Territory was held at the
Fourteenth Ward Assembly Rooms, Friday
evening Oct. 6th. Of the Twelve Apostles
there were present Elders John Taylor, Wil-
ford Woodruff, F. D. Richards, and George Q.
Cannon. There were representatives from
nearly all the counties and the time was occu-
pied mostly by hearing from the superintend-
ents; the reports of the progress of the move-
ment being very encouraging, and indicated
that the Bishops and other leading men were
taking a more lively interest in it than hereto-
fore.
	THE SUNDAY School is one of the greatest
educators of the young that has ever been
instituted; and upon the youth of any na-
tion, or people depends the future strength
and power for good or evil. It is the influ-
ence of Sunday Schools in a very great de-
gree that gives sublimity of tone to the
aspirations and ambitions of the young.
The subject is one which involves the highest
and holiest interests of a community. Here
they are, or should be taught to reverence
every thing that is sacred and pure, to imitate
the blessed Savior in humility and meek-
ness which were the striking characteristics of
His life and death.
	Let every mother be awakened to renewed
interest in this direction, and inspire, if possi-
ble, all within her circle with the same zeal
and earnestness. To the children of the poor,
and to the orphan the Sunday School is a bless-
ing which it is impossible to estimate. God
bless the Sunday School; it is one of the most
potent workers of the redemption of Israel.
	DR. ELLEN B. Ferguson to whom reference
has been made in this paper, has returned from
St. George and taken up her residence in Pro-
vo, where she will continue the practice of
medicine. Mrs. Ferguson is a regularly grad-
uated physician, has studied her profession
in England and America, and spent four years
of her life in the hospitals of London and
Paris; has had twelve years experience in an
extensive practice in the United States. We
would recommend her to the sisters in
Provo and vicinity who need the services of a
skillful and experienced physician. She is al-
so a fine musician and vocalist and will give
lessons in vocal and instrumental music at her
residence. Office and residence in A. F. Mac-
donald's house, Provo, Utah Co. Mrs. Dr.
Ferguson has traveled and lectured extensively
in different countries; she is a lady of most
pleasing and attractive manners, her voice is
clear and full of melody, and in notices we
have seen from the Eastern press, she has been
spoken of as a woman possessing "rare intel-
lectual ability and literary culture; a thorough
knowledge of all that belongs to the human
system, and the pathology of disease;" Mrs.
Dr. Ferguson is also spoken of as "one having
in a large degree those qualities of hert and
mind that must always be the crowning glory
of true and enlightened womanhood."
	Mrs. Ferguson's course of lectures on Gyne-
cology, delivered in the East, treat of the Con-
stitution, Idiosyncrasies and Diseases of Wo-
man, together with the Laws of the Social and
Physical relations of the Sexes.
	A Woman's Convention.—On Wednesday,
Oct. 4th, at 2 o'clock p. m., the officers and
members of the Relief Society from all the
different wards of the city and vicinity con-
vened in the Council Hose by previous ap-
pointment. President Miss E. R. Snow arose
and explained to the sisters that President B.
Young would not be present, as had been an-
ticipated, but would send his private Secretary
with a communication. She thenn nominated
Mrs. M. I. Horne to preside over the meeting,
and Mrs. E. B. Wells to act as Secretary,
which motions were unanimously carried.
	After the opening exercises, the assemblage
being too large to be accommodated in the
Council House, the meeting adjourned to the
Old Tabernacle. Elder George Reynolds, hav-
ing arrived with President Young's communi-
cation, arose and addressed the sisters on the
object of the meeting and read the following
letter from President Young:
			"Salt Lake City, Oct. 4th, 1876.
		"To the President, and Members of the Re-
lief Societies, in meeting assembled at the
Council House this afternoon.
	"Dear Sisters.—It would be very gratifying
to us if you could form an association to start
business in the capacity of disposing of home-
made articles, such as are manufactured among
ourselves, for the benefit of the Latter-day
Saints; also to make a selection from among
the sisters, members of this association, to take
charge and carry on this business on commis-
sion or by purchase, and to transact all the
business pertaining to this institution. If you
cannot be satisfied with the selection of sisters
from among yourselves to take charge, we will
render you assistance by furnishing a compe-
tent man for the transaction of the financial
matters of this establishment.
		"Your brother in the Gospel,
					"Brigham Young."
	After reading this letter, Brother Reynolds
made some very suggestive and appropriate re-
marks on home industries and the great desire
President Young felt to have the people push
forward in the development of home manufac-
ture.
	An organization was formed by electing the
following persons as officers of the association.
Miss E. R. Snow, President; Mrs. Priscilla
Stains, Vice President; Mrs. Elizabeth Davis,
Secretary; Mrs. Bathsheba Smith, Treasurer;
Mrs. Sarah M. Kimball, Mrs. Lydia Ann
Wells, Mrs. Phebe Woodruff, Mrs. Presendia
Kimball, Directors.
	Many excellent remarks were made by the
officers and such suggestions by Miss E. R.
Snow and others, as were adapted to the occa-
sion and organization of the association. After
all the business had been transacted which
seemed requisite at the time, the officers of the
board were requested to meet at the Old Con-
stitution building at 6 o'clock the same eve-
ning, and after singing and prayer the Conven-
tion adjourned sine die.
	The Relief Society Woman's Mercan-
			tile Association.
Has opened a store for the sale of home man-
ufactures in the building lately occupied by
the Woman's Centennial Territorial Fair,
known as the Old Constitution Building, op-
posite Z. C. M. I. store.
	We are now ready to receive home made use-
ful and ornamental articles of all kinds, which
we will sell on commission.
	Many of our citizens have felt the want of a
place devoted exclusively to Utah productions,
and now that we are, in accordance with the
wishes of President Young, uniting our hum-
ble efforts in this direction, we confidently
trust that we shall not only have the support
of all branches of the Relief Society through-
out the valleys of the mountains, but also of
our brethren and all others who feel an inter-
est in the future welfare and prosperity of
Utah.
	Commencing as we do without capital we
wish it distinctly understood that at present
we can only sell on commission.
				Eliza R. Snow, President.
				Priscilla M. Stains,
						Vice President.
				Elizabeth Davis,
						Secretary.


WOMAN'S EXPONENT.


']
