Name: 38_fair_primary_little_articles
Representative_Docs:
["EAST BOUNTIFUL.

	Report of Primary Fair held at East Boun-
tiful, July the 11th, 12th and 13th, under the
direction of Mrs. Ann Dustin, President, with
her assistants. Many things were on exhibition
that were useful and ornamental. One pair of
stockings knit by a little girl nine years old.
Two cushions made of velvet and satin, one of
unraveled yarn. One chemise made by a little
girl twelve years old, one quilt made by a little
girl seven years old; three balls of carpet-rags
sewed by a little girl; two quilts pieced by two
girls, ages eleven and thirteen,and a number of
quilts pieced by others; a number of blocks
pieced by very small girls; a number of pieces
of lace; two petticoats nicely embroidered; one
pair of garters knit by a little girl, and em-
broidered pillowslips; a number of wool mats
and home knit curtains; a fine beaded necklace
made by a girl eleven years old; a very pretty

[Column 3]
case of wax flowers; a home-made basket with
specimens from Soda Springs; a nice bunch of
wool flowers; one beautiful case of wool flowers
in a frame; one case worked on velvet; one box
of wool flowers handsomely made by a little
girl; twenty-five tidies; fifteen mottoes beauti-
fully worked; one worked well by a little girl
six years old; quite a number of cornucopias
and hanging baskets; ornamental match boxes;
pictures framed by little girls; a horse and two
cats cut out of cloth by a little girl; some very
fine bread made and baked by a little girl; one
cake nicely made and ornamented; also bottled
fruit, raspberries aad rhubarb; two maps of the
United States by a girl age fourteen years, and
one of the Western States by a girl eight years
old. The boys' department consisted of two
wheel-barrows, one large and one small; a
medium sized rake; a shovel; plow; a cultiv-
ator and one shovel; one knife and fork; one
rolling pin, and potato masher; one map of
South America drawn and painted by a boy
ten years old; hard words spelled and printed
by a boy eight years old; some vegetables
raised by the little boys, also some fruit. The
house was tastefully decorated with ever-greens
and vases of flowers and smoke-tree.
	Sister Porter, President of Centerville Pri-
mary, and Counselor, with a number of Pri-
mary children, came to visit the FAIR, and
President Hatch with her Counselor and some
of their Primary children came to visit us
and we had a very interesting time.
			ANN DUSTIN, President.
			SARAH DAVIS, Asst. Secretary.


", 'PRIMARY FAIR, SOCIAL HALL.
	The Primary Fair of this Stake was held
in the Social Hall June 18, 19, and 20th,
under the direction of Ellen C. Clawson and
her Counselors. Several of the wards were
unrepresented, but of the Associations that
were represented there was a very good show-
ing of work. It was utterly impossible to
observe all the nice things even, but among
those that we did notice were of boy’s work,
Johnie H Boberg, Draper 9 years old,ladder,
rake and boot jack, Willie Turner South
Cotton Wood 13 years old, a puzzle rattle
cut in wood, also Geo. C. Brown, of the same
place, ladder and rake. Earl Cummings 13th
Ward this City, 3 brackets, Josiah Midgley 7th
Ward,13, tin bucket, Charlie Spencer 7 years
old, crochet covered ball, Stanley Partridge


[Column 1]

aged 15 years, doll settee and chairs cut wood-
work; Charles Madson 4th Ward, 13 years
old, small lounge made and upholstered; stone
work carving of letters, by Eddie aged 11 and
Elias Ashton aged 10, 15th Ward, Ray Jacobs
17th Ward 10 years old, paper weight and
penholders cut from cigar box and gilded very
tasteful; hearth rugs and woolen patchwork
by Ralph Grames 3rd Ward, aged 9 years,
bedstead for doll, Geo. J. Fox 14th Ward aged
13, a very complete miniature pile driver by
Johnie Bowers 7th Ward, he also melted the
lead and shaped it. Brackets and scroll work
Walter Wallace 7th Ward, 13 years old, Ray
Williams Mill Creek, double harrow 12 years
old, Forestdale in miniature by Alma
Bullock of the 16th Ward, 10 years old was
the most attractive piece of work made by any
of the little boys. It was perfect in ap-
pearance though very small, streets laid out,
shade trees, houses exact even to the sign and
advertising, the proportions were all good it
was made principally of pasteboard and painted
in imitation of Forestdale itself. It was quite
a novel idea. Lee Richards of the 20th ward
had a very handsome satin banner on exhibi-
tion, ornamental painting and letter-
ing, certainly very creditably done and
artistically finished off for a little boy. showing
considerable talent in this direction.
	The work of the little girls was in very
great variety and it is impossible to more than
give a passing notice to a few of the most
uoticable. One quite attractive little thing
was a large bunch of paper snowballs hanging
pendant so true to nature that several of the
visitors thought that they were real, these
were made by Chloe Hunter aged 9 years,
water lilies in waxwork, Annie Gundson aged
12 years, Ardelle Cummings aged 12, had
several specimens of drawn needle work that
were very pretty and quite creditable for a
child. Sophia Youngberg, Draper, aged 14
had some very fine plain hand sewing, work
on ribbon done in arasine very handsome and
fine work, Phronia Nelson Draper. aged 11
years, Gertrude Winder aged 13, handsome
lamp-mat made of ribbon fastened with cord
of chenille, May Gilbert banjo and mandolin
silk work, covered pasteboard, there were
very odd and quite pretty. Rhoda Miller,
Mill Creek, aged 10, babies bibs worked neatly,
Edna Jackson paper platting very nice aged
7, Olive Cushing 11th ward, 7 years old
woolen scarf crocheted. Louie Coulam 11th
ward 9 years old stocking darning very neat.
Sophia Youngberg aged 14, yarn' home spin-
ning, very fine, Evelina Youngberg 7 years old,
very fine darning. Fannie Coulam 11th ward
12 years, old cut fitted and made a dress waist
sewing very good, button holes well made.
Hannah Thatcher 9 years old, cut fitted and
made in goodstyle a doll's dress. Pearl Carlisle,
Mill Creek, 15 years old, large, heavy table cover
embroidered on felt in chenille, arasine,
riberdine. very elaborate in design and quite
elegant as a whole, showing talent, patience,
and neatness. Birdie Carlisle younger sister,
a table scarf handsomely embroidered. Belle
Weiler 4th ward, 13 years old a pretty apron
made by hand trimmed with novelty lace and
insertion done very neatly. Free hand draw-
ing by Alice Clawson 14 years old, these
copies and drawings showed considerable
talent; cross stitching on gingham by little
girls six and seven years old, horse shoe
watch case, covered and filled in with silk
filled and wound round with ribbon. This
was a very attractive article and as it hung
attracted considerable attention. Laura Jones
16th ward, large sofa cushion of silk, pieced
log-cabin pattern. Janette Pitt aged 9 years,
carriage robe, made of woolen pieces in form
of rosettes raised and worked in colors. Ella
Keyting 17 years old elegant silk banner,
ornamental, delicately painted and mounted.

[Column 2]

Table cover from the 17th ward Primary
intended for the Logan Temple, embroidered
in the four corners. Louie Groesbeck 12,
corner marguerites, Cathie Dougall sun-
flowers, Geneva Ellerbeck, poppies; Stella
Jacobs, fuchias, Alice Taylor 17th ward,
10 years old darning, mending, marking,
tucking, in fact of all kinds of needle work,
very good indeed. Ray Grant 13th ward,
handsome handkerchief case made when
eleven years old. Edith Dinwoody 7th ward,
aged 14, painting on China very well executed.
Ray Chamberlain 8th ward, 6 years old,
paper cutting kindergarten work very pretty.
Bessie Frost 8th ward, 13 years old, apron
hand sewing, neatly done, crochet trimming
fine and even. Eva Sheets 8th ward, 14
years old a handsome bunch of artificial
roses. Minnie Oakey, 8th ward, aged 12, 3
owls on a thorny branch very natural attracted
a great deal of notice.
	Bessie Binder [--]th ward, 13 years old, a
child's white dre[--] hand sewing with 23 tucks
all even and ne[--] stitches. Clara Hanson 7
years old croche[-] work: cream colored scrim
apron and croche very odd and pretty, pillow
shams worked in birds and snnflowers crochet
lace very deep, edged with colored floss to
correspond with the embroidery, sofa pillow
and head rest, embroidered with chenille and
arasine, in moss roses and pansies several
specimens of penmanship, and dozens and
scores of other things too numerous to mention.
Machine embroidery a swan in the water
among the rushes was quite pretty; this was
by Lutie Ashton 9 years old. There were
only two essays notwithstanding the call made
through the papers and the offer of a prize.
	There were some very well executed paint-
ings in oil by Kate V. Moris 15 years old, 15th
ward. The best was a copy of Conway
Castle in North Wales; Scene in the Yellow
stone Park painted when she was only 13
years old; Council Bluffs a copy from one of
Fred Pierce's paintings taken when she was
14. Two plaques painted in flowers, one of
roses, leaves just falling. and one of poppies;
also a larga piano cover painted quite
elaborately.
	In addition to these there were many other
things well deserving mention if our space
would permit, besides cooking of an excellent
quality and in great variety. The Fair
was a success in every respect and the children
deserve great credit.
			E. B. W.


WOMAN'S EXPONENT


Mrs. Martha D. Lincoln, of Washington
D. C., better known as "Bessie Beech," be-
gan her literary work at sixteen, contribut-
ing to the Morning Star of Dover for several
years. Since then, she has been a contributor,
correspondent and reporter for many papers.
For several years past she has been engaged
on "Central Figures in Science," a book now
nearly finished. If she ever makes a collec-
tion of her "Beech Leaves,"they will cover al-
most every event of prominence in social life
at the capital for twenty years after the war.

	An effort will be made by influential
women in New York to send one of their
number to sit in court whenever a woman is
tried, in order to give her the protection that
women so much need under such circum-
stances. An official who was consulted said,
"But, ladies, you will hear much that will be
unfit for ladies to hear." After attending a
trial, one of the ladies said to him, "We
heard nothing unpleasant." "Oh," he said,
"of course not--when ladies are present." If
the presence of good women would purify the
atmosphere of our courts, it is a strong argu-
ment for having some motherly woman present
whenever an unprotected girl is to be tried.


', 'PRIMARY FAIR.
	The Children's Fair was open for four days,
commencing on Wednesday, the 12th inst., and
continuing until the end of the week. There was
quite a creditable representation made of the little
people's work. As it is utterly impossible "to tell
it all" we shall not make the attempt, but only
particularize some of the very best articles on ex-
hibition.
	Of the country wards we noticed Big Cotton-
wood, Mill Creek, Draper and North Jordan had

[Column 2]
a variety of useful and ornamental articles, nicely
arranged on the tables. From Big Cottonwood
there was a hammer and chisel in iron work.
made by little boys, and so well done, that no one
could help admiring the workmanship; also
bread and pies by little boys and very
fine potatoes and other vegetables, raised
by them. A map of the United States, drawn and
colored by a little girl, showed very good
ability; there was also, from quite small boys of 
that ward, a ladder and rake, and from a girl old-
fashioned heavy woolen gloves, hooked, patchwork
quilts, plain sewing, ornamental needlework, and
many pretty things beside.
	Draper Ward, there was a very well made and
perfect shoe made by a boy; also vegetables
raised by boys, and cooking done by them; from
one of the girls a piece of home-made carpet, well
woven; the same little girl can put a piece into the
loom herself.
	North Jordan Ward had wax flowers, some
other handsome work, but the most unique article
was a bridle made of cord and horsehair, by a
little fellow who had also made the machine in
which it was manufactured; the bridle is very
strong and quite a curiosity.
	Mill Creek Ward, velvet pincushion worked in
the finest silk, very elaborately, and several other
articles of value. 
	Third Ward, Salt Lake City, had so many very
good things on their table, that it was rather
difficult to select what was most appropriate to
mention. In patchwork there was some over and
over hand sewing by a little girl four years old;
that child deserves to be praised; comb case cut
in wood work by a little boy, horseshoe covered
with velvet, edges corded, very neatly done, for
ornament, knitted tidy, made in flowers, very
handsome throw downs, in beautiful colors;
a patchwork quilt cover, neatly made, each seam
carefully pressed, the work all done by a small
boy; some drawings in very good taste by a boy
of the same ward.
	Of the city wards, there were many useful and
ornamental articles, made by little girls and boys,
all of them quite young, ranging in age from four
to fourteen, but as it is quite impossible to particu-
larize, we can only give a few, and say there were
many more just as good.
	Second Ward, very odd and handsome white
apron, elaborate crochet inserting and edging,
little girl.
	Fourth Ward, a cup and saucer in crochet work
by a little girl, that attracted the attention of all
who came in; flower painting, by a little girl from
the same ward, well done; also underclothing in
plain hand sewing.
	Sixth Ward, home-knit stockings, little girl;
card receiver in stars, made of thread on cut
pasteboard and bound, bows of ribbon; ribbon
work, banner worked on velvet, and other articles.
	Eighth Ward, a handkerchief bag made of a
towel, very pretty, by a little girl; sun-flowers,
large sized ones, so perfectly natural that any one
might pick them up thinking they were real; also
pretty wax flowers, and many other things.
	Tenth Ward made a good representotion of
various articles; fruit very good, and a handsome
quilt.
	Thirteenth Ward had an Essay on the First
Principles of the Gospel, written by a little girl
which deserves special mention, as to correctness
of style and expression; also from that ward a
very handsome ornamental banner, silk and tinsel
work, and a child's apron in colors, done in etch-
ing, and a handkerchief and reticule in novelty
lace.
	Fourteenth Ward had some hand sewing on
underclothing, very neatly done, and child's dress
made by hand, tucking, embroidery and button
holes, all very good indeed; wool flowers, hand-
somely framed, and very unique yellow and scarlet
tidy, crochet work; also daisy mats and crochet
lace.

[Column 3]
	Fifteenth Ward, there was quite a number of
useful and ornamental articles; there were the two
small trunks made by quite young boys; they were
examined carefully, and pronounced excellent
work; some very neat stocking darning by little
girls; very pretty and showy crazy quilts.
	Sixteenth Ward, an engine complete on wheels
made by a little boy (U. N. Narrow Guage); also a
wooden sled, by a boy, and a wheelbarrow from
another.
	Seventeenth Ward, a handsome reticule, made
of a silk handkerchief; crochet skirt, handsome
tidy, velvet and ribbon tidies, aprons, etc.; doll's
bed, made and trimmed, spread, shams and the
ruffles on the sham sheet composed of 72 scraps of
embroidery, neatly and regularly put together, by
a little girl; this was one of the curiosities. 
	Eighteenth Ward, some pencil sketches, by a
very little girl; also ornamental snowball, made of
white paper.
	Twenty-first Ward made quite a display. A
little wooden cradle, by a boy, was quite well
made; also a wooden knife box, made and painted;
there was very nice hand sewing by the little
girls, small dresses for children, made by hand,
pillow slips, etc. Of tidies, quilts, stockings,
cookery, preserves, and many odd things, there
was a great variety, and all very good.
	Certainly a great deal has been accomplished
by the children through the agency of the Prim-
aries, and the perseverance, patience and untiring
energy of the sisters who have had charge of
these helpful associations. They most assuredly
deserve the praise and the encouragement of all
interested in the rising generation. Theirs is a
great and noble work, and they should have all the
aid and assistance needful to help them in their
arduous labors.


']

Name: 39_new_york_new york_states
Representative_Docs:
['﻿EDITORIAL NOTES.
			     ——
	Heavy floods in Cambridge and vicinity,
England.
	Lady notaries public are the latest
novelty in official life. Terre Haute, Indi-
ana, has got one.
	Murderers and convicts, who have
escaped the penalty of offended laws, con-
tinue to court notoriety, now, as lecturers.
	A singular death occurred in Vernon,
Iowa, a few days ago. A child fell and hurt
its gums, when bleeding commenced at the
place injured, and the child died of it.
	A religion without bigotry, which in-
culcates love, truth and mercy, and whose
believers practice these virtues, is sadly
needed throughout the Eastern States.
	People find it frequently more conve-
nient to see and act wrong than right,
because, while there are numerous wrong
ways there is but one right way to look at
or perform anything.
	A first class mathematician might find
considerable of a problem, and spend some
time in recconing the number and cost of
the disasterous fires which have occurred
within the United States, since the Boston
conflagration.
	A hint to young ladies who design enter-
ing into the house-keeping business might
be gleaned from the example of a wedding
which lately took place, at whcih each guest
paid 25 cents admittance, which was handed
over to the bride as a little start in the
way of “setting up shop.”
	Among the last sayings of Horace
Greeley was “The country is gone; the
Tribune is gone; and I am gone!” While
his last words were: “I know that my Re-
deemer liveth!” These will do to remember
and jot down when making memoranda of
the dying words of eminent men.
	The British Embassador at St. Peters-
burg is reported to have warned Prince
Gortchakoff that if Russian-troops enter the
countries between Khiva and Afghanistan,
England will intervene in support of Afghan-
istan. If this statement be true, a quarrel
between England and Russia, may next be
apprehended.
	ONE OF the questions agitating the country
just now is, whether the people of Louisiana
will control the affairs of that State; or
whether they will be governed by a set of
“carpet-baggers” who have no special inter-
est in the State except to rob it.  The
question is important enough for considera-
tion.
	In the new public school building, No. 5,
Jersey City, New York, which was partially
destroyed by fire on the morning of the 23
of December, were about one hundred and
fifty young ladies, all of whom escaped un-
harmed; and likewise members of the Board
of Education which was in session at the
time. With such good fortune, the loss of
$60,000 need not be complained of.
	A little over a week ago two compositors
skated down the Missouri river from Sioux
City to Omaha, over two hunnred miles;
being some two days making the trip;
[new colum]
which was more fool-hardy than sensible.
Frozen ears for both, with a frozen cheek and
heel for one of them, were the result. If
there is any daring, but foolish thing to be
done, American boys are ready to undertake
it.
	A great curiosity in the form of a stone
has been found by Mr. A. M. Stewart, a
locator of the gravel deposits on the summit
of the Sierra Nevada mountains, in Califor-
nia. This stone is said to be in shape like a
magnifying, glass thick in the centre, and so
hard that the hardest of files tried upon it
produces not even a scratch. Viewed through
a powerful magnifying glass, it shows the
most wonderful rays of light. The owner
believes it to be a rare gem, unknown to
the lapidaries.
	Some time ago, “Woodhull & Claflin’s
Weekly” charged Mrs, Theodore Tilton
with marital infidelity, and Rev. Henry
Ward Beecher as the other guilty party in
the alleged criminality. Mr. Beecher paid
no attention to the charge, nor did Mr. Tilton
up to the present. Now, it is said, he will
publish a denial, although it comes at a
very late hour. Evidently New York
“Society" must have some very hideous-
looking “skeletons” in its cupboards, if they
were exposed to view.
	Among the most terrible disasters of late
occurrence, is recorded the railroad accident
near Carry, Pennsylvania, on the afternoon
of December 24th by which the train, con-
sisting of one passenger car and one baggage
car, the engine and tender, was thrown off
the track; falling a distance of over twenty
feet; the cars were turned directly bottomside
upward, caught fire, and through the fearful
crushing and burning, some twenty souls
were immediately ushered into eternity and
about as many more were dreadfully injured.
But one person out of forty-six who were
on the train, is known to have escaped un-
harmed.
	About three hundred men, women
and children were assembled in the recep-
tion room, up stairs, of the Newberry Baptist
Church, Pennsylvania, on Christmas day,
having a Sunday School Christmas Celebra-
tion; when the floor gave way and the mass
of human beings were precipitated to the
floor beneath. Fourteen were killed out-
right and a large number, more or less
injured. This is one of the most heartrend-
ing occurrences reported of the late holiday;
and should give a text to the bigoted of
other denominations, who forget what the
Savior said concerning the falling of the
tower of Siloam.	
	Train, whose given name is George
Francis, has not reached the White House,
but he has the inside of a New York prison,
charged with publishing an obscene periodi-
cal. But it was not the obscenity in Train’s
publication that roused virtuous New York,
where the most degradingly disgraceful
publications of the age are circulated by
wholesale and are on the stand of almost
every newsvender. It was because he pub-
lished statements concerning prominent
New Yorkers which were hard to meet and
yet could not be passed over in silence. If
New York will rid itself of obscene publica-
[new column]
tions, professional murderers who kill life
ere it has passed from its embryotic condi-
tion, and of its public destroyers of chastity
and innocence, it will be in a better condition
to pronounce judgment upon Train and his
publications than it is at present.
	The Christmas season just passed was
prolific of horrors in the United States.
Destructive fires occurred in many parts of
the country; the upper floor of a Pennsylvania
Baptist church fell in, killing fourteen
persons and injuring nearly fifty more, a
number of them severely; a number of rail-
road accidents occurred, by one of which
some twenty persons were literally roasted
alive through the burning of an overturned
car on which they were, and in addition to
these and other horrors, one of the most
violent snow-storms, accompanied with in-
tense cold, swept over the Eastern, Middle
and Southern States, that has been known
for seventeen years. These are but signs of
the times on which the wise will ponder;
while the unbelieving and the foolish will
not heed the warnings given of an approach-
ing consummation in fulfillment of prophecy.
	The Credit Mobilier is exciting consider-
able interest at present. Speaker Blaine
demanded an investigation into the
charges made against a number of
the members and ex-members of Congress—
Senators and Representatives; the principal
charge being that certain members, Blaine
among the number, were bribed in 1864 by
the Union Pacific Railroad Company’s Credit
Mobilier, to support the measure of that
corporation and vote for the amendments of
1862, to increase the land grant and ex-
change the Government’s first mortgage
on the road for a second one, the first being
allowed to the company. The Chicago
“Evening Post” has the following hit con-
cerning the affair: “Enough has been devel-
oped in the Credit Mobilier examination
to make it desirable to develop more. We
trust the full-sized cat will be let out of the
bag, no matter what Senator she bites, or
what member she scratches, or what editor
she spits upon. So interesting an animal
cannot get her back up too soon.”
	Some time ago Professor Tyndall pro-
posed to test the efficacy of prayer as a
healing medium, by having prayer offered
up in certain wards of an hospital, while
other wards were to be left prayerless; and
if the patients in the wards where prayer
was offered would be restored to health more
quickly than those in the other wards, then
prayer was to be accepted as efficacious in
the healing of dseases. It is needless to
say that Professor Tyndall is an infidel. A
conference of Methodist preachers consid-
ered his proposition, and after some discus-
sion decided that it was blasphemous. Now,
as the Methodist preachers in question,
with the entire denomination, and the
Christian sects generally, repudiate the gift
of healing, or the scriptural mode of healing
the sick by faith and prayer, the question
is an open one, wherein they differ from or
are better than Professor Tyndall? And
whether is the infidelity of the Professor or
the preachers worse, seeing the former
avows his unbelief in the Bible and the
latter profess belief in it?


WOMAN’S EXPONENT.


', "EDITORIAL NOTES.
				—
	ln Burlington, Iowa, the street lights
are extinguished at 10 o'clock. It is pre-
sumable that the people there are temperate
and moral.
	After the horses, first came the
chickens and then the hogs, as sufferers by
the epidemic among the animals first named.
Will man come next?
	The fair famed English poetess, Eliza
Cook, is reported ill beyond recovery. Does
she still cling to the “Old Armed Chair” of
which she has so sweetly sung?
	From present indications the coming
winter will be a hard one. Some time ago
work on the Nothern Pacific R. R,. had to be
suspended in consequnce of the severity of
the weather.
	Pioche is anxious for the railroad track
to be extended form the terminus of the
Utah Southern to Bullionville, that the
coal mines of Kanarra may be bound to the
silver mines of Pioche
	A Pennsylvania woman lately sold her
two weeks’ old baby for $10, but wanted it
back soon after, because another party had
offered $25 for it. Some people have
curious ways of making money.
	The last present ever received by Abra-
ham Lincoln, an elegant family Bible given
him by the colored people of the South, has
just been presented by Mrs. Lincoln to Rev.
Dr. Miner of Belvedere, Illinois.
	Fires and storms on land and sea, inun-
dations and general devastation, reports
of which daily crowd the telepraphic columns
of the newspapers, seem indicative of a
universal crisis in some direction, at no
distant period.
	A friend announces that the whole world
is apparently on the point of committing
suicide, but what with its present intemper-
ance, insubordination, insolvency and gen-
eral inconsistencies it might philosophically
be considered as being already in the very
act.
	In Marseilles, France, a jury recently
acquitted a beautiful young brunette for the
crime of assassinating her lover for refusing
to purchase a gold watch to which she took
a fancy. Another decided case ol insanity;
for if the murderess was “compos mentis”
the jury certainly not.
	Boston’s relief fund, for the sufferers
by its great fire, has reached about two
hundred thousand dollars. Wealth has not
made the Bostonian heart altogether hard,
nor frozen up its fountains of charity; or
else the great conflagration has softened
the one and thawed out the other.
	Scotland seems to be highly favored
with visits from distinguished people this
year. The Queen and the Prince and
Princess of Wales are there; The French
Empress and the Prince Imperial but lately
left; Gladstone and John Bright have gone
there; Stanley has been there; and Lowell
is somewhere in that part of the Globe.
	Cooper told of the “last of the Mohicans”
years ago, but it appears that about 100 of
the tribe still survive and have been in-
vested with the rights of citizenship by the
[new column]
Legislature of Connecticut; and that they
are as industrious and well informed as any
of their white neighbors. America may
yet be proud of her aborigines.
	Senator Sumner has returned from
Europe, and on Wednesday last he met
Senator Henry Wilson, the newly elected
Vice-President, in the office of the New
York “Tribune,” the two greeting each
other cordially. Thus the political hatchet,
it appears, is being buried by the “Tri-
bune,” and the rival factions of Republicans.
	Mrs. Greeley, formerly Miss Mary G.
Cheney, whose death lately occurred at the
residence of Alvin Johnson, in New York,
is said to have been a lady of highly culti-
vated intellect, taste and social attainments.
True sympathizers will condole more with
the old Chappaqua Farmer on her loss than
upon any political disappointment which he
has lately sustained.
	Diamonds and other precious stones are
becoming so plenty, or so plentifully talked
of, in this western region, that we do not
wonder at mothers when referring to their
babies, speaking of eyes like diamonds, and
lips like rubies. It is to be feared that some
of the investors in diamond expeditions,
may have reason to look blue as sapphires
before their experience is ended.
	A political crisis in France, more in-
surrection in Spain, Rome threatened with
radical uprisings, trouble in Prussia between
the Emperor and the national Diet; with
every leading nation, including our own,
talking of strengthening its powers of offense
and defense. These are the evidences of
“peace” which the civilized world presents,
and of which there has lately been so much
vaunting.
	On Wednesday, Nov. 20, Boston sufferd
loss by another large fire, although it was
small compared with the great conflagration
which so short a time before had preceded
it. The total loss reached nearly $350,000;
and the large printing establishment of
Rand & Avery was destroyed, with the
December number of “Old and New,” one
issue of “Littell’s Living Age” and other
periodicals.
	A Sensational New York paper reported
recently that Greeley was insane, and the
story was telegraphed over the country. If
Greeley is crazy, who in New York is wise?
He suffered deeply in feelings and endured
much loss of sleep during the last illness
and death of his beloved wife—prostrating
him for a time—but he is likely to live and
prove himself much more sane than those
who gave currency to such a tale.
	The Woodhull and Claflin sisters have
been saying and publishing fearful things
about Henry Ward Beecher, Mrs. Theodore
Tilton, and New York “society” generally.
They were arrested, lodged in jail on the
charge of publishing “obscene literature,”
and also held on a charge of libel against one
Challis, a New York banker or broker. If
one tenth part of what they charge—and
they defend much of it—be true, then the
corruption of “Gotham” is something terri-
ble to think of.
	Miss Kate Janaway is the daughter of
an ex-Mayor of Memphis, Tennessee, but
she has learned that woman’s accomplish-
[new column]
ments should include more than a little
piano and a large amount of fashion and
novel reading. She caried off premiums at
the late Agricultural Fair for roast beef,
plum pudding, and her style of preparing a
variety of vegetables; enough to give her
the claim to be considered the best cook in
Tennessee. And she received twenty-five
offers of marriage on the spot! How much of
romance, and how much of prosaic Common
Sense were in those offers?
	Henry Berge, “the guardian angel and
the patron saint of the bestial race,” stand-
ing at the junction of Third and Fourth
avenues, at the Cooper Institute, New York,
during the horse distemper, displayed his
professional shield, and in the name of the
august Society for the Prevention of Cruelty
to Animals, ordered men to cease driving
their gasping horses. And being reinforced
by the police he carried the day. Good for
Henry. But what kind of beings must
they have been who had to be compelled to
unhiteh their sick and panting beasts?
	We clip the following from the Wash-
ington “Star” of November 20, where it
appeared under the heading “Hero and
Heroine,” without further comment: “Syl-
vester Frambes, of Tippecanoe, Spencer
county, Ind., was drawn into a sugar crusher
and had his arm crushed to tbe shoulder one
day last week. His wife came to his assist-
ance, and when she had released him by
backing the horse she fainted away. With
great nerve and endurance he carried her to
the house on his uninjured arm, and re-
stored her to consciousness, when she went
for a surgeon, who disjointed the arm at
the shoulder. He will probably recover.”
	Vermont does not believe that capital
punishment is “legal murder,” as some “ad-
vanced minds” term it. The Legislature
of that State, by a large majority, has re-
fused to abolish the death penality. That
whiich is needed to repress alarming and
heinous crimes throughout the country, is
not abolishing of capital punishment, nor
the passage of severer statutes; but the due
honoring of wise and just statutes and their
rigid enforcement. But while men can kill
with impunity, and gloat over their deeds
of blood, and can be rescued from the
clutches of the law,unharmed and un-
punished, by influence or money, what will
bad men care whether such a penalty
remains upon the statute books of States or
is formally abolished.
	Another paragraph on this page refers
to the story that Horace Greeley had gone
insane, refusing to credit it, and looking
forward to a length of life for him. Later
reports confirm the belief in his sanity, but
destroy the hope of his prolonged life. The
great editor is gone. He died on Friday
evening, 29th ult., at ten minutes to seven
o’clock, nearly sixty-two years of age. The
cause of his death was virtually sleepless-
ness. He could not sleep, after the exertion
and exhaustion consequent upon the death
of his wife, and the strain upon him during
the Presidential campaign; and in about four
weeks after she was carried to the grave,
his spirit passed from earth, the sleepless-
ness having turned to inflamation of the
brain. And so another great and wise man
has fallen!


WOMAN’S EXPONENT.


﻿	Written for the Exponent.
", "CURRENT EVENTS.
(Read at the U. W. P. C. May 31, 1900.)
Madame Chairman and Ladies:
	In order to get over the worst part first, I
will commence this paper with the awful
calamity at Scofield, by which a large num-
ber of women and children were
left homeless and helpless. A calamity like
this is unparalleled in the United States.
	On the morning of May 1st over two hun-
dred men went to work as usual in the
mine. In a few hours their mangled re-
mains were being brought to the surface. A
large proportion of these were heads of
families. My sympathy went out to the
poor Finn women, who could speak no
English, and could only sit and moan.
Imagine ourselves where we could not
speak the language, and our loved ones
buried in a debris of fallen earth and tim-
ber.
	The disastrous results of this accident
have only been exceeded by one, which oc-
curred in Wales some years ago, in which
five hundred were killed in a mine explo-
sion. The largest one in the United
States, previous to the Scofield accident, oc-
curred in Pennsylvania, in which over one
hundred men lost their lives.
	We should be proud of the citizens of
Utah, and also citizens of other states, who
responded so nobly to the call for finance.
It is well, for we know not the hour that
we may need help ourselves.
	It is not war alone that makes heavy
drafts upon the courage and happiness of
women, since in many of the hazardous
vocations “Men must work and women
must weep.” The work of the world calls

[Column 2]
for sacrifices every hour. Every day lives
are snuffed out, and every day the world is
engaged in burying its dead. After the
funeral, the work has to be picked up and
carried on, so it is an endless chain, and life
itself is but a long funeral procession, until
it is closed at the grave.
	Almost heart-rending reports come from
India, bringing news of the pitiful condi-
tion of the country more than ever to the
public view. Cholera is gathering in its
deadly hold ninety millions of people, who
are living in the midst of pestilence and
misery.
	The government is exerting itself to
meet the emergency, but not more than five
million can be relieved. The roads are
crowded with ghastly bands seeking to es-
cape from stricken territories, but for lack
of food and water succumb in the attempt,
and the roads are dotted with heaps of dead
and dying. In some parts of India the
bodies lie exposed to the sun, as the inhabi-
tants are dying more rapidly than the sur-
vivors can provide for the dead.
	An appeal from London says that the
United States ought to at least contribute a
million of dollars toward a relief fund. An-
other from Calcutta says that owing to the
fact that the English are engaged in the
somewhat expensive task of reducing the
South African Republic to the same Colon-
ial vassalage, the government has not been
able to do as much for the succor of the
people of India as it might under other
circumstances.
	Every American sympathizes with these
sufferers in far off Asia. The deplorable
condition produced by crop failure has been
intensified by the financial system enforced
on the people of the unhappy land by the
London bankers. No doubt the United
States would raise the one million, without
much trouble, but while we are sending
money into this British colony, the British
are sending money out to aid in the spoila-
tion in other lands.
	The Philadelphia Ledger has this to say
about England.
	She is sending millions out in carrying out an
aggressive and unjust war against South Africa,
while neglecting the manifest duties towards sub
jects who being under paternal sway, should be
equally the subjects of paternal solicitude. If
England can afford to spend so much money for
the taking of the lives of the Boers, she should be
able to spend a tithe of the sum to save the live
of her starving people in India. If England in-
sists upon spreading death and desolation in South
Africa and squandering her millions in this way,
what is left for humanity to do, but to feed and
care for her starving subjects and colonists in
Asia?
	The Paris Exposition of 1900 was formal-
ly opened on April 14, M. Milerand, the
Socialist Minister of Commerce, was chosen
to deliver the inaugural address. Like all
expositions the present one was opened
some weeks before the buildings were
ready. The number of American exhibi-
tors is second only to those of France, and
far ahead of all other competitors. Where
England has only one thousand, Germany
three thousand, the United States has seven
thousand exhibitors.
	The American section occupies three
hundred and twenty-nine thousand, and
fifty-two square feet, with forty-seven dis-
tinct exhibition spaces. There is every
reason to believe that the exhibition will
have good effect beyond its commercial suc-
cess.
	After three years of almost incessant tur-
moil, it need hardly be said that the Fre


(Column 1)
people have put forth every effort to make
the Exposition successful. The number of
French exhibits reach thirty thousand,
twelve thousand more than all foreign
competitors put together. The grounds
have been laid and the buildings erected in
accordance with the French taste which is
so universally admired.
	Germany has shown particular friendli-
ness, and the Russian section was opened
with special ceremonies, the occasion being
the presentation of a personal gift from the
Czar to France.
	The Queen of Saxony visited the Exposi-
tion, and set an example that will probably
be followed by all the Royalty of Europe.
	One of the most delightful places to live
in is the little village of Ætenburg, Europe.
It is ruled by no monarch, has no soldiers,
no police and no taxes. It is where four
countries meet, but belongs to none.
	The railway system in the Phillipine
Islands consists of a single line of antiquated
cars running over a road of less than 125
miles. To give us an idea of the form of
travel and transportation under the long
Spanish regime: This road connects Manil-
la with Dagupan, a town on the east coast
of the Island of Luzon. The gauge of the
road is three feet six inches, the ties of the
finest hardwood, the locomotives are insig-
nificant. The speed averages less than fif-
teen miles an hour. When the American
troops took possession of the Island, their
railroading astonished the natives, the speed
being frequently doubled. Compartment
coaches are used, each apartment seating
eight people. The first class are provided
with cane chairs: the second class, rough
wooden benches; the third class, entirely
bare and are usually crowded with natives
carrying baskets and bundles of all de-
scriptions.
	The recent visit of Queen Victoria to the
Emerald Isles was an occasion of rejoicing
and the heartiest welcome possible. All
accounts agree that as a mere matter of
artistic display, the Queen’s reception sur-
passed the Jubilee celebration of 1897. And
far more remarkable was the irrepressible
delight of the Irish people at having the
Queen again among them, after an absence
of nearly forty years. The visit was
undertaken in recognition of the bravery
of the Irish soldiers in South Africa, and no
attempt was made on either side to give it a
political coloring.
	On the 24th of this mouth was the 81st
birthday of this noble queen. For sixty-
five years she has dignified the English
throne with the noblest attributes of en-
lightened womanhood. As wife, mother
and monarch she has presented an example
of honor, virtue, piety and patience which
has served as a constant inspiration to her
subjects. The world has seen few such
queenly women, never a more womanly
queen.
	Alaska promises to be of more service in
other ways than producing gold. A curi-
ous and interesting part is the effort to save
our wild animals from extinction. Thirty-
five small, bare Alaskan islands are occupied
by fox farmers who are breeding wild foxes.
They pay the United States $100 a year for
these islands. The blue fox has been in
line of extinction with the buffalo, wild
pigeon and the ruffed grouse. The fox
farmers pay from $150.00 to $200.00 per
pair for breeding animals, and mnst wait a
dozen years for profit, then the profits are
large.	The animals are fed on unmarket-

(Column 2)
able fish, blubbers and cornmeal. Each
island is in charge of a keeper and two or
three native assistants. The Island of
Kadiak has 1275 foxes bred by an enter-
prising Long Islander, who believes that
this will lead to the domestication of many
other fur bearing animals.
	The Supreme Court decision just handed
down in the Kentucky gubernatorial con-
test puts an end to a disgraceful controversy.
In Kentucky for over a century the ques-
tion of disputed governorship has been one
for the legislative assembly to decide. 	It
did decide who was Governor and there
the case should have rested. 
	The present season has witnessed an epi-
demic in strikes, unlike anything the coun-
try has seen for years. 	Chicago gets her
share of strikes especially among the
machinists. 	A short time ago three thou-
sand machinists were out in Chicago, and
two thousand more in Cleveland and Colum-
bus, Ohio.
	The unrest in the trade threatened the
suspension of work among the Union
machinists all over the country. In Chicago
the violence which the strikers have resorted
to has not been beyond the power of the
officials to control. In New York the
militia have been called upon to preserve
order.
	Another strike a few days ago, in St.
Louis produced blood shed and death.	A
street car strike in Kansas City is also re-
corded. The car strikes are disastrous to
people living miles out of town, and who
are obliged to depend on car service, especi-
ally working people who are liable to lose
positions if not in time for work.
	Japanese emigration continues to excite
the laboring men in this country, and no
wonder. Hundreds of the Orientals are
coming in on every ship, some to take the
place of section hands on railroads and
others to work in factories.	It is reported
that the Japanese are leaving their country
by thousands because of the danger of a
conflict between Japan and Russia. It is
estimated that ten thousand Japs landed on
the Pacific coast last month.  They dis-
embarked in British Columbia and made
their way across the border to get employ.
ment when and where they might.
	The Bubonic plague seems to be a serious
matter. The Chinese are in mortal terror
of innoculation. They gather in mobs and
the Federal authorities are obliged to take a
hand in the disturbances.	The Chinese
highbinders have threatened to assassinate
any Chinese who submit to innoculation
against the plague. There are twenty-three
thousand Chinese in the Chinese quarters
in San Francisco, and they are terrorized at
the prospect of vacination. With the
“Haffkine Brophylactic” most of the
Chinese were at first willing to submit to
the innoculation, until the  highbinders
issued the ultimatum that the whites wanted
to kill off the Chinese and took this method
of doing so.	The plague is a high fever,
indisposition one day, collapse the next and
the following day death. There seems to
be no medical treatment for it but the serum
which is injected into the blood, and in that
way destroys the food of the bacilli and the
fever is abated and recovery assured.	Rats
are a ready victim to the disease and die
in numbers, they are the most dangerous
means of carrying the infection. In the
last dispatches from San Francisco the
board of health had quarantined China-
town, and all that section of the city is sur

(Column 3)
rounded by police who refuse to let any one
pass in or out. The quarantine also in-
cludes street cars, which are not allowed to
pass through the district.
	A session that we have been much inter-
ested in this month is the Mothers’ Con-
gress. The papers and oral arguments
gave subjects for consideration.	An inter-
change of thoughts and ideas is good for us
all.	After the reception of Professor Cris-
mon,s paper at the Mothers’ Congress at
Des Moines, Iowa, I am inclined to think
that the gentleman will not be so free to
offer his valuable information.
	The resolution that was passed at our
Congress to prevent young girls from enter-
ing wine rooms and the sale of cigarettes to
minors, deserves the thanks of every mother
and father in Utah. We have laws here
against these very things and every one
should pay the penalty for disregarding
these laws. If the City Council pays no at-
tention to the appeal of the mothers, then
it is time for them to take a hand in the
municipal affairs of our fair city.
	Last, but not least, in our current events
of this month, is Decoration Day.	This
day is for a tribute of love and honor to
the nation’s dead.	No other government
has a day like this, others have achieved
their independence, at the cost of blood,
but they have never thought of setting
apart a day for the decoration of the graves
of the dead soldiers.
	In Utah and in other states new graves
have been made in the last two years. The
brave lads who heard the call for soldiers
sprang to arms in response. Some of these
were boys who but a little while ago were
in our public schools, and when they re-
turned to us were hidden away in their final
homes and were given back dust to dust.
Many a mound which covered the crumb-
ling dust of heroes was decorated yesterday
with flowers and wreaths, symbols of a sor-
row with which we would not part; tributes
of a love that lives while memory lasts.
				LUELLA RHODES.


6          WOMAN'S EXPONENT.


"]

['LATTER-DAY SAINTS' COLLEGE.
INTERESTING COMMENCEMENT EXERCISES.
	At the Social Hall, beginning at 11 a. m., the
commencement exercises of the fourth academic
year of the Latter-day Saints' College were held.
There were present on the stand: Of the General
Board of Education, President Wilford Woodruff
and Secretary George Reynolds; of the Stake
Board, Secretary Wm. B. Dougall. Bishop Wm.
T. Reid of Manti, Don C. Young, C. E., Mrs
Zina D. Young, Mrs. Zina Y. Card, and a goodly
number of visitors.
	The exercises opened with a hymn, "Great God
attend," by the choir.
	Prayer was offered by Instructor Newton
Noyes.
	A vocal duet, "Beautiful Moonlight," was
creditably rendered by Misses Ettie Penrose and
Jennie Smith.
	Brother Nathan Gedge, a student of the Inter-
mediate Department of the college, delivered an
address in behalf of that department.
	Brother Nathan Porter, of the Academic De-
partment, delivered an eloquent address on be-
half of the students of that department.
	An instrumental selection, "Heavenward," was
performed pleasingly by Miss Kate Romney.
	This was followed by an address "Our Lady
Students," by Miss Amy Woolley. The general
sentiment of the lady students of the college is
one of the greatest satisfaction at the opportun-
ities afforded them for their advancement. A
tribute was paid to the spirit to be observed in
the institution, and the thought was expressed
that the education of the ladies cannot be dis-
pensed with.
	Instructor Joseph Nelson spoke upon the sub-
[Column 2]
ject of the "Business Courses," which have been
under his care.
	Instructor Willard Done followed with remarks
bearing upon the course in literature and history,
comprising the studies of English literature,
general history, civil government, and elementary
German, in which branches one student has
passed.
	The Principal followed by reading the names
of the following students who have successfully
passed the course prescribed by the Faculty as
necessary to advance them from the Intermediate
to the Academic Department: Emma Carroll,
Philip Maycock, Emily Hillam, Hattie Symons,
Nora Wheeler, Frank Brown, Jane Parrish, Clare
W. Reid, Nettie Davis and Donnette Smith.
	Certificates were then presented to Frank
Pomeroy and Alva Butler in the business course,
and to Laura Hyde in the course in literature and
history.
	After the rendition of a song, "Only a Rose,"
by Miss Maudie Bowring, the following report
was read by Principal J. E. Talmage:
	PRINCIPAL'S REPORT.
	To the Salt Lake Stake Board of Education, ex-
officio the Board of Directors of the Latter-day
Saints' College:
	Dear Brethren—With feelings of sincere pleas-
ure I respectfully submit to you the following re-
port of the Latter-day Saints' College, for the
second term of the fourth academic year, which
began January 6, 1890, and which ends May 23,
1890.
	THE DEPARTMENTS.
	The Intermediate Department has been con-
ducted in two divisions. Instructor William
Done has had charge of Section A, and Instructor
Newton Noyes Section B. Instructor Willard
Croxall has devoted his attention to classes in
each of these divisions.
	The Academic Department has remained in
charge of Instructor Joseph Nelson.
	In accordance with the announcements through
the annual circular for the fourth academic year,
the studies of this department have been arranged
in courses, with reference to the final graduation
of the students. Six such courses have been
provided, viz.: Physical Science, Natural Science,
Mathematics, Business Courses, Literature and
History, and Phonography.

	The Theological exercises have been conducted
during the term with no essential variation from
the methods previously reported.
	We have been specially favored during the
term with valuable instructions from Elder
George Reynolds, who has conducted the aca-
demic class in Book of Mormon study, twice per
week.
	The lady students have continued in the labors
of the Ladies' Class under the careful direction
of our Lady Superintendent, Mrs. Camilla Cobb.
The class has met in two sections, arranged ac-
cording to the ages of the members. Section A
comprised 87 ladies and has held during the term
21 sessions. Dr. Mattie P. Hughes rendered
very valuable assistance to the organization by
delivering a series of six lectures upon the sub-
ject "Personal Hygiene for Women." Class B
consisted of 14 members all under the age of fif-
teen. Of this division 16 sessions have been
held during the term.
	The Students' Society has been conducted un-
der the presidency of Instructor Done, with Miss
Anna Erickson associated as secretary. Even-
ing meetings have been held at the close of each
week with but few omisions.
	LECTURES.
	Bishop O. F. Whitney, one; Dr. R. B. Pratt,
one; Elder John Nicholson, one; Don C. Young


C. E., two; Elder F. F. Hintze, one; Hon. F. S.
Richards, one; and Elder B. H. Robers, one.

	The buildings at present occupied by the
college afford but very inadequate accommoda-
tions for the departments of the institution.	*	*
	Our acknowledgments are due the railway com-
panies of this region, notably the various
branches of the Union Pacific system, the Denver
& Rio Grande, the Utah Central and the Salt
Lake City Railway for the privileges of reduced
fares which have been extended to our students
under prescribed rules; also to the proprietors
and officials of the various industrial and manu-
facturing establishments in and around the city
for numerous courtesies extended to the students
in visiting such places for study; and to the
publishers of the following periodicals for current
copies of their journals: Deseret Evening News,
Salt Lake Daily Herald, Ogden Standard, Utah
Journal, Richfield Advocate, Southern Idaho, In-
dependent, Juvenile Instructor, WOMAN'S EX-
PONENT, Utah Enquirer, Young Ladies' Journal,
Contributor, and Puck, (the last named being re-
ceived through the courtesy of Margetts'
Brothers.)

', 'HOME AFFAIRS.

	MESSRS. G. M. Ottinger and C. R. Savage
have started for Philadelphia where they pur-
pose remaining a few days as visitors to the
great Centennial Exposition.
	THANKS FOR the "Utah Musical Times,"
number six of the publication: it is the first
we have had the pleasure of perusing. A jour-
nal devoted to music, art and literature ought
certainly to be of great advantage in a com-
munity to encourage refining and elevating
influences. We are pleased with its contents
and wish it success.
	MRS. DR. Ellen Fergurson who is at present
residing in St. George, recently gave a lecture
in that city. Her subject was Rome. We had
the pleasure of seeing Mrs. Ferguson and her
husband when they were in Salt Lake on
their way to St. George. Mrs. Ferguson is a
woman of very pleasing manners and appear-
ance, has traveled extensively, and has had a
superior classic and a thorough medical educa-
tion, with considerable practice.
	HON. GEORGE Q. Cannon delegate to Con-
gress from Utah, with his wife and daughter,
has returned home, arriving Monday evening
Aug. 21st. He has given most complete satisfac-
tion by his labors the past years, to those most
interested in the future welfare of Utah, and
truly merited the good feelings of the people he
has served. We greet him welcome to Utah
and his mountain home.
	THE DESERET University opened as usual at
the University Buildings, Mo[-]day Morning,
Aug. 21st, Dr. J. R. Park, Pri[--]cipal. The
rules and regulations and the system of in-
struction are similar to that of pre[-]ious years.
The teachers substantially the same; the at-
tendance is as large as could be expected so
early in the season. Dr. Park and those en-
gaged with him spare no pains to make the
school in every respect equal to similar insti-
tutions in the East. Dr. Park himself is a
teacher of vast experience, and one who mani-
fests the truest interest in the development
and education of the young.


[Column 1]
	MISS M. E. Cook will open her Graded
School in the Social Hall on the first Monday
in this month, September 4th, assisted by her
former teachers. Miss Cook has taken great
pains and been untiring in her efforts to es-
tablish this system of school here; she has so
far succeeded as to draw forth encomiums of
praise and commendation from persons inter-
ested in the subject of schools and education in
Utah, and from strangers who have visited her
school and taken pains to go through her rooms
and hear recitations. Miss Cook has taught for
many years and is a thorough and energetic
worker in the cause of education in the school
room and elsewhere. Her terms for tuition
will be the same as usual.
	MRS. WELLS: Yesterday I had the pleasure
of an interview with Mrs. Porter; a Silk Rib-
bon weaver.
	Mrs. Porter has just arrived from England
and has brought harness, reeds, shuttles &c.
&c., with a model of her loom. This morning
she goes to Bountiful, Davis Co., where she
expects to locate, and, as early as practicable,
resume her accustomed occupation.
	It is to be hoped that through the assistance
of our energetic sisters in Bountiful and else-
where, we can trim our hats with Ribbons of
Home Manufacture.				E. R. S.
	S. L. City, Aug. 19.
	THE NORMAL Institute closed its regular
session Thursday, Aug. 17th. There has been
a great deal of instruction given during this
term which cannot fail to be an advantage to
those engaged in teaching, and indeed to all.
There was a report from the committee on res-
olutions tendering thanks to Prof. J. R. Park
and others who had assisted him, the rail-
road companies, and all who had favored the
Institute in any way. Prof. Park returned
his thanks and also in behalf of all interested,
and made some most excellent closing remarks
which it would be well for every person to re-
member and profit by. He exhorted the teach-
ers to remember always at all times and in all
places, in the schoolroom and out of the school
that their conduct should be most exemplary.
He told them how closely children watched
the conduct of teachers and those who were
placed over them; consequently how much
evil a bad example might work upon the
minds of those who were too young to discrim-
inate for themselves. He said the future hap-
piness and prosperity of a nation depended up-
on the training and education of the young;
and that teachers could not be too careful and
judicious in the course they pursued, them-
selves; and by example as well as precept in-
stil into the minds of pupils true principles.
	The Institute in its session this year number-
ed 158 members, including some from almost
every county in Utah. These reunions will
serve to give tone and elevation to the system
of education and by bringing together the best
talent of the country promote the advance-
ment of literature and science.
	HON. JOHN Taylor and Hon. J. F. Smith,
with the teachers of the territory, met in the
University Building Sat. Aug. 12th, and
Monday Aug. 14th, to discuss the question of
suitable school books for the territory. It is
well known that some of the superintendents
and teachers of schools have declined accept-
ing some of the schools books adopted by the
convention. The result of the present conven-
tion was the adoption of certain specified reso-
lutions which we give in full below.
	Resolved, That the following resolutions be
submitted by the secretary of this convention
to the honorable superintendent, O. H. Riggs,
of Utah, as the sentiments of the teachers of

[Column 2]
this territory, and that the same should be
published in the leading newspapers of said
territory.
	Whereas, The acts of the late convention
held by the superintendents of schools of the
territory April 6th, 7th and 8th, 1876, for the
purpose of adopting text books in accordance
with section 16 of the school law of Utah, fail-
ed to meet the approval of many of the citi-
zens of said territory, in consequence of which
said act failed from being carried into execu-
tion; and
	Whereas, Said failure has been attended
with much inconvenience to our schools, in
some instances even breaking up classes, thus
placing our teachers in a very embarrassing
position.
	Therefore, it is the voice of this convention
that the Hon. O. H. Riggs call a convention
for the purpose of unravelling the present
difficulty, and if possible bring about a change
before opening our winter schools.
	In offering this resolution this assembly does
not presume to criticize the acts of said con-
vention, but simply from necessity calls for re-
lief, and appeals to those whose right it is to
say what text books we shall have.
			CHARLES WRIGHT, Chairman.
	ARTHUR PARSONS, Sec:
	WE HAVE had the pleasure of a call from
Elders' M. B. Young and M. H. Hardy who
have been traveling for some months past,
most of the time through the settlements in
the interest of the Young Men's Mutual Im-
provement Associations. They visited most of
the settlements South as far as St. George, and
organized in all the towns where it was deem-
ed practicable; they express themselves as
having been treated with the utmost kind-
ness and hospitality by bishops and people,
and everything done as far as possible to ad-
vance the special mission in which they were
engaged. Br. Young while South made quite
a collection of minerals and curiosities to add
to his cabinet; among them were specimens of
petrified corn which must have lain at least a
hundred years, but were perfect in outline.
Upon the top of a very high mountain he
found a great number of petrified oyster shells,
perfect, and some of them closed together, nev-
er having been opened, proving beyond contra-
diction that the sea had once covered that por-
tion of the country.
	Bro. Hardy informed us they had, while
traveling, visited the day and Sunday Schools
and spoken to them whenever they could. At
Monroe they had been very much gratified
with the manner in which the school was con-
ducted by Mrs. Bean, a lady who seemed to
have a thorough understanding of her work.
She had a class in the Book of Mormon which
was very interesting; this Book as a history of
this country and its former inhabitants and
government, ought to be well understood by
the rising generation, and especially by the
children of the Latter-day Saints.
	On the 10th of July Elders' Young and
Hardy started North to visit that part of the
country on the same mission; they held their
first meeting at Brigham City, with the young
people there in a general assembly and had an
interesting time. They then proceeded through
Cache Valley visiting almost every town,
organizing associations at Hyrum, Smithfield,
and Wellsville; thence to Soda Springs and on 
through Bear Lake Valley holding meetings
and organizing Associat[-] societies in nearly all
of the towns and settlements. Br. Hardy in-
formed us that he had been distributing tracts
and pamphlets containing the first principles
of the Gospel, through the Sunday Schools and
associations in the settlements; it will no doubt

[Column 3]
have a tendency to facilitate the work of the
Sunday School—teachers very—considerably.
There are many of the children who are not
sufficiently informed of the fundamental prin-
ciples of the Gospel. Elder Hardy has been
for some years assistant to Dr. J. R. Park in
the Deseret University in Salt Lake City, he
has now gone to assist Br. Carl G. Maeser in
the Academy at Provo. We wish them the
success and patronage we are sure they emi-
nently deserve.


﻿WOMAN'S EXPONENT.


', 'WOMEN IN THE GRANGE AND WOMEN AS 
				FARMERS.
	As all the world knows, Utah was once a
vast desert, and the first thing for the pioneers
to do was to build homes and cultivate the
earth, that the necessaries of life might be
provided; in those days, with no railroads, no
roads of any kind, save the one trail marked
by their own tired oxen and worn out wagons,
it was impossible to obtain supplies from any
source than the earth, where their homes were
chosen.  So, all the settlers became farmers,
and as new comers were yearly arriving, and
the farms in Salt Lake valley grew, families
spread out in other directions and began sub-
duing the soil in other valleys, toiling unceas-
ingly, until now nearly the whole Territory is
covered with farms and homesteads. We have
all from earliest infancy heard of the Pilgrim
Fathers, and after considerable time and reason-
ing the world acknowledges that we also had
Pilgrim mothers. It is a well known fact
whenever there is work to do the women—
wives and mothers—stand by and aid the men
—husbands and fathers. It is perfectly
right that they should, and we always find they
are pretty welcome, too. No one doubts that
the Pilgrim mothers helped with the clearing,
the planting, the tending, though their names
have not been handed down the ages as the
first great settlers of the old bay state. It
used to be enough to mention the men, but
sometimes the father and husband dies, and the
woman has her own home to make, and the
day is fast approaching when woman's name
will also be hurled down the ages, and her
praises sung in different climes. Here, in this
far off west, we claim Pioneer Fathers and
Pioneer mothers, too, who might have had for
their motto the beautiful words which the
Rev. Annie H. Shaw addressed to the young
women in her Sunday sermon— "If you have a
bit of Truth, hold fast to that which God has
given you; let no power, no injustice, no ob-
stacle, no scorn, no opposition, let nothing ex-
tinguish this flame.”  Against all opposition,
all persecution, all persuasion, these mothers
held fast to the "bit of Truth” that God had
given them; with their husbands and fathers

[Column 3]
they braved the perils and trials of the great
desert journey, and in these valleys have toiled
hard and long, building homes and cultivating
the earth, and now when the valleys are
blossoming and verdant, and these brave,
white-haired women can walk in their own
gardens, gathering fruit and flowers, each one
truly feels that she has been given the fruit of
her hands, and her own works praise her in
the gates. Owing to the Relief Society, there
is no particular need in Utah of a Grange
Association, as this Society alleviates the
monotony and humdrum of farm life, and
affords those advantages to women in Utah
that the Grange does in other places. Our
women are alive to the necessity of caring for
the grain, and have a separate organization for
that purpose; they at present are the owners
of many well stored granaries. 
		WOMEN IN EDUCATION.
Among the first company, the pioneers, were
only three women—Clara Decker Young,
Ellen Sanders Kimball and Harriet P. Wheeler
Young—whose names should ever be remem-
bered and claim a glorious place in every page
relating to the early history of Utah. There
were no children; but the September following
five hundred families arrived. In the south 
western division of Salt Lake City remains
now a single ten acre square, without building
or improvement of any kind save a garden and
the gardener’s lodge; this square is familiarly,
known as “The Old Fort;” here were the first
homes, rude log walls surrounding the entire
square, secure and plain, the doors and win-
dows facing an inner courtyard, the outer walls
without opening or ornament of any sort, in
this way protecting the dwellers from wolves
and other wild things with which the country
then abounded. Simultaneously with the
constructing of homes, the subduing of lands,
began educational work in Utah; it was like
all things else a difficult and rather unpleasant
undertaking, as there were so few conveniences;
but it became the task of a noble and bright
young woman—Mary Jane Dilworth, afterward
wife of Bishop F. A. Hammond—to collect in
her brother-in-law’s tent, even before the houses
were built, and instruct the children who were
playing away the sunny hours in the court-
yard of The Old Fort. This was the first
school in Utah, and the first school teacher
was a woman. This same Mrs. Hammond,
nee Miss Dilworth, in 1849 accompanied her
husband to the Sandwich Islands, and there
gathered around her the native children, teach-
ing the first English school in Laie. Return-
ing again to Utah, she became a pioneer in
settling other parts of the Territory, and
opened in three different places the first school.
Her Sister, Mrs. Rebecca Riter, in speaking of
her work, very touchingly says, “She was in-
deed a pioneer in all her movements after ally-
ing herself with the Church, in teaching, in
helping to settle new countries, in the mission-
ary field, in philanthropy, and even in her
burial, being the first person laid away in the
new graveyard at Huntsville.”
	As other companies of children arrived in
the valleys and the people spread out in other
directions, other schools were started, and the
demand for teachers became greater. Mrs.
Emmeline B. Wells, our editor, pleasingly tells
of a little school of 65 kept by herself in
1851-2 in a log building in the upper part of
the city; how without desks, charts, black


[Column 1]
boards, or scarcely two books of the same kind,
she toiled as an instructress. The task was
not so easy then as now, when we have all the
modern appliances for school rooms, and large,
comfortable, airy buildings. The remunera-
tion was likewise trying, one bringing a piece
of salt pork, another a bucket of flour, a third
perhaps a skein of yarn, to repay the ever toil-
some task of school teaching; the work was
unceasing; the day spent in the school room
and the night in planning and working for the
morrow, whole nights being spent in cutting
fancy paper patterns and writing thereon verses
of her own composition, in blue ink, and
working on bits of card-board, mottoes in vari-
ous colored silks for rewards, to urge the
children to diligence and good behavior. This
is but a sample of the work school teachers
had in those days; it is amusing now, but was
trying then. Since the first winter, when Miss
Dilworth opened the little school in the Old
Fort, there has ever been a school, and a woman
teacher in Utah. Here, as throughout the
west, co-education has always existed. The
University and all the schools, district and
Church, barring, of course, the Catholic, ad-
mitting to all departments women as well as
men, and as educators, women have almost the
same advantages as men. The Territorial
University has but once in its long history
boasted a woman teacher, notwithstanding the
graduates from the normal department average
very nearly equal, there being from the years
1876 to 1888, eighty-five female and one hun-
dred and twenty-eight male graduates, certain-
ly a fair showing in an institution controlled
throughout by men. Then, as in all other
things, the wages are less (women, you know,
don’t require as much as men), if they do men
don’t think so, and we have not yet any women
trustees. The average salary per month for
teachers the past year was, males, $46-85
and females, $26-70. The total num-
ber of schools in the Territory, primary,
intermediate and mixed, was 467; the number
of teachers, 561, with 60 assistants; out of this
number 258 were women teachers, and 48 as-
sistant women teachers, giving the men the
small majority of nine.
Women have certainly done a great deal in
advancing education in Utah; to the Misses
Cook, Mary and Ida, is due the grading of
schools; this would doubtless have been done
had they not come, but not so early. When
they opened in the Social Hall of Salt Lake
City a common school, they found a sad mix-
ture of books among their pupils, some far ad-
vanced in one study and far behind in others,
no order, no uniformity, no regular books, and
they had quite a struggle to grade their school
properly, the pupils rebelling against such an
unheard of system. I remember very well how
I cried when Miss Cook placed me way down
in the second reader, for I was one of the very
best readers in school, read so well that my
previous teacher had allowed me to skip the
third reader altogether, and I read now in the
National Fifth. This was my argument, and
I ventured to prove it by reading something
very hard for her benefit, and as for geography,
why I was always head, but, alas! for arithme-
tic, that was the point on which we were to be
graded, and, well, compound numbers was my
highest limit, and Miss Cook thought “it
wouldn’t hurt me to begin at the beginning.”
Tears and arguments were alike unavailing;
the school had to be graded, and our knowledge
of arithmetic told where we ought to be. How
we “hated” it then, but as the school assumed
that order and uniformity obtainable only in a
well graded system, even, the pupils began to
admire it and work more diligently in all
studies, not slighting one for another more
pleasant and easy. Miss Cook’s school became 
an example, and the Territorial Superintend-
ent, then Robert L. Campbell, began grading 

[Column 2]
the district schools, frequently calling on Miss
Cook for advice and assistance. There were a 
few, some eighteen or twenty, as fine teachers 
as Utah can boast trained under Miss Mary 
Cook. Miss Ida's work, after leaving the 
University, where she was one year principal,
has been mostly confined to Cache Valley.
She held for some years the position of principal 
of the Brigham Young College, the largest 
school in northern Utah, and was elected to 
the office of school superintendent for Cache 
County, but owing to existing laws against 
women holding office was not allowed to act; 
the word male in the organic act prohibiting 
women as teachers from occupying positions of
emolument and trust. Where women have 
the greatest success is among the children, 
and there they should claim the entire con-
trol. 
The Kindergarten has been very gladly re-
ceived wherever it has been started, but it has 
not yet extended far in the Territory. If 
more of the young women teachers would 
spend some time in learning the method and 
introduce it in their schools they would not 
only afford themselves much pleasure, the 
little ones much happiness, but would gladden 
the hearts of the mothers as well. Some of 
the different church schools have a Kinder-
garten department, and Miss Dickey,  of the
Presbyterian school, herself a student of 
Baroness Maronholtz Beulow, the friend and 
pupil of Froebel, has trained some few teachers, 
who now keep most successful and interesting 
Kindergartens in different parts of the city. 
Such a delightful and pleasing thing ought to 
grow most rapidly in a community which
boasts so many beautiful and bright children.
We have, it is true, the Primary Association 
for the children, which in the prettiest possible 
way instils into the little hearts bright truths,
instructs the little hands to do nice work, and 
fills the little souls with happy thoughts; busy
little working bees, indeed, are the children of 
the Primary; but these Associations are held
but once a week, and the remainder of the 
time the little ones are not very much em-
ployed, unless, indeed, they are old enough for 
the district schools. If to each district school 
a Kindergarten might be attached, it would 
certainly be a great benefit to the community.


WOMAN'S EXPONENT.


[column 1]
']
