	The funeral services held in the large

[Column 2]
Tabernacle were numerously attended and
the great organ pealing forth its grandly
sublime notes awakened the tenderest emo-
tions of the human heart, albeit attuned to
sadness, and yet to victorious triumph.
	The first hymn by the choir was Presi-
dent Woodruff’s favorite, “God moves in a
mysterious way.” The prayer was offered
by Apostle F. M Lyman, and a male chorus
of the best singers rendered the funeral
anthem, “Not dead, but sleepeth.”
	The speakers of the occasion were the
Apostles and First Presidency, men with
whom his life had been spent in loving ser-
vice for the Gospel, and many glowing
tributes were paid to his memory by these
great and noble men who had labored with
him and knew whereof they spoke.
	During the service Elder R. C. Easton
rendered “Sister Eliza’s” immortal invo-
cation, “O, my Father,” in a most impres-
sive style, his superb voice filling the
Tabernacle with melody. A male quartette
gave beautifully, “O, Love Divine,” and
the last musical number was Evan Stephen’s
anthem, “When the Soul is Sad and Weary
Seek Relief in Prayer.”
	The white draping festooned with heavy
white silk cord and tassels and rosettes and
bows of white satin ribbon made the place
sacred and fitting for such service over one
whose life had been consecrated to the ser-
vice of his Lord and Master.
	The floral emblems were suggestive of the
various places he had filled and the work he
had performed. The flowers were the
choicest and rarest varieties to be obtained.
	The gift of the Sunday School was a large
open book encircled with a wreath and a
tablet with the words “The Childrens’
Friend.” From the family a floral mauso-
leum with solid roof, and floor of pink and
white carnations and roses, and columns
twined with smilax and flowers, the word
“Father” embedded in the banked flowers.
	There were many other exquisite floral
offerings and the effect was beyond any
mere description on paper or canvas, and
must have been seen to be realized or appre-
ciated. The fragrance and beauty com-
bined with the exquisite taste of the ar-
rangement reminded one of the poet who
wrote,

	“Flowers preach to us if we will hear.”

	After the funeral cortege had reached the
cemetery and while the casket was being
lowered into the grave the quartette rendered
the good old hymn, “Come ye disconsolate.”
	The grave was dedicated by Apostle
Anthon H. Lund, and Bishop John R.
Winder expressed, in behalf of the family,
gratitude for all the kindness and the at-
tention shown them in their time of sorrow
and affliction. The quartette sang, “When
the Swallows Homeward Fly.” The grave
was literally embowered in flowers. Presi-
dent Cannon was buried at the side of his
wife, Elizabeth, who died in 1882, and his
two sons, Abraham and David, and
other children.

', "ABRAHAM HOAGLAND CANNON.

	IT is quite impossible for one who knew
and loved Brother Abraham to write any-
thing good enough to satisfy one’s self, and
yet one is unwilling to be silent and not
offer a tribute to his memory. He was in-
deed one of “nature’s noblemen”, liberally
endowed with lofty characteristics; not only
those which entitled him to foremost places
among his fellowmen and his brethren in
the Church, but the finer qualities of the
heart which endeared him to all those who
love superior virtues, purity of life, refine-
ment and heavenly charity. “None knew
him but to love him” may truthfully be
said of Brother Abraham.
	So much has already been said by the
very ablest editors and writers, so many
tender and beautiful tributes paid to his
life and character, that one approaches the
subject with great diffidence; and yet the
writer feels in her inmost heart, to cast in
her offering among the many who admired
and felt an exceptional friendship for the
deceased, cut off in the midst of his great
usefulness, when every prospect of future
promise was at its highest and brightest. It
was said in days of old,
	“Whom the gods love die young.”
And surely our Heavenly Father Himself
loved such a one as Elder Cannon; and cer-
tainly His blessing was upon him, for in all he
did and in all that transpired with him he
seemed highly favored and blest of the
Lord. Looking back upon his course from
his boyhood until the time when his spirit
took its departure from mortality, one must
acknowledge his remarkable personality,
which attracted all who came into his
presence. Very few men possess this
personal attraction in as great a degree as
did Brother Abraham. His simplicity of
manner and of language were among his
pleasing traits. He was eloquent in his
public speaking, in plain simple language,
no ostentation, no show of oratory, but
powerfully impressive, straight-forward and
clear in all his sermons and addresses. He
had an intuitive understanding of people,
and therefore knew how to deal tactfully
with individual peculiarities, and eccentri-
cities of men and women with whom he
had business relations, or even casual ac-
quaintance.
	Brother Cannon was more than courteous,
he was naturally an exceedingly gracious
gentleman; beloved by men and women
everywhere, in all places where he had
traveled either on business, or as a mission-

[Column 2]
ary. The people and the Latter-day Saints
who knew him most intimately, and real-
zed his arduous labors and the heavy
burdens and responsibilities that rested up-
on him, had no thought of his dying; it
seemed remote from the minds of all. To
be sure he was sometimes warned that he
was doing too much, but still there seemed
more and more given him to do, and he
was ever and always willing. He possessed
business capacity in a very unusual degree,
and all public and private interests pros-
pered in his hands by means of his thrift,
diligence and successful management. But
say what we will, extol him as we may, he
has gone, and we shall see him no more in
this life. The young Elders in Israel
would do well to follow in his footsteps, to
emulate his example, to be as true, as
honest, as faithful and as obedient to coun-
sel and to the laws of God, as was this
young and valiant hero, in the battle of
life. His name and fame will live forever
in the hearts of this people, and his child-
ren have a heritage far more precious than
gold, or silver, or precious gems, or houses,
or lands, or other worldly possessions; the
unsullied life of an Elder in Israel, an
Apostle of Christ, whose example he sought
to follow; verily, “A good name is better to
be chosen than great riches.”
	Abraham Hoagland Cannon was born
in Salt Lake City, March 12, 1859; his
parents were George Q. and Elizabeth
Hoagland Cannon. President Cannon was
away in the Eastern States upon a mission
at the time of his birth. In his boyhood he
had the best advantages of school educa-
tion the times afforded in the ward schools,
and afterwards at the Deseret University.
He was naturally studious, and made good
use of his opportunities.
	His first mission was to Europe, and he
labored first in the Nottingham Conference
in England, until he was assigned to the
Swiss and German mission, where he
labored assiduously and converted many to
the truth of the Gospel; he became quite
“proficient in the German language, and
wrote some of the hymns which the Ger-
man Saints now sing in their congregations.”
He returned home in June 1882. His
mother died while he was absent upon this
mission.
	“On the 9th of October 1892, Elder Can-
non was ordained one of the First Seven
Presidents of Seventies, and on October 6,
1889, he was sustained at the General Con-
ference of the Church as one of the Twelve
Apostles. From that time he has been the
jnnior member of the Council of Apostles.”
	In all the duties pertaining to his calling and
ministry in the Church, he was scrupulously
exact and faithful; his fidelity and integrity
to the truth and in every relation of life,
are unquestioned and unquestionable. The
persecution borne by the Saints at the time
of the enforcement of the Edmund’s law,
he endured without a murmur; and his
heroic conduct on the occasion of his sen-
tence, and the explanation made to the
Judge, as published in the Deseret News,
from which we have copied it here, fully
illustrates his views, and is abundant testi-
mony of the sincerity of his sublime faith
in the principle of the Gospel for which he
was to suffer imprisonment.
	“As illustrative of his zeal and fidelity to
the Gospel may be cited an incident in
connection with the imprisonment referred
to, when he gave expression to views
characteristic of his whole life in its highest

[Column 3]
integrity to his convictions of right. He
had entered into the order of plural mar-
riage, and on March 17, 1886, being at that
time twenty-six years of age, he was sen-
tenced by Judge Zane to a term of six
months in the penitentiary for his obser-
vance of that law of God. When called to
receive the judgment of the court he said:”
	“I would like to state, your honor, that I have
always endeavored to keep the laws of the United
States, because I have been taught by my parents
that the Constitution was a sacred instrument.
That I have failed in this respect, and now stand
before you convicted of the crime of unlawful co-
habitation, is due to the fact that I acknowledge
a higher law than that of man, which is the law
of God; and that law being a part of my religion,
sir, I have attempted to obey it. When I em-
braced this religion, I promised to place all that I
had, even to life itself, upon the altar, and I ex-
pect to abide by that covenant which I made
and, sir, I hope the day will never come when I
must sacrifice principle, even to procure life or
liberty. Honor, sir, to me, is higher than any-
thing else upon the earth; and my religion is
dearer to me than anything else that I have yet
seen. I am prepared, sir, for the judgment of
the court.”
	Brother Cannon’s illness and death have
cast a gloom over the community, and it
will be some time before his friends (even
outside the family) recover from the shock;
but the Saints know what it is to bow to
the Providence of God in the greatest sor-
row, and acknowledge the hand of the
Lord in the death however sudden, of their
beloved ones, though taken as Brother
Abraham was in the prime of life, with his
bright and spotless armor on, full of zeal,
of courage, and of hope. And though we
mourn his absence and deeply feel his loss, 
yet we are comforted for as Paul says,
	“But I would not have you to be ignorant,
brethren, concerning them which are asleep,
that ye sorrow not as others thvt have no hope.”
	Apostle Abraham H. Cannon departed
this life, at his home in Cannon Ward, Salt
Lake City, July 19, 1896, at fifteen minutes
after four o’clock in the morning of the
Sabbath day. His funeral services were
held in the large Tabernacle in this City on
the following Sunday, July 26. The dec-
orations at the Tabernacle were very
elaborate and elegant. White crepe draped
tastefully and looped with cord and tassel
in the most graceful way on the stands
and the pillars near, as well as the great
organ. Ornamental flowers and shrubs
adorned the stands and platform in all the
available places. Tributes of flowers in
artistic designs came from many of the
relatives and friends. Several of the floral
pieces were surmounted with white doves
emblematic of the purity of the life of the
deceased. The effect of the whole was
loveliness itself, and surpassed anything
ever before attempted in the building, at
any time.
	The seating arrangements were as perfect
as possible; officers and employees of com-
panies and organizations of which Brother
Cannon was a member, were seated at the
right of the center aisle; the family and re-
latives with President George Q. Cannon,
(the father) at the head in the seats at the
left of the center aisle. There was no dis-
play of mourning apparel by any of the
family, and there was no need, for their
grief was too intense for outward sem-
blance.
	In the stand of the First Presidency were
Presidents Wilford Woodruff and Joseph
F. Smith, Patriarch John Smith, and of the
Apostles, President Lorenzo Snow, Elders


[Column 1]
F. D. Richards and H. J. Grant. The
next stand was occupied by Elders Brigham
Young, J. H. Smith, F. M. Lyman, Geo.
Teasdale, J. W. Taylor and M. W. Mer-
rill of the Quorum of the Apostles and
Elder Moses Thatcher who came in later,
and Elder Jos. E. Taylor and C. W. Pen-
rose of the Salt Lake Stake Presidency.
On the third stand were the Presiding
Council of Seventies and of High Priests
Quorums, and on the fourth, the Presid-
ing Bishopric and others.
	The casket was placed in the center of
the front platform between the floral em-
blems, and on the elegant white casket
were some of the most beautiful pale and
delicately arranged flowers. And one
could not help realizing these touching
lines of the poet,
	“AFTER the burden snd heat of the day,
		The starry calm of night;
	After the rough and toilsome way,
		A sleep in the robe of white.”
	The funeral was conducted under the
auspices of the quorum of the Twelve
Apostles, President Lorenzo Snow presid-
ing. Elder H. J. Grant announced the
opening hymn (reading the first verse,)
“The Resurrection Day.” A most sublime
prayer was offered by Elder F. D. Richards.
Mrs. Nellie Druce Pugsley sang the well
know soprano solo from the Messiah,
“I know that my Redeemer liveth. ”
	President Lorenzo Snow was the first
speaker, and he paid a glowing tribute to
the departed Apostle.
	The venerable President of the Church,
Wilford Woodruff, who spoke next, opened
his discourse with the words “Blessed are
the dead which die in the Lord from hence-
forth; yea, saith the Spirit, that they may
rest from their labors, and their works do
follow them.” His voice was clear and
yet one detected the tone of inward grief in
the intonation. He spoke reverently and
tenderly of the father, President George Q.
Cannon, and affectionately of the wives and
children and of the principle through and
by which he had received them, and ex-
horted them to be faithful and resigned,
and closed his remarks after occupying
about fifteen minutes in speaking sympa-
thetically and blessing them all and said:
“I hope and trust we may all pursue that
course that we may be justified and prepared
to pass to the other side of the vail as he
has done; God grant it for Christ’s sake,
Amen.”
	Brother R. C. Easton the famous tenor,
then sang to the organ accompaniment by
Prof. Daynes, that favorite invocation by
our beloved sister and poet, Eliza R. Snow,
“O my Father.”
	President Jos. F. Smith though deeply
affected delivered an eloquent discourse
enumerating the many arduous labors,
and characteristic virtues of the deceased,
and appealed to the congregation to follow
his righteous example and labor as diligent-
ly in the cause of Christ as he had done.
He read a portion of a Revelation from the
Doctrine and Covenants given through
Joseph Smith, the prophet, to the people of
the world in this dispensation, which com-
mences with, 
	“Behold mine house is a house of order, saith
the Lord God, and not a house of confusion.”
“Will I accept of an offering, saith the Lord,
that is not made in my name!” “Or will I re-
ceive at your hands that which I have not ap-
pointed!”

[Column 2]
	After President Smith finished his ser-
mon which occupied the remainder of the
afternoon, Horace Ensign and the choir
sang the Anthem.
		“When the soul is sad and weary,
		Seek relief in prayer.”
	The funeral procession was in charge of
a special Committee, General R. W. Young
being Marshal. Held’s band in neat uni-
forms were at the head of the column, and
carriages containing the Authorities of the 
Church, including Presidents Woodruff
and Smith and the Apostles, first, then the 
hearse; next the families; a beautiful sight
were the children in the large Drag, Utah,
all in white;—the procession was over a
mile in length and the streets were lined on
both sides the entire distance to the grave-
yard with people. The cemetery was also
packed with those who were anxious to
show their love and respect for one so high-
ly esteemed. When the casket had been
lowered, a quartette led by H. G. Whitney
sang
		“Rest, rest for the weary soul.”
	Elder F. M. Lyman offered the dedi-
catory prayer—and the grave of the depart-
ed was covered and literally embowered in
flowers. Elder H. J. Grant extended the
thanks of the family to all who had assisted
in their hour of sorrowing bereavement.
	May the consolation of the Holy Spirit be
and abide with those who mourn, and
above all may they seek to reconcile them-
selves to the rulings of an All Wise Provi-
dence, and strive to do the work he has left
unfinished inasmuch as it is possible, rely-
ing upon Him who sees even the sparrows
fall, and who has promised never to leave
or forsake those who put their trust in Him.


WOMAN'S EXPONENT.


[Column 1]
", "PRESIDENT WILFORD WOODRUFF.
		“Who soweth go[-]d seed shall surely reap;
		The year grows rich as it groweth old
		And [-]ife's latest sands are its sands of gold.”
	How true this is of the life just gone out
in this world, the life of that great, good and
reverend man, President Wilford Woodruff.
True is it that the last years of his life were
full of golden deeds, that will shine for-
ever—deeds of love, of mercy and of that
abundant charity that never faileth. His
name will go down to remotest ages be-
cause of his great kindness, his gentle ten-
derness and his love of simplicity in all
things that help to make life sweet and
beautiful. He was in all places and at all
times essentially a peacemaker, and the
Savior said, “Blessed are the peacemakers,
for they shall be called the children of God.”
	All the virtues that are most dear and
sacred in the home and in the friendships
and relations of human life among one’s
associates and fellow men characterized the
life of President Woodruff. In this respect
he was greatly honored of the Lord; he had
no enemies, his humility was most remark-
able in a man raised to an exalted position
among his brethren in the Church, and it
drew the affections of the people to him to
a degree unsurpassed in the history of men
in our day. It has been truly said of him
that he was without guile, honest, straight-
forward, industrious, and in fact he was
possessed of all the best qualities that make
men worthy of honor in the world and be-
fore all men. He was not only as Presi-
dent George Q. Cannon described him, a
natural man, but naturally a Latter-day Saint.
For the Gospel he was willing to endure all
things and all hardships  and to promulgate
its truths he traveled by land and by sea
thousands and tens of thousands of miles,
and encountered untold suffering, dangers
and exposure.
	Our readers are most of them familiar
with the excellent traits of their venerable
and dearly beloved President who has just
left us to join the innumerable company of
Saints in the worlds on high, but it seems
only proper to let the whole world know
how grandly he stands out from and above
the men of his time. His departure was so
sudden even after the advanced years of his
long and useful life, that his shadow still 
lingers on our pathway and it is difficult to
realize that we shall see him no more in our
midst in the assemblies of the Saints.

[Column 2]
	Sketches of President Woodruff’s life
have been written by some of our ablest
men, and in our small paper it is impossi-
ble to do justice to the remarkable career,
public and private of one whose days have
been so rich in interesting incidents and so
replete in experiences of an eventful char-
acter.
	President Woodruff is of Puritan
lineage of English descent and was born
March 1, 1807, in Connecticutt in Hartford
Co., in the town of Farmington, now called
Avon; his forefathers were among the first
settlers in Hartford county. He inclined
to be religious from his boyhood but did
not become connected with any of the 
churches until he heard the Gospel of the
Church of Jesus Christ of Latter-day Saints.
	On December 29, 1833, he heard
Elders Zera Pulsipher and Elijah Cheeny
preach the Gospel as proclaimed by Joseph
Smith and also read from the Book of Mor-
mon, he was convinced of the truth and
two days afterwards was baptized and con-
firmed by Elder Pulsipher. He shortly
after went to Kirtland and became acquaint-
ed with the Prophet Joseph. On the first
of May the same year he went up to Mis-
souri with Zion’s Camp. January 13, he
started on his first mission without purse or
scrip to preach the Gospel and traveled in
Arkansas, Tennessee and Kentucky. This
was the commencement of his missionary
life. From that time he devoted himself
the greater part of his time to carrying the
Gospel to the nations in our own and fore-
ign lands. While away proclaiming the
Gospel he received word that he had been
chosen by revelation to fill a vacancy in the
Council of the Twelve Apostles, and that
he was to go to Far West, Missouri, and 
prepare to take a mission to England. April
26, 1839, Wilford Woodruff and George A.
Smith were ordained members of the
Quorum of the Twelve Apostles. In Eng-
land in connection with Heber C. Kimball
he performed a wonderful mission which is 
definitely recounted in the history of the
Church. Afterwards he filled several other
missions, and August 28, 1844, was sent
from Nauvoo to preside over the British
mission, and returned early in 1846 just as
the first company of Saints were leaving for
the West.
	Of President Woodruff as a pioneer the
people are familiar, he was one of the first
company and assisted in various ways to
found this now flourishing State, not only
in colonizing and exploring, but in agricul-
tural and horticultural pursuits and was
prominent in public places in the city and
territory and became very eminent in Ec-
clesiastical positions. President Woodruff
labored some years as president of the St.
George Temple, was afterwards made presi-
dent of the Quorum of the Twelve Apostles
and at the death of President John Taylor
succeeded him as President of the
Church of Jesus Christ of Latter-day Saints.
	President Woodruff was a most inde-
fatigable worker, never resting except
when he could toil no longer. His journal
and correspondence were faithfully kept up
to the very last, and must necessarily be of
the greatest interest to his family and of 
immense value as a matter of history.
	President Wilford Woodruff’s death took
place in the City of San Francisco where he
was spending a few weeks resting from the 
arduous labors of his office and calling, and
which he was enjoying to the utmost. His
wife was with him and also his first coun-

[Column 3]
selor in the presidency of the Church, and 
everything possible was done to save his
precious life that human skill and the best
nursing could accomplish.
	President Woodruff departed this life
after only a brief illness peacefully and ap-
parently without pain, sinking into a child-
like slumber from which he never woke,
but entered into that world of spirits of
which he so often spoke while here, and
where he anticipated so much joy and satis-
faction in meeting with those famous and
worthy men renowned in the Church, with
whom he had labored so many years in
promoting the interests of Zion and carry-
ing the Gospel to the nations of the earth.
There is every reason to believe his recep-
tion would be a glorious and magnificent
one.
INTERESTING INSTRUCTIONS.
	President Woodruff in a signed but un-
dated document wrote this concerning him-
self. 
	“Concerning My Death and Burial—I
wish to say that at my death I wish the
historian of the Church to publish a brief
account of my life, labors and travels, as an
Elder and an Apostle in the Church of
Jesus Christ of Latter-day Saints. I wish
my body washed clean and clothed in clean
white linen, according to the order of the
Holy Priesthood, and put into a plain, de-
cent coffin, made of native wood, with
plenty of room. I do not wish any black
made use of about my coffin, or about the
vehicle that conveys my body to the grave. 
I do not wish my family or friends to wear
any badge of mourning for me at my funer-
al or afterwards, for if I am true and faith-
ful unto death there will be no necessity
for any one to mourn for me. I have no
directions to give concerning the services of
my funeral, any further than it would 
be pleasing to me for as many of the Presi-
dency and Twelve Apostles who may be
present to speak as may be thought wisdom.
Their speech will be to the living. If the
laws and customs of the spirit world will
permit, I should wish to attend my funeral
myself; but I shall be governed by the 
counsel I receive in the spirit world. I
wish a plain marble slab put at the head of
my grave, with my name and age, and that
I died in the faith of the Gospel of Christ
and in the fellowship of the Saints.
	(Signed) WILFORD WOODRUFF,
President of the Council of the Twelve
	Apostles.”
A WHITE FUNERAL.
	President Woodruff’s funeral may with
all propriety be designated a white funeral.
The services were held in the Tabernacle
in Salt Lake City, which had been beauti-
fully and most artistically draped in white
and prepared for the solemn occasion. The
appearance of the building was such as to 
impress with a reverential feeling every one
who had the privilege of attending the
ceremonies. The floral decorations were
profuse and magnificent and everything
harmonized with the spirit of peace that
brooded over the entire arrangements, and
pervaded the assembly and remained to
soothe the feelings of all, for the entire peo-
ple were bereaved of their great prophet
and leader, and though outwardly all were
calm, yet many hearts bowed in grief with
love and sorrow at the inevitable parting
with one so fondly cherished and beloved.
	An elegant life-sized painting of Presi-


[Column 1]
dent Woodruff—very natural as he appeared
a few years ago—hung in front of the
great organ against the stars and stripes of
the country he loved. Above the picture
were the words, “Being dead yet speaketh,”
the words were illuminated during musical
exercises; the white drapery fastooned about
the organ was in graceful folds and curves;
on each side of the organ were the figures
1847 and large bunches of sagebrush and.
sunflowers further up tops of pine trees; repre-
senting the valley were furze and brush, a
unique and realistic idea of Utah when he
who lay there sleeping entered the valley.
On the right and left were sheaves of
wheat and oats and more floral offerings, a
great bank in pure white. The stands were
draped in white cashmere tied with ribbon,
also the posts and at each end and at the top
of each a lovely bouquet. The folds of
cashmere tied by silk cords and tassels and
other appropriate draping and flowers were
almost beyond description; the coffin was
white and over it was laid a floral tapestry
—inscribed “In loving remembrance of the
children and grandchildren of Phebe Carter
Woodruff and Mary Jackson Woodruff.”
On top of the casket a bank of carnations
and lilies with the word “Father,”—a floral
crown from the Relief Society was placed at
the head, a large circle of cut roses from the
Y. M. M. I. A. with the motto in pure 
white blossoms, “The glory of God is in-
telligence,” a beautiful floral harp from the
choir, a basket of roses and asters from the
Primary Association and many other de-
signs by devoted friends, associations and
bodies of citizens. The entire effect of these
floral tributes was simplicity, harmony and
purity.
	The services commenced at 10:30 by an
organ rendition of a march composed for
the occasion by Prof. J. J. Daynes, and its
effect was most solemn and impressive. The
General Authorities of the Church were
seated in their order in the priesthood, the
opening hymn was given out by President
George Q. Cannon, “Thou dost not weep
to weep alone.” Apostle F. D. Richards
offered a fervent and eloquent prayer.
Elder George D. Pyper and the choir then 
sang the hymn “O my Father Thou that
dwellest.”
	President Joseph F. Smith was the first
speaker, and Mrs. Maggie C. Hull then
sang “The Beautiful City,” assisted by the
Tabernacle choir. Then President Lorenzo
 Snow, Apostle F. D. Richards and Presi-
dent George Q. Cannon each made remarks,
after which the choir sang President Wood-
ruff’s favorite hymn, “God moves in a
mysterious way,” and the closing prayer
was offered by Apostle Brigham Young.
	The funeral cortege was very long and
imposing, bands of music, mounted men
and the National Guard in uniform follow-
ing the train of carriages; thousands of peo-
ple stood on either side of the way to show
their respect for this distinguished man—
the men with heads uncovered, the little
children who loved and honored him united
with the throng of people old and young
who followed the procession to its final
resting place. The Dead March in Saul
and other funeral dirges were played and
the bands all discoursed sweet music by the
way and at the cemetery, where a great con-
course of people were already waiting. The
Harmony Glee Club sang exquisitely the
selection “Not Dead but Sleepeth,” and
Apostle F. M. Lyman offered the dedicatory
prayer.

[Column 2]
	And then the mortal body of this great
prophet, seer and revelator was consigned
to mother earth to await the morning of
the first resurrection when he will again
come forth clothed upon with an immortal
body, eternal and imperishable.
	May the grace of God soften the affliction
to the loving ones bereft, and in contem-
plating his glorious life and exalted character
may they find such consolation as shall,
lead them to follow his eminent example
of truth and righteousness, and thus to be
prepared to dwell with him in the great
hereafter throughout the endless ages of
eternity.
	“Lives of great men all remind us
		We can make our lives sublime,
	And, departing, leave behind us, 
		Footprints on the sands of time.”


WOMAN’S EXPONENT.


"]

Name: 46_grain_wheat_sisters_storing
Representative_Docs:
['CONTINUED.
	Miss E. R. Snow said: "I am pleased to
hear the report of what our sisters have ac-
complished; the subject was new to us, we
had many obstacles to meet, but I thank the
 Lord that we have succeeded as well as we
have; still it looks very little compared to
what is before us. The Lord never speaks
in vain; we can depend on His word, and
we know that a famine is coming. We
should take into consideration, that we are
standing as Egypt formerly stood, that is,
preparing for the time when the people of
the world will want food. We ought to
have at least 50,000 bushels of wheat next
year; we have but little time to prepare for
the great events that are coming.
	We are very thankful for the help we
have received from the brethren; in some
places they have rendered great assistance
to the women in this important undertak-
ing. Yet, in attending to the gathering
and storing of wheat, we must not let other
important duties be set aside. I consider
it as very important to guide and lead the
young people in the ways of righteousness
and truth, and we have so much to do, and
so many duties to perform, that every mo-
ment of our lives is called for, either in re-
flection or action."
	Mrs. M. I. Horne said: "I look on the
grain movement as a very important one;
we have done pretty well, considering the
amount of discouragement we had to con-
tend with. I often think that some of the
brethren need converting in this cause, for
they ridicule the idea of women being
called on to store and take care of wheat. I
feel that in the future we will accomplish
the object we have set out to perform—I
feel the Lord will bless our efforts.
	"We should ascertain the best way to
keep our grain good and sound; every set-
tlement should have a store-house, built in
such a manner as to keep the grain in good
condition, and it should be turned over and
examined once or twice a year, to see that
no insect, or anything that would destroy
it, is in it. We must not weary in well-
doing. I am pleased with the remarks of
our brethren; we know that the man and
the woman is one before the Lord. If we
educate and qualify ourselves to be wise
mothers, we will elevate the coming gener-
ations. I trust that our report next year
will be much larger than it is this, that we
may have a larger than it is this, that we
may have a large amount of wheat on hand,
well and carefully stored."
	Miss E. R. Snow said: "If our sisters will
use their persuasive powers, the young
brethren will help them to put up the gran-
aries, and I am sure they will feel honored
in being solicited to assist in such impor-
tant business. It has been reported that the
wheat has been sold: I hope my sisters will
pay no attention to any such story; no one
has any right to touch a grain of it, only to
see that it is in good condition."
	Mrs. E. B. Wells said: "I feel that this is
an important occasion. I am thankful for
the good instruction we have received from
our Presiding Bishop and from President
Taylor. It looks as though we had not
done very much, but considering every-
thing I think we have done very well. I
hope we will be able to report 50,000 bushels
stored, at our next Annual meeting. There
are some wards in this city, and several set-
tlements, that have not sent in any report,

[Column 2]
and we know they have wheat stored. We
keep separate books for the wheat, and at
our first meeting we advised all the Socie-
ttes to do so.
	Our reports that we have received specify
that the wheat reported is in good condi-
tion, and how stored; whether in bins
owned by the Societies, or whether in bins
lent them by the Bishops, or other brethren.
We have had the wheat stored by the Cen-
tral Committee examined, and I feel safe in
reporting to you that it is in good condition
and all there. It should be fully under-
stood, that the wheat we are now using our
energies to collect and store away, is stored
for Zion.
	I do not think it wise to take the wheat
that has been donated for building store-
houses; but if the sisters will, as Sister
Snow says, use their persuasive powers,'
the young men will help in erecting store-
houses. I believe that the sisters all feel
united to do our best to carry out the instruc-
tions we have received. There is a little
more time, and I would like to hear from
some of the sisters."
	Bishop Hunter said: "It is very import-
ant and highly commendable to gather and
store wheat, I wish to say that the dona-
tions for the Temple, from the Relief Socie
ty, have been very liberal indeed; they
have amounted to thousands of dollars.
The donations sent in for the St. George
Temple by the sisters were truly astonish-
ing; even this week there has come into
the Tithing Office dollars and dollars, as
donations to our Temple here. For your
information, I will try and get my clerk,
Brother Goddard, to write out a state-
ment of what has been given in a short
time. I know that the people will take
pleasure in giving in their tithing and their
offerings, and the Lord will bless them for
it. I know that many would have suffered
only for the efficient aid rendered them by
the Relief Societies. Many things are in
your power to do, and in doing them you
will be blessed, and you are blessed."
	Mrs. Jane S. Richards, from Ogden, said:
"I came here to-day to receive instruction,
but I can say that Weber County is trying
to help in this good work; we have as our
report to the President tells, over 500 bush-
els stored, and we hope to do better next
year. I would have been glad to have heard
some instructions or suggestions given in
regard to taking care of our wheat, as we
are young in the business we need some
counsel."
	Mrs. E. A. Whitney said:  "I am glad to
be with you, my sisters; I have been away
resting for some time past, and am thank-
ful to be able to come among you again.
Joseph Smith said that the Relief Society, if
they followed the connsel of the Priesthood,
would be the greatest help in doing good
that ever was known on the earth, and
would be the means of doing a great work.
The teachings we have heard to-day are
true. They are blessings given by the Holy
Spirit, and they must not to be passed over
carelessly. I have held on to the promises
given us in early days in this church. Jo-
seph also said that the cities that would reject
the Gospel would feel the judgments, and I
thought then I should not live to see it ful-
filled, but I have. These things are being
fulfilled every day. I know there is great
faith among the sisters; I have felt the in-
fluence of their prayers, and been healed
when the sisters have prayed for me in
their meetings." Sister Whitney then sang
a song of Zion. Meeting adjourned until
Nov. 18, 1878. The congregation rose and
sand "Praise God," &c. Benediction by
President John Taylor.


', 'ON THE GRAIN MOVEMENT.

	The meeting was held in the Social Hall
on the 17th of November 1876. Called to
order, and opened with prayer by Mrs. E.
Howard. President of Central Committee,
Mrs. E. B. Wells, remarked as follows: "It
is very generally understood that this meet-
ing is called to discuss the GRAIN QUESTION,
It would be well to let our thoughts and
words be concentrated on the subject; we
want to make the best use of our time and
use all dispatch, that the sisters may get
home in good season. We would like the
Presidents of Relief Societies to let us
know what their ideas are on the subject.
	Miss E. R. Snow said: It is a very im-
portant occasion that has called us together,
probably such as never occurred on the
earth before. The Lord, through his pro-
phet, has called on the mothers in Israel to
prepare for a famine, which makes the sub-
ject we are called on to discuss a grave one.
We are well assured that the time is fast
approaching when the Lord will pour out
His indignation on this country, and al-
though we should escape, we will feel the
effects of it in a National capacity. Our
hearts must be hard indeed, if we will not
feel for those who may come to us for help.
The Lord showed his servant Joseph that
such things would come, and it was of such
a nature, and so distressing and revolting

[Column 2]
to humanity to witness, that he asked the
Lord to close it up. We can see, from what
has passed, that the predictions of the Ser-
vant of God, have been fulfilled to the let-
ter. He has now called on the women of
Zion to purchase and take care of wheat,
and I hope we may act with wisdom, that
the blessing of God may rest upon our la-
bors. We want to decide how to procure
the grain and take care of it; and whether
we will act in Wards or in a general capac-
ity; we have been told that there is room
in the Tithing Office to store it. But per-
haps some of the Wards have a good place
for storing; if so, and they prefer it, they
can do so.
	Mrs. E. B. Wells said: Bishop Hunter told
me we could store the wheat in the Tithing
Office, and that the wards could also if they
wished, as there was plenty of room unoc-
cupied now; but whatever we do there, we
will have to employ help, as the brethren
who labor there are all fully employed.
	Mrs. S. M. Kimball said: We have ap-
pointed a committee to visit in our ward.
They report that the feelings of the people
have been liberal and good. All we want
to know is what steps we are to take in or-
der to push the work forward. I feel that
the Lord will bless us in our undertaking.
	Mrs. E. B. Wells asked if any of the
sisters could inform the meeting where
wheat could be purchased.
	Mrs. W. East: I expect Mrs. Horne can
tell us where we can buy wheat, when
she returns from the South. I have been
told that the brethren residing in the coun-
try places, are awakening up, and see the
utilily of saving grain, and it may not be
a very easy matter for the sisters to procure
it. We held a meeting in the 14th Ward a
short time ago exclusively for this ques-
tion. The sisters feel the importance of the
mision and will do all in their power to ac-
complish it. We feel that the Lord will
open up the way for us to get wheat; and
that he will bless us in our efforts.
	Mrs. Elizabeth Goddard: I have heard
that wheat can be bought for a dollar a
bushel; and I think it would be well to take
flour also, it can be kept good in sacks for a
long time.
	Mrs. E. S. Barney: I can report from
three counties where they have no grain
for sale; Morgan, Summit and Wasatch,
were distressed with an early frost, which
destroyed nearly all their wheat; but feel
sure that we can make this business a suc-
cess.
	Mrs. R. Grant: The 13th Ward intend
saving grain. We have had a large bin of-
fered us for that purpose, which we will ac-
cept. It has long been preached that the
brethren should prepare for a day of famine;
it now seems as if the women were to have
a hand in it, and I hope the Lord will give
us strength to perform our work nobly.
	Mrs. Sarah Howard: I feel it is a priv-
ilege the Lord has given us, and we will try
and be united in it. For my part I will try
and do all I can, and I feel that the Lord
will open a way whereby we can obtain
grain, although it is late in the season.
Our Retrenchment Association has com-
menced a fund for that purpose, we will
work in concert with the 20th Ward Relief
Society.
	Mrs. Savage said: In the 20th Ward,
they intend doing the best they can to help
in this work, but just at the present time
there are no funds in the Treasury.
	Mrs. M. M. Hyde, 17th Ward, said:
We intend to build a hall for the use of our
Society; when we heard of this grain busi-
ness, we talked the matter over, and think
of making the hall smaller, and putting up

[Column 3]
a bin for wheat in part of it. I would just
suggest, that in the States, wheat some-
times spoils, through dampness, but our at
mosphere being so dry, I expect it would
keep good for some time."
	Mrs. E. B. Wells: President Young said
the sisters were to solicit help from their
husbands and the brethren, for this purpose;
by that means we would be able to get
funds to purchase the wheat with.
	Mrs. Reid, of the 16th Ward, said: The
sisters of our Ward are willing to do all
they can to carry out counsel.
	Mrs. L. G. Richards: I think that if
we unite our means, and have one energetic
sister to buy up the wheat; and by hav-
ing one, two or three hundred dollars, quite
an amount could be procured, and I think
would be better than being mixed up in
wards.
	Miss E. R. Snow said: What I have to
give, I intend putting it with the bulk. I
know no separate interest. I hope energet-
ic sisters will be appointed to attend to it;
one or two in each ward say, or a commit-
tee be formed, as may seem necessary.
	Mrs. E. B. Wells remarked: I presume
all the boards of the Relief Society will co-
operate in this work, and I think it will be
well for us to unite.
	Mrs. Chase of the 1st Ward: I have
talked on this subject in our meeting, and
we intend to unite with the rest in what-
ever plan they may suggest. I have a large
bin in which a great amount of wheat
could be stored; perhaps all we could get
this year. We have quite a number of
poor, but will be able to help some in all
good calls. At one time my husband put up
a good deal of wheat but after some time
the weevil spoiled it. But I hope we can
plan some way to take care of that which
the Lord will bless us with.
	It was then moved and seconded that a
general committee be appointed to purchase,
but that will not hinder wards from hav-
ing ward committees.
	The following ladies were elected as a
purchasing committee: Mrs. E. S. Barney,
Mrs. E. Howard, and Mrs. S. M. Kimball.
	Mrs. E. B. Wells said: Let every sister
secure and store away every bushel of
wheat that it is possible for her to obtain.
She also said it will be necessary to have a
Treasurer in some central place. Mrs. P.
M. Staines was sustained as Treasurer.
	It was proposed that a record be kept of
the proceedings in each ward, every name
and donation put down in a distinct man-
ner.
	It was suggested that we commence at
once and have no delay about it. In Manti
they have put up 60 or 70 bushels, and we
can't be too prompt in this matter. Many
of the brethren seem to wish to help us.
	Mrs. Mercy Thompson [---]: Flour that
is well sacked and kept in a dry place would
keep as well, if not better, than wheat. I
have some flour now using, that has been
sacked four years, and it is as good, if not
better than new flour. I know that the Lord
will bless us in our undertaking.
	Considerable business was transacted in
regard to the Woman's Book about to be
published; and the propriety of the sisters
making a carpet for the Historian's Office
was taken into consideration and the sisters
donated liberally for that purpose.
	Meeting adjourned until Nov. 24th, at 3
o'clock p. m. Sung "The Spirit of God like
a fire is burning." Benediction by Mrs.
Rachel Grant.


IN, what ship has the greatest number of
people been wrecked?—Courtship.


THE WOMAN'S EXPONENT.

THE WOMAN'S EXPONENT.

', 'STORING GRAIN.
As the time draws near to hold the annual
meeting of the Woman's Central Grain Com-
mittee, wo feel called npon to make some sug-
gestions upon the subject, as it is a matter of 
some importance to the women of this people.
It is well known that President Young in his 
lifetime counseled the sisters to store up grain
for future emergencies, and also that he ad-
vised them to solicit their husbands for dona-
tions of grain and means to build storehouses.
	In view of the fulfillment of prophecies an-
cient and modern, in which this people pro-
fess to have implicit faith, it is consistent that
wise and needful steps be taken in this direc-
tion, and that much earnestness be manifested
in the matter, by those who are engaged in this
labor, that others may be inspired with the
necessity of storing up grain, for it cannot be
by the labor of a few that this Mammoth work
is to be accomplished, namely, the storing of 
sufficient grain to feed the hungry thousands
who will flock to Zion when famine and sick-
ness cover the face of the whole land.
	This work seems an arduous one for the s[-]s-
ters; and many brethren have remarked that
it was too hard, but no complaints have ever
reached us of any discouragement on account
of its laboriousness, but on the contrary, even
the sisters who have gone into the fields and
gleaned have felt much satisfaction in their 
work, a[-]d have realized the blessing of God
upon their efforts.
	Since the annual meeting in the interest of 
storing grain, held last November, several ex-
cellent storehouses have been built in different
parts of the Territory, some in this city. These
things should be reported, in order that all
facts concerning the work may be known to
those interested, and that others who have not
yet built may have the benefit of the experi-
ence gained in practical work of this kind.
Circumstances have much to do with this work
of building, as well as gathering the grain; in 
some places the sisters' organizations have ac-
cumulated a considerable amount of means,
and have not many poor to support, or to aid
in supporting, consequently they are able to
give more towards storing grain, or helping to
build granaries. All who have read the article
published in WOMAN'S EXPONENT, No. 10,
Vol. 5, October 15, 1876, written by Pres.
Young's wish and reviewed and approved by
him previous to its being published, cannot, it
seems to us, have even a shadow of doubt as to
the necessity of taking hold of this enterprise
energetically. I know many questions are
a[-]ked and remarks made in regard to the expe-
diency of this undertaking, and that if left in 
the hands of women not acquainted with the
nature of grain[-]it is likely to be made a failure

[Column 2]
instead of a success. The sisters generally are
aware that it is a question on which wise coun-
sel is needed, and they refer to Bishops, farm-
e[-]s and business men to ascertain all facts con-
nected with this peculi[-]r, benevolent enter-
prise.
	We have not only Pres. Young's words on 
this subject to refer to, but we have remarks
by Pres. Taylor at the Grain Meeting held in 
Salt Lake City:
	"I consider this gathering and storing of
wheat as of great importance. I am pleased to
find that you have succeeded as well as you
have, yet you have not a great deal, consider-
ing the size of the Territory, but you will in-
crease it. It puts me in mind of the history of 
Joseph; it took seven years in that day to pre-
pare for the seven years that were to come; we
have not got any defined time. * * *
	"If you ladies can help us and succeed in
saving wheat for a case of emergency, it will
be one of the greatest acts of statesmanship
that could occur. We have been careless, we
did not save our wheat as we should have done,
and now if the sisters take heed to these things
the men will find out that their wives have
been wise in doing this thing according  to
counse[-].  The ladies are generally considered
the weaker and more beautiful sex; but if we
all do our part we will be in a position to re-
ceive the blessings of the Lord. I am pleased
to see that our young sisters are taking an ac-
tive part in this work." * * *
	And here are some remarks by Bishop Hunt-
er on the subject at the same time:
	"It is very i[-]portant and highly commend-
able to gather and store wheat. * * *
It has been said that bread is the staff of life.
When we first came to these velleys, and in-
deed since then, we have suffered for the want
of it. We don't want to go through so severe a
trial again, at all times we should have wheat
on hand. The Lord has been mercifnl to us;
He has blessed us with much grain, and much
of it has been wasted. But in the future I
hope we will be more careful of what the Lord
has so abundantly blessed us with. Our Relief
Societies are doing much good; they have
saved much suffering, and have been a great
he[-]p to the B[-]shops.  They have the priesthood
—a portion of priesthood rests upon the sisters.
They are doing a good work, and particularly
the storing of grain is commendable. May
you continue and succeed in your work."
	Our readers will see by these extracts, re-
published from the EXPONENT of Dec. 1877,
that the sisters not only have the advice of
Pres. Young in this connection, but the sanc-
tion of living oracles in this peculiar mission.
It is strange that any Latter-day Saint
should believe the rumors in regard to the 
wheat stored by women having been exported.
It would seem too absurd to talk about, only
when sensible men and women give credence
to such vague reports, it becomes a duty, in
some instances, to contradict them. Is it not
ridiculous to suppose that women who have
plenty to do in their own homes, and have no
occasion whatever (except the desire to do
good) to labor outside, should appropriate the
proceeds of a few bushels of grain, which they
themselves had perhaps gone into the fields and 
helped to gleam?
	"It must be so" is the cry, as some one as-
serted to us to day in reference to a matter that
we knew all about, and they only heard from
"Dame Rumor;" as a humorous poet once
wrote:
		"It must be so, for Thomas Brown Esquire,
		Heard Blab's wife tell the son of Mr. Smith
		Him that was christen'd John after his sire—
		Men often to transmit their names desire—

[Column 3]
		That Higgons sa[-]d, while he was walking with
		That charming lady aged forty,
		'Yclept Miss Catchum, (Higgons was her beau)
		She told him (confidentially) that naughty
		And prattling gossip, Mrs. Wilkins, thought she
		Heard Polly's cousin's sister's aunt say so."
	This is no doubt similar to the origin of the
story of the wheat being mysteriously disposed
of.
	The various Relief Societies and Young La-
dies' Associations will please be prompt in send-
ing in reports of the wheat they have stored.
If granaries have been built in their locality,
or are in the course of erection, let us have
particulars, that all may bo recorded concerning
 this remarkable and interesting enterprise.
The meeting will be held in the Council House
on Saturday, Nov. 15, at two o'clock P. M.
We hope to receive reports from all parts of the
Territory, and that all officers and members of 
Relief Societies adjacent to this city will at-
tend who can make it convenient to do so.
Brethren, especially Bishops, are cordially in-
vited to be present. 
			E.B.Wells


']

Name: 47_dress_fashion_fashions_women
Representative_Docs:
["ADVICE TO LADIES UPON COSTUME

	In no way has civilized man played more
fantastic tricks, and sacrificed his reason
more entirely to folly, than in the matter of
dress. The clumsy and inconvenient gar-
ments of the savage, are attributed to his
ignorance of domestic arts; but what can be
said in excuse for civilized man, when he
wears shoes that project half a yard beyond
his feet, or exchanges his own locks for
a periwig filled with powder and pomatum;
when the graceful motion of a lady’s head is
sacrificed to the stiff movements necessary
in balancing a tower of linen and wire,
half a yard high, with draperies that flow
from the top of it to the floor; when the
wavy lines of a female form are disguised
under a stff circle of whalebone, which im-
prisons the body from the hips upward, and
a buckram cage so surrounds the lower limbs,
that she can with difficulty walk or sit.
Some false standard of beauty, invented
perhaps to conceal some deformity, is set
up, and then the very bones and mucles of
the perfect body must be made to conform
to it. When this is carried so far as it is in
the case of small feet in China, its absurdity
strikes us at once; but we may find, nearer
home, instances of a standard as false, and
consequences even more fatal to health and
happiness than the little feet of the Chinese.
	The history of national costume in the
civilized countries of Europe shows, that for
many centuries the progress of art and
manufactures only led to greater extrava-
gance in dress, and more preposterous
fashions. One enormity was only displaced
to make way for the opposite extreme, as
in the case of the peaked-toed shoes already
mentioned; these were followed by shoes of
only the length of the foot, but as broad as
they were long. At one time men’s coats
were so short that they resembled boy’s
jackets of the present day, and soon after
they were so long and full that they looked
like female attire. Women's sleeves were
sometimes made so long that they were tied
in knots, to prevent the wearer from tread-
ing on them, and nine yards of cloth was a
moderate quantity for each sleeve; and then
they were made as tight as skin, and
reached no father than the elbow. It would
seem that for centuries the whole ingenuity
of a nation was taxed to invent monstrous
forms of clothing, as well as inconvenient
and useless appendages, and that comfort
and ease were the things most of all to be
avoided in dress.
	After the general fashion of hoops had
passed away, and when they were gazed
after, on gala days only, as a raree-show, it
was difficult to imagine rational beings
reconciled to them, and considering them
an indispensible part of female attire, or to
believe that they were once so universal in
London, that maid-servants were seen
washing down the door-steps in hoop--petti-
coats. Yet such was actually the case before
they were left off by the higher classes.
	This reflection upon past fashions should
make us look with jealous eyes on those
which prevail in our day, in order to dis-
cover whether we are not in the practice of
something which will appear equally absurd
to future generations. The French nation
has long been the arbiter of fashion for most
civilized countries and the political revolu-
tion of the last century was accompanied by
a revolution in dress almost as signal; for it
banished wigs and buckles, powder and
pomatum, stiff stays and full petticoats,
long waists, and high-heeled shoes. One
extreme led to another, and the ladies who
had been encased in whalebone, bukrum.
and abundance of quilted petticoats, stepped
forth as Grecian goddesses, without any
corsets, any petticoats, any fulness to their
garments, or any heels to their shoes.
White muslin dresses of the scantiest
dimensions, drawn closely round the figure,
with the shortest possible waists, and not a
fold or a plait that could form any drapery,
were the order of the day in France and
quickly spread into England. Only look
at the portraits in the historical pictures of
that period—such for instance, as the coro-
nation of Napoleon—and you will be so
shocked at the tight sleeves, short waists, and
narrow skirts of the women, that it will be
difficult to believe that such a style of dress
ever looked well.
	The influence of fashion is so strong in
corrupting the eye, and perverting the taste,
that it has led some persons to doubt the
existance of any true standard of beauty as
applicable to costume; but as long as some
forms of dress, when out of fashion, look
better to us than others, we may fairly con-
clude that there are some immutable princi-
ples of taste connected with the subject,
and that those articles which we admire,
after they have ceased to be the reigning
mode, conform in some degree to true taste.
Such for instance, is the simple cap called
after Mary Queen of Scots. When the pre-
vailing fashions are most opposed to the flat
shape of the Mary-Queen-of-Scots cap, it still
appears to us beautiful; and when adopted
by a modern fine lady, as her costume in a
portrait, or her dress at a masquerade ball, it
is pronounced to be highly becoming. Now,
this is not the case with the monstrous
constructions of gauze, ribbon, and wire,
that was called caps fifty years ago; nor with
any of those head-dresses, which outrage
common sense, and set all proportion at de-
fiance. As soon as the enormous horse-hair
cushions, over which the locks were combed
and plastered with powder and pomatum,
went out of fashion, that style of head-dress
was condemned as hideous.
	Now, if there are principles of true taste
involved in the mysteries of a lady’s toilet,
is not the study of them worthy of a refined
and intellectual being; and would not her
time and thoughts be better spent in con-
forming her style of dress to them, than in
eagerly following every change of fashion
dictated by the love of novelty, apart from
real beauty?—“English Paper.”

	Paint.—To get rid of the smell of oil
paint, plunge a handful of hay into a pail
full of water, and let it stand in the room
newly painted.


WOMAN'S EXPONENT


", 'THE LITTLE HEALTH OF LADIES.

BY FRANCES POWER COBB.

*	*	*	*	*	*	*	*	*

	We have now touched on the subject of
dress, which plays such an important part
in the health of women that it must here
be treated somewhat at length. A little
girl in a London Sunday School, being ask-
ed by a visitor "why God made the flowers
of the field?" replied (not unconscious of
the gorgeous paper poppy in her own bon-
net), "Please, ma'am, I suppose for patterns
for artificial flowers." One might antici-
pate some answer scarcely less wide of the
mark than that of this sophisticated little
damsel, were the question to be put to not
a few grown women, "Why do you wear
clothes?" Their most natural response
would obviously be, "To be in the fashion."
When we have visibly wandered a long
way from the path of reason, the best thing
we can do is to look back to the starting
point, and find out, if possible, where we
have diverged. In the matter of raiment
that starting-point is not hard to find—in-
deed, to mark it is only to state a series of
truisms.
	Human clothing has three "raisons

[Column 2]
d'etre," which, in order of precedence, are
these:
	I. HEALTH.
	II. DECENCY.
	III. BEAUTY.
	HEALTH demands—
	1. Maintenance of proper temperature of
the body by exclusion of excessive heat
and cold.
	2. Protection from injury by rain, snow,
dust, dirt, stones to the feet, insects, etc.
	3. Preservation of liberty of action to all
the organs of the body, and freedom from
pressure.
	DECENCY demands—
	4. Concealment of some portions of the
human frame.
	5. Distinction between the habiliments of
men and women, sufficient to avert mis-
take.
	6. Fitness for the age and character of
the wearer.
	7. Concealment, when possible, of any
disgusting personal defect.
	BEAUTY demands—
	8. Truthfulness. The dress must be gen-
uine throughout, without any false pads,
false hair, or false anything.
	9. Graceful forms of drapery.
	10. Harmonious colors.
	11. Such moderate consistency with pre-
vailing modes of dress as shall produce the
impression of sociability and sauvity, and
avoid that of self-assertion.
	12. Individuality: the dress suiting the
wearer as if it were an outer body belong-
ing to the same soul.
	Glancing back over the above table, we
find this curious fact: The dress of men in
all Western nations meets fairly all the con-
ditions of health and decency, and fails on-
ly on the side of beauty. The dress of wo-
men, on the contrary, ever variable as it is,
persistently misses the conditions of health;
frequently violates the rules of decency;
instead of securing beauty, at which it aims
first instead of last, achieves, usually ugli-
ness.
	It is to be remembered for our consola-
tion and encouragement that men have ar-
rived at their present good sense in dress
only within two or three generations. A
hundred years ago the lords of creation set
beauty above health or convenience, just
as the ladies do now, and peacocked about
in their peach-blossom coats and embroid-
ered waistcoats, surmounted by wigs, for
whose stupendous discomfort even a seat
on the judicial bench can scarcely reconcile
the modern Englishman. Now, when the
men of every European nation have abjur-
ed such fantastic apparel, we naturally ask:
Why have not the women followed their
example? Why is the husband, father
and brother, habited like a being who has
serious interests in life, and knows that his
personal dignity would be forfeited were
he to dress himself in party-colored, be-rib-
boned garments, and why is the wife,
mother, or sister, bedizened like a macaw,
challenging every observer to note how
much of her time, thoughts and money,
must have been spent on this futile object?
The answer is one which is not pleasant to
make, discreditable as it is to both sexes.
The women who set the fashions dress for
admiration; and men like women who dress
to be admired; and the admiration given
and received is a very poor and unworthy
admiration, not much better than a salmon
gives to a glittering artificial fly, and hav-
ing very little more to do with any real
aesthetic gratification—as is proved too
clearly by the thoroughly unbeautiful de-
vices to which fashion has recourse. It is
the well-got up woman (to borrow a very

[Column 3]
expressive phrase,) not the really well-
dressed woman, who receives by far the
greatest share of homage.
	And now let us see how all this concerns
the health of women—how much their
"petite sante" is due to their general neg-
lect to make health the first object of dress,
or even an object at all compared to fashion.
	Tight-lacing among habits resembles envy
among the passions. We take pride in all
the rest, even the idlest and worst, but
tight-lacing and an envious heart are
things to which no one ever confesses. A
small waist, I suppose, is understood to be-
long to that order of virtues which Aris-
totle decides ought to be natural and not
acquired, and the most miserable girl who
spends her days in a machine more cruel
(because more slowly murderous) than the
old "Maiden" of Seville, yet always a[-]sures
us, smiling through her martyrdom, that
her clothes are "really hanging about her!"
It would be waste of time to dwell on this
supreme folly. Mrs. Haweis, in her very
noteworthy new book, "The Art of Beauty,"
has given some exceedingly useful dia-
grams, showing the effects of the practice
on the internal organs and skeleton—dia-
grams which I earnestly recommend to the
study of ladies who may feel a "call." to
perform this sort of English suttee for a 
living husband. Mrs. Haweis says that
sensible men do not love wasps, and have
expressed to her their "overallishness"
when they behold them. Considering how
effectively they have hitherto managed to
display their disapproval whenever women
have attempted to introduce rational at-
tire, it is a pity, I think, that they do not 
"pronounce" a little more distinctly against
this literally moral folly.
	I have already alluded to the brain-heat-
ing chignons, just gone out of fashion after
a long reign of mischief; and along with
them should be classed the bonnets which
expose the forehead to the cold, while the
back of the head is stewed under its cush-
ion of false hair, and which have the still
more serious disadvantage of affording no
shelter to the eyes. To women to whom
the glare of the sun is permanently hurt-
ful to the sight, the necessity of wearing
these bonnets on pain of appearing singu-
lar, or affectedly youthful, constitutes al-
most a valid reason against living in Lon-
don. And the remedy, forsooth, is to hold
up perpetually a parasol!—a yet further in-
cumbrance to add to the care of the drag-
gling train, so that both arms may be occu-
pied during a whole walk, and of course
all natural case of motion rendered impos-
sible. In this, as in a dozen other silly
fashions, the women who have serious con-
cerns in life are hampered by the practice
of those who think of nothing but exhibit-
ing their persons. And ladies of limited
fortune, who live in small rooms and go
about the streets on foot or in cabs, are com-
pelled (if they wish to avoid being pointed
at) to adopt modes of dress whose sole
"raison d'etre" is that they suit wealthy
"grandes dame" who lounge in their ba-
rouches or display their trains over the
carpets of forty-feet-long drawing-rooms.
What snobbery all this implies in our whole
social structure! Some ten millions of wo-
men dress, as nearly as they can afford, in
the style fit at the most for five thousand!
	The practice of wearing "decollettee"
dresses, sinning equally as it does against
health and decency, seems to be gradually
receding—from ordinary dinners, where it
was universal twenty years ago, to special
occasions, balls, and court drawing-rooms.
But it dies hard, and may kill a good many
poor creatures yet, and entail on others the


life long bad health so naturally resulting
from the exposure of a large surface of the
skin to sudden chills.
	TO BE CONTINUED.
—Cotemporary Review.


WOMAN'S EXPONENT.


', 'LADIES UPON DRESS.

	The rich dress and costly ornaments that
become maturer life, and ceremonious par-
ties in large cities, are unsuited to the very
young, who need no such "foreign aid."
Some persons toil early and late and strain
every nerve, to procure an expensive gar-
ment, and think, that once arrayed in it,
they will look as well as some richer neigh-
bor, whose style of dress they wish to im-
itate; but they forget, that if it does not ac-
cord with their general style of living, if it
is out of harmony with other things, it will
so strike everybody, and this want of fit-
ness will prevent it looking well on them.
Let a true sense of propriety of the fitness
of things regulate all your habits of living
and dressing; and it will produce such a
beautiful harmony and consistency of char-
acter, as will throw a charm around you
that all will feel, though few may compre-
hend. Always consider well whether the
articles of dress which you wish to pur-
chase are suited to your age, your condi-
tion, your means; the climate, to the par-
ticular use to which you mean to put them;
and then let the principles of good taste
keep you from the extremes of the fashion,
and regulate the form, so as to combine util-
ity and beauty, while the known rules of
harmony in colors save you from shocking
the eye of the artist with incongruous mix-
tures.
	The agreeable effect that all wish to pro-
duce in the eyes of others, depends much
more on just, proportions in the parts, thus
forming a pleasing whole, than on little or-
namental additions, which, though pretty
in themselves, add nothing to the general
effect. Thus in making a pelisse, it is all-
important that the cape should be of the
right size and shape, to agree well with the
sleeves and back, and that the collar should
be well proportioned to the cape; but it mat-
ters very little whether it have one or two
rouleaus of satin or velvet around it, or
whether it have none; and so it is with a
thousand other little particulars, which take
much time in the making and add much to
the expense, without really adding any-
thing to the general good appearance. It is
a happy thing for the diffusion of good
taste, that it may, in many things, be cul-
tivated without any pecuniary expense. The


[COLUMN 1]

price of colored muslin or printed calico, is
the same whether the figure be pretty or
ugly; the ribbon that is tastefully disposed
upon the bonnet, costs no more than the
one that is ill arranged. The shawl is the
same in value whether it is dragged round
the shoulders like an Indian's blanket, or
worn in graceful folds. It costs no more to
buy colors that harmonize than those that
do not. Indeed, true taste will generally
be found on the side of economy, because
simplicity is;—the first cost is thus lessen-
ed, and garments that are really well shap-
ed, are longer in favor. The exercise of
good taste, therefore, need not be consider-
ed the privilege of the few; it may regulate
the toilet of her who earns what she ex-
pends upon it, as well as for her whose bills
are paid by a rich father; the more it is
studies the more good sense and simplicity
will be consulted, because these are includ-
ed in the principles of true taste.
	Whilst taste may be made to regulate the
dress of all, even of the working classes,
wealth, unaccompanied by it, cannot com-
mand its beautiful results. Your clothes
may be ordered of the best dressmaker, and
everything you wear may be of the most
costly materials, and in the latest Paris
fashion; and yet you may spoil your appear-
ance by your manner of putting them on,
and by wearing those articles together
which do not accord. The most expensive
dresses at a ball are seldom those that pro-
duce the best effect; and nothing so effect-
ually defeats its object, as an excess of or-
nament. Some persons seem to have an
inherent love of finery, and seem to cling
to it pertinaciously, even when their under-
standings are convinced that it is repug-
nant to the feelings of refined minds, and
that it is a trait common to all barbarous
tribes: they cannot reason upon their pref-
erences—they can only say, that what oth-
ers condemn as tawdry, looks pretty to
them. This persuasion generally takes
place very early, and is much to be regret-
ed, as it prevents the growth of purer prin-
ciples. We have often thought that the
very bad taste in which dolls are usually
dressed, may have something to do with
this early love of finery. Children have of-
ten a real affection for their puppets; and
when they are bedizened in all the colours
of the rainbow, and decked in all the odds
and ends of finery that can be stuck upon
them, the little dears learn, by this associa-
tion, to love this tawdry ornament: whereas
a well-dressed doll would have an import-
ant influence in establishing a correct taste
in the mind of the child. We once knew a
family where the dolls were all very neatly
dressed, like babies and little children, and
not as fine ladies, on purpose to make them
a more rational and useful source of amuse-
ment; and we would beg all young ladies
who dress dolls for little girls, to do it in
such a manner as not to foster a love of fin-
ery.
	We have heard a mother who guarded
her daughters against this bad taste by mak-
ing it one of their childish punishments to
weak a very tawdry cap full of feathers, and
flowers, and bows of ribbons of all colours.
Judging by what we sometimes see worn
by grown up people, we might suppose that
such a cap had been their reward in child-
hood rather than their punishment, and was
thus recommended to their best affections.
The love of finery is rarely cured, and forms
an insurmountable obstacle to the cultiva-
tion of pure taste. Whosoever is conscious
of possessing it, ought to mistrust her own
judgment in matters of taste, and be will-
ing to take the advice of others. No plain-

[COLUMN 2]

ness of dress can ever be construed to your
disadvantage; but ornamental additions,
which in their best state are a very doubt-
ful good, become a positive evil when de-
faced, or soiled, or tumbled. Shabby feath-
ers, and crushed or faded artificial flowers,
are an absolute disgrace to a lady's appear-
ance; while their total absence would never
be remarked. Next to soiled stockings,
soiled bonnet-caps are the most offensive
things in a young lady's dress; and the lat-
ter are but too common. Whatever ap-
proaches the face should be particularly
clean; and it is better always to wear those
materials which will wash than to use silk
net after it is at all sullied by wear. Color-
ed gauze hankerchiefs, that are worn till
they are faded, or sullied, or the color
changed by perspiration, are a disagreeable
sight, and will spoil the neat appearance of
a person, however well dressed otherwise;
cleanliness being the first requisite in a
lady's dress.
	Such qualities of mind are brought into
action in connection with dress that we
cannot wonder at its having great influence
on the opinions that are formed of us. Who,
that sees a young lady very carefully array-
ed at a ball, and finds her when at home,
and not expecting company, in a torn or
soiled dress, can fail to draw conclusions un-
favorable to the character of the individual?
If you have a friend who has lately be-
come insolvent, and you see his daughters
appear, soon after, in new and costly gar-
ments, what are your feelings? Is not all
admiration of their costume lost, in the
sense of its betraying a want of common
honesty, thus to spend the money that be-
longs to their father's creditors? And does
not this exhibition of lax principles do
them more harm than any personal decora-
tion can do them good? Dress is a very fair
index of a young woman's neatness, indus-
try, economy, good sense, modesty, and
good taste; and she, who is at all times, in
her private as well as in her public hours,
perfectly well dressed, according to all that
we include in that term, must have many
of the substantial qualities that constitute a
good character and are essential to domes-
tic comfort. The character is much more
shown in the style of dress that is worn ev-
ery day, than in that which is designed for
great occasions; and when we see a young
girl come down to the family breakfast
in an untidy wrapper, with her hair in pa-
pers and her feet slip-shod, and an old silk
handkerchief round her neck, we know
that she cannot be the neat, industrious,
and refined person whom we would like for
an inmate. We feel equally certain too,
that her chamber is not kept in good order,
and that she does not set a proper value up-
on time. However well a lady has appear-
ed at a party, we would recommend to a
young gentleman, before he makes up his
mind as to her domestic qualities, to ob-
serve her appearance at the breakfast-table,
when she expects to see only her own fam-
ily; and, if it be such as we have just de-
scribed, to beware how he prosecutes the
acquaintance.
	To begin the day well it is necessary to
rise early enough to perform all the ablu-
tions necessary to health and cleanliness,
and to be neatly and completely dressed be-
fore breakfast. Your morning dress should
always be such that you would not be
ashamed to be seen in, by any accidental
visitor; and a clean muslin collar, or ruffie,
of the plainest materials, give an impress-
ion of far greater neatness than a colored
handkerchief. If you are suitably dressed
or the business of the morning, you are

[COLUMN 3]

ready to enter upon it at once and much
time is saved; you are not discomposed by
unexpected guests; and, if particularly en-
gaged, you can wear the same dress all day.
Very yonng girls are apt to think that the
most important item in their dress is the
material of which their frocks are made,
whereas that is really of the least conse-
quence. Clean stockings, neat shoes and
strings, smooth, well-brushed hair, and
delicately clean hands, nails, and teeth,
would make them look more lady-like, and
better dressed, in a cheap calico, than they
would be in the finest merino, or most costly
French silk without these accompaniments.
Those things which are most essential to a
neat appearance, are most within the reach
of everybody, and therefore the neglect of
them is not to be excused. Everybody can
mend stockings and gloves, however old
they may be; everybody can avoid break-
ing shoe-strings by wearing them too tight,
or having them become untied, by tying
them in false knots. Clean hands and nails,
and well-brushed hair and teeth, it is with-
in everybody's power to possess; and with-
out constant attention to these particulars,
the most expensive garments will fail to
produce the effect you desire. The same
honesty and self-respect which would keep
you from making a saving that you are
ashamed of, should prevent you wearing
anything, even out of sight, that you would
be ashamed to have seen, if sudden indisposi-
tion caused it to be exposed before stran-
gers. We should gladly adopt every con-
trivance for saving time and abridging the
labour necessarily bestowed upon dress.
Those who know how to use the needle af-
ter the best fashion of our grandmothers'
days, may next acquire the art of slighting
certain parts of their work, where it will
not interfere with durability, and so save
some thousand superfluous stitches in a
week. If it has been the misfortune of
any of my readers to have grown up with-
out being made good needlewomen, the
sooner they undertake to supply this defi-
ciency the better. A woman who does not
know how to sew is as deficient in her ed-
