ucation as a man who cannot write. Let
her condition in life be what it may, she
cannot be ignorant of the use of her needle,
without incommoding herself and others,
and without neglecting some important du-
ties. Besides this, there is, in this truly
feminine employment, a moral power which
is useful to the sex. There is a soothing
and sedative effect in needle-work; it com-
poses the nerves, and furnishes a corrective
for many of the little irritations of domes-
tic life. Let no woman think herself ex-
empt from the duty of "plying the polish-
ed shaft." In every situation of life, she
will find herself the better for an early and
thorough acquaintance with plain sewing.
	SELECTED.


WOMAN'S EXPONENT.		39


']

Name: 48_facts figures_figures_acres_facts
Representative_Docs:
['﻿FACTS AND FIGURES. 
				
	OXFORD Univerity celebrates its 1000th
anniversary this year.
	THE new Freshman class of Harvard
already number 191.
	THE Free Traders of England propose an
international conference.
	THE Boston Gas Co. has a well 1,100 feet
deep without striking water.
	MARYLAND cotton factories consume
30,000 bales of cotton per week.
	THE population of Paris is said to have
decreased 500,000 in the past six years.
	THE colored voters of the country are
said to number 900,000, and the politicians
are after them.	
	MORE people have been killed by light-
ning in England this summer than has ever
been known before.
	FALL RIVER, Mass., has now in opera-
tion thirty mills, containing 1,100,000
spindles, representing $25,000,000 capital,
and employing 15,000 operatives.
	THE year 1816 was the coldest ever
known in New England. There was no
summer, and ice formed in June, July and
August.	
	THE New Haven undertaker had recently
twenty-seven dead children to care for in
one day, the deaths being caused mainly
by cholera infantum.
	THE new Catholic cathedral at Boston is
approaching completion, and, when finished,
its tower will rival the State, House as a
prominent landmark for people approach-
ing Boston.
	THE new armory, or city prison of Chi-
cago, will cost about $40,000, and will be
80x100 feet in size, two stories high. The
lockup department will contain twenty-four
cells, each 7x12.
	A MAN in Jamaica, Vt., within the last
six years has had born to him by his first
wife a pair of twin daughters, she dying,
and by a second wife one pair of twin boys
and another pair of twin daughters—being
three pair of twins by two mothers.
	THE cost of living in the larger cities in
this country is yearly increasing, but this
is also true of the cities of the Old World.
One thousand dollars in London at the pres-
ent time will purchase no more than seven
hundred dollars ten years ago.
	IT HAS been proved by repeated experi-
ments that it is possible to rule lines with a
diamond point on glass so close together
that upwards of 60,000 of them are com-
prised in the space of one inch. The appar-
ent limit of vision in the best microscope
does not, however, reach beyond lines sep-
arated one eighty-thousandth of an inch.
	CAPT. Hervey’s experiment with Chinese
workmen in his laundry at Belleville, N. J.,
is so far successful that another troop has
been sent for, and a new building for their
accommodation is in process of construction.
All animosity between the Irish girls in the
laundry and the “haythens” appears to have
subsided, and they get along very amicably
together.
	NARROW-GAUGE railroads, according to a
paper recently read before the New York
society of engineers, have an advantage
over broad-gauge roads of 25.48 per cent, in
road bed and road construction. It was also
asserted that there is a saving of 5 per cent.
in the cost of stations, of 28 per cent. in
the cost of car shops, and 21.07 per cent. on
the cost of rolling stock. The average sav-
ing on all items, it was stated, would be
24.03 per cent.


', 'FACTS AND FIGURES.
	It is estimated that the population of New
Orleans is 239,985.
	Miss Hannah Drury has bought a 2220 acre
plantation in North Carolina.
	A 299-year old newspaper of Stockholm,
Sweden, circulates 15,000 copies daily.
	Many negroes who left Georgia for Missis-
sippi are returning to their old homes.
	"John Smith" of Chicago has sent $600
to the United States Treasurer's Conscience
Fund.
	According to an Ohio mathematician, one
man dies from the use of alcohol every sev-
en minutes.
[Column 2]
	A club of farmers in Chesterfield, Ill., takes
$100 worth of magazines and newspapers
annually.
	A New Jersey man claims a clear profit
on seventy chickens and nineteen ducks,
last year, of $77.
	Five districts in Chester County, Pennsyl-
vania, have elected women to the office of
school directors.
	A tract of land in Missouri containing 40,
000 acres has been bought for a party of
French emigrants.
	There is trouble in the Kansas Agricul-
tural College. Three professors have been
retired by the Board of Regents. The stu-
dents are indignant.
	Minnesota is losing the Scandinavian em-
migration, which is drifting to Kansas, Ne-
braska and Colorado.
	President Grant recommends liberal ap-
propriations by congress of the Philadelphia
Centennial Exposition.
	Upward of 2,500 men have deserted from
the army while stationed in the Pacific States
and Territories since 1871.
	A Mississippi legislator introduced a bill
prohibiting the sale of liquor within five
miles of the State University.
	Mr. Ruskin advocates the purchase by the
National Gallery of a splendid Madonna by
Raphael. The cost will be £40,000.
	Mrs. Sarah E. Beard, Fayetteville, N. Y.,
has given $5000 to Hamilton College as a
fund for the aid of needy students.
	The proudest man in the United States is
in Montgomery, Pa. He carried off 42 pre-
miums at the Doylston Poultry Show.
	Yale College is to have a new chapel, a
plain, but substantial stone structure, seat-
ing 1,200 persons, and costing $120,000.
	A movement to celebrate the Centennial
by a grand exposition in Washington, to be
confined to the United States, is talked of.
	Mr. Gerrett Smith has given, free of con-
ditions, the sum of $10,000 for the benefit of
Hamilton College, of which he is a graduate.
	The Queen, at the recommendation of
Gladstone, has granted a pension of $1,000
per annum to the children of Dr. Living-
stone.
	The statue of Mr. Seward will be shipped
to New York in the spring of 1875. It will
cost $25,000 nearly all of which has been
subscribed.
	Some of the New Hampshire Patrons of
Husbandry are buying flour at $8.25 per bbl.
by the car-load, whereas they had to pay $11
per bbl. for the same article at retail.
	Robert W. Scott of Kentucky states that
he has sold the wool of Angora goats through
the agency of several New York commission
merchants at from 60c. to $1.25 per pound.
	At a recent exhibition in Zanesville, Ohio,
a wedding-dress worn in 1788 was exhibited.
The waist was about two inches long, but
what astonished the ladies most was that
the dress contained only two and a half
yards of silk.


﻿WOMAN’S EXPONENT


', '﻿FACTS AND FIGURES.
			—
  THE Wisconsin tobacco crop for 1872 is
estimated at 10,000,000 pounds.
  THE Odd Fellows of Indiana, in even
numbers, are 20,000 strong.
  THE biggest sale of real estate ever made
in Minnesota was that of 31,500 acres in
Todd and Otter Tail counties sold the other
day to a London firm for $80,000.
   KOOPMANSCHAP has gone to wreck at
last, on the Mongolian wave, with liabilities
of $260,000 and any quantity of assets in
Chinamen’s notes, quotable at a cent a pound
for old paper.
  IN 1871 the iron mills of Illinois rolled
40,026 tons of rails, iucluding 2,800 of sheet
rails, and rounded 51,152 tons, making a to-
tal production of 91,178 tons of railroad bars,
which places Illinois next to Pennsylvania
in the manufacture of rails.
   FRANZ ABT works a whole year in Ger-
many for about $1,500, while in this coun-
try twice that sum is lavished in his enter-
tainment almost nightly.  America must be
to him the Eldorado of his dream days.
  THE total membership of the Orthodox
Quakers at present in the United States is
57,105, being an increase of 715 in 20 years.
   AT THE Methodist General Conference it
was stated the other day that the church
has $70,000,000 of property, and that before
long it will amount to $100,000,000.
  THERE are 215 cadets at the Naval Acad-
emy, 39 of whom were appointed by the
President, four are naval apprentices, three
Japanese students, one is from the District
of Columbia, and 169 from the various
States.
  THE American Printworks at Fall River,
Mass., are arranging to suspend operations
for a season on account of the price of goods.
The amount of business done at the works,
requires $2,100 a day to carry it on, and
nearly $3,000,000 worth of cloth is in process
of printing from the time it goes into the
mill before it is finished.
   THERE are over 650,000 scholars in atten-
dance at the public schools in Illinois.
Teachers number over 20,000. School
houses, log, frame, brick and stone, 10,773.
Expenditures for school purposes for the
past year, $7,000,000.
   LEXINGTON, Ky., has nine hemp facto-
ries, employing an average of about sixty
hands each. The average amount of hemp
consumed per day the year round by these
firms, is from twenty to thirty tons, necessi-
tating the planting by the farmers to supply
this demand of about 30,000 acres of the
best land.
   THE following are the dimensions of the
great bridge now being erected between
New York and Brooklyn: Total length,
5,682 feet; length of central span, 1,600 ;
elevation above high water, 130 ; Width of
bridge floor, 80 feet; height of towers above
high water, 268 feet; base of towers at wa-
ter line, 121 by 56 feet.
  THE Vulcan Iron Works, Carondolet, Mo.,
employs 800 men in relays of five hours
each. The furnaces are turning out from
seventy to eighty tons of pig iron daily.
One of them is making pig iron directly
from pure. Iron Mountain (Mo.) ore, to fill a
contract with the Bessemer Steel Works at
Chicago. The rail mill is making forty tons
per day. The largest vertical, direct-acting,
blowing engine west of Pittsburg is here ;
its steam cylinder has a diameter of five feet
and stroke of nine feet; the blowing cylin-
der, a diameter of ten feet and stroke of nine
feet. Its capacity is 20,000 cubic feet of air
per minute.


WOMAN’S EXPONENT.


﻿The ladies of the Congregational Society
of San Mateo, realized from their “Mother
Goose party” over $300.


﻿Ohio has enacted by law that eighty-
eight cubic inches of bituminous coal shall
weigh just sixty pounds avoirdupois.


A Connecticut company is putting up a
factory for the building of carriages made
entirely of India rubber, excepting the axles
and tires. A decided superiority is claimed
for the material over wood.


﻿In 1787 the Emperor of China issued the
following decree: “All persons of the female
sex are forbidden, upon any pretext what-
ever, to enter a temple or quit their houses,
except in cases of absolute necessity.
Fathers, husbands, sons or relatives are
commanded to keep them at home on pain
of being themselves severely punished.”
What a believer in equal rights! The men,
most likely, had as hard a time of it as the
women.


The Massachusetts Labor Union Conven-
tion met in Boston on the 28th ult. Wendell
Phillips offered a series of resolutions, which
were adopted after a long debate, approving
all labor associations, announcing the belief,
expediency and necessity of strikes, but
denouncing violence; advocating congres-
sional establishment of labor unions; declar-
ing that all dividend paying companies
should be obliged to divide half of the net
profits among employes, and that imported
pauper contract work should be dutyable.

Name: 49_law_mormons_mormon_people
Representative_Docs:
['SENATOR CALL'S SPEECH.

	October 2nd, 1888, Senator Call, of Florida,
made the following speech upon the appropria-
tion of $80,000 for the Industrial Home in
this city, which is printed as reported in the 
Congressional Record:

	Mr. Call—I voted for this appropriation, not
only as a member of the Committee on Educa-
tion and Labor when it was first considered,
but also as a member of the Committee on Ap-
propriations and of the Senate. I objected 
to and would now object to and vote against
the legislation of this body persecuting these
people because they had certain religious views
[Column 2]
or views on the subject of religion which were
opposed to my own and to those of our people 
generally. I consider that in all that legisla-
tion we not only violated the Constitution, but 
we violated all the principles of civil liberty
upon which our institutions are founded. I 
have never changed that opinion. I think we 
went back eighteen hundred years in the pro-
gress of the world to the days of barbarism, to
use the courts, the civil power of the State, to 
compel by force a change in the opinions of 
men upon the subject of faith or of their reli-
gion.
	The Constitution says we shall take no action
whatever, exercise no power of government re-
specting the establishment of religion, and yet
the language of this bill is plain and direct,
offering inducements to leave the Mormon
Church. We have forgotten even that we 
have no power to legislate upon the subject of
a man's belief on the subject of religion,
whether it be true or untrue, and so in all this
legislation in which we have prepared a preju-
diced jury, in which we have prepared a preju-
diced judge, and selected him for the purpose 
of trying men of different opinions on the 
subject of religion from himself, we have viola-
ted not only the principles of the Constitution,
but we have violated all the institutions upon
which our present civilization is founded, and
we only need to go a few steps further to intro-
duce the power of the state to punish in direct 
terms a man for being a Methodist, or a 
Catholic, or a Presbyterian, or for holding
some different form of faith from that which
that dominant party in the state may profess.
	But, Mr. President, I approve the principle
of the legislation in this appropriation. If 
the Mormons are to be converted, or any other
set of people, it is to be in accordance with 
the divine religion of Christ, by persuasive
methods, and not by the exercise of power.
The legislation of this body has done more to 
perpetuate polygamy and establish it by mak-
ing martyrs of them than any and all other
causes and instrumentalities that could have
been brought together. 
	We live in an age in which the principles of 
Christian tolerance, the teachings of Christ
that the sword is not the power to create
opinion, to produce conviction, but it is argu-
ment, it is the preaching of the divine word, it
is the missionary, and not the power of the 
sword, and the power of the civil law. The
law by which these courts are established and
vested with unusual powers cannot be distin-
guished from the exercise of the power of the 
state in creating the Spanish Inquisition as an
arm of the civil power to compel observance
of a particular faith. The two are precisely
the same, and no argument can distinguish be-
tween the legislation of Congress in respect to 
the Mormons and the exercise of the civil 
power of the state in the shape of the Inquisi-
tion to suppress another religion or faith, either 
by delegating it to an eccleiastical body or 
exercising it directly.
	The constructions of law under which these
people have been tried and punished, if cor-
rectly reported, are a lasting shame and dis-
grace to our country and to the administration
of the law, and to ourselves for permitting it to 
continue. I have seen it stated by persons
said to be reputable in character and truthful,
that men and women have been convicted and
sentenced for unlawful cohabitation upon 
proof alone that they had contributed to the 
support of the families, the women and children 
toward whom they had at some former period
of time sustained the polygamous relation,
and that in other cases women had been sen-
tenced and imprisoned because they thought it
proper to refuse to disclose the name of the 
man towards whom they had sustained these
relations.
	But this legislation providing a home for the
[Column 3]
people who may be convinced that they are 
pursuing a wrong method of life and a criminal 
one, of providing a home for the women and 
children who may be left destitute in conse-
quence of a change of life, met the approval
of my judgment. It was in the line of per-
suasion, in the line of the only successful
policy, of the policy convincing the moral
sentiment which the Almighty has provided as 
the means of reformation for all men every-
where. It cannot be that you can change 
opinion, thought, by power, by oppression, by 
persecution, by the sword; by compelling men
to believe. This is the method of the stake,
the iron boot, the fearful implements of the 
cruelty and crime of the Dark Ages, and 
these we adopted in place of the gentle persua-
sion of the divine Christ.
	Mr. President, if the statements of the Sena-
tor from Main are true, it is the severest
arraignment of this body that could possibly
be made. Can it be that the Senate of the
United States and the House of Representa-
tives have sat here and allowed such a condi-
tion of things to exist as the instances de-
scribed by him represent? Is it true that peo-
ple convinced of the error of their lives and 
subjected to extreme cruelty, have been com-
pelled to remain in that condition for the want 
of even the ordinary necessaries of life? Is it 
true that children of twelve years of age are
 compelled to enter into polygamous and unlaw-
ful marriages, and the Senate and Senators
from Maine and Vermont have quietly re-
mained here providing no legislation but these 
denunciations of the Mormon people? If 
these statements be true, the evidence should
be obtained and prompt legislation provided
by which that evil should not exist a day longer
—the evil of a people honestly desiring to con-
form to the laws and yet unable to do it be-
cause the laws have provided no efficient meth-
ods for their protection and their subsistence.
	I am not ready to believe these statements.
I do not believe that polygamy, however
wrongful it is of necessity and in itself, de-
stroys the natural love and affection which the 
Divine Being has implanted in the human
heart, that charity can not exist where it pre-
vails. We derive our religion from a poly-
gamous people. I have been taught to rever-
ence the Scriptures of the Old and New Testa-
ment as a divine revelation and the characters 
they present as the highest types of humanity.
And while we believe with the confidence of 
knowledge that our Christianity is the true and 
higher civilization and the only true religion,
it is not necessary that we should halt and de-
nounce those who differ from us in faith and 
practice. I do not believe that either amongst 
the Mormons or any other people surrounded 
by the influence of modern civilization such 
cruelties would be permitted. 
	There are two sides to these statements; the 
Mormons deny them, their representative denies 
them in a speech delivered in the House of 
Representatives. Travelers and men of high
standing both in our own and foreign countries
profess to have examined carefully into this 
subject, and describe these people as a mild 
and truthful, industrious and virtuous people,
comparing very favorably in many respects
with the people of other countries, save only 
in the permission for polygamous life and re-
presenting that to be limited in practice. I 
do not know anything as to the truth of these
statements either on one side or the other, but
I am not willing to bear false witness against
any one or to condemn without full and fair
evidence on both sides.
	I have never seen a Mormon except those 
who have been here before the two Houses of 
Congress, and it is only just to say that these
had the appearance and manner of intelligent,
educated and kindly men. I have never known 
but one of their faith, and his family life in


[Column 1]
this community was in all respects like our
own, and his charities aud personal character-
istics such as gave him respectful consideration
and regard of excellent persons of our own
Christian faith who are themselves of unblem-
ished life and reputation. I am not willing,
therefore, that my vote for this appropriation
shall be placed upon the ground that these 
people are destitute of all the moral virtues
and all the kindly sympathies of humanity.
	I do not believe it. I vote for this appropria-
tion because I wish to provide every possible
agency to give full and free influence
to the persuasive power of the divine teachings
of Christ, and furnish the opportunity to all
who wish to leave the polygamous life to do so, 
basing the law to be enforced against the prac-
tice of polygamy amongst them as amongst
other people, in the spirit of fairness and
justice, in  gentleness and in mercy, and not
with the cruelty and violence of either the re-
ligious or the political bigot. I have more
faith in the persuasive power of the gospel of
Christ and the Christian ministry than in all
terms of the law, even when enforced with the 
thumb-screw and the stake, the dagger of St.
Bartholmew's massacre and Coligni's assasin,
or all the thousand cruelties which stain the 
pages of history, and which we are beginning
to repeat.
	Mr. President, we had better enact some
legislation in this direction. We had better
ascertain the facts. I am in favor of an ap-
propriation, however large it may be, to pro-
vide a home and substance and employment
for any of these people who may be convinced
that their methods of life are improper and un-
lawful. Opposed as I am to persecution and
disgraceful persecution, if I had the power I
would remove the judges and the civil officers
who have been guilty of crime themselves, if
the statements I have read be true, of a distor-
tion of the law by the severity of the punish-
ment of these peeple. I read an instance in 
the memorial presented in aid of this very 
bill, of a woman and a child, a woman of 
eighteen years of age, I think, with an infant
of three months, confined in the jail, and that
infant dying of measles, because she would not
reveal the name of the man with whom she 
had been led into this improper relation, suffer-
ing and dying, the memorial states, and there
was no charity, no kindness from the judge or
the marshal or any one towards this poor
young mother confined there like a common 
felon, a disgrace to civilization and a disgrace
to humanity and fo those who have the power
to correct it. 
	But, Mr. President, the question here is
whether the public money shall be placed in
private hands with no responsibility except by
a bond for carrying out these purposes. In
the English law it was found necessary to pro-
vide a system of visitation, even of private
foundations of charity. It was found that the
abuses which followed these institutions in
private hands were so great as to pervert and
defeat the purposes of the founder until a
power of visitation was exercised by the courts
and prescribed by the statutes.
	Why, then, should we depart from that
course and vest the power of disbursement of
this money in the hands of these individuals
without any authority on the part of the
Treasury officials to supervise or direct it? It
seems to me that the answer is clear to those
who sincerely desire the conversion of these
people, who desire to furnish protection to 
those who wish to leave, who recognize the
power of the Christian religion (which the
legislation upon this subject denies) to convert
and persuade those people who believe in
Christ and Christ's religion and not in the per-
secutions which this body and the other House
have established against these people. Those
who believe as I do in Christ's commission,

[Column 2]
"Go ye into all nations and preach the Gos-
pel," and who desire the success of this charity
and to protect these people when the power of
the Christian faith may convert them, as it
will convert them, would have the Government 
retain in its control over the expenditure of
this money.
	If you choose to give this building or the
use of it to this charitable organization, I shall
have no objection, and should support a pro-
vision of that kind, provided they exercise
that charity in the spirit of love and not of de-
nunciation, not of hatred and bitter reproach;
provided they remember that the evil of
which they complain is not now for the first
time known to the civilization of the world, 
that the Christian religion has been fighting it
successfully for eighteen hundred years, not
with the sword and the stake, but with the
teachings of Christ and that the religion
of Christ alone has reclaimed hundreds 
of missions of people from the practice
of the greater part of the world who have 
lived in polygamy and with whom it has been
an institution—England and those who derive
their civilization from her.
	Mr. President, there has been too much of
political persecution in this matter, and too
little of Christian faith and Christian practice.
Let the government appropriate its money, let
it build this house, let it make ample provis-
ion for all who shall be convinced that the
Christian system is the true one, and make it
ample for the industrial education and subsist-
ence of these people for a reasonable time,
that this experiment may be promptly tried,
but let it be done by the government and 
under the supervision of bonded officers of the
government, and then, if we choose, give the
management of this charity to these people 
whose purposes, I have no reason to doubt, are
correct, and those of philanthropy and of good
to mankind; but let it be required to be done
in the spirit and according to the precepts of
the religion of Christ.
	*	As far as I am concerned I have no 
particular objection to the provision passing
in the shape in which the Senator from Maine
desires it; but I wish to state that I did not say
there was no precedent for such an appropria-
tion of money. I say it is not a custom of the
government, I say it is not a good, wise public
policy. It has been done in the District of 
Columbia, which is under the immediate super-
vision of Congress. There is some difference 
between this district and a remote Territory,
which has a Territorial government and an au-
tonomy of its own, even though it be subject
to the supervising authority of Congress. But
be it a precedent or not, except in the case of
an established charity, it is a bad precedent to
appropriate a sum of money and put it in the
hands of private individuals for the construc-
tion of buildings for a charity. The plan
should be prescribed by the government.
	Mr. Salisbury, In whom is the title?
	Mr. Call. The title, I understand, is in this
corporation; but it is proposed to place the title
in the government if so desired. Be that as
it may, it is surely not a wise public policy,
without limitations and conditions, to put a
large sum of money in the hands of any num-
ber of persons or any corporation, however
meritorious they may be.
	The point in this matter is that the advance-
ment of this work, this charity, the rescuing
and protection of these poeple, the continuance
of the charitable purposes of this corporation
in its highest possible efficiency, granting all
that is said for it, has nothing whatever to do
with the disbursement of the money. The
mere hands through which this money 
shall pass are immaterial to the pur-
poses of charity. If the building is con-
structed with proper speed and diligence,
and in a proper style and with proper conven-

[Column 3]
iences, that is what the charity demands. It is
not required that the money shall be disbursed 
by A or B or any one else. The singular part
of it is, that it is so insisted, not for the effi-
ciency of the charity, but for the disbursement
of the money by particular individuals, and
the proposition manifestly has no connection
whatever with the purposes of the charity. I
myself am in favor of the charity, and I have 
no great objection to the provision passing in
this shape.


WOMAN’S EXPONENT.


Michigan women voted by thousands at the
school elections at Michigan last week. Many
were elected to office.


', 'COMPLICATED CASES.

	The confiscation of Church property under the
Edmunds law of 1887, has been the cause of more
anxiety, seemingly, to the outside element here in
Utah, than to the interested parties themselves.
The "Church Suits," so called, have been argued
in the Utah Courts, and have been appealed to the
Supreme Court of the United States. Since the
appeal was made, questions have arisen here in
regard to the the compensation of the Receiver
(Marshal Dyer), and the fees of the lawyers em-
ployed in the cases. The parties representing
the interests of the schools of Utah, to which,
according to the provisions of the Edmunds law,
the property thus confiscated will finally be de-
voted, are greatly exercised over the exorbitant
fees and compensation of those who have been
handling, or holding the Church property in trust
until the final adjustment of the case.
	These parties have demanded of Marshal Dyer
the Receiver, an explanation of the whole affair,
and have brought the case before the Commission-
er's court, and it has been laid over to be submit-
ted to the Territorial Court, which meets in
January next. Meantime the Receiver has filed
his answer to Judge Zane, who has charge of the
affair in the interest of schools, and takes issue
with the Judge on all material points named.
What the result of this controversy will be it is
not easy to determine, but it is very clear they are
after the "spoils."
	Now comes Senator Edmunds to the front
again, with a resolution from the judiciary com-
mittee of the Senate of the United States, "call-
ing on the Attorney General for information con-
cerning the execution of the act of March 3, 1887,
in reference to bigamy, and also to report the
names of all United States officers, who had any
connection with such execution, in what capacity
and what compensation had been received by
them. He explained that the act declared for-
feited the property of the Church of Latter-day
Saints; that it was stated, the judges in Utah had
appointed the marshal of that territory, receiver of
[column 2]
said property; that the marshal had employed as
his private and personal counsel in the receiver-
ship the district attorney of the United States;
that some of the property had been sold and a
fund opened; that enormous and exorbitant al-
lowances were demanded for the services of the
receiver and his counsel, and that the school
boards, for whose benefit the property had been
forfeited, had been obliged to employ private
counsel to resist such demands."
	So it would appear that there is to be a real in-
vestigation, if Senator Edmunds' influence can
bring it about, and when judges and lawyers get
hold of the affair, it is likely to be in dispute for
some time, for who shall decide when they dis-
agree? and they are not very likely to agree. The
CASE, however, as far as the Church interests are
concerned, has been advanced in the calendar of
the Supreme Court of the United States, and will
be argued January 14, 1889. Hon. F. S. Richards,
lawyer for the Church, now in Washington in the
interest of the Church cases, will be there
when the case dissolving the corporation of the
Church of Jesus Christ of Latter-day Saints and
confiscating its property, is argued in the highest
tribunal in the land; and also the still more im-
portant question of the powers of Congress to
interfere in matters relating to ecclesiastical or-
ganizations, and to escheat property to the United
States belonging to dissolved corporations instead
of its distribution to the individual owners accord-
ing to established rules of legal precedure."
	The reasons assigned by Mr. Richards why the
court should advance the case are such as should
be understood by all parties interested, and briefly
and clearly explained are as follows:

	First—It is a cause in which the United States
are concerned as a party.
	Second—It is a cause which involves matters of
public interest, very nearly and seriously affecting
a very large portion of the citizens, residents and
property-owners of the Territory of Utah.
	"Third—It is a cause involving the constitution-
ality of certain Acts of the Congress of  the
United States.
	"Fourth—Under the decree appealed from, a
very large amount of property belonging to the
Church of Jesus Christ of Latter-day Saints has
been seized and is now held by a receiver. Much
of said property being of a perishable nature,
great and irreparable loss and damage will ensue
if the property seized and held is left in the hands
of the receiver for the three years or more before
this case could be reached in regular order. If
said receivership is so long continued, the costs
and expenses thereof, and the loss sustained by
reason thereof, will amount to a virtual confisca-
tion of very large amounts of property, and will be
in effect a denial of justice.
	"Fifth—Said cause involves a question of
general public interest to the inhabitants of all the
Territories, because it involves the question of
the power of Congress to control and restrict the
rights of all church corporations, of all kinds and
denominations, in the matter of acquiring and
holding real and personal property, and the power
and right of Congress to dissolve church corpora-
tions and declare the property of such corpora-
tions forfeited and escheated to the United
States.
	"Sixth—Under said decree appealed from, the
United States has seized and now holds by its
receiver possession of several hundreds of thou-
sands of dollars worth of personal property be-
longing to the Church of Jesus Christ of Latter-
day Saints, and claims that the same has become
forfeited and escheated to the United States, with-
out the shadow or semblance of any law or laws
granting any such right or power.
	"Seventh—Under said decree appealed from,
the United States have seized, and now hold by a
receiver, possession of real estate belonging to
he Church of Jesus Christ of Latter-day Saints


[Column 1]
of the value of several hundreds of thousands of 
dollars, and claim the right to forfeit and escheat
said real estate to the United States, notwith-
standing the fact admitted of record, that said real
estate has been possessed, held and used by or in
trust for said Church of Jesus Christ of Latter-
day Saints ever since the year 1848, under the full
power and sanction of the statutes of the Terri-
tory of Utah and the laws of the Congress of the
United States, and nothwithstanding the further
fact that a portion of said real estate so sought to
be escheated to the United States has been for
more than ten years last past, and was at the time
of the passage of said act of Congress of March
3, 1887, held and occupied exclusively for the pur-
poses of a parsonage for said Church.
	"Eighth—There are now pending in the supreme
and district courts of the Territory of Utah seven
other suits which involve the same questions that
arise in this case, and the final determination
of them will depend upon the decision of this
court as to the constitutionality of the aforesaid
acts of Congress, and the other points involved 
herein.
	"For the foregoing and other good and sufficient
reasons appearing of record in this cause, the
appellants pray this honorable court to advance 
the case on the docket and assign it final hearing
at an early day."
	This is a matter of great moment to all the peo-
ple of these United States, for it involves ques-
tions of the utmost importance, and it is "devoutly
to be wished" that the lawyers of the Supreme
Court, who are really the interpreters of all laws
enacted, shall render a decision that will be an
honor to the Supreme bench, and the country
they represent in the most essential manner in the 
interests of justice and equity. It is not so much
the question of dollars and cents, or of property
rights, as it is the rights of individuals to, "life,
liberty and the pursuit of happiness." In this
free and grand Republic it is strange indeed if an
organization such as the Church of Jesus Christ of
Latter-day Saints, must be restrained, or restricted
in the use of means directly devoted to religious
and benevolent purposes, and that has been freely
donated for this purpose by the people. Other 
churches and various denominations of Christians
own large amounts of property, and places of
worship worth immense sums of money, and when
one church can be thus treated and its property
confiscated by the government, it must also follow
naturally that other churches may be similarly de-
prived of the same rights. No doubt the cry 
would be in reference to this subject, that "Mor-
monism" is not religion;—because the popular
religious element of the world is against it, but
who is there that is authorized to declare what the
Gospel is, or what true religion consists in, except
as it compares with the Bible, the sacred word of
God? And when that is done, the Gospel in
which the Latter-day Saints believe and have
accepted, as taught in this day, is an exact type of
the Gospel as preached by the Savior and His
disciples. There are many who have been in-
duced to join in the persecution against this people
and to urge special legislation against them, that
have no correct idea whatever of the doctrines
or the precepte taught. In fact, they want the Ed-
munds law enforced, without knowing what the
law really is. Men and women, strangers in this
city, have expressed their views in this office on
this very subject, and have said, "Mormonism
must go," "The laws must be honored," "Youg
people are an alien people," and many other simi-
lar assertions, and yet would betray their ignor-
ance of the situation and the doctrine, and the re-
cently enacted laws to suppress polygamy and
punish unlawful cohabitation, etc., and the conse-
quences of the enforcement of the law, and were
entirely unaware either of what had been done, or
of what the conditions of the people were. Many
outside of Utah consider all the people as law-
[Column 2]
breakers because  they have read sensational
paragraphs in the newspapers, or heard the ru-
mors that float, as it were, upon the air, and swoop
down upon the innocent and unsophisticated, fill-
ing them with prejudice and hatred they know not
why.


WOMAN'S EXPONENT.


', 'HAVE THE MORMONS ANY RIGHTS?

	Joseph Smith, Jun., the Latter-day Saints'
Prophet, predicts, among many other occurrences,
the coming of a time when the Constitution of the
United States would be practically worthless be-
cause of a violation of its principles that contend-
ing political parties might gain a temporary advan-
tage, to make way for subversive expediencies,
and to satisfy the clamor and unrighteous appetite
of a headstrong nation. He predicted further,
that the Mormons would defend that Constitution;
and though driven, persecuted, despoiled and
murdered under its protection, they would even-
tually rescue and rehabilitate it, and offer an asy-
lum to the oppressed and God-fearing of all na-
tions. The painful fulfillment of the greater por-
tion of this prophecy, stamps—if there were no
other proof—Joseph Smith gifted with almost
divine foresight. And this I shall prove section
by section, article by article.
THE FIRST AMENDMENT TO THE CONSTITUTION
says: "Congress shall make no law respecting an
establishment of religion, or prohibiting the free
exercise thereof." Every Act passed by Congress
which directly or indirectly affects the religious
principle of Mormon plural marriage is a clear
and incontrovertible violation of this article of
the Constitution. Polygamy is an integral part of
the Mormon faith. It is a sacrament. The
United States' officers have themselves so declared
it. Not only has the Constitution been infringed,
but, in order to justify the enactments against the
Mormon faith, Congress has been compelled to
declare that "Mormon" polygamy is not religion,
and in that very declaration have legislatively
stated what is religion; for to say what is not is
emphatically to declare what is religion. No
power exists under the Constitution which gives
the right to force Mormons to accept a system of
marriage foreign and repugnant to their faith be-
cause it happens to be the pet method of modern
and existing Christianity.
	In clause 3, Sec. 9, Art. 1, we read,
"NO BILL OF ATTAINDER OR EX POST FACTO
LAW SHALL BE PASSED."
But the Edmunds Law of March, 1882, says that

[Column 2]

"if a citizen believes it right for a man to have
more than one living and undivorced wife at the
same time, or to live in the practice of cohabiting
with more than one woman," this "shall be suffi-
cient cause of challenge to any person drawn or
summoned as a juror or talesman." Thus, without
even the farce of a trial, a man is deprived of a
right that belongs to him as a citizen. This is at-
tainder—this, the Constitution distinctly forbids.
Moreover, the Edmunds' Law forbids any polyg-
amist voting at any election, and declares him
"ineligible for election or appointment to any
place of public trust, honor, or emolument;" and to
carry this out Commissioners were appointed who
"reversing the presumption of law and declaring
the whole population to be guilty, proceeded to
convict individuals by a test oath of their own
fabrication,', and thus, without trial, they are de-
prived of a right, which Judge Black says is the
inalienable part of citizenship. It is both attain-
der and ex poste facto, operating against those
who entered polygamy long before opposing meas-
ures were ever dreamt of. Section 8 of the Edmunds
Law regarding the holding of offices is a most
painful and palpable infraction of the sixth article
and third clause of the Constitutional Amend-
ments. "No religious test shall ever be required
as a qualification to any office or public trust under
the United States."
	Article VI. of Amendments provides that
"IN ALL CRIMINAL PROSECUTIONS
the accused shall enjoy the right to a speedy and
and public trial by an impartial jury of the State
and district wherein the crime shall have been
committed." This provision the fifth section of
the Edmunds Bill contravenes by excluding from
a jury any man who may even believe it right for
another to have two wives living and undivorced
at the same time; and as there are, notoriously,
only two classes in Utah—Mormons, and those
who hate them heartily—an impartial jury is an ab-
solute impossibility. As a result, the arrest of a
Mormon is equivalent to conviction. Proof of the
act which consummates marriage is not necessary
to conviction. If a man has lived apart from his
wives for an indefinite period, but fails to disown
them, he is convicted. Marshal Fred Dubois, of
Idaho Territory, admitted under oath, before the
Court of which he was an important officer, that
he had made use of the language here given: "I
have now got a jury that will convict every Mor-
mon brought before it on a charge of unlawful co-
habitation, innocent or guilty. It would convict
Jesus Christ himself if he were brought into Court
on that charge." The crusade is a religious one,
in every respect. It is not only unconstitutional,
but anti-constitutional; but the United States' Su-
preme Court has sustained and continues to sus-
tain these fatal violations of the Constitution.
JUDGE BLACK, THE GREATEST DEMOCRATIC CON-
STITUTIONAL LAWYER OF HIS AGE, 
referring to this act in a speech before the Judici-
ary Committee of the House of Representatives,
February 1, 1883, predicted: "To carry into effect
the laws already passed, it is necessary and proper
that you should have a police force composed of
spies and delators, who will thrust themselves
into the kitchens and bed-chambers of all families;
employ eavesdroppers, who will watch them at
keyholes and windows; or in default of that,
change the rules of evidence (as a committee of
the Senate has actually proposed), and compel the
lawful husband and wife to testify against one
another in contemptuous defiance of the great
principles which protect the sanctities of the
family, and lie at the basis of civil society." This
warning has been realized to the very letter, not
only as to spies, but to forcing the lawful wife to
testify against the husband. Scarcely a day passes
that does not witness the degrading fulfillment of
Mr. Black's ptediction; against this, also, the

[Column 3]

fourth article of the Constitution stands. It pro-
vides that "The right of the people to be secure
in their persons, houses,papers, and effects against
unreasonable searches and seizures shall not be
violated." Sick women and tender children, at
all hours of the day and night, are dragged by
coarse and vulgar officers from their beds, forced
to give evidence against the husband and father,
and subjected to such vile and indecent question-
ings as would cause the blush of shame to mount
to the cheek of a libertine; and this, too, in open
Court, before vulgar mobs, by the demand of the
public prosecutor, and with the countenance and
sanction of the judge. It is impossible that Eng-
lishmen could comprehend the infamous depth to
which the prostitution of justice has gone in
America where the Mormons are concerned..
	Article 2 of the Amendment says:—
"NO PERSON FOR THE SAME OFFENCE SHALL
BE TWICE
put in jeopardy of life or limb." But Lorenzo
Snow was three times convicted for the one offense,
unlawful cohabitation. The only proof against
him was furnished by himself, wherein he admitted 
having more than one woman to be entitled to the
name of wife. He had never violated the Ed-
munds Law. That was distinctly proven. Yet,
he is now suffering imprisonment under three con-
victions for the same offence. By a system of
segregation, if a man live with two women as his
wives for one month, according to the rulings of
the lesser and sanction of the higher Courts, he
may be convicted, in the discretion of the court,
for an offence for every hour. Thus, allowing
thirty days in the month, a man could be sent to
jail for a period of 360 years, and be fined £34,200
whereas the law puts the extreme penalty at six
months’ imprisonment and £60 fine. Besides, this
action necessarily involves the infraction of still
another constitutional principle (Article 8 of the
amendments) that "excessive fines" shall not be
imposed, "nor cruel and unusual punishments in-
flicted." The first part of this article reads: "Ex-
cessive bail shall not be required." Yet Geo. Q.
Cannon, one of the first Presidency of the Church,
was arrested under three indictments for unlawful
cohabitation. If, on trial, he were convicted, and
the extreme penalty which the law provides were 
inflicted, he could have been imprisoned for
eighteen months and fined £180. He demanded
to be admitted to bail, but before being released
the judge required him to furnish the unimpeach-
able security to the sum of £9,000. The custom-
ary bail in cases of manslaughter, assaults, and
forgery is from £100 to £300; but, because of his
prominence, Mr. Cannon had to give £9,000. The
Constitution says: "Excessive bail shall not be
required." And this is a country where liberal in-
stitutions and freedom are the basis upon which
the Constitution and Government are founded,
and where all men are equal!
THE MORMONS,
though citizens of the United States,
ARE ALLOWED NO MILITIA.
The legislation against them is special and class;
the rules of evidence have been outrageously per-
verted and practically destroyed; the fundamental
right of local self-government is largely denied
them; attempts are being made to rob and despoil
them as a religious community, and this with the
weight of sanction which the United States Con-
gress gives, and to deprive them of the last ves-
tige of citizenship; the organic law which the
parent Government gave them has by that authori-
ty been viciously violated; test-oaths are imposed,
no constitutional provision behind which they
could take the slightest refuge has been respected.
They are harassed, and worried, and hounded,
and despoiled of liberty, property, peace, and
character, by a set of impecunious, incapable,


ignorant, fanatical and dishonest, blackguards;
who, under the sanction of the "greatest Govern-
ment on earth," would rob, malign, and assassin-
ate them literally as they have done to their-
characters.'
	To crown all, a new proposition has been made
in Congress with the approval and applause of the
leading newspapers, to pass an amendment to the
Constitution, making it constitutional to deal with
the subject of polygamy. If the laws already
passed, and now doing such effective work against
polygamy, are constitutional, why this proposed
amendment? But if the proposed amendment be
necessary to justify anti-polygamic legislation,
what becomes of the enactments already passed,
now in full operation, and which the United
States Supreme Court has held to be constitu-
tional? If these acts are constitutional, the pass-
ing of the proposed amendment is like saying,
"We did right in doing what we have done, and
now we will make it more right by saying we did
right in doing right;" which is sheer and unmiti-
gated idiocy. Inconsistency is the inevitable and
invariable result of departure from principle. The
inconsistency may be long delayed, but it is of
unerring certainty. No truth could be more clear-
ly exemplified than in the dealing of American
statesmen (save the mark!) with the Mormon
problem.
	How long will a Government last, when men
who profess to love it can sanction its prostitu-
tion.
	How much respect can a Government claim
where it shakes its angry locks over the imprison-
ment of a common brawler, and threatens a war
unless he is released, as in the case of the man
Catting held recently by Mexico, and yet pays no
heed to the wail and petition of 200,000 of its op-
pressed and down-trodden citizens at home,
whose only desire is that their charge of insuffer-
able grievances may be investigated? In this un-
hallowed and unconstitutional persecution the
Mormons, in defending their own rights, are fight-
ing the battle of human liberty and constitutional
rights, and seem to be the only friends the Con-
stitution has left. When grave and thoughtful
men, who live in and love America, who are
among its chief intellectual and literary lights,
freed from the prejudices and the excitement inci-
dental to active political strife, hold it to be merely
a question of time, when it shall be disrupted and
overthrown because, of internal and political im-
morality, the weight of, Joseph Smith's prophecy,
that the end hastens, receives a weight of testi-
mony that should make those who are wise in
their generation pause.
		D. H. Wells.


WOMAN'S EXPONENT.


[Column 1]

["A PRAYER FOR ZION.
Here on this humble mount, my God, to Thee
In humble, fervent prayer we bow the knee
To plead for Zion, bending ’neath the rod
In bondage on Thine own and freedom's sod.

The storm-clouds now doth spread this fair land o’er,
And lightning flash around from shore to shore;
In this dark hour we come, our God and friend,
To Thee, Thou canst with tempests, blessings blend.

We know the gold must be well purified;
We know the tares together Thou wilt bind.
O, give us now Thy Spirit’s strengthening power,
That we may truly stand the trial hour.

Forgive the follies of each passing day,
And help us now to put all sin away,
That nearer we may come, our God, to Thee,
Praying the time is near when Zion shall be free,

Her sons go forth from out the prison wall,
Proclaiming peace and liberty to all,
Bearing glad tidings of the Joys to come,
When all Thy chosen ones are gathered home.

Now for our leaders do we pray to Thee,
Even Thine own anointed, chosen Three
O, may their lives be precious in Thy sight,
For faithful do they stand for Thee and right.

They are tried and proven, even seven times;
They have testified of Thee in many climes:
Now in this hour, O Father, guard them well,
And from ten thousand hearts glad praise shall swell.

For Thine Apostles, messengers of life
To nations withering in sin and strife,
Yearning to save would they but heed the voice
Of truth and in Thy love rejoice.

Their words more precious far than glittering gold,
The hearts they touch not, must indeed be cold,
Where’er, their lot be cast on sea or land,
May they e’er have Thy power and guiding hand.

We pray for all now scorned for Thee and Thine
Appointed laws, most sacred and divine;
Be Thou their help in every time of need,
And with the bread of life Thy children feed.

Oh, may our prayer, O God, to Thee ascend,
And with the voices of the angels blend;
And may “Thy Kingdom come, Thy will be done,”
When Christ and all Thy saints shall be as one.
									HOPE.
Tempe, Feb. 24, 1885.


", 'A NEW YEAR'S OFFERING.—1883.

	The way of this world is to praise and bless,
Extol and venerate the memory
Of dear and faithful friends when they are dead.
Too oft while they are with us, we forget, 
Or fail to realize their priceless worth;
We do not sense how much they are to us,
How much our happiness depends on them,
Till they are gone, and then it is “too late!"
Too late e’er to recall one needless pang,
One harsh expression, which we may have giv'n,
Or offer love long due, too long withheld.
	The Gospel light, restored in latter-days,
Corrects—with every other—this mistake;
And those who will accept it, in its strength,
Its beauty and its fulness, "need not err,"
In greater or in lesser things than these.
"The testimony of the Lord is sure,
And maketh wise the simple"—happy truth!
To those who will receive it, it is peace.
But doubts and bitterness will cling to those
Who shut their hearts against its kind approach.
	The year just closed is like a buried friend.
And if its gifts, its precious days and hours,
We've failed to value in their faultless course,
Failed to fill up with useful thoughts and deeds,
Then o’er its grave we may indeed lament,
With sad, unfeigned though unavailing tears.
But if, instead, we've wisely spent the wealth
Of opportunities for doing good,		
That crowded on us in the fleeting time;
Watchful that nothing worth a stroke should pass,
Which we might aid in doing, or might do,
Forming fair shrines for tender memories,
Then truly, we can find no cause to weep
For this kind friend, more than for the vast throng
Of cherished favorites, the sweet and pure,
The good, and wise, and staunch, among our own,
Who have been summoned in the year now past,
To higher spheres of action and of thought.
That they have triumphed, winning fadeless crowns,
We cannot doubt; therefore we will not mourn.
But with the gen'rous feelings, that should live
Within the heart of every Saint of God,
We'll praise and bless Him for His matchless love;
And ceaseless thanks we'll offer for His care, 
And kind protection which salvation wrought,
Anew for us throughout the vanished year,
And—through obedience—is promised still;
And with our thanks this humble prayer we'll raise:
	Our Father, God! Bless Thou Thy chosen one,
Thy servant whom Thou'st called to lead Thy Saints,
Whose head is silvered with the time and thought,
Which he hath spent to glorify Thy name 
In the salvation of his fellowmen.
Bless him with health and with Thy strength, and let
The light of revelation dwell with him,
That he may comprehend Thy mind and will 
Concerning Zion—yea, 'the pure in heart,' 
And with him bless his Counselors; the Twelve,
Their Counselors, and all whom Thou hast called
To bear Thy Holy Priesthood here on earth;
Make harmless every weapon that is hurled
'Gainst those who bear Thy truth into the world;
The young among Thy sons and daughters bless,
Surmount them with the shield of righteousness;
Let faith and union with Thy Saints increase, 
Throughout the Stakes of Zion spread Thy peace;
And through the year just op’ning, may we stand,
Or follow, as Thou wilt, Thy guiding hand.
We ask all things, and say Thy will be done,
In the dear name of Thy beloved Son;
Thine, be the glory, and, let once again,
All Israel say Amen! Amen!! Amen!!!'"
	LULA.


[Column 1]

', 'TO SISTER ELIZA.

Sister Eliza, how shall I convey
The wishes that surround your natal day?
Or voice the love, the vast appreciation
That prompted this, your birthday celebration?
My inability had disinclined me
To undertake the present task assign'd me,
Knowing that abler ones, and older, too,
Could best bestow a tribute worthy you;
Yet if I fail to fitly grace my theme,
I trust you'll still accept my warm esteem.

Thousands of Saints to-day would fain express
Their recognition of your usefulness;
Their voices, as "the sound of many waters,"
Would own you first of Zion's veteran daughters;
Yea, one and all to you this place accord,
Assigned you by the servants of the Lord,
Nor could our brethren have ordained a wiser,
Or naturally, more able organizer.
You've led your sisters on in righteousness,
And systematically soothed distress;
Have formed societies whose purpose pure
Has helped the helpless and relieved the poor;
Nor have you toiled and traveled thus for gain,
Nor have your faithful labors been in vain.
In Zion's stakes, through all their breadth and length,
Lo! Zion's daughters have increased in strength,
Till with the Priesthood of "High Heaven" they stand
Shoulder to shoulder like a marshalled band,
Willing and waiting, disciplined and true,
Ready indeed the will of God to do.
Our little ones have also had a share
Of your unceasing and iustructive care;
Many the blossoms that we trust will bring
A glorious and abundant harvesting.
Even under God this state of things is due,
In a great measure, to your efforts true;
And yet to-day, in spite of cares so weighty,
Body and mind, you're sound and staunch at eighty.
You number fourscore years, without a doubt,
Pardon the pride that makes me tell it out;
I know you're not ashamed of being old,
Or else your age you never would have told.
Why is it some who've sense commit the blunder
Of underating "Eather Time," I wonder?
Why should we wish his traces could be hidden,
Why treat him coldly, like a guest unbidden?
'Tis just as well for folks to condescend
To take Time's hand and own him as a friend.
Doubtices he's partial to a special few,
And thus it is he's gently dealt with you;
Long may he favor you as heretofore,
At least another twenty years or more,
Unless of life you weary, may you see
Enough of time to make a century.

Fourscore to-day! Oh! what you must have seen;
A wonderful experience yours has been;
Honor'd indeed to be the mate, the wife,
Of one whose sacred mission cost his life;
Consort of Joseph, martyr, saint and seer,
Whose memory unto all of us is dear;
How much more so to those we cannot tell
Whose privilege it was to know him well;
Who were by him beloved, and to him sealed
By the grand principle from heaven revealed.
Rare courage they possessed, (the faithful few)
Who entered into covenants so new,
Who, while their aim as falling snow was pure,
Had ev'ry kind of slander to endure.
To wrestle, not alone with vain tradition,
But with vindictive, murderous opposition,
Through deepest waters truly have you past,
Through persecution's scathing, fiery blast,
Endured the days when our loved leader fell
To glut men's malice and the spleen of hell,
Yet firm your faith, e'en while yonr heart strings bled,
For Christ yon knew was still "our living head."

Soon as for you the dearest ties were riven
Lo! from their rightful homes the Saints were driven,
To seek upon the pathless prairies wide

[Column 2]

The common rights their country had denied;
Their only shelter and their only home,
Were canvas walls; or heaven's vaulted dome.
Deprived of all the luxuries left behind
They murmured not, but all their strength combined
To reach the distant, designated spot,
Where mobs no more against their peace would plot.
Mighty aud strong God raised them up a man
Of dauntless courage to direct and plan,
As Moses led the Hebrews through the sea
So Brigham Young (a man of destiny)
Was called to guide God's people day by day
And lead them in "a solitary way."
The sense of freedom so supremely sweet
Invigorated weary hearts and feet.
And thus they reached the valleys of the West,
And 'mid the "everlasting hills" found rest;
Yet whilst they journey'd on the lonesome plains
The camp of Isr'el sang your cheering strains,
Such glowing words that bade them courage take
To bear their burdens for the Gospel's sake.
And so for years your glorious gift of song
Has helped the weary and the faint along.

One incident 'mong recollections rife
I fain wonld mention of my early life;
Grant me forbearance while I thus recall
One epoch more eventful than them all.
Auspicious time, when truth's eternal light
Flooded my soul with its effulgence bright.
Truth found me seeking it when but a child,
Happy was I to find it undefiled;
But those I fondly hoped would share my joy
Daily endeavored to my faith destroy.
So ridiculed by all and frowned upon,
These words of Sister Snow's oft cheered me on,
"My heart is fixed I know in whom I trust."
Immortal lines, they cannot, will not rust.
Full oft I ponder'd them within my heart,
Till of my daily thoughts they formed a part,
And ever as I conned them o'er and o'er,
So ev'ry time my faith was strengthen'd more,
Nor can I help but wish their anthor blest,
Whose high resolves my youthful mind impressed,
Who "touched my heart' and wakened the desire
That God would thus my soul inspire.

How shall we e'er forget that hymn of praise
Familiar to the Saints of Latter-days,
Even "O my Father?" The exalting strain
Uplifts the spirit into heaven again,
And makes us recollect our royal birth,
However humble is our lot on earth.
Even as incense doth the song ascend
When Zion's singers' sweetest voices blend,
And as we list to the triumphant swell
We feel that "Zion prospers, all is well."

But time would fail me, nor do I desire,
To count the fruits of Zion's earliest lyre,
Enough that you have used the talent given.
And rendered back the interest due to heaven.
Ah! who shall prostitute a gift divine
To laud themselves alone in every line?
If God to one or all a gift imparts
Give Him the praise with undivided hearts;
Give Him the glory, as 'tis right we should,
By helping up the struggling multitude.
And this indeed excites our admiration
That Sister Snow has used the concentration
Of mind and body, heart and soul and brain
To build up Zion and to right maintain.
Devotion to the truth, yea, consecration,
Aloof from love of public estimation,
Has won for her a more enduring crown
Than present praise or cheaply bought renown.
	S. L. City, Jan. 21st., 1884.


[Column 1]
']
