['A DOUBLE BIRTHDAY CELEBRATION.
	The 31st of January, 1900, was made
memorable by the General Board of the
Relief Society whose members celebrated
appropriately the birthday of the President
and Vice-President of the organization.
	Mrs. Zina D. H. Young, the President,
was born on January 31st, 1821, and was
consequently 80 years of age. Mrs. Jane
S. Richards, First Vice-President, was born
on January 31st, 1823, two years later.
	Mr. and Mrs. F. S. Richards opened their
handsome and commodious residence on A
Street, in this city, for the birthday party
which was set for 2 p. m., Thursday the
31st. The parlors and library were beauti-
fully decorated with vines and white carna-
tions, the design being principally green
and white, though pink and American
beauty roses were disposed tastefully in
suitable places and added to the brilliancy
of the effect. The two honored guests of
the occasion were seated side by side in the
front parlor, where they received the con-
gratulations of their distinguished friends.
	President Lorenzo Snow and wife were
among the first to arrive. President George
Q. Cannon and wife, President Joseph F.
Smith and wife, Apostle Brigham Young
and wife, Apostle John Henry Smith and
wife, Bishop John R. Winder and wife and
Mrs. W. B. Preston, President Angus M.
Cannon and wife, President Joseph E. Tay-
lor and wife, President C. W. Penrose and wife,
Bishop O. F. Whitney, Mrs. Robert Pat-
rick, Counselor Wm. Barton and wife; also
Mrs. Julia C. Howe, Mrs. Mary Alice
Lambert, Mrs. William Jennings, Mrs.
Elizabeth Webb and Dr. E. S. Barney; also
Mrs. N. Twiss Young, Margaret P. and
Amelia F. Young and Mrs. Maria Y. Dou-
gall, Mr. Chariton Jacobs and wife, and his
son, Zebulon and wife. The Ogden party
were Hon. C. C. Richards and Mrs. Josephine
R. West, Mrs. D. H. Perry, Mrs. William
Driver and Professor Moench and wife, Mr.
and Mrs. Walker West, Mr. and Mrs. John
Herrick, Elder Samuel W. Richards and
wife, Mrs. Maria W. Wilcox, Mrs. H. P.
Richards, Mrs. Heber John Richards, Elder
Levi W. Richards and wife, Mr. and Mrs.
W. B. Dougall, Mr. Hugh Dougall, Mrs.
Mary C. Cannon, Misses Grace and Vera
Cannon and Miss Clarice Dougall.
	Two distinguished guests were Elder
Nathan Tanner, father of Mrs. F. S. Rich-
ards, and his sister, Mrs. Maria Lyman,
mother of Apostle F. M. Lyman. Of the
General Board, besides the two senior presi-
dents mentioned, there were Presidents
Bathsheba W. Smith and Sarah Jane Can-
non, E. B. Wells, Romania B. Pratt, Susan
Grant, Julina L. Smith, Emily S. Richards,
Rebecca Standring, Julia P. M. Farnsworth,
Elizabeth J. Stevenson and Annie T. Hyde.
	There were a number of others invited who
for various reasons could not be present, but
the party was an exceedingly pleasant one

[Column 2]
and all seemed to enjoy meeting the two
veteran women, whose good works of love 
and charity and benevolence are known 
throughout Israel.
	At one long table in the library were 
seated Sisters Zina Young and Jane S.
Richards and President and Mrs. Snow, 
President and Mrs. Cannon, President and 
Mrs. Smith, Brigham and Mrs. Young, 
John Henry and Mrs. Smith, Sisters B. W. 
Smith and Amelia F. Young, Mrs. Maria
Lyman and Mr. Nathan Tanner, Sen.
	Small tables in the parlors accommodated
the other guests. President Snow pro-
nounced the blessing. The dinner was de-
licious and admirably served. During the
dinner telegrams of congratulation were
read from absent members of the family and 
from friends. Also some letters containing
both congratulations and regrets for absence.
	After the guests had partaken of the
bounteous repast tables were cleared and
President Snow was invited to make a few
remarks. He responded briefly, speaking
of the long acquaintance he had enjoyed
with the sisters whose birthday was
being celebrated and congratulating them
upon the success they had made of their
life work, and wishing them happiness and
long life here and an eternity of glory
hereafter. Alluded to the mothers in
heaven and referred to the beautiful hymn
of his sister, Eliza, with which he believed
we were all familiar.
	President George Q. Cannon spoke next.
He eulogized the lives of these good 
sisters, and alluded to the trials through
which they had passed without wavering in 
their faith, and dwelt much on the integrity
of the Saints, who had endured suffering for
principle and to establish righteousness.
	President Joseph F. Smith followed in a
similar strain, noting the great strength of
character, fortitude and endurance of the 
women who had helped to lay the founda-
tion of righteous principles in the early 
days, and of the great blessings in store for
the faithful mothers in Israel. He also 
congratulated them on having lived so 
many years in such wonderful times, having
known the Prophet Joseph and others who 
had succeeded him, and said many more 
good and excellent things.
	Sister Zina responded in a few words of
grateful appreciation and spoke of her long
experience in the Church and her great love
for the Latter-day Saints and the work of 
the last days.
	Elder Hugh Dougall then sang “The
Holy City,” and thrilled every heart pres- 
ent with the melody of his superb voice.
	Elder John Henry Smith made a brief 
address representing the quorum of the
Apostles.
	Bishop John R. Winder spoke briefly and
Sister Bathsheba W. Smith expressed her
love for the work in a few well chosen
words, alluding to the days of Joseph and
some of her own personal experiences, and
her deep appreciation of the blessings en-
joyed by the Saints.
	Elder Nathan Tanner, who was one of
the oldest member in the Church, gave a 
few of his ideas of the past and present of 
the Latter-day work.
	Elder Samuel W. Richards was one of 
the speakers and gave some reminiscences 
of his acquaintance with the Prophet Joseph. 
	Elder Dougall, by request, rendered 
another sacred song entitled, “The Palms,”
his voice and expression doing ample justice 
to the theme and delighting the audience. 
Poems were read by Sisters Susa Young


Gates, L. L. Greene Richards and Ruth
inda H. Moench. Mrs. Richards poem
appears in this number, the others will be
given in a later number.
	President Jane S. Richards in a few
appropriate remarks expressed her feelings
at the honor shown and spoke of her great
love for the Relief Society work in which
she delighted and urged the younger sisters
to become acquainted with the labors of the
sisters who were now actively engaged
in the organization.
	During the evening souvenir cards with a
very small picture of Mrs. Young and Mrs.
Richards were given each guest. The cards
were tied with white ribbon and contained a
motto suited to the work in which they have
spent so many years. Sister Zina’s motto is
"Charity never faileth,” Sister Richard’s,
“He that giveth to the poor lendeth to the
Lord.”
	At the close of the impromptu exercises,
for no program had been prepared before-
hand by the committee, the audience stand-
ing sang “Praise God from whom all bless-
ings flow,” and Patriarch John Smith pro-
nounced the benediction.
	The spirit of the gathering was heavenly
and the party was certainly one to be re-
membered by all who were present. The
committee of the General Board of the Re-
lief Society feel grateful to all, who by their
presence and their generous words of appre-
ciation and blessing upon these noble and
devoted mothers in Israel, added to the suc-
cess of the entertainment, and especially ex-
press their thanks for the hospitality of
Brother and Sister F. S. Richards who so
generously gave their home and heartily
welcomed the invited guests.
	The General Board only regretted that so
many were absent that had been invited,
and that it was not possible to entertain all
the dearly beloved friends, associates and
fellow-laborers of Sisters Young and Rich-
ards, who have given so many years of
faithful service for the advancement of
women in all that pertains to a higher life
here and an exaltation hereafter.


WOMAN’S EXPONENT.


', 'PRESIDENT BATHSHEBA W. SMITH.
HER BIRTHDAY ANNIVERSARY.
	“Growing old gracefully” may well be
applied to our President of the Relief Socie-
ty, there is not time or space to tell all the
reasons why this saying is applicable, but if
one looks at her as she mingles in society or
as she moves about in her own home, and
more especially in the Temple, one cannot
but feel that she is a good illustration of the
poem given above.

[Column 2]
	Among the Latter-day Saint women, who 
are a connecting link between the past and
the present, from the days of the Prophet
Joseph Smith down to our own time, but
few remain. Foremost of the illustrious few,
we are proud and happy to mention Sister
Bathsheba W. Smith, whose birthday anni-
versary was recently celebrated with appro-
priate honors and the respect befitting her po-
sition as a Mother in Israel; one indeed, who
has ministered scores of years in holy places,
and officiated in sacred ordinances, to many
hundreds of the Daughters of Zion.
	Sister Smith holds the position of General
President of the Relief Society of the Church,
an organization founded by President Joseph
Smith himself sixty-seven years ago in the
city of Nauvoo—Illinois, (she became a 
member on the first day) and which Society
 has reached out into all countries where the
Gospel tidings have been carried and women
converted thereto.
	Through the systematic correllation of its
branches, women have been able to regulate
the principle of giving charity, and doing ac-
tive philanthropic work for the betterment
and uplifting of all the members of this So-
ciety everywhere and for others outside; re-
lieving both temporal and spiritual needs in
an unobtrusive and dignified way, and pro-
moting moral and educational progress
among those whose opportunities had been
limited, along these lines of advancement;
often owing to their surroundings and en-
vironments.
	Sister Smith has not only been at the head
of great public movements and benefits, both
presiding and assisting in managing, but she
has always been a delightful home-maker;
industrious, an early riser, economical, thrifty
even resourceful; and in the best sense
motherly.
	Her life has not been free from thorns and
briers, yet it has really been very beautiful
in many ways, and she has borne herself
heroically, when tried as by fire, and her 
faith has never wavered.
	Her illustrious husband Hon. George A.
Smith, cousin of the Prophet, and one of the 
most distinguished men in the Church, died 
over thirty years ago.
	A few years after his demise she built a
smaller home near the Temple and has re-
sided there permanently ever since.
	Although it is not a large or “stately man-
sion” she entertains her friends hospitably
and with the most cordial good cheer, for
she is a Southerner to the heart’s core, and
proud of her dear old Virginia birthplace.
	She has always cultivated flowers and her
neat, white cottage with green shutters, and
yard with its lilacs, roses, trailing myrtle,
holly-hocks, lackspur and other old-fashioned
flowering plants and shrubs are the admira-
tion of her friends, and the passers by, and
give a sort of tone to the dwelling place in-
dicative of peace and restfulness.
	However we are not giving our readers a
descriptive narrative or a story of this famous
woman, who has lived as long, and seen
so much, and associated with so many great
men and women, but only wish to tell about 
her eighty-seventh birthday anniversary.
	The lady in question gave a reception at

[Column 3]
her residence on Monday, May 3, 1909, from
2 p. m. to 8 p. m. to which she invited her
relatives, friends and co-workers. The parlor
and other rooms were artistically decorated
with flowers and ferns etc., festooned with
ribbons as for a bride; pretty vases and
bowls were filled with fragrant blossoms,
tastefully arranged, and all was done in the
most perfect harmony of color.
	Sister Smith wore with becoming grace,
an elegant, white, homemade silk dress,
with exquisite fine lace at the neck and 
wrists, and a very fine white lace scarf over
her beautiful soft white hair; altogether she
presented a queenly appearance, as she sat
in state, to receive her guests. She was as-
sisted in receiving by her only daughter Mrs.
Clarence E. Merrill, and Mrs. Susan E. Smith;
her charming and attractive grand-daughters
Mrs. David Allen, Mrs. George A. Horne,
Mrs. Ben. E. Lloyd, Mrs. Joseph Fisher,
Mrs. Stephen L. Richards, courteously meet-
ing the guests doing the honors and deftly
dispensing light refreshments to the nume-
rous callers, who came to offer congratula-
tions to the reverend hostess, on this auspi-
cious occasion; and to wish her continued
health, happiness, prosperity and future use-
fulness, and activity, as long as life seemed
desirable to her. Many of her friends brought
bouquets, pretty books and a variety of gifts,
remembrances, and messages, tokens of love
and esteem.
	During the entire afternoon until eight
o’clock in the evening Sister Smith was
shaking hands and greeting her guests, in
the most kindly and gracious way.
	Among the distinguished callers were Pre-
sident Joseph F. Smith, President John R.
Winder (who is near the same age as the 
lady herself) and President Anthon H. Lund,
also the venerable Patriarch John Smith,
and other notable people from far and near,
about two hundred in all, and every one 
seemed delighted to show honor and rever-
ence to the beloved woman leader.
	During the day a telegram of loving con-
gratulations and good wishes was received
from Los Angeles, California, signed John
Henry Smith, George Albert Smith, John and
Lucy Smith Acomb.
	The following day the General Board of
the Relief Society, according to a previous
arrangement, gave a sort of informal
but most delightful banquet at the home of
their president at three o’clock Tuesday,
May 4, 1909.
	A competent committee had the affair in
hand, to whom much credit is due for its
enjoyment and success. Consent of the
hostess having been given, the committee
took possession inside the cottage, and
made the needful preparations. Thereupon
the other members of the Board were re-
lieved of anxiety. The President, in her
pretty white costume, received all the mem-
bers with her sweetest smiles, and cordially
welcomed them to the home they had in-
vaded. The table looked very inviting with
its snowy linen napery, and elegant old-
fashioned china dinner-set and other rare
old plates; platters, bowls, etc., of antique
style, but the most interesting of all was the
President herself the Guest of Honor.


[Column 1]
	Everything had been done beforehand
with a view to sociability, and seated at
the head of the table was the reverend lady
we had gathered to honor while partaking
of the feast. On her right her counselor
Ida Smoot Dusenberry was seated, and
next to her, Sister Julina L. Smith, and on
the other side the secretary, Sister Emmeline
B. Wells, and next to her Sister Clarissa S.
Williams, the treasurer. Other members of
the Board present were Sisters Sarah Jenne
Cannon, Susan Grant, Emma S. Woodruff,
Emily S. Richards, Priscilla P. Jennings,
Phebe Y. Beatie, Alice M. Horne, Annie W.
Cannon, Minnie H. James, Elizabeth S. Wil-
cox, Rebecca Nibley, and Harriet B. Harker.
Rebecca E. Little, who had been seriously
ill for a long time, was at the banquet ac-
companied by her intimate associate and
friend Sister Elizabeth Crismon.
	It is scarcely possible to enumerate the
good things prepared for the delectation of
the sisters of the party, but if one dared tell
who this efficientc ommittee were, the secret
would be made public for the ladies are
famous in giving entertainments. The table
fairly groaned with the weight of the feast—
the roast beef looked very tempting and all
the vegetables of the season served in the
most appetizing style and all the condi-
ments that go with a dinner, and famous
salads, and after these dainties and
delicacies and ice cream and cakes of great
variety, and fruit to set off the picture of the
table, and a birthday cake, the most deli-
cious that could be made, and candy and
nuts and grape juice to give zest to the
toasts.
	A blessing was pronounced by Sister B.
W. Smith upon the delicious menu, and the
feasting began, all were ready to converse
socially until the chairman of the committee
announced that toasts were expected, and
the ladies began to think what to say.
	Aunt Emm. Wells was given the task of
opening the subject and after congratulat-
ing the President and telling her how long
she had known her, etc., she asked Sister
Dusenberry to respond, and in a few well-
chosen words the lady gave a pretty senti-
ment in her delightful manner. Sister
Julina Smith came next and spoke most
tenderly and lovingly of her Aunt Bath-
sheba, and in fact there seemed in her mind
a little rivalry between herself and Aunt
Emm. Wells as to who loved Aunt Bath-
sheba the best, but that was rather more
amusing than serious, and naturally Sister
Julina is dearer, being her own sister’s
daughter and according to the old saying,
blood is thicker than water.
	Sister Clarissa S. Williams could scarcely
speak when she recalled the first recollec-
tions of Aunt Bathsheba, associated as she
was in her mind with her own mother and
her dear father, her feelings almost over-
came her, but she choked down the sob fn
her throat and spoke eloquently of those
dearest memories of her childhood, and
closed with some beautiful lines from Long-
fellow’s poem; “THE GOLDEN MILESTONE.”
Each man’s chimney is his milestone,
Is the central point from which he measures
		Every distance
Through the gateway of the world around him.
We may build more splendid habitations
		But we cannot
Buy with gold the old associations.
	Sister Emma S. Woodruff spoke of the
many years she had been acquainted with
Sister Smith and their very intimate associa-
tion in the temple, and in the work of the

[Column 2]
Relief Society, and of her great life work,
and congratulated her on her long life.
	Sister Sarah Jenne Cannon who is al-
ways original as well as dignified, spoke
briefly of her life-long recollections of Aunt
Bathsheba and her nearness to her in the
Relief Society and Temple work.
	Sister Priscilla Jennings wished Sister
Smith all the blessings here and hereafter
that could be showered upon her to make
her life happy; rejoiced that she had been
so closely associated with her. Sister
Susan Grant, modest and demure, spoke
sweetly and tenderly of the past and present
in the finest words possible. Sister Emily
S. Richards alluded to the intimate assosia-
tion of the Richards family and Aunt Bath-
sheba, and could not recall the time when
she had not known her, and referred to Sis-
ter Jane S. Richards intimacy with Aunt
Bathsheba for so many years.
	Sister Alice Merrill Horne was particular-
ly grateful to all who felt to honor her dear
grandmother, and spoke of her grandmoth-
er’s energy and perseverance being an incen-
tive to all, and the sisters were all grati-
fied with her kindly words and feelings so
beautifully and tenderly expressed.
	Sister Annie Wells Cannon gave a few of
her childish recollections of the way she
used to scale the high stone wall that separ-
ated the Wells home from the Historian’s
office, when Uncle George A. (as the chil-
ren called him) sat on the porch in his old
arm chair, and referred to the great rever-
ence she felt for him and Uncle Brigham
and her own father, associating them to-
gether as educators and benefactors, and her
great love for Aunt Bathsheba, which she
could give more fully, she thought, in some
lines which she quoted:
	“I would flood your life with sunshine,
		I would fence you from all ill,
	I would crown you with all blessings
		If I could but have my will.
	Aye, but human love may err, dear,
		And a power all-wise is near,
	So I only say God bless you,
		And keep you through the year.”
	Sister Phebe Y. Beatie spoke tenderly of
the love she had for Sister Smith, who al-
ways reminded her of her mother (Sister
Zina Young) and how all through her girl
life she had watched them so intimate in
every place, and it was gratifying to recall
scenes and associations of the earlier days.
	Sister Minnie H. James spoke of the inti-
macy of Sister Smith with her own dear de-
parted mother, Sister M. Isabella Horne, for
so many years, and referred to the beauti-
ful past as she recalled it to memory.
	Sister Elizabeth S. Wilcox had the same
tender spot in her heart concerning her own
mother, who was so closely associated with
Sister Smith. Sister Rebecsa Nibley begged
to be excused, but spoke a word or two in
appreciation of her friendship with our
President, Sister Hattie B. Harker felt the
honor it was to be with us and expressed
great kindness and love for our President.
	Sister Bathsheba W. Smith spoke a few
precious words to us all, expressing her love
and kindly feeling and her good wishes to
all who had come to her home. She blessed
us and desired we should all keep the faith,
love our religion and keep the covenants we
had entered into sacred. She blest us with
the choicest of heaven’s blessings, peace,
prosperity and love, and hoped we should
all meet again and rejoice together and fesst
as we had today,
	It was indeed a happy day and the only
regret was that some of the members could

[Column 3]
not attend, especially Sister Jane S. Rich-
ards, who at all great gatherings of this kind
has been one of the most active in assisting,
and Sister Romania B. Penrose, who is now
abroad, Sister Julia P. M. Farnsworth and
Sister Carrie S. Thomas who were unavoid-
ably detained at home.
	The General Board may be congratulated
on the success of the banquet, and if the
President felt happy, which we hope and
trust she did, and will remember the day
with pleasure and satisfaction, knowing she
has the love and reverence and devotion of
her coworkers.			E.B.W.


WOMAN’S EXPONENT.


', 'BATHSHEBA SMITH MERRILL.

	After the program there were short con-
gratulatory and appropriate speeches, one
by Sister Julina L. Smith, distinctively ten-
der, and brought tears to the eyes of most
of those present. Sister Julina is a niece
of Sister Bathsheba’s and had spent many
years of her young life in her aunt’s home.
	President Zina D. H. Young (now of
blessed memory) spoke very eloquently of
her intimate association with Sister Smith.
	Sister Emily D. P. Young, a very dear
friend of Sister Smith’s, and exceedingly
diffident about speaking, being called upon
asked that Sister Emmeline B. Wells be her
spokesman, Sister Wells responded to the
request.
	Bishop John R. Winder also spoke brief-
ly, and then Patriarch John Smith gave a
number of reminiscences of the past, allud-
ing lovingly to the patriarchal life of his
cousin, President George A. Smith, and re-
ferred to the numerous circle of relatives
and friends, who could not be present to
participate in this happy occasion. The
party joined in singing “Hard Times Come
Again no More,” and Miss Priscilla Smith,
another daughter of Sister Susan and Presi-
dent George A. Smith, gave a very pleasing
recitation, which produced considerable
merriment. The banquet served by loving
hands, we leave to the imagination of the
readers of these hastily written pages, and
will only add that the day closed happily

[Column 2]
with an evening program literary and
musical.
	On May 3, 1901, Mrs. B. W. Smith’s birth-
day was celebrated at the elegant residence
of Mrs. Julia P. M. Farnsworth, and was
given by that gracious and hospitable host-
ess and Mrs. Alice S. M. Horne.
	We republish the notice from the
WOMAN’S EXPONENT of May 15, 1901:
	The seventy-ninth birthday anniversary
of our revered and honored Mother in
Israel, Sister Bathsheba W. Smith, was
celebrated at the handsome residence of
Mrs. Philo T. Farnsworth, in this city,
May 3, 1901.
	The beautiful parlors and library were
artistically decorated with flowers, flags and
historic pictures, the parlor and library in
sweet peas, the dining room in red and
white roses and carnations. The music
was by some of the best talent in the city,
Prof. Joseph Anderson and Prof. A. C.
Lund, and the selections were from the old
masters. It was a great addition to the
delightful entertainment.
	Sister Smith was dressed in white and
looked the veritable “Queen of hearts and
homes,” loveable, motherly and altogether
charming. Those who received with her
were Mrs. Zina D. H. Young, Mrs. Jane S.
Richards, Mrs. E. B. Wells, Mr. and Mrs.
P. T. Farnsworth, Mr. and Mrs. George
H. Horne, Mrs. B. S. Merrill and Mrs. D.
R. Allen. Several beautiful young women
assisted in the dining room and a few love-
ly little girls acted as ushers, among them
some of Sister Farnsworth’s daughters.
	“A Tribute to Aunt Bathsheba,” by the
hostess, Mrs. Farnsworth, was given be-
fore the program proper was rendered,
remarks by Bishop Winder and Apostle
Brigham Young; and Mrs. L. L. Greene
Richards read an original poem. Mrs.
Thomas Hull sang “The End of the Way”
in a delightfully Scotch way.
	The toastmaster was Judge W. H. King,
late representative to Congress, whose elo-
quence is proverbial, and who carried off
fresh honors in this rather difficult role.
	Prayer by Bishop O. F. Whitney, and
the first response to a toast was given by
President Lorenzo Snow, “George A. and
Aunt Bathsheba” “Our Family,” John
Henry Smith. “The Old Home,” Clarissa
Smith Williams. “Noted Virginians,”
Bishop Whitney. “The Prophet,” Presi-
dent Joseph F. Smith. “Father’s First
Counselor,” Brigham Young. “When Old
Friends Meet,”(poem) by Mrs. E. B. Wells.
“Sister Bathsheba in the Relief Society,”
by Mrs. Zina D. H. Young,” was given by
Sister Zina Y. Card, as her mother had re-
tired from the room. “Sister Bathsheba at
the Temple,” Mrs. A.W. McCune. “Child-
hood Reminiscences,” Mrs. Bathsheba S.
Merrill. “Grandfather and Granddaughter,”
Mrs. D. R. Allen. “Great Grandmother,”
Mary S. Horne. “Aunt Bathsheba,” Jul-
ina L. Smith. “Aunt Bathsheba in the
Ward,” Bishop Beatie. The Bishop having
left, his wife responded for him.
	The exercises were so varied by the
toastmaster’s original introduction that the
program could not possibly seem to have
any sameness. A list of the names of the
guests is too long for our little paper, but
suffice to say, it could not include all Sister
Smith’s friends and admirers, for they fill
these valleys of the mountains and extend
far away from here into other lands and
climes.
	Congratulations and good wishes were
simply showered upon the honored guest of

[Column 3]
the day, her praises were said and sung
and the banquet was in every way desir-
able, and the tables so arranged as to give
all an opportunity to see and hear. There
seemed nothing lacking in the management
of the entire affair, which owed its success
to the tact and artistic skill of the two
ladies who gave the party, Mrs. Farnsworth
and Mrs. Alice Merrill Horne, to whom
all the guests feel indebted for the great
pleasure and honor of participating in doing
honor to one in every way so worthy of
our love and esteem as Sister Bathsheba
W. Smith.
	Sister Bathsheba is modest and retiring
in manner and has never sought office, but
the people have sought her. When the
Senior Retrenchment Society was organized
in 1870, and President Brigham Young
called Sister M. Isabella Horne to preside,
Sister B. W. Smith was chosen one of the
counselors in that organization, and Sis-
ter Horne and Sister Smith are now the
only two living who were appointed at that
time.
	Sister Smith was first counselor to Sister
Rachel R. Grant in the Thirteenth Ward
Relief Society for many years, until she re-
moved from the Thirteenth Ward to the
Seventeenth Ward (where she now resides)
and was very soon after elected president
of the Relief Society in that ward and held.
the office thirteen years. In December,
1877, when the Relief Society of Salt Lake
Stake was organized, she was elected treas-
urer, and held the position until about a
year ago. In October, 1888, she was chosen
second counselor to Sister Zina D. H.
Young in the General Society. Although
very domestic in her tastes she has done a
vast amount of public duty, and now when
nearing eighty years of age is actively and
constantly engaged in serving the people in
the Temple where she presides as well as in
the General Relief Society of which she is
president.
	At the special conference held November
10, 1901, Sister Bathsheba W. Smith was
unanimously elected President of the Re-
lief Society of the Church of Jesus Christ of
Latter-day Saints, with Annie Taylor Hyde
first counselor and Ida Smoot Dusenberry
second counselor, Emmeline B. Wells secre-
tary and Clarissa Smith Williams treasurer.
	That she may live many years to fill the
position of the Elect Lady in this great and
grand society is the earnest wish of all who
know and appreciate her labors and her
many charming attributes and charac-
teristics.
	In concluding this brief sketch of the life
of our dearly beloved President we can truly
say she has verified the words of Solomon
in describing a perfect woman:
	“She seeketh wool and flax, and worketh
willingly with her hands.”
	“She layeth her hand to the spindle, and
her hands hold the distaff.”
	“She stretcheth out her hand to the poor;
yea she reacheth forth her hands to the
needy.”
	“She openeth her mouth with wisdom;
and in her tongue is the law of kindness.”
	“Her children rise up, and call her
blessed; her husband also, and he praiseth
her.”
		(To be continued.)


WOMAN’S EXPONENT.


Mrs Ida H. Harper contributes to the
March number of Pearson’s an interesting
article on “The home Life of Susan B.
Anthony.”


WOMAN’S EXPONENT


']

['ARTICLES OF ASSOCIATION.
	Article I. This Association shall be called
the Woman Suffrage Association of Utah, and
the officers and delegates shall pay an initia-
tion fee of 50 cents.
	Article II. The object of this Association
shall be to work for the enfranchisement of all
women without regard to party, sect or creed,
and to take such steps from time to time as
shall be wise and expedient for the political
advancement of woman.
	Article III. Sec. 1. The officers of this
Association shall be a president, three vice-
presidents, a secretary, corresponding secretary
and treasurer, with an executive committee of
not less than five, and two auditors. The
officers named in this section shall be nomi-
nated at the business session preceding the
annual convention. The three persons receiv-
ing the highest number of votes for any office
shall be considered the nominees of said con-
vention for such office.
	Sec. 2. The officers of this Association
with the executive committee shall elect an-
nually from the veterans of the cause, hon-
orary vice-presidents, who shall have all the
privileges of other vice-presidents, and whose
duty it shall be to advance the cause.
	Artical IV.—Sec. 1. the officers of this
association with the executive committee shall
have power to change, or amend the by-laws
and regulations at an executive session, and
such changes or amendment, shall be submitted
at the regular meeting where they may be
adopted by a majority vote.
	Sec. 2. They shall also devise plans of
work suited to the development and progress
of the association, and encourage young people
to speak, write essays, and discuss questions
upon political matters and civil government
and such other subjects as relate thereto.
	Article V.—Sec. 1. All county associa-
tions organized throughout Utah may become
auxiliary to this association by adopting this
constitution and that of the National Ameri-
can Woman Suffrage Association, and paying
annually their percentage specified in the basis
of representation.
	Sec. 2. The president of such association shall
be considered a vice president of the Utah Wo-
man Suffrage Association, and will be expected
to attend conventions, report progress, have a
voice in electing of officers, and entitled to all
the privileges pertaining thereto.
	Article VI. The names of the delegates to
the annual convention of the National Ameri-
can Woman Suffrage Association at Washing-
ton shall be nominated at the executive sessions
of the association, and be presented to the
annual convention, where they shall be elected
by a formal vote.
	Article VII.—All official papers and docu-
ments shall be signed by the President and
Secretary, or in the absence of the President
the Vice-President next in order. All official
correspondence must be signed by the chair-
man of the executive committee, or the presi-
dent and corresponding secretary.

', 'THE NEW CONSTITUTION.

	The following is the Constitution proposed
for the new National-American Woman Suf-
frage Association. This constitution has been
approved by a majority of the committee on
union, appointed by the American W. S. A.,
and is now submitted to a vote of the members
of the Association, the vote to be taken by
mail, in accordance with the resolution passed
at the last annual meeting of the Association,
held at Cincinnati, O.
	HENRY B. BLACKWELL,
		Cor Sec, A. W. S. A. 
	ARTICLE I. The name of this Association
shall be the National-American Woman Suf-
frage Association.
	ARTICLE II. The object of this Associa-
tion shall be to secure protection in their right
to vote to the women citizens of the United
States by appropriate National and State legis-
lation.
	ARTICLE III. Section 1.—All citizens of
the United States subscribing to this Constitu-
tion and paying not less than one dollar
annually into the treasury of the Association,
shall become members thereof and shall be en-
titled to attend all its meetings, to participate
in all discussions that may arise, and to receive
reports and other documents published by it;
but such membership shall not carry with it
the right to vote.
	Section 2.—The payment of fifty dollars
($50) into the treasury of this Association
shall constitute any citizen of the United
States a life member of the Association with
all the privileges belonging to the regular
annual members.
	ARTICLE IV. 	Section 1.—The officers of
this Association shall be a president, a vice-
president at large, a recording secretary, a cor-
responding secretary, a treasurer, a chairman
of the executive committee and two auditors.
The officers named in this section shall be
nominated by an informal ballot at a busi-
ness session of the annual convention of the
Association. The three persons receiving the
highest number of votes for any office, shall
be considered the nominees of the convention
for that office, and the will of the Association
shall be taken for a formal ballot.
	Section 2.—Wherever State and Territorial
associations, auxiliary to the National Ameri-
can Association exist, the presidents of such
associations shall be considered vice-presidents
of the National-American Association, repre-
senting therein their respective States and Ter-
ritories.
	Section 3.—Each State or Territorial auxil-
iary association shall elect from its members-
ship one person to serve on the executive com-
mittee of their National-American Association.
	Section 4.—The president of the National-
American Woman Suffrage Association shall
appoint a vice president to represent on its
executive board, any State or Territory where
no State or Territorial Association exists auxil-
iary to the National-American Association.
	Section 5.—The officers enumerated in the
preceeding sections of this article shall con-
stitute the committee of this Association: of
these officers, fifteen shall constitute a quorum
for the transaction of business.
	Section 6.—The executive committee shall
elect annually from the veterans of our cause
ten or more honorary vice presidents.
	ARTICLE V. This Constitution may be
amended by a majority vote at any annual
meeting, notice of the amendment having
been given at any preceeding annual meeting.

['A WOMAN MISSIONARY.

	[Mrs. Addison Pratt on the Society Islands
1850.]
		(Continued.)
	Today I called the native sisters together
to instruct them in their religious duties.
Ellen acted as interpreter, and succeeded
remarkably well. This she has been able
to do for several months. The sisters spoke
in turns, each expressing a desire that I
might soon have a knowledge of their
language, and speak with my own lips
directly to them. I shall soon make an
attempt to do so, and having their faith
and prayers, shall be certain of success.
	October 6th. Memorable day ! Confer-
ence convenes today in Salt Lake City. The
remembrance creates deep sensations in my
mind. I sigh to be there, although I do
not wish to leave my labors here. Before
another month rolls around “Paraita” will
be gone to Ravavai, then we shall be still
more lonely. How much it would animate
us could we see business going on; see men
plowing, sowing grain, driving teams;
have the sound of axes and hammering;
see houses building. But here monotony
reigns supremely. All I hear is the sound
of mallets making native cloth.
	On the 8th, (Wednesday) a party of us
went on a pleasure excursion to some small
islands five miles distant. We landed on
the largest, nineteen souls in all. It is a
beautiful island covered all over with tama-
na and cocoanut trees. The soil is fine,
white sand, which contrasts beautifully
with the green trees. I walked around the
Island, picked up shells and some fine
specimens of coral. The men caught fish,
made an oven in the ground and cooked us
a meal consisting of fruits and vegetables
growing on the Island. The men threw
up a shelter for us with brush and leaves,
under which we spread our bedding and
slept well through the night. The children
explored the Island thoroughly, climbed the
trees, made new discoveries in shrubbery
and flowers. We sailed home with a
pleasant breeze, and passed the village
where the workmen were engaged building
a schooner. They saluted us with loud
voices, "Iu ora nu otou to have ra mai,"
(peace be to you in returning home.)
	I sometimes walk to the opposite village
where the ship is building; distance five
miles. I can walk under the shade of trees
the whole way, sit down in a cooling shade,
rest myself and renew my jonrney. Thus
we resort to changes, and trifling though
they be, they serve to enliven the mind.
	Mr. Pratt preaches regularly in the
native tongue, which I can now under-
stand tolerably well. Baptisms occur
nearly every Sabbath. I wrote an address
to be read in a woman’s meeting, which Mr.
Grouard translated into the Tahitian for me.
They seemed greatly to admire it. I read
to them the 12th chapter of Isiaah, con-
taining, proof of the origin of the Book of
Mormon. Ellen explained and commented
upon it. They inquired if the ancient
Nephites were Europeans? I told them
they were the ancient fathers of the Tahi-
tians. At this they appeared greatly inter-
ested, and wished to learn more about the
book. No organ of their craniums is more
prominent than marvelousness.
	October 19th. Sabbath it is with us, but
not with those at home. One day is lost in
coming here via Cape Horne, one gained by
coming via Cape Good Hope. The natives
have a superstition that if they should fail

[Column 2]
to keep the right Sabbath someone of their
number would sicken and die. Yesterday
Captain Johnson’s vessel sailed. It has
been lying here several days. We were
hoping it would bring us letters from home,
but only one came to us from Tahiti, writ-
ten by Elder Dunn, who is now nearly
ready to embark for America. He is in
very poor health, and we feel in doubt
about his reaching home, but pray earnest-
ly that he may. So our dear little Mary
must leave us with her step-father, Captain
J.   Seven months has she been a visitant
in our family, and by her modest behavior
has endeared herself to us all, more especial-
ly Frances, who has been her constant
teacher. As she has never been fond of
children, her attachment to Mary, we hope,
may effect a change in her. So submissive
and obedient has she ever been to Frances,
she cannot avoid feeling deep sympathy for
her.
	The 24th. Today arrived a schooner
from the Chain Islands, Captain Hervey.
The king of Ravaivai was on board. He
informed us that the missionary house
built of late has been thrown down in a
gale of wind. Last evening native dancing
was heard, much to our annoyance. Their
music consisted of two drums, a small
instrument made of a weed which grows
here, making a low, sweet sound. Captain
Hervey and Mr. Bourne attended the exer-
cises, drank too freely, quarelled and fought.
Today they had a court over the affray.
Such is the example white men set before
the natives.
	The 27th day. Set sail for Raivavai
with a fine breeze; eighty natives on board.
The air being pleasant, we all slept on
deck. Some of the Island women had
never been so far from home before. They
were very sea sick, although all their lives
accustomed to go out in open boats.
	The second day at ten o’clock we made
the island. It is more mountainous than
Tahiti. The small town near where we
landed presents a lively appearance from
the sea. The house of worship looks like a
city hotel, with white walls and green
window blinds. A long row of ito trees
standing in front, small trunks and large
tops, gives the town the appearance of an
Eastern village at a distance, but as you
draw near the scene changes. All around
the “Fere bare ra” the place is thoroughly
clean. A wide stone pavement surrounds
it. The inside work displays taste and
ingenuity. The pulpit is built very high
and the stairs leading up to it are painted
green. As I entered the building, I thought
how much the poor people had striven to
imitate civilized life. The seats are made
of dark wood the color of mahogany, with
a great variety of carved work upon them.
	Queen Rupah, from Tupuin, came with
ns. She brought her fine clotting given
her by the French merchants. She dressed
very handsomely to go on shore, but would
not wear shoes. The king of the Island
appears a kind, good man, is full in the
faith that his people will receive the Gospel.
Those belonging to the English church
commenced looking in their Bibles to forti-
fy themselves with arguments, should any
attack be made against, their doctrines. The
king built a house, intending to have Paraita
and his family remain there, but its being
destroyed by a wind storm was a premoni-
tion to us that it was not the will of the
Lord that we should make a lengthy stay
there.
		(To be continued.)


Woman's Exponent.

Terms: one copy one year, $1.00; one copy six months.

ered by mail, extra for postage one year, 25 cts.

space one time $2.50; per month, $3.00. A liberal dis-
count to regular advertisers.

Street: Business hours from 10 a. m. to 5 p. m. every
day, except Sunday.

		Mrs. E. B. WELLS,

second-class matter.
	SALT LAKE CITY, UTAH, JANUARY 1, 1900.


', 'A WOMAN MISSIONARY.
	[Mrs. Addison Pratt on the Society Islands,
1850.]
			[Continued.]
	For the first time in my life I took food
as a medicine, forcing it down as I would a
nauseous draught given to save my life.
Two or three times a flying fish found on
deck was dressed and cooked for me: the
sweetest morsel I tasted on the voyage.
The weather was fine, a few days’ head
wind was all the difficulty we had to en-
counter; and had it been possible for me
to have shaken off my seasickness, I should
have passed the time pleasantly. We
spoke an American whale-ship on the pas-
sage. The captain came on board with his
little daughter, six years of age, a very
pretty child. His wife, who was sailing
with him, had given birth on board ship to
twin boys, then six months old. The
captain from his vessel held them up to our
view. We praised the beauty of the ocean-
children, and hoped he might carry them
safely to the end of the voyage. Five days
before we reached Tubaui we saw the
Pamutus at a distance.
	We looked through a spy-glass and saw
cocoanut trees. Hope began to spring up
in my heart that the voyage would soon be
over. Two days before we landed I had a
fainting spell, the most severe I ever had in
my life. I called on the Elders to adminis-
ter to me, which they did, and from that
hour my darkness fled; I was relieved in
body and mind!
	As we drew near the island where we
expected to land, the children began to
talk of meeting their father. It never
once occurred to them there could be a dis-
appointment at hand. Frances, our second
daughter, had often been sad on the way.
She would frequently retire to some seclud-
ed corner to indulge in silent reveries.
When the ship hove in sight of Tubaui,
her countenance changed. On the 19th of
October she was constantly on deck look-
ing toward the desired harbor, her coun-
tenance brightening every moment. The
wind was contrary for a whole day. We
looked through a spy-glass and saw cocoa-
nut trees. We strained our eyes to catch a
glimpse of something to confirm our hopes
that all would be well with us when we
landed. Nothing could we see resembling
human life. The great cocoanut trees
towered their majestic heads far above the
horizon, and their long branches, waved in
the breeze! But the living beings were
enshrouded in the thick foliage of the
shrubbery which grows so luxuriantly ove


[Column 1]
the Islands. My highest anticipations were
to set my feet on terra firma once more; to
get fried chickens and fish and fruits of
various kinds—something to satisfy my 
longing appetite, and a soft bed to sleep on.
	In the afternoon of October 21st the pilot
came off to us. I gazed at him from head
to foot as a representative of the people
with whom I must dwell. He was huge
in size, but I thought him good looking.
I told the captain to ask him if “Paraita
was on the Islands?” He replied: “He is
not.” In an instant Ellen’s face was like
marble! She sat down with folded hands
and closed lips. I was prepared for such
tidings; had often thought that such an
event would only be coincident with many
others of a like nature which had fallen to 
my lot. To be thus informed was not the
bitterness of it. He was a prisoner on
Tahiti! The French governor had him in
custody, he was not allowed to leave the
Island. It did not alarm me, so seared
was my heart to hard things. The captain
sent word to Elder Grouard to come on
board. During the interval the children
walked thoughtfully about the deck. At
length Messrs. Grouard and Alexander
came off to the brig. They could scarcely
believe their own eyes! To see so many
American brethren coming to dwell with
them, to relieve them of their solitude,
filled them with joy and surprise! Mr.
Grouard had been for seven years shut out,
as it were, from the world, having been
sent with Mr. Pratt in the year ’43 from
Nauvoo. He has acquired an air of dig-
nity and sobriety, assimilating him with a
Catholic monk. His eyes are sunken, ap-
parently with sorrow, and his whole ap-
pearance at the time was grave and majestic.
I had seen him in the vigor and bloom of
youth, and thought him a gay, lively man.
Now his look was full of wisdom and years.
He very respectfully invited us to go on
shore.
	We were conducted over the coral reefs
with great care. The Island at a distance
presents a picturesque appearance, the
beach being skirted with ito and burau
trees, also with bananas and cocoanuts
and a variety of shrubbery of the most vivid
green. It being the 21st of October, the
season we expect to see the leaves turning
yellow and falling off, not to see the least
sign of decay conveys to the mind a feeling
of admiration.
	The buildings, though far from elegant,
have an appearance of romance. Little
white cottages in the woods,shining through
the green trees, away from the bustle of
the busy world far over the mighty ocean!
To find the remnants of Israel enjoying,
though in a rude state, an incessant spring!
The idea was exalting to one tired of the 
sea as I was. The buildings are low but
very long, plastered inside and out with
lime made from the coral rock; there can-
not be a purer white. I thought of eating
the good fruit, and weak as I was, I walked
with a light step. Multitudes of people
assembled on the beach to welcome us. As
soon as they could reach our hands, they
saluted us in Tahiti: “Ea ora nu ae, te fetii
nu Paraita,” each by our Tahitian names,
as we were introduced by Mr. Grouard.
	A native man who held the office of
“alcalda,” according to French jurispru-
dence, came and took me by the hand, say-
ing, “vau araitai oe” (I will lead you.)
He had a piece of blue drilling bound
about his loins, a sailor jacket, which he
wore without a shirt: that was his com-

[Column 2]
mon suit. On Sabbath days he dressed
like a white man. We took the lead; the
remainder of the company followed at a re-
spectful distance. I could hear them
laughing behind us, but I did not look
back, so intent was I in talking to my
guide. We soon reached the place where
the former friends of “Paraita” resided: I
mean those with whom he had his home,
they were all his friends. Nabotu and
Terii were their names. The latter was
sick with the measles. As soon as she
heard “Paraita’s” family had come, she
rose by faith above the disease and com-
menced arranging supper for us. In the
same house we found Mr. Grouard’s
native wife with a young babe. She ap-
peared exceedingly timid, would not stay
in the house. We tried to win her con-
fidence, talked by an interpreter, assuring
her we were her friends, that we had come
to do her good. The supper was set before
us, every dish entirely new, except fowl
and fish. Everything was relished. I felt
no aversion to anything that was called
food, it was all delicious to my taste.
	The whole population assembled to
greet us: “Ia ora na otou te haera ra mai to
Tubuai nai,” (Peace be to you in coming
to Tubaui) went around and around.
Evening drew near, the singers came in to
sing. Long grass was laid for a floor, on
which they were seated. The music was
delightful! Their voices are loud and
clear, and no people can excel them in
keeping time. Queen Rapah came with
the rest, and I urged her to be seated in a 
chair, but she refused, so modest was she
and unwilling we should think she wished 
to assume any superiority above the other
women. The moon was then at its full,
and the house was surrounded with shade
trees covered with bright yellow flowers. I
looked upon the humble people seated on
the ground singing the praises of God. I
gazed  upon the scenery which surrounded
the dwellings: the pale rays of the moon
shining through the trees in a thousand
shapes; the tall cocoanuts waving their
branches high up in the azure vault; the
great itoes above all the rest; seen by the
queen of night, while the imagination was
warm with the reflection of the great dis-
tance we had come and the suffering we
had endured, all conspired to fill the mind
with ideas great with meaning. I said,
“God is in it all!”
	Brother Grouard sought to encourage us.
He believed that when the Messenger
(king’s vessel) returned from Tahiti,
Brother Pratt would come: it did not thus
prove. The idea of his being a prisoner
was painful to me, except when I con-
sidered it was for the Gospel’s sake we had
come to a strange world.
	A whole week was spent in conversa-
tion. Mr. Grouard had been preaching
among the Islands for seven years, had
heard very little from the Church. Great
things had transpired, he had many ques-
tions to ask. Hoatan, the native chief
who had the charge of us, ordered the
house repaired and the lot fenced. Seventy
banana trees were planted, and a garden
made with the seeds we had brought. Near-
ly all were new to the natives, they had
never seen any of our common vegetables.
Here in October we make our gardens.
Such cabbages I never saw grown before:
mammoth in very deed! Very soon we
had a fine flower garden to look upon. We
planted corn in November. We had only
to dig up the ground with a spade, it was

[Column 3]
loamy and mellow, everything grows ap-
parently by magic.
	Sister Grouard soon became accustomed
to our habits, and would in a small degree
conform to them. We find her rather a 
sensible, intelligent, ingenious woman.
	A letter arrived stating that the governor
on Tahiti would not permit Mr. Pratt to
leave that Island until all the Mormon
Elders would assemble in Tahiti and sign a
document, entering into an agreement to
teach nothing derogatory to the French
government. This was a trial of our pa-
tience, but we strove to bear it as graciously
as was possible. The good people were so
sympathetic they were ready to weep over
us. 
	The second week we all went to Mahu, a
village on the opposite side of the Island,
five miles over a mountain. We could
walk beneath the shade of trees the whole 
distance. It was where Mr. Pratt landed
when first he came on his mission. The
same family were still living in the same
house where he took up his abode.
They were very friendly. Their little
daughter, thirteen years of age, was the
wedded wife of Brother Alexander, the first
man on the Island baptized into the Mor-
mon faith. She looked like a mere child
and conducted herself like one. The old “Fere
bare ra” (prayer house) stood near the
beach, a tall ito tree in front of each door.
I went into the house where six years be-
fore my husband had been wont to pray. In
the pulpit, just as the sun was setting, I
kneeled before the Lord to thank Him for
preserving all our lives while crossing the 
great waters.
	The second day I returned on horseback.
The children complained of being lonely,
mourned the absence of their father. We
amused ourselves by teaching the native
women how to knit, and studying the lan-
guage.
	On Christmas Sister Crosby and I made
a feast for our families, inviting what few
white brethren and sisters were on our side
of the Island. We had string beans, cucum-
bers and green corn. That was something
to communicate to our kindred in the cold
regions of Lower Canada, where snow at
that season is often six feet deep.
	The people were very anxious to teach
us their language. Nearly all our evenings
were spent in reading and translating the
scriptures. It was a source of great
amusement: there are so many mistakes
made it excites merriment. The house
was thronged at night with natives reading
and singing. Many of them had never
seen a European woman. Our eldest
daughter played the flutina, which amused
and delighted them. It was no little diver-
sion for us to exhibit the little curiosities
we brought with us from San Francisco, to
witness their pleasure and admiration, hear
them eulogize America, and marvel that it
should contain so many wonderful things !
	I brought with me the likeness of the
Prophet Joseph Smith and of Hyrum, which
I hung against the wall of a room in the
mission house. A native brother from the 
Chain Islands, one evening while we were
all seated around the table reading, with-
drew and went into the room to look at the
picture. He kneeled before it in order
that the painting might come in range
with his eyes. For a quarter of an hour
he gazed steadfastly upon it, I believe,
without turning his eyes. I did not suppose it
was an act of worship, but he wished to
impress the lineaments of the features upon


[Column 1]
his mind. I could not help admiring his
devotion. I knew his faith was strong in
the Gospel, and he felt a solemn pleasure in
surveying the features of the murdered
Prophet and Patriarch.
	The Island sisters have great faith in the
consecrated oil, which was blessed by Presi-
dent Brigham Young previous to our leav-
ing Salt Lake City. When I told them
from whence it came and the designs of it,
they would invariably bring their sick chil-
dren to me and request me to anoint them
with the holy oil, nothing doubting but
a cure would be effected; and, according to
their faith, often was it done unto them.
	Their habits of devotion are really com-
mendable. At all seasons of the year they
rise at early dawn. The first exercise is to
call the family and fall upon their knees in
prayer. Often before their first meal,
they ring the bell and go to the house of
worship, read a chapter in the Bible, sing a
hymn, and, if their teacher is present, they
ask questions upon portions they do not fully
understand, seldom continuing their ex-
ercises more than a half hour. They make
many feasts, sometimes calling all within
hailing distance to come and partake. At
other times they send the food to their
kindred and neighbors, and a generous,
share to their teachers.
	On January 4th, 1851, Elder Grouard
made a great feast to celebrate his 33rd
birthday. It was held at his own dwelling
house, a very long building. About one
hundred people were invited. The food
was purely native, with the exception of
fifty mince pies, which the missionaries’
wives contributed. The guests were not
assembled until the food was all arranged
in order. Mats were laid the whole length
of the building, large leaves laid for plates,
likewise plaited for tablecloths. Through
the centre was laid the food, a cocoanut at
each plate; the shells being a bright orange
contrasted beautifully with the green leaves.
	I shall never forget the admiration I felt as
I gazed upon that table spread upon the
ground! Never in my life had I seen any-
thing to exceed it! Mats were laid for the
guests to be seated. A few there were who
had knives and forks. All present seemed
to have merry hearts. Mr. Grouard rend-
ered thanks to the Great Disposer of events,
that he had lived to see that joyful day,
even to greet so many of his friends from
America and have them present on that
grand occasion! When all had feasted to a
fullness, and while remaining seated, Mr.
Grouard arose and addressed the assembly
in a most appropriate manner. He referred
to his long confinement on the Islands, be-
lieving it to be his duty to remain there till
the Elders were sent to relieve him. Thirty-
three years of his life had passed away;
truly his life had been an eventful one.
He felt that the Lord had heard his pray-
ers, in that he had lived to see the faces of
so many of his former friends, and to re-
joice with them in the midst of a humble
and loving people, whose hearts were
warm towards us and were ready to re-
ceive the truth. Thirty-three years from
that day, when he should have attained his
sixty-sixth year, he said, "I will make
another feast." All present, if living,
should be invited. We all responded a
hearty amen!
	Mr. Grouard is a gentleman distinguished
for beauty and graceful appearance; though
by being excluded too much from society,
he has acquired too large a share of gravity
and sedateness. He combines the orator, phil-

[Column 2]
osopher and preacher of righteousness, with
the sailor and native man of the Island. He
has married two Island women. The first
died, leaving him a little daughter one
month old. The second has borne him three
sons. The girl, now four years of age, was
placed in our eldest daughter’s charge, a very
sprightly child. She had never learned
one word of English from her father, which
to us seemed very surprising. With her
new teacher she made rapid improvement.
[To be continued.]


WOMAN’S EXPONENT.


WOMAN’S EXPONENT.


[Column 1]
', 'AWAY FROM HOME.

	By courtesy of Brother Geo. H. Taylor,
supt. of 14th Wd. Sunday School, we are
enabled to publish the following interesting
epistle from Elder Samuel P. Richards.

	I well remember with what feelings it
was that I arose before the school to bid
good bye to my mates, and the school with
which I had been connected ever since its
first organization in the ward; realizing at
the time, that I was to leave probably for
years, and possibly for ever, to go forth and
labor in the vineyard of the Lord, in a for-
eign land and among a people speaking a
language foreign to my own, and to pro-
claim to them the truths of the everlasting
Gospel, trusting in God alone for assist-
ance.
	My associations with the Sunday School
were always pleasant and many are the
good principles and ideas I there learned,
that have been useful to me, and which I
never shall forget.
	I left home, relatives and friends on the
first day of Nov. 1873, accompanied to Og-
den by some of my relatives, whom I bid
good bye at the depot at 7.15, p. m. when
the cars started and we sped swiftly on the
way.
	Although the separation from those near
and dear to me left feelings of sadness in
my heart, yet it was not long before they
were expelled by the excitement with
which I was surrounded, and through my
desire to see the new scenery and every
thing that was of interest to be seen on the
way, for it was a beautiful moonlight night,
and objects at a considerable distance, could
be seen almost as distinctly as at noon-day.
	Our little party consisted of six persons.
viz. R. G. Lambert and wife, H. S. Wool-
ley and wife, B. Stringham Jr. and myself,
all young, the eldest of the company being
but 23 years old.
	We were all of a somewhat lively dispos-
ition and consequently did not hug sorrow,
which somewhat surprised the passengers
on the train, for numbers of them asked us
"how it was that we, leaving our homes
under such circumstances, could do so and
feel so light hearted?" Of course they did
not know as we did, that we were going ac-
cording to the call of the true servants of
God, to do His will and His work, and that
He did not require us to pull long faces and
to go with downcast countenances.
	To enter into minutiae and tell about all I
saw on that short, swift ride of two days
and two nights, over nine hundred miles
of road of the most beautiful scenery,
would take up too much of my time, and the
space of this letter, besides tiring your pa-
tience. Suffice it to say that every minute,
almost, exhibited some thing new and in-
teresting to one who had never before left
home in the valleys of the Rocky Moun-
tains.
	The second day of our traveling we pass-
ed a great deal of beautiful scenery through
Truckee, from where large quantities of
lumber are shipped to S. L. City, and over
the Sierra Nevada mountains. Over that
part of the road, there are a great many R.
R. stations where little towns have sprung
up and where the trains stop for a few min-
utes at each place. Shortly before reach-
ing a station the engine whistle would
blow which would apprise the people of the
near approach of a train. Hardly would
the gratings of the wheels of the stopping
cars cease, when numbers of boys and girls,

[Column 2]
who probably never had the privilege of
attending Sunday school, would be seen
running to and fro with baskets on their
arms, crying out to the passengers to buy
their fruits; for they were little fruit ven-
ders.
	When we arrived at Sacramento, and
while the train was stopping for half an
hour, I went on board a large steam boat
standing in the Saeramento, near its bank.
	There were also many other vessels near
by and these were the first that I had ever
seen. It was also the first time that I had
ever seen a really large river and city,
and also so many people gathered together
at one place; with the exception of when
the semi-annual conferences were held in S.
L. City. But the Sacramento R. R. depot
is t[--]s thronged nearly every day.
	At 7.30 p. m. on Monday the 2nd day
from home, the cars drew up at "Oakland"
ferry, on the opposite side of the harbor
from San Francisco and we—the passengers
—were ushered, or huddled rather, into
what at first appeard to be a sheep pen, in-
closed by a high picket fence, but which we
soon found out to be the entrance to the
harbor ferry boat.  The way we went in
there was like driving sheep into a corral,
for there were a great many people waiting
to go in and the entrance, or gate, was a
narrow one, admitting only one person at a
time, for no one was allowed to go in who
did not have a ticket to present to the keep-
er.
	At the wharf on the opposite side there
was an immense crowd assembled and it
was with difficulty that we followed our
guide to the coach that was to take us to
the hotel. It seemed but a minute 'til I
found myself on top of that coach with a
number of other,s and was riding through
crooked winding streets and cross roads, in
the midst of rumbling vehicles and clatter-
ing of horses hoofs over the pavement of
rough cobble stones, and we came to a stand
in front of the hotel.
	What a relief it seemed to get into a
quiet room where we could hear ourselves
talk. I almost imagine I can hear that
rumbling noise now...We previously ex-
pected to spend but one day in San Francis-
co, but the sailing of the vessel, which was
to take us, was postponed from Wednesday
to Saturday. We passed away the time
in looking about the city and visiting places
of note and interest.
	'Twas here that I first saw, in large num-
bers the news boys and boot blacks, of
whom most Sunday School children in Utah
have heard so often. It was really amus-
ing to see and hear them parading the
streets at their different avocations. One
thing I did not notice in the metropolis of
California, but which is prevalent in most
large cities, and that was many beggars in
the streets asking for alms.
	Our time was both pleasantly and profita-
bly spent in the large city, and the rustic
country lad saw many things to open his
eyes and cause him to wonder. My young
Sunday School friends can never realize how
many people there are in the world, 'til
they have the privilege of visiting some of
the large cities of the world.
	Saturday afternoon, Nov. 8th, we went
on board the Bark "Comet," which was to
carry us across the great Pacific ocean to
our destination. Sails were raised, and a
small vessel, called a "tug" was hitched
along side of the "Bark" and hauled it out
past the "Golden Gate," the name given to
the entrance to the harbor. There are many
beautiful sights to be seen in San Francisco
but that from the deck of a vessel while leav-

[Column 3]
ing the city and sailing out through the
bay is really grand to behold.
	While the "tug" was fast to our vessel, it
glided smoothly over the surface of the
sea, and everybody on board seemed to feel
first rate; but just as soon as the "tug" let
go, the vessel began to roll and rock, and
then, O! what a change came o'er their (the
passenger's) dreams. One by one they be-
gan to descend into the cabin, and to hunt
for their berths, or beds, and long before
we were out of sight of the "Golden State,"-
the once crowded deck of the vessel was al-
most entirely deserted. This was the re-
sult of sea sickness, something which af-
fects nearly all, who live on land entirely,
when they first go to sea.
	Not being troubled with sea sickness my
self I could not sympathize with those who
were; but it is such a disagreeable thing
that those who are afflicted with it do not
care, at the time, whether the "ship sinks
or floats" or whether they "live or die."
All but two of our little party were sick,
but in the course of three or four days they
were well and hearty, and we all enjoyed as
best we could, the balance of our long tedi-
ous sea voyage.
	On the morning of the 17th day out from
San Francisco, we arose from our berths
and found ourselves in sight of land, the
vessel sailing busily along the island of
"Oahu," the principal of this, the "Sand-
wich Islands," group. This made every
heart glad, for all were weary of the sea
voyage and wishing to get to land.
	Of course the vessel was immediately in
an uproar, the sailors making preparations
to enter the harbor and the passengers pre-
paring to go ashore. Before we came in
sight of Honolulu, the pilot boat was seen
sailing toward us, for the pilot had pre-
viously been signaled to come aboard and
take the vessel in.
	Having been sent to labor amongst the
inhabitants of these islands, I was, of
cource, naturally anxious to see what the
people looked like and also to hear their
language. I therefore drew near where the
boat came along side of the "Bark." Imag-
ine my astonishment on beholding some
half dozen large, dark-skinned, naked na-
tives; at least their only article of clothing
was what is termed in native, a "malo,"
tied about their loins. When talking it
seemed to me that what they said was one,
long, continuous word, uttered in one breath,
and the question immediately sprung up
within myself; "I wonder if I shall ever be
able to understand any one, who speaks as
fast as they appear to talk?" Then remem-
bering the promise of the servants of God
"that I should obtain a knowledge of the
language of the people" the question was
speedily answered.
	When we left home (S. L. City) the trees
had shed their leaves, winter had put on
its own peculiar robes, and we had seen the
snow laying on the ground. But on arriv-
ing here it seemed like passing from win-
ter to spring; for here the grass was green
and growing on the hills, the leaves green
on the trees and the flowers in bloom in the
neat, pretty gardens.
	It was the sudden change that made
every place in Honolulu, (the harbor city
and capital of this group) and on the island
appear so beautiful. It was, to us, like en-
tering into a paradise.
	As soon as the native saints heard of our
arrival they flocked to where we were stop-
ping, to see us; but as they could not speak
a word of English, nor we a word of Hawa-
iian, we could not converse with them
only by motions until they brought in an in-


terpreter, when we told them all the news
from the "Valleys of the Mountains."
	We rode on horse back 32 miles to Laie,
the head quarters of the church on these
islands, and over the most beautiful part of
that island. The natives, men, women
and children, gathered around as soon as we
arrived to see the new comers, and brought
us "Leis" (wreaths of flowers strung like
beads) with which they nearly covered the
wives of the brethren; and they also sang
a variety of native songs for us.
	Our reception was indeed a pleasant one,
the only regret we had being, that we could
not utter a sentence of native language.
	We reached Laie on the 28th of Nov., and
on the 13th of the following month (Dec.) I
left the brethren and sisters (from Zion,) and
the pleasant associations with them, and
started off to live amongst the natives and
study their language. One of the brethren
who understood the native tongue accom-
panied me to a little village called Kawai-
kepal," about 22 miles from Laie, and after
holding a meeting with the saints, introduc-
ing me to them and seeing me provided
for, he returned and left me alone with the
natives.
	Such questions as "Wasn't you home sick?
Didn't you get the blues? Were you not very
lonesome there all alone with no one to talk
to but natives?" Might arise in the minds
of some; but I can truly say, in answer to
such questions, no, not at all.
	Had I had nothing to do I certainly would
have been lonely and home sick, but I had
plenty to occupy my time in studying the
language, committing words and sentences
to memory, and in trying to converse with
the natives for practice.
	Whenever a tired or lonely feeling would
come over me I would take a stroll and hunt
for some one with whom to try and con-
verse, and all such feelings would soon be
expelled.
	In this way I spent three months study-
ing, at that one place, returning to Laie pre-
vious to our April conference, and a very
short three months it seemed to me, my
mind being so engrossed in seeking for the
language, that the time passed very rapidly.
	I presume that most of the Sunday School
scholars, at least the larger ones, have heard
at some time or other, about the principal
article of native food called "Poi," and have
formed the opinion, or conceived the idea,
that it is a very disagreeable thing for a
white person to eat. Let a person who had
never tasted, look at it and he would natur-
ally think so; but when a person once gets
used to it there is no food, which I can think
of, that is more pleasant and agreeable; es-
pecially when hungry.
	It is made of a vegetable that grows in
the water and is somewhat similar to the
beet in shape, but which grows with the
larger end downwards. After the vegeta-
ble has been cooked with steam, being pla-
ced over red hot rocks and then covered
over tightly to prevent the steam from es-
caping, it is then pounded up fine on a
board with a rock, after which it is mixed
with water and formed into a paste of suit-
able thickness to suit the persons eating it.
The natives always eat it with their fingers,
It is eaten with fish fowl or meat of some
kind.
	We seldom find knife, fork or spoon, at a
native house, and not being accustomed to
eat with our fingers, we therefore have to
take such articles along with us in our trav-
els. "Poi" is not the only article of food;
but there are potatoes, squash melons, and
a variety of delicious fruits. It may seem
strange to the young people of S. L. City,
to be told that water melons are now ripe
here in the month of December, which is
almost the middle of winter there, but it is
so. I have, myself, eaten water melon here
this month, and there are ripe fruits of
some kind, fresh and growing, to be had all
the year round.
	We have no such thing as snow or frost
on these islands, it is summer all the year
round; consistently it is only by thinking
of the months that I can remember when it
is winter at home.
	There are many peculiarities of the na-
tives that would be interesting to relate, 
but I have not time to enlarge upon them.
The people generally, are kind and very
hospitable, and in all my travels I never
was refused hospitality but two or three
times when I asked for it. They are al-
ways free to give when they have, especi-
ally anything to eat; and it is a custom
with them, when ever a stranger or neigh-
bor comes to their place, to ask him, or her
to eat, providing they have anything, and
as it is not etiquette to refuse, a native will
sometimes when travelling around, eat from
three to ten times a day. I have often had
to eat when I really did not wish to, to
avoid giving offense.
	Natives seldom take thought for to-mor-
row, but if they get a sumptuous supply to-
day they turn in and eat it all up if they
can, and let the morrow take thought for
itself. Through this way of living they are
often compelled to go hungry.
	The men and boys usually do all the cook-
ing, and the women and girls work in the
"Lois" (where the food is cultivated in wa-
ter) and in the potato patches along with
the men.
	This people are generally very fond of
animals and it is no unusual thing to see
them keeping pigs, dogs, cats, chickens,
turkeys and geese, and allowing them to
run in and out of the house just as they
please; but when we visit and stay with
them they drive all the animals and fowls
out of doors.
	I have slept in native houses when there
were dogs, cats, and pigs within, besides
any quantity of little animals such as mice,
cockroaches, fleas and mosquitoes. Natives
can live and be happy in the midst of all
these things.
	How thankful to the Lord the children of
the Latter-Day Saints shoudl be, that they
have neat, clean, comfortable houses in
which to live, and kind parents to care for
them and teach them cleanly habits.
	Not so with the children of this people;
they run in a manner wild; go where they
please; when they please; and do just
about as they like. The parents have very
little control over their children.
	According to the law of the land every
child, when old enough, is compelled to at-
tend school so many hours a day; conse-
quently nearly every body, old and young
can read and write; but when out of school
their time is spent in play, for they seldom
do any work while attending school, which
is until they are from 12 to 16 years old.
	If it was not that they are dependent on
their parents for food, they would not work
at all or obey their parents commands. But
as it is they have to work a little and give
some heed to their parents in order to get
their food. Unless the young girls and
boys go to some English or high school, they
are not taught to be industrious, to do any
neat or useful work, learn trades or keep
themselves neat and tidy.
	Of course there are honorable exceptions
to this general rule but they are very few.
I believe the children living on Laie dress
better, are more cleanly, better behaved,
and know more about useful and beneficial
employment than do the children of any
other place on these islands, outside of the
English schools where they make it a point
to teach these things.
	Again, there is some little respect shown
there, to virtue and morality, which is not
the case with the mass of the people through-
out these islands. The almost total disre-
gard to these great and Godly principles is
the great cause of the astonishlingly rapid
decrease in the population of these islands
from over eighty thousand in 1850, to about
twenty thousand at the present time. An-
other cause is, that they have the true and
everlasting Gospel preached in their midst,
but they disregard its mandates, will not
obey its principles, nor will they hearken to
the councels and instructions of the ser-
vants of God laboring in their midst; there-
fore the wrath of the Almighty rests up-
on them.
	I have traveled around every island of this
group, where people are living, and we are
allowed to go; and many times have the
natives pointed out to me places where
there were cities, villages and lands that
were thickly populated with natives when
Geo. Q. Cannon and a number of the breth-
ren came down here in 1850, to open up the
Gospel of Life to the people, that now are
desolate and barren wastes.
	When the remnants of Israel living on
these islands, are gathered out to the land
of their inheritance, their numbers will be
very few indeed unless the Lord works a
wonderful change with the people.
	Since October conference I have been
traveling continually from one island to an-
other, returning to head quarters at the end
of each six months, to attend the confer-
ence, rest a couple of weeks or so, and then
start off again.
	Our labors amongst the outsiders, in pro-
claiming the Gospel to them is, to look at it
naturally, almost ineffectual, for they have
