heard it so often, and have set their minds
against it through fear of their priests, that
they do not want to hear it any more.
	We therefore cannot do much but travel
amongst the sains, or the members of the
church, and teach and encourage them to do
right, live their religion, and keep the com-
mandments of God.
	It is that there are a few souls, who are
honest and trying to keep the command-
ments of the Lord, that gives us encourage-
ment in our labors. Much good can be done
by setting a good example if in no other
way.
	I have often attended the public meetings
of the Calvinists and Catholics the only
Christian sects established among the na-
tives, but never had the opportunity grant-
ed me of speaking in their churches but
twice. The priests fear to grant us the priv-
ilege lest we convert their members and
they (the priests) thereby loose the dollars
and cents that they get from them (the
members). They labor for money, and that
only.
	Br. Stringham (my fellow laborer) and
I recently visited the leper settlement on
the island of "Molokai." It almost seems
that the Lord foresaw the calamity that was
coming upon this people, and therefore pre-
pared that wonderful and applicable natu-
ral prison for those of the loathsome dis-
ease; for there are but two ways of entrance
or to egress from the place, and those are by
sea and down a very high and steep moun-
tain path.
	We slept at the settlement one night held
meeting with the members of the church,
and preached to an audience of lepers. It


is a sorrowful and heart-rending sight to see
the condition in which those poor human
beings exist, for it is a mere existence.
Not only are grown people affected with it
but also children and even babes. On my
former visit I saw them in every form of
the disease, from the first outbreaking on
the babe to the poor helpless being who
was within a few hours of undergoing that
great separation of soul and body, for then I
visited the hospital wards; but the last vis-
it I avoided the wards, not wishing to again
behold the awful sight. God will punish
those who disregard His laws.
	Bro. J. H. Napela, whom many may re-
member having seen in Utah in 1870, and
who is still at the settlement attending on
his wife, wishes a kind remembrance to all
with whom he made acquaintance while in
Salt Lake City.
	There are now seven hundred lepers
at the settlement, and about that many
more not yet gathered in from amongst the
people. Many having the disease are secre-
ted by their friends.
	As I have to write in a rather uncomfort-
able position, laying flat on my stomach on
a native mat, for in the native houses we
seldom ever find either stools, chairs or ta-
bles, I begin to feel tired. And I fear that
the length of this letter will wear out your
patience. What I have written I sincerely
hope will be of interest to all who pursue or
hear it read; for I have penned just such
thoughts as came to my mind that I sup-
posed would be of interest.
	I would say to the children of the Sun-
day School: Continue to be punctual attend-
ers; hearken to and carry out the counsel
and instructions of your teachers; store your
minds with useful knowledge, by reading
good books, and especially the publications
of the church; cease to read all fictitious
novels for they will never do you any good;
always be obedient unto your parents and
God will bless you.
	If opportunity offers I may at some fu-
ture day, again write to the school. *	*


WOMAN'S EXPONENT.	143


144		WOMAN’S EXPONENT.


']

Name: 54_mrs_regent_revolution_daughters revolution
Representative_Docs:
['DAUGHTERS OF THE REVOLUTION.
	The Utah State Society Daughters of
the Revolution celebrated in a patriotic
manner the anniversary of the Battle of
Lexington, April 19, at the headquarters in
the Templeton Building in this city, with
sentiments and speeches. The decora-
tions were in the Society colors, also
flowers and vases, stars and stripes being
conspicuously displayed. The Regent,
Mrs. Mary A. H. Cannon, presided with
dignity and grace. There were a few
invited guests present who took part in
the proceedings.
	The 16th of May the members of the
Society were the guests of the Regent and
the Corresponding Secretary, and were
entertained at the residence of Mrs.
Emily H. C. Willey Corresponding Sec-
retary. The elegant new home was dec-
orated in the Society colors and beauti-
fully draped, and a display of flags large
and small and vases of wild flowers, buff
and blue were scattered about the rooms
giving it quite an artistic, floral appear-
ance. The Regent gave the report of
her year’s work which was very interest-
ingly written and perfectly given and


elicited considerable praise. Although
the day was very unfavorable and kept a
good share of the members away, yet it
was an enjoyable afternoon and when
the guests assembled in the dining room,
the dainty manner in which the viands
were decorated in colors was indeed a
happy surprise, and evinced the most
delicate ingenuity on the part of the
hostesses. The daintiness, the color
scheme, the ribbons and appurtenances,
did not detract from the flavor or deli-
ciousness of the various dishes prepared
by these graduates of the Boston Cook-
ing School, and all enjoyed the luncheon
and were bountifully served. It was a
very happy day and will long be remem-
bered by those who attended. Much
regret was expressed for the absent mem-
bers.
	The annual meeting and election of
officers was held on June 12, 1906, in the
Society Room, the Regent, Mrs. M.A H.
Cannon, presiding. Minutes of the an-
nual meeting in June, 1905, were read
and approved, and brief reports were
given by all the officers present, and the
Treasurer’s report, which was written,
and very complete, was read by the Sec-
retary in the absence of the Treasurer.
The election of officers followed and re-
sulted in the following officers for the
ensuing year. Regent, Mrs. Mary E. R.
Webber, First Vice Regent, Mr Alice
M. Horne, Second Vice Regent, Mrs.
Diana F. Rich; Recording Secretary Mrs.
Isabel M. W. Sears, Assistant Recording
Secretary, Mrs. Emily Y. Clawson, Cor-
responding Secretary, Mrs. Clarice Y.
Spencer, Treasurer, Mrs. Phoebe Y.
Beatie, Registrar, Mrs. Maria Y.
Dougall, Historian, Mrs. Julia P. M.
Farnsworth, Chaplain, Mrs. Sarah F.
Smith. This completed the business of
the day,and the next annual meeting will
be held in June, 1907. The regular
monthly meetings will commence again,
September 16, allowing three months
summer vacation.


[Column 1]
', "ANNUAL MEETING D. R.
	The annual meeting of the Utah State
Society daughters of the Revolution was
held on Friday, June 10, 1904, at 10:30 in
room 509 Templeton Building, Salt Lake
City, Regent Isabel M. Whitney Sears pre-
siding, a majority of members present.
After prayer, the secretary, Mrs. Lucy
Woodruff Smith, read the minutes of the
May meeting and the last annual meeting,
June 10, 1903. The minutes of both meet-
ings were approved.
	A letter was read from the General Soci-
ety D. R. acknowledging the ten dollars
given by the Utah Society towards the tes-
timonial tendered to Miss Adeline W. Ster-
ling, the retiring Pre[-]ident General. Letter
was read from Mrs. Julia P.M. Farnsworth,
Chaplain, who was absent in Beaver at the
time, expressing her good wishes, and re-
gret at being away on so important an oc-
casion.
	Reports were then given by the officers
who had served during the past year. The
Regent, Mrs. I. M. W. Sears, referred to
the report made by her to the General So-
ciety, and expressed briefly her great pleas-
ure in the year’s work, thanking the mem-
bers for their support. Reports were given
by First Vice-Regent, Mrs. Phebe Young
Beatie, and S cond Vice-Regent, Mrs. Myra
Young Rossiter, Rec Sec’y, Mrs. Lucy W.
Smith, Cor. Sec’y, Mrs. Florence S. Sears,
Treasurer, Mrs. Elizabeth R. Wright, Reg-
istrar, Mrs. Maria Young Dougall, and
Historian, Mrs. E. B. Wells.
	It was moved by Mrs. Annie L. King,
seconded by Mrs. Ella W. Hyde, that all
the reports be accepted with thanks—car-
ried. It was moved by Mrs. Beatie and
seconded by several that a full report of the
meeting, including the Regent’s report,
to the General Society, be published in the
WOMAN'S EXPONENT—carried.
	Regent announced that the next business
would be the election of officers for the en-
suing year. The following members were
elected, nominations from the floor:
	Regent, Mrs. Phebe Young Beatie; First
Vice-Regent, Mrs Mary E. R. Webber;
Second Vice-Regent, Mrs. Annie Louise L.
King; Rec. Sec’y, Mrs. Clarissa Y. Spencer;
Asst. Sec’y, Mrs. Carol Y. Cannon; Cor.
Sec’y, Mrs. I. M. W. Sears; Treas., Mrs. Ella
W. Hyde; Registrar, Mrs. Maria Y. Dougall;
Historian, Mrs. E. B. Wells; Chaplain,
Mrs. Lucy W. Smith.
	Vacancies in the Council will be filled by
appointment of the Regent later. Annual
meeting closed with prayer and adjourned
to June 10, 1904.	E. B- W.


The Duke of Norfolk’s carelessness of
rank and homely manner make it almost
impossible to think of him as a duke.
There is probably some truth in the stories
which have their origin in his disregard of
dress. The Duke was going to Stonyhurst
College for Speech day, and a carriage met
him at the railway station, Two tall and
well-dressed gentlemen preceded him, and
as they reached the carriage an official
asked, “Stonyhurst, College, gentlemen?”
Upon replying “Yes,” they were allowed
to enter the carriage. Behind them came a
man not by any means faultlessly dressed,
and as he was about to step into the carri-
age the official said, “Stand away, please—
Duke of Norfolk’s carriage!” “Yes, I
know,” said the other,—“I am the Duke
of Norfolk!”


WOMAN'S EXPONENT.


For the Exponent.]
THE FLOATING TEA PARTY.
A MEMORY OF BERLIN.
From Wansu's pier we sail away,
	Adown the Havel fair;
By verdant banks and castles gray,
	Bright sunshine in the air.
How clear the water shimm’ring flows,
	As swift we speed along!
The small craft ever by us goes, 
With greeting and with song.
The band plays in the steamer’s bow,
	Songs famed in ev’ry land,
No foreign thought these guests allow,
	Where warm is proffered friendship’s hand.
O, Potsdam rising there in view,
	Where palaces abound;
Of thee come mem’ries not a few,
	Thy grandeur all around.
And there dwelt lovely Queen Louise,
	So famed in history;
The palace almost hid by trees,
	Her refuge proved to be.
Though many years have passed away.
	She is beloved still;
Her virtues living e’en today,—
	And they pure thoughts instil
Romantic, green is yonder isle,
	The steamer circles round;
While strains of music e’er beguile
The throng, with restful sound.
All hearts are beating full of praise,
	For honor freely shown.
The dainty viands—German ways,
	Politeness all their own.
All hail Berlin, most generous host!
	All hail: Berlin the fair;
‘‘Yet greater be” this is our toast.
	Thou city of “The Bear.”
					LYDIA D. ALDER.


", "NEW YORK CITY.

	The Annual Meetings of the General
Society of the Daughters of the Revolution,
held in New York City, Monday and
Tuesday, April 23rd and 24th, 1900, were
of an interesting character and well attended
by members from almost all parts of the
country, and the usual routine business of
such gatherings was transacted.
	A preliminary meeting was held jn the
Society’s headquarters, and a nominating
committee elected, to prepare a ticket, etc.
The public meetings convened in the same
building, situated on Fifth Avenue and
corner of 20th Street, a large and commo-
dious hall, handsomely decorated for the
occasion, with the stars and stripes taste-
fully draped and pictures of heroes and
heroines, etc. Each state was designated
by a banner with the name, and all dele-
gates were supplied with badges.
	The President General, Mrs. Henry
Sanger Snow occupied the chair. She is a
very charming woman and has completely
won the hearts of all the Daughters who
have been associated with her during her
term of office. Her address was very bril-
lian and concise, dealing with facts in a
very gentle and persuasive manner, and
was specially significant of the work in
hand.
	The society moves as smoothly as the
sun and his satellites toward one common
object Loyalty and patriotic effort are al-
ways the general aim. Everywhere in
local chapters and State societies the gener-
al work has gone on with unity, strength
and enthusiasm of purpose. We have come
together to transact the most important
business in our societies life—to elect the
officers who are to carry on the work of the
organization for the two coming years. In
the fulfillment of this duty we are to per-
mit nothing of local or personal prejudice
and jealousy to influence us, and we are to
accept with the fullest loyalty the expres-
sion of the general will, whatever our in-
dividual disappointment may be.
	The Recording Secretary General in a
very gracious and appropriate speech
made the presentation of a “Loving Cup,”
the gift of the Society at large, which Mrs.
Snow responded to in a very brief but affec-
tionate and particularly happy manner.
	Reports from other General Officers fol-
lowed this part of the exercises, most of
them very brief, relating to each one’s own
department of work. We noted the His- 
torian General mentioned her report from
the Utah Historian, who looked after her
domestic affairs, managed her home, was a
member of the Legislature and had a baby
four months old; this elicited considerable
applause.
	These reports extended into the afternoon
and after they were finished the election of
officers began. There was considerable dis-
cussion upon the floor, meantime tellers
were appointed. Several nominations
were made, but finally the president Gener-
al was elected, Miss Adeline W. Sterling,
First Vice-President of the recent adminis-
tration.
	Tuesday morning the delegates were,
early in their seats and the election began.
There were several nominees for Vice-
President, but when it came to the Record-
ing Secretary General, eighteen candidates
were nominated before it was possible , to
get one to accept the office, as it is well


[Column 1]
known it is one of the most arduous posi-
tions in any organization. The Registrar
was re-elected by acclamation. She had
been a very efficient officer and was appar- 
ently a great favorite.
	The officers elected were: President
General, Miss Adeline W. Sterling, N. J.;
First Vice-President General, Mrs. Nathan-
iel S. Heap, Penn.; Second Vice-President-
General, Mrs. Mary A. Chapman, Mass;
Recording Secretary-General, Mrs. Carlton
M. Moody, Penn.; Corresponding Secretary-
General, Mrs. George B. Wallis, Jr., N. Y.,
Treasurer-General, Mrs. Joseph J. Casey,
N. Y.; Historian-General, Mrs. J. R.
Ditmars, N. J.; Librarian-General, Mrs.
Alexander M. Ferris, Mass.
BOARD OF MANAGERS.
	Mrs. James B. Grant, Colorado; Mrs. Henry
Sanger Snow, L. I.; Mrs George F. Dan-
els, Mass.; Mrs. David C. Carr, N. Y.;
Mrs. George Hodges, N. J.; Miss Tar-
quinia L. Voss, Indiana; Mrs. Andrew W.
Bray, N. J.
	The time was so fully occupied with the
election that the reports from the several
states had to be read during the intervals
when the tellers were out counting votes.
Massachusetts came in with the largest
number of votes, being over seventy, and
naturally the report was excellent as re-
garded numbers and work, for besides a
State Society, Massachusetts has thirty-two
chapters, and they have historical ground
and many things in their favor, which those
at a remote distance do not enjoy. New
York ranks next in membership and has a
State society and sixteen chapters and have
been successful in many ways. New Jer-
sey and Pennsylvania come next in im- 
portance in the Society D. R’s. Utah,
though the youngest of the Daughters, pre-
sented a report that was specially well re-
ceived and was the only one that extended
an invitation to the General Society to come
and hold an Annual Meeting here sometime
in the near future. Nineteen States in all
were represented, most of the reports were
written and all were read by the Regent of
the State except Utah, which was read by
the delegate, Miss Snow, and two or three
reports that came by mail and were read
by the secretary.
	The election having been decided and the
announcements made, the Amendments to
the Constitution that had been sent out
previously were carefully considered,
some of them causing animated discussion,
and finally after a word of prayer and the
singing of “America,” the Convention ad-
journed after six p m., to meet in Brooklyn,
Long Island, in April, 1901.
D. R. ENTERTAINMENTS.
	Wednesday morning, April 25, at eleven
o’clock, the Daughters of the Revolution
were invited to be present at the unveiling
of a Historical Tablet to be given by the
New York State Society to the famous 
Old Dutch Reformed Collegiate Church, of
that city, Fifth Avenue and Forty-Eighth
Street. 
	The church is quite a handsome one in
its appointments and on this occasion there 
were perhaps two hundred of the D. R.
members assembled to witness the cere-
monies. The music was excellent, and the
tablet which was inscribed “In Memory of
the Officers, Soldiers and Sailors who felt in
the War of the Revolution,” was unveiled
by Miss Mary Gage Lambert of Rowley,
Mass., the youngest child of Jonathan

[Column 2]
Lambert, who when, sixteen years old,
took part in the battle of Bunker Hill.
Miss Lambert is now past eighty years of
age.
	The presentation address was made by
Mrs. Charles F. Roe, Regent of the New
York State D. R., and the wife of Major
General Roe. The tablet was received by
the minister of the Church, Rev. Mr. McKay,
who assured the ladies that it should be
sacredly kept in the archives of the Church.
	During his acknowledgement of the gift
he spoke of the denomination to which this
Church belongs, being the oldest in New
York, it having been founded in 1629 by
the Dutch settlers at Manhattan, and was
afterwards used for barracks in the time of
the Revolution. The present building is
erected on the same site as the original
church; he also stated that the bell which
now rings to call the people together is the
one hidden away in war time, and found by
the sexton when the war was over, and
used by the same sexton many years after-
wards.
	At one o’clock of the same day Mrs.
Henry Sanger Snow, the retiring President-
General of the General Society, gave an
elaborate breakfast at the Savoy Hotel to
both the retiring and incoming officers of
the D. R., and the Regents of the several
States represented at the Convention. The
banqueting hall was handsomely decorated,
the stars and stripes, as also the Society
colors, blue and buff, being effectively
used. The round table which seated fifty
guests was dazzlingly magnificent beyond
description. In the centre was an em-
bankment of green, literally piled with yel-
low lilies and blue corn flowers, and in
regular order around the circle were smaller
ones in circle and semicircle; elegant vases
of roses and carnations added to the artistic
decorations, and at each place a pretty silk
flag stood waving; but the bright appear-
ance of the brilliant women who surrounded
this unique “round table” was the most
pleasing picture of all, and one could not
help wondering why therewas not a photog-
rapher at hand, for it certainly would
have been a good opportunity, and, of
course, each guest would have purchased
a picture.
	During the breakfast Mrs. Snow, in a very
sweet and gracious manner, rose, and while
lifting the “Loving Cup,” which had been
previously brought in, turned to the new
President-General, Miss Sterling, saluting
her first, and then bowing to all seated at
the table, drank a toast to the assembled
guests in a few choice words of loving
tenderness, mentioning the fact that there
were represented nineteen states. The new
President-General also toasted her pre-
decessor in office and then the cup went
round, all the ladies tasting the old Moselle
wine from the new “Loving Cup.” After-
wards handsome little ornamental  favors
were given each guest, that were designated
by the ladies “Our little Juniors.”
	The breakfast was a perfect success, cer-
tainly these social functions bring women in
public life closer together in conversation
and acquaintance than is possible in Con-
gresses and Conventions.
	At three p. m. of the same afternoon the
New York State Society gave a reception to
the D. R. from all parts of the country in
the elegant parlors of the Waldorf-Astoria.
Mrs. Roe, State Regent, and her two
“Vices,” received from three to six p. m.,
in the most cordial and gracious manner;
about three hundred “Daughters” attended.

[Column 3]
	It is not possible to describe the dresses
(as the writer has been asked to do on
several occasions), but suffice to say, there
were many elderly daughters who were
quite as elaborately and as shiningly ar-
rayed, in costly apparel, wearing diamonds,
pearls and precious jewels, as the younger
portion of the D. R’s. There were also
others plainly dressed in simple costumes,
but no sort of distinction was apparent 
The elaborately dressed lady conversed with
those in plain attire and a general feeling
of good fellowship prevailed. The music
was of the highest order of excellence and
the delicate and delicious refreshments
were served in the daintiest style.
	Thursday morning two hundred or more
of the D. R.’s started on a trip up the Hud-
son by the West Shore R. R. to West Point.
All arrangements had been made before-
hand by a committee of New York State
and the General Society D. R., Mrs. Charles
W. Dayton, chairman. All the places of
interest were thrown open to the visitors,
the library and museum were the most at-
tractive places. After luncheon many of
the ladies were driven to interesting points
outside the college grounds. Col. Hein,
Commandant of Cadets, caused an exhibi-
tion of field artillery to be given in honor of
the ladies, and later on there was a grand
review of the corps of cadets. The day
was fine and the opportunity given the
ladies to visit and become acquainted with
each other was a source of pleasure and
gratification, as these busy women living
long distances apart, have many interests in
common, and to exchange ideas and com-
pare, is edifying and educational.
	Returning to New York on the evening
train, and in the boat when crossing the
Bay, there was opportunity for parting
messages and fervent farewells. The
writer had a most interesting conversation
with Miss Hunt, of Boston, on the return
trip. Miss M. Mabelle Snow, delegate 
from the Utah D. R., and Miss Abbie
Wells, daughter of Mr. and Mrs. Junius F.
Wells, (a Salt Lake girl now sojourning in
New York,) were the youngest members of
the party.


WOMAN'S EXPONENT.


"]

Name: 55_marriage_plural_god_plural marriage
Representative_Docs:
['SOME FACTS PLAINLY SET FORTH.

	Occasionally I meet with those who claim to be
Latter-day Saints that are in doubt about the
principle of plural marriage being the revealed
will of God to His Saints in this dispensation, yet
hey verily believe in celestial marriage, and ex-
pect to be exalted in the new and everlasting
covenant. How they can believe in the one and
not in the other is a mystery to me, when they are
both contained in the same revelation.
	Those who doubt the authenticity of plural
marriage, use for one of their strongest argnments
against it, a portion of the second chapter of
Jacob, in the Book of Mormon, claiming that he
spoke against it, and that if it was not right then
it s not right now. I have frequently and care-
fully read the passage referred to, and invariably
fail to find one word of condemnation of plural
marriage in it, when it is entered into according to
the law of the Lord.
	He was not reproving the Nephites for having
more wives than one, but because they "excused
themselves in committing whoredoms, because of
the things which were written concerning David,
and Solomon his son." Whoredom is a sin
which has been condemned from the beginning,
but nowhere do we find in holy writ anything
condemnatory of lawful plural marriage, but much
to show that it was in many instances justified by
the Lord.


Jacob also said, "Behold, David and Solomon
 truly had many wives and concubines, which thing
was abominable before me, saith the Lord." This
is a point held to most tenaciously by the opponent
of plural marriage as an argument that cannot be
refuted, claiming that the having of more wives
than one is the abomination to the Lord. He does
not say so, or carry the idea in the least tha
David and Solomon had done anything wrong in
having more wives than one at the same time,
but it was the many wives and concubines they
had that was abominable to the Lord, which thing
(so many wives and concubines) would also be
abominable to any chaste and virtuous person. It
is recorded in the Bible that the Lord gave to
David his wives, and would have given him more
if He had asked Him for them. By this I under-
stand that the Lord justified David in having more
wives than one at the same time, but it was the
having of many wives and concubines that was
abominable to Him.
	Jacob again says, "For there shall not any man
among you have save it be one wife, and concu-
bines he shall have none." The question is fre-
quently asked, "Why did the Lord give this
command to the Nephites, if plural marriage is
right?" There are many reasons why, but the
following one I think is sufficient:
	In the revelation on celestial marriage, given
through the Prophet Joseph Smith, it is recorded
that "there is never but one on the earth at a time
on whom this power and the keys of the Priest-
hood are conferred." We have no record of
Lehi, or any one of the Nephites ever holding the
keys of this power, or ever having the right or the
authority to seal wives to any man. If they had
not this right then it was wrong for any man among
them to have more wives than one at the same
time.
	Jacob still further says, "For if I will, saith the
Lord of Hosts, raise up seed to me, I will com-
mand my people." This presupposes that the
Lord would, at some future time, command His
people to enter into plural marriage; virtually fore-
telling that the Lord would yet command His peo-
ple to enter into it for the purpose of increasing
the posterity of the Saints upon the earth.
	We are now living in the time when the Lord
has restored that order of marriage held sacred
among His ancient people, and the Saints have
been commanded to enter into the order of patri-
archal marriage, or "be damned, sayeth the
Lord."
	Paul says, "The Gospel is the power of God
unto salvation to all those who believe," and also
that "man is not without the woman, nor the
woman without the man in the Lord." If it is so
that man and woman must necessarily be together
in the Lord, then the Gospel plan of salvation
would be incomplete without the principle of plural
marriage being practiced in the Church, whereby
every woman might be joined with man in holy
matrimony for time and all eternity, that they may
eternally increase in an exaltation in the celestial
kingdom of God. There has been, and is now, a
numerical preponderance of women over the men
in the Church of Jesus Christ of Latter-day Saints,
and probably will be, with an increase, until, as
Isaiah has said, seven women shall take hold of
one man to be called by his name to take away
their reproach.
	Much might be written on this subject, but a
"word to the wise is sufficient," and all will under-
stand, when they fully comprehend the plan of
salvation, that plural marriage is necessary to
make that plan complete, so that all who will may
attain to that glory and exaltation promised to the
faithful.		S. F. A.
	July 9, 1885.


WOMAN'S EXPONENT.


[Column 1]

', 'TO THE EDITOR OF THE ÆGIS:

	You wish to know how, and in what way I
know that Plural Marriage is right. By revela-
tion. I know by the same way that Peter knew
that Jesus was the Christ. "Jesus asked His
disciples whom do men say that I am, and they
answered and said, some say that Thou art John
the Baptist; some Elias; others Jeremiah or
one of the Prophets. He saith unto them, whom
do ye say that I am, and Simon Peter answered
and said Thou art the Christ, the Son of the
living God; and Jesus answered and said unto
him, blessed art thou Simon Barjona; for flesh
and blood hath not revealed it unto thee, but
my Father which is in Heaven. I say unto
thee thou art Peter, and upon this rock will
I build my Church, and the gates of hell shall
not prevail against it." Matthew, from the
13th to 18th verse, 16th chap.
	What rock was that? Was it Peter? No.
It was the rock of revelation. God revealed
it unto Peter by the Holy Ghost, the Comforter.
In the same way God has revealed it unto me
that Plural Marriage is right.
	You ask me how I got my faith, or religion,
and further ask, "was it not given to you or
established for an authority over you by men."
I say most emphatically that it was not. Faith
and true religion can not be given by man.
Man himself is dependent upon God for those
gifts, and can not bestow them upon others.
	"Faith is a gift of God. Without faith it is
impossible to please God. Even so faith if it
hath not works is dead, being alone. But
wilt thou know, vain man, that faith without
works is dead." James, 3d chap.
	I said in my last article I was born in this
Church, and will now add, in the valleys of
these mountains, and am a free born American
citizen, though now disfranchised for obeying
the laws of God. From my earliest recollection
I had been taught to believe that the Bible
meant what it said, and that God could see and
hear me and that if I would pray He would an-
swer my prayers. I used to hear these passages
of Scripture quoted by our Elders: "If any of
you lack wisdom let him ask of God, who giveth
unto all men liberally and upbraideth not, and
it shall be given him." James 1, 5. "Ask
and it shall be given you; seek and ye shall
find; knock and it shall be opened unto you;
for every one that asketh, receiveth, and he
that seeketh findeth, and to him that knock-
eth it shall be opened." Matt. 7, 7. I wanted
to prove if those passages were true, if God could
really answer my prayers. I asked in childish
simplicity for a temporal gift and I received it;
this strengthened my faith and gave me con-
fidence to ask for things of more worth than
earthly gifts. I wanted to know for myself if
the principles taught me were true. I was
earnest. I went to God in humility and asked
for a testimony of the truth of the Gospel of
Jesus Christ. My prayer was heard. I was
answered. The Holy Ghost, the Spirit of God,
filled me with joy and gave me a testimony
that man can neither give nor take away.

[Column 2]
	I have not lived very long upon the earth,
but I have lived long enough to know the value
of prayer. I have made God my friend while
I am young by living near to Him, pleading
daily for strength and wisdom for the fellowship
of His Holy Spirit to enable me to perform the
duties required of me. I praise His name and
thank Him that He has answered my prayers.
You say that I am blind to my own rights and
God given privileges and am under the "mystic
shadows of priestcraft." It is not true. I
enjoy light and intelligence that God has given
me, that I am afraid you know nothing about.
You are certainly under the "shadows of priest-
craft" and are blind to the things of God and
His Kingdom or you would not talk about a
people and their religion as you do about the
"Mormons." What do you know about "a
Mormon woman's only hope or guarantee of
Heaven?" or what is publicity "sanctioned" in the
Mormon Church. You haven't enough of the
Spirit of truth to discern it when placed before
you, but there are none so blind as those who
"do not want to see."
	If you read the WOMAN'S EXPONENT you are
constantly reading the testimonies of Latter-
day Saint women, that are true. You not
only reject their testimonies, in defense of their
religion, but you misrepresent them, for which
you will have to give an account in the great
"hereafter."
	We are not such meek, submissive creatures
as you imagine we are. What we do, we do
because we know by the Spirit that we possess,
is right. We do it intelligently because we love
to do right. We do not believe we can be
saved in ignorance. God has provided the way
wherin we can obain "hidden treasures of
knowledge," that is, in "doing the will of the
Father." "If any man will do His will he shall
know of the doctrine whether it be of God or
whether I speak of myself." John 17, 17.
	We have the moral courage to live the prin-
ciples of our religion in spite of all the derision,
sneers and opposition of the whole world. Mr.
Edmunds, Mr. Cullom and "fifty millions of
people," can not take from us our faith and
confidence in God, nor the testimony He has
given us concerning His Church that He has
established never more to be "thrown down."
They are in the hands of God and can do no
more than He will permit them to do. It is
our business to do the will of God. He revealed
the principle of plural marriage and com-
manded us to obey it.
	We can not give it up; we must work the
works of Abraham or we can not go where he
is. God walked and talked with Abraham
after he had taken a second wife, and told him
that he would make a covenant with him and
multiply him exceedingly and make him a
father of many nations. The son of his plural
wife also received a great promise from God:
"I will make him fruitful and will multiply
him exceedingly; twelve princes shall he beget
and I will make him a great nation. " Gen.
17, 20.
	That didn't look as though God thought he
was illegitimate. Jacob, who had four wives,
was a man whom the Lord loved; the twelve
tribes of Israel descended from those polygamic
wives. Moses, the great lawgiver of Israel,
who talked with the Lord and saw many marvel-
ous manifestations of His power, made special
provisions for the children of polygamous mar-
riages, and he himself practiced it.
	The Prophet Samuel was the son of a polyg-
amist, yet he lived in the temple and was an
object of God's favor. Many more names might
be mentioned to show that God blessed and
prospered ancient Israel, who practiced the
patriarchal order of marriage, but we do not
practice it because they did; we do not say that
because they were justified, we are justified.
We do these things because we have been
commanded to do them. We worship the same

[Column 3]
God that walked and talked with them. This
is the same Gospel, with all its gifts and bless-
ings, that they enjoyed.
	In this Church men and women are equal,
they are one, their interest is one; one is not
complete without the other. "Neither is the
man without the woman, nor the woman with-
out the man in the Lord." 1 Cor. 11, 11. Mar-
raige is honorable in all and the bed undefiled,
but whoremongers and adulterers God will judge.
Heb. 13, 4. There is no law of public sanction
or custom for men to break their marriage vows
in "Mormonism." Divorce is very rare. God
delights in the chastity of His daughters; so do
His servants, and adultery is ranked next in
crime to murder among the "Mormons." Those
who practice such things are severed from the
Church. They are not connected with the Latter-
day Saints; they cut themselves off from the
Priesthood. A man makes solemn covenants
to be faithful to his wives, he does not deceive
one and betray another, but each one knows
that he has other wives whom he loves and who
love him. You ask "how can a woman gratify
her love on a creature that shares his with more
than one woman, that is, if he has any love or
knows what it is?"
	Let me ask you a question, if you are married
do you not love your husband, and does he not
love his mother and sisters, and if you are a
mother, does he not love your children? How
then can you gratify your love upon him, when
his is shared with so many? Does not a woman
love all her children equally? Does she lavish
all her affections on one child, and neglect
the others?
	It is your tradition that a man can only love
one woman; but what a poor "creature" a man
would be, with a soul so small, whose affection
was so limited he could only love one woman.
	We are told in the Bible that "God is love,"
well, then, if we have His Spirit we are filled
with love. This Spirit enables us to live in
this order, and to bear and forbear with each
other's imperfections.
	It makes us generous, kind and obliging.
We think it a blessing to have a good husband;
then why is it not equally a blessing for our
fellow sisters to have good husbands, and as
there are not good husbands enough for each
woman to have one alone, why not share with
each other in lawful marriage. We have the
Priesthood of God in our midst, not priestcraft,
who have the authority to marry for time and
all eternity, having the Apostleship or power
to seal on earth. We are the only people on
the earth who have this power and authority.
	You are married by man, "until death doth
you part." We are married by the power of
the Priesthood, to enjoy endless lives and asso-
ciations in the presence of God, if we keep the
faith and endure to the end.
	"But as it is written, eye hath not seen, nor
ear heard, neither hath it entered into the heart
of man, the things which God hath prepared
for them that love Him. But God hath re-
vealed them unto us by His Spirit; for the
Spirit searcheth all things, yea, the deep thing
of God. For what man knoweth the things of
a man save by the spirit of man which is in
him? Even so the things of God knoweth no
man but by the Spirit of God. But the natural
man receiveth not the things of the Spirit of
God: for they are foolishness unto him: neither
can he know them because they are spiritually
discerned." 1 Cor. 11.
	In the absence of this Spirit we can not ex-
pect you, or any one else, to comprehend the
difference between Priesthood, which we share
with our husbands and enjoy that equality you
would like to see established, and priestcraft,
which has governed the world for ages.
	"For except a man be born of water and of
the Spirit he can not enter the kingdom of
God."				M. E. TEASDALE.
	Nephi, Jan. 19th, 1884.


WOMAN'S EXPONENT.


[Column 1]
', 'MARRIAGE.

BY BLANCHE BEECHWOOD.

 It is known to the world far and wide
that the Latter-day Saints believe in direct
revelation.  Through this channel (revela-
tion) they obtained the knowledge of the
principles of plural marriage and in no oth-
er way would pure and devotional women,
who had been educated and traditionated
in the ideas of the present age accept it. It
is truly the sacred phase of polygamy
which gives it prestige.
 The law of Celestial Marriage given to
Joseph Smith was a renewal of the same
order of matrimony practiced by the peo-
ple of God in ancient times.
 The angels sent to deliver the message
and these keys of knowledge, informed the
prophet Joseph that this holy order was
that which had existed forever, by which
men and women could become exalted; that
through no other covenant could they ob-
tain crowns and kingdoms, principalities
and eternal lives in the world to come, but
would be ministering servants to those who
had through obedience to this and all other
holy ordinances, obtained the fulness of
blessings and prepared themselves to enter
upon a higher plane.
 By this solemn covenant men and wo-
men could be joined together for eternity;
and only upon this principle could men take
more wives, or be sure of receiving again to
themselves their companions who had died
previous to their receiving a knowledge of
this law, except through this direct chan-
nel and power vested in the Holy Priest-
hood; which priesthood is after the order of
Melchizedek, to which reference is made
many times in the Old and New Testament.
This revelation was not given to be played
with, or as an idle saying, but as a com-
mandment from God; and one which in-
volved the most solemn obligations and re-
lationships.
The taking of more wives increases in a
very great degree a man's responsibilities;
not only is he bound to cherish them ten-
derly with his holiest and best affections;
but he should esteem them as precious jew-
els committed to his charge, for which he
must render an account of his stewardship
at the highest tribunal of Justice.  And it
would be well for the world to consider
that it was with a knowledge of all that
would be required, that Joseph Smith and
his brethren entered upon the practice of
the higher law.
 Any person conversant with the Holy
Scriptures may easily comprehend that even
David, although he was king over Israel,
received his wives at the hand of Nathan
the prophet, the one who held the keys of
this holy order of matrimony.  Through
this direct lineage the Savior descended;
yet the people in this so called enlightened
day look upon plural marriage as unholy
and seek to pass laws and devise ways and
means to criminate those who practice the
patriarchal form of marriage.  In ancient
days men and women talked with God and
were likely to understand His will.  To
look at the condition of the world, all who
make science a study may see that there is
some grievous misapplication of the laws of
life. People look on and try to find out
the cause, some will say women ruin their
health, consequently their offspring, with
their mode of dress, with dissipation, with
this or that excess, but they pass blindly
over the great primal cause, viz. the im-
proper, unjust and unholy marriage regula-
tions.  Men and women had so far degener-
ated from the true standard that they were
incapable by their own wisdom to arrive at
any succesful solution of the great problem
of life.
 Therefore God in His infinite wisdom re-
vealed to those who had proved their in-
tegrity to Him, by promulgating the Gos-
pel of truth, the law of Celestial Mar-
riage, and remember when He did so, it
was with a knowledge of the true science
of life; He knew that it would be an inno-
vation that men who were wise after the
manner of the world would not receive be-
cause of its unpopularity.  He inspired the
mind of Joseph Smith to make it known to
a few, those few, men and women of strict
moral principle, who had proven themselves
ready to die if necessary in the vindication
of truth.  They feared to disobey God more
than man.
 Religion according to its acceptation in
the nineteenth century relates to a spirit-
ual life only, and applies to spiritual works;
but TRUE religion covers all the ground of
one's temporal, as well as spiritual life
from the cradle to the grave, and reaches on
to eternal progression, and should enter into
the pre-natal influence of every mother
who is cognizant of the importance of bear-
ing the souls of men.  In many instances
it is pre-natal influences which stamp
themselves irremediably upon offspring so
that no after cultivation can wholly eradi-
cate the evil result.  If women desire to
bear children who will be able to perpetuate
themselves in races that will be honorable
and endurable, they should choose for their
companions, and the father of their off-
spring men of correct moral principle, who
fear God and strive to keep His laws.  It
is useless to tell women that they must
control and discipline themselves during
maternity in order to have wholesome and
superior children, if the husband is not pure
as well as the wife; for though the wife be
purity itself (if that were possible,) the
father if wicked and corrupt, stamps his na-
ture and temperament sufficiently upon his
offspring to contaminate the fountains of
life. An adulterated spring sends its poi-
sonous influence down through all its ave-
nues, and though scarcely perceptible, yet
the taint is there and impregnates those
who partake.  Grapes are not produced
from thorns nor figs from thistles.
	The world acknowledge that women sur-
pass men in innate purity, and if they
would preserve themselves pure through
their offspring, let them exact similar puri-
ty of the man with whom they unite in the
most sacred covenant ever consecrated, that
which is to be the promoter of a new life,
and perpetuate the human family.  Are
men who are unwholesome and dissipated
fit to become fathers?  Yet such men mar-
ry; and they never choose women of their
own caste, Oh no! a breath of suspicion
must  not shadow those whom they would
consign to lives of misery and degradation.
Women who have preserved themselves
chaste as the angels, whose offspring would
have been pure and sound, unite them-
selves with men who entail upon their
posterity such appetites and passions, as no
amount of self-denial, or discipline on the
part of the mothers, or after cultivation can
ever wholly obliterate.  When fathers
learn that God requires them to be pure as
well as the mothers, they will have ad-
vanced somewhat in the lessons of life.  God
knew that women were better prepared
than men to keep this higher law and that
there were more devotional women whan
men, and he desired them to become moth-
ers to bear a righteous and holy seed who
would serve Him, therefore He introduced
again the patriarchal form of marriage, and
gave to men who would abide His law the
privilege of having more wives, and He
will through this holy order purify men
and women and there will be at least a few
who will become a "fit temple for the Holy
Spirit to dwell in."  But God never gives a
law that has not a penalty attached if it is
broken, and the higher the law the more
fearful the consequences of breaking it.
Therefore those who desire to obtain the
promises, and higher exaltation must live
according to the requirements of this high-
er law, and will be called upon to make the
sacrifice of a broken and contrite heart not
after the spiritualized signification but lit-
erally.
 We are frequently asked the question
whether polygamy does not tend to indif-
ference in the marriage relations, or from
the very nature of the circumstances be-
come alienated in their affections.  We can
answer truthfully, No!  Plural marriage
makes woman more the companion and
much less the subordinate than any other
form of marriage; and when women have
learned to be self-reliant and self-sustain-
ing, and comprehend that in marriage
there is a higher purpose than being a man's
pet or even housekeeper, they will if edu-
cated in the most refined womanly sense,
choose to become the wives of men who are
living for lofty purposes, and their love
will partake more of the exalted tone and
sentiment, and their children become more
spiritual and inherit the elements of a high-
er nature.  It is grossly absurd to say plural
marriage is a sin in the sight of heaven when
we have abundant testimony before us, that
God has blessed this people in a most won-
derful manner ever since its practice.  More
than this, physicians, men of science, and
phrenologists have all failed to discover in
the children born in plural marriage any
inequalities, or lack of brain, or muscle and
fibre that would characterize children born
of unsuitable parentage.  There is a wide
field of knowledge open before us, and we
have only just begun to learn the true laws
of life, but if we keep ourselves pure and
humble, God will teach us how to overcome
all evil and obtain the exaltation promised
to those who will abide in the new and
everlasting covenant.
	S. L. City, Aug. 1877.


']

Name: 56_hospital_nurses_nurse_institution
Representative_Docs:
['DESULTORY NOTES.

	We are often asked why there is not more in
the EXPONENT. concerning the Deseret Hos-
pital. To give definite particulars that the
public might like to know would occupy consid-
erable space, and would scarcely be of sufficient
importance to give so large a share of a very
small paper, which is published in the interest
of women generally, both young and old, and
not one particular organization or department
of either charity, industry, or politics. We
may say, however, with all safety at the pres-
ent time, that thus far the Deseret Hospital has
prospered. With the many difficulties to sur-
mount that ever attend the establishing of an
institution of this character, and, taking the
whole matter into consideration, it cannot but
be said the ladies so far have done well. To
be sure they were most of them inexperienced
in hospital work, but time, patience and per-
severance accomplish wonders when the heart
is interested, and certainly the ladies of this
city and adjacent cities and counties have mani-
fested a true spirit of benevolence and liberality
in bestowing of their means, and a desire to
help carry on this enterprise, which is most
praise-worthy and admirable. There has been
obstacles to encounter and emergencies to meet
which were not anticipated in the beginning,
but such things are incidental in all depart-
ments of life and work, and therefore, scarcely
need commenting upon. The people were ap-
pealed to for free donations to assist in furnish-
ing and fitting up the building, and the res-
ponse was general from both men and women,
as well as prompt and generous.
	A great many have feit very anxious to help
forward the institution by giving public enter-
tainments; perhaps, because they knew more
of the needs of such an institution, and it is
customary to aid in this way, and certainly
it has been a great blessing and help to the
Hospital. The proceeds of the concert given in
the large Tabernacle last July, under the direc-
tion of a very able Committee, Elder George
Goddard, Chairman and general manager of the
affair, did considerable towards helping this in-
stitution in its incipiency. Since then a Pri-
mary Concert has been given in the Elevnth
Ward in this city, and the proceeds handed over
to the Hospital, $41, from the children, exceed-
ingly good indeed for the little folks; of course,
a great deal was due to the excellent manage-
ment of Mr. Louie Felt and her associates in
getting up the entertainment.
	The Unity Club, which waa organized in
November, 1882, have made a donation of $500
(five hundred dollars) to the Deseret Hospital
for the benefit of that institution. It is certain-
ly most commendable in these ladies to work
and aid with material help, a place for the sick

[Column 2]
and injured to be cared for and made comforta-
ble as they could not be in their own homes.
Tne money given was the proceeds of entertain-
ments and parties, for which the members of
the club worked with all diligence and enthu-
siasm, realizing they were laboring in a charita-
ble cause for an institution just in its infancy,
struggling for permanent establishment.
	For years the sisters actively interested in
the Relief Society in this Church have seen and
felt deeply the need of a place of this kind.
But where to obtain the means to found a hos-
pital seemed to be the difficulty in the way of
its accomplishment. There did not appear
beforehand, at least in the minds of many, so
much apprehension about maintaining it were
it once practically started. However, having
undertaken this benevolent enterprise, the
ladies of the Executive Board find that it will
require continuous application to meet the
monthly expenses, as there are necessarily more
patients that can only pay a small sum towards
their care and treatment, than those who can
pay in full the expenses for board and medical
attention.
	Every person must know what it is to go into
an empty house and furnish it with everything
for comfort and convenience, and besides the
furnishings such as are necessary for family use
in a home, a hospital must have a dispensary
supplied with medicines, and also instruments
for operations of all kinds. In this respect the
Deseret Hospital is well fitted up. The instru-
ments were purchased in New York City, and
are of the very best material and manufacture.
	All the apartments and wards are neatly and
suitably furnished, and the accomodations are
such as the patient might enjoy in a home,
more than like an ordinary hospital. All the
people employed there strive to make the
house home-like and its inmates happy. Ser-
vices of the Church of Jesus Christ of Latter-
day Saints are held in the reception room every
Sunday afternoon, and the sacrament is admin-
istered under the direction of the Bishop of
the Ward and his Counselors. The sick have
the privilege of the administration of the Elders
of the Church, which is certainly an advantage
and blessing to the suffering, which the Latter-
day Saints can appreciate.
	The medical board is ample for all emergen-
cies. Dr. W. F. Anderson, the regular visiting
surgeon, has an established reputation as a
physician, of more than a quarter of a century’s
standing in this community. Several lady
physicians are on the visiting and consulting
board who are well and favorably known as suc-
cessful practitioners and a house surgeon, who,
though a young lady, has already won laurels
in the profession, and made hosts of friends in
the calling she has chosen.


', 'ANNUAL MEETING OF THE D. H. A.

	The first Annual Meeting of the Deseret Hos-
pital Association was held in the Council House,
in this city, Saturday, May 12th ult., Mrs. Eliza
R. Snow Smith presiding. After singing the
hymn beginning "Let Zion in her beauty rise,"
prayer was offered by Mrs. Sarah M. Kimball,
then singing again, "O, my Father, thou that
dwellest," after which the following report of
the secretary for the past year was submitted:
	One year ago to-day a meeting was held in
this house, to consider the matter of organizing
an Association, with a view to the establishment
of a hospital. About twenty-five ladies were
present, and nominations of officers for the as-
sociation were made, and a decision arrived at
to pay an initiatory fee of one dollar each as a
condition of membership, and to elect by vote a
President, Vice-President, Executive Board,
Secretary and Treasurer. Accordingly the fol-
lowing ladies were elected: President, Eliza R.
Snow Smith; Vice-President, Zina D. H. Young;
N. Hyde, Phoebe Woodruff, Bathsheba M.
Smith and Jane S. Richards; Secretary, E. B.
Wells; Treasurer, M. M. Barrat; as an Honor-
ary Committee, all the Stake Presidents of Re-
lief Societies; Dr. E. B. Ferguson was engaged
as Resident Surgeon; Dr. R. B. Pratt was chosen
Visiting  Surgeons; Mary Emma Vanschooven,
clinical registrar and dispensary clerk; and
Mary Ann McLean, Matron; Visiting Commit-
tees and a Finance Committee were subsequent-
ly chosen. At a meeting held on the 15th.
of May, it was decided to rent a building until
some better arangement could be made, and
committees were appointed to canvass the sever-
al parts of the city, and adjacent districts, and
see what means could be obtained as donations
towards defraying the expenses of establishing
such an institution. A petition was drawn up
and submitted to President Taylor for approval,
and the canvassing committees began their
work. These ladies were enegetic and success-
ful, and meetings were held twice and even
three and four times a week, and reports were
made from the several committees, who had
the labor in hand. President Taylor was noti-
fied from time to time of the proceedings of the
committees, and gave advice in regard to the
movement.
	A circular was issued throughout the various
Stakes of Zion, giving the names of the officers
 of the institution and regulations made to enter
oractically upon the work. One of these was
the suggestion that each Belief Society and Y.
L. and Y. M. M. I. Association should pay
statedly one dollar per month as a regular sub-
scription to assist in defraying the current ex-
penses of the hospital, and that the amount
paid stand as a credit in favor of any member of
the respective Society or Association that should
require the benefit of the same.
	Finally a building considered suitable for the
purpose of a hospital was rented in the Twelfth
Ward, in this city, at $60 per month, and prep-
arations made to formally open the hospital.
A committee waited upon President Taylor,
and invited him to be present at the dedication,
with others of the leading brethren of the
Church, on Monday, July 17th, at ten o'clock a.
m., and at that time the Firsst Presidency of the
Church were all present, also Apostle F. D.
Richards and other brethren, and of the presi-
dency of this Stake of Zion, Elder Joseph E.
Taylor. After the dedicatory services the Resi-
dent Surgeon and Matron were set apart by the
brethren. Patients were received at the hospital,
and business transacted in the name of the
Deseret Hospital Association.
	Some necessary changed have been made since
the organization, from time to time, two ladies
have been added to the Executive Board; Dr.
Romania B. Pratt and Dr. Ellis R. Ship and Dr.
E. S. Barney, to the Visiting Surgeons; the Res-
ident Surgeon resigned at the regular monthly
meeting, Oct. 2nd, and Dr. M. P. Hughes was
engaged to fill the vacancy. In November it
was considered wise to dispense with the ser-
vices of the Dispensary Clerk, in order to econo-
mize. In March the Matron gave notice of her
desire to resign, and at the meeting in April it
was decided to engage Mrs. Harriet Ellis in her
place. Mrs. Ellis was installed as Matron on
the first of May, and is now practically filling
the position.
	The people have donated liberally to the hos-
pital, but the officers of the Association find
that with rent to pay, and necessary help to
keep up the institution, the enterprise requires
strict economy and active executive ability. The
ladies of the Executive Board, who manage the
benevolent institution have certainly shown
great zeal and earnestness in this philanthropical
work, and been determined to make the effort a
successful one for the benefit of the sick and
afflicted among the Latter-day Saints. Through
good and evil report they have been untiring in
diligence and good works, and still feel deter-
mined to persevere in well doing. Only those
who have practically labored in this direction
can appreciate the immense effort that has been
made, and the arduous labors undertaken to ac-
complish what has already been done in this di-
rection, with the sole motive of ameliorating the
condition of suffering humanity. At the pres-
ent time the Association has enrolled eight-hun-
dred and forty-five members, who are expected
to pay an annual fee of one dollar per year.
	Financial report. Total cash received during
the year, $4,772.65; orders, $1,092.05; total, $5,-
864.70. There have been other donations of
lumber, flour, meat, cow and calf, produce and
other things; quilts, blankets, bed linen, etc.
Most of THESE articles have been given by the
Relief Society and Y. L. M. I. A. in the several
Stakes of Zion. Expended in cash for fitting up
and furnishing the building as well as current
expenses since opening the Hospital, July 17,
1882, $3,705.28; Total amount of expense in or-
ders, etc., $1,044.55; Total expense, $4,749.83; Re-
maining in cash, $1,067.37; Orders, 47.50; Total,
$1,114.87; The fees from patients received during
the year amount to $754.75 cash; Other things,
$337.30; making in all, $1,092.05.
	After the report had been read and approved
the President made a few remarks, reviewing
briefly some of the labors of the past year and
expressing her gratitude to all who had aided in
the work in any way. She also expressed her
gratitude to President Taylor for his wise counsel,

[Column 3]
and excellent judgment which had been of
great benefit to her, and those associated with
her in the labor. Referred gratefully to the ef-
ficient help and aid received from the Bishop of
the ward, and thanked all who had been helpful
in any way, for it had certainly been a great
blessing to comfort and benefit the sick and
aflicted, and to think that our people can have a
place of their own, where they could receive
medical and surgical assistance, and the admin-
istration of the ordinances of the Gospel where
faith could be exercised for them. The Presi-
dent said the Unity Club had made great efforts
to help the institution and contributed liberally,
indeed they stood foremost in that respect, i. e.,
aiding the hospital. There had been many other
large and small donations.
	Sister M. Isabella Horne made a few remarks,
and added, Sister Eliza expressed her feelings on
the subject. She realized that many of the
patients had received great blessings from the
Lord through fasting and the laying on of hands.
	Dr. Elvira S. Barney stated to the meeting
the proposition she had made to the sisters to
teach a class in obstetrics, anatomy and physi-
ology. It was to the effect that she would give
the teaching free, and only receive bare remun-
eration for room and other services rendered.
The students must find their own provisions,
but a cook would be furnished to prepare the 
meals, also to do the washing for the class.
Sister Barney made other explanations in detail.
	Sister Jane S. Richards of Ogden said she was
pleased with Dr. Barney's proposition, thought
it a very liberal one, thought it essential we
should have more efficient nurses and compet-
ent accouchers through the country settle-
ments, referred to her living at a distance from
the hospital, which made it more inconvenient
for her to attend the meetings of the Executive
Board than the other ladies, but had always
made it a point to come when it was possible,
and also to assist all in her power in every de-
partment of the labor to benefit the institutione
	Dr. Romania B. Pratt said that as far as she
was acquainted with the practical workings of
the hospital it was managed in the best interests
of the association and doing great good. That
the physicians connected with it acted upon the
hightest principles of skill and science, their
works assisted by faith. Being a young institu-
tion there were many disadvantages under
which those interested had labored.
	The motion was made to change the name of
the Finance Committee to Ways and Means
Committee, which was carried unanimously.
The Committee was authorized to act in behalf
of the association, in making arrangements for
the concerts to be given in the Tabernacle, on
the 24th of July, and the Chairman of the Com-
mittee was authorized to call to her aid such
other help as she needed for the work. The
several Canvassing Committees were honorably
discharged from further duty. After some re-
marks from two or three of the Ways and Means
Committee in reference to their special depart-
ment the meeting was adjourned for one year.


', 'THE TRAINING OF A NURSE.

BY MARY CADWALADER JONES.

	Within the memory of most of us nearly
every family boasts some member who was
said to be a "born nurse," who came to the
fore in time of sickness, and whose labor of
love was sometimes shared by a paid outsider,
usually a motherly body supposed to have a
great deal of experience. But that time is
past, and now no one who can afford a
trained nurse thinks of taking a patient
through an illness without one, any more than
a captain willingly takes his ship through a
dangerous channel without a pilot.
	This means that a new trade or profession
has been created for women, and it may be
told once more that they owe it to one of the
noblest women of her time.
	At the close of the Crimean War the
passionate gratitude of the English people to
Florence Nightingale found expression in a
great public meeting, at which fifty thousand
pounds was subscribed as a testimonial to her.
She refused, however, to take it for herself,
and at her request it was devoted to a founda-
tion which was quaintly termed "An Insti-
tution for the Training, Sustenance, and
Protection of Nurses and Hospital Attendants,"
in connection with St. Thomas's Hospital,
London; and thus the first English training
school for nurses was started in June,
1860.
	Accounts of this great reform, which spread
in England from year to year, reached this
country more or less vaguely, but were with-
out result until, in 1872, the men and women
belonging to that branch of the State Charities
Aid Association which visited the sick in
Bellevue Hospital felt that they could not do
any good or lasting work until the existing
system, or want of system, should be entirely
changed. The nurses were too few in number,
nearly all illiterate, some immoral, and others
intemperate, and had sought their places
simply as a means of livelihood, and not because
they had any aptitude for, or knowledge of their
profession. The members of the Bellevue
Association therefore applied to the Com-
missioners of Charities and Correction for
permission to establish a school for nurses at
Bellevue Hospital, pledging themselves to pay
the additional salaries and all other expenses
of a better class of women and to put two
more nurses in each ward. The consent of
the Medical Board of the hospital, to whom
the Commissioners refered this appeal, having
finally been given to what many physicians
considered a doubtful experiment, the Bellevue
Training School for Nurses was started on
May 1, 1873, with a superintendent and five
nurses, having five wards under their care.
	In 1890 the school had 62 pupils and has
graduated 345, while as a direct outgrowth of
that modest beginning there are three other
great schools in New York alone. These are
the New York City, which has 64 pupils and
has graduated 263; the New York Hospital,
48 pupils and 132 graduates; and Mount
Sinai, with 50 pupils and 111 graduates. There
are also smaller schools in the city, but, great
or small, Bellevue must always be honored as
the pioneer. Her graduates are at the head
of most of the important schools and hospitals
in the country, and have even gone so far
afield as England, Italy, and China.
	The next school to be established was the
New York City, which was started by the
Commissioner of Charities and Correction in
1877, and is entirely supported by the City.
Until last year it was known as the Charity
Hospital School, because it began there, but
as it grew its work spread until the old name
was misleading and had to be changed. It is
now the largest and in some respects the

[Column 2]

most important of all the schools, as it,
nurses five different hospitals—Charity and
Maternity on Blackwells Island, the Infant's
Hospital on Randall's Island, Gouverneur at
Gouverneur Slip, and Harlem, at the foot of
East 120th Street, the two last being accident
or emergency hospitals, while at Charity the
cases are largely chronic. Besides the pupils
of the school, there are thirty-two permanent
trained nurses at Charity and Randall's
Island, making nearly a hundred in all, for
whom the superintendent is directly responsible
and over whom she has full authority. The
other schools in the city are supported from
the funds of the hospitals which they nurse.
	I have said that nursing is a trade or pro-
fession, for it is really both—being a trade, in
that it exacts manual skill and dexterity, and
a profession because it requires mental ability,
judgment and progressive knowledge. The
hospital is therefore at once a workshop and a
college, with this essential difference, however,
that its scholars exist because it has need of
them, not they of it. So much talk has been
made about nursing as a noble "vocation"
that it is easy to lose sight of the fact that
hospital training schools are run first of all
because hospital patients must be taken care
of. When Florence Nightingale led her
little band of workers out of England it was
not in order that women should have a new
vocation, but because men were dying like
flies in the hospitals at Scutari, and the
women who started the Bellevue School did
so because they found the hospital could be
well nursed in no other way.
	In most of the schools the nurses receive
each $10 a month during the first year of
their service, and $15 the second, and at the
present time there is some discussion as to
whether they should be paid at all, or should
give their time in return for their professional
training, as the house physicians do. This
seems reasonable enough to an outsider, but in
the first place much of a nurse's work is of a
routine kind, repeated far oftener than is
necessary for her education, and such as a
doctor is rarely called upon to do, and in the
second the most desirable pupils are those
who could be self-supporting outside the schools
and will not be a burden on their families while
in them. In this country there is a large class
of conscientious and industrious women whose-
education and early associations lead them to
look for some higher and more thoughtful
labor than household service or work in shops,
who have received the good education of our
common schools, and who are dependent on
their own exertions for support. These women
can be trained to make the best possible nurses,
and it is the unanimous opinion of the superin-
tendents of the large schools that it would be
false economy to seek to deprive such pupils of
the small salary which now keeps them inde-
pendent during two years of very hard work.
	We will suppose that a woman of this kind
has decided go into one of the large schools,
and has applied to the superintendent for infor-
mation. She receives in return a circular
giving the rules, requirements and course of
study, and in due time finds herself with other
candidates waiting for examination in the
superintendent's office. When her turn comes,
and if her credentials are satisfactory, the
superintendent usually talks to her a little while
in order to find out what grade of nurse she is
likely to make; for candidates are admitted
only oh their own merits, and where there are
more applicants than vacancies it is important
to secure the best. A short examination in
spelling, dictation, and simple arithmetic
follows, and also in reading aloud, but this is
often past over if a candidate is evidently too
nervous to do herself justice.
	Varions experiments have been tried as to
Examining Boards, but the best result is always

[Column 3]

gained by choosing a good suprintendent, and
then leaving her free to select her own nurses,
without fear or favor, from those who present
themselves, as she must train discipline, and
live with them for two years, and has therefore
every reason to take only those who are likely
to do her credit.
	Apart from articles in professional journals,
much that has been written about hospital life
is apt to strike one familar with it as somewhat
vague and sentimental, and there may there-
fore be some interest in the following sketches,
by pupils now in the New York City Training
School. The first gives a general outline of
the work.
	(To be continued)


']

['PEACE MEETINGS.
	We are pleased to state that in the North
Weber Stake, Peace meetings have been held
in every ward, also that the meetings were
all held on Sunday evening, May 15th, 1910.
	There are twelve wards in this stake. The
programs rendered were under the auspices
of the Relief Society and the Y. L. M. I. A.
and were as follows:
