['A CHRONICLE OF THE RELIEF SOCIETY.
				III.
	There was at Favorsham, Kent, Eng-
land a branch of the Relief Society or-
ganized at an early date, we have not the
positive date unfortunately, or we would
give it here but we have a notice of its
holding a semi Annual Conference on
March 21, 1881—in which it is stated much
of the time was occupied with reports, a
synopsis of which was sent to the London
Conference. The President’s name is
given, Sister Ann Cornford. This Relief
Society Conference was held in the after-
noon and at the close of the meeting a
social tea provided by the President and
her Counselors was enjoyed, and in the
evening the Relief Society held a joint
session with the priesthood of the Favors-
ham branch. This Society numbered
twenty members and they visited among
the Saints. This report is from a letter of
Elder Edward King’s who was at that
time laboring there, as was also Elder
Joseph A. Jennings.
	Auckland, New Zealand, Relief Society
was organized by Elder George Batt, De-
cember 22, 1880, Sister Isabel Lucy ap-
pointed President; Sisters Day and Fox,
Counselors; Sister Williams, Secretary;
Sister Miles, Treasurer. June 9, 1881, the
President and her Counselors left New
Zealand for Zion and Sister Hannah Cox
was elected President by unanimous vote
of the members. This Society was in a
flourishing condition in 1881, at the date
of the letter received, and much means
had been donated and earned by sewing.
Meetings were held semi-monthly.
	The first Relief Society in the Swiss and
German Mission was in Berne Switzer-
land by Elder P. F. Goss, June 30, 1882.
At the time of the visit of Sister Annie
Wells Cannon in 1883, she wrote concern-
ing this Society of Berne, and others, she
says: Notwithstanding that it has lost
many members through the emigration it
still has a membership of 39. Regular
meetings are held every fort-night, in one
meeting the sisters speak and bear testi-
mony, and in the other report the districts
to which they have been assigned. In
addition to these meetings, work meetings
are held occasionally. The last report
dated January 15, 1884, stated that the
cash on hand amounted to Frs. 26.32, and
that they had an outlay during the preced-
ing six months of Frs. 33.60. The officers
were Rosina Steiner, President; Magda-
lene Meyer and Maria Schneider, Counse-
lors; Sophia Kindlisbacher, Secretary.
	The Relief Society of Schaffhausen was
organized by Elder Alvis Boner, the 5th
of February, 1883, though a very small
Society it has been a very industrious and
active one. In the one year it has held
36 meetings, most of them work meetings.
Sister Barga Haage the energetic Presi-
dent has been untiring in her endeavors,
and much good has been done by this
little Society though only numbering
seven members. They started with a
working capital of Frs. 4.63, which they
have increased nine fold. They have as-
sisted some of their emigrating sisters
and purchased many useful Xmas gifts for
the needy children of the branch of the
Church in that place.
	The Relief Society of Scherll was or-
ganized September 24, 1883, by Elder John
Stucki. There are now eleven members.
Elizabeth Michel, President; Eliza Kohler

[Column 2]
and Kathrine Michel, Counselors; and
Anna Eliza Michel, Secretary. The mem-
bers express themselves much improved
strengthened and encouraged by these
meetings and better able to help each
other in different ways.
	The Relief Society of Linach was or-
ganized on February 3, 1884, by Elder
Avis Bauer. There are ten members;
Barbara Naef, President; Maria Fretz
and Babette Fischer, Counselors; and
Rosina Baahardt, Secretary. The annual
Conference of the Swiss and German Mis-
sion was held in Berne on the 23rd and
24th of December 1883. The last even-
ing of the Conference, Dec. 24th, the
Relief Society held a meeting. Eleven
Elders and one sister from Utah were
present (Sister Annie Wells Cannon) and
a large attendance of Saints besides many
strangers. Sister Rosina Steiner presided
with ease and coolness. Good and inter-
esting testimonies were borne, and en-
couragement offered to the sisters in the
work in their different languages, English,
French and German, to say nothing of
the different dialects, that might be called
many more languages.
	They were delighted with the things
told them by Sister Cannon about the
work of the Relief Society in Zion and
especially pleased when told a Hospital
had been founded under the auspices of
the Society here in Utah.
	Sister Cannon wrote, those engaged in
the Society work have discovered the
great benefit of the organization in times
of sickness and need, that they are of
much help to each other and to the breth-
ren and that the Society is a great sup-
port to the branch of the Church to which
it belongs.
	While Sister Cannon was in Europe on
the Continent, she prepared a sort of
Epistle, “To the Relief Society of the
Swiss and German Mission” which was
published in the “Stern.” This Epistle
set forth the work of women in the
Church, the organization of the Relief
Society by the Prophet Joseph Smith in
Nauvoo, Illinois, March 17, 1842, and in
fact gave a sketch of the Society up to
that time mentioning the General Officers
and certain departments of the work,
storing grain, the Woman’s Exponent,
sericulture, the Deseret Hospital the en-
couraging of young women in the study
of medicine as a profession, and many
other things incidentally. The epistle
was highly approved by Sister Eliza R.
Snow then General President of the So-
ciety, who commended it and wished it
published, in the Woman’s Exponent
here in Utah, which was accordingly done
by her request.
	It is only just to add that at the time
Sister Cannon visited these organizations
abroad, she was only a very young woman
twenty three years of age.


', "WAYNE STAKE.
Editor Woman's Exponent:
	WAYNE STAKE was organized May 27th
and 28th, 1893. Apostles F. M. Lyman
and M. W. Merrill being present. Apostle
F. M. Lyman presiding. Our stake Presi-
dency are William E. Robinson President,
Hans Hanson and Gastian Bastian Coun-
selors, they are very good kind men, and
are much esteemed by the people.
	The Presidency of the Relief Society of
Wayne Stake are Jane S. Coleman, Presi-
dent, Mary Ellen Hanks and Sarah S.
Forsythe Counselors, Florence M. Williams
Secretary. After the Stake organization
myself and Counselors considered it best
to visit each R. S. in the Stake and hold
meetings with them and get acquainted, that
a united feeling might prevail. We have
done so and realize it will be productive of
much good, we were received with kind-
ness by all of the branches. Commenced
to visit June 20th, held meeting with Fre-
mont R. S. their Bishop and a number of
brethren favored us with their presence,
much good instruction was given.
	Next day 21st, held meeting with the
Loa R. S. President Robinson and the
Bishopric attended also other brethren, a
variety of subjects were spoken of that
were edifying and elevating in character.
	The Loa R. S. was reorganized with
Margaret J. Taylor, President, Elizabeth
C. Jeffry and Bathsheba Grundy, Counse-
lors, Pauline Brown, Secretary, Sarah Ann
Robinson, Treasurer.
	In the afternoon of the 21st, met with
East Loa R. S. Bishop B. F. Brown and
Counselor J. Grover present, had an enjoy-
able meeting spoke of the instructions re-
cieved at the general Relief Society Con-
ference in Salt Lake City.
	Held meeting with Thurber R. S. on the
22nd, at 10 a. m. Bishop Wm. Meeks and
other brethren present, the remarks made
were timely and well recieved.
	Held meeting at Teasdale 2 p. m. Bishop
George Coleman present, same day re-
organized Teasdale R. S. with Mary
Eleanore Adams, President, Lydia C.
Adams and Maria J. Chaffin, Counselors,
Maggie S. Adams, Secretary, Emma Fran-
com, Treasurer, good remarks were made
exhorting to being faithful in the discharge
of every duty.
	On the 23rd held meeting with Grover
R. S. Of Teasdale Ward every member was
in attendance. U. V. Stewart Presiding
Priest present, a good spirit was enjoyed
good instructions given and the visit of the
Presidency of the Relief Society appreciated
by the sisters.
	On July 12th we wrote to each President
to have read in their meetings, “Utah
Women in Chicago’’ and June 15th num-
ber of the WOMAN'S EXPONENT.
	Also have their teachers canvas their
districts for subscribers for WOMAN'S
EXPONENT hope the result will increase
your list and be a pleasure to those who
subscribe.
	August 7th started for the lower settle- 
ments, held meeting at Cainesville. Au-
gust 8th at 2 p. m. was happily surprised to
have President Robinson and Counselor
G. Bastian attend our meeting also, the
Bishopric and several brethren; we spoke
upon the merits of the R. S. also the bene-
fits accrueing, and there-from a Relief
Society was organized with Eliza Rust,
President, Isabelle Dalton and Alvira Mur-
dock, Counselors, Agnes Carrol, Secretary,
Olive Carrol, Treasurer. President Robin-
son and Counselor G. Bastian made remarks
favorable to the Relief Society, showing
forth the good it accomplished.
	August 9th at 11 a. m. held meeting at
Aldridge of Cainesville Ward, Bishop
Walter Hanks and a number of brethren
present, good instructions were given and
the spirit of the Gospel characterized the
remarks of the speakers. In times past they
had a R. S. in that place, their President
Sarah C. Durfee had done a good work but
had moved. A Society was reorganized with
Thersa Coombs, President, Patre Turgenson
and Maria Smith, Counselors, Electa John-
son, Secretary, Elisabeth Gardner,
Treasurer.
	August 10th at 2 p. m held meeting at
Blue Valley. President W. E. Robinson,
Counselor G. Bastian, Bishop Heber J.
Wilson and a number of brethren were
present, good practical instructions were
given pertaining to building on their town
site, nearly all are living on their farm.
The subjects were union, charity and the
like and will be productive of good results.
A Society was organized with Mary Ann
Lords, President, Eliasbeth Giles and Annie
Bingham, Counselors, Sister Cook, Secre-
tary, Mary Ann White, Treasurer. 
	August 11th at 10 a. m. held meeting at
Graves Valley of the Blue Valley Ward.
President W. E. Robinson, Counselor G.
Bastian, the Bishopric of the ward and a
number of the brethren were present, we
had a very good meeting, the spiritual and
temporal of our holy religion was protrayed,
the organization of the R. S. by the
Prophet Joseph Smith. A Relief Society
was organized. Lucy Thompson, Presi-
dent, Jane Sylvester and Martha Stoddard,
Counselors, Hannah A. Elliot, Secretary,
Nettie Sanford, Treasurer.
	We appreciated the presence of our
President W. E. Robinsou and Counselor 
G. Bastian and the good counsel given by
them to each R. S. after the organization
was effected, we had very interesting
meetings and enjoyed the spirit of our
high calling, felt the blessings of our
Heavenly Father were with us in our
labors, and have faith that the Wayne
Stake Relief Society will not be behind in
good works.
	JANE S. COLEMAN, President.
", "A CHRONICLE OF THE RELIEF SOCIETY.
I.
	The spirit of fraternity, and of love for
one another began in the Latter-day
Saint Church in the days of Kirtland,
about the time the Prophet Joseph Smith
and his wife Emma came to that historic
town in February, 1831. This fact might
be demonstrated by many little incidents
that transpired in those early days among
the sisters, and possibly will be when a
complete history of the Society which
was afterwards organized is written. How-
ever, for the present, after pointing out
the spirit of sisterly love and charity that
existed prior to the establishment of this
grand and flourishing organization, it is
only the design of the writer to give a few
facts chronologically.
	The Relief Society proper was organized
by the Prophet Joseph Smith at a meet-
ing held in the Lodge Room, in Nauvoo,
Illinois, where the Saints settled after
leaving Missouri. The Temple was con-
templated and the people were preparing
to build. Some few sisters were very
anxious to assist in this great undertak-
ing in some systematic way, and talked
the matter over; finally the subject was
presented to President Joseph Smith,
who told them to meet together and he
would organize a society suitable for be-
nevolent purposes. The spirit of the
work was in the hearts of these good,
God fearing women, and those who knew
of the project came together as des-
ignated. There were only eighteen
sisters present and they nominated their
own officers and elected Emma Smith
President, Sarah M. Cleveland and Eliza-
beth Ann Whitney, Counselors; Eliza R.
Snow, Secretary; Phebe Ann Wheeler,
Assistant Secretary; and Elvira S. Coules,
Treasurer. The President was the wife
of the President Joseph Smith, and she
was designated “The Elect Lady” by
revelation given some years previous, and
was then ordained to expound the Scrip-
tures. Her nomination and election by
the sisters in that remarkable meeting of

[Column 2]
women, in that early time shows they
“builded better than they knew.” Looking
back it is wonderful to contemplate, yet we
who know so much of the Prophet Joseph
cannot but believe that he foresaw what
the future of this important Society would
be. Emma Smith’s was the first ordina-
tion to this calling of any woman, and
took place many years before any of the
churches extant had ordained women
ministers. (Their names are all given in
the minutes of that meeting.) Only one
of all that number is living at the present
time, our honored and beloved President,
Sister Bathsheba W. Smith. How emi-
nently appropriate that she should occu-
py the position of “Elect Lady” in this
ripe hour of the Society when it has at-
tained such permanency, and celebrity
throughout the world. Identified with it
as she has been from the very commence-
ment, and through the entire sixty-four
years and more, promoting in every pos-
sible way its best interests; she stands to-
day a living witness of its growth and de-
velopment through all the various stages
of its onward progress, and has always
been foremost in any great enterprise for
the women of Zion.
	From the little handful of sisters who
met on that ever memorable day, March
17, 1842, in the next two years the increase
in membership reached nearly 2,000, and
the amount of charity work was some-
thing marvelous for those early times
when womens’ organizations were un-
known. Strange indeed that these young
sisters—for they were all under forty
years, except one—should have asked for
organization, and given the Society
a name, the Relief Society, that has re-
mained with it, acceptable to all those
who have come into the ranks since; even
with the newer ideas and modern ad-
vancement among womankind; doubtless
the name will be held in honorable repute
while there are works of charity to be
done for suffering humanity, and while
there is need of kindly ministrations.
	The Relief Society could not operate
as an organized body of workers when the
people were scattered hither and thither,
at the time of the exodus from Nauvoo,
but the secretary, Sister Eliza R. Snow,
brought away the records, which was an
important part of the Society, and served
a legitimate purpose in reopening the
work after the Saints had gathered here
in these mountain vales.
	President Brigham Young following in
the footsteps of his predecessor, called
upon the secretary, Sister Eliza R. Snow,
in the year 1855, to go forth and assist
the Bishops of the various wards, (that
had been organized under his direction)
in organizing a Relief Society in each
ward. He suggested that she take as
her assistant Sister Zina D. Young.
	Previous to this there had been some
temporary organizations and charitable
work had been done necessary to the con-
ditions of the people living where the na-
tive Indians came freely among them, it
was positively necessary to provide some
clothing for them, as well as food. Much
of this was done by the sisters in these
temporary associations. To be sure there
was much to be done for neighbors and
friends, but little to handle in the way of
means until a few years had elapsed;
then came the time when permanent soci-
eties were provided as mentioned, and
Sister Eliza R. and Sister Zina D. went


[Column 1]
from ward to ward helping the Bishops
to organize as indicated. From that time
onward the Society continued to grow
although there was no linking together
into one great whole until some years
later. In these earlier temporary socie-
ties she who stands at the head now was
diligent and faithful.
	It is not the purpose of this article to
tell the story, only to give a few pertinent
facts in relation to a society, that has
taken a very foremost place, not only as
an auxiliary in the Church of Latter-day
Saints, but it is known in the world outside,
among those not of our faith, as one of
the most flourishing organizations of wo-
men banded together for the higher ad-
vancement and permanent growth of all
that tends to the betterment and uplift-
ing of women; and of life itself in its de-
velopment religiously, socially, mentally,
morally, and physically.
	The Relief Society is not alone a charit-
able institution, but stands for all that
will tend to bless and elevate home life,
the community and the great common-
wealth; in short, the universal brother-
hood and sisterhood of the world. The
spirit of the work permeates the soul of
those actively engaged in the performance
of its duties and responsibilities. But it
is not the object of the writer to elabor-
ate, but to chronicle events in their order
as relating to the grand achievements so
far as it has progressed until the present
period of its status.
	As towns and settlements grew under
the direction of President Brigham Young,
and those associated with him in settling
and colonizing Utah, and the surrounding
territory, there was in every ward one of
these societies of sisters, each with its
perfect equipment of officers and teach-
ers, such helps as were necessary to take
care of all those who needed assistance
of whatever name or nature, and the
local society and the Bishop of the ward
and brethren associated with him worked
in unison and harmony in building up the
ward and looking after the public inter-
ests. Meetings were held by the sisters
regularly, weekly or semi-monthly accord-
ing to the conditions of the people and
the surroundings in the respective so-
ciety. Finally there came a time when
President Young thought best to solidify
the wards into stakes consisting of a num-
ber of ecclesiastical wards convenient to
group together, and appointed a president
and two counselors to preside over each
stake.
	This same theory was afterwards put in
practice with the organizations known in
each ward as the Relief Society, President
Brigham Young himself leading out in
this direction.
	On the 19th of July, 1877, Sister Jane
S. Richards, of Ogden City, who had
been for some time presiding over the
wards within the city, and had been suc-
cessful in promoting several kinds of
employment for women and girls, invited
President Brigham Young to come up
and attend a meeting in Ogden City, and
to bring with him those he wished to ac-
company him; meantime Sister Richards
had invited the sisters and brethren of
the county from far and near to attend
the meeting, little anticipating what
would transpire; President Young, his
Counselor, President D. H. Wells, and
some of the Apostles, Sister Eliza R.

[Column 2]
Snow and the writer of this article also
being of the number, went up on the
morning train, and were present when
President Brigham Young called and ap-
pointed Sister Jane S. Richards to pre-
side over all the womens’ organizations in
Weber Stake of Zion. This was the com-
mencement of the consolidating of the
various branches of the Relief Society
into a stake organization.
	After some months President Jane S.
Richards chose her counselors, Mrs. Hat-
tie C. Brown and Mrs. Sarah A. Herrick,
and Mrs. Amelia M. Frodsham, secretary.
This constituted the Presidency of the
Relief Society of the Weber Stake.
	The second organization of a Relief
Society in a stake capacity was Salt Lake,
December 22, 1877. Notice having been
duly given President John Taylor and
some of the Apostles were invited to the
meeting, but were detained by business.
Presiding Bishop Edward Hunter and
Elder A. Milton Musser attended. Miss
Eliza R. Snow was elected chairman of
the meeting, and Mrs. E. B. Wells, secre-
tary, Miss Amy Adams assistant secretary.
The chairman explained the object of the
meeting and stated that President Brig-
ham Young had inaugurated a movement
previous to his death which she consid-
ered it proper to carry out. The meet-
ing was conducted strictly in accordance
with parliamentary usage. Mrs. Mary
Isabella Horne was elected President of
the Relief Society of the Salt Lake Stake
of Zion. Mrs. Elmina S. Taylor and
Mrs. Sarepta M. Heywood, counselors,
and Mrs. Elizabeth Howard, secretary
Mrs. Maria W. Wilcox cor. secy.; at a
subsequent meeting Mrs. Bathsheba W.
Smith was elected treasurer.
	Very soon after, at a meeting purposely
called at Farmington, Thursday, April 3,
1878, the branches of the Relief Society
of Davis County were grouped together
in a stake organization, Mrs. Sarah I.
Holmes was elected pres[-]dent, Mrs. Mary
S. and Mrs. Nancy A. Clark counselors,
and Miss Minerva Richards secretary.
	On April 21, 1878, at the Nephi meet-
ing house (Sunday), President George
Teasdale officiating, Apostle Erastus
Snow and Jacob G. Bigler being present,
the Juab Stake Relief Society was organ-
ized. The officers were Mrs. Amelia
Goldsborough president, Mrs. Elizabeth
Kendall and Mrs. Jane Picton counselors,
Mrs. Mary Pitchforth secretary, and Mrs.
Amy L. Bigler cor. sec’y.
	Box Elder Stake Relief Society was
organized at Brigham City, May 14, 1878
(Sunday). Bishop John Welch of that
place presented the names of the officers
who had been previously selected, they
were unanimously sustained: Mrs. Har-
riet A. Snow president, Mrs. Susannah
P. Booth and Mrs. Mary Stark counsel-
ors, Mrs. Mary E. Snow secretary, and
Mrs. Emilia D. Madsen cor. sec’y.
	The Relief Society of Kanab Stake was
organized June 9, 1878 (Friday), by Pres-
ident L. John Nuttall. Officers—Mrs.
Elizabeth Nuttall president, Mrs. Harriet
D. Bunting and Lucy R. Johnson coun-
selors. Mrs. Mary E. Little secretary, Mrs
L. A. Johnson asst. secy., Mrs. E H. D.
Solomon treasurer.
	The Relief Society of Morgan Stake
was organized July 20, 1878 (Sunday),
President Willard G. Smith officiating.
Officers elected were Lydia Rich presi-
dent, Ann Fry and Martha Tonks coun-

[Column 3]
selors, H. Cordelia Smith secretary and
Sarah A. Rawle cor. sec’y.
	Tooele Stake Relief Society was organ-
ized September 20. 1878 (Friday), by Presi-
dent Francis M. Lyman. Mrs. Mary Ann
Hunter president, Mrs. Elizabeth Clegg
and Mrs. Agnes Rich counselors, Mrs.
Mary A. House secretary and Mrs. Ann
Tate cor. sec’y.
	Utah Stake Relief Society was organ-
ized September 27, 1878 (Friday), by
President A. O. Smoot in Provo at the
meeting house. Sister Eliza R. Snow
was elected president of the meeting and
Albert Jones secretary. President Smoot
stated the object of the meeting and as-
sisted in the selection of the officers.
Mrs. Margaret T. Smoot president, Mrs.
Mary John and Mrs. Margaret A. Cluff
counselors, Mrs. Caroline Daniels secre-
tary, Mrs. Lucy W. Kimball asst. sec’y.
	The Relief Society of St. George Stake
organized at St. George September 13,
1878 (Friday), by Apostle Erastus Snow.
Officers, Mrs. Minerva W. Snow presi-
dent, Mrs. Anna S. Iyins and Mrs. Han-
nah H. Romney counselors, Mrs. Eliza S.
Calkins secretary and Mrs. Elizabeth P.
Bentley asst. sec’y.
	Summit Stake Relief Society organiza-
tion was effected October 25, 1878 (Sat-
urday), Apostle Franklin D. Richards
and President W. W. Cluff officiating
The officers sustained were Mrs. Sarah
S. Richards president, Mrs. Ann Cluff
and Mrs. Mary W. Reynolds counselors,
Mrs. Jane S. Boydon secretary.
	Millard Stake Relief Society was organ-
ized January 28, 1879, by President Ira
N. Hinckley, at a mass meeting held at
Fillmore (Tuesday). The officers sus-
tained were Mrs. Belinda M. Pratt presi-
dent, Delilah K. Olsen and Sarah L. Par-
tridge counselors, D. K. Olson secretary.
	Bear Lake Stake Relief Society was
organized April 17, 1879 (Friday), at
Paris, Idaho, by Apostle Charles C. Rich.
Mrs. Julia P. Lindsay was elected presi-
dent, Mrs. Elizabeth Collings and Mrs,
Maria Rich counselors, Mrs. Alice M.
Rich secretary and Mrs. Elizabeth Pug-
mire Assn’t secretary.
	The Relief Society of Sanpete Stake
was organized at Manti May 19, 1879
(Tuesday), by President Canute Peter-
son, the officers having been previously
appointed at a stake conference. Mrs.
Mary Ann P. Hyde president, Mrs. Eliza-
beth Allred and Mrs. Annie U. Larsen
counselors, Mrs. Fanny Kofford cor.
secy.
	The Relief Society of Sevier Stake was
organized at Richfield June 16, 1879
(Tuesday), by President R. K. Thurber,
Miss Eliza R. Snow and Mrs. Isabella M.
Horne. Mrs. Elizabeth Bean president,
Mrs. Jane Spencer and Mrs. Thirza M.
Thurber counselors, Mrs. Catherine Hunt
secretary.
	Wasatch Stake Relief Society was or-
ganized at Heber City September 3, 1879
(Wednesday), by President Abram Hatch,
Miss Eliza R. Snow and Mrs. Emmeline
B. Wells. The meeting was held in the
Heber City meeting house. Mrs. Emma
Brown was elected president, Mrs. Sarah
Alexander and Mrs. Mary Daybell coun-
selors, Mrs. Mary Bronson secretary.
	Parowan Stake Relief Society was or-
ganized September 28, 1879 (Sunday), at
the stake conference, President Henry
Lunt and Bishop Arthur officiating. Offic-
ers were Ellen W. Lunt president,


Mary Ann Lunt and Huldah D. Mitchell
counselors, Sarah Chatterley secretary
and Jane Mackelprag cor. secy.
    Panguitch Stake Relief Society was or-
ganized February 3, 1880, Apostle Eras-
tus Snow and President James Henrie
officiating. Officers elected were Mrs.
Barbara Myers president, Mrs. Sarah P.
Crosby secretary.
    Beaver Stake Relief Society organized
by President John R. Murdock. Mrs.
Lucinda Hand president, Mrs. Flora S.
Shipp secretary. 
    June 28, 1880, the Eastern Arizona
Stake was organized, with Mrs. Willmirth
East president, Mrs. Emma S. Smith and
Lois R. Hunt counselors, Ida F. Hunt,
secretary.

['JOSEPH—SEER, REVELATOR, PROPHET
	To the Latter-day Saint, no figure save
that of the Master himself, stands out with
such clearness and perfection as that of
Joseph Smith the Prophet. 
	Like most, if not all of the prophets of
old, his mission was placed upon him when
he was still a boy. The advent of the dif-
ferent prophets has seemed to be called for
by the necessity of the age, to divert the
thoughts of mankind from evil and dark-
ness into spiritual light, to turn the turbu-
lent waters stirred by the adversary again
to the peace and calm of God. 
	Joseph, son of Jacob, when a child, saw
in dreams his future power and greatness,
and with the guilelessness of youth related
these dreams to his father and brothers.
Joseph’s comeliness and beauty, his gentle-
ness and spiritual nature, which appealed so

[Column 2]

[picture of Joseph Smith]

strongly to his father’s affections, had al-
ready created feelings of jealousy and envy
in his brothers’ hearts, and when, aided by
his natural gifts, he assumed to declare his
future as seen in his dreams their envy
knew no bounds, and he was sold by them
into Egypt, which unknowingly hastened
the very fulfilment of his dreams. That the
power and gift of God was with him, even
as a child, is evident. 
	Joseph, the latter-day prophet, had
dreams and interpretations thereof, and
visions, and revelations, when only a child,
and, like Joseph of old, related them to his
father’s household, most of whom believed
and credited what he said. Like Joseph,
also, he was comely, straight and beautiful
to look upon; his soul shone in his coun-
tenance and his ways were gentle. 
	Samuel, also, was a mere lad when the
gift of prophecy came upon him. He
had been reared from infancy in the ser-
vice of God by a religious mother. His
early duties had been rather to serve than
to dictate, and yet when the occasion came,
though still a youth, his mighty power was
manifest and we read how he predicted to
Eli the fall of his home because of the wick-

[Column 3]
edness of his sons. In later life he became
a judge and great prophet in Israel. 
	Joseph Smith was reared by a religious
and devout mother, who never doubted the
divine mission of her son when he related
to her his marvelous experiences. Like
Samuel he became a prophet who brought
about great reforms, warned the wicked,
and taught most wondrous truths. 
	Daniel, another of the great prophets,
manifested spiritual gifts when a youth. He
foresaw and foretold the destiny of nations
and to him alone was made plain the writ-
ing on the wall. To him also was given
the interpretation of dreams. Like others
of the prophets he received divine protec-
tion so that no one could do him bodily
harm until his mission was ended. 
	Joseph Smith had like gifts and power. 
Like Daniel, he was many times miracu-
lously saved from bodily injury and possi-
ble death. He also foretold the destiny of
nations and warned the people of that most
terrible of strifes—the Civil war.
	David first attracted attention as a shep-
herd boy, and even then his prowess, his
agility, his strength and more than all these
physical qualities, his lofty spirit, made him


[Column 1] 
the superior of all his house. As a great
ruler and warrior, he is admired; as a
prophet and a poet he is loved. “In the
whole realm of lyric poetry” there is noth-
ing that approaches the songs of David,
and the tenderness and pathos of these
songs if there had been nothing else, would
have immortalized his name.
	Joseph Smith, too, was athletic, straight
and powerful in build, he could throw the
strongest in a wrestling match, yet he
never assumed to use his physical strength
for any purpose other than playful exer-
cise. He never attained political power
yet no king was more beloved nor revered
by his subjects than the later-day prophet
by his followers. His words of admoni-
tion, wisdom, prophecy, praise and glorifi-
cation of the Most High, like the songs of
David, vibrate the heart strings and will
echo down through the generations of
time.
	So all along the line of prophets are the
same attributes noticeable and much the
same of adventure and experience. In his
later years, Joseph, while continuing his
prophetic teachings, had that tender mourn-
fulness and sublime resignation which have
always marked the character of the mar-
tyrs. Though hated, derided, persecuted,
even unto death, there was never a sign of
weakness nor retraction in the true proph-
ets, for a power not of earth sustained
them.
	It was when Joseph was a lad of four-
teen that he went with troubled spirit into
the woods and prayed the Lord to show
him the right way; and it was in answer
to this prayer that he received his first
vision. From that time until his martyr-
dom, at the age of thirty-nine, were re-
vealed through him mighty and everlasting
truths to lead those who would believe to
eternal happiness. Of himself he said: “I
am a rough stone. The sound of the ham-
mer and chisel was never heard on me until
the Lord took me in hand. I desire the
learning and wisdom of heaven alone.”
Yet what a testimony to the world is given
in the wondrous things revealed by this 
humble, uneducated man—uneducated, as
he says, save by the power of God!
Through him the gospel of Christ was
brought again to the earth—the priesthood
re-established.
	The great progress in the status of
woman which is now so marked was fore-
told by Joseph, when he organized the
women into the Relief Society. A “School
of the Prophets” for religious teaching was
organized in Kirtland. The gathering of the
pure in heart from among the nations of the
earth that Zion might be built and her light
shine over all the world, was inaugurated.
The stranger, noting the unanimity and har-
mony that existed among the people, asked
the question of the Prophet, ‘How do you
govern these people?” The reply was, “I
teach them correct principles and they gov-
ern themselves.” He revealed the Word of
Wisdom, teaching mankind the necessity of
pure lives that death and disease may be
stayed; the thinkers of today who are striv-
ing most to assist men to care for life ad-
vance the same ideas.
	He revealed the great and glorious work
of baptism for the dead, thus fulfilling the
prophecies that “the hearts of the children
shall turn to their fathers.” In a word, dur-
ing the short period of twenty-four years
marvelous and remarkable truth’s were

[Column 2] 
given to the people concerning the things of
God which had furnished food for thought
and study for all the years following his
death. We are only now developing his
thoughts and trying to understand and com-
prehend his marvelous life and teachings.
The impress he has made upon this people
makes all Latter-day Saints feel as though
he was a part of them. How, then, must
those feel who knew and associated with
him in life! Their testimony is that he
was different from other men, so that those
who had never seen him before could dis-
tinguish him in a crowd; could feel the
magnetism of his presence, would rather be
a silent onlooker when he was near than
participate in any kind of pleasure. These
are testimonies of his possesion of some
power and influence more than natural.
	Let the Saints forever retain in their
hearts a reverence and love for so sublime
a character. Perhaps no words better por-
tray the man than the following from the
pen of President George Q. Cannon:
	“He had been a humble farmer lad—Di-
vine authority sat so becomingly upon him
that men looked at him with reverent awe.
He had been unlearned in the great things
of art and science—he walked with God un-
til human knowledge was to his eyes an
open book, and celestial light beamed
through his mind. His lofty soul com-
prehended the grandeur of his mission upon
earth; and with divine fortitude he fulfilled
the destiny which God had ordained for him
before he was.”

OR 
SPIRITUAL  AND  TEMPORAL
BY  AVONDALE.
CONTINUED.
 Is it not strange  that  professors  of  religion,
who  call  themselves  Christians, should  perse-
cute the  Latter-day Saints? So the  Pagans*
persecuted  the  disciples  of  Jesus  Christ  an-
ciently, whom they called "Christians," as men
now call his  followers  in the Chruch of Jesus
Christ of Latter-day Saints "Mormons." Strange
that  history  should  thus  repeat  itself  so ex-
actly!
	Mr. Missionary Unipod  had  an  intense an-
tipathy to the followers of Joseph Smith, with-
out knowing the reason why;  in this respect he
was  not  alone  among  professing  Christians.
The  conversion  of his  son  to  "Mormonism"
only increased  the  zeal  of this  gentleman  in
the  direction  of  antagonism  to the obnoxious
Latter-day Saints, of which  spirit the mission-
aries subject to his influence naturally partook.
	"Could you  not  find  any  denomination of
Christians to  ally yourself with without going
to these 'Mormons?'" asked the missionary  of
his  son,  as  though  the  Mormons  were  not
worthy  of  the  name  of  Christians.  "These
Mormons" was  spoken  in  a tone  of derision,
which the good man would  not have indulged
in had  he known  that the  Latter-day Saints
are believers in that same Jesus Christ that the
missionary  himself  extolled  so highly -- that
same Jesus who  in  his day was hated, as were
his  followers, for  the  truth's  sake, and  at last,
slain for the testimony of Jesus.

*The Pagans were heathens who lived in the
days of  the  early  Christians;  they  were  the
last  to  receive Christianity.  Pagan sacrifices
were  denounced  by edict  in  385 (by Theodo-
sius)  and  the  heathen  temples  closed, their
shrines and sacrifices were abolished. But Pagan
ceremonies  were  revived  in  the Christian
churches in the beginning of the fifth century,
and with them  the  dark  ages  of  Christianity
were  commenced  and  perpetuated. Then
Christianity become a  fashionable religion, as
it is in various forms to this our day.

	"Father," said  the  Mormon  convert,  "You
know nothing of the inner life of that religion
which  the  world  is  pleased  to  call 'Mormon-
ism,'  or  you  would  not, you  could not oppose
it;  the  very spirit  of  that Gospel  which Jesus
taught is incorporated into every article of our
faith."  "Your  faith,"  replied  the  father, "is
man made;  Joseph  Smith  was a money digger,
profligate and profane, pretending to have reve-
lations  and  the  visits  of  angels, even  of our
Savior Jesus Christ himself."
	"Father, you  have  listened  to  the  voice of
the  enemies of  Joseph Smith,"  replied his son,
"and not  to  his  friends, those who know him,
who have  received the  Gospel he was the hon-
ored  instrument  of  restoring  to  man.  Were
you to listen to the enemies of Jesus Christ, he
was 'a gluttonous  man  and  a  wine-bibber, a
friend  of  publicans  and  sinners.'  You would
not  countenance  the  acts  of  men who stoned
Stephen becasue the heavens were opened to
him, and  'he, being  full  of  the  Holy  Ghost,
looked up steadfastly into  heaven, and saw the
glory of God, and  Jesus standing on the right
hand of  God.'  You used to tell me, years ago,
that  'Jesus  is  coming  to  reign  on the earth,'
that 'the  prayers  of the  Saints  are to prevail
and  to  be  answered,' that  'the  kingdom  will
come, and his will be done on the earth as it is
in  heaven.'  How can  his  kingdom be estab-
lished  without  his  authority?  How  can  his
will be done  on  earth 'as it is done in heaven,'
unless the plan is revealed from heaven? Surely
you will  concede  to  Jesus  the right to restore
the Gospel, and that He had the right to choose
his own instrument.  Joseph Smith was a man
of large faith in God;  he prayed for revelation
from heaven, and God  appeared  to  him, with
his  Son  Jesus Christ, and  the  authority was
given to Joseph Smith to organize the  Church
of Jesus  Christ upon the earth again, as it ex-
isted  when  established  by  Jesus.  As  to the
humble position occupied by Joseph Smith, and
his  lack  of  the learning  of  the  age, the Jews
preferred  similar  charges  against  Jesus; they
challenged  his  authority: 'How  knoweth  this
man letters, having never learned?'  Jesus an-
swered  them,  and  said:  'My doctrine  is  not
mine, but his  that  sent  me.  If  any man will
do  his  will,  he  shall  know  of  the  doctrine,
whether  it  be  of  God, or  whether  I speak of
myself.  He  that  speaketh of himself seeketh
his  own  glory;  but he  that  seeketh his glory
that sent him, the same is true, and no unrigh-
teousness  is  in  him.'  The  Prophet Joseph
Smith always  honored  Jesus in  all his minis-
trations; he  never  took  glory  to  himself, but
gave all the  honor to God."
	"What a  pity  it  is,"  said  the  missionary,
"that  so  much  beautiful truth is embodied in
'Mormonism;'  it  is  this that deceives the igno-
rant and unwary."
	"Father," said the  Mormon convert, "it is a
system of truth, planned in the heavens before
the world was, for  the salvation of all the hu-
man family."
	"Well," said  the  missionary  father  to  his
Mormon son, "I believe you are sincere in your
religion, and I will pray for you;  I have prayed
for you  for  years, you  have  been remembered
at the  Throne  of  Grace, that  you might be able
to assist  in  the  great work in which I and my
fellow missionaries are  engaged--to preach the
Gospel  and be  numbered with  me in the Lon-
don City Mission."
	"Father," replied  the  son, overcome  by the
spirit of love  manifested  in  the words of his
father,  "your  prayers  are  answered;  I  have
embraced  the  Gospel, I have  lifted  up  my
voice in the highways and byways of this great
metropolis, calling  on men  to  have  faith  in
God and in our Lord Jesus Christ, to repent of
their sins  and be baptized for the remission of
sins, to receive the gift of the Holy Ghost by
they laying on of the hands of the Elders; and
although I have been met and opposed by some
of  the  missionaries  of  your  society,  as  have
many of my brethren  who  have been engaged
in preaching the Gospel, I have  rejoiced in my
labors, I have  realized that  your prayers have
be answered  in  my  behalf.  And  when  I
came to tell you, at my last  visit, that I had
received  the  truth, it was that I might cheer
your  heart  with  the  glad tidings  that I had
heard and obeyed the Gospel!"
	To these conversations between the mission-
ary father and his "Mormon" son many listened
attentively, and very few became angry;  when
the love  of  God fills  the bosom, and the peace
of God, which passeth all understanding, takes
possession  of  the  heart, there  will be  no ten-
dency  to  hate.  If no  other  good  can be  di-
rectly  traced  to  these  interviews, ever after-
wards  the  "Mormon"  missionaries,  in  going
forth  to  preach  the  Gospel  in  the  streets of
London, they were not opposed by the brethren
of the London City Mission.
	Could we  but  realize the breadth and com-
prehensiveness of the Gospel, we should all see
the necessity of this great Latter-day work, to
bring  to  pass  the  purposes  of  God,  as  made
known  to  the  ancient  fathers. "In thee, and
in  thy seed,  shall  all  the  nations of the earth
be blessed;"  this was the promise to Abraham,
and  renewed  to  his  offspring, the ancient pa-
triarchs.  This  is  why  God  has again spoken
to man, and  sent his angel to carry out his de-
signs -- those of  blessing  the  entire  human
family, "to  turn  the  hearts  of  the fathers  to
the children  and the hearts of the children  to
the  fathers."  Such  is  the  work begun by the
Prophet  Joseph  Smith  in  these  days, and  it
will be carried on to its final consummation !
		To be Continued.


', "THE BOY PROPHET.
	One hundred years ago, in an obscure
town in the Green Mountain State, Ver-
mont, a baby boy first saw the light of 
day; this babe, as we know now, was
destined to bring about great changes in
the religious world and in the lives and 
fortunes of many peoples; changes for
the betterment of physical conditions as
well as to teach great truths with the 
wisdom not of books, or the learning 
gained from great teachers familiar with
sciences, or from men of higher educa-
tion, but with the understanding quick-
ened by the Holy Spirit that flows from 
the fountain of light, and is like a well-
spring of living water that never fails.
	Unless one accepts this theory it is not
possible to account for the marvelous
works which have been brought to pass
through the power of the Priesthood,
this boy received from those who came
and ministered unto him from the regions
of glory, the world of heavenly light.
Think what a wonderful manifestation
was given this lad when but fourteen 
years of age, he went into the woods to 
pray, to inquire of the Lord, which of all
the different churches was the right one.
	The boy was so wrought upon in the 
midst of a great religious revival that pre-
vailed in the vicinity, that he knew not 
what to do. He searched the Scriptures
for light and found the passage in the
Epistle of James which reads, “If any of
you lack wisdom let him ask of God who
giveth to all men liberally and upbraideth 
not and it shall be given him,” and this
young Joseph, with implicit faith, sought
the Lord in humility and prayer, he was
alone, it was his first prayer vocally, and
he felt the powerful influence of nature.
The poet says “the groves were God’s first
temples,” and no doubt like Jacob of old
he wrestled mightily in solemn prayer, 
and a voice from on high spoke to him, 
and directed him what course he must 
take. The world would not believe him.
Many times afterwards heavenly messen-
gers were sent to minister unto him, and
he suffered almost continuous persecution
because of unbelief.
	Subsequently he received knowledge of
the hidden record and translated it by the
gift and power of the Holy Ghost, the
work was beginning to grow, the restor-
ation of the Everlasting Gospel in the Lat-
ter-day dispensation.
	When one reflects upon what the
Prophet Joseph did in the few years he
was permitted to teach and preach, look-
ing at the matter as a serious problem,
taking the bare facts of the case into con-
sideration, an unlettered boy, one cannot
but feel it to be a marvel and a wonder.
Founding and organizing a church and
enunciating doctrines, that cannot be con-
troverted by the most celebrated divines
––a church that has held people together
as a body of religious worshippers for three

[Column 2]
quarters of a century, people too of many
nations, looking at it naturally it seems
incredible, and yet it has been demon-
strated in the eyes of the whole world. 
	Nothing could produce the effect of 
uniting together people of such a great
variety of nations, languages, customs,
habits and sentiments to live in peace and 
harmony and establish themselves in
communities, fulfilling temporal duties
and obligations except the Spirit of the
Lord; all testify alike of the divinity of
the Gospel as revealed though the Proph-
et Joseph Smith, and carried forward by
his successors in office down to the pres-
ent time.
	The truths proclaimed by the Prophet
Joseph have permeated the world, not
only has the Everlasting Gospel as taught
by the Savior and His Apostles been
preached in many lands and in thousands
of cities bringing converts by tens of thou-
sands, but the influence accompanying
these divine principles has spread like
the mustard seed, blown hither and thith-
er, and has developed a vast change of 
sentiment among the children of men,
light has come into the world, the glori-
ous, radiating light that will eventually
revolutionize the world of mankind, and
men yet unborn will come to acknowledge
the boy Prophet a great benefactor, as
well as Seer and Revelator.
	The saying of the celebrated Josiah
Quincy of Massachusetts, who visited the
Prophet Joseph Smith at Nauvoo, Ill.,
in company with Hon. Charles Francis
Adams, Sr., on the 15th of May, 1844,
forty-three days before the martyrdom,
has at present every likelihood of coming
to pass. In describing his impressions of
this man whom he saw, among other re-
marks in his published report, he made
use of the following language: “It is not
at all improbable that some text book for
the generations yet unborn might contain
this question: What historical American
of the nineteenth century has exerted the
most powerful influence upon the desti-
nies of his countrymen. And it is by no
means impossible that the answer might 
be Joseph Smith the Mormon Prophet.”
	The erection of the handsome and
durable marble monument on the spot
where this baby boy was born marks an
event that will mean much to the chil-
dren of Zion, more potent in creating
sentiment than volumes of written his-
tory; it is an object lesson that will be
of lasting duration, and will attract the
attention of strangers, as well as our own
people. 
	This hundredth anniversary has had a
telling effect in many ways; it has revived
old memories of those who knew the
Prophet personally, and brought out many
incidents of his life and recollections of
the man himself, his majestic appearance,
his commanding figure, his dignified bear-
ing, his gracious manner, his innate re-
finement, his spiritual eloquence, his great
gift of healing the sick and afflicted, and
in short everything pertaining to his re-
ligious, social and domestic life, that
could be told by those most familiar with
him under all the peculiar circumstances
that he had to meet and combat with,
in introducing and carrying forward the
work to which he had been called by
divine authority.
	Joseph Smith was as tender-hearted as
a little child, this is the testimony of all
and yet he had the courage of a lion, it is

[Column 3]
a true saying that “the gentlest are the
bravest.”
	Joseph Smith the Prophet was assured-
ly a philanthropist in the broadest accep-
tation of the term. In all his labors he
proved the philanthropic nature of his 
character. The Church he founded, hav-
ing been called of God, gave to man and
woman equal privileges of voting on all
subjects, and at that time, in 1830, such
a thing was unheard of, as far as our
knowledge of churches extends; and he
was the first man to organize the women
of the Church into a philanthropic so-
ciety, taking the initiative himself, retir-
ing from the meeting while they elected 
their own officers; advising them to ob-
serve parliamentary usage in their meet-
ings. This was March 17, 1842, long be-
fore woman’s organizations had become
a part and parcel of the world’s activities.
This benevolent society named by the few
women who met together in that first 
meeting “The Relief Society” has grown
and flourished ever since, and of the small
number assembled on that occasion, 
there is now just one representative. wo-
man to testify of the commencement of
that event which marks an epoch in the
progress of women in this “dispensation
of the fulness of times.” This woman,
Bathsheba W. Smith, is the General
President of the Society, that at the pres-
ent time reaches around the world, and
is not only national but international in
its scope, having branches in many for-
eign lands and on the islands of the sea,
and it is a favorite saying of its President
and others, that the sun never sets upon
the Relief Society, which is verily true.
	The women of the world who deride
the name and who defame the character
of Joseph Smith, should take a little time
to look up on his advanced ideas of wo-
manhood. His gentle, chivalrous and
reverential manner towards all women
was a distinguishing trait in the character
of this most remarkable and wonderful
man. One could not meet him even
casually without feeling the potent influ-
ence of the atmosphere that enveloped
him, as entirely different from other men
of the same period. Though he was in-
deed the greatest man of his time (that 
the writer has ever seen) yet one of his 
chief attributes was humility, and also
forbearance with the weaknesses and
frailties of those with whom he was
brought in contact.
	The Prophet Joseph Smith revealed to
this generation many mighty truths
hitherto unknown in the world of man-
kind, and the work founded by him has
brought salvation to many thousands,
and they have been shown how to live,
and what to do, that future happiness in
the family relation might be secured.
There is no other church that teaches
these fundamental truths. One of the 
greatest testimonies of the divinity of
the Latter-day work established by the 
Prophet Joseph through revelation and
authority from on high is the knowledge
obtained concerning sacred temples,
wherein holy ordinances are performed
for the living and the dead. To those who
have entered into this sublime work with
pure hearts, it has given them higher
views of this life, and has opened up
vistas of light and understanding that no
gold or riches could purchase from them.
Can one imagine greater progress than
this, the unfolding of hidden mysteries,


the receiving of keys to open doors closed
for centuries, whereby those who sit in
darkness may be admitted into light and
be eventually redeemed and partake of the
blessings promised through the ordinances
of the Gospel?
	The recent visit of President Joseph F.
Smith to the State of Vermont is an 
event of great significance more so than
has yet appeared. Greater things will
grow out of this journey than even the
dedication of this beautiful monument,
which is in itself a splendid tribute to the
martyred Prophet, and although much
has been accomplished among many peo-
ples of many lands, since the Prophet
Joseph Smith was born, yet greater works
are yet to come, and those who live to-
day will see more temples reared, to the
name of the Lord, more waste places
made habitable, and though calamities
may come, and wars not cease, and the
very elements of the earth be in commo-
tion as has been prophesied not only by
ancient prophets but by the Prophet
Joseph Smith in this our day, yet Zion
will prosper and grow famous, and those
who are faithful and true will have part
in the blessings that will be poured out
in rich abundance upon this glorious
land, America.


WOMAN'S EXPONENT


"]

['VISIT IN ST. GEORGE STAKE.
	Sisters Ann C Woodbury and Emma
Morris, of the St. George presidency of the
Relief Society, and Mary G. Whitehead,
of the Primary, with Brother Samuel Miles
as driver, left St. George, Thursday, the
28th of July, and stopped overnight at
Chadburns. 29th, went on to Pine Valley,
held meeting at 2 p.m. with the Relief
Society, were very pleased to have the
Bishop and a number of the brethren meet
with us. At 4 p.m. we met with the Pri-
mary, a good spirit prevailed. The bishop
blessed us with his presence, which was
very encouraging. We had a good rain
that night, which was very gratifying, also
a boon to the country, as the crops needed
it badly. Went on to Pinto and met with
the Primary at 10 a.m. next day. It was
refreshing to meet with the bright children,
who paid good attention. We took dinner
with the Relief Society president, Sophia
H. Forsyth, after which we had a meeting
of the Society, and then went to Enterprise.
President Lydia Winsor entertained us for
the night. Sunday morning we met with
the Sabbath school, then we held a Primary
meeting. We took dinner with Sister Bar-
locher.
	In the afternoon, after the sacrament
was administered, the Bishop turned the
meeting over to the sisters, and we had an
enjoyable time. Bishop gave us much en-
couragent. Afterwards went on to Thomas
Terry’s ranch, where we were kindly en-
tertained. August 1st we traveled on to
Clover, there we met Brother Lyman Woods
and family, they are all that live there. We
held a meeting, with twenty-one present,
counting the children, and had an enjoy-
able time. Brother and Sister Woods are
in poor health, their farm is almost de-
stroyed through the railroad draining their
meadow land; but we rejoiced to meet with
a people that were thirsting for righteous-
ness. We spent the night there. The fol-
lowing day traveled twenty-five miles to
Panacca. President Sarah Lee gave us a
hearty welcome; and held a meeting at 10
a.m. next day with Primary. We were
invited to Brother John Lee’s to dinner,
and had a nice time with the sisters. In
the afternoon we met with the Relief Soci-
ety and had a lovely lot of sisters present.
President Lee invited all the sisters to meet
at her house at 7 o’elock p.m.. and had a
nice sociable time—ice cream and cake,
with a select program.
	On the 4th we traveled to Bristol Wells,
thirty-five miles; had to draw water from a
well for the horses; we laid our quilts on the
ground under a tree and had a good night’s
rest. In the morning we found a rattle-
snake under our carriage; it did not hurt
us, so we let it go. On the following day
drove to the Lewis ranch, where we slept
and rested well. Next d[-]y went to Lund,
and met there Brother Adolphus White-
head, Sister Mary Whitehead’s son, and
his wife, who is Sister Morris’ daughter,
and I was welcomed at the home of my
nephew, Brother George Gardner.
	On the 7th of August we met with the
Sabbath school and bore our testimony to
the truth of the Gospel, as it was fast
day. In the afternoon, after sacrament,
Bishop Orin Snow turned the meeting
over to the sisters, and we had a good time
and excellent spirit.
	On the 8th we went to Preston held
meeting at 10 a.m. with the Relief Society,
and at 2 p.m. with the Primary; were en-
tertained at Bishop Arnoldson’s. The peo-
ple all came out to our meetings; we feel
it is a pleasure to meet with the Saints of
God. Returned to Lund that night; the
9th we met at Eva Whitehead’s with the
prominent sisters, talked on the duties of
the officers of the Relief Society in regard
to storing grain and creating a fund for the
benefit of the poor in Zion.
	Aug. 10, started home 13th reached E[-]gle
Valley, held an evening meeting, had a
good turnout; there isn’t many here that
care anything for the Gospel. We reached
Modena on the 14th, rested our horses and
traveled about fifteen miles, and camped
for the night; next morning drove to Holt’s,
for breakfast.
	We arrived home all right, having trav-
eled between four and five hundred miles,
gone eighteen days, held thirteen meetings,
and trust we have done some good in urg-
ing the people to their duties. Ever pray-
ing for the welfare of Zion, I remain,
		Your sister in the Gospel.
', 'AN INTERESTING TRIP.
	President Ann C. Woodbury and Emma
J. Morris, her counselor, of the presidency
of the Relief Society, and Rosinia Jarvis,
president of the Young Ladies and aid to
the Primary of the St. George Stake, left
August 12 to visit the western settlements,
including White River.
	We started out early, this being a hard
day’s drive, owing to the road being sandy
and up hill. We arrived at Pine Valley in
the rain, which was very welcome. We
held a meeting with the Primary. Here
we met a lot of well behaved children, and
the sisters gave them good instruction. A
very small girl, Maggie Gardner, played
the organ, the children sang beautifully.
	The next morning we held a Relief
Society meeting, Sister Ann Snow presid-
ing. We urged the sisters to store grain,
live their religion, feed the poor, clothe the
naked and comfort the hearts of those that
were bowed down.
	After partaking of Sister Jane Gardner’s
hospitality, we went to Pinto and met with
the Relief Society in the afternoon, in the
evening with the Young Ladies, where all
bore their testimonies. We had a delight-
ful time. Sister Neil Forsyth entertained
us nicely, and in the early morning we met


[Column 1]
with the Primary, mothers and all. The
Lord is surely with this people.
	We had an enjoyable time and went on
to Hebron, held meeting in the afternoon
and met a few sisters. The Lord has
said, "Where there are a few met together,
in my name, I will be there, and that to
bless them.” Sister Huntsman had died
away at a ranch, and some of the people
were taking care of the dead, and others
were out on ranches
	We had a nice meeting and went on to
Sister Mary A. Terry’s ranch, where we
were kindly cared for. We then went to
Clover Valley and met with the Relief
Society and Primary. There are but few
people here. It is truly gratifying to meet
with people in these little out of the way
places. The Gospel is planted in their
hearts and meets with a ready response
when they hear it.
	Next morning we went and reorganized
the Primary. Clara C. Lytle is the new
president.
	We traveled on to Panacea and held
Primary in the evening. Here we met a
bright lot of children. Sunday Sister Mor-
ris and I visited an afflicted sister and com-
forted her, also attended Sabbath School.
We had the privilege of talking to the chil
dren. We went to meeting, and after the
sacrament was administered the Bishop
called on Brother Miles and Rosinia Jarvis
to speak. At the close of the meeting we
met with the Young Ladies and had a joy-
ful time. In the evening we met with the
Relief Society. We urged the sisters to
provide for the poor, and let no one suffer
in their midst. All the trouble is we did
not stay long enough. After meeting, the
young ladies treated us to ice cream and
cake. Here we were kindly entertained
by Sister Sarah Lee, president of the Relief
Society, who gave us fifty pounds of oats
for our horses.
	We went on to Bristol Wells where we
camped for the night, then went to Brother
John Lee’s ranch, where we were warm-
ly welcomed. They have a fine ranch with
large springs and excellent crops. The
White River valley has many fine ranches
in it.
	We went to Lund and met Brother
Thomas Judd and wife, former residents of
St. George. We met many other friends,
and next day held a meeting with the
Primary, had a good congregation of both
parents and children. The sisters gave
good instructions.
	We then went on to Preston. Here the
people turned out en masse, and one or two
vehicles came loaded from Lund to join our
meeting. There is truly a good people.
Many moved here from Moroni, Sanpete
Co., mostly young men, to make homes.
We had a nice time, in the evening had a
meeting of the Relief Society, but I think
almost everybody was there. We had an
excellent meeting, and afterwards were en-
tertained by Bishop Arnoldson and wife.
They asked us to visit with them on Satur-
turday evening on our return.

[Column 2]
	On our way to Georgetown we passed
through the Ely mining town, there is
quite a population there seemingly, lots of
business going on. The water belonging
to George Town passes by there. The
miners want the water, they have made an
offer, for it. The people think of selling
out to them and get further away from a
mining camp.
	In the afternoon we met with the Pri-
mary, in the evening with the Relief
Society and Young Ladies and talked on
the duties of the different associations.
Bishop Morley said he would walk twenty
mites to attend such a meeting, and pro-
posed to give us a vote of thanks which
was unanimous. The people here are
broken up on account of expecting to sell
their water. They are so near a mining
camp that it will be a blessing to get away
from it. They wished us to spend more
time with them.
	The next day we returned to Preston
and held meeting there. Brother Miles spoke
upon his knowledge of the Prophet Joseph
Smith and the persecutions of the Saints.
Brother Samuel Miles, seventy-six years
old, could remember many things about the
Prophet. Sisters Jarvis, Morris and Wood-
bury bore testimony.
	We went on to Lund next day and at-
tended the Sabbath School. Brother Miles
and the sisters talked to the children. In
the afternoon after the sacrament had been
administered the meeting was turned over
to the sisters and Brother Miles who spoke
to an appreciative audience. In the evening
we met with the Relief Society. Sister
Mary J. Judd resigned as president of the
Relief Society on account of moving away.
The resignation was accepted, and a vote
of thanks was given her for her past ser-
vices. Emily A Burgess was voted in
as president, Ann E. Walker and Mary L.
Oxborough, counselors. We had an en-
joyable time.
	The next day we left Lund arrived at
the Reardon ranch. They made us very
welcome and invited us to dinner. They
had lived here 35 years. Last year they
sold $300.00 worth of stock. Here are love-
ly springs and is a nice location for a num-
ber of families. Wishing them well we
we went on to Brother John Lee’s, where
we spent the night and were made very
welcome by his entire family.
	In the morning we left Brother Lee’s
and went a couple of mites and called on
Mrs Lewis, who has a big ranch of a hun-
dred acres, fenced, and with immense
springs to water it. We went on to Bris-
tol Wells and camped. Next day we went
on to Pages, where an old lady was alone.
She pumped the water for our horses. Her
husband was away on a ranch they had
fourteen miles away, and she had no neigh-
bors that we could see.
	We went on to Eagle Valley, where we
held meeting in the evening, Sister Sarah
Hammond presiding. There are very few
Latter-day Saints in this valley. It is a
flourishing valley, we went on to Modena

[Column 3]
and met friends. Brother Brigham Lund,
gave us a sack of oats for our horses.
	We started for Enterprise and found our-
selves at Hebron where we spent the night
with Bishop Holt’s interesting family.
	We went to Enterprise next day and held
meeting at ten a. m., had a good meeting.
The people are living in hope that a big
reservoir that is being built will benefit
them.
	We then went on to Mouseley’s and
spent the night.
	On the 30th we arrived home, having
traveled five hundred miles, been gone
nineteen days and held nineteen meetings.
We feel that the Lord has blessed and pre-
served us, and give him the praise for the
joy and satisfaction we have had in visit-
ing the Saints.
', 'VISIT THROUGH ST. GEORGE STAKE.

	I thought a few lines from this part of
the country might interest your readers.
	President Ann C. Woodbury and Coun-
selor Emma Morris, of the Relief Society,
and Rose Jarvis, of the Y. L. M. I. A.,
and Mary Whitehead, of the Primary, of
the St. George Stake of Zion, with Leon
Jarvis as teamster, left St. George on the
17th of August, to visit the settlements on
the Rio Virgen River.
	We arrived at Toquerville the same eve-
ning, and held meetings with the Relief
Society, Young Ladies’ and Primary.
	On the 28th we went on to Virgen City
at 10 a. m., met with the Relief Society
and had a good time. At 3 p. m. we met
with a nice lot of the Primary children. At
8 p. m. met with the Young Ladies. After
the sisters had spoken, all the girls present
bore their testimonies.
	The 18th we went on to Grafton, where
there is a small settlement. We held
meeting at 10 a. m. with the Relief Society
and Primary together, and after partaking
of Bishcp Ballard’s hospitality, we went on
to Springdale, where we met in a bowery
with the sisters and a very fine lot of chil-
dren. Brother Alpheus Gifford, who has
recently lost his eyesight and is quite blind,
and one of the Bishop’s counselors met
with us. Here they have very fine crops,
and we truly breathed the air of freedom.
	The 20th, which was Sunday, we came
back to Rockville. At 10 a. m. met with
the Sabbath School. At 2 p. m. we met
with the Saints in their regular meeting.
After the sacrament had been administered,
the meeting was given over to the sisters.
We urged the Saints to do their duties.
After the services we met with the Primary.
At 8 p. m. we met with the Mutual Im-
provement Association and enjoyed it very
much.
	On the 21st we traveled to Leeds. At 4
p. m. we met with the Relief Society and
Primary, and at 8 p. m. with the Young
Ladies. We had a joyful time. Almost
all the girls bore their testimonies. We
were entertained by Sister Ira McMullin,
whose husband is on a mission.
	On the 22nd we went to Harrisville
There they have no water; everything is
almost dried up. It is a small settlement.
We held a Primary meeting and invited all
to come. We felt well in meeting with the
sisters and children. We had dinner with
Sister Laney and then returned home,
having been away six days. We remained
home eleven days, and then started to visit
the western settlements of the St. George
Stake.
	On the 2nd of September, Brother Rich-
ard Morris, Stake superintendent of the
Sabbath School; President Ann C. Wood-
bury, of the Relief Society; Rosa Morris, of
the Y.L.M.I.A., and Mary G. Whitehead
left St. George. We arrived at Pine Valley
in the evening and put up at Bishop Snow’s.
Sunday we attended Sabbath School.
Brother Morris and the sisters talked. They
have a nice Sabbath School there. We met
with the Saints in sacrament meeting, after
which we met with the Primary and re-
organized it. At 7:30 p. m., we met with
the Relief Society and Y. L. M. I. A. We
had an interesting time, many of the sisters
bore testimony to the truth of the work
in which we are engaged.
	On the 4th we went to Grass Valley. We
met with the people and had a joyful time.
All bore testimony to the truth of the Gos


pel. We had dinner with Brother Rancher
and family. At 5 p. m. left for Pinto and
met with the Primary at 7:30 p. m. We
held meeting with the Relief Society and
Y. L. M. I. A.: had a nice time. 
	The 5th we traveled on to Enterprise.
At 3 o’clock we met with the Primary. At
8 p. m. we organized a Relief Society. Re-
organized a Y. L. M. I. A.
	Talked with the sisters in Pine Valley
on the silk industry. We were showing a
sample of the silk we had made. They
said, “Why don’t you talk it up?” I
thought I had talked enough. They said
“We can’t raise silk here, but we can help
about the ribbon loom.” I talked about it
here and a gentleman, not of our faith, by
the name of Marshal, gave me a dollar to-
wards the silk. Bishop Hall met with us
and we had an excellent meeting.
	The 6th we went on to Hebron. At 10
a. m. we met with the Primary; lots of fine
children. Met with the Relief Society.
Bishop Holt attended all the meetings.
	The 7th we went on to Clover Valley.
Met with the Sunday School and Primary
children at 7:30 in the evening, also with
the Relief Society, and Y. L. M. I. A.
	On the 8th we continued our journey on
to Panacca. On the 9th held Primary
meeting at 4 p. m.; also met with the Re-
lief Society and Y. L. M. I. A., and re-
organized the latter.
	The 10th being Sunday, we attended
Sunday School and sacrament meeting,
after which we were invited to a water
melon feast at Brother John Lee’s. Sister
Jarvis met with the Young Ladies’ at 5 p.
m., and held a testimony meeting. We
also had a general meeting at 8 p. m., and
had an enjoyable time. While at Panacca
we stayed at Brother Arthur Lee’s and
were invited out to other places and shared
the hospitality of the people.
	On the 11th we continued our journey
over a broken country to Eagle Valley.
The people, owing to being in such an out
of the way place, have not been visited by
missionaries very often. They thoroughly
appreciated our visit. We met with the
Sunday School at 4 p.m.,and held a general
meeting at 8 p. m. The people were in
from Spring Valley. The first principles
of the Gospel were dwelt upon and we
truly had a time of rejoicing.
	On the 12th we left Eagle Valley and
started for Deer Lodge. We took the
wrong road and traveled on a wood road
about two and a half miles. Brother John
Hammond overtook us and said we would
have a “dear lodge” that night if we
kept on that road; so we returned and ar-
rived at Deer Lodge at 12 o’clock. We
took dinner with Sister Whitehead’s daugh-
ter, Mamie Lund. At 1 p. m. we started
for Bishop Holt’s. We stopped at Modena
and fed and watered the horses. We ar-
rived at Brother Holt’s having traveled
sixty-five miles that day. Sister Holt
kindly entertained us.
	On the 13th we left for St. George and
arrived in the evening, having traveled
there hundred and twenty-five miles and
held thirty-seven meetings. We trust we
have done some good in the cause of truth.
			Your sister in the Gospel,
